# Life Dashboard — Product Requirements Document

**Product Name:** Life Dashboard
**Internal Codename:** APEX (engineering / architecture / backend)
**Legal Entity:** Apex Leadership LLC
**Version:** 1.27 (Living Document)
**Document Owner:** Founder & CEO
**Last Updated:** April 22, 2026
**Status:** Draft — Living Document
**Classification:** Internal / Investor-Grade

---

## Naming Convention

Throughout this document:

- **"Life Dashboard"** is the user-facing product name — used in UI strings, App Store listings, marketing, and anything a user sees.
- **"APEX"** is retained as an internal codename for engineering prose — the backend, the platform, the API layer, cost attribution tables, data infrastructure. Where "APEX" appears in technical sections (§5 Architecture, §6 Data/Privacy, §7 Security, §8 Connections, §11 Infrastructure), it refers to the backend platform that powers Life Dashboard.
- **"Apex Leadership LLC"** is the legal entity.
- The four category names are **Fitness**, **Nutrition**, **Finance**, and **Time**. The rollup across all four is the **Overall Score** (no brand prefix).

# Section 1 — Executive Summary

## 1.1 What Life Dashboard Is

Life Dashboard turns scattered life data — meals, workouts, bank accounts, emails, tasks, calendar, sleep — into a single intelligent dashboard that tells users what matters most today.

Life Dashboard unifies four domains of a person's life into a single real-time view scored on a 0–100 scale per category and overall. The four categories are **Fitness**, **Nutrition**, **Finance**, and **Time**. Each category draws from connected data sources (Apple HealthKit, Strava, Garmin, Plaid, Google Calendar, Gmail, manual input) that normalize into a common daily metrics model. A deterministic scoring engine applies research-backed setpoints and user-specific baselines to produce scores that are fast, free, reproducible, and explainable. An AI chatbot layer sits on top of the unified data to answer context-aware questions about what to eat, how to spend, when to work out, and which tasks to prioritize right now.

## 1.2 The Problem

The target user runs their life across six to ten apps that don't talk to each other: MyFitnessPal for meals, Apple Health for body metrics, a bank app or Mint for money, Google Calendar for schedule, Gmail for communication, Notion or Apple Reminders for tasks, a fitness app for workouts. Each app is good at its domain in isolation but none of them combine to answer the questions users actually ask themselves: *"Am I on track this week?"* *"Should I skip this workout?"* *"Can I afford this dinner out?"* *"What should I do with the next two hours?"*

The result is cognitive overhead. Users spend mental energy stitching together a picture that should be automatic. They miss connections — sleep debt that's hurting their workouts, spending patterns that correlate with stress, meetings that are destroying their deep work time. They set goals and lose track of them across the fragmentation. They know they should be more disciplined but the tools fight them.

## 1.3 The Solution

Life Dashboard collapses the fragmentation. One dashboard. Four cards. One overall view. Automatic data inflow from every major platform. A chatbot with full life context that can answer any question about what the user should be doing.

The four categories are the spine of the product:

- **Fitness** — body, recovery, sleep, HRV, weight, workouts, performance trends
- **Nutrition** — calories, macros, hydration, pantry, meal consistency
- **Finance** — spending, budget, bills, savings rate, discretionary control
- **Time** — the shape of the day itself: sleep regularity, attention fragmentation, location intentionality, schedule density balance, rhythm adherence

Three of these four — Fitness, Nutrition, Finance — map directly to how the target user already thinks and tracks today. They are the hard-edged optimizable domains each with dedicated data sources and clear metrics. The fourth, Time, is genuinely novel: a score that measures how coherent a user's day was, derived entirely from passive platform data (HealthKit sleep, Screen Time, CoreLocation, Calendar) that the user did not consciously produce. The user cannot game the Time score because they are not asked to log anything. The phone watched the day happen and the score reflects what the phone saw.

The scoring system is the differentiator. Every category uses deterministic math, not AI, which means scores are:

- **Fast** — render in milliseconds, no API round-trips
- **Free** — no variable cost per score calculation
- **Reproducible** — the same inputs always produce the same score
- **Explainable** — users can drill into any score and see exactly what drove it
- **Personalized** — scores use the user's own 30-day baselines once established

AI is reserved for specific jobs where it is the best available tool for reducing user friction or delivering intrinsic value: cleaning messy data (meal parsing from text or photo), powering the life-aware chatbot, and drafting content the user then approves (email replies, meal suggestions). AI is never used for AI's sake; every AI call must justify its cost in user value and API spend. Unsolicited AI prose is banned everywhere in the product except the Notifications surface (§4.9), which is the sole user-opted-in channel for proactive AI copy. Descriptive AI — narrating already-computed numbers, user-invoked via an explicit "Explain" action — is allowed. Prescriptive AI — "you should do this" language — is banned globally.

## 1.4 The Success Ladder

Life Dashboard creates user value through a specific cascade:

1. **Cognitive load reduction** — Users no longer juggle six apps. Data flows in automatically; the dashboard is the single view.
2. **Better decisions** — With full context available, users make better calls about food, workouts, spending, and time allocation — either by looking at the dashboard or by asking the AI.
3. **Behavior change** — Better daily decisions compound into measurable behavior change: more consistent workouts, more tracked meals, more replied-to emails, more completed tasks.
4. **Goal achievement** — Behavior change translates into users reaching the specific goals they set (weight targets, savings targets, project completion) at higher rates than without the app.
5. **Identity shift** — Over time, users see themselves as more disciplined people because of Life Dashboard. The app becomes part of how they view themselves, not just a tool they use.

Product investment priorities follow this ladder. V1.0 must nail steps 1 and 2. V2.0 proves step 3 with data. V3.0+ is where identity-level features (streaks, badges, community, emotional scaffolding) live.

## 1.5 Primary User

The Ambitious Generalist with Optimizer tendencies. Ages 20–30. Building a career, running a side hustle, training seriously, managing real money. Currently juggling six or more apps to run their life and frustrated by the fragmentation. Competent but stretched thin. Willing to pay for tools that make them better.

They already use MyFitnessPal or Whoop or Oura. They already have a Plaid-connected app (Copilot, Monarch, YNAB). They already use Google Calendar. They are not new to self-tracking — they are tired of self-tracking in seven different places. Life Dashboard's value proposition is not *"start tracking your life"* — it's *"stop juggling your life."*

## 1.6 Business Model

Two paid tiers, no free tier. **Pricing locked in v1.21 (see §13):**

- **Life Dashboard Core** — **$4.99/month or $49.99/year** ($4.17/mo effective on annual). The unified life dashboard for most users.
  - 10 AI chatbot queries per day
  - Plaid included (all your bank accounts)
  - Unlimited email and calendar accounts across Gmail, Outlook, Google Calendar, Apple EventKit
  - All device-native and cloud connections (HealthKit, Health Connect, Strava, Garmin if approved, Screen Time, Location, Weather)
  - All four category scores, Overall Score, full goals library, full deterministic features
  - Full meal logging including standard AI photo scan (Sonnet), text/voice parse, barcode, saved meals
  - Full workout logging including AI plan generation (1/30 days) and revision (3/plan)
  - 90-day hot history
  - CSV data export
  - Chatbot Audit 30-day retention
  - For users running a typical life who want one serious dashboard

- **Life Dashboard Pro** — **$9.99/month or $99.99/year** ($8.33/mo effective on annual). The power-user version for heavier workflows.
  - 50 AI chatbot queries per day (with 300/day soft cap for abuse protection)
  - Premium Scan (Opus-model meal photo analysis, 10/day)
  - Pantry Scanner (10/day)
  - AI-Drafted Email Replies with style learning (20/day)
  - Higher plan generation quota (3/30 days) and revision quota (10/plan)
  - Unlimited archived history beyond 90-day hot window
  - PDF report export (CSV is Core)
  - Historical score immutability extended to 30 days (vs 7 for Core)
  - Priority email support
  - For users with heavier AI workflows and deep historical needs

**14-day free Pro trial at signup.** Trial end forces a choice screen — user picks Core or Pro to continue; no auto-downgrade or auto-upgrade. Full details in §13.4.

The v1.18 decision to drop the 2-account cap on Core is preserved in v1.21. Multi-account connection breadth is not a tier differentiator; Pro's differentiation is AI feature richness, history depth, and formatted export.

This pricing positions Life Dashboard aggressively at launch to drive volume over margin. Core at $4.99 sits below the psychological subscription floor for serious tools; Pro at $9.99 preserves the sub-$10 impulse bracket. Together they undercut the combined monthly cost of the specialty apps Life Dashboard replaces (Strava Premium + MacroFactor + Copilot Money + MyFitnessPal Premium = ~$35-50/month) by a wide margin, supporting the volume-over-margin strategy.

Unit economics, cost modeling, pricing review cadence, and the deferred Lite tier consideration are specified in §13.

This pricing sits in the Gen Z sub-$10 sweet spot and matches the structural pattern of Cal AI (sub-$30/year drove millions of downloads) — consumer apps with variable AI costs that price for scale rather than margin.

Target unit economics are contribution-positive at blended user distribution on every paid tier after mobile platform fees, with acceptable margin compression on the ~10% heavy-user cohort protected by per-feature quotas and cost monitoring alerts. The business is designed around deterministic scoring, tightly scoped AI usage, and disciplined packaging of cloud and vendor-metered features so that gross margins improve with scale rather than depending on scale to exist.

## 1.7 Platform & Launch

React Native application deployed simultaneously to Apple App Store and Google Play Store. No web app at launch (deferred to v2.0+). Backend in Node.js + TypeScript using an AWS-first serverless architecture for compute, queues, storage, and key management, with PostgreSQL as the primary relational datastore. Early launch may continue to use leaner managed PostgreSQL infrastructure where appropriate for startup speed and low idle cost, while long-term production architecture targets AWS-native scale patterns.

### Launch Requirements (Connection Floor)

Life Dashboard will not ship v1 without at least one of **Strava** or **Garmin** connected as a native Fitness integration. The product's target user is an Ambitious Generalist with Optimizer tendencies — a large fraction of whom use Garmin or Strava as their primary training log. Shipping without either connection would ship a visibly downgraded fitness experience compared to their current stack and undermine the core value proposition.

**Strava** is the v1 floor. It is already specified in §8.9 as a read-only connection with AI-exclusion (per the November 2024 ToS change prohibiting AI forwarding of Strava data). Strava API access uses OAuth and is commercially available to new developers without lengthy approval cycles.

**Garmin** is applied-for at Week 0 of build. Garmin's Health API and Activity API use a two-stage approval: (1) developer account acceptance (typically 1–4 weeks), (2) production scopes for consumer apps (historically 4–12 weeks, varies by app category and data scope requested). If Garmin approval is secured before v1 launch, the app ships with both Strava and Garmin connected. If Garmin approval is delayed past Week 16 of build, v1 ships Strava-only and Garmin is added as a v1.1+ connection when approval completes.

### Launch Sequence

- **Pre-alpha (current)** — Flask PWA running with 3 users, hardened and ready for migration
- **Alpha (weeks 6–10 of build)** — React Native app in TestFlight and Play Console internal track, 10–20 hand-picked friends as daily users
- **Closed beta (weeks 16–18)** — 50–100 users via TestFlight external testing and Play closed beta
- **Public launch (week 22)** — App Store and Play Store production release with launch marketing

## 1.8 Why Now

Three market forces converge to make Life Dashboard timely:

**Data unification has become possible.** Plaid covers 12,000+ financial institutions. Apple HealthKit is on 90%+ of iPhones with passive data already collecting. Google APIs give programmatic access to email and calendar. Strava offers OAuth to consumer apps. Five years ago, building this product would have required enterprise-level integration partnerships. Today a solo founder can assemble the data sources.

**AI made the chatbot layer viable.** Pre-2024, a life-aware assistant would have required a trained-in-house model or a prohibitively expensive GPT-4 bill. With Claude Haiku at affordable per-token pricing and context-stripping techniques that cap messages at ~500 tokens, the chatbot costs pennies per user per day. The unit economics only started working in the last 18 months.

**Gen Z's relationship with subscription apps is mature.** The cohort that grew up paying $9.99/month for Spotify, Duolingo, and Headspace has no friction paying for apps that genuinely improve their daily life. RevenueCat's 2025 data shows Gen Z subscribes to an average of seven paid apps and Health & Fitness is the most monetizable category on mobile. The market is ready.

## 1.9 Competitive Position

Life Dashboard does not compete with any single tracker. It competes with the aggregate pain of using all of them.

| Category | Best-in-Class Competitor | Life Dashboard's Position |
|---|---|---|
| Nutrition | MyFitnessPal, MacroFactor | Matches on logging, beats on context |
| Fitness/Body | Whoop, Strava, Oura, Garmin | Does not replace; ingests their data |
| Finance | Copilot, Monarch, YNAB | Matches on aggregation, beats on cross-context |
| Calendar/Productivity | Google Calendar, Sunsama | Ingests calendar as Time score signal |
| Habit/Life Tracking | Finch, Way of Life, Habitica | Beats on data connection |
| AI Life Coach | None at meaningful scale | Clear category opening |

**Note on Strava/Garmin data coverage at launch:** Strava integration is read-only at v1 (users see their Strava activities in Life Dashboard's Fitness category, but Strava-sourced data is excluded from AI context per the Nov 2024 Strava ToS). Garmin, when approved, provides richer data fields including HRV quality, body battery, training load, and stress score — all of which pipeline into Fitness scoring and Time-category baselines. Until Garmin approval, users with Garmin watches can pipe summary data via HealthKit / Health Connect but lose the structured advanced metrics. This gap is documented in §8.9 and surfaced to users in the Connections onboarding (§4.1.7).

The competitive moat is not any single feature. It is the unified context that makes the AI chatbot possible, plus the Time category — a score derived from passive platform data that no single-domain tracker can build without expanding into other domains. No finance app will add calorie tracking; no nutrition app will add Plaid; no productivity app will read your HealthKit sleep rhythm. The integration work required to match Life Dashboard is itself the moat.

## 1.10 Non-Goals

Life Dashboard explicitly does not:

- **Tell users what to do in a prescriptive way.** The app surfaces information and offers suggestions; it does not lecture, judge, or override user choice. AI-generated prescriptive copy ("you should…") is banned throughout the product. Descriptive AI (narrating computed numbers, user-invoked) is permitted.
- **Claim to know more than the user.** Users are the authority on their own lives; Life Dashboard is a tool they use, not a coach they submit to.
- **Become a medical device or financial advisor.** Life Dashboard stays firmly within the FDA General Wellness safe harbor and the SEC/FINRA non-advisor definition. It never diagnoses, prescribes, or recommends specific securities.
- **Replace specialized apps.** Life Dashboard is not a better MyFitnessPal or a better Monarch. It is an aggregation and intelligence layer on top of those ecosystems.
- **Serve minors under 13 at all.** COPPA compliance is achieved by excluding this population entirely. Users 13–17 are served with a reduced feature set.
- **Become a social network.** No friend feeds, no public profiles, no comparison metrics against other users.

---

# Section 2 — Product Vision & Target User

## 2.1 Vision Statement

Life Dashboard is the single intelligent dashboard for the ambitious generalist — the user who is juggling physical training, financial health, career momentum, and personal commitments, and who is tired of doing all of that across fragmented tools. Life Dashboard consolidates the data, scores the user's life across four dimensions (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time), and uses AI to answer any real-time question about what to do next.

The product's north star is **reducing cognitive load without reducing agency**. Users should feel that Life Dashboard makes their life simpler to manage without making decisions for them. The dashboard is a mirror, not a coach. The chatbot is a consultant, not an authority.

## 2.2 The Four Categories

Each of the four category cards on the home dashboard is a first-class citizen with its own score, sub-metrics, drill-down view, and contribution to the Overall Score. The four were chosen deliberately:

**Fitness, Nutrition, and Finance** are the three hard-edged optimizable domains. Each has clear metrics, reliable data sources, and an existing population of users who already track them obsessively in separate apps. These three get dedicated real estate because the Optimizer target user already thinks of them as separate concerns.

**Time** is the category Life Dashboard uniquely introduces. It is not a catch-all "other" bucket — it is a rigorously-scoped category that measures the **shape** of a user's day derived entirely from passive platform data: sleep regularity (HealthKit), attention fragmentation (Screen Time), location intentionality (CoreLocation), schedule density balance (Calendar), and rhythm adherence (cross-domain anchor timestamps). The Time score is un-gameable because the user is not asked to log anything; the phone watched the day and the score reflects what the phone saw. No single competitor aggregates this territory or scores it in this way.

The four-category structure is a deliberate product decision. Six cards would crowd the home screen and force the user to scroll the primary dashboard. Two or three would merge domains the target user thinks about separately. Four is the size that fits cleanly on a mobile home screen and matches the mental model of a high-agency generalist.

## 2.3 Primary User Persona — "The Ambitious Generalist"

### Demographic

- Age: 22–28 (median: 25)
- Income: $55,000–$150,000 annual
- Education: Four-year degree or equivalent specialized training
- Location: Urban or suburban, U.S. primary market with EU/UK secondary
- Employment: Early-career professional, founder, consultant, military-adjacent, or skilled tradesperson

### Psychographic

- Already self-identifies as disciplined or aspiring to be
- Reads productivity, fitness, or personal development content regularly
- Tracks metrics voluntarily — weight, macros, workouts, spending, tasks
- Values data and evidence over intuition
- Willing to pay for tools that measurably improve their life
- Frustrated by tool fragmentation more than by any single tool's limitations
- Has tried and abandoned multiple productivity systems
- Buys premium products when the premium is earned
- Comfortable with AI as a tool; sees it as leverage rather than threat

### Current Toolkit (before Life Dashboard)

- MyFitnessPal, Cronometer, or MacroFactor for nutrition
- Whoop, Oura, Garmin, or Apple Watch for body metrics
- Copilot Money, Monarch, YNAB, or a bank app for finance
- Google Calendar for schedule
- Gmail or Outlook for email
- Apple Reminders, Things, Todoist, or Notion for tasks
- Apple Health or Google Fit as the hub that doesn't quite work

### Daily Reality

- Opens six to ten apps per day to check on their life
- Has abandoned self-tracking multiple times due to friction
- Re-enters the same weight or mood data into multiple apps
- Loses track of goals between apps
- Feels behind more often than they actually are

### Why They Buy Life Dashboard

- The promise of one dashboard instead of six
- The chatbot that can answer "what should I do right now"
- The personalized baselines that make their data feel known
- The defensive value of unified history when they want to look back

### Why They Stay

- Cognitive load reduction is immediately felt and hard to give up
- The longer they use Life Dashboard, the more personal baselines sharpen, creating switching costs
- The chatbot becomes more useful the more connections are connected
- Streaks and history create emotional attachment

## 2.4 Secondary User Personas

**The Transitioning Adult (15% of users).** Recently left structured environments (college graduation, military separation, moving out of parents' home). Suddenly responsible for managing their own health, finances, schedule. Life Dashboard is scaffolding during the transition. They convert heavily to Pro because the tool genuinely fills a gap. Higher churn after ~18 months as they stabilize.

**The Recovering Over-Tracker (20% of users).** Spent years using seven apps and got burned out. Wants to keep the data flowing but stop managing seven apps. Life Dashboard is relief, not addition. They use Core more than Pro because unlimited AI isn't the draw — consolidation is.

**The Optimizer (25% of users).** High-performer, often founder or athlete-adjacent. Already tracks everything. Buys Pro on day one. Stress-tests the product and becomes the highest-signal user for feedback. Small segment by count, disproportionate by influence and retention.

## 2.5 Users Life Dashboard Does Not Target

- **Heavy medical users** — Users whose data tracking is part of managing a diagnosed condition. Life Dashboard does not claim medical device status and cannot serve these users responsibly.
- **Users philosophically opposed to AI** — Users who reject AI tools on moral, environmental, ideological, or personal grounds. Life Dashboard's core value proposition depends on AI-powered context, chatbot answers, and automatic data cleaning. Marketing should lead with AI as a feature, not a footnote, which will naturally filter this segment out before they download.
- **Enterprise / B2B** — Wellness programs, insurance partnerships, coach marketplaces. Deferred to post-launch roadmap with explicit architectural separation if pursued.
- **Children under 13** — Blocked by design. COPPA compliance.
- **Heavy privacy holdouts** — Users unwilling to connect any data sources. Life Dashboard's value is unified data.
- **Global South / low-income markets** — Current pricing and connection coverage assume U.S./EU/UK financial and health infrastructure.

## 2.6 Brand Voice & Product Tone

- **Direct, never preachy.** Life Dashboard tells users facts about their life and lets them draw conclusions. It does not lecture.
- **Premium, never corporate.** The app should feel like a tool made by a small team that cares, not an enterprise product from a large company.
- **Calm, never urgent.** Life Dashboard is not a productivity guilt machine. Missing a day is not a crisis. Streaks are celebrated but breaking them is not punished.
- **Intelligent, never boastful.** The AI is smart but speaks plainly. Users never see "as an AI assistant" phrasing.
- **Respectful of agency.** The app suggests, surfaces, reminds. It does not command.

The voice target: Whoop's precision, Apple Fitness's warmth, Notion's competence, with none of the corporate polish that makes enterprise wellness apps feel sterile.

## 2.7 What "Good" Looks Like — Product Quality Bar

When a user opens Life Dashboard for the first time on launch day, the experience should feel like:

- **Fast** — Dashboard renders in under 500ms from app open
- **Complete** — Even with zero connections connected, the app shows value through manual entry and a working chatbot
- **Premium** — Every screen feels designed, not templated. Animations are intentional. Colors are consistent. Typography is deliberate.
- **Smart** — The chatbot, even with limited context, gives non-generic answers. The scoring system, even with 1 day of data, shows something meaningful.
- **Trustworthy** — Privacy and data handling feel obvious and safe. Every third-party connection explains exactly what data is used and why.
- **Theirs** — The app learns fast. By day 14, the dashboard feels personalized through baselines. By day 30, the AI feels like it knows them.

This quality bar is non-negotiable for public launch. Alpha and closed beta can ship with rougher edges; public launch requires every one of these feels to be achieved.

---

# Section 3 — Design Principles

These are the non-negotiable rules that govern every product and technical decision in Life Dashboard. When two possible implementations of a feature conflict, these principles decide. When Claude Code or any future engineer faces an ambiguous choice, these principles are the answer.

## 3.1 Ruthless Friction Reduction

**Principle:** Every field the user fills in, every tap, every screen, every typed character is a failure. Eliminate as many as possible at every layer of the app.

**Application:**

- No blank form fields when a smart default is possible
- No manual entry when data can be pulled from an connection
- No double entry — if a user gives information once, every part of the app that needs it uses it
- Voice input available anywhere text input is
- Photo input available anywhere relevant (meal logging, receipt capture, barcode, pantry)
- One-tap repetition ("log yesterday's breakfast again") for recurring behaviors
- AI-drafted content (email replies, meal suggestions, task breakdowns) that user approves rather than writes from scratch
- Auto-suggestions based on time of day, pantry contents, historical behavior, and current context
- Keyboard shortcuts and gestures for power users
- Intelligent form pre-fill on every input screen (never blank when we have data)

**Onboarding friction target:** Collect maximum useful information with minimum user input — ideally under 90 seconds from signup to first dashboard view for the minimal path.

## 3.2 AI Only When It Is The Best Tool

**Principle:** AI is never used for AI's sake. Every AI call must justify its cost in both user value and API spend. When a deterministic algorithm, a lookup, or a simple rule can do the job, it wins.

**AI is the right tool when:**

- It reduces user input friction meaningfully (photo-to-macros, natural language meal parsing, workout description parsing)
- It synthesizes unified context the user couldn't get elsewhere (chatbot answers to "what should I do right now")
- It cleans messy data that would otherwise require manual user work (meal parsing, ingredient identification)
- It drafts content the user would have had to write themselves (email replies, meal suggestions)

**AI is the wrong tool when:**

- Simple math can do it (all scoring, calorie calculations, streak counting, deficit math)
- A lookup can do it (food database, exchange rates, timezone math)
- The user doesn't care about the output (background summaries nobody reads)
- The output is likely to be wrong or hallucinated (specific medical advice, specific investment advice)
- A deterministic rules-based classifier works with acceptable accuracy (email importance uses a deterministic multi-signal classifier per §4.6.8, not per-email AI)
- The same result can be cached from a previous call

### Descriptive vs. Prescriptive AI (v1.18 refinement)

Life Dashboard distinguishes two modes of AI-generated natural language. Only one is permitted outside the Notifications surface.

**Descriptive AI (permitted, user-invoked only):** AI that narrates already-computed numbers when the user explicitly asks. Examples: tapping an "Explain" button next to a score to get a paragraph describing what drove it; asking the chatbot "why is my Fitness score down this week?" and receiving a summary that references the deterministic signals that changed. Descriptive AI does not tell the user what to do. It describes what is. The scoring math runs first; the AI layer translates numbers into a sentence if the user asks for translation.

**Prescriptive AI (banned as an UNSOLICITED surface in v1):** AI that tells the user what they should do, coaches them, nudges them, moralizes, or generates unsolicited insights. Examples of what is banned: "today looks like a good day for a run," "you should eat more protein this afternoon," "consider skipping your workout because your HRV is low," AI-generated motivational paragraphs anywhere on the dashboard or in detail views. The user did not ask; AI did not get invited.

**Two explicit exceptions where prescriptive AI is permitted:**

1. **Notifications (§4.9).** Push notifications are the one proactive channel where AI-composed prescriptive copy is permitted, because the user explicitly opted into receiving proactive prompts when they granted notification permission. The Notifications surface is the sole permitted location for AI prose the user did not directly invoke.

2. **AI Chatbot (§4.7) when directly user-invoked.** When a user asks the chatbot a prescriptive question ("should I work out today?", "what should I eat tonight?", "which bill should I pay off first?"), the chatbot is permitted to answer prescriptively. The user invoked the prescriptive output by asking a prescriptive question; the AI did not volunteer it. This exception does not extend to the chatbot offering prescriptive content when the user asked a descriptive question — the chatbot matches the mode of the user's query.

Both exceptions require explicit user opt-in (notification permission) or user invocation (chatbot question). Neither is a license for unsolicited AI prose elsewhere in the app — dashboard, detail views, patterns, goals, history, and every other surface remain descriptive-AI-only, and all descriptive AI there must be user-invoked.

**Why the distinction matters:** Flask's original "Pattern Insight" paragraph and "momentum insight" AI prose are cut not because AI-generated sentences are inherently bad, but because they fired without the user asking. A user-invoked "Summarize my week" paragraph is a different product from a dashboard that writes you a sentence every time you open it. The former respects agency; the latter nags.

## 3.3 Deterministic Where Possible, AI Where Necessary

**Principle:** The core scoring system, streak counting, calorie math, macro calculations, and budget arithmetic are all pure functions of data. They are never routed through AI.

**Why:**

- Scores must be reproducible — same inputs, same output, every time
- Scores must be explainable — users can drill down and see exactly what drove the number
- Scores must be fast — render in milliseconds, not seconds
- Scores must be free — no variable cost per calculation
- Users must trust the math — a deterministic score is auditable; an AI score is not

**Where AI enters:** AI is used upstream of the scoring engine to clean data (parse a meal, categorize a transaction) and downstream of the scoring engine to explain results in natural language or answer questions. AI is never in the scoring math itself.

## 3.4 User Agency Is Absolute

**Principle:** Life Dashboard surfaces, suggests, and reminds. It never commands, overrides, or assumes it knows better than the user.

**Application:**

- The dashboard is a mirror, not a coach
- The chatbot offers answers when asked; it does not lecture when not asked
- AI-drafted emails, meals, and content are always approved before action
- No behavior is auto-logged without user confirmation unless the user explicitly opted in (e.g., "auto-sync my Garmin workouts")
- No judgment language ever ("you should have," "you failed to," "you're behind")
- Users can always override any score, any suggestion, any baseline
- Users own their data and can export or delete it at any time with no friction

## 3.5 Privacy As A Feature, Not A Footnote

**Principle:** Life Dashboard is trusted with users' most sensitive data — their health, their money, their communication. Privacy is not a compliance checkbox; it is a core product attribute that users should feel every time they interact with the app.

**Application:**

- Every connection explains in plain language what data is pulled, what is done with it, and what is sent to third parties (including Anthropic AI)
- Granular per-source consent toggles, all defaulting OFF at signup
- Per-data-type consent inside connections (e.g., Plaid can be on while "send Plaid to AI" is off)
- Raw financial transactions never sent to Anthropic — only derived categorical summaries
- Full email bodies never sent to Anthropic — only 200-char snippets and metadata
- Audit log accessible to user showing what was sent to AI in each prompt
- One-tap data export (GDPR Article 15)
- One-tap account deletion with 30-day purge of all data, including backups
- No ads, ever
- No data sales, ever
- No tracking pixels in the app

## 3.6 Performance Is A Promise

**Principle:** Every interaction feels instant. The app opens, screens render, data appears, and actions complete without perceptible delay.

**Application:**

- Dashboard renders in under 500ms from app open
- Every local interaction (tab switch, drill-down) completes in under 100ms
- AI responses start streaming within 2 seconds of the user tapping send
- Connection syncs happen in the background; the UI never blocks on network calls
- Offline-first: the app is fully functional without network; writes queue and sync later

## 3.7 Every Connection Is Optional

**Principle:** Life Dashboard works for a user who connects nothing. It works better for a user who connects one thing. It becomes indispensable for a user who connects everything. No connection is required for the core value to be delivered.

**Application:**

- Every category card gracefully degrades based on available data
- The chatbot works with whatever context is available — partial is still useful
- No onboarding step blocks on connecting an connection
- Connections can be disconnected at any time without breaking the app
- Dormant connections fade quietly — no error screens, no broken UI

## 3.8 Margins Are A Product Decision

**Principle:** Every technical choice considers cost. The app must be contribution-positive from user one, not reliant on scale to justify the economics. Margin discipline is a product design constraint, not a finance department problem layered on top.

**Hard rules:**

- Every materially metered feature is either capped, scope-limited, Pro-expanded, or otherwise packaged intentionally — nothing metered ships without a cost envelope
- User-facing "unlimited" language does not imply infinite backend spend; "unlimited" always means "within the abuse and soft-cap protections that keep the tier economically sound"
- Local logging and deterministic scoring are the economic foundation of the product — every score, progress calculation, and structured log path avoids AI cost entirely
- Finance is part of Life Dashboard's core value and is included in Core. v1.21 locks unlimited email, calendar, and bank account connections at all tiers. Tier differentiation comes from AI feature richness (chatbot volume, Premium Scan, AI Draft Replies), history depth, and formatted export — not account breadth.
- Hot storage and archived storage are treated as different cost classes — recent data stays hot for fast interactions; older data moves to cheaper archival storage while remaining exportable

**Application:**

- AI calls use the cheapest model that achieves the required quality (Haiku for most; Sonnet for reasoning-heavy tasks like meal photo scan and email drafting; Opus only for narrow Pro-tier premium surfaces)
- Prompt and context assembly is cached aggressively — system prompts, stable context containers, and recent computation are reused rather than regenerated
- AI responses are cached by content hash where determinism allows
- Background jobs are batched and scheduled, not per-user polled
- Vendor-metered APIs are invoked only when they produce user-visible value — no speculative pre-fetching
- Cloud connection breadth is unlimited at both tiers (v1.21 locked). Cost discipline for cloud accounts is handled at the AI gateway (redaction before Anthropic calls) and at sync cadence (configurable per-account sync frequency), not at the account-count gate.
- Device-native and local connections (HealthKit, step counters, local logging) are broadly available because their marginal cost is effectively zero
- Historical data is tiered — the hot interactive window shrinks for Core and expands for Pro; archival storage is used for older history across both tiers
- Unit economics are monitored as a first-class product metric, observable per user and per feature surface

---

# Section 4 — Feature Requirements

Section 4 specifies every feature of the Life Dashboard product in enough detail that an engineer (human or AI) can build against it without ambiguity. Each subsection covers one feature area: user flows, screen specifications, data requirements, AI usage, friction-reduction commitments, and edge case handling.

---

# Section 4.1 — Onboarding Flow

## 4.1.1 Overview

Onboarding is the single most important user experience in Life Dashboard. It is the only screen every user sees. It determines signup conversion, the perceived quality of the app, how personalized the dashboard feels on day one, and ultimately whether the user stays past day seven.

The onboarding flow is structured around a core design tension: extracting the maximum useful information from the user with the minimum possible input. Every question asked must either (a) be necessary for the scoring engine to work correctly, (b) be required for the dashboard to feel personalized, or (c) unlock functionality the user will immediately benefit from. Everything else is deferred.

**Onboarding is a first-time-only flow (v1.27).** Once a user completes onboarding, they do not re-enter it. Profile fields captured during onboarding are editable individually via §4.8.4 Profile (Body Stats, Daily Life, Diet Preferences sections — each is a direct-edit section with minimum-necessary computation on save). Goals are edited in the Goals screen (§4.10). Connection state is managed in the Connections screen (§4.8.6). There is no "redo onboarding" action in Settings.

### Success Metrics

- **Signup completion rate** — percentage of users who reach the Home Dashboard from the Splash screen. Target: 85%+
- **Minimal path time** — median time from app open to first Dashboard view (user skips Workout Builder). Target: under 90 seconds
- **Full path time** — median time from app open to first Dashboard view (user completes Workout Builder). Target: under 5 minutes
- **Connection rate** — percentage of users who connect at least one connection during onboarding. Target: 60%+
- **Trial-to-paid conversion** — percentage of users who complete onboarding, enter trial, and convert to paid at day 7. Target: 40%+

### Onboarding Flow Structure

The flow is organized as eight sequential phases:

1. Splash & Authentication — app launch, sign-up or sign-in
2. Biometric Enrollment — Face ID / Touch ID / fingerprint setup
3. Core Personalization — three-step wizard (body stats, goal, daily life)
4. Profile Generation — AI generates personalized profile with plan
5. Training Plan Path — user chooses workout builder or skips
6. Workout Builder (conditional) — 8-step training plan wizard
7. Connection Connect — user connects available data sources
8. First Dashboard View — arrival on Home Dashboard

The paywall does not appear during onboarding. All AI usage during onboarding is provided free. The paywall triggers after onboarding completes, based on AI usage in the main app (see Section 4.1.9).

## 4.1.2 Splash & Authentication

### Purpose

Get the user past the commitment threshold of account creation with as little friction as possible. Collect the absolute minimum credential data. Every additional field on this screen costs signup conversion.

### Screen Specification

The Splash & Authentication screen displays the Life Dashboard logo and product name centered above a primary action card. The card presents a tabbed interface with two states: Sign Up (default for new installs) and Sign In (default for users who have previously logged in on any device).

**Default tab on fresh install:** Sign Up. This is a change from the current PWA, where Sign In is default. Mobile app installs are overwhelmingly new users and should land on account creation without extra tap.

### Sign Up tab (primary state)

Three OAuth buttons stacked vertically, in this order:

- Continue with Apple (required by App Store guideline 4.8 when any social login is offered)
- Continue with Google
- Continue with Email

The "Continue with Email" option expands to show email address field plus password field. No password requirements visible upfront (rules shown only if user enters something that fails: minimum 8 characters, at least one number).

Below the buttons, a single line of microcopy:

> *"Your health, financial, and calendar data stay yours. We never sell it, never train AI on it, and you can delete everything instantly."*

Below the microcopy, two legal links in small text: "Terms of Service" and "Privacy Policy" (both required by App Store and Play Store).

### Sign In tab

Same three buttons (Apple, Google, Email). Email expands to email + password with "Forgot password?" link that triggers magic-link reset via email.

Username-based authentication is deprecated. Existing Flask PWA users will be migrated to email-based accounts during the React Native migration.

### Friction Reduction Commitments

- No CAPTCHA on sign-up (Clerk's built-in bot protection is sufficient)
- No email verification required before onboarding begins (verification email sent async; onboarding proceeds)
- No two-factor authentication required during onboarding (available opt-in later in Settings)
- No phone number collection
- No name field on this screen (collected in Core Personalization where it has context)
- OAuth one-tap on iOS/Android when the user is already signed into the OS-level account

### Edge Cases

- Email already exists on sign-up attempt → switch to Sign In tab with email pre-filled, show "This email is already registered — sign in below"
- OAuth authentication fails (user cancels, network error) → return to tab with error toast, no data loss
- Password reset requested → email sent with one-tap magic link, no manual password entry required
- User is under 13 (determined in Core Personalization) → account is immediately deactivated and email sent explaining COPPA restriction. User is not allowed to continue.

## 4.1.3 Biometric Enrollment

### Purpose

Enroll biometric authentication (Face ID, Touch ID, or fingerprint) for app unlock. This is offered immediately after account creation because the user is primed to complete security setup, and because Face ID/Touch ID is a core part of the premium feel of the app.

### Screen Specification

Single screen, appears immediately after successful sign-up. The screen shows an icon representing the user's device biometric capability (Face ID icon on Face ID devices, fingerprint icon elsewhere) centered above the title.

Title: "Secure Life Dashboard with Face ID" (or "Touch ID" or "Fingerprint" based on device capability)

Body copy: "Unlock Life Dashboard instantly without typing a password. You can change this any time in Settings."

Two buttons:

- **Enable Face ID** — primary action. Tapping triggers the OS biometric enrollment confirmation.
- **Skip for Now** — secondary action. Text-only link below the primary button.

### Conditional Display

- If device has no biometric capability → this screen is skipped entirely
- If user declines OS-level biometric permission → fall through to next phase without error
- If user enables biometric → success toast "Face ID enabled" appears briefly, then proceed

## 4.1.4 Core Personalization

### Purpose

Collect the minimum data needed for the scoring engine to produce meaningful Fitness, Nutrition, and Time scores on day one. This phase is structured as a three-step wizard, preserved from the existing Flask PWA implementation (which is well-designed and user-tested).

### Step 1 — "Let's Get to Know You"

The first personalization screen collects body statistics required for Resting Metabolic Rate (RMR) calculation.

**Header**

Life Dashboard wordmark at top. Progress indicator: "Step 1 of 3". Title: "Let's get to know you". Subtitle: "Your name and body stats — used to calculate your metabolism."

**Fields**

- **Name** — "What should we call you?" Single text field. Pre-filled from OAuth name if available (Apple and Google provide first name on sign-up). If OAuth did not provide a name, field is blank with placeholder "Your first name".

- **Height** — Two inline number fields: "Height (ft)" and "Height (in)". Pre-filled to median user values if no data (5'10" for male-presumed, 5'5" for female-presumed). Unit displayed matches device locale (imperial US, metric elsewhere). Inches field supports decimal values (no 2-inch increment constraint). Users in metric locales see single cm field instead of ft+in split.

- **Birthday** — Date picker. Pre-filled to median user age (25 years ago from today). User adjusts. On submit, age is calculated from birthdate and stored separately. This preserves accurate age throughout the user's lifecycle with the app.

- **Current weight** — Single decimal field (e.g., 174.8). Unit displayed matches device locale (lbs US, kg elsewhere). No preset; user enters their weight. Smart keyboard shortcut: "+5" and "-5" buttons for quick adjustment.

- **Sex** — Two mutually-exclusive buttons: "Male" and "Female". Below the buttons, small clarifying text: "used for calorie calculations". This is a clinical requirement for the Mifflin-St Jeor equation; users with gender identities differing from birth sex can select the option that matches their biology or adjust their RMR manually in Settings.

**Live Feedback**

**BMI readout** — Below the form fields, a calculated BMI appears as soon as height and weight are entered: "BMI: 23.1 — Healthy weight". BMI category label uses WHO categories (Underweight, Healthy, Overweight, Obese). This live feedback demonstrates that the app is already working with the user's data and sets expectations that every input will be put to use immediately.

**Actions**

- Back button (disabled — this is the first personalization step)
- Continue button (enabled once all required fields have values)

### Step 2 — "What's Your Goal?"

The second personalization screen captures the user's primary goal, which drives calorie target, macro split, and dashboard framing.

**Header**

Progress indicator: "Step 2 of 3". Title: "What's your goal?". Subtitle: "This determines your calorie target, protein, and macros." Helper text: "Pick the one that sounds most like you right now."

**Goal Options**

Four cards in a 2x2 grid, each with an icon, title, and description:

- **Get Leaner** — "Lose body fat while keeping as much muscle as possible"
- **Get Bigger** — "Build muscle and strength, even if it means gaining some fat"
- **Lean & Muscular** — "I want to lose fat and build muscle at the same time" (recomp)
- **Stay Where I Am** — "Maintain my current weight and body composition"

**Help Option**

Below the four cards, a text link: "Not sure? Help me decide". Tapping this opens a brief modal with three or four questions that recommend a goal based on current state (body fat estimate, training experience, what they're trying to achieve).

**Conditional Follow-Up**

After the user selects a goal, the screen transitions to a summary:

- Selected goal icon and label centered
- Plan summary in plain English (e.g., "Life Dashboard will set a moderate calorie deficit to lose fat while protecting your muscle. Protein is elevated to preserve lean mass.")
- Conditional question (shown only for Get Leaner and Get Bigger goals): "Do you have a specific goal weight?" Yes/No buttons.
- If Yes → inline weight picker appears with current weight as default
- If No → weight target inferred from goal type (5-10% body weight adjustment)
- "← Change goal" link at top to re-select

**Plan Voice**

Plan summary copy must be in active, confident voice. Compare:

- **Before (existing):** "We'll calculate a moderate calorie deficit to lose fat while preserving your muscle. Protein is set high to protect lean mass."
- **After (target):** "Life Dashboard will set a moderate deficit to lose fat without sacrificing muscle. Your protein target is elevated to protect lean mass."

### Step 3 — "Your Daily Life"

The third personalization screen captures occupation-related activity factors that inform Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE).

**Header**

Progress indicator: "Step 3 of 3". Title: "Your daily life". Subtitle: "How you work and your stress level affect your calorie needs."

**Occupation Field**

**What do you do for work?** — Text field with autocomplete dropdown. As the user types, common occupations appear as selectable suggestions: "Student", "Software Engineer", "Nurse", "Teacher", "Consultant", "Founder", "Designer", "Military", "Construction", "Retail", "Healthcare", "Sales", etc. If the user's occupation isn't in the list, they can type their own (min 2 chars). This is an improvement over the free-text field in the current PWA — preserves flexibility while producing cleaner categorical data for analytics and the chatbot context.

**Work Style**

Select one chip:

- Desk / seated (pre-selected by default)
- Standing / moving
- Physically demanding
- Remote / home
- Mixed

**Stress Level**

**Typical stress level** — Four discrete options, shown as tappable chips (change from existing 0-10 slider which produces low-signal data).

- Low — "Manageable most days"
- Moderate — "Some tough weeks, generally okay"
- High — "Frequently stretched thin"
- Very High — "Overwhelmed most days"

Stress level is used as one input to the Time score (weighting pending further design decisions in Section 4.6) and to inform chatbot responses.

**Actions**

- Back button (returns to Step 2)
- Primary action: "Build My Profile →"

## 4.1.5 Profile Generation

### Purpose

Transition the user from personalization to dashboard via a visual sequence that demonstrates Life Dashboard's intelligence while the profile engine actually computes the user's metabolism, targets, and plan rationale. This is the moment where the product reveals itself as smart.

The Profile Generation step is **fully deterministic at v1**. RMR, TDEE, calorie target, and macro targets are computed by formulas — not by AI. No AI calls occur during profile generation (per v1.19 — earlier drafts specified an archetype classifier and an Insight Block narration; both have been cut). AI may still run downstream of Profile Generation if the user chose "Build My Plan" (Workout Plan Builder in §4.1.6), but that is a separate flow the user has explicitly opted into.

### Loading Screen

Appears immediately after "Build My Profile" tap. Full-screen centered content:

- Life Dashboard logo at top
- Circular progress indicator (subtle, animated)
- Title: "Building your profile..."
- Body: "We're analyzing your responses and generating your personalized profile. This could take up to a minute."

**Actual latency target:** under 1 second. The deterministic formulas (RMR, TDEE, macro targets, BMI) compute server-side in under 100ms; the extra budget covers round-trip and rendering. The "up to a minute" copy is defensive expectation-setting held over from Flask days when profile generation included an AI call — users experience much better than the copy suggests, feel positive. The copy is retained for defensive reasons; engineering should aim for sub-second reveal in practice.

### Deterministic Computation Path

The following values compute before the Profile Reveal renders:

**RMR (Resting Metabolic Rate) — dual-formula selection:**

The engine selects one of two validated RMR formulas based on whether the user provided a body fat percentage during personalization:

```
IF body_fat_pct IS PROVIDED AND 0 < body_fat_pct < 100:
    // Katch-McArdle (more accurate when body fat % is known)
    lbm_kg = weight_kg × (1 − body_fat_pct / 100)
    RMR = round(370 + 21.6 × lbm_kg)
    formula_used = "Katch-McArdle"
ELSE:
    // Mifflin-St Jeor (default when body fat % unknown)
    base = (10 × weight_kg) + (6.25 × height_cm) − (5 × age_years)
    IF biological_sex = male:
        RMR = round(base + 5)
    ELSE:
        RMR = round(base − 161)
    formula_used = "Mifflin-St Jeor"
```

The UI's profile reveal displays which formula was used in an "info" chip next to the RMR value. If the user later adds body fat % via a smart scale sync or manual entry, the engine switches to Katch-McArdle on the next auto-adjust cycle (per §4.4.10), and the transition is disclosed transparently per §4.4.10's user-confirm transparency.

**TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) — four-component formula:**

```
TDEE = RMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF

Where:
  NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis):
    OCC_BASE = { sedentary: 200, standing: 400, physical: 700 }
    KCAL_PER_STEP = 0.04
    STEPS_PER_MILE = 2000

    workout_steps = 0
    FOR each workout in recent window:
      skip if workout.description matches: cycl|bike|row|swim|ellip|strength|lift|bench|squat|deadlift|press|curl|pulldown
      IF description contains "(mi|mile)" pattern:
        workout_steps += miles × STEPS_PER_MILE
      IF description contains "(km|kilo)" pattern:
        workout_steps += km × 0.621371 × STEPS_PER_MILE

    net_steps = max(0, total_daily_steps − workout_steps)
    NEAT = OCC_BASE[occupation] + round(net_steps × KCAL_PER_STEP)

  EAT (Exercise Activity Thermogenesis):
    EAT = sum of logged workout calorie burns (from HealthKit / Health Connect / Strava / manual AI burn estimate)

  TEF (Thermic Effect of Food):
    IF macros are known (P/C/F specified):
      TEF = round(protein_g × 4 × 0.25 + carbs_g × 4 × 0.08 + fat_g × 9 × 0.03)
    ELSE IF only calories known:
      TEF = round(calories × 0.10)  // flat approximation
```

**NEAT workout-step subtraction rationale:** A device-logged 5-mile run already contributes its own calorie burn as EAT. Its step count would double-count if included in NEAT. The regex-based subtraction excludes non-ambulatory activity types (cycling, rowing, lifting, swimming, etc.) where workout duration is not step-driven. This algorithm is preserved verbatim from Flask because it materially improves TDEE accuracy for users who both strength-train and walk.

**Calorie target — goal-adjusted, RMR-floored:**

Each primary goal has a calibrated calorie adjustment from TDEE:

| Primary Goal | `cal_adjust` |
|---|---|
| Get Leaner (lose_weight) | −0.20 (20% deficit) |
| Get Bigger (build_muscle) | +0.10 (10% surplus) |
| Lean & Muscular (recomp) | −0.10 (10% deficit) |
| Stay Where I Am (maintain) | 0.00 |

```
raw_target = round(TDEE × (1 + cal_adjust))
calorie_target = max(raw_target, RMR)  // never below RMR
```

**Protein target — goal-specific g/kg × reference weight:**

| Primary Goal | `protein_g_per_kg` | Reference weight |
|---|---|---|
| Get Leaner | 2.0 | goal weight (if set and < current); else current weight |
| Get Bigger | 1.8 | current weight |
| Lean & Muscular | 2.2 | current weight |
| Stay Where I Am | 1.6 | current weight |

```
protein_target_g = round(reference_kg × protein_g_per_kg)
```

**Fat target — higher of percentage or g/kg floor:**

```
fat_from_percentage = round((calorie_target × 0.25) / 9)
fat_from_gkg_floor = round(current_weight_kg × 0.7)
fat_target_g = max(fat_from_percentage, fat_from_gkg_floor)
```

**Carb target — remainder with minimum floor:**

```
protein_kcal = protein_target_g × 4
fat_kcal = fat_target_g × 9
carb_kcal_remaining = calorie_target − protein_kcal − fat_kcal
carb_target_g = max(100, round(carb_kcal_remaining / 4))
```

**BMI and BMI category — WHO classification, deterministic.**

All of the above is deterministic. The computation takes < 50ms server-side.

### Profile Reveal Screen

Sparkle icon at top. Title: "Your Profile is Ready". Subtitle: "Life Dashboard now understands your numbers. Your dashboard is calibrated to your goals, lifestyle, and metabolism."

**Profile Summary Card**

Displays key computed values in a table format:

- Name
- RMR — calculated kcal (with a small info chip showing which formula was used: "Katch-McArdle" or "Mifflin-St Jeor")
- TDEE — kcal/day (expandable to show RMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF breakdown)
- Calorie target — kcal/day
- Protein target — g/day (with a small info chip showing the g/kg reference and the reference weight used)
- Fat target — g/day
- Carb target — g/day
- BMI and BMI category

**Archetype:** *Removed in v1.18.* Earlier versions of this document specified an AI-generated archetype label (Optimizer, Transitioning Adult, etc.) assigned during profile generation. This field is cut. It had no downstream effect on scoring, chatbot behavior, or feature surfacing — it was a one-time label shown on the reveal screen and never referenced again. Cutting it removes one AI call from the onboarding path and eliminates a feature that provided demo value but no durable user value.

**Insight Block:** *Removed in v1.19.* Prior versions specified a 2-3 sentence Haiku-generated narration of the computed profile. This has been cut. The Insight Block was structurally identical to the Pattern Insight and "momentum insight" slop that was killed earlier — an AI paragraph appearing unsolicited on the reveal screen, narrating numbers the user can see for themselves. Per §3.2, descriptive AI must be user-invoked; the Insight Block appeared without the user tapping anything. The citation drawer, Plan Block, and TDEE breakdown carry the "we actually thought about this" signal better than an AI paragraph. Users who want an explanation of their numbers can ask the chatbot at any time (user-invoked, compliant with §3.2). Removing this call saves one AI call per onboarding completion.

**Your Plan Block**

Below the Profile Summary Card, a detailed plan breakdown:

- Plan name (matches goal: "Lose Weight", "Build Muscle", etc.)
- Plan description (one sentence from a static template, not AI-generated)
- Calorie target card with breakdown: TDEE → adjustment percentage → RMR floor applied (if triggered)
- Protein card with breakdown: reference weight × g/kg → rounded target
- Fat card with breakdown: max(% of calories, g/kg floor)
- Carbs card with breakdown: remainder after protein and fat, with minimum floor
- NEAT + EAT + TEF expandable breakdown ("Show the math" drawer)

**Scientific Sources Citation**

A "Based on" line with a tap-to-expand drawer listing every peer-reviewed reference used in the computation. Citations are drawn from a canonical reference pool:

**Metabolism / RMR references:**
1. Mifflin et al. (1990). *American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*, 51(2): 241–247. — RMR equation
2. Katch & McArdle (1996). *Exercise Physiology: Energy, Nutrition, and Human Performance* (4th ed.). — RMR from lean body mass

**TEF / energy balance references:**
3. Ravussin & Bogardus (1992). *American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*, 55(1 Suppl): 242S–245S. — TEF components by macronutrient
4. Westerterp (2004). *Nutrition & Metabolism*, 1:5. — Diet-induced thermogenesis
5. Hall et al. (2012). *Lancet*, 378(9793): 826–837. — Energy balance modeling in free-living humans

**Protein / macronutrient targets:**
6. Helms et al. (2014). *Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition (JISSN)*, 11:20. — Protein requirements during caloric restriction
7. Morton et al. (2018). *British Journal of Sports Medicine*, 52(6): 376–384. — Protein dose-response for muscle mass
8. Phillips & Van Loon (2011). *Journal of Sports Sciences*, 29(Suppl 1): S29–S38. — Protein for athletes
9. Jäger et al. (2017). *JISSN*, 14:20. — ISSN Position Stand on Protein
10. Iraki et al. (2019). *JISSN*, 16:51. — Natural bodybuilding nutrition
11. Barakat et al. (2020). *Strength and Conditioning Journal*, 42(5): 7–21. — Body recomposition

**NEAT / activity thermogenesis:**
12. Levine et al. (2005). *Science*, 307(5709): 584–586. — Non-exercise activity thermogenesis in lean vs obese
13. Tudor-Locke & Bassett (2004). *Sports Medicine*, 34(1): 1–8. — Pedometer-determined activity

Each citation is displayed as a short-form inline reference (e.g., "Helms 2014, JISSN") with full details available in the drawer. Any given goal plan cites the subset of references that applied to its specific calculation (Get Leaner cites Mifflin or Katch-McArdle for RMR, Helms 2014 for protein during cut, Ravussin/Westerterp for TEF; Get Bigger cites the RMR reference plus Morton/Phillips for hypertrophy protein dose; etc.).

The citation drawer is a Life Dashboard differentiator against competitors (MyFitnessPal, MacroFactor) who give the user a number without defending it.

**Training Plan Choice**

At the bottom of the profile reveal, a "Workout Plan" block asks: "How do you want to set up your training?" with two equally-weighted buttons (per v1.18 decision — prior versions made "Build My Plan" the primary action with "I'll do my own" as secondary; v1.18 presents them as equal choices):

- **Build My Plan** — launches Workout Builder (Section 4.1.6). Full sub-flow. Nothing saves until complete; user can cancel at any step and return to this choice point.
- **I'll do my own** — skips Workout Builder and proceeds to First Goal Selection (4.1.5a).

Both buttons are presented with equal visual weight. The Workout Builder is also accessible post-onboarding from the Fitness tab (§4.3.10) at any time, whether or not the user chose it during onboarding.

## 4.1.5a First Goal Selection

### Purpose

After Profile Reveal shows the user their calculated plan, this step captures the user's first goal — a concrete, trackable objective from the §4.10 goal library.

Per the v1.27 unified goal system (§4.10.2), there is no separate "primary goal" nutrition configuration distinct from library goals. The user picks one goal from the library here, and if it's a fitness body-composition goal, the calorie/macro targets derive from it immediately as a deterministic side effect. If the user picks a non-fitness goal, or skips this step, the calorie engine uses maintenance defaults until a fitness body-composition goal is added post-onboarding.

### Screen Specification

**Header:** "Pick your first goal"

**Subtitle:** "Life Dashboard updates progress automatically from your data. You can add more goals anytime."

### Goal Card Filtering

The picker defaults to **fitness body-composition goals** (FIT-01 Reach goal weight) as the primary option, because the user has just completed body-stats onboarding and calorie/macro targets are expected to derive from this goal.

**Default pre-fill card** (based on body stats captured in §4.1.4 Step 2):
- If current weight is provided and target weight captured: pre-fill "Reach [target_weight] lbs by [auto-inferred date]" with auto-inferred date of today + (|lbs to lose/gain| × 7 days) at 1 lb/week for loss, 0.5 lb/week for gain.
- If weight-composition direction captured without target weight: prompt for target weight inline.
- If no weight-composition direction captured: show the full fitness goal picker (FIT-01 through FIT-06).

### Actions

Primary button (when pre-fill present): **"Use this goal"**
- Tap: creates the pre-filled goal and marks it as the user's **primary fitness goal** (the one that drives calorie math)
- Calorie and macro targets deterministically recompute from the goal's direction and target weight
- Proceeds to Connections step (§4.1.7)

Secondary link: **"Customize"**
- Tap: opens the Goal Customize screen (§4.10.8) with pre-fill values as starting point
- After save, proceeds to Connections step

Tertiary link: **"Browse all goals"**
- Tap: opens the full Goal Picker (§4.10.7) with all 22 library goals available
- After selection + save, proceeds to Connections step
- If user picks a non-fitness goal (e.g., Pay off credit card) → goal is created and tracked, but calorie engine uses maintenance defaults (matches the body-comp direction captured, or pure maintenance if none was captured)

Footer link: **"Skip for now"**
- Tap: proceeds to Connections step with no goal created
- Calorie engine uses maintenance defaults
- User can add goals anytime from Goals Dashboard post-onboarding

### Behavior

- Created goal is inserted into the unified `user_goals` table with `is_primary_fitness_goal = true` if it's a fitness body-composition goal (otherwise false)
- `auto_restart_enabled` defaults to true for cumulative numeric types
- Created goal is visible on Home Dashboard from first session
- Fitness body-composition goal creation triggers immediate deterministic recompute of calorie target, protein target, fat target, carb target
- If user skips, Home Dashboard goal strip is hidden on day 1

### Calorie Math Implications

| User selection | Calorie engine state |
|---|---|
| Selects fitness body-comp goal (FIT-01) with target weight | Deterministically derived targets — deficit for fat loss, surplus for muscle gain, maintenance-plus for recomp |
| Selects fitness non-body-comp goal (FIT-02 through FIT-06) | Maintenance targets (user can add FIT-01 later to shift) |
| Selects non-fitness goal (Finance, Time, non-body-comp Nutrition) | Maintenance targets |
| Skips entirely | Maintenance targets |

No AI call fires during target recomputation — it's pure deterministic math (Mifflin-St Jeor / Katch-McArdle RMR × TDEE multiplier + goal-direction adjustment).

### Edge Cases

- If the user chose not to set a target weight in Step 2 despite the direction being set, the pre-fill card prompts for a target weight inline before tapping Use
- If onboarding is interrupted at this step (app crash, force-quit), user resumes at this step on next session; goal is only created on explicit confirmation
- Users who reach this step but choose "Skip for now" are NOT re-prompted during onboarding
- A user who picks, say, "Pay off credit card" as their first goal still sees Connections next; calorie engine is maintenance, and a banner on the Home Dashboard day 1 reminds them they can add a fitness goal for personalized macro targets

### Integration With Existing Flow

Existing flow before v1.11:
4.1.5 Profile Generation → 4.1.6 Workout Builder → 4.1.7 Connections → 4.1.7a Notification Permission → 4.1.8 First Dashboard

Updated flow:
4.1.5 Profile Generation → 4.1.5a First Goal Selection → 4.1.6 Workout Builder (optional) → 4.1.7 Connections → 4.1.7a Notification Permission → 4.1.8 First Dashboard

First Goal Selection is NOT optional in the flow navigation sense (user always sees it), but its outcome is optional (they can skip goal creation). Matches the pattern of Connections (always shown, outcome optional).

## 4.1.6 Workout Builder (Optional Sub-Flow)

### Purpose

Generate a personalized training plan for users who want Life Dashboard to program their workouts. This is a sophisticated 8-step wizard that produces a plan matching the user's experience level, schedule, equipment, preferences, and goals.

The Workout Builder is optional. Users who already have a training program can skip it via the "I'll do my own" button on the Profile Reveal screen. Users who opt in receive a complete plan that populates the Fitness category with scheduled workouts.

### Flow Structure

Eight sequential steps with a progress bar and "Close" button (returns to Profile Reveal with no changes saved). Each step is a single screen with tappable options — no typed input required in the core flow.

### Step 1 — Training Experience

Title: "How long have you been working on your fitness?"

- Just Starting Out — "Little to no consistent training history"
- Some Experience — "6 months to 2 years of regular training"
- Well Trained — "2 to 4 years of consistent structured training"
- Highly Experienced — "4+ years, familiar with periodization and progressive overload"

### Step 2 — Schedule

Two-part screen:

**Which days can you train?** — Seven day chips (Mon-Sun). User taps each day they're available. Counter shows "N days selected".

**How long do you have per session?** — Four options: Quick (under 30 min), Standard (30–60 min), Extended (60–90 min), As Long as It Takes (90+ min).

### Step 3 — Equipment Access

Title: "What equipment do you have access to?"

- Commercial Gym — "Full access: barbells, cables, machines, dumbbells, cardio equipment"
- Home Gym with Weights — "Dumbbells, a barbell, or cables — no full machine rack"
- Minimal Equipment — "Bodyweight, resistance bands, or a few light weights"
- Mixed — It Varies — "Gym some days, home or travel on others"

### Step 4 — Recovery

Two-part screen:

**Sleep** — "Fat loss happens outside the gym too. How much are you sleeping?" Four options: Under 6 Hours, 6-7 Hours, 7-8 Hours, 8+ Hours.

**Training Time** — "When do you prefer to train?" Four options: Morning, Midday or Lunch, Evening or After Work, No Preference.

### Step 5 — Split Preference

Based on experience level and days available, the user sees a recommended training split preview (e.g., "Upper/Lower — 4 days/week", "Push/Pull/Legs — 6 days/week", "Full Body — 3 days/week"). User can accept or cycle through alternatives.

This step is skipped for "Just Starting Out" users who receive a pre-selected full-body split automatically.

### Step 6 — Favorite Exercises

Title: "Pick your favorites". Subtitle: "These will always be in your plan. Select as many as you want." Helper: "Every exercise you select is guaranteed to appear in your plan."

Exercises are grouped by muscle category with chip-style selection:

- Chest (10 options)
- Back (10 options)
- Shoulders (7 options)
- Biceps, Triceps, Legs, Glutes, Core (varies)

Counter at top shows "N exercises selected". No minimum required (user can proceed with 0 selected and the plan uses defaults).

### Step 7 — Cardio

Multi-screen sub-flow:

**Cardio inclusion** — Three options: "I Have My Own Cardio Plan", "Build Me a Cardio Plan", "No Cardio".

If user selects "Build Me a Cardio Plan", three additional screens:

- **Goal** — Burn Fat, Build Endurance, Heart Health, Sport Performance
- **Intensity** — Easy (Zone 1-2), Moderate (Zone 2-3), Hard (Interval-Based), Mix It Up. Plus days-per-week slider.
- **Preferred Activities** — Multi-select from list of 18 activities (Running, Cycling, Rowing, Swimming, Hiking, etc.)

If user selects "I Have My Own Cardio Plan", they enter their current cardio routine in a simplified 2-question format.

### Step 8 — Analysis & Plan Generation

Loading screen: "Analyzing your training history..." with defensive up-to-a-minute messaging and actual target latency under 10 seconds.

On completion, user is transitioned to Connection Connect phase (Section 4.1.7). The generated plan is saved to their profile and displayed on the Fitness category detail screen after onboarding.

## 4.1.7 Connection Connect

### Purpose

Offer the user the opportunity to connect external data sources that will auto-populate their dashboard. This phase is presented after all personalization is complete but before the first dashboard view, so the user understands what connecting each connection will unlock.

**Note on specifics:** The specific connections available, their technical implementation, and approval status are specified in Section 8 — Connections. This section describes the pattern of the connection flow; the specific list of connections available at launch will be finalized when we reach Section 8 based on which data sources are realistically accessible.

### Screen Structure

Header: "Make Life Dashboard more accurate"

Subtitle: "Connect the data sources you use. Every connection is optional and takes about 30 seconds. You can add more any time in Settings."

Below the header, a list of available connections presented as cards. Each card contains:

- Connection icon and name
- Which category it improves (e.g., "Populates your Fitness card")
- Specific data it pulls (e.g., "Sleep, HRV, steps, weight")
- Privacy note (e.g., "Data stays on your device. Only derived metrics are sent to AI.")
- Tap target: "Connect →"

### Connection Order

Connections are ordered by "most value per minute to connect" — which connection activates the largest amount of passive data with the smallest user effort. Specific ordering will be determined in Section 8 based on which connections are available at launch.

### Per-Connection Consent Flow

When user taps Connect on any connection card, a modal appears with:

- Clear description of what data is pulled
- What data is sent to third parties (including Anthropic AI for chatbot context)
- Toggle options for data categories (e.g., "Include financial data in AI context: ON / OFF")
- Primary button: "Connect" — launches OAuth or system permission flow
- Secondary button: "Cancel"

All per-category consent toggles default to OFF for third-party AI sharing. Users must explicitly opt in to have their data included in chatbot context. Core connection functionality (pulling data to the dashboard) works without AI consent.

### Skip Behavior

At the bottom of the screen: "Skip for now" button. Tapping this proceeds to First Dashboard View with no connections connected. The dashboard still renders and functions — cards that depend on connection data show manual-entry prompts instead of auto-populated metrics.

**Note:** "Skip for now" is an explicit button here — this is different from the Core Personalization flow which has no skip option. Connections are genuinely optional; personalization is required for the scoring engine to function.

## 4.1.7a Notification Permission

### Purpose

Present the notification permission ask with context immediately after the user has connected their data sources, while they're still in consent-mode flow. This is the highest-grant-rate moment to ask — the user has just seen what Life Dashboard can do with their data and is receptive to enabling the channels that deliver ongoing value.

Per Section 4.9 architectural decision, notification permission is requested during onboarding rather than on-demand post-install.

### Screen Specification

**Header:** "Smart notifications"

**Subtitle:** "Life Dashboard can send you a few notifications a day about things that need your attention — bills due, important emails, workout reminders, and smart cross-category alerts when it matters."

**Value prop rows (three):**
- "We only notify you when it's actionable"
- "You pick how aggressive: Low, Medium, or High — the default is Medium"
- "Turn it off anytime in Settings"

**Privacy microcopy (muted):** "Notifications never show dollar amounts, merchant names, or specific health data on your lock screen. We keep the sensitive stuff private."

**Primary button:** "Turn on notifications"
- Tap: triggers the OS-native permission prompt (iOS `UNUserNotificationCenter` / Android `POST_NOTIFICATIONS` runtime permission)
- On grant: registers push token with server, sets aggressiveness=Medium, all category toggles ON, proceeds to First Dashboard View
- On deny: proceeds to First Dashboard View, no push token registered

**Secondary button:** "Skip for now"
- Tap: proceeds to First Dashboard View without asking OS permission

### Behavior Post-Decision

- No re-prompting during onboarding regardless of outcome
- If user later wants to enable: Settings → Notifications shows "Notifications are off for Life Dashboard" with an "Open System Settings" button
- The first-week activity nudge (Section 4.9.13) may fire a single in-app prompt for active users who granted permission but have seen zero notifications after 3 days — this is deferred, not here

### Friction Reduction

- Context-rich ask (right after Connections) rather than cold ask on first app open
- Both CTAs clearly labeled — skip is a real option, not buried
- Privacy microcopy addresses the most common objection preemptively
- Aggressiveness level mentioned so user knows this isn't an all-or-nothing permission

### Edge Cases

- iOS devices where notifications are restricted at the parent-account level (Screen Time family sharing): OS-level permission prompt may fail silently. Life Dashboard treats as "denied"; user can reconcile in OS Settings.
- Android devices pre-13 (API < 33): notifications granted at install; this screen still shows but the "Turn on notifications" button is a no-op (proceeds directly, no OS prompt). Screen copy adapts: "Manage notification preferences in Settings."
- User backgrounds the app mid-prompt: OS prompt dismisses; returning to foreground shows the onboarding screen again (user can tap primary button to re-present OS prompt if system allows — otherwise skip).

## 4.1.8 First Dashboard View

### Purpose

The arrival moment. The user has completed onboarding and now sees the home dashboard with their personalized data. This screen's quality determines whether the user says "this is exactly what I wanted" or "this is just another tracker."

### Onboarding Transition

After the Connection Connect phase completes (whether connections were connected or skipped), the user is transitioned to the Home Dashboard with a brief animation (500ms max) that presents the four category cards populating with their data. This animation is not a loading state — it is a visual reveal of an already-computed dashboard.

### First-Time Dashboard Differences

The Home Dashboard on first arrival has subtle differences from repeat views (detailed in Section 4.2 — Home Dashboard):

- Welcome toast at top: "Welcome to Life Dashboard, [Name]" (dismisses on scroll)
- Gentle tap hints on each category card (subtle pulse animation) to encourage drill-down
- Connection suggestions appear on any category with no connected data source
- First-session walkthrough available via floating "?" button (dismissable)

### First-Session Score Behavior

On day one, scores are computed from the minimal data captured during onboarding plus any connection data pulled in the first sync. Scores are intentionally shown with a "learning" badge for the first 14 days, communicating that baselines will sharpen as the app learns the user.
