Missing data does not produce empty cards — it produces cards with prompts like "Log your first meal" or "Connect Apple Health for auto-sync".

## 4.1.9 Paywall Trigger System

### Purpose

Life Dashboard does not show a paywall during onboarding. All AI usage during onboarding (profile generation, plan generation) is free. The paywall triggers after the user has completed onboarding and begins using AI features in the main app, giving them concrete experience of Life Dashboard's intelligence before being asked to pay.

### Counter Mechanism

After onboarding completes, the user has three free AI interactions available for use anywhere in the app. AI interactions include:

- Sending a message to the chatbot
- AI meal parsing from text, voice, or photo
- AI workout description parsing
- AI daily insight generation
- AI email draft
- AI transaction categorization (first manual categorization only; automatic categorizations do not count)

Each AI interaction decrements a shared counter from 3 down to 0. The counter is visible in the AI-invoking UI (e.g., chatbot shows "2 of 3 free AI interactions remaining").

### Paywall Appearance

When the user attempts the 4th AI action, a paywall modal appears. The paywall does not block the current view — it overlays the screen as a dismissable sheet.

**Paywall Content**

Header: "Keep going with Life Dashboard Pro"

Body (active voice, honest framing):

> *"You've tried three AI features in Life Dashboard. You're clearly getting value from what we built. Life Dashboard runs on real infrastructure — AI responses, cloud connections, and data services all cost money to operate. We've chosen not to show ads or sell your data, so a subscription is how we keep the app honest and fast. Try Pro free for 14 days. Cancel any time."*

**Subscription Options**

- Life Dashboard Pro — $9.99/month (recommended, 14-day free trial)
- Life Dashboard Pro — $99.99/year (save 17%)
- Life Dashboard Core — $4.99/month
- Life Dashboard Core — $49.99/year (save 17%)

**Required Actions**

The paywall requires credit card entry before trial starts. Per RevenueCat 2025 data, trials with credit card required convert 40-60% to paid vs. 10-15% without.

Below the subscription options:

- Primary button: "Start Free Trial →"
- Secondary button: "Compare Pro vs Core"
- Tertiary text link: "Continue with Core" (user declines trial and subscribes to Core immediately)
- Dismissal: close X in corner returns user to where they were with the 4th AI action blocked

### Post-Dismissal Behavior

If the user dismisses the paywall:

- The AI action they attempted is not executed
- Dashboard continues to function normally for non-AI features
- Manual logging, scoring, and non-AI category views remain available
- Subsequent AI-attempts re-trigger the paywall
- After 24 hours, one additional "free trial offer" appears to re-engage without feeling pressured

## 4.1.10 Data Collected During Onboarding

Summary of every data point captured during the full onboarding flow. This becomes the input spec for the Core Personalization API endpoint.

| Field | Type | Source | Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Authentication ID | String (Clerk user ID) | OAuth or email signup | Yes |
| Email | Email string | OAuth or user entry | Yes |
| First name | String | OAuth or user entry | Yes |
| Height (ft/in or cm) | Integer pair or decimal | User entry | Yes |
| Birthday | ISO date | User entry | Yes |
| Weight (lbs or kg) | Decimal | User entry | Yes |
| Sex | Enum {Male, Female} | User entry | Yes |
| Goal | Enum {4 options} | User entry | Yes |
| Target weight | Decimal | User entry (conditional) | No |
| Occupation | String (autocomplete) | User entry | Yes |
| Work style | Enum {5 options} | User entry | Yes |
| Stress level | Enum {4 options} | User entry | Yes |
| Training experience | Enum {4 options} | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Available training days | Array of weekdays | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Session duration | Enum {4 options} | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Equipment access | Enum {4 options} | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Sleep duration | Enum {4 options} | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Training time preference | Enum {4 options} | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Favorite exercises | Array of exercise IDs | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Cardio preferences | Object (goal, intensity, activities, days/week) | User entry (workout builder) | No |
| Biometric enabled | Boolean | System / user consent | No |
| Connections connected | Array of connection IDs | User action | No |
| Per-connection AI consent | Boolean per connection | User consent | No |

## 4.1.11 Derived Profile Data

After Profile Generation runs, the following values are computed and stored for use throughout the app. The vast majority are deterministic outputs of the formulas specified in §4.1.5. A single short AI call produces the insight narration; it does not compute any targets.

| Field | Computed From | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| `rmr_kcal` | Mifflin-St Jeor OR Katch-McArdle (selected on body fat % availability per §4.1.5) | Nutrition scoring, chatbot, scoring engine baseline |
| `rmr_formula_used` | Runtime branch in RMR computation ("mifflin_st_jeor" or "katch_mcardle") | Profile reveal info chip, chatbot explanations, auto-adjust reason strings (§4.4.10) |
| `neat_kcal` | OCC_BASE[occupation] + (net_steps × 0.04), with workout-step subtraction per §4.1.5 | TDEE, scoring engine |
| `eat_kcal` | Sum of logged workout burns (rolling 7-day average) | TDEE, scoring engine |
| `tef_kcal` | TEF formula: protein×4×0.25 + carbs×4×0.08 + fat×9×0.03, OR 10% flat | TDEE, "Show the math" drawer |
| `tdee_kcal` | RMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF | Nutrition scoring, chatbot, rollover/auto-adjust |
| `calorie_target_kcal` | max(round(TDEE × (1 + cal_adjust)), RMR) | Nutrition scoring, daily ring, macro bar denominators |
| `protein_target_g` | round(reference_kg × protein_g_per_kg) | Nutrition scoring, macro bars |
| `protein_g_per_kg` | Goal-specific value (1.6 / 1.8 / 2.0 / 2.2) | Profile reveal chip, explanations |
| `protein_reference_weight_kg` | Current or goal weight per goal rules (§4.1.5) | Profile reveal, auto-adjust |
| `fat_target_g` | max(round(calories × 0.25 / 9), round(weight_kg × 0.7)) | Nutrition scoring, macro bars |
| `carb_target_g` | max(100, remainder after protein and fat) | Nutrition scoring, macro bars |
| `bmi` | weight_kg / (height_m)² | Profile reveal display, chatbot |
| `bmi_category` | WHO classification bands | Profile reveal display |
| `goal_rationale_text` | Templated per-goal explanation (deterministic lookup from `goal_config`) | Profile reveal Plan Block |
| `scientific_sources[]` | Citation array from `goal_config` reference pool (§4.1.5) | Profile reveal citation drawer |
| `training_plan` | Deterministic structure from Workout Builder answers + AI structured-output call (if user chose to build) | Fitness category, schedule |

**Removed from prior versions:** The `archetype` field (cut in v1.18) and the `personalized_insight` field (cut in v1.19). Profile Generation at v1 has zero AI calls — the only AI surfacing from the onboarding flow is the optional Workout Plan Generation call (§4.1.6), which the user explicitly opts into.

## 4.1.12 AI Usage Summary

AI calls during onboarding are kept minimal. The profile itself computes deterministically; AI is used only where it genuinely adds value (narrating numbers for the insight block, structuring Workout Builder output). All onboarding AI calls are free to the user regardless of subscription status (not counted against the 3-free-use paywall counter per §4.1.9).

### Profile Insight Call

*Removed in v1.19.* Prior versions specified a Haiku call to narrate the computed profile with a 2-3 sentence paragraph. The call is cut. Profile Generation is now fully deterministic; there is no AI in the onboarding profile path. See §4.1.5 "Insight Block: Removed in v1.19" for full rationale.

### Workout Plan Generation Call (conditional — only if user chose Build My Plan)

- **Model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6 (reasoning-heavy structured output — plan generation requires actual inference)
- **Input:** Workout Builder answers (~300 tokens)
- **Output:** Full weekly training plan with exercises, sets, reps, progression (~800 tokens)
- **Target latency:** <10 seconds
- **Cost per call:** approximately $0.008
- **Fallback:** If Sonnet fails, use deterministic rule-based plan generator as backup

### Workout Plan Understanding Call (conditional — bundled with Plan Generation)

- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Input:** the generated plan + user's goal context (~400 tokens)
- **Output:** 3-sentence rationale explaining the plan structure (~80 tokens)
- **Target latency:** <4 seconds
- **Cost per call:** approximately $0.0009
- **Fallback:** If call fails, plan displays without the understanding paragraph
- **Descriptive-AI compliant:** the paragraph narrates what the Plan Generation call produced; it does not generate new advice. User-invoked (user chose to build a plan).

### Total Onboarding AI Cost

Maximum total cost per onboarding (both conditional calls, only if user chose Build My Plan): approximately $0.009. Users who chose "I'll do my own" incur zero AI cost during onboarding. Target onboarding-to-paid-trial conversion of 40% means AI cost per paying user is approximately $0.023 in the worst case — less than 0.5% of first month revenue at Pro tier. Economically viable without restriction.

## 4.1.13 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Every decision in Section 4.1 maps to a specific commitment under Design Principle 3.1 (Ruthless Friction Reduction). The following table documents the reductions made relative to a naive implementation.

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Separate signup vs personalization | Removed — signup is minimal, personalization starts immediately |
| Password complexity shown upfront | Removed — shown only if entered password fails |
| Email verification blocks onboarding | Removed — verification sent async |
| Name required on signup screen | Removed — collected in Step 1 with context |
| Age slider | Removed — replaced with birthday date picker (auto-adjusts forever) |
| 2-inch height increments | Removed — decimal inches supported |
| Blank height/weight fields | Removed — smart median defaults |
| Activity level as separate question | Removed — inferred from occupation + work style |
| Generic goal descriptions | Removed — each goal has specific, active-voice description |
| Free-text occupation | Modified — autocomplete with common options + custom fallback |
| 0-10 stress slider | Removed — replaced with 4 discrete plain-language options |
| Profile card without live feedback | Removed — live BMI displayed as user types |
| Demo screens before onboarding | Removed — value is shown through live feedback, not separate demos |
| Connection required before dashboard | Removed — dashboard works with zero connections |
| Paywall blocking dashboard | Removed — paywall triggers only on 4th AI action |
| Credit card required to start trial | Kept — RevenueCat data shows this doubles trial-to-paid conversion |
| CAPTCHA on signup | Removed — Clerk bot protection is sufficient |
| Workout plan required | Removed — optional 'Build My Plan' vs 'I'll do my own' |

## 4.1.14 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast minimum (4.5:1 for body text)
- All tappable targets are minimum 44pt on iOS, 48dp on Android
- VoiceOver and TalkBack tested end-to-end through onboarding
- Dynamic type supported up to iOS accessibility size Large
- Color is never the sole indicator of state (every color change also has icon or text change)
- All form fields have visible labels (not just placeholders)
- Error messages appear inline next to the failing field, not as toasts only
- Biometric enrollment has text alternative ("Skip for Now" always visible)

## 4.1.15 Edge Cases & Error Handling

- **Network loss mid-onboarding** — user data is cached locally; onboarding resumes from last completed step when network restores; no progress is lost
- **User force-quits during onboarding** — onboarding state is preserved; on next app open, user lands on the step they abandoned
- **Profile Generation API fails** — fallback to Haiku with simplified prompt; if both fail, use deterministic rule-based profile with notice to user
- **User under 13 detected** — account is deactivated immediately; email sent to account address explaining COPPA restriction
- **User 13-17 detected** — account continues but Plaid connection, progress photos, and some AI features are blocked; disclosed in onboarding
- **OAuth provider returns incomplete data** — missing fields (e.g., no name from provider) are collected during Step 1 instead
- **Workout Builder abandonment** — user can Close at any step; incomplete workout data is discarded; user returns to Profile Reveal screen
- **Connection OAuth fails** — user returns to Connection Connect screen with error toast; other connections remain available; Skip For Now still works

## 4.1.16 Analytics Events

The following events are captured during onboarding for product analytics. All events respect user privacy preferences (see Section 6 — Data & Privacy). No personally identifiable information is sent to analytics platforms.

- `onboarding_started`
- `auth_method_selected` (apple, google, email)
- `auth_completed`
- `biometric_enrolled` (true, false, not_available)
- `step1_completed`
- `step2_goal_selected` (enum)
- `step3_completed`
- `profile_generation_started`
- `profile_generation_completed` (latency_ms)
- `workout_builder_entered`
- `workout_builder_step_completed` (step_number)
- `workout_builder_abandoned` (step_number)
- `workout_builder_completed`
- `connection_connect_presented`
- `connection_connected` (connection_id)
- `connection_skipped`
- `first_dashboard_viewed`
- `paywall_triggered` (ai_action_type)
- `paywall_subscription_started` (tier)
- `paywall_dismissed`

---

# Section 4.2 — Home Dashboard

## 4.2.1 Overview

The Home Dashboard is the screen users see most. It is the product's single most important surface. Every design decision here is amplified across thousands of daily views.

The dashboard's job is to let a user understand their current state in under two seconds, identify where to drill deeper in another two, and take a quick action (log food, log workout, ask AI) in one tap.

### Dashboard Philosophy

**Raw data is the signal. Scores are the summary. Everything else supports why the scores are what they are.**

The user opens the dashboard and sees actual meaningful numbers — calories left, weight, steps, tasks complete, spending against budget. These are the things they already care about. Above and around those numbers sit the four category scores and the Overall Score, providing a quick aggregate signal. Scores without supporting data feel abstract; supporting data without scores feels disorganized. Together they give users the scan-and-act experience the dashboard exists for.

### What The Dashboard Does Not Do

- **No AI-generated insights or "Today's Focus" content.** If a prompt or insight is worth surfacing, it's surfaced deterministically or through the chatbot on demand.
- **No daily check-in prompts.** Too much input friction for signal that can be inferred.
- **No drill indicators (chevrons, "see more" text).** Premium apps trust users to discover interactivity.
- **No personal baseline comparisons on dashboard surface.** Baselines exist and inform scores but aren't displayed as "+5 vs avg" clutter on home. Baseline details appear when the user drills into a category.
- **No settings, account info, or notifications list.** Header profile icon leads to settings; notifications live in their own view.
- **No tutorials, upsells, or "rate us" modals that interrupt the dashboard view.**

## 4.2.2 Layout Structure

The dashboard is a vertically scrollable view. On the smallest supported screen (iPhone SE, 4.7" display), the primary content fits above the fold without scrolling. On larger devices, content density remains the same — we don't add extra elements just because there's space.

### Top to bottom

**1. Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)**

- Left: Life Dashboard wordmark
- Center: Weather context indicator (small, subtle) — current temperature and a condition icon (sun / cloud / rain / snow). Tap: expands briefly to show today's high/low and condition text. Hidden if weather permission is denied or weather fetch fails.
- Right: Profile icon (tap → Settings)
- Height: minimal, ~48pt

**Weather data source:**
- iOS: Apple WeatherKit (free for Apple developers, native framework)
- Android: OpenWeatherMap free tier (60 calls/min, 1M calls/month; ample for 10k-user scale given client-side caching)
- Location resolution: coarse (zip code or city-level), never precise GPS; weather for the user's current approximate location
- Refresh cadence: on app open + every 60 minutes while foregrounded; never in background
- Permission: piggy-backs on the general location permission; if user denies location entirely, weather is simply hidden (no separate weather permission prompt)
- Privacy posture: weather data is low-sensitivity ambient context. Sent to AI only when user has enabled AI consent for the `weather` source (default OFF like all AI consent toggles per 6.4.2). The weather display itself works regardless of AI consent — this is a display feature, not an AI feature.

**2. Overall Score**

- Centered number (0–100) with a faint 7-day sparkline behind it
- Typography: large but not hero-sized (~48pt)
- Subtle color state indicator (dot or small pill): green/amber/red based on score band
- No descriptive label — the number is self-explanatory

**3. Goal strip (v1.27)**

Horizontal goal strip showing all active goals. Per the v1.27 unified goal system (§4.10.2), all goals appear here regardless of category or primary-fitness-goal designation.

**Layout:**
- Each goal rendered as a compact progress card (approximately 130pt wide on a standard 4.7"-6.7" phone viewport)
- Card contents: category chip (small colored dot — Fitness blue, Nutrition green, Finance orange, Time purple), goal name (truncated to single line, ~18 chars), progress bar (thin, inside card), progress label below bar (e.g., "3.2 lbs to go" or "$6,600 remaining")
- Primary fitness goal (if the user has one) is leftmost, marked with a subtle "•" indicator adjacent to the category chip
- Remaining goals follow in reverse-chronological creation order
- Tap any card: opens Goal Detail View for that goal

**Display rules by goal count:**
- **0 goals:** strip hidden entirely
- **1 goal:** single card displayed at full width with slightly larger padding (~170pt); left-aligned
- **2-3 goals:** cards laid out side-by-side, fitting full strip without scroll; typical Core user pattern
- **4-6 goals (Pro only):** cards shown at standard ~130pt width; strip scrolls horizontally; first ~2.5 cards visible on standard viewport; right-edge fade hints at scrollability; momentum scrolling; snap-to-card on release

**Density considerations for 6-goal case:**
- Goal name truncation is aggressive (~18 chars with ellipsis) to preserve card readability at ~130pt width — users with goals named "Save $10K for down payment on house" see "Save $10K for down…"
- Progress labels are shortened to minimum readable form ("$6,600" not "$6,600 remaining") when card width is constrained
- On smaller phones (iPhone SE viewport at ~320pt goal-strip width), the layout falls back to a single-card-visible-with-scroll pattern for 4+ goals rather than attempting multi-card side-by-side
- Accessibility: VoiceOver reads each card's goal name, progress, and target; scroll gesture announced

**Minimum touch target:** each goal card maintains a 44pt minimum tappable height regardless of strip density, per accessibility requirements (§14.12.2).

**Hidden if no active goals.**

**4. Streak Bar (90-day horizontal scroll)**

A horizontal scrollable dot bar replacing the minimal "7-day streak" indicator used in earlier versions. This is a direct elevation of the Flask PWA streak bar pattern, preserved verbatim per v1.18 decision.

- Horizontal scroll showing the most recent 90 days, today at the right edge, oldest on the left
- Each day is rendered as a small dot (approximately 12pt diameter, tappable target expanded to 36pt hit area for accessibility)
- Dot states:
  - **Filled (accent color)** — day had a qualifying interaction
  - **Empty (outline only)** — day had no qualifying interaction
  - **Today (distinct inner glow)** — today's dot is visually distinguished regardless of fill state to anchor the scroll position
- Overlay elements:
  - Flame icon above today's dot with an unlimited numeric counter showing consecutive logged days (can exceed 90 — the counter is not capped at 90 even though the scroll window is)
  - Small "90 days" label at the top-left of the bar, with a subtle gradient fade on the far-left edge to hint at scrollability
- Tap any day: opens the Day Detail screen (§4.2a) for that date — not a mini-view, the full Day Detail with all categories populated
- Swipe horizontally: pans through the 90-day window (does not extend past 90 days on home; longer-range history lives in category Progress/History tabs per §4.3/§4.4/§4.5/§4.6)

**Streak "logged" definition (v1.18, expanded from Flask):**

Per v1.18 decision, a day is considered "logged" if ANY of the following occurred during that day (local timezone):

- A meal was logged (any calories > 0)
- A workout was logged (manual or auto-imported from HealthKit/Strava/Garmin)
- Body weight was entered or synced
- Steps recorded (total_steps > 0 from HealthKit/Health Connect)
- A financial transaction was categorized, reviewed, or manually entered
- A task was marked complete
- A calendar event was created from within Life Dashboard
- A sleep record was synced from HealthKit/Health Connect

The definition is deliberately permissive ("any meaningful interaction any category") so that users get streak credit for engaging with any corner of the product, not just logging food. This matches the cross-category breadth of the app.

**Streak computation formula:**

```
IF logged(today) is true:
    start_offset = 0   // today counts toward streak
ELSE:
    start_offset = 1   // today hasn't been logged yet; streak is still alive if yesterday was logged

streak_count = count of consecutive days-ago (starting at start_offset)
               where logged(today - days_ago) is true,
               stopping at the first unlogged day
```

Today not yet being logged does NOT break the streak — the count bridges over today until the user logs something or the day ends at local midnight. At local midnight on a day with no qualifying interaction, the streak breaks.

**Streak is purely decorative in v1.** Streak status does not feed into any score. It is an affirmation mechanic, not a scoring signal, preserved from Flask because users valued the visible consistency anchor. The Streak Bar sits above the four category cards as a subtle but persistent emotional anchor.

**5. Four category cards — 2×2 grid**

- Each card occupies roughly a quarter of the remaining screen
- Cards are the spine of the dashboard — everything above them is summary, everything in them is substance
- Detailed card specification in 4.2.3 below

**6. Day Timeline preview strip**

- Horizontal scrollable ribbon showing today's activity timeline
- Approximately 6 items visible: past-completed items to the left, upcoming items to the right, current "now" marker in the middle
- Each item shows: small icon + short label + time (e.g., "🏃 Run 7:15am" / "📅 Standup 10:00am" / "🍽️ Lunch 12:30pm")
- Past items are dimmed; upcoming items are bright
- Data sources: sleep/wake times, calendar events, logged meals/workouts, location changes, scheduled tasks
- Tap any item: jumps to Day Timeline full view in Time tab (Section 4.6.5)
- Empty state: if user has no connections or data for today, strip is hidden entirely (not shown as empty)
- Strip appears between the four category cards and the FAB — a glanceable "shape of today" summary

**7. Floating Action Button (FAB)**

- Bottom right, fixed position, overlays scroll
- Single "+" icon
- Tap: opens the chatbot overlay (Section 4.7)
- The overlay contains two distinct surfaces: the chatbot (for asking questions about your data) and shortcut buttons (Log Meal, Log Workout, Add Task, Add Transaction) that open native logging cards directly. Logging does not go through the chatbot.

**8. Bottom tab bar (fixed)**

- Five tabs, equal weight: Home, Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time
- Currently selected tab has a subtle indicator (filled icon or accent color)
- Tapping a category tab navigates to that category's detail view (specified in Sections 4.3–4.6)
- Settings is not a tab — accessed via the profile icon in the header

## 4.2.3 Category Cards

Each card follows the same structural template but surfaces different data per category. Every card contains:

- **Category name** — label at the top
- **Category score** (0–100) — the primary visual element on the card, prominent typography
- **Subtle color state indicator** — small colored dot or pill reflecting score band
- **Supporting raw data** — the specific metrics that make the score feel grounded and actionable

The user taps anywhere on the card to drill into that category's full detail view (Sections 4.3–4.6).

### Fitness Card

Supporting data displayed:

- Weight (current, most recent measurement)
- Steps today
- Calories burned today
- Last workout summary (e.g., "Push day, 47 min" or "5K run, 28:15")

### Nutrition Card

Supporting data displayed:

- Calories remaining today (hero metric — largest on the card besides score)
- Burn minus eaten equation ("2,340 burned − 1,612 eaten = 728 remaining")
- Macro bars (protein, carbs, fat with consumed/target)

The calories remaining metric is the Nutrition card's hero because it directly answers the question the user asks most: "What can I eat right now?"

### Finance Card

Supporting data displayed:

- Weekly spend against budget
- Budget remaining this week
- Next bill due (amount and date)

In v1 before Plaid is connected, the card displays an empty state: "Connect your bank to activate Finance." The card remains visible and tappable but the Finance score defaults to null and does not drag down the Overall Score.

### Life Card

Supporting data displayed:

- Tasks completed today (N of M)
- Next calendar event (name and time)
- Email response status (e.g., "3 important emails unread" or "Inbox on top")

In v1 before Calendar/Gmail are connected, the card displays an empty state similar to Finance. Time score defaults to null until data sources are connected.

## 4.2.4 Scoring System (Framework Only)

Category scores and the Overall Score appear on this screen, but the specific math and data inputs for each score are specified in Section 9 (Scoring Engine). This subsection is the authoritative principles reference for all downstream sections.

The following principles about scoring are established here and referenced by downstream sections:

- **Every score is 0–100.** Higher is better.
- **Every score is deterministic.** No AI is used in the scoring path.
- **Scoring uses a penalty-from-perfect-day model** (v1.18 locked). Each category score starts at 100 (a perfect day) and penalties subtract from it based on per-signal deltas from targets or baselines. The user sees the result as positive progress toward a perfect day, not as a literal running subtraction. Display is "you're at 87 of 100"; internal math is "you lost 13 points across these signals" — mathematically equivalent, user-facing framing is the positive one.
- **Every category score uses only that category's data.** Cross-domain correlations (e.g., Fitness affecting Time) are not part of v1 scoring math. Cross-domain questions are answered by the v1 chatbot (Section 4.7) using typed context containers, but they do not influence category scores. The Time category was previously specified as using cross-domain inputs; in v1.18 this was revised — Time uses only Time-domain signals (sleep regularity from HealthKit, attention fragmentation from Screen Time, location intentionality from CoreLocation, schedule density balance from Calendar, rhythm adherence from cross-domain anchor timestamps). No score folds in other-category goal adherence; Overall is where cross-domain aggregation happens.
- **Each category contributes up to 25 points to the Overall Score.** The Overall Score is computed as Σ(category_score × category_weight / 100) where the four category weights default to 25% each and sum to 100%. Mathematically this means: if all four categories score 100, Overall = 100.
- **Overall Score weights are user-adjustable with constraints.** Each category weight must be in the range [5, 55] and all four must sum to 100. This prevents a user from zeroing out a category entirely while still allowing meaningful personalization (e.g., a user who cares most about Fitness can push Fitness to 55 and the others to 15 each).
- **Signal-specific safe ranges** (v1.18): each underlying signal within a category has a "safe range" — a tolerance band within which full points are earned. Exact bands are signal-specific (e.g., protein ±10g of target, calories ±100kcal of prorated target, sleep rhythm ±45 min of personal baseline). Section 9 specifies all bands.
- **Tiered proration** (v1.18): the time-of-day proration inherited conceptually from Flask's Momentum system is restricted to *accumulating signals only* (calories consumed vs target, steps vs goal, screen time vs typical). *Event signals* (workout happened, bill paid, weight logged) score instantly on occurrence with no proration. *Trailing-window signals* (7-day strength volume, 30-day savings rate, 14-day sleep rhythm) use their natural rolling window and are not prorated. Each prorated signal tunes its own curve; Section 9 specifies each curve.
- **Scores degrade gracefully with missing data.** A category with insufficient data shows "—" and does not drag down the Overall Score (the Overall re-normalizes over the remaining scored categories). Minimum data to compute a category score: at least 2 signals in that category have at least 1 data point each. Minimum data for the Overall Score: at least 2 categories have computed scores.
- **Scores improve in personalization as data accumulates.** Personal baselines (30-day rolling medians for signals where baselines apply) replace hardcoded population defaults after 14 days. Baselines are computed per-signal, not per-category, and only for signals where personalization is meaningful (Section 9 specifies which). A subtle "Calibrating" pill appears on category cards during the first 14 days, then auto-removes.
- **Day 1 behavior:** a user with fewer than the minimum data points shows "—" with a CTA ("Connect data sources or log something to see your score"). As soon as the user meets the minimum threshold, the score populates with population-default baselines and refines over the warmup window.
- **Color bands:** category score cards use green/amber/red visual cues mapped to score bands. Specific thresholds are defined in §4.2.9 (Color & Status Approach). The color is a quick status glance; the numeric value remains the primary signal.

The exact score composition per category is deferred to Section 9 (Scoring Engine). Section 9 also defines: the specific safe-range bands per signal, the curves for prorated signals, the subsystem weights within each category, and the Overall Score recomputation pipeline.

## 4.2.5 Goal System (Framework Only)

Goals are surfaced on the dashboard as a single primary goal progress bar. The complete goal system — the predetermined library, selection flow, progress computation, and completion/sharing experience — is specified in Section 4.10.

The following principles about goals are established here and referenced downstream:

- **Users pick goals from a predetermined library**, not open-ended text entry. This ensures every goal has a defined data source, deterministic progress calculation, and automatic tracking. v1 does not support free-form custom goals — a tracked goal requires a deterministic data hook, and arbitrary user-typed goals would either be untrackable or require per-goal AI processing that isn't economical at launch scale.
- **The library contains 5–6 goals per category** (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time), curated for data-hook cleanliness. Total: 22 goals in v1.
- **Users can have up to 3 active goals on Core, up to 6 on Pro** (v1.27). Among active goals, at most one is the user's designated "primary fitness goal" — the body-composition goal that drives calorie and macro target recomputation (§4.10.2).
- **Goal parameters (targets, dates) are customizable** after the goal is selected from the library.
- **Goal progress updates automatically** from category data — users never manually update goal status.
- **Goal completion triggers a celebration flow** with a shareable social card and a prompt to pick a new goal. Celebration and social card design is specified in §4.10.

Full goal library specifications are deferred to Section 4.10.

## 4.2.6 Floating Action Button (FAB)

### Purpose

The FAB is the single entry point for the chatbot overlay. The overlay contains two distinct interaction surfaces — the chatbot for answering questions about the user's data, and shortcut buttons for opening native logging flows directly. The two surfaces are intentionally separate; logging does not route through the chatbot.

This separation reversed an earlier design that had the chatbot act as a universal intelligent router for both questions and logging. That model introduced ambiguity (is "3 eggs" a meal to log or a calorie question?), burned AI budget on tasks that don't need AI, and produced unpredictable behavior. The current two-surface model is cleaner, faster, and cheaper. Full reasoning is in Section 4.7.2.

Separating logging from chatbot routing is a cost-control decision as well as a UX one. Deterministic and native logging paths are used whenever the task does not require reasoning — entering a meal, logging a set, recording weight, marking a task done. These paths carry zero AI cost. Chatbot bandwidth is reserved for what AI is actually good at: questions, synthesis, drafting, and ambiguity resolution. Every dollar of AI spend in Life Dashboard is a dollar that earned its place by doing something deterministic code could not.

### Behavior

- Fixed position bottom right of the dashboard, overlapping scroll
- Single large "+" icon, ~56pt diameter, Life Dashboard accent color
- Tap: the "+" rotates to "X" and the chatbot overlay spawns (detailed animation and layout in Section 4.7.4)
- Tap on the X (where the + was), tap outside, or swipe down: dismisses the overlay
- Tap on a shortcut button: opens the native logging card for that action directly (does not enter the chatbot)
- Type or speak into the chat input: sends a question to the AI for a streamed response

### Overlay Contents (Accessed Via FAB)

When the FAB is tapped from the Home Dashboard, the overlay appears with:

- **Chat input bubble** — compact rounded pill to the left of the X button, with placeholder "Ask anything" and a voice-input mic icon
- **Shortcut buttons** — stacked vertically above the X button:
  - Log Meal
  - Log Workout
  - Add Task
  - Add Transaction (visible only if Plaid is connected)
- **Conversation area** — materializes above the chat input after the first message and grows upward as the conversation fills in

The detailed overlay layout, animation, shortcut behavior, context container architecture, and AI call pipeline are authoritatively specified in Section 4.7 (AI Chatbot). Section 4.2.6 establishes the FAB entry point; Section 4.7 defines what happens inside the overlay.

### Category-Specific Shortcut Sets

The FAB adapts which shortcut buttons appear based on the user's current screen. The chatbot input itself is identical across all screens.

- **From Home Dashboard:** cross-category shortcuts (Log Meal, Log Workout, Add Task, Add Transaction)
- **From Fitness category:** Log Workout, Log Weight, Log Freestyle Workout
- **From Nutrition category:** Log Meal, Scan Meal, Barcode, Saved Meals, Log Water (if hydration active), Pantry Scan (Pro-only)
- **From Finance category:** Add Transaction, Mark Bill Paid, Check Balances
- **From Time category:** Add Task, Add Calendar Event, View Today's Schedule

The chatbot is a single universal question-answerer across all screens — the user can ask any cross-domain question from any category detail view, regardless of which shortcut set is showing.

## 4.2.7 Pull-to-Refresh

### Behavior

User pulls down on the dashboard to trigger a refresh. This:

1. **Immediately re-fetches the latest computed scores from the backend** (under 500ms, feels instant)
2. **Triggers connection syncs in the background** (Apple Health, Plaid, Calendar, Gmail, etc.)
3. **Shows a subtle loading indicator at the top** of the dashboard while background syncs complete
4. **Updates the dashboard progressively** as each connection returns data

This gives the user the "I did something and it responded" feedback loop immediately, while the slower work happens out of the way.

### Auto-Refresh

The dashboard also auto-refreshes when:

- The app returns from background after being away for 5+ minutes
- The user navigates to Home from another tab
- A background sync completes and produces new data

Users should never feel compelled to pull-to-refresh for up-to-date data — the app stays fresh on its own. Pull-to-refresh is an affordance, not a requirement.

## 4.2.8 First-Day Dashboard

### Same Layout, Adapted Content

The dashboard on day 1 has the same structural layout as day 30. The difference is content:

- **Overall score** appears but shows "Learning" state with a subtle indicator for the first 14 days while baselines form
- **Goal progress bar** appears only if the user selected a goal during onboarding (if not, hidden)
- **Streak indicator** starts at 1 day
- **Category cards** show whatever data is available:
  - For data the user provided during onboarding (weight, goal, macros), the card shows those
  - For data from connected connections (if any), the card shows those
  - For data requiring connections not yet connected, the card shows a "Connect X" prompt
- **FAB** is fully functional on day 1

### No Tutorials On Day 1

The dashboard does not show a first-run tutorial overlay, welcome modal, or tooltip walkthrough. The structure is self-explanatory for the target user. If the user wants help, a small "?" icon in the header opens a lightweight help view.

### First-Day Empty States

When a card has no data to show:

- **No score visible** — shows a generic placeholder ("Tracking your Time score" with small info icon)
- **Category-specific prompt** explaining what connecting or logging will activate the card
- **Tap target** — the card is still tappable and leads to the category's setup flow

Example for Finance card on day 1:

> **Finance**
> Connect your bank to activate
> [Tap to connect]

The prompt is plain and low-pressure — not "Missing data!" with an alarm icon.

## 4.2.9 Color & Status Approach

- **Category color identity:** Each category has a subtle accent color used consistently throughout the app (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time each get their own). Specific palette is determined in the design system, not specified here.
- **Score state (v1.18 — color bands on scores themselves):** Category scores and the Overall Score display with a colored indicator (dot, pill, or subtle card background tint) based on score band:
  - **Green** ≥ 75 — "good" band, primary daily state
  - **Amber** 50–74 — "attention" band, signal that something is drifting
  - **Red** < 50 — "course correct" band, used sparingly
  - **Grey / neutral** for "—" (insufficient data or Day 1)
  The color is a quick glance cue; the numeric value remains the primary signal and sits larger than the indicator. Color is always paired with the number and a delta arrow, never used as the sole status cue (WCAG compliance).
- **Prior spec:** Earlier versions of this document stated "no color bands on scores." v1.18 reverses that decision after user testing signal indicated users scan-read scores by color before reading numbers. Bands are back, but subtle — the card is still primarily neutral, color appears in a small indicator.
- **Overall composition:** Dashboard is primarily neutral (dark or light mode per user setting, both fully supported). Color appears at the accent level and at the score indicator level, not as the dominant visual.

## 4.2.10 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast minimum (4.5:1 for body text)
- All tappable targets: minimum 44pt iOS, 48dp Android
- VoiceOver and TalkBack tested end-to-end
- Dynamic type supported up to iOS Large accessibility size
- Color is never the sole indicator of state (color change always paired with icon or text)
- FAB has VoiceOver label "Add new entry" (expands to accessible list on activation)
- Pull-to-refresh has accessibility announcement on trigger

## 4.2.11 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Specific reductions applied to the dashboard relative to a naive implementation:

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Manual refresh required | Auto-refresh on app return and tab switch |
| Tap to see macros or calories | Displayed directly on Nutrition card |
| Drill chevrons cluttering cards | Removed — cards are tappable without announcement |
| Multi-step logging | FAB with direct actions (Log Meal, Log Workout, Chat) |
| Separate navigation to chat | Chat is a FAB action, one tap from anywhere on dashboard |
| Mood check-in blocking dashboard | Killed entirely |
| "Today's Focus" interruption | Killed entirely |
| Settings as a tab | Moved to header profile icon, freeing a tab slot |
| Notifications dragging attention | Not on dashboard at all |
| Tutorials interrupting first view | Removed; optional help button only |
| Modal welcome screens | Removed |
| Rate-us prompts | Not on dashboard |

## 4.2.12 Performance Requirements

- Dashboard renders in under 500ms from app open to fully visible (per Design Principle 3.6)
- Category card tap → category detail view: under 200ms transition
- FAB expansion: under 150ms
- Pull-to-refresh acknowledgment (scores refresh): under 500ms
- Background connection sync completion: varies by connection, but UI never blocks

## 4.2.13 Analytics Events

Events captured on the Home Dashboard:

- `dashboard_viewed` (with sources_connected_count, active_goals_count)
- `dashboard_refresh_triggered` (source: pull, auto, return)
- `category_card_tapped` (category: fitness | nutrition | finance | life)
- `overall_score_tapped`
- `goal_progress_tapped`
- `streak_tapped`
- `fab_expanded`
- `fab_action_selected` (action: log_meal | log_workout | chat | other)
- `empty_state_cta_tapped` (category)

All events respect user privacy preferences (Section 6). No PII is sent to analytics platforms.

## 4.2.14 Edge Cases & Error Handling

- **No internet connection:** Dashboard renders from cached data; badge shows "Offline" subtly; writes queue locally and sync when online
- **Backend returns error on score fetch:** Dashboard shows last-known scores with a subtle "Last updated X min ago" indicator
- **All connections disconnected:** All category cards show empty states; overall score is null; dashboard still functional for manual logging
- **User in first-day, no data yet:** Empty states shown; FAB still works; streak = 1
- **Score computation fails server-side:** Category card shows "—" for score but still shows raw data; error is logged but not surfaced to user
- **Goal removed while on dashboard:** Goal progress bar disappears with a brief animation; no empty state left behind
- **New day rollover while app is open:** Scores recompute for the new day; streak increments if yesterday was logged; brief indicator "New day"
- **Streak broken (no activity for a day):** Streak resets to 0 silently; no punitive messaging

---

# Section 4.2a — Day Detail

## 4.2a.1 Overview

Day Detail is a dedicated screen that presents a complete picture of any single day — today or historical — across every category in the app. It is the deep equivalent of the Day Timeline (§4.6.5) but organized as a stat grid + categorized entry lists rather than a time-axis visualization. The two views complement each other: Day Timeline shows when things happened; Day Detail shows what happened, grouped by category, with direct edit access to every entry.

Day Detail is the most-reached-from screen in the app. Entry points include the Streak Bar (§4.2.2), the Activity Calendar (§4.3.X — Fitness Progress tab), all category history charts, category-specific date pickers, and tap-through from Day Timeline blocks. Any surface that represents a date should lead here.

This subsection formally specifies Day Detail because the prior PRD did not — the Flask PWA had a Day Detail Overlay that was a core workflow surface (users relied on it to backfill, correct, and review), and preserving it explicitly in v1 is a v1.18 commitment (per Flask parity decision Q21).

## 4.2a.2 Entry Points

Day Detail is reachable from:

- **Streak Bar (§4.2.2):** tap any dot in the 90-day scroll → Day Detail for that date
- **Activity Calendar (Fitness Progress tab):** tap any cell → Day Detail for that date
- **Weight trend chart (§4.3.4):** tap any data point → Day Detail for that date
- **Deficit/surplus chart (§4.4.X Nutrition Progress tab):** tap any data point → Day Detail for that date
- **Any history list view** (meals history, workouts history, transactions history, tasks history, etc.): tapping a date header jumps to Day Detail
- **Day Timeline (§4.6.5) Timeline Deep-Dive:** the "view this day in full" action → Day Detail
- **Calendar view in Time tab:** tap date → Day Detail
- **Chatbot deep-link:** if the user asks "show me Tuesday" or "what did I do yesterday," the response can include a deep-link button that opens Day Detail for the referenced date

Day Detail for *today* is always available; Day Detail for past dates is subject to the user's history tier (Core: 90 days hot; Pro: long-term with archived tier per Section 13).

## 4.2a.3 Layout

Day Detail is a vertically scrollable screen with a fixed header. The header is consistent with other detail views but includes date-specific affordances.

### Header (fixed)

- **Back button** (returns to the surface that launched Day Detail)
- **Date title** — formatted as "Tuesday, April 16" with year suppressed for current year, "Tuesday, April 16, 2025" for other years; uses user's locale and date format preferences
- **Day-of-streak chip** — small pill showing the streak position if this day was logged (e.g., "Day 7 of current streak" or "Part of a 12-day streak that ended Friday")
- **Previous/next day arrows** — quick-swipe between adjacent days, matches keyboard arrow key navigation on physical keyboards (desktop-web v2+)

### Summary Stat Grid (fixed-height, immediately below header)

A compact 2-row × 3-column grid showing the six most important numbers for the day. Values are tappable where they jump into the category-specific view for that date.

| Row 1 (top) | | |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight** (lbs or kg) — tappable → Fitness Body detail | **Calories** (eaten / target) — tappable → Nutrition for that day | **Steps** — tappable → Fitness Movement detail |

| Row 2 | | |
|---|---|---|
| **Deficit** (burn − eaten) — tappable → Nutrition detail | **Spending** (day total across tracked categories) — tappable → Finance Spending detail | **Sleep** (hours + sleep quality if available) — tappable → Fitness Sleep detail |

If a metric has no data for the day, the cell renders as "—" (not zero). Empty cells are still tappable and open the relevant category detail view, which will show the empty state for that day.

### Category Sections (scrollable)

Below the stat grid, six category sections stack vertically, each with a collapsible header. All sections are collapsed by default on first Day Detail load; the user's expansion state is remembered per-session. Sections appear in this order:

1. **Fitness** — workouts logged (with exercise detail, volume, duration), body metrics (weight, body fat), PRs detected, plan adherence for this day if a plan was active
2. **Nutrition** — meals logged (with expanded breakdown on tap), daily macros actual vs target, hydration if active, rollover/auto-adjust events
3. **Finance** — transactions for this day (grouped by category), bills paid/due, budget remaining as of EOD
4. **Time** — calendar events, email activity summary (sent / received / important counts — never full bodies), tasks completed and added, screen time summary with top app categories, location visits
5. **Goals** — goal progress deltas for this day (e.g., "Lose Weight: 3.2 lbs to go, was 3.6 yesterday — net −0.4 today")
6. **Day Timeline** — a compressed vertical strip version of the Day Timeline for this day, tappable to open the full Timeline Deep-Dive (§4.6.5) scoped to this date

Each section's collapsed header shows a one-line summary. Example: "Fitness — 1 workout (Push Day, 47 min), weighed in at 184.8 lbs." Tapping the header expands the full list.

Every individual entry within a category section is editable via its native edit flow (§4.4.11 for meal edit, §4.3.12 for workout edit, etc.). Long-press on an entry opens a quick-action menu (Edit / Delete / View detail).

### Add Entry for This Date (bottom)

A floating "+" action button (distinct from the global FAB) at the bottom of the Day Detail screen opens a contextual add menu:

- Log Meal (for this date)
- Log Workout (for this date)
- Log Weight (for this date)
- Add Transaction (for this date)
- Add Task (due this date)

When a logging flow is launched from Day Detail, the timestamp/date defaults to the viewed date (not today). This is the primary pathway for backfilling data — users who missed logging on Tuesday open Tuesday's Day Detail, tap the "+" action, and log to that date.

Backfilling any data for a past date automatically triggers a score recompute for that date per §9's unbounded backfill rule (capped at 7 days if the recompute would incur AI cost per v1.18 locked decision).

## 4.2a.4 Empty States

Day Detail must handle several empty conditions gracefully:

- **No data for the entire day:** stat grid shows all "—", each category section is collapsed with a "No [Fitness/Nutrition/etc.] activity logged for this day" summary. The "+ Add for this date" action is prominent — empty past days are exactly when users want to backfill.
- **Day is before user signup:** header shows a muted note "You joined Life Dashboard on [date]." No category sections shown, no add action. Viewing pre-account days is allowed but adds nothing.
- **Day is outside user's history tier:** for Core users viewing Day 95 or older, the stat grid shows archived summary (stored via the hot-to-archived migration per Section 6.8), individual entry lists show "Archived — upgrade to Pro to see full details" with an upgrade CTA.
- **Day is in the future:** Day Detail for future dates is permitted but shows only calendar events (from connected calendars) and scheduled tasks. No logging is allowed (can't log a meal you haven't eaten). Stat grid cells show "—" for actuals; "target: X" for forward-looking targets where relevant.

## 4.2a.5 Performance Requirements

- Day Detail for today: loads from already-cached data in under 200ms
- Day Detail for any date in the 90-day hot window: loads in under 500ms
- Day Detail for dates in archived storage (Pro): loads in under 3 seconds; shows skeleton loaders for sections that are still loading
- Scroll performance: 60fps on iPhone SE and equivalent Android reference devices
- Swipe prev/next between dates: load next day's data preemptively when user lingers on current date for >1 second

## 4.2a.6 Analytics Events

- `day_detail.opened` (fired with source: streak_bar / activity_calendar / chart / chatbot / timeline / calendar)
- `day_detail.section_expanded` (fired with section name)
- `day_detail.entry_edited` (fired with entry type)
- `day_detail.entry_added` (fired with entry type, date_distance_from_today: 0 / 1-7 / 8-30 / 31-90 / archived)
- `day_detail.swipe_navigation` (fired when user swipes prev/next date)

## 4.2a.7 Data Persistence

Day Detail is a read-only projection of existing data — it has no persistence model of its own. It queries the meal_logs, workout_logs, transactions, bills, tasks, calendar_events, location_visits, screen_time_daily, body_weight_logs, and daily_scores tables filtered by user_id and date. No dedicated `day_detail` table is needed.

For Pro users viewing archived dates, Day Detail reads from the archived-storage tier per §6.8.5 (hot-to-archived migration).

## 4.2a.8 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast minimum
- Collapsible section headers announce state ("expanded" / "collapsed") via VoiceOver and TalkBack
- Swipe-between-days gesture has an equivalent button control for users with motor difficulties
- Stat grid cells announce their metric name + value + unit ("Weight, 184.8 pounds")
- Empty-state copy is descriptive, not just visual

---

# Section 4.3 — Fitness Category

## 4.3.1 Overview

The Fitness category is the Life Dashboard domain covering everything body, training, and recovery. It is the most complex category in the app — more data types, more connection surface area, and more user workflows than any other category. Users reach it by tapping the Fitness card on the Home Dashboard or selecting the Fitness tab from the bottom navigation.

The Fitness category must serve a heterogeneous user base with different training philosophies. A marathon runner, a CrossFit athlete, a bodybuilder, and a general-fitness user all live in the same app but care about different metrics. The design challenge is surfacing the right data for each user without forcing any of them to see irrelevant subsystems or abandon the app because it feels like it wasn't built for them.

The category's approach: one unified Fitness detail view composed of seven subsystems. All users see all subsystems. Subsystems without data show helpful prompts (connect an connection, log your first workout, set a goal). Subsystems with data light up with the metrics and visuals appropriate to that domain. The score at the top summarizes the aggregate state; the subsystems explain why.

### Relationship To Other Categories

Fitness owns body, training, movement, sleep, and recovery data. It does not own:

- **Nutrition and macros** — owned by Section 4.4 (Nutrition)
- **Cross-domain correlations** like "your sleep is affecting your productivity" — these are handled by the v1 chatbot (Section 4.7), not by category scoring
- **Calendar-based workout scheduling reminders** — notifications owned by Section 4.9

### Current State vs Target State

Some Fitness features exist in the current Flask PWA and are preserved into the React Native build. Others are new v1 additions. Each subsystem specification below marks whether its components are:

- **Existing** — already implemented in Flask, migrated to React Native
- **New in v1** — specified here for the first time, built fresh in React Native
- **Pro-gated** — premium-tier features; the specific tier cut-line is deferred to Section 13 (Business Requirements)

### Success Metrics

- **Daily active logging rate** — percentage of users who log at least one workout, meal, or weight entry on a given day. Target: 55%+
- **Workout-to-repeat rate** — percentage of users who log a second workout within 7 days of their first. Target: 70%+
- **Plan adherence rate** — for users with an active training plan, percentage of scheduled workouts completed. Target: 60%+ average across users
- **Connection-connected percentage** — percentage of Fitness users with at least one body/recovery connection. Target: 50%+ by day 30

## 4.3.2 Fitness Detail View Layout

The Fitness detail view uses a tab-bar layout tuned to the category's nature (per v1.18 decision — each category has its own tab structure rather than a forced-uniform layout). Fitness gets three tabs: **Today / Progress / History**. The tab bar is fixed just below the header.

### Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)

- Back arrow to Home (or category tab indicator if arrived via bottom nav)
- Title: "Fitness"
- Profile icon (tap → Settings)
- Overflow menu (•••) with options: Edit goals, Edit subsystem visibility, Export data, Help

### Tab bar (fixed, below header)

Three tabs: **Today** · **Progress** · **History**. "Today" is the default tab. Tab switching is a client-side state change; no full re-fetch unless crossing into Progress or History for the first time.

### Today tab (default)

**1. Score summary block**

- Large Fitness score (0–100) with 7-day sparkline
- Score state indicator (colored dot/pill per §4.2.9)
- Single label beneath: aggregate short-form state (e.g., "Strong recovery week" or "Training consistency down") — deterministic based on score trend, not AI
- No "what changed" narrative, no AI insight at this level

**2. Active goals strip**

- Any Fitness-category goals from the user's 3 active goals appear here
- Each shows: goal name, current value, target, progress bar
- Up to 2 Fitness goals visible; if more, a "+N more" indicator that expands
- Tap a goal: opens goal detail with history and option to adjust

**3. Today's summary card**

- Single card showing today's fitness state in one scan
- Contents (reading left-to-right, wrapping on narrow screens):
  - Calories burned today
  - Steps today
  - Active minutes today (if available)
  - Last workout (type + time ago, e.g., "Chest/Triceps — 3h ago")
- Tap: opens Day Detail (§4.2a) for today

**4. Seven subsystem cards (stacked vertically)**

- Order: Plan, Strength, Cardio, Body, Movement, Sleep, Recovery
- Each subsystem is its own card with its own data, charts, and quick actions
- Order rationale: Plan first because most actionable today; Strength and Cardio next because highest user engagement; Body in the middle; Movement/Sleep/Recovery at bottom because they're mostly passive ingestion
- Cards without data show empty states (see each subsystem below)

**5. Floating Action Button (FAB)**

- Same FAB pattern as Home Dashboard (Section 4.2.6) — opens the chatbot overlay
- The overlay contains two surfaces: the chatbot for fitness and cross-domain questions, and shortcut buttons that open native logging flows directly
- Fitness-specific shortcut buttons:
  - Log Workout (opens workout logger with plan template if available, otherwise freestyle)
  - Log Weight (opens body weight entry card)
  - Log Freestyle Workout (opens freestyle workout logger explicitly)
- Logging does not go through the chatbot. Workout descriptions typed into the chat are redirected to the Log Workout shortcut; the chatbot answers questions about training, body metrics, sleep, and recovery.

**6. Bottom tab bar**

- Standard five-tab bar; Fitness tab is selected/highlighted

### Progress tab

The Progress tab is the chart-and-visualization surface for Fitness — where longitudinal data lives. This pattern (a Progress tab as a second-class citizen of each category detail view) is a v1.18 addition per Flask parity: Flask's Progress tab had the Activity Calendar, weight trend, deficit chart, strength progression, and macro trend charts together in one place, and users relied on it as the "where am I going" view. v1 preserves the pattern, tuned to each category's data.

**Progress tab contents (top to bottom):**

1. **Scale toggle** — 7D / 30D / 90D / All-time (Pro). Applies to all charts on the tab. Default: 30D.
2. **Activity Calendar** (see §4.3.2a below)
3. **Weight trend chart** (moved here from the Body subsystem's detail view — still reachable via Body subsystem tap, this is the same component)
4. **Strength volume trend** (weekly total weight lifted)
5. **Cardio volume trend** (weekly minutes of cardio)
6. **Steps trend** (daily steps)
7. **Sleep duration trend** (if sleep data connected)
8. **HRV trend** (if HRV data connected)

Each chart supports: toggle between raw readings and smoothed overlay; tap any data point to open Day Detail (§4.2a) for that date.

### History tab

Full chronological list of logged workouts, weight entries, and body measurements. Filterable by type (workouts only, weight only, etc.) and by date range. Each entry is tappable to view/edit. This is the "I want to find that one workout from three weeks ago" surface. List uses infinite scroll; lazy-loads in 30-day chunks for Pro users with long history.

### Layout Constraints

On iPhone SE (smallest supported screen), the Today tab's Score summary block + Active goals strip + Today's summary card are all visible without scrolling. The seven subsystems sit below the fold and require scrolling to discover.

On larger devices, the layout density is unchanged — no bonus content added for extra screen space. Content breathes rather than expanding.

## 4.3.2a Activity Calendar

The Activity Calendar is a month-grid visualization of the user's training activity, preserved from Flask. It lives at the top of the Fitness Progress tab.

### Visual Structure

- 7-column grid (Sunday through Saturday by default; user's locale may reorder)
- Rows: configurable via the Progress-tab scale toggle. 30D = 5 weeks, 90D = 13 weeks, All-time = scrollable by month
- Each cell represents one day and is color-coded by activity type:
  - **Strength** (solid accent, category color for strength)
  - **Cardio** (solid accent, category color for cardio)
  - **Both** (split fill showing both colors in the same cell)
  - **Rest** (subtle dot, indicates the day was logged but no workout — data present, workout intentional zero)
  - **No data** (empty cell, indicates no log of any kind for that day)
- Today's cell is visually distinguished with a border ring
- Week labels ("Wk 1", "Wk 2", etc.) on the left edge; day-of-week letters across the top

### Interaction

- Tap any cell → opens Day Detail (§4.2a) for that date
- Long-press → quick-preview tooltip showing workout name(s) and duration without navigating
- Scroll vertically within the calendar (when in 90D or All-time scale) to see earlier months

### Legend

A small inline legend below the calendar indicates the color coding:
- ● Strength
- ● Cardio
- ● Both
- ○ Rest day (logged no workout)
- · (muted dot) No data

### Edge Cases

- A day where the user logged a meal/weight/other data but no workout displays as "Rest" (the day is logged, the workout was intentional zero)
- A day with zero data of any kind displays as "No data" (empty cell)
- Multiple workouts on the same day: if any strength + any cardio, shows "Both"; if multiple strength only, still shows "Strength"

### Accessibility

- VoiceOver/TalkBack: each cell announces "Tuesday April 16, Strength workout and Cardio workout" or "Wednesday April 17, no data"
- Color is never the sole cue; tapping cells also reveals a sheet-based date detail, accessible regardless of color perception
- Dynamic type: labels reflow; cell size is responsive

### Data Source

The Activity Calendar reads from the workout logs, meal logs, and weight logs tables, projecting each date into the color-code state. No dedicated storage; this is a pure view.

## 4.3.3 The Subsystem Model

The Fitness category is composed of seven subsystems. Each subsystem is a self-contained module covering one aspect of fitness. All users see all subsystems on the Fitness detail view's Today tab. Subsystems with no data display an empty state that either prompts the user to act (connect, log, set a goal) or simply describes what the subsystem would track once data is available.

### Why All Subsystems Are Always Shown

During Section 4.3 design, three modularity approaches were considered:

- **All subsystems shown (chosen for v1)** — simplest to build, best for discovery, respects that users may not know what Life Dashboard can track until they see it
- **Hide after 14 days of inactivity** — added complexity for minimal benefit; risks hiding subsystems users might return to
- **User explicitly picks relevant subsystems during setup** — adds friction, contradicts Design Principle 3.1

The chosen approach (show all) means users who train only strength still see Cardio, Sleep, and Recovery subsystems — but those subsystems show empty states rather than competing for visual real estate. Over time, users may disable specific subsystems via Settings if they find them distracting. This is a Settings option, not a mandatory choice.

### Subsystem Summary

| # | Subsystem | Primary Data | Manual Input Possible? | Connection Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plan | Today's workout, adherence rate, upcoming sessions | No — generated from Workout Builder | No |
| 2 | Strength | Exercises, sets, reps, weight, PRs, volume | Yes — full manual logging | Optional (Hevy/Strong import planned v2+) |
| 3 | Cardio | Sessions, distance, duration, pace, HR, zones | Yes — simple (duration + distance) | Optional (rich data requires connection) |
| 4 | Body | Weight, body fat %, measurements | Yes — weight entry | Optional (smart scale auto-sync) |
| 5 | Movement | Steps, active minutes, calories burned | Yes — steps only | Optional (native HealthKit/Google Fit) |
| 6 | Sleep | Duration, stages, consistency | No — connection only | Yes (Apple Health, Garmin, Oura, Whoop) |
| 7 | Recovery | HRV, resting HR, readiness | No — connection only | Yes |

## 4.3.4 Body Subsystem

The Body subsystem tracks weight, body composition, and measurements. It is the foundation for RMR calculation, goal progress tracking (weight loss, muscle gain), and long-term trend analysis.

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Body"
- Current weight (large, primary metric)
- 30-day weight trend sparkline
- Delta vs goal (if user has a weight-related goal): "-3.2 lbs to goal" or "+1.1 lbs above goal"
- Body fat % if available (secondary metric, smaller)
- Last logged timestamp
- Tap: opens Body detail view

### Body Detail View

**Current state block:**
- Weight with unit (auto-detected from locale, user-overridable)
- Body fat % if available
- BMI calculation with category (same WHO categories as onboarding)
- Body measurements if user is logging them (chest, waist, hips, arms, thighs)

**Weight trend chart:**
- Core users: 90 days of weight data; Pro users: long-term history with recent data hot and older data archived (per Section 13 cut-line)
- Line chart with smoothed trend overlay
- Scale toggle: 7D / 30D / 90D (Pro: also All-time / custom range)
- Toggle for: raw readings vs 7-day moving average
- **Goal weight reference line** — horizontal dashed line at target weight if user has a weight goal
- **Projection line (v1.18, preserved from Flask)** — dashed trajectory from the most recent logged weight toward the target weight, using the user's current daily deficit to compute pace:
  ```
  lbs_to_lose = current_weight - target_weight
  daily_deficit = TDEE - current_avg_daily_calories_consumed
  projected_weeks = ceil(lbs_to_lose / (daily_deficit × 7 / 3500))
  projected_date = today + (projected_weeks × 7 days)
  ```
  The projection renders as a dashed line from today's weight point to the (projected_date, target_weight) point on the chart. Visual treatment is subtle — the projection is clearly distinct from the actual trend line (dashed vs solid, muted color) so users understand it's an estimate, not measured data. If the deficit is zero or negative (user is maintaining or gaining), the projection line is not drawn for cuts; similarly for inverted cases.
- **Projection is opt-out** via a toggle on the chart ("Show projection" — on by default for users with an active weight goal, hidden by default for users without). The toggle state persists per-user.
- Tapping any data point on the chart opens Day Detail (§4.2a) for that date.

**Log weight flow:**
- Accessible from FAB "Log Weight" action or from the Body detail view "+" button
- Single decimal field pre-filled with user's last logged weight
- +/- buttons for quick 0.2 lb adjustments
- Optional body fat % field (hidden by default, appears if user has previously logged body fat)
- Save: one tap, logs to server-side weight history table

**Weight prompt behavior (per decision #6):**
- If user selected a weight-related goal during onboarding (Lose Weight, Build Muscle, Stay Where I Am with weight target), a gentle in-dashboard prompt appears after 7 days of no weight log: "Log your weight — last recorded 8 days ago"
- If user selected a non-weight goal or declined a weight target, no prompts appear
- If smart scale connection is connected, no prompts ever — weight auto-syncs
- Users can toggle weight prompts in Settings (on by default only for weight-goal users)

### Data Persistence (Existing + Migration)

Weight data in the Flask PWA currently lives in the `daily_activity` table with only the `weight_lbs` column active. For React Native migration, this becomes a proper `body_weight_logs` table with columns: user_id, log_date, weight_lbs, weight_kg (for metric users), body_fat_percent, source (manual / smart_scale / healthkit), created_at.

Body measurements (chest, waist, hips, arms, thighs) are new in v1 — table `body_measurements` with user_id, log_date, measurement_type, value, unit, source.

### Connection Dependencies

**Smart scale support via native health platforms:**
- Apple HealthKit → reads Weight and Body Mass Index data types from any connected scale (Withings, Renpho, Eufy, etc.)
- Google Fit / Health Connect → reads equivalent weight data from connected scales

Specific smart scale vendor connection details are deferred to Section 8 (Connections).

### Empty State

If user has no weight goal and no connected scale:
> **Body**
> Log your weight to track trends, or connect a smart scale for automatic sync.
> [Log Weight] [Connect Scale]

If user has a weight goal but no data yet (day 1):
> **Body**
> Your goal weight: 165 lbs. Log your current weight to start tracking.
> [Log Weight]

Empty state is low-pressure and explains what the subsystem does, not "missing data!" alarm.

### AI Usage

None in the Body subsystem. All calculations are deterministic (BMI, trend line, delta to goal).

## 4.3.5 Strength Subsystem

The Strength subsystem tracks resistance training: workouts, exercises, sets, reps, weight, and progression. This is the most data-rich fitness subsystem and where most active users spend the most time logging.

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Strength"
- This week's volume (total weight lifted, or sets completed, depending on user preference)
- Workouts completed this week (N of M if plan is active; standalone count otherwise)
- Last workout summary: type + duration + key highlight (e.g., "Push Day — 47 min — Bench PR 195×5")
- Tap: opens Strength detail view

### Strength Detail View

**Tabs within view:**
1. **This Week** — current week's workouts and volume
2. **Exercises** — per-exercise history and progression
3. **History** — full chronological workout log

**This Week tab contents:**
- Week summary card: total workouts, total sets, total volume (lbs lifted), muscle groups trained
- Scheduled workouts from Plan subsystem if active, color-coded by completion status
- Ad-hoc workouts logged this week
- Tap any workout: opens workout detail view

**Exercises tab contents:**
- Searchable, scrollable list of exercises the user has logged
- Per-exercise card shows: exercise name, last performed, current 1RM estimate (Epley formula), 30-day trend mini-chart
- Tap exercise: opens exercise detail with full history of every set ever performed, PRs marked, progression chart

**History tab contents:**
- Full chronological list of strength workouts
- Scrollable calendar view (alternative to list)
- Pro-gated: long-term history with recent data kept hot and older data archived in cheaper storage; Core limited per Section 13

### Workout Logging — Three Paths

Per decision #3, three paths are supported:

**Path A: Template-Driven (when user has a plan and today is a scheduled day)**

- User opens FAB → Log Workout
- Arrives at today's scheduled workout pre-populated with the plan's exercises, target sets/reps, target weights (based on last performed)
- User taps into each exercise, enters actual weights used, checks off sets as completed
- Rest timer auto-starts after each set (configurable duration, default 90 seconds for strength)
- On save: workout logged, PRs detected automatically (see below), plan adherence incremented

This path matches the current Flask PWA's strength workout checklist overlay pattern. Migrated to React Native with equivalent UX.

**Path B: Repeat Last Workout**

- User opens FAB → Log Workout
- Sees "Repeat last workout" button at top if a previous workout exists
- Tap clones the last workout's exercise list with empty weight/rep fields (or pre-filled with last performed values — user preference in Settings)
- User edits and saves
- Fast for users who do the same routine repeatedly

**Path C: Freestyle Workout**

- User opens FAB → Log Freestyle Workout
- Arrives at empty workout canvas
- Three entry methods:
  1. **AI parse (primary method per decision #10)** — large text input "Describe your workout," user types or dictates "3x8 bench press at 185, 4x10 incline dumbbell press at 65s, superset tricep pushdowns and bicep curls 3 rounds of 12"
  2. **Add exercise manually** — exercise picker with search, then tap + on each set
  3. **Start from template** — choose a saved workout
- Save: exercise list locked in, user can execute or mark as done immediately

### AI Workout Parsing (New In V1)

Mirrors the approach proven successful for cardio burn estimation in the current Flask PWA, extended to parse structured strength data.

- **Model:** Claude Haiku
- **Input:** Free-text workout description (typed or transcribed from voice)
- **Output:** Structured JSON with exercises, sets, reps, weights, notes
- **Target latency:** <4 seconds
- **Cost per call:** approximately $0.002
- **Handling:** Parsed result shown for user review before saving; user can edit any field
- **Counts against paywall counter:** Yes (this is an AI interaction per Section 4.1.9)

**Example flow:**
- User types: "I did 3 sets of 10 bench press at 185, then 4 sets of 8 at 135 for overhead press"
- AI returns structured output with two exercises, set counts, rep counts, and weights
- User sees the parsed workout, corrects any misread values, taps Save

**Error handling:**
- If the AI cannot parse any exercises from the input, fall back to manual entry with the raw text preserved in a notes field
- If the AI parses partial data (e.g., misses weight for one exercise), fields are left blank for user completion
- User always has full edit control before save

### PR Detection (New In V1, per decision #5)

Automatic PR detection runs after every strength workout save. PRs are detected deterministically (no AI) by comparing the current workout's sets against the user's exercise history.

**PR types detected:**
- **Weight PR for rep range** — heaviest weight ever lifted for X reps (where X is 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20+)
- **Volume PR for exercise** — most total volume (weight × reps × sets) in a single session for that exercise
- **Estimated 1RM PR** — new high on the Epley 1RM estimation (weight × (1 + reps/30))

**Celebration flow:**
- On PR detection, brief 2-second confetti animation fires on the post-save confirmation screen
- A "New PR" badge appears next to the PR'd set in the workout summary
- PR is persisted in a `personal_records` table with timestamp, exercise, type, value, superseded PR reference
- Historical PRs are viewable in the Exercises tab under each exercise

**Deliberately excluded:**
- No push notification on PR (would feel like pressure rather than celebration)
- No social sharing prompt (goal completion cards are specced in Section 4.10; mid-session PRs are private)

### Exercise Library

The exercise library is the data dictionary of all strength exercises supported by Life Dashboard. It covers:

- Primary lift name (e.g., "Barbell Bench Press")
- Muscle groups targeted (primary, secondary)
- Equipment required
- Common variations/aliases (for AI parsing to recognize "bench," "bench press," "BP")
- Default rep range and common set counts
- Demonstration media (photo or short loop animation) — v1.1 addition, not v1.0

The exercise library is pre-populated with approximately 150 exercises covering standard strength training. Custom exercises can be added by the user via the exercise picker's "Add custom exercise" option.

**Fuzzy matching in AI parsing:** When AI parses a workout description, exercise names are matched against the library with lenient string similarity (Levenshtein distance + common abbreviations dictionary). "bench" → "Barbell Bench Press"; "db press" → "Dumbbell Bench Press"; "ohp" → "Overhead Press."

### Data Persistence (New + Migration)

Current Flask PWA stores strength workout structure in localStorage only (`workoutPlan`, `weeklyPlan`, `LS_TIMER_KEY`). This is a known migration blocker — for React Native cross-device sync, strength workouts must live server-side.

**New tables required:**

- `strength_workouts` — workout-level: user_id, log_date, start_time, end_time, template_id (nullable), plan_day_id (nullable), notes, total_volume
- `strength_sets` — set-level: workout_id, exercise_id, set_number, weight, weight_unit, reps, rpe (optional), rest_seconds, is_warmup, is_pr
- `exercise_library` — master exercise list: id, name, muscle_groups, equipment, aliases, is_custom, created_by_user_id
- `personal_records` — user_id, exercise_id, pr_type, value, reps_or_set_count, achieved_at, superseded_by_id

**Migration path:**
- Existing users' localStorage workout plans are pulled from device on first React Native app open
- Parsed into the new schema and uploaded to server
- User receives a one-time notification: "Your workout history is now synced to your account"

### Rest Timer

The current Flask PWA has a rest timer during strength workouts. Preserved in React Native with improvements:

- Auto-starts when user checks off a completed set
- Configurable duration per exercise type (user preferences in Settings): compound lifts default 2 minutes, isolation default 60 seconds
- Visual countdown + optional audio cue at 10 seconds remaining
- Notification-ready: timer continues running if user backgrounds the app, with push notification when timer completes
- User can skip or extend timer with single tap

### Empty State

Never empty in the sense that the subsystem always shows the card — but if user has logged zero strength workouts:

> **Strength**
> Log your first workout to start tracking sets, reps, weights, and PRs.
> [Log Workout]

### AI Usage In Strength

- **Workout parsing (new):** Claude Haiku, ~$0.002/call, <4s latency, counted against paywall
- **Workout label shortening:** Existing from Flask (claude_nutrition.py shorten_label), Haiku, ~$0.0002/call — shortens workout descriptions for list display

### Pro-Gated Features

- Long-term strength workout history (recent hot, older archived in cheaper storage; Core cut-line deferred to Section 13)
- Advanced progression analytics (1RM projection over time, volume-per-muscle trend) — Pro only
- Export strength data to CSV — Pro only

### 4.3.5a Live Strength Session — The Workout Checklist Overlay
