The Workout Checklist Overlay is the live in-session tracking screen used during a strength workout. It is the most used surface in the Fitness category for active trainers — the screen that's open on their phone between sets — and it must feel polished, fast, and reliable. This pattern is preserved verbatim from the Flask PWA per v1.18 decision (Q13 — this is a core feature that works well in Flask and must stay).

#### 4.3.5a.1 Entry Points

- From Path A logging: after a user starts a scheduled plan-day workout, the overlay opens automatically with the plan's exercises loaded
- From Path B logging: after a user taps "Repeat last workout," the overlay opens with cloned exercises
- From Path C freestyle logging: after a user saves their freestyle exercise list, the overlay opens
- From an in-progress workout banner (§4.3.5a.7): tapping the minimized banner re-opens the overlay

#### 4.3.5a.2 Layout

Full-screen modal, not a sheet. Occupies the entire screen height. Layout from top to bottom:

**Header strip (fixed)**
- **Workout title** — large ("Push Day — Tuesday") or user-edited custom title
- **Elapsed session timer** — live counter since session start ("27:14"), prominent
- **Minimize button** — dismisses overlay but keeps session active (see §4.3.5a.7)
- **End Workout button** — completes the session

**Exercise blocks (vertically stacked, scrollable)**

Each exercise block contains:
- Exercise name (large) with muscle-group icon
- Target sets × reps × weight if a plan/template drove this exercise (e.g., "3 × 8 @ 185 lb")
- A set-grid table with one row per set:
  - Set number
  - Target weight (editable inline)
  - Target reps (editable inline)
  - Actual weight (numeric input, defaults to target or last performed)
  - Actual reps (numeric input, defaults to target)
  - Checkbox (unchecked by default; checking marks the set done and logs its values)
- "+ Add set" button below the set grid (for adding sets beyond the target)
- Exercise notes field (collapsible, free-text for the user)

Completed sets (checkbox checked) are visually dimmed so the active set is always clear. The next unchecked set is highlighted (subtle accent border) — that's the set the user is currently doing.

**Rest timer bar (fixed at bottom of overlay, appears after a set is checked)**

- Countdown from the user's rest duration preference (default 90 seconds for strength, configurable in Settings)
- Progress bar animates from full to empty
- "Skip rest" button to dismiss immediately
- "+30s" button to extend rest if needed
- When rest timer reaches zero:
  - Haptic pulse (iOS/Android)
  - Sound (if user has sound enabled in Settings)
  - OS-level notification fires if the app is backgrounded (see §4.3.5a.5)

**Primary action button (fixed bottom, distinct from rest timer)**

- While workout in progress: "End Workout" — large, accent color, full-width
- On tap: workout completes, AI burn estimate runs (§4.3.5a.4), workout logs to history

#### 4.3.5a.3 Per-Set Tracking Behavior

When the user checks a set's checkbox:
1. The actual weight and actual reps values lock in (editable for 5 seconds as a correction window)
2. The set row visually dims
3. The next unchecked set highlights
4. The rest timer starts counting down at the bottom
5. PR detection runs against the strength workout history; if this set is a PR (§4.3.5a.6), a small celebration appears

If the user un-checks a set (mistake), the dim reverses and the rest timer cancels.

The user can add more sets beyond the planned count at any time via "+ Add set." Volume tracking counts all completed sets, whether planned or added.

#### 4.3.5a.4 End Workout Flow

When the user taps "End Workout":

1. Confirmation prompt: "End workout? Your progress will be saved." (one-tap dismissible — don't make users confirm harder than necessary)
2. On confirm, the workout is saved to `strength_workouts` with all sets
3. An AI burn estimate call runs:
   - **Model:** Claude Haiku
   - **Input:** list of exercises + sets × reps × weight + total session duration + user's profile (weight, sex, age)
   - **Output:** estimated calories burned + short label for display
   - **Cost:** ~$0.002
   - **Latency:** <4 seconds; if it times out, a deterministic fallback estimate is used (volume-based heuristic) and the workout logs with "estimated" tag
4. A success screen appears briefly showing: session duration, total volume, new PRs (if any), calories burned estimate
5. Return to Fitness detail view

All workout data is persisted server-side; the overlay state (in-progress or completed) is mirrored to the client's local DB for cross-device visibility.

#### 4.3.5a.5 Persistence Across Backgrounding

Users backgrounding the app mid-workout (to check a message, look up an exercise form video, etc.) must not lose state. Persistence strategy:

- **Session state is mirrored to AsyncStorage on every set check or edit.** Keys: `active_workout_session` (the full session object — exercises, sets, timestamps), `active_workout_started_at` (epoch ms).
- **On app foreground, if `active_workout_session` is present and `active_workout_started_at` is less than 6 hours old, the overlay re-opens to the last state.** The elapsed timer continues from the actual start time (not the backgrounding time) — elapsed keeps counting during backgrounding.
- **If the session is older than 6 hours:** on foreground, user sees a prompt "You have an unfinished workout from earlier. Continue or end now?" — choosing "End now" prompts for end-time and saves the workout; choosing "Continue" re-opens the overlay.
- **If the app is force-quit:** state survives because AsyncStorage persists across app launches. Timer state is reconstructed from `started_at`.
- **If the device reboots:** state also survives.

Server-side mirroring of the active session is NOT done during the workout — this is a client-side workflow. The server only receives the completed workout on End.

#### 4.3.5a.6 PR Detection & Celebration

When a set is checked and its weight × reps combination beats the user's previous best for that exercise (at that rep range), a PR is detected deterministically:

- Per-exercise 1RM estimated via Epley formula: `estimated_1rm = weight × (1 + reps / 30)`
- If estimated_1rm exceeds the user's previous best for this exercise, the set is a PR
- Also: absolute weight PRs (heaviest single rep), rep-PR at a given weight (most reps at that weight)

PR celebration is subtle — a small banner appears above the set row with a trophy icon and a two-word label ("NEW PR!"). No full-screen interrupt, no modal, no push notification. Per the "calm, never urgent" principle (§2.6), PR celebration should feel earned, not loud.

PRs are stored in `personal_records` with full history; §4.3.5 Exercises tab shows PR history per exercise.

#### 4.3.5a.7 Minimize-to-Banner Behavior

A user may need to leave the overlay mid-workout to look something up in another part of the app (check their plan for the rest of the week, look at their sleep score, check a meal log). The minimize pattern preserves session continuity:

- Tapping the **Minimize** button collapses the overlay to a persistent banner
- The banner appears at the top of whatever screen the user navigates to (Home, Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time, Settings)
- Banner contents: "Workout in progress · 27:14" with a "Resume" chip on the right
- Banner is a persistent strip, ~40pt tall, distinct from notification banners (doesn't auto-dismiss)
- Tapping the banner re-opens the Workout Checklist Overlay to its current state
- The rest timer continues counting down while minimized; OS notifications fire as normal on rest-timer completion

The banner disappears when the user either re-opens the overlay and ends the workout, or when the session is force-ended via the long-idle prompt (below).

#### 4.3.5a.8 Long Idle Prompt

If the user has not logged a set in 15 minutes AND the app is foregrounded:
- A gentle prompt appears: "Still working out? No set has been logged in 15 minutes."
- Options: "Yes, still going" (dismisses prompt, restarts the 15-min timer), "End workout" (triggers End Workout flow), "Cancel workout" (discards session with confirm)

The long idle prompt does not fire when the app is backgrounded; the session simply continues.

#### 4.3.5a.9 Accessibility

- Full VoiceOver and TalkBack support for all interactive elements
- Checkbox accessible via voice control ("check set 3")
- Rest timer announces completion audibly (screen reader + sound)
- Set tables navigable via keyboard (physical keyboard on iPad, web v2+)
- Dynamic type respected up to large accessibility sizes (set tables reflow vertically on large text)

#### 4.3.5a.10 Data Persistence (Schema)

Workout session data lives in:

- `strength_workouts` — user_id, workout_id, session_start, session_end, session_duration_sec, total_volume_kg, calories_burned_estimated, plan_id (nullable), template_id (nullable), title, notes
- `strength_sets` — set_id, workout_id, exercise_id, set_number, target_weight, target_reps, actual_weight, actual_reps, is_completed, completed_at, is_pr (boolean), estimated_1rm
- `personal_records` — user_id, exercise_id, record_type (absolute_1rm / weight_at_reps / reps_at_weight), weight, reps, achieved_at, superseded_at (nullable)

Client-side AsyncStorage keys (active session state):
- `active_workout_session` — full session object (serialized JSON)
- `active_workout_started_at` — epoch milliseconds
- `active_workout_rest_timer_ends_at` — epoch milliseconds (for rest timer continuation across backgrounding)

---

## 4.3.6 Cardio Subsystem

The Cardio subsystem covers any aerobic or conditioning work: runs, rides, rows, swims, HIIT, classes. Per decision #4, the design splits cleanly between two experiences:

- **Manual logging** — simple, fast, available to everyone, captures minimum viable data
- **Connection-rich display** — beautiful cards with routes, pace charts, HR zones, elevation profiles, available when connections are connected

The connection-rich experience is intentionally ambitious: Life Dashboard is building a Strava-equivalent cardio experience inside the app for users who connect their data sources.

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Cardio"
- This week's total (distance + duration, e.g., "18.4 mi / 2h 42m")
- Zone 2 time this week (if HR data available)
- Last session summary (activity type + distance + time + pace)
- Tap: opens Cardio detail view

### Cardio Detail View

**Layout:**
- This week summary (total distance, total duration, session count, average pace)
- Activity breakdown: bar chart showing this week's sessions by activity type
- Weekly progression chart: last 12 weeks of cardio volume
- Zone distribution (if HR data): stacked bar showing time in each zone
- Recent sessions list: each session with date, activity, distance, duration, pace

### Session Detail View (Connection-Rich)

For a session with full connection data (e.g., a Garmin run), the session detail view is the "Strava-equivalent experience":

**Card contents:**
- Activity type + date + total time
- Distance + pace + elevation gain
- Route map (if GPS trace available) — interactive, zoomable
- Pace curve chart — pace over distance/time
- Elevation profile chart — elevation over distance
- Heart rate chart — HR over time with zone color coding
- Zone time breakdown — time in Z1/Z2/Z3/Z4/Z5
- Splits table — pace per mile or kilometer
- Calories burned (estimated)
- Weather at session time (if connection provides)
- User's subjective notes (optional text field, no character limit)

**Map rendering:**
- Uses a mobile-native map library (MapKit on iOS, Google Maps on Android)
- GPS trace rendered as colored polyline (can be color-coded by pace or elevation)
- Pins for start, finish, and any manually set landmarks
- Fullscreen map option with pinch-to-zoom, pan, and gesture controls

**Performance:**
- Rich session detail view must render in under 500ms (per Design Principle 3.6)
- Map tiles cached aggressively
- Large activities (ultra-distance, multi-hour) lazy-load chart data

### Session Detail View (Manual Logging)

For a session logged manually with the simple flow, the detail view shows what the user provided plus any derived calculations:

- Activity type + date + duration + distance
- Estimated pace (calculated from duration/distance)
- Estimated calories burned (AI-estimated from current burn estimation or deterministic lookup)
- User's notes field

No map, no pace curve, no HR data — these only exist with connections.

### Manual Logging Flow

- User opens FAB → Log Workout → selects "Cardio"
- (Or: user opens FAB → Log Workout → AI parses their description and detects it's cardio)
- Fields on the cardio log screen:
  - Activity type (picker with common options: Run, Walk, Bike, Row, Swim, HIIT, Elliptical, Other)
  - Duration (minutes, required)
  - Distance (optional, with unit)
  - Intensity (optional: Easy / Moderate / Hard / Intervals)
  - Notes (optional)
- Save: logs the session, calls burn estimation AI, stores result

Target log time: under 15 seconds.

### AI Cardio Parsing

Same AI flow as strength workout parsing (decision #10), handling cardio-specific inputs:

- "30 minute run at 8:30 pace"
- "45 min bike ride, moderate intensity"
- "5K in 28:15"

AI returns structured data for activity type, duration, distance (if derivable), pace, intensity.

### AI Burn Estimation (Existing)

The current Flask PWA has `estimate_burn()` in claude_nutrition.py (Haiku, ~$0.0008/call). Preserved in v1 — every cardio session gets a burn estimate that contributes to the day's total calories burned on the Home Dashboard's Nutrition card.

### Connection-Rich Display Requirements

For v1 to deliver the "Strava equivalent" experience, the app must consume and display:

- **GPS traces** — latitude/longitude sequences for route rendering
- **HR data** — heart rate samples with timestamps
- **Pace/speed data** — pace samples over time
- **Elevation data** — altitude samples over time
- **Split data** — per-mile or per-kilometer metrics

Specific connection sources for this data (Apple Health, Garmin via Health Connect, Strava direct, etc.) are specified in Section 8. Strava is supported at v1 as a read-only connection with AI context exclusion — Strava activities appear in cardio cards, session detail views, and deterministic scoring, but are filtered out of AI context per the November 2024 API Agreement restriction on AI use. Activities from other sources (Apple Health, Garmin via Health Connect) remain available to AI context normally.

### Empty State

- If user has no cardio sessions and no connections:
  > **Cardio**
  > Log a run, ride, or any aerobic activity, or connect Apple Health / Garmin for automatic tracking.
  > [Log Cardio] [Connect Wearable]

- If user has connections connected but no sessions yet:
  > **Cardio**
  > No cardio sessions yet. Your next ride or run will appear here automatically.

### Pro-Gated Features

- Long-term cardio history (recent hot, older archived)
- Detailed pace/HR analytics
- Year-over-year comparison
- Export cardio data

(Cut-line deferred to Section 13.)

## 4.3.7 Movement Subsystem

The Movement subsystem covers everyday activity outside of structured workouts: steps, active minutes, calories burned from NEAT (non-exercise activity thermogenesis), standing hours.

This is the lowest-engagement subsystem per user — most users glance at it, nod, and move on. But it's important for total energy expenditure calculations and for users whose primary activity goal is "move more."

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Movement"
- Steps today (large, primary metric)
- Active minutes today (secondary)
- Calories burned today from movement (tertiary)
- 7-day steps trend sparkline
- Tap: opens Movement detail view

### Movement Detail View

**Layout:**
- Today's metrics block: steps, active minutes, calories from NEAT, floors climbed (if available), standing hours (if available)
- Daily progress ring toward step goal (default 10,000 steps, customizable via Settings or via step-goal selection from Goals library in Section 4.10)
- 30-day steps chart with day-of-week pattern visualization
- Hourly breakdown for today (bar chart of steps per hour, if connection provides)
- Weekly summary table: 7 rows showing steps/active minutes/goal met for each day

### Data Sources

- **Primary: Apple HealthKit / Google Fit / Samsung Health** — native platform data, aggregated from phone and connected wearables
- **Secondary: Garmin, Fitbit, Whoop (via platform sync to Apple Health or direct)** — alternative sources for users with specific wearables
- **Fallback: Manual steps entry** — existing in Flask PWA (localStorage `stepsToday`, `stepsLog`). Migrated to proper DB table

### Manual Entry Flow

- Users without connections can tap "Log steps" on the Movement subsystem card
- Single number field, pre-filled with yesterday's value as hint
- Save: logs to server-side `daily_movement` table

Not promoted prominently — the friction of manual daily steps entry is high and the signal is low-quality. Passive tracking is the expected path.

### Connection-Derived Data

When HealthKit/Google Fit is connected:

- Steps auto-sync throughout the day (delayed by ~15 minutes due to platform caching)
- Active minutes derived from the platform's own calculation (Apple's Exercise minutes, Google's Heart Points, etc.)
- Calories burned from NEAT pulled directly (Apple Health distinguishes "Active Energy" from "Basal Energy")
- Hourly step breakdown available for today

### Empty State

- No data, no connection:
  > **Movement**
  > Your phone can auto-track your steps. Connect Apple Health / Google Fit.
  > [Connect]

- Connection connected, no data today (e.g., first app open of the day, phone was off, etc.):
  > **Movement**
  > Waiting for today's data to sync.
  > [Refresh]

### Data Persistence

New table in v1: `daily_movement` — user_id, log_date, steps, active_minutes, calories_active, floors_climbed, standing_hours, source (healthkit / google_fit / manual / garmin / etc.).

Existing Flask localStorage-only approach is deprecated on migration.

### AI Usage

None. Movement data is displayed and aggregated deterministically.

## 4.3.8 Sleep Subsystem

The Sleep subsystem tracks sleep duration, sleep stages (when available), sleep efficiency, and consistency. Per decision #7, sleep requires an connection — manual sleep logging is not supported in v1.

### Rationale For Connection-Only

Manual sleep logging is low-quality (users misremember bedtime/wake time, don't distinguish time in bed vs asleep, can't accurately report sleep stages). The data that makes Sleep actually useful — efficiency, stage breakdown, HRV correlation — comes exclusively from wearables and smart beds. A lower-quality manual log would pollute the data and degrade the feature for users who later connect a wearable.

This is a trade-off: users without wearables see an empty Sleep subsystem. Per decision #8's logic applied here, that empty state is acceptable because the connection prompt clearly explains what Sleep would track.

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Sleep"
- Last night's duration (primary metric, e.g., "7h 32m")
- Sleep score if available (secondary, 0–100 — comes from connection, not computed by Life Dashboard)
- Last night's efficiency (e.g., "88% time in bed asleep")
- 7-day average sleep duration mini-chart
- Tap: opens Sleep detail view

### Sleep Detail View

**Layout:**
- Last night summary card
  - Total sleep time
  - Time in bed (if distinguishable)
  - Efficiency percentage
  - Sleep stages breakdown (Deep / Light / REM / Awake) with timeline bar
  - Resting HR during sleep (if wearable provides)
  - Sleep score (if source provides)
- 7-day sleep chart: bar per night showing duration + quality
- Sleep consistency chart: bedtime and wake-time variation over 30 days (shows how consistent user's sleep schedule is)
- Weekly average and trend indicator

### Data Sources

- **Apple HealthKit** — Sleep Analysis category, available from Apple Watch + iPhone (automatic sleep detection) + third-party apps
- **Garmin Connect** — detailed sleep with stages from Garmin watches
- **Oura** — via Apple Health or direct connection
- **Whoop** — via Apple Health or direct connection
- **Fitbit / Google Fit** — via Health Connect on Android

Specific approved connections at launch deferred to Section 8.

### Empty State

> **Sleep**
> Sleep tracking requires a wearable. Connect Apple Health (Apple Watch), Garmin, Oura, or Whoop to see sleep duration, quality, and trends.
> [Connect]

### Data Persistence

New table in v1: `sleep_logs` — user_id, sleep_date (the "for" date, typically morning after), bedtime, wake_time, total_sleep_minutes, deep_minutes, light_minutes, rem_minutes, awake_minutes, efficiency_percent, sleep_score, source.

This replaces the dormant `sleep_logs` table that was dropped during Flask hardening.

### AI Usage

None in v1. Sleep data is displayed raw. Cross-domain insights about sleep affecting training (e.g., "your strength sessions after poor sleep nights are lower volume") are deferred to v2+ chatbot features — not scoring math, not dashboard content.

### Pro-Gated Features

- Extended sleep history (cut-line deferred to Section 13)
- Sleep consistency scoring and trend analysis

## 4.3.9 Recovery Subsystem

The Recovery subsystem surfaces physiological readiness metrics: HRV (heart rate variability), resting heart rate, readiness score (vendor-specific). Per decision #8, the subsystem is always shown with a "Connect wearable" prompt when no data source is connected.

### Why Show When Empty (Decision #8 Rationale)

Unlike Sleep, Recovery metrics are genuinely novel for many users — they don't know their device even tracks HRV, and they don't know why it matters. A visible (empty) Recovery subsystem with a clear explanation serves as product education: "Life Dashboard tracks this when you connect a wearable, and here's why it matters."

The friction trade-off: users without wearables see a subsystem they can't populate. This is acceptable because:
- The prompt is low-pressure ("connect when ready," not "missing data")
- Users with wearables get immediate value
- Product surface educates users about features they might adopt later

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Recovery"
- Today's HRV (if available) — primary metric
- Resting HR (if available) — secondary
- Readiness score if wearable provides (0–100, e.g., Oura Readiness, Whoop Recovery)
- 7-day HRV trend sparkline
- Tap: opens Recovery detail view

### Recovery Detail View

**Layout:**
- Today's metrics card
- HRV trend chart (30 days): line chart with 7-day moving average overlay
- Resting HR trend (30 days)
- Readiness score history (if applicable)
- Correlation callouts (v2+, not v1): e.g., "Your HRV tends to drop 24h after high-volume training sessions"

### Data Sources

- **Apple HealthKit** — HRV (SDNN), resting HR, from Apple Watch primarily
- **Garmin Connect** — Body Battery, HRV, resting HR
- **Oura** — Readiness score, HRV, resting HR (via Apple Health or direct)
- **Whoop** — Recovery score, HRV, resting HR (via Apple Health or direct)

### Empty State

> **Recovery**
> Heart rate variability (HRV), resting heart rate, and readiness scores tell you how recovered your body is. Requires a wearable that tracks HRV overnight.
> [Learn about recovery] [Connect wearable]

The "Learn about recovery" link opens a brief help article explaining what HRV is, why it's useful, and what wearables track it. This doubles as marketing for wearable adoption.

### Data Persistence

New table in v1: `recovery_logs` — user_id, log_date, hrv_sdnn, hrv_rmssd, resting_hr, readiness_score, readiness_source, source.

### AI Usage

None in v1. Recovery metrics are passively tracked. Interpretation (what HRV trend means for today's training) is deferred to v2+ chatbot features.

### Pro-Gated Features

- Extended recovery history
- Training-load-vs-recovery correlation (v2+)

## 4.3.10 Training Plan Subsystem

The Training Plan subsystem surfaces the structured weekly workout plan generated by the onboarding Workout Builder (Section 4.1.6). This subsystem is first in the Fitness detail view order because it's the most actionable — "what should I do right now?"

### Subsystem Card On Fitness Detail View

- Title: "Plan"
- Today's workout (large, primary): "Push Day — 6 exercises"
- Adherence rate (secondary): "This week: 3 of 4 completed"
- Next upcoming session: "Tomorrow: Pull Day"
- Tap: opens Plan detail view OR opens today's workout directly (user preference)

**If no plan exists:**
> **Plan**
> You don't have a training plan yet. Build one to see your weekly schedule here.
> [Build a Plan]

### Plan Detail View

**Layout:**
- Today's workout card (prominent): exercises, target sets/reps/weights
  - Start Workout button → begins template-driven logging (Path A in 4.3.5)
  - Mark as Completed Without Logging button → records adherence without detailed data
- This week calendar strip: 7 day indicators showing which days have scheduled workouts + completion status
- Adherence stats: weekly %, monthly %, all-time %
- Upcoming week preview: next 7 days of scheduled workouts
- Plan management: "Edit Plan" opens the Workout Builder with pre-populated answers; "Switch Plan" offers option to deactivate current plan and build new

### Plan Adherence Tracking

Adherence is tracked automatically as users log workouts. A logged strength workout with plan_day_id matching today's scheduled day counts as "completed." Manually marked "Completed Without Logging" also counts.

Adherence rate feeds into the Fitness score (composition details in Section 9) and is one of the primary signals of whether the user is following through on their training intent.

### Plan Flexibility

Plans are not rigid. The system accommodates:

- **Skipping a day** — no penalty; adherence rate reflects it, but no guilt messaging
- **Training on an unscheduled day** — logged as an ad-hoc workout, counted toward weekly volume but not plan adherence
- **Training on a different day than scheduled** — if user skips Monday's Push Day and does it Tuesday, the app offers to shift the plan (user choice)
- **Plan modifications** — user can edit the plan at any time through Plan Detail View

### Plan Sources (Existing + New)

**Existing (Flask PWA):** Plans are generated by `generate_comprehensive_plan()` and stored in localStorage as `weeklyPlan` JSON. Migration to React Native requires server-side plan storage for cross-device sync.

**New table in v1:** `training_plans` — user_id, plan_name, plan_json (structured schedule), start_date, end_date (nullable), is_active, created_at, updated_at.

**New table in v1:** `plan_adherence` — user_id, plan_id, scheduled_date, scheduled_day_type (push/pull/legs/etc.), completed, workout_id (nullable if marked complete without logging), marked_at.

### AI Usage In Plan Subsystem

- **Plan generation (existing):** `generate_comprehensive_plan()` — Claude Haiku, ~$0.014/call. Onboarding and Plan Edit flows both use this.
- **Plan revision (existing):** `revise_plan()` — Claude Haiku, ~$0.014/call. User types "I want to swap deadlifts for trap bar" and AI updates the plan.

Per Section 4.1.12, onboarding AI calls (including initial plan generation) are free. Plan revision after onboarding is a regular AI interaction and counts against the paywall counter.

### Pro-Gated Features

- Multiple active plans (e.g., strength plan + cardio plan simultaneously) — Pro only
- AI plan revision via natural language — Pro only (Core users can rebuild from scratch but not revise conversationally)
- Plan templates from "coach library" — Pro only (v2+ feature, just flagged here)

## 4.3.11 Fitness Score (Framework Only)

The Fitness category produces a single 0–100 score that appears on the Home Dashboard Fitness card and at the top of the Fitness detail view. The exact math for computing this score is specified in Section 9 (Scoring Engine). This subsection establishes the framework only.

### Framework Principles

- **Deterministic only** — no AI involved in scoring
- **Fitness-domain data only** — the Fitness score uses only Fitness subsystem data; no cross-domain inputs (sleep does not affect Time score, etc.)
- **Graceful degradation** — missing subsystems do not drag the score down; the score weights redistribute across available subsystems
- **Personalization after baselines form** — for the first 14 days, scores use population defaults; after 14 days, scores use the user's personal 30-day rolling baselines
- **Weighted composition across subsystems** — specific weights for each subsystem deferred to Section 9

### What Contributes (Directionally)

Each subsystem contributes signals to the Fitness score. Exact weighting is in Section 9, but the conceptual contributions:

- **Body** — weight trend vs goal, consistency of logging
- **Strength** — workout frequency, plan adherence (if plan exists), volume trend
- **Cardio** — session frequency, weekly volume vs baseline
- **Movement** — daily step achievement relative to goal or baseline
- **Sleep** — duration vs personal baseline, consistency
- **Recovery** — HRV vs personal baseline, readiness score if available
- **Plan** — adherence rate (only if user has an active plan)

### Weight Data Handling (per decision #6)

Users without weight data (no goal, no scale, no manual entries) do not have weight contribute to their Fitness score. The Body subsystem weight signal is simply absent and does not penalize the score.

### Missing Subsystem Handling

If a user has no sleep data (no wearable connected), the Sleep subsystem contributes nothing to the score. Other subsystem weights increase proportionally. A user with only Strength and Cardio data has a meaningful Fitness score based on those two domains.

This is the "graceful degradation" principle: the score works for everyone from a manual-logger-with-no-wearables to a fully-integrated Whoop + Garmin user.

## 4.3.12 Logging Flows

This subsection consolidates the complete UX flows for logging Fitness data. Each flow is specified with its steps, fields, AI usage, and target latency.

### Flow 1: Log Strength Workout (Template-Driven)

- **Trigger:** User opens FAB → Log Workout. If a plan exists and today has a scheduled workout, arrive at template view.
- **Screen contents:**
  - Workout title at top ("Push Day")
  - Start time auto-recorded when screen opens
  - Exercise list with collapsible cards
  - Each exercise: name, target sets × reps (e.g., "3 × 8–10"), weight suggestion from last performed, set checkboxes
  - User taps a set checkbox → enters actual weight and reps → set is marked complete
  - Rest timer auto-starts on set completion
- **Save:** User taps Complete at top when all sets done (or marks workout complete early)
- **Post-save:**
  - PR detection runs; celebration if triggered
  - Plan adherence updated
  - Workout appears in today's summary
- **Target total time:** 30–90 minutes (the workout itself); logging time within that is negligible

### Flow 2: Log Strength Workout (Freestyle with AI)

- **Trigger:** FAB → Log Freestyle Workout
- **Screen contents:**
  - Large text input: "Describe your workout"
  - Voice input button
  - Alternative: "Add exercises manually" link
- **User input:** Types or dictates workout description
- **AI call:** Claude Haiku parses into structured JSON (~3s, ~$0.002)
- **Review screen:** Parsed exercises/sets shown for user review and edit
- **Save:** User confirms; workout saved with all structure captured
- **Post-save:** Same as Flow 1 (PR detection, summary update)
- **Target time from tap to saved workout:** under 60 seconds (including AI parsing)

### Flow 3: Log Strength Workout (Repeat Last)

- **Trigger:** FAB → Log Workout → "Repeat Last Workout" button
- **Screen contents:** Exercise list cloned from last strength workout; weights/reps pre-filled from last performance (or blank, per user setting)
- **User action:** Edit any values that differ; complete sets
- **Save:** Same as Flow 1

### Flow 4: Log Cardio (Manual Simple)

- **Trigger:** FAB → Log Workout → detect cardio from AI parse or user selects "Cardio"
- **Screen contents:**
  - Activity type picker (Run / Walk / Bike / Row / Swim / HIIT / Elliptical / Other)
  - Duration field (required)
  - Distance field (optional)
  - Intensity (optional: Easy / Moderate / Hard / Intervals)
  - Notes (optional)
- **Save:** Logs session, runs burn estimation AI (Haiku, ~$0.0008)
- **Target time:** under 15 seconds

### Flow 5: Log Cardio (AI Parse)

- **Trigger:** FAB → Log Freestyle Workout → user types cardio description
- **AI call:** Parses description, detects cardio activity type, extracts duration/distance/pace
- **Review screen:** Parsed cardio data shown
- **Save:** Same as Flow 4

### Flow 6: Log Weight

- **Trigger:** FAB → Log Weight OR Body subsystem "+" button OR dashboard weight prompt tap
- **Screen contents:**
  - Weight field pre-filled with last logged weight
  - +/- buttons for adjustment
  - Optional body fat % (hidden unless user has logged body fat before)
- **Save:** One tap, <2 seconds total flow

### Flow 7: Log Steps (Manual, Fallback)

- **Trigger:** Movement subsystem "Log steps" button (only visible if no connection connected)
- **Screen contents:** Single number field
- **Save:** One tap
- **Not promoted:** this flow exists as a fallback; users are continuously prompted to connect HealthKit/Google Fit for passive tracking

### Flow 8: Log Sleep (Not Supported in v1)

Per decision #7, manual sleep logging is not supported. The FAB does not show a "Log Sleep" action. Sleep data comes exclusively from connections.

## 4.3.13 Connection Dependencies

This subsection maps which Fitness subsystems depend on which connection categories. Specific connection vendors, authentication flows, and approval status are specified in Section 8.

| Subsystem | Works Without Connection? | Connection Enhances | Connection Required For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plan | Yes (full function) | None | — |
| Strength | Yes (full function) | None v1 (Hevy/Strong import v2+) | — |
| Cardio | Yes (basic logging) | Rich map, pace, HR, zones | Route maps, HR zones |
| Body | Yes (manual entry) | Auto weight sync | None required |
| Movement | Yes (manual) but effectively broken | Passive tracking | Practical daily use |
| Sleep | No | All sleep data | Everything |
| Recovery | No | All recovery data | Everything |

The connection categories that power Fitness are:

- **Native health platforms:** Apple HealthKit (iOS), Google Fit / Health Connect (Android)
- **Wearable vendors:** Garmin Connect, Oura, Whoop, Fitbit
- **Strava** — read-only OAuth connection for v1. Strava activities populate cardio history, session detail views, and deterministic scoring. AI context assembly explicitly excludes Strava-sourced rows at v1 per Strava's November 2024 API Agreement restriction on AI use. Written clarification with Strava developer relations is being pursued in parallel; if Strava confirms the "feedback to users" exemption covers Life Dashboard's inference-only user-facing insights, the AI filter is lifted in v1.1. Details in Section 8.
- **Smart scale vendors** — typically integrated via native health platforms rather than direct

## 4.3.14 AI Usage In Fitness

Summary of all AI calls in the Fitness category, their costs, and their frequency assumptions.

| Call | Where Used | Model | Cost/Call | Counted Against Paywall? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Burn estimation (cardio) | Every cardio save | Haiku | $0.0008 | Yes |
| Workout parsing (strength) | Freestyle strength logging | Haiku | $0.002 | Yes |
| Workout parsing (cardio) | Freestyle cardio logging | Haiku | $0.002 | Yes |
| Label shortening | Every workout save | Haiku | $0.0002 | No (background, not user-invoked) |
| Plan generation | Workout Builder during onboarding | Haiku | $0.014 | No (onboarding free) |
| Plan generation | Plan edit after onboarding | Haiku | $0.014 | Yes |
| Plan revision | Natural-language plan edit | Haiku | $0.014 | Yes |
| Plan understanding | Brief plan explanation | Haiku | $0.0006 | No (background) |

### Cost Modeling Per User

**Daily AI cost from Fitness (estimated heavy usage):**
- 1 strength workout logged via AI parse: $0.002
- 1 cardio session logged manually (burn estimate only): $0.0008
- 2 label shortenings: $0.0004
- Total daily Fitness AI cost: ~$0.003

This is well within the broader per-user-per-day target of $0.10 across all categories.

## 4.3.15 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Specific reductions applied to the Fitness category relative to naive implementations:

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Separate logging flows for each workout type | Unified FAB → Log Workout; AI parses and routes |
| Manual exercise picker for every set | Template-driven logging pre-populates from plan |
| Re-entering weights for the same exercise every session | Previous performance pre-filled as suggestion |
| Rest timer requires manual start | Auto-starts on set completion |
| PR tracking requires manual marking | Automatic detection after every set save |
| Weight logging prompts for all users | Only shown to users with weight-related goals or smart scales |
| Sleep/recovery manual entry | Not supported; passive only |
| Cardio requires full split/HR/elevation manual entry | Manual logging is duration + distance only; rich data only from connections |
| Plan adherence tracked manually | Tracked automatically from logged workouts |
| Goal weight recalculation on weight change | Automatic |
| Unit conversion (lbs ↔ kg) | Auto-detected from device locale, user-overridable in Settings |

## 4.3.16 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast minimum (4.5:1 for body text)
- All tappable targets: minimum 44pt iOS, 48dp Android
- VoiceOver and TalkBack tested for workout logging flows end-to-end
- Rest timer announces remaining time to screen readers every 30 seconds; announces completion
- Charts have accessible text alternatives (VoiceOver reads trend direction + key values)
- PR celebration animation respects reduced motion settings (fades instead of confetti if user has motion reduced)
- Voice input available for all workout description fields
- Color state indicators paired with text labels or icons (never color alone)

## 4.3.17 Edge Cases & Error Handling

- **AI workout parse returns empty result** — fallback to manual entry with original text preserved in notes field
- **AI workout parse returns partial data** — missing fields left blank for user to complete; no auto-guessing
- **User logs a workout for a past date** — supported via date picker on log screen; does not affect today's streak but does update history
- **User logs a workout for a future date** — not supported; log screen restricts date to today or earlier
- **Plan exists but user deletes it mid-week** — completed workouts retain adherence credit; future days stop being tracked
- **Connection disconnects mid-day** — data syncs up to disconnection time are retained; subsystem shows "Reconnect" prompt
- **Smart scale data conflicts with manual entry on same day** — most recent timestamp wins; user notification explains
- **HRV or sleep data missing for a day** — subsystem shows gap in trend chart (not zero, not hidden); 7-day average excludes missing days
- **User changes weight unit (lbs → kg or vice versa)** — all stored data auto-converts; display updates immediately
- **Workout saved, then rest timer fires** — timer still functions even though workout is technically complete (user may be doing accessory work)
- **PR detection on first-ever logging of an exercise** — does NOT trigger PR celebration (would always trigger; not meaningful)
- **PR detection on equipment change (e.g., bench press → incline bench press)** — treated as separate exercises; no false PR
- **Large historical import (migrating from Strong or Hevy)** — PRs computed retroactively; no celebrations for historical PRs
- **Workout timer running when app is killed** — timer state persisted to local storage; resumes on next app open
- **Plan day skipped but user trains different session on that day** — ad-hoc workout logged; plan day remains incomplete; user prompted next-day whether to shift plan

## 4.3.18 Analytics Events

Events captured in the Fitness category:

- `fitness_detail_viewed`
- `fitness_subsystem_tapped` (subsystem: plan | strength | cardio | body | movement | sleep | recovery)
- `workout_log_started` (source: fab | plan | repeat_last | freestyle)
- `workout_log_completed` (type: strength | cardio, duration_seconds, exercise_count, via_ai_parse: bool)
- `workout_log_abandoned` (stage: which step user left at)
- `strength_set_logged` (exercise_id, set_number)
- `rest_timer_started` (duration_seconds)
- `rest_timer_completed`
- `rest_timer_skipped`
- `pr_detected` (exercise_id, pr_type, value)
- `pr_celebration_shown`
- `cardio_session_viewed` (source: manual | connection)
- `weight_logged` (source: manual | smart_scale)
- `body_measurement_logged` (measurement_type)
- `plan_workout_started` (plan_day_type)
- `plan_workout_completed_detailed`
- `plan_workout_marked_complete_without_logging`
- `plan_revised_via_ai`
- `plan_rebuilt`
- `connection_connect_tapped_from_fitness` (target_connection)
- `ai_workout_parse_succeeded` (exercise_count, latency_ms)
- `ai_workout_parse_failed` (reason: empty | partial | timeout)
- `ai_workout_parse_edited_before_save` (fields_edited_count)

All events respect user privacy preferences (Section 6). No PII is sent to analytics platforms.

---

# Section 4.4 — Nutrition Category

## 4.4.1 Overview

The Nutrition category is the Life Dashboard domain for everything a user consumes: food, drink, macronutrients, and (when enabled) hydration. It is the most mature category in the current Flask PWA and also the most feature-dense category in the v1 React Native build. The Nutrition category answers the question users ask themselves more than any other: *"What can I eat right now, and what should I eat next?"*

Users reach the Nutrition category by tapping the Nutrition card on the Home Dashboard or selecting the Nutrition tab from the bottom navigation.

### Why Nutrition Is The Richest Single Category

Fitness has seven subsystems but only two active logging flows (strength and cardio). Nutrition has one conceptual subsystem (food intake) but six distinct input modes:

- Text entry with AI macro parsing
- Voice input with AI macro parsing
- Photo scanning with AI meal identification
- Pantry scanning with AI meal suggestion
- Barcode scanning with Open Food Facts lookup (AI fallback)
- Saved meals with smart-suggest

Plus hydration tracking, macro goal management, calorie rollover logic, auto-adjusting targets, and micronutrient summaries. The category is conceptually simple (food → macros → score) but operationally the densest in the app.

### Philosophical Anchor

**Calories remaining is the hero metric of the entire category.** Every screen, every flow, every AI call, every decision in this category ultimately serves the user's answer to "what can I eat right now." If a feature doesn't make that question easier to answer, it doesn't belong in the Nutrition category for v1.

### Relationship To Other Categories

Nutrition owns meal logging, macro tracking, hydration, pantry/suggestion, and nutrition-specific scoring. It does not own:

- **Workout calorie burn** — owned by Fitness (Section 4.3), feeds into Nutrition's "calories remaining" equation through the burn minus eaten math
- **Meal timing as productivity signal** (e.g., meal spacing affecting energy) — v2+ chatbot correlation, not v1 scoring
- **Shopping list, grocery tracking** — deferred to v2+; current Flask PWA does not have this and it's out of scope for v1

### Current State vs Target State

Most Nutrition functionality already exists in the Flask PWA. Each subsection below marks components as:

- **Existing** — implemented in Flask, migrated to React Native with equivalent UX
- **Enhanced** — exists in Flask but expanded in v1
- **New in v1** — specified here for the first time, built fresh in React Native
- **Pro-gated** — premium-tier features; tier cut-line deferred to Section 13

### Success Metrics

- **Meals logged per day (active users)** — target: 2.5+ average
- **Daily protein target achievement rate** — target: 70%+ of users hitting 80% of target
- **Meal scan success rate** — percentage of photo scans that user accepts without editing macros. Target: 75%+
- **AI meal parse accuracy** — percentage of text/voice parses accepted without editing. Target: 85%+
- **Saved meal re-log rate** — percentage of logged meals that came from saved meals or smart-suggest. Target: 40%+ (indicates the suggestion engine is working)

## 4.4.2 Nutrition Detail View Layout

The Nutrition detail view uses a tab-bar layout (per v1.18 pattern): **Today / Progress / History**. The tab bar is fixed just below the header.

### Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)

- Back arrow to Home (or tab indicator)
- Title: "Nutrition"
- Date selector (today by default; swipe or tap to navigate historical days) — on the Today tab only
- Profile icon (tap → Settings)
- Overflow menu with options: Edit targets, Export data, Help

### Tab bar (fixed, below header)

Three tabs: **Today** · **Progress** · **History**. "Today" is the default.

### Today tab (default)

**1. Hero block — Daily Nutrition Summary**

- Full-width card dominating the top of the screen
- Contents specified in detail in Section 4.4.3
- Primary visual: large "calories remaining" number with animated daily progress ring around it

**2. Macro bars**

- Protein, carbs, fat bars with consumed/target and visual fill
- Color-coded per macro (existing Flask pattern preserved: "Unique colors for each macro and micro")
- Tap any bar: expands to show contribution per meal
- Swipeable to page 2: sugar, fiber, sodium — with their targets (per v1.18: FDA defaults, user-adjustable in Settings)

**3. Hydration widget (conditional)**

- If user has logged water at least once, widget appears here
- If user has never logged water, widget is hidden — no empty state, no prompt
- Contents: water consumed today, goal (default 64 oz, customizable), +8oz / +16oz / +24oz quick-add buttons
- Per decision #3: opt-in and silent by default

**4. Today's meals list**

- Chronological list of meals logged today
- Each row: time, meal description (shortened by AI), calories, macros in condensed form
- Tap meal: expands to full detail view with items breakdown, edit, delete options
- Swipe actions: edit (left swipe), delete (right swipe with confirmation)

**5. Recent Meals quick-chips (v1.18, elevated from Flask)**

- Below the meals list, above the smart-suggest block if both are active
- Up to 5 quick-log chips (v1.18: trimmed from Flask's 20 — 5 is the cognitive sweet spot; more becomes noise)
- Ranked by a deterministic composite score combining:
  - Frequency (how often this meal is logged, normalized)
  - Recency (exponential decay with ~14-day half-life)
  - Time-of-day correlation (is this meal typically logged near the current hour?)
- Tap chip → confirm-preview sheet → log meal (no editing needed for re-log, but user can adjust before confirm)
- AI-logged meals will never be exactly the same twice (food descriptions drift slightly even for "same" meal). The chip ranking groups near-identical meals by normalized description similarity.

**6. Smart-suggest meals block (conditional)**

- Shown above the FAB only when the suggestion engine has high-confidence predictions for this time of day
- Up to 3 suggested saved meals shown as horizontal cards
- Tap to instantly log that meal
- Per decision #8: only shown when confidence threshold is met; never padded with random suggestions
- Algorithm specified in Section 4.4.7

**7. Floating Action Button (FAB)**

- Same FAB pattern as Home Dashboard and Fitness category (Section 4.2.6) — opens the chatbot overlay
- The overlay contains two surfaces: the chatbot for nutrition and cross-domain questions, and shortcut buttons that open native logging flows directly
- Nutrition-specific shortcut buttons:
  - Log Meal (opens the text/voice meal parse flow)
  - Scan Meal (opens the camera directly)
  - Barcode (opens the barcode scanner directly)
  - Saved Meals (opens the saved meals list)
  - Log Water (visible only if hydration has been activated; opens +8/+16/+24 oz buttons)
  - Pantry Scan (Pro-gated; opens pantry camera)
- Logging does not go through the chatbot. Meal descriptions like "3 eggs and toast" typed into the chat are redirected to the Log Meal shortcut; the chatbot answers questions about macros, calories, meal timing, hydration, and cross-domain nutrition questions.

**8. Bottom tab bar**

- Standard five-tab bar; Nutrition tab highlighted

### Progress tab

The Progress tab is the longitudinal chart surface for Nutrition. Scale toggle at top: 7D / 30D / 90D / All-time (Pro).

**Progress tab contents (top to bottom):**

1. **Deficit/Surplus chart (v1.18, restored from Flask)** — daily line chart plotting `TDEE − calories_consumed` per day. Positive values = deficit; negative values = surplus. Target line at the user's goal-appropriate daily deficit/surplus (−500 for most cut goals, +250 for most bulk goals, 0 for maintenance). Tap any data point → Day Detail.
2. **Calorie adherence chart** — daily calories consumed vs target. Shows drift over time.
3. **Protein adherence chart** — daily protein (g) vs target.
4. **Body weight trend** — mirrored here from the Fitness Progress tab (same data, same component). Nutrition users care about weight change; surfacing it here avoids forcing a tab switch.
5. **Macro composition trend** — stacked-area chart showing protein/carbs/fat as % of daily calories over time.
6. **Sugar/fiber/sodium trends** — three small trend lines for the micronutrients, with their user-adjustable targets rendered as reference lines.

Tap any data point on any chart → Day Detail (§4.2a) for that date.

### History tab

Full chronological list of meals logged. Searchable by description, filterable by date range. Infinite scroll within the user's history tier. Tap any meal to view/edit.

### Layout Constraints

On iPhone SE, the hero block + macro bars fit above the fold on the Today tab. Today's meals list, Recent Meals chips, and smart-suggest block require scrolling. This is acceptable because the hero block answers the core user question ("what can I eat") without any scrolling.

## 4.4.3 Daily Nutrition Summary (The Hero)

The Daily Nutrition Summary is the most important single visual in the Nutrition category. It is what users come to the category to see.

### Visual Structure

**Center of the card:**

- **"Calories remaining"** label
- **Large number** — calories remaining today (target minus consumed, plus burn if rollover is on and calories were burned)
- Number animates on change (e.g., after a meal is logged, the number decrements smoothly)

**Ring around the number:**

- Circular progress ring showing consumption progress: 0% at top going clockwise
- Ring fills as user consumes calories toward target
- Color state:
  - Green: under target (0–90% consumed)
  - Soft amber: at or near target (90–105% consumed)
  - Red: significantly over target (>110% consumed)
- Ring is subtle; it complements the number without dominating

**Below the ring:**

- **Burn minus eaten equation** (as specified in Section 4.2.3 Nutrition card, consistent across dashboard and detail view):
  - "2,340 burned − 1,612 eaten = 728 remaining"
  - Numbers are tappable: tap "2,340 burned" to see today's workouts summary; tap "1,612 eaten" to jump to today's meals list

**Above the number (small, secondary):**

- User's current calorie target with context
- If auto-adjust is on: "Today's target: 2,200 kcal (adjusted from 2,100 based on this week's activity)"
- If rollover is on and they have rollover credit: "+150 from yesterday's deficit"

### Interactive Details

- Tap the ring center: shows a breakdown tooltip — target, consumed, burned, rollover, final remaining
- Long-press: shows debug/reasoning info (for troubleshooting user confusion about numbers)

### Data Flow

The "calories remaining" value is computed deterministically:

```
remaining = calorie_target + (burn_if_rollover_on) + (rollover_from_previous_days) - consumed_today
```

All variables are available server-side:
- `calorie_target` — from user's goal profile (see Section 4.1.11)
- `burn_if_rollover_on` — sum of today's workout burns if rollover setting is enabled
- `rollover_from_previous_days` — deficit from previous days if rollover is enabled
- `consumed_today` — sum of all meal calories logged today

### AI Usage In Summary

None. The summary is pure deterministic math. AI is used in meal logging to produce the inputs (calories consumed per meal); the summary just does arithmetic on those inputs.

## 4.4.4 Meal Logging — Text and Voice Input

The primary and most-used logging flow. User describes what they ate in natural language; AI parses and returns structured nutrition data; user confirms and saves.

### Entry Points

- FAB on any screen → Log Meal → Text input appears
- FAB → Chat with Life Dashboard → user types meal description (routes to same parser with visual confirmation of destination)
- Nutrition category FAB → Log Meal
- Voice button on any input screen triggers speech-to-text

### Screen Flow

**Step 1: Input**

- Header: "What did you eat?"
- Large text input with placeholder: "e.g., chicken salad with olive oil, apple on the side"
- Voice input button (microphone icon, always visible)
- Photo input button (opens scan meal flow — Section 4.4.5)
- Small text below: "Life Dashboard will estimate calories and macros"
- Submit button: "Estimate" (existing Flask behavior preserved)

**Step 2: AI processing (<3s target)**

- Subtle loading indicator
- Input field remains visible with user's text
- No blocking overlay

**Step 3: Review and confirm**

- AI-parsed meal appears above the input field
- **Shortened label** (AI-generated via `shorten_label`): "Chicken salad w/ apple"
- **Items breakdown** — itemized list of identified components with individual macro contributions:
  - "Grilled chicken breast — 6 oz — 280 kcal, 53g protein"
  - "Mixed greens with vegetables — 2 cups — 40 kcal, 3g protein"
  - "Olive oil — 1 tbsp — 120 kcal, 0g protein"
  - "Apple — 1 medium — 95 kcal, 0g protein"
- **Totals card** — aggregated macros:
  - Calories: 535
  - Protein: 56g
  - Carbs: 29g
  - Fat: 17g
  - Sugar: 22g, Fiber: 6g, Sodium: 340mg (secondary row, smaller)
- **Edit controls** — tap any item or macro to edit
- **Destination indicator** — small line of text below the preview: *"Logging to Nutrition · will count toward today's calorie budget"*
  - Per decision #3 guidance: make it obvious where data goes when AI parses
- **Save button** — primary button, "Log Meal"
- **Discard button** — secondary text link, "Cancel"

**Step 4: Post-save**

- Meal appears at bottom of today's meals list with brief entry animation
- Daily Nutrition Summary calories remaining decrements smoothly
- Haptic feedback on iOS (subtle tap confirmation)
- Macro bars update with animation
- If macro target crossed (e.g., user was under protein, now over): no interruption, macro bar just fills
- FAB remains accessible for next log

### AI Call Specification

- **Function:** `estimate_nutrition` (existing Flask, preserved)
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Input:** User's meal description (~50–200 tokens)
- **System prompt context:** User's profile (calorie target, macro targets, dietary preferences from onboarding if relevant)
- **Output:** Structured JSON with itemized components and totals
- **Target latency:** <3 seconds
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.0024
- **Timeout:** 30 seconds (existing)
- **Counts against paywall counter:** Yes

### AI Call: Shorten Label

After the meal is parsed but before display, a parallel call to `shorten_label` produces a concise 2–5 word label for the meal list display. Existing Flask behavior preserved.

- **Function:** `shorten_label`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku
- **Output:** Short string (e.g., "Chicken salad w/ apple")
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.0002
- **Runs:** Background, in parallel with `estimate_nutrition` or on result return
- **Counts against paywall:** No (background, not user-invoked)

### Voice Input Flow

Per decision #11 — voice input with confirmation preview (not hands-free save).

- User taps mic icon
- Speech recognition runs (iOS SFSpeechRecognizer, Android SpeechRecognizer)
- Transcribed text appears in the input field as user speaks (real-time)
- User reviews transcription, edits if needed
- User taps Estimate to trigger AI parse
- Remainder of flow identical to text input

**Accessibility note:** Voice input respects device VoiceOver/TalkBack settings and pauses speech recognition when screen readers are actively narrating.

### AI Parse Error Handling

- **AI returns empty response (no recognizable food):** Show error message "Couldn't parse that. Try describing each item, or [take a photo / add manually]"; preserve user input
- **AI returns partial data:** Show what was parsed, flag missing fields for user input
- **AI times out:** Retry once with exponential backoff; if still fails, show error with manual entry fallback
- **AI returns suspicious values (e.g., 10,000 calorie single meal):** Flag with warning "This seems high — double-check before saving"; allow override

### Manual Entry Fallback

- Users can always bypass AI and log manually
- Tap "Log manually" link below the AI input
- Form fields: description (text), calories, protein, carbs, fat, optional sugar/fiber/sodium
- Save without AI call

## 4.4.5 Meal Logging — Photo Scanning

AI-powered meal photo scanning. User takes a photo of their meal; AI identifies items, estimates portions, returns macros.

### Entry Points

- FAB → Scan Meal (camera opens directly)
- Within Log Meal flow, tap the photo icon to switch to scan mode
- Nutrition detail view FAB → Scan Meal

### Screen Flow

**Step 1: Camera**

- Full-screen camera view
- Overlay guidance: "Point at your meal"
- Options at bottom: Shutter button (center), flash toggle (left), flip camera (right)
- Option to select from photo library instead (upper right icon)
- Gallery access allows users to scan meals they photographed earlier

**Step 2: Photo captured**

- Photo preview with options: Use This Photo, Retake, Cancel
- Optional: crop/rotate tools (v1.1, not v1.0)

**Step 3: AI processing (target <5s, up to 10s acceptable)**

- Loading state with photo visible
- Progress indicator

**Step 4: Review and confirm**

- Similar to text parse review, but with photo thumbnail at top
- Itemized components the AI identified
- Totals
- **Confidence indicator** (per decision #5):
  - High confidence (>80%): standard preview, no special indicator
  - Medium confidence (60–80%): subtle note "If portions look off, tap to edit"
  - Low confidence (<60%): prominent prompt "AI had trouble with this photo — want to retake or edit?"
- **Pro escalation (per decision #4 hybrid):**
  - For Pro users or any user on low-confidence scan: "Try Premium Scan for better accuracy" button
  - Tapping triggers a second scan using Opus model
  - For Core users: Premium Scan is locked with "Upgrade to Pro for Premium Scan"
- **Destination indicator** — same pattern as text parse
- **Save and Edit controls** — identical to text parse

### AI Call Specification (Meal Photo Scan)

- **Function:** `scan_meal_image` (existing Flask, model changed)
- **Model (v1):** Claude Sonnet 4.6 (upgraded from Opus for cost efficiency)
- **Model (Pro premium scan):** Claude Opus 4.6 (retained for highest-accuracy option)
- **Input:** Base64-encoded image + structured prompt (see Section 4.4.5a below)
- **Output:** Structured JSON with identified items, portions, confidence score, totals
- **Target latency:** <5 seconds (Sonnet); <8 seconds (Opus)
- **Cost per call (Sonnet):** ~$0.012
- **Cost per call (Opus):** ~$0.060
- **Timeout:** 30 seconds
- **Counts against paywall counter:** Yes (either tier)

### 4.4.5a Prompt Engineering Strategy For Meal Scanning

Meal photo accuracy directly affects user trust. The prompt engineering for `scan_meal_image` is specified in detail here because it is one of the highest-leverage design decisions in the entire product.

The prompt uses nine prompt-engineering techniques, applied together, to maximize accuracy:

**Technique 1: Explicit itemization step.**
The prompt instructs the model to list every distinct food item visible before estimating macros. Forces per-item attention rather than gestalt guessing.

**Technique 2: Serving vessel identification.**
The prompt first asks the model to identify the serving vessel (dinner plate ~10–11 inches, salad plate ~7–8 inches, bowl with size estimate, cup/mug). This anchors portion estimation to a known scale reference.

**Technique 3: Portion anchoring with visual references.**
The prompt provides calibrated reference points:
- Deck of cards = 3 oz of meat
- Baseball = 1 cup
- Golf ball = 2 tbsp
- Thumb = 1 tbsp of oil/fat
- Fist = 1 cup of vegetables or grains
- Cupped palm = 1/2 cup

The model is instructed to reference these when estimating portions.

**Technique 4: Hidden ingredient prompting.**
The prompt explicitly asks: "What cooking ingredients might be present but not visible? Consider cooking oil, butter, sauces, dressings, marinades, cheese, and hidden sugar. Add conservative estimates for these."

This catches the silent 100–300 calories in most restaurant-style meals that AI would otherwise miss.

**Technique 5: Confidence scoring.**
The model outputs a confidence score (0.0–1.0) based on:
- Image clarity
- Item recognizability
- Portion estimation certainty
- Hidden ingredient reasoning

Low confidence (<0.6) triggers the user prompt to retake or escalate to Opus.

**Technique 6: Structured JSON output.**
The prompt requires output in exact JSON schema:
```json
{
  "items": [
    {
      "name": "string",
      "portion": "string",
      "portion_reference": "string (e.g., 'deck of cards', '1 cup', '6 oz')",
      "calories": number,
      "protein": number,
      "carbs": number,
      "fat": number,
      "sugar": number,
      "fiber": number,
      "sodium": number
    }
  ],
  "hidden_ingredients": [
    {
      "name": "string",
      "estimated_amount": "string",
      "reasoning": "string",
      "calories": number,
      "fat": number
    }
  ],
  "totals": {
    "calories": number,
    "protein": number,
    "carbs": number,
    "fat": number,
    "sugar": number,
    "fiber": number,
    "sodium": number
  },
  "confidence": 0.85,
  "notes": "string (optional reasoning notes)"
}
```

Structured output prevents hand-waving and forces the model to commit to specific numbers.

**Technique 7: Few-shot examples.**
The system prompt includes 2–3 example mappings: a sample photo description → expected JSON output. Examples cover different meal types (simple plate, complex restaurant meal, mixed salad).

**Technique 8: Chain-of-thought reasoning.**
The prompt asks the model to reason step-by-step before outputting the JSON:
1. Identify serving vessel
2. List every visible item
3. Estimate portion for each using reference anchors
4. Identify likely hidden ingredients
5. Calculate macros per item
6. Sum totals
7. Rate confidence

Chain-of-thought reasoning significantly improves accuracy for multi-step tasks.

**Technique 9: User feedback loop (v1.1+).**
When a user edits the AI's output after a scan, capture the deltas:
- Which items were added (AI missed)?
- Which items were removed (AI hallucinated)?
- Which portions were adjusted (AI miscalibrated)?

Over time, these deltas form a calibration dataset. In v1.1+, use them to tune the prompt (e.g., "Portions are often over-estimated for X food type — tend toward conservative estimates") or for fine-tuning if the feedback volume warrants it.

### Prompt Template (Concrete Spec)

The actual prompt sent with each scan call is approximately 600 tokens and includes:

1. System role: "You are a nutrition expert helping a user log a meal. Accuracy in portion estimation is critical for their health goals."
2. User profile context (100 tokens): age, sex, calorie target, macro targets, known dietary preferences
3. Reasoning framework (the 7-step chain-of-thought from Technique 8)
4. Portion reference anchors (from Technique 3)
5. Hidden ingredient reminders (from Technique 4)
6. Output schema (from Technique 6)
7. Few-shot examples (from Technique 7)
8. Image input

The exact prompt text is maintained in the codebase as a versioned template. Changes go through A/B testing against a held-out photo evaluation set.

### Multi-Item Photo Handling

For a photo with multiple distinct meals or plates (e.g., a dinner plate with three separate foods), the model itemizes each distinct food separately. For a photo with one plate containing a mixed meal (e.g., a burrito bowl), the model itemizes each ingredient within the meal.

If the photo is ambiguous (e.g., blurry, poorly lit, partially obstructed), confidence drops and the retake/Opus-escalation prompts appear.

### Camera Overlay Improvements (vs Flask)

- Better low-light guidance ("Use flash or move to better light")
- Framing guide ("Fill the frame with the meal")
- Multi-shot option (take 2–3 photos at different angles for hard-to-ID meals — all photos sent as a batch for analysis)
- Gallery selection for retroactive logging

## 4.4.6 Meal Logging — Barcode Scanning

Barcode scanner for packaged foods. Existing Flask feature, enhanced with AI fallback per decision #7.

### Entry Points

- FAB → Barcode
- Within Log Meal flow, tap barcode icon

### Flow

**Step 1: Scanner**

- Camera opens with barcode detection UI
- Reticle overlay indicating scan target area
- Automatic detection when a valid barcode is in frame
- Manual entry option if scanning fails

**Step 2a: Barcode found in Open Food Facts**

- Product details card appears:
  - Product name
  - Brand
  - Product image (if available)
  - Serving size dropdown (if multiple serving options available)
  - Macro preview for selected serving size
- User adjusts serving count (e.g., "2 servings")
- Save to log

**Step 2b: Barcode not found in Open Food Facts (per decision #7)**

- Fallback message: "Not in our database. Want AI to estimate from the product name?"
- Manual text input: "Brand and product name?"
- User types (e.g., "Trader Joe's Spicy Cashews")
- AI estimation runs (same as text parse)
- Results shown with standard preview
- Option: Save the AI estimate as a custom product in `custom_products` table for future barcode scans of the same product

**Step 3: Save**

- Standard meal save flow

### Device Compatibility (Resolving Flask Tech Debt)

Current Flask PWA has a known issue: barcode button shows on all browsers even when unsupported (P3-22). React Native resolves this cleanly:

- Barcode scanning uses native iOS/Android barcode APIs (available on all supported devices)
- No browser compatibility concerns
- Barcode button always available

### AI Fallback Specification

- **Function:** `estimate_nutrition` with product-focused prompt variant
- **Model:** Claude Haiku
- **Input:** Brand + product name + optional serving hint
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.0024
- **Counts against paywall:** Yes

### Data Persistence

- **Existing:** Flask does not persist barcode results (re-fetched from Open Food Facts each time).
- **New in v1:** `custom_products` table for user-contributed products:
  - user_id, barcode, product_name, brand, serving_size, calories, protein, carbs, fat, sugar, fiber, sodium, source (of_facts / ai / manual), created_at
- Custom products are per-user (not shared across users) to respect privacy and avoid data-quality issues
- User can flag a custom product as "add to community database" (Pro-gated, v1.1+ feature)

## 4.4.7 Meal Logging — Saved Meals with Smart-Suggest

Saved meals are user-curated meal templates for frequently eaten foods. Per decision #8, v1 adds a smart-suggest layer that predicts what the user might want to log.

### Saving A Meal

- After any meal is logged, the meal detail view has a "Save this meal" option
- On tap: meal is added to `saved_meals` table (existing Flask table, preserved)
- Saved meal includes: description, macros, items breakdown, user-chosen label

### Retrieving Saved Meals

- FAB → Saved Meals → scrollable list of all saved meals
- Search within list
- Tap any saved meal: opens meal log screen with all fields pre-filled from the saved meal
- User can edit portion size (e.g., "1.5 servings of my usual breakfast") before saving
- Delete saved meal via swipe action

### Smart-Suggest (New in v1)

The smart-suggest block on the Nutrition detail view shows up to 3 saved meals predicted to be what the user might want to log right now.

### Smart-Suggest Algorithm (Deterministic, No AI)

Every saved meal is scored for current-moment relevance using a weighted formula:

**Signal 1: Time-of-day match (weight: 40%)**
For each saved meal, look at the historical log times of that meal. Compute the circular mean and standard deviation of log hour. Current hour's match score:
- Within 1 SD of mean: 1.0
- Within 2 SDs: 0.5
- Beyond 2 SDs: 0.0

**Signal 2: Day-of-week match (weight: 15%)**
Historical logs of this meal by weekday. If user has logged this on 3+ of the last 8 same-weekdays, score 1.0. Otherwise proportional.

**Signal 3: Recency boost (weight: 15%)**
If logged within last 7 days: 1.0. Within last 30: 0.5. Otherwise: 0.0.

**Signal 4: Frequency (weight: 15%)**
Total log count, normalized to 0–1 against the user's most-logged saved meal.

**Signal 5: Macro fit (weight: 15%)**
Does this meal's macros fit in what's remaining today?
- All three macros (protein, carb, fat) fit under remaining: 1.0
- 2 of 3 fit: 0.7
- 1 of 3 fits: 0.3
- None fit: 0.0

**Total confidence score:** Weighted sum (0.0–1.0)

### Suggestion Threshold And Display

- Only meals scoring above 0.5 are suggested
- Show top 3 suggestions above the threshold
- If no meals clear the threshold, show nothing (per decision #8: "truly guessing what you might log" — don't pad with random saved meals)
- Suggestions rendered as horizontal cards, each showing: meal label, calories, quick-log tap target
- Tap: logs the suggested meal immediately with same macros as saved (user can still edit after)

### Feedback Loop

When a user rejects a suggestion (taps "not right now" or simply ignores it for that meal occasion), the meal's suggestion weight for that time-of-day is reduced for the next 24 hours. Over time, rejected suggestions surface less often.

When a user accepts a suggestion, the meal's score reinforces for that time-of-day.

This feedback is tracked in a `suggestion_feedback` table (user_id, saved_meal_id, suggested_at, action: logged / rejected / ignored, context_hour, context_weekday).

### Performance Requirements

- Suggestion computation: <50ms per request
- All signals are precomputable; cached at the user level, invalidated on new meal log
- No AI calls, no external API calls

### Smart-Suggest Visibility

- Only appears on Nutrition detail view (not on Home Dashboard — dashboard stays data-forward per Section 4.2)
- Hidden if user has fewer than 3 saved meals (not enough data for meaningful suggestions)
- Hidden if user is in first 7 days (no historical pattern yet)

## 4.4.8 Pantry Scanner (Pro Feature)

Photograph ingredients; AI identifies items in the photo; AI suggests meals that fit the user's remaining macros for the day. Per decision #6, this becomes a Pro-tier feature in v1.

### Entry Points

- FAB → Pantry Scan (Core users see "Pro feature" badge and upgrade prompt)
- Pro upgrade page showcase

### Rationale For Pro Gating

The pantry scanner makes two AI calls per use (identify_ingredients + suggest_meal), each costing more than simple meal logging. Combined cost per use: ~$0.005. Used casually (e.g., "I wonder what I could make") rather than out of necessity (I need to log what I ate), so it's a fair Pro differentiator.

### Flow (Pro Users)

**Step 1: Scanner**

- Camera opens
- Prompt: "Point at your pantry, fridge, or groceries"
- Capture multiple photos supported (up to 5 per session)
- Next button advances to step 2

**Step 2: Meal type selection**

- Quick-pick: Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner, Snack
- User selects one

**Step 3: AI processing**

- Loading state
- Two calls run in parallel:
  - `identify_ingredients` — list ingredients visible in photos
  - Pre-fetch of current day's remaining calories/macros
- Then:
  - `suggest_meal` — generate 3 meal suggestions using: identified ingredients, meal type, remaining calories
- Total expected latency: <6 seconds

**Step 4: Suggestions display**

- Three meal cards, each with:
  - Meal name and brief description
  - Calorie estimate
  - Macro preview
  - "Make this" button (logs a placeholder meal the user can edit after preparing)
  - "Not for me" dismiss
- User can generate new suggestions if none appeal: "Show me more" (counts as additional AI interaction)

### AI Call Specifications

**identify_ingredients (existing Flask, preserved):**
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5 (vision)
- **Input:** 1–5 photos (~1500 tokens per image)
- **Output:** Flat list of identified ingredients
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.0018 (single photo), up to ~$0.009 for 5 photos
- **Target latency:** <3 seconds

**suggest_meal (existing Flask, preserved per decision #6 answer A):**
- **Model:** Claude Haiku
- **Input (per recent simplification):** ingredients + meal type + calories remaining only (no full macros, no profile context)
- **Output:** 3 meal suggestions with names, brief descriptions, estimated macros
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.0036
- **Target latency:** <3 seconds

### What Happens When User Taps "Make This"

- Placeholder meal logged with estimated macros from the suggestion
- User prepares and eats the meal
- Tap "Did you eat this?" notification 30 minutes later (if user has enabled meal tracking notifications)
- OR user navigates back to the meal in history and updates actual portions

### Pro Gating UI

**For Core users:**
- Pantry Scan is visible in the FAB with a subtle "Pro" badge
- Tapping shows upgrade prompt: "Pantry Scan uses AI to suggest meals fitting your macros. Available with Life Dashboard Pro. Start your 7-day free trial."
- Matches the paywall pattern from Section 4.1.9 rather than a separate upsell

## 4.4.9 Macro Tracking and Targets

The Nutrition category continuously tracks four primary macros: protein, carbs, fat. (Calories is the aggregate.) Three secondary nutrients are tracked for health awareness: sugar, fiber, sodium.

### Target Derivation

Targets come from the user's goal profile (computed during onboarding in Section 4.1.11):

- **Calorie target** — goal-adjusted TDEE, RMR-floored
- **Protein target** — goal × reference weight × protein g/kg (e.g., 2.0 g/kg for weight loss)
- **Fat target** — max of (25% of calories) or (0.7 g/kg floor)
- **Carb target** — remainder after protein and fat, with 100g/day minimum

These are computed once during onboarding and stored in `user_goals`. Re-computed only when:
- User changes their goal (triggers re-compute)
- User's weight changes by >2% (if auto-adjust is on per Section 4.4.10)
- User manually edits targets in Settings

### Macro Bars Display

Throughout the app (dashboard, nutrition detail view, meal detail view), macros are displayed with horizontal progress bars:

- Bar shows consumed/target
- Color-coded per macro (existing Flask pattern: unique colors for each macro)
- Fill animates as meals are logged
- Overshoot renders as the bar filling past 100% with a distinct overshoot color (not red — overshoot on protein is often good, on fat sometimes okay)

### Macro Edit Flow

From the Daily Nutrition Summary, tap the macro bars area → expanded view with:

- Per-macro consumed and target
- Distribution by meal (which meals contributed to today's protein, etc.)
- Edit targets button (opens Settings with targets prefilled)

### Secondary Nutrient Targets (v1.18, restored from Flask)

Sugar, fiber, and sodium are tracked per meal and displayed against user-adjustable daily targets.

**Default targets (FDA Daily Values, rounded for clarity):**

- **Sugar:** 50 g/day (FDA Daily Value; this is a soft ceiling — being under it is desirable)
- **Fiber:** 30 g/day (FDA-recommended intake for adults; this is a soft floor — being at or above it is desirable)
- **Sodium:** 2,300 mg/day (FDA Daily Value; ceiling)

**User customization:** Targets are editable in Settings → Nutrition → Secondary Targets. Each row allows the user to override the default, revert to the FDA default, or disable the target entirely (the micronutrient is still tracked but no target line is drawn and no contribution flows into the Nutrition score).

**Display:**

- Secondary nutrients render on page 2 of the swipeable macro card (page 1: P/C/F; page 2: sugar/fiber/sodium). This matches the Flask pattern preserved in v1.18.
- Each secondary nutrient shows: consumed value today / target, with a horizontal progress bar
- Directional framing in the bar fill color:
  - **Sugar and sodium (ceilings):** green while under target, amber at 80–100%, red over 100%
  - **Fiber (floor):** amber while under 80%, green once 80%+ is reached; going far above target is never flagged (no upper limit concern)
- No strong visual alarms — these are awareness signals, not hard targets

**Scoring contribution:** Secondary nutrient adherence contributes to the Nutrition score only at a small weight (Section 9 specifies; framework placeholder: ≤5% of Nutrition score total). Users who disable a target entirely don't have it factor into their score at all.

### Edge Case: Macros Don't Sum to Calories

Due to rounding and the difference between "metabolizable energy" and macro × 4/9 arithmetic, the sum of (protein × 4 + carbs × 4 + fat × 9) rarely exactly equals total calories. Handle by:

- Storing both independently (calories is the AI's direct answer; macros are separate)
- Not auto-computing one from the other
- Not alerting user to discrepancy (minor, unavoidable, not actionable)

## 4.4.10 Calorie Rollover and Auto-Adjust

Two user-toggleable settings inherited from Flask PWA, preserved and tightened in v1.18.

### Calorie Rollover

- **What it does:** If user is under their daily calorie target at end-of-day, the unused deficit rolls over to the next day (user has more to eat tomorrow). Similarly, if user overshoots, the surplus reduces tomorrow's target.
- **Default:** OFF
- **Rationale for off-by-default:** Novice users think of their target as a hard daily ceiling; rollover can cause confusion. Experienced users (many of your target Optimizers) explicitly want rollover because it reflects how the body actually handles energy balance over days.
- **Cap (v1.18, new):** Rollover is capped at ±200 kcal per day. A user who is 600 kcal under target on Monday does not get +600 added to Tuesday's target — only +200. Cap applies in both directions (surplus rollover is also capped at −200). The cap exists to prevent rollover from becoming a binge-enabler (user under-eats several days, accumulates +1,200 kcal of "credit," then has a massive surplus day framed as "within target") and to prevent demoralizing negative rollovers that make Tuesday's target feel unreachable.
- **Visible indicator when active:** On the Daily Nutrition Summary (§4.4.3), whenever rollover is active AND has affected today's target, an inline display shows the rollover transparently:
