  - Example display: "Today's target: 2,200 kcal (2,000 base + 200 rollover from yesterday)"
  - The rollover portion of the target is visually distinct (subtle accent color or prefix chip)
  - If the rollover is negative: "Today's target: 1,800 kcal (2,000 base − 200 from yesterday's surplus)"
- **Setting location:** Profile → Nutrition Settings → Calorie Rollover
- **Rollover computation cadence:** Calculated at local midnight as part of the nightly score-snapshot job. Today's displayed target reflects yesterday's rollover.

### Auto-Adjust Targets

- **What it does:** Recomputes user's TDEE and macro targets based on observed weight changes and activity patterns over time.
- **Algorithm (14-day window, ±5% per adjustment, ±15% cumulative cap):**
  - Every 14 days, evaluate actual weight change vs expected rate (goal-derived: −1 lb/week for standard cut, +0.5 lb/week for bulk, 0 for maintenance)
  - If actual change is within 20% of expected: no adjustment
  - If actual change is slower than expected (e.g., user is losing slower than planned on a cut): decrease calorie target by 5% — the user may be under-reporting food intake or NEAT is lower than estimated
  - If actual change is faster than expected (e.g., user is losing too fast): increase calorie target by 5% — the user is losing too aggressively, risk of muscle loss and rebound
  - Cumulative cap: adjustments stop once the current target has drifted ±15% from the onboarding-computed original; further adjustment requires user to re-evaluate their goal or manually override
- **Default:** ON
- **Setting location:** Profile → Nutrition Settings → Auto-Adjust Targets

### Auto-Adjust Evaluation Trigger (v1.18 clarification)

Per v1.18 decision: the 14-day evaluation runs **on weight log** (not nightly). Specifically:

- When the user logs a body weight (manual or auto-synced from HealthKit / smart scale), the auto-adjust engine checks whether ≥14 days have elapsed since the last adjustment (or since onboarding if never adjusted)
- If ≥14 days elapsed AND the user has logged at least 5 weight entries in the evaluation window (minimum signal quality): the engine computes actual vs expected and decides whether to adjust
- If <14 days OR insufficient weight entries: no evaluation runs

This avoids spurious nightly cron calls for users who aren't logging weight and ensures every adjustment is grounded in fresh weight data.

### Auto-Adjust User-Confirm Transparency (v1.18, tightened)

Prior versions made auto-adjust changes silently with a passive notification after the fact. v1.18 elevates this: **auto-adjust never changes the user's target silently**. When the engine decides an adjustment is warranted, it:

1. **Presents a confirm card** on the user's next app open (or as a dismissable in-app banner that persists until dismissed):
   > *"Based on 14 days of data, your weight loss is tracking slower than expected. Life Dashboard recommends lowering your daily calorie target from 2,200 to 2,090 kcal (−5%) to match your goal pace.*
   >
   > *The math: expected −2.0 lbs over 14 days, actual −1.2 lbs. Recommended adjustment: −5% of calorie target.*
   >
   > [Apply adjustment]   [Keep current target]   [Explain more]"

2. **"Apply adjustment"**: targets are updated, change is logged to a `target_adjustments_history` table, and a small confirmation toast appears.

3. **"Keep current target"**: the adjustment is suppressed for this cycle. The engine will re-evaluate at the next weight log after another 14-day window has elapsed.

4. **"Explain more"**: opens a detail drawer showing:
   - Current goal and expected rate
   - Actual weight change over the evaluation window (chart view)
   - Full adjustment math (with references to the formulas in §4.1.5)
   - History of all prior auto-adjustments and user confirmations
   - Option to disable auto-adjust entirely

No prescriptive language ("you should…"), no nagging — the card is descriptive, the user holds final authority. This satisfies Design Principle 3.4 (User Agency) and the §3.2 descriptive-AI rule (all copy in the confirm card is deterministic-template text, no AI-generated copy).

### Data Persistence

Current Flask `user_goals` table already stores the toggle states alongside goal data. Preserved unchanged in React Native migration. New in v1.18: `target_adjustments_history` table with columns `(user_id, adjustment_date, field_changed, old_value, new_value, reason_code, user_confirmed_at, computation_inputs_json)` to support the Explain More drawer and future audit needs.

## 4.4.11 Meal Edit and Delete

All meals can be edited or deleted. Edit can be done manually or via AI re-estimation.

### Manual Edit

- Tap any meal in the today's meals list or history view
- Meal detail view opens with all fields editable:
  - Meal description
  - Calories
  - Protein, carbs, fat
  - Sugar, fiber, sodium
  - Items breakdown (if AI-parsed)
  - Timestamp (editable — user can correct time if logged late)
- Save: updates meal in DB, recomputes day totals

### AI-Assisted Edit (Existing Flask, Preserved)

Users can correct an AI-parsed meal by describing the correction in natural language:

- Meal detail view → "AI Edit" button
- Input: "Actually it was 8 oz of chicken, not 6" or "I forgot, I also had a cookie"
- AI receives original meal + correction + returns updated parse
- User reviews and confirms
- Cost: ~$0.0024 (same as initial parse)
- Counts against paywall

### Delete

- Swipe delete on meal row in list view, OR
- Meal detail view → Delete button (with confirmation)
- Day totals recompute immediately

## 4.4.12 Hydration Tracking (Opt-In Silent Default)

Per decision #3, hydration is part of the Nutrition category but opt-in and silent by default. A user who never logs water never sees a hydration widget.

### Activation

Hydration activates when:

- User logs water for the first time via any input method:
  - FAB → Log Water (when FAB is invoked, Log Water appears as an option after user has activated hydration OR is always available; see activation note below)
  - Unified AI input parses a hydration-relevant message: "8 oz water" or "had a bottle of water"
  - HealthKit connection syncs water data from Apple Health
  - User explicitly enables hydration in Settings

- Once activated, the hydration widget appears on the Nutrition detail view

### Activation Via AI Parse (Per Decision #3 Connection)

When the unified AI input parses something like "8 oz water" or "I drank a bottle of water":

1. AI recognizes hydration content
2. Routes to hydration logger with visual confirmation: *"Logging 8 oz to Hydration"*
3. Hydration subsystem activates silently if not already active
4. User sees the log appear in the hydration widget

### Hydration Widget

When activated and visible on Nutrition detail view:

- Today's water intake (primary metric)
- Daily goal (default 64 oz, customizable — e.g., 80 oz for weightlifters, 100 oz for endurance athletes)
- Visual progress bar or ring
- Quick-add buttons: +8oz, +16oz, +24oz (or +250ml, +500ml, +750ml for metric)
- "Other amount" field for custom quantities

### Log Water Flow (When Active)

- FAB → Log Water (one tap)
- Quick-add buttons with last-used amount highlighted
- Save: increments today's total instantly

### Data Sources

- **Manual:** Quick-add buttons and custom entry
- **AI parse:** "8 oz water" routes here
- **HealthKit / Health Connect:** Reads water intake data type if user has enabled it
- **Other connections:** Smart water bottles (HidrateSpark, etc.) via HealthKit

### Data Persistence

New table in v1: `hydration_logs`
- user_id, log_datetime, amount_oz, amount_ml, source (manual / ai_parse / healthkit / smart_bottle)
- Aggregation per day is computed from this log

### Scoring Impact

Hydration contributes to the Nutrition score only when active. Users without hydration tracking do not have a hydration signal feeding their Nutrition score — graceful degradation per Section 9 framework.

### What Hydration Is Not

- Not a required input for anything else
- No nagging notifications ("You haven't logged water today")
- No empty state for users who don't use it
- No color-shaming for being under goal (same no-judgment principle as other categories)

## 4.4.13 Nutrition Score (Framework Only)

The Nutrition category produces a single 0–100 score surfaced on the Home Dashboard and at the top of the Nutrition detail view. Exact math specified in Section 9 (Scoring Engine).

### Framework Principles

- **Deterministic only** — no AI in scoring
- **Nutrition-domain data only** — meals logged, macro hits, hydration (if active)
- **Graceful degradation** — users who don't track hydration get a score based on calorie and macro adherence alone
- **Personalization after baselines form** — 14-day warmup; after that, personal patterns inform the score

### What Contributes (Directionally)

- **Calorie adherence** — how close to target (not over, not dramatically under)
- **Protein hit rate** — daily protein ≥ 80% of target
- **Macro distribution** — consumed macros within reasonable bounds of targets
- **Logging consistency** — did the user actually log meals today? (not just a hero metric but a signal they're engaged)
- **Hydration hit** (if active) — within range of daily goal

Exact weights deferred to Section 9.

## 4.4.14 Meal History and Trends

History and trend analysis for the Nutrition category, following the same 90-day-hot + Pro-long-term pattern from Fitness (decision #12).

### History Access

- Nutrition detail view header: date picker (today by default, swipe or tap for other dates)
- Tap date: loads that day's nutrition data
- 90 days hot available to all users; Pro extends to long-term archived history (cut-line in Section 13)

### Day Detail View

Mirrors Flask's existing pattern:

- Day's calorie total and target
- Day's macros vs targets
- All meals logged that day with timestamps
- Workout burn for that day
- Weight logged that day (if any)
- Delete meal option from this view

### Trends View

New in v1: Nutrition Trends tab within Nutrition detail view.

**Tabs within Trends:**

- **This Week** — daily calories vs target, macros vs target, weight trend
- **This Month** — 30-day averages, consistency metrics
- **All Time** (Pro only) — full history aggregated into charts

**Metrics displayed:**

- Average daily calories (vs target)
- Average protein (vs target)
- Days hit target (count and %)
- Longest streak of hitting protein target
- Highest and lowest days (outliers)
- Correlation with weight trend (v1.1+ — not v1.0, deferred until cross-domain work begins)

## 4.4.15 Connection Dependencies

Nutrition is primarily AI-powered and has minimal external connection surface compared to Fitness.

| Data Type | Source | Required? |
|---|---|---|
| Meal macros | AI (`estimate_nutrition`, `scan_meal_image`) | Required for AI logging flows |
| Barcode data | Open Food Facts API + AI fallback | For barcode scanner only |
| Water | HealthKit / Health Connect (optional) | No — manual entry primary |
| Historical meal data from other apps | Not supported in v1 | N/A |

Future connections to consider (v2+):

- MyFitnessPal data import — allow users to import historical meals from MyFitnessPal
- Cronometer data import — for users coming from Cronometer
- Restaurant menu APIs (Nutritionix, etc.) — to improve accuracy for chain restaurant meals
- Grocery delivery APIs (Instacart) — to auto-log groceries as pantry context

All deferred to v2+.

## 4.4.16 AI Usage In Nutrition

Summary of all AI calls in the Nutrition category, including their costs and paywall handling.

| Call | Where Used | Model | Cost/Call | Counted Against Paywall? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| estimate_nutrition | Meal text/voice parsing | Haiku 4.5 | $0.0024 | Yes |
| estimate_nutrition (barcode fallback) | Barcode not in Open Food Facts | Haiku 4.5 | $0.0024 | Yes |
| scan_meal_image (standard) | Meal photo scanning | **Sonnet 4.6** (upgraded from Opus) | $0.012 | Yes |
| scan_meal_image (Premium, Pro only) | User-requested premium re-scan | Opus 4.6 | $0.060 | Yes |
| identify_ingredients | Pantry scan (Pro only) | Haiku 4.5 | $0.0018 | Yes (Pro) |
| suggest_meal | Pantry scan (Pro only) | Haiku 4.5 | $0.0036 | Yes (Pro) |
| shorten_label | Every meal save (background) | Haiku 4.5 | $0.0002 | No |
| ai-edit-meal | AI-assisted meal correction | Haiku 4.5 (reuses estimate_nutrition) | $0.0024 | Yes |

### Key Model Change From Flask PWA

Flask currently uses Opus for `scan_meal_image`. Per decision #4, v1 downgrades the default to Sonnet 4.6 (5x cheaper, quality acceptable for most meals) while keeping Opus available as a Pro-tier Premium Scan for users who need maximum accuracy. Net cost impact per average user: ~$0.06/month reduction for Core/Pro users not using Premium Scan; Pro users may use Premium Scan occasionally at additional cost.

### Cost Modeling Per User

**Daily AI cost from Nutrition (estimated heavy usage):**
- 3 meal logs via text parse: 3 × $0.0024 = $0.0072
- 1 meal scan: 1 × $0.012 = $0.012
- 5 label shortenings (background): 5 × $0.0002 = $0.001
- Estimated total daily Nutrition AI cost: ~$0.020

This matches the broader per-user AI cost target of <$0.10/day across all categories.

**If Pro user uses pantry scan once/day:**
- Add $0.005 = total ~$0.025/day

**If Pro user uses Premium Scan once/day:**
- Add $0.048 = total ~$0.073/day
- Still viable at Pro pricing of $9.99/month = ~$0.33/day revenue; Premium Scan at $0.08/call against 10/day cap is cost-controlled per §10.7.2

### Prompt Engineering Maintained

Per Section 4.4.5a, meal scan prompts use nine specific techniques to maximize accuracy. Prompt templates are versioned in the codebase. Quality is measured against a held-out evaluation set of real meal photos with ground-truth macros (internal tooling, not user-facing).

## 4.4.17 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Specific reductions applied to the Nutrition category relative to naive implementations:

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Separate UIs for text logging, photo scanning, barcode, etc. | Unified FAB with one-tap routing to each mode |
| Typing full meal description every time | Saved meals + smart-suggest predicts what user wants to log |
| Blank macro fields for manual entry | AI pre-fills macros from description; user edits only |
| Manual portion entry | AI estimates portion with calibrated visual anchors |
| Counting hidden ingredients | AI prompted explicitly to account for cooking oils and sauces |
| Re-scanning every product by hand | Barcode scanner with AI fallback for unknown products |
| Rebuilding favorite meals every time | Saved meals persisted with one-tap re-log |
| Finding saved meal in long list | Smart-suggest surfaces likely meals at top |
| Manual water entry requiring navigation | +8/+16/+24 oz quick-add buttons |
| Separate hydration app required | Integrated hydration tracking (opt-in, silent when not used) |
| Users forgetting to log meals hours later | History edit with timestamp adjustment |
| AI getting meals wrong | AI-assisted edit lets user describe correction in natural language |
| Wondering what to eat | Pantry scanner generates suggestions fitting remaining macros |
| Re-computing targets manually on weight change | Auto-adjust targets optional setting |
| Hitting target but feeling wasted food today | Calorie rollover optional setting preserves unused calories for tomorrow |

## 4.4.18 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast (4.5:1 for body text)
- All tappable targets: 44pt iOS, 48dp Android minimum
- VoiceOver and TalkBack tested end-to-end for meal logging flows
- Voice input available for all meal description fields (independent of screen reader state)
- Macro bars have text labels alongside color (never color alone)
- Photo scan results read aloud item by item with VoiceOver
- Camera overlay has alternative text for screen readers
- Barcode scanner has manual entry fallback for users who can't use camera
- Haptic feedback on save respects device haptic settings
- Animations respect reduced motion settings

## 4.4.19 Edge Cases and Error Handling

- **User logs meal but backend save fails** — meal is queued in local storage; sync retry on next successful network call; user notified only if sync fails repeatedly
- **User goes offline mid-meal-log** — meal save queues locally; AI parse attempted on reconnect; if user was mid-typing, text is preserved
- **AI parse returns wildly wrong values** (e.g., 50,000 calories) — flagged with "This seems high, please check" prompt before save
- **AI parse returns no recognizable food** — fallback to manual entry with user's text preserved
- **User scans wrong photo** (e.g., accidentally scans a non-food object) — AI returns empty or null confidence; user prompted to retake
- **Multiple scans of the same meal** — each scan is independent; user can save or discard each; no automatic deduplication
- **Barcode scanner on a device with no camera** — feature hidden entirely; no broken UI
- **Open Food Facts returns stale data** (e.g., product reformulated) — user can edit and save corrected version; custom_products table updated
- **User changes calorie target mid-day** — day totals displayed use new target; historical days show old target
- **Timezone change** (e.g., user travels across timezones) — meals logged today stay on "today" per user's current local timezone; historical days are anchored to the day they were logged
- **User logs meal at 11:59 PM, then 12:02 AM** — meals appear on their respective logged days
- **Smart-suggest has no qualifying meals** — block is hidden, no placeholder
- **User deletes a saved meal that's in smart-suggest** — suggestion disappears on next recompute
- **Hydration goal set too high/low** — app doesn't validate beyond sanity bounds (e.g., 10 oz/day or 300 oz/day both allowed)
- **User activates hydration, never logs, then deactivates** — widget hidden on next view; data preserved in case of reactivation

## 4.4.20 Analytics Events

Events captured in the Nutrition category:

- `nutrition_detail_viewed`
- `meal_log_started` (source: fab | unified_ai_input | chat | quick_log_suggestion)
- `meal_log_method` (method: text | voice | scan | barcode | saved_meal | smart_suggest | manual)
- `meal_log_completed` (method, via_ai: bool, edit_before_save: bool, latency_ms)
- `meal_log_abandoned` (stage)
- `meal_parse_succeeded` (method, item_count)
- `meal_parse_failed` (method, reason)
- `meal_scan_confidence` (confidence_score)
- `meal_premium_scan_used` (user_tier)
- `meal_edited_after_save` (fields_changed)
- `meal_deleted` (age_since_log)
- `saved_meal_created`
- `saved_meal_reused`
- `smart_suggest_viewed` (suggestion_count)
- `smart_suggest_accepted` (suggestion_position)
- `smart_suggest_rejected` (suggestion_position)
- `smart_suggest_ignored` (suggestion_position)
- `barcode_scanned` (found_in_of_facts: bool)
- `barcode_ai_fallback_used`
- `pantry_scan_used` (user_tier)
- `pantry_suggestion_logged`
- `hydration_activated` (via: manual_log | ai_parse | healthkit_sync | settings)
- `hydration_logged` (amount_oz, source)
- `hydration_goal_changed`
- `calorie_rollover_toggled` (new_state)
- `auto_adjust_toggled` (new_state)
- `auto_adjust_target_changed` (delta_percent)
- `macro_target_edited` (macro)

All events respect user privacy preferences (Section 6). No PII sent to analytics platforms.

---

# Section 4.5 — Finance Category

## 4.5.1 Overview

The Finance category is the Life Dashboard domain covering the user's financial life: transactions, spending, budgets, bills, savings rate, and financial goals. It is the most privacy-sensitive category in the app — financial transaction data is the most personal data Life Dashboard will ever handle.

Finance is also the category with the **strongest connection dependency** in the app. Unlike Fitness (which works with manual workout logging) and Nutrition (which works great with manual meal logging), Finance without a bank connection is effectively non-functional. A user who doesn't connect an account will see an elegant connection prompt rather than a functional Finance category.

The Finance category does not exist in the current Flask PWA. It is specified here for the first time as a v1 feature for the React Native build.

### The Plaid Dependency

Finance v1 is built entirely on top of Plaid. Plaid provides the data infrastructure for:

- **Auth** — account authentication and balance data
- **Transactions** — ongoing transaction sync from connected accounts
- **Identity** — account holder verification (used for fraud prevention, not displayed to user)

Plaid covers 12,000+ financial institutions in the US and provides a mature SDK for React Native. The technical details, authentication flow, and approval timeline are specified in Section 8 (Connections).

**Contingency:** If Plaid approval is not secured by v1 launch, the Finance category is deferred to v2 entirely. Section 4.5 assumes Plaid is available.

### Why Finance Matters To Life Dashboard's Value Proposition

Life Dashboard's core value proposition (Section 1.3) is unified context. Finance data makes the chatbot dramatically more useful:

- *"Can I afford this dinner tonight?"* — needs spending and budget context
- *"Should I cancel my gym membership?"* — needs recurring charges plus fitness activity
- *"Am I on track for my emergency fund?"* — needs account balances and savings rate
- *"Did my spending drop when I got serious about my weight loss?"* — needs transaction history plus body metrics

Without Finance, the chatbot is a fitness+nutrition assistant that can't answer the questions users actually ask themselves most. With Finance, Life Dashboard becomes the unified life dashboard it promises to be.

### Competitive Framing

The established finance apps (Copilot Money, Monarch Money, YNAB, Rocket Money) are mature and well-designed. Life Dashboard is not trying to beat them on finance depth — it's trying to match them at core functions while integrating financial context with Fitness, Nutrition, and Life.

The Finance category's quality bar: a user who currently uses Copilot Money should be willing to switch to Life Dashboard Finance **because** it's unified with their other life data, not because Life Dashboard has better categorization. If Life Dashboard Finance feels inferior to Copilot on basic functions, the value proposition fails.

### Success Metrics

- **Plaid connection rate** — percentage of Finance-viewed users who connect at least one account. Target: 70%+ (Finance is hard to use without a connection)
- **Transaction categorization accuracy** — percentage of auto-categorized transactions that user accepts without edit. Target: 85%+
- **Budget adherence** — percentage of users whose weekly spending is within their budget. Target: 60%+ (not a forced goal, just measurement)
- **Bill detection accuracy** — percentage of user-confirmed bills that were auto-detected vs manually added. Target: 80%+
- **Finance-context chatbot query rate** — percentage of chatbot interactions that reference finance data. Target: 15%+

## 4.5.2 Finance Detail View Layout

The Finance detail view is the home for everything financial. Users reach it by tapping the Finance card on the Home Dashboard or selecting the Finance tab from the bottom navigation.

### Top to bottom

**1. Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)**

- Back arrow (or tab indicator if arrived via bottom nav)
- Title: "Finance"
- "Hide amounts" toggle icon (eye icon — tap to toggle hide state)
- Profile icon (tap → Settings)
- Overflow menu (•••) with: Edit budget, Manage accounts, Export data, Help

**2. Hero block — Safe to Spend**

- Full-width card at top
- Content specified in Section 4.5.3 (hero is "Safe to Spend This Week")
- Primary visual: large "safe to spend" dollar amount

**3. This week overview**

- Spent this week (primary number)
- Budget this week
- Week progress bar (left-fill as week progresses Mon–Sun)
- Day-of-week indicator showing where in the week user is

**4. Active financial goals strip**

- Any finance-category goals from user's 3 active goals appear here
- Each shows: goal name, current progress, target, percent complete
- Up to 2 finance goals visible; more collapse to "+N more"
- Tap: opens goal detail

**5. Four subsystem cards (stacked vertically)**

- Order: Budget, Spending, Bills, Savings
- Each is a mini-view into its own subsystem
- Tap any card: expands to that subsystem's detail view
- Subsystem specs in sections 4.5.5–4.5.8

**6. Investments (placeholder for v1)**

- Small card at bottom: "Investment tracking coming in a future update"
- v2+ feature; placeholder establishes that the category will expand

**7. FAB**

- Same FAB pattern as Home Dashboard (Section 4.2.6) — opens the chatbot overlay
- The overlay contains two surfaces: the chatbot for finance and cross-domain questions, and shortcut buttons that open native logging flows directly
- Finance-specific shortcut buttons:
  - Add Transaction (opens the manual transaction card)
  - Mark Bill Paid (opens the bills list with paid toggle)
  - Check Balances (opens savings detail)
- Logging does not go through the chatbot. Transaction descriptions typed into the chat are redirected to the Add Transaction shortcut; the chatbot answers questions about budgets, safe-to-spend, bills, and cross-domain affordability.
- Full chatbot interaction model is specified in Section 4.7

**8. Bottom tab bar**

- Standard five-tab bar; Finance tab highlighted

### Layout Constraints

On iPhone SE, the hero block + this week overview fit above the fold. Subsystem cards require scrolling. The hero must answer "how's my money right now" without scrolling — critical for in-store, at-restaurant, "can I afford this" moments.

## 4.5.3 The Hero — Safe to Spend This Week

Per decision #1, the Finance category's hero metric is "Safe to Spend This Week" — a forward-looking actionable number that answers the most common day-to-day finance question.

### Visual Structure

**Center of the card:**

- **"Safe to Spend This Week"** label
- **Large dollar amount** — the calculated safe-to-spend value
- Number animates on change (e.g., after a transaction syncs, the number adjusts)
- Hide-amounts toggle replaces this with dots ($••••) when enabled

**The Math**

Safe to spend is calculated deterministically:

```
weekly_budget = user_configured_weekly_budget
spent_this_week = sum of transactions Mon-today (categorized as spending, not transfers)
bills_remaining_this_week = sum of detected/added bills due before Sunday that are unpaid
safe_to_spend = weekly_budget - spent_this_week - bills_remaining_this_week
```

**Visual state:**

- Green: $50+ remaining
- Amber: $0–$50 remaining
- Red: negative (over budget)
- Hide-state: all dots regardless of color

**Below the number:**

- **Budget breakdown equation:**
  - "$800 budget − $412 spent − $180 bills = $208 safe to spend"
  - Numbers are tappable: tap "$412 spent" to see this week's transactions; tap "$180 bills" to see upcoming bills; tap "$800 budget" to edit
- **Day of week context:**
  - "3 days left this week"
  - Helps user calibrate: $208 for 3 days is different from $208 for 6 days

**Above the number (small, secondary):**

- Current week date range (e.g., "Week of Apr 14")
- Next week's budget note if different ("Next week's budget: $800")

### Interactive Details

- Tap hero card: opens Budget subsystem detail
- Long-press: shows calculation debug view (which transactions counted, which excluded, bills detected)
- Swipe left/right on hero: navigate to previous/next week

### Monthly Context

Per decision #3 — both weekly and monthly budgets, weekly is hero.

A small secondary metric below the hero shows monthly context:

- "This month: $1,847 of $3,200 (58%)"
- Progress bar
- Tap: opens monthly budget detail

The monthly number keeps the bigger picture visible without distracting from the actionable weekly hero.

### Data Flow

All inputs come from:
- `user_budget` table (user-set weekly and monthly targets)
- `transactions` table (Plaid sync + manual entries)
- `bills` table (auto-detected + user-added)

No AI calls in the hero calculation. Pure deterministic arithmetic, rendering in <200ms from cache on every dashboard view.

## 4.5.4 Privacy Posture and Data Handling

Finance is the most privacy-sensitive category in the app. Every design decision in Section 4.5 is filtered through the privacy principles established in Design Principle 3.5 (Privacy As A Feature, Not A Footnote).

### What Gets Sent To Anthropic (AI Calls)

Per Design Principle 3.5 and the user-facing commitment in Section 4.1.2 ("We never sell it, never train AI on it, and you can delete everything instantly"):

**Allowed to send to Anthropic:**
- Merchant name (e.g., "STARBUCKS #1234 SEATTLE WA" — shortened for privacy)
- Transaction amount
- Plaid-provided category hint
- Transaction date
- User profile context (calorie target, goals, etc. — not finance-specific)

**Never sent to Anthropic:**
- Full bank account numbers
- Routing numbers
- Card numbers (full or partial)
- Social Security Numbers
- Account login credentials (these never leave Plaid's systems anyway)
- Bank name combined with balance (account → balance mapping stays local)
- Full unredacted transaction descriptions if they contain location data deemed sensitive

**Redaction step before AI calls:**

Before sending any transaction data to the AI categorization call (Section 4.5.11), a server-side redaction function runs:

1. Truncate merchant names to remove likely account numbers and store IDs (e.g., "STARBUCKS #1234 SEATTLE" → "STARBUCKS SEATTLE")
2. Strip credit card last-four digits that some merchants include in descriptions
3. Remove any alphanumeric strings longer than 12 characters (likely sensitive IDs)

The redacted payload is what reaches Anthropic. The original unredacted data stays in Life Dashboard's database.

### Encryption At Rest

- **Plaid access tokens:** Encrypted with AWS KMS-managed key; stored in `plaid_tokens` table
- **Transaction data:** Stored unencrypted in Postgres (industry standard — Copilot, Monarch, YNAB all store transactions unencrypted in their databases, relying on infrastructure security)
- **Account balances:** Stored unencrypted, updated on Plaid sync
- **User's plaintext balances in memory:** Flushed from memory on logout

### Hide Amounts Toggle (Per Decision #9)

The Finance header has an eye icon that toggles amount visibility. When enabled:

- All dollar amounts throughout the app replaced with dots ($1,847 → $•••• or $••)
- Persists across sessions until user toggles off
- Transaction names and categories remain visible (knowing you spent at Starbucks is not as sensitive as the amount)
- Account balances show dots
- Hero "Safe to Spend" shows dots
- Bills show dots

When disabled:
- All amounts visible normally

**Why persistent:** Users in public settings (coffee shops, offices, public transit) can flip it on once and trust it stays on. Auto-hide on inactivity was considered but rejected — it creates surprising UI state.

**Accessibility:** VoiceOver reads "hidden" for hidden amounts so screen reader users don't hear the actual dollar values when the screen is intentionally hidden.

### Data Deletion

Per Design Principle 3.5: one-tap account deletion with 30-day purge.

Finance-specific deletion details:
- Deleting the user's Life Dashboard account revokes all Plaid access tokens within 24 hours
- All transaction history purged within 30 days
- Plaid is notified via their API to sever the connection on their end
- User can also disconnect a specific account (not delete Life Dashboard account) via Manage Accounts → Disconnect

### Read-Only Access

Life Dashboard uses Plaid's read-only data products (Auth, Transactions). Life Dashboard cannot:
- Initiate transfers between user accounts
- Pay bills
- Make purchases
- Modify the user's bank data in any way

This is a deliberate product decision to minimize risk surface. The user's financial data is for awareness and decision-making, not for Life Dashboard to act on.

## 4.5.5 Account Connection and Management

The first thing a Finance user does is connect their bank(s). The flow balances speed of setup with clarity about what's happening.

### Entry Points

- Onboarding Connection Connect (Section 4.1.7) — user can connect Plaid during onboarding
- Finance category empty state — "Connect your bank to activate Finance"
- Settings → Connected Accounts → Add Account

### Connection Flow

**Step 1: Explainer**

- Full-screen card before launching Plaid:
  - "Connect your bank to unlock Finance"
  - Three bullet points:
    - "Life Dashboard uses Plaid, trusted by Chase, Wells Fargo, and 12,000+ banks"
    - "Life Dashboard has read-only access — we can see balances and transactions, not move money"
    - "Your login credentials never touch Life Dashboard servers"
  - Primary button: "Connect via Plaid"
  - Secondary link: "Why Plaid?" (opens brief help article)

**Step 2: Plaid Link (native SDK)**

- Plaid's own branded UI takes over
- User selects bank
- User authenticates (OAuth where available, credentials for smaller banks)
- User selects accounts to connect (checking, savings, credit cards)
- Multi-factor authentication if bank requires
- Returns to Life Dashboard with connection success

**Step 3: Initial Sync**

- Loading screen: "Setting up your Finance dashboard..."
- Plaid syncs the last 2 years of transactions (~15-30 seconds typical)
- AI categorization runs on transactions with missing categories (batch job)
- Bills auto-detection runs on recurring patterns (deterministic algorithm)

**Step 4: First Finance View**

- User arrives at fully populated Finance detail view
- Confetti-light animation (per premium-feel design principle)
- Welcome toast: "Finance is ready. Review your detected bills and set your budget to get started."
- Two suggested actions on first view:
  - "Set your weekly budget" — opens budget setup
  - "Review detected bills" — shows auto-detected bills for user confirmation

### Managing Connected Accounts

- Settings → Connected Accounts
- List of all connected institutions with connection status
- Per-account toggle: include in Finance calculations (e.g., user might have a joint account they don't want included in personal spending)
- Per-account actions: Sync now, Disconnect, Rename
- Errors surface here with reconnection prompts (tokens expire, banks require re-auth)

### Multi-Account Handling

Users often have multiple accounts across multiple banks:
- One checking account at Chase
- One savings at Ally
- One credit card at Capital One
- Maybe an investment account at Fidelity (displayed as placeholder for v2+)

The Finance detail view aggregates across all included accounts:
- Spending = all transactions flagged as spending across all accounts
- Savings balance = sum of savings account balances

User can filter any view by account if they want ("show me only Chase transactions this week"). Filter state doesn't persist — always resets to all accounts on next view.

### Data Persistence

New tables in v1:
- `plaid_tokens` — user_id, institution_id, access_token (encrypted), item_id, connected_at, last_sync_at, status
- `connected_accounts` — user_id, plaid_account_id, institution_name, account_name, account_type (checking/savings/credit/loan), mask (last 4 digits), is_included, nickname, created_at
- `transactions` — user_id, plaid_transaction_id, account_id, transaction_date, amount, merchant_name, plaid_category, user_category, is_pending, is_transfer, is_hidden, notes, created_at, updated_at
- `bills` — user_id, bill_name, amount, frequency (monthly/weekly/biannual/annual), typical_day_of_month, next_due_date, account_id (optional), is_active, is_user_added (vs auto-detected), created_at
- `user_budget` — user_id, weekly_amount, monthly_amount, category_budgets_json (nullable if user is in simple mode), created_at, updated_at

## 4.5.6 Budget Subsystem

Per decision #2: simple total budget is default; optional category breakdown available for users who want more specificity.

### Subsystem Card On Finance Detail View

- Title: "Budget"
- Weekly: spent/budget (e.g., "$412 of $800")
- Monthly: spent/budget (e.g., "$1,847 of $3,200")
- Trend indicator: "On track" / "Over-pacing" / "Under-pacing" (deterministic based on current progress vs day-of-week or day-of-month)
- Tap: opens Budget detail view

### Budget Detail View

**Simple Mode (default):**

- Weekly budget and monthly budget displayed prominently
- Current week / current month progress
- Budget setting controls:
  - Weekly budget: numeric field with +/- buttons
  - Monthly budget: numeric field with +/- buttons
  - Auto-sync weekly → monthly (4.33 × weekly) — user can override
- Spending breakdown for current week/month:
  - Horizontal bar chart of spending by category (Plaid-derived)
  - Each bar: category, amount, % of total spending
- "Switch to Category Budgets" button to expand to Mode B

**Category Mode (optional expansion):**

- Each category gets its own budget line
- Categories: Groceries, Dining & Drinks, Transportation, Entertainment, Shopping, Bills, Health, Travel, Other
- User sets budget per category; sum constrains to total budget
- Each category row shows: name, budget, spent, remaining, progress bar
- Over-budget categories highlighted
- "Switch back to Simple" toggles back

**Historical Budget Performance:**

- Month-over-month view: did you hit last 6 months' budgets?
- Simple bar chart with budget line overlay

### Budget Setup Flow

**First-Time Setup (triggered by first Finance detail view):**

- Dialog: "Set your weekly spending budget"
- Smart default: Life Dashboard analyzes last 3 months of transactions and suggests a budget ~10% lower than user's recent average
- User can accept, adjust, or skip
- Follow-up: "This sets your monthly budget to ~$X" with ability to override

**Ongoing Edits:**

- Always editable from Budget detail view
- No confirmation prompts for small changes
- Historical budgets preserved (changing current budget doesn't rewrite history)

### Budget Math Details

- Week starts Monday, ends Sunday
- Month uses calendar month boundaries
- Transactions counted toward budget: all "spending" transactions (not transfers, not refunds, not income)
- Bills counted toward budget separately in the hero calculation
- Pending transactions: included in budget calculations (user might see slight adjustments when they clear)

### Friction Reduction

- Smart default budget eliminates "blank slate" problem
- Auto-sync between weekly and monthly prevents user from having to do math
- Simple mode is the default; category mode is opt-in

### AI Usage

None in the budget subsystem. All math is deterministic.

## 4.5.7 Spending Subsystem

The Spending subsystem is where users review transactions: where money went, what patterns exist, what they can learn.

### Subsystem Card On Finance Detail View

- Title: "Spending"
- This week's spending total
- Top 3 categories by dollar amount this week
- Largest transaction this week (with merchant)
- Tap: opens Spending detail view

### Spending Detail View

**Tabs:**

1. **Transactions** — chronological list of all transactions
2. **Categories** — spending breakdown by category
3. **Merchants** — top merchants by spending
4. **Trends** — month-over-month spending charts

**Transactions tab:**

- List of all transactions (chronological, newest first)
- Each row: date, merchant, amount, category chip
- Pending transactions visually distinct (slightly dimmed)
- Filter bar: by category, merchant, amount range, date range
- Search: search merchant names and notes
- Tap row: transaction detail view with edit options

**Transaction Detail View:**

- Full merchant name (unredacted)
- Amount
- Date
- Account (which card/bank)
- Category (editable)
- Notes field (user-added)
- Tags (user-added, free-form)
- Mark as transfer (excludes from spending budget)
- Mark as refund (reverses toward budget)
- Hide from dashboard (still in history, excluded from spending calculations)
- Similar transactions: "You've also spent at this merchant X times in the last 30 days — tap to see all"

**Categories tab:**

- Horizontal bar chart of spending by category (this week or this month)
- Each category: name, amount, % of total, transaction count
- Tap category: filtered transactions view showing only that category

**Merchants tab:**

- Top 10 merchants by spending this month
- Each merchant: name, total spent, transaction count, avg per transaction
- Identifies recurring merchants automatically and flags potential subscriptions

**Trends tab:**

- Monthly spending line chart (last 12 months for all users; Pro extends to long-term archived history)
- Category-over-time: how has your grocery spending changed over 6 months?
- Comparisons: "vs last month" / "vs same month last year" (Pro)

### Transaction Editing

Users can correct any transaction:

- Change category — AI categorization learns from corrections
- Rename merchant (local only — displayed version, not the raw data)
- Add notes
- Add tags
- Mark as transfer (to exclude from spending)

Changes are persisted per-user. They do not affect Plaid's data or other users.

### AI Usage In Spending

- **Transaction categorization** — specified in Section 4.5.11 below. Fills gaps where Plaid doesn't provide a category or where user corrections suggest the default is wrong.
- No other AI calls in spending views themselves (just display).

## 4.5.8 Bills Subsystem

Per decision #5: both auto-detection from Plaid transactions and manual user entry.

### Subsystem Card On Finance Detail View

- Title: "Bills"
- Next bill due (name, amount, date)
- Total due this week
- Overdue bills count (if any, highlighted red)
- Tap: opens Bills detail view

### Bills Detail View

**Sections:**

1. **Upcoming This Week** — bills due within the next 7 days
2. **Upcoming This Month** — bills due within the current month
3. **Detected Bills (Unconfirmed)** — auto-detected that user hasn't confirmed yet
4. **All Bills** — full list, editable

**Upcoming sections show each bill as:**
- Bill name (e.g., "Rent" or "Netflix")
- Amount
- Due date (with day count: "Due in 3 days" / "Due tomorrow" / "Overdue by 2 days")
- Visual status:
  - Green: more than 7 days out
  - Amber: within 7 days
  - Red: overdue
- Tap: bill detail view with edit/delete options
- Mark as Paid button

**Detected Bills (Unconfirmed) section:**

Auto-detection produces a list of candidate bills the user hasn't yet confirmed:
- Each shows detection reasoning: "Detected: $15.99 from Netflix, appeared monthly for the last 6 months"
- Confirm → adds to active bills
- Reject → removes from detected list, doesn't re-detect for 6 months
- Edit → lets user adjust before confirming

### Auto-Detection Algorithm (Deterministic, No AI)

Bills are detected by analyzing transaction patterns:

1. Group transactions by merchant
2. For each merchant, look at amounts and frequencies
3. A recurring bill is identified when:
   - Same merchant appears at least 3 times
   - Amount is consistent (within 5% across instances)
   - Frequency is regular (monthly ±5 days, or weekly ±2 days, or biannual, annual)
4. Compute next expected due date based on the pattern

**Detection runs:**
- Once after initial Plaid sync (catches all established bills)
- Once daily after that (catches newly established bills and adjusts existing ones)
- Triggered by user on demand via "Re-scan for bills" in Bills detail view

### Manual Bill Entry

- "Add Bill" button on Bills detail view
- Form fields:
  - Bill name (required)
  - Amount (required)
  - Frequency (monthly / weekly / biannual / annual / custom)
  - Next due date (required)
  - Linked account (optional — for tracking which account pays it)
  - Notes (optional)
- Save: adds to bills, shows in upcoming sections

### Mark As Paid

When a user marks a bill as paid:
- Bill advances to next due date based on frequency
- If the bill is linked to Plaid transactions and the detection algorithm was tracking it, the transaction is auto-matched as the payment
- User is thanked briefly and the bill disappears from upcoming until next due date

### Bill Reminders

Per decision #10: notifications deferred to Section 4.9. Section 4.5 notes that bills feed into the notification system — an upcoming bill within 3 days should be a candidate for a reminder. Detailed notification rules in 4.9.

### AI Usage In Bills

None. Bill detection is deterministic. Bill payment matching is deterministic.

## 4.5.9 Savings Subsystem

Savings tracking is a secondary Finance feature focused on savings rate, balance trends, and goal-linked progress. Net worth tracking is deferred from v1 because v1's Plaid scope (Transactions only) does not include liabilities (student loans, mortgages), and net worth without debts is not meaningfully net worth. Users with significant debts would see a misleadingly inflated number.

### Subsystem Card On Finance Detail View

- Title: "Savings"
- Primary metric: this month's savings rate (% of income saved)
- Secondary metric: total balance across connected savings accounts
- Tap: opens Savings detail view

### Savings Detail View

**Savings Rate Section:**

- This month's savings rate: (income - spending) / income
- Income detection: Plaid categorizes direct deposits as "income" — we use that
- Historical savings rate chart (last 12 months)
- Target savings rate (user-configurable, default 20%)

**Balance History:**

- Per-account balance over time across connected savings accounts
- Line chart showing each account's balance day-over-day
- Identifies trends (e.g., savings account growing steadily, checking oscillating)
- Data source: Plaid Transactions product provides cached daily account balances — sufficient for trend display without requiring separate Balance product billing

**Savings Goals Integration:**

- Savings-type goals from Section 4.10 surface here with their progress bars
- Emergency fund goal displayed prominently if set
- Tap a goal to deep-link to its detail view

### v1 Scope Limitations

**Why no net worth at v1:**

Net worth calculation requires complete visibility into both assets AND liabilities. Life Dashboard's v1 Plaid scope is Transactions only, which does not include:
- Student loans (requires Plaid Liabilities product — v1.1+)
- Mortgages (requires Plaid Liabilities product — v1.1+)
- Investment accounts (requires Plaid Investments product — v2+)

A "net worth" number using only checking + savings + credit card balances while ignoring student loans and mortgages would be misleading for most users in our target demographic. Rather than ship a partial calculation with disclaimers, v1 focuses on savings rate and balance trends — metrics that are complete and actionable from the data we have.

**v1.1+ roadmap:**

- Plaid Liabilities integration → adds student loan and mortgage balances → enables net worth
- Plaid Investments integration → adds brokerage and retirement balances → completes net worth picture
- Manual asset/debt entry (real estate, vehicles, crypto) → completes net worth for items Plaid doesn't cover

When these land, the Savings subsystem expands to include a Net Worth section.

### Data Persistence

- `account_balances_history` — user_id, account_id, balance_date, balance — polled daily from Plaid Transactions
- Manual asset/debt tables deferred to v1.1+ when Liabilities lands

## 4.5.10 Finance Score (Framework Only)

The Finance category produces a single 0–100 score surfaced on the Home Dashboard Finance card and the top of the Finance detail view. The exact math is specified in Section 9 (Scoring Engine).

### Framework Principles

- **Deterministic only** — no AI in scoring
- **Finance-domain data only** — spending, budget, bills, savings, not cross-domain
- **Graceful degradation** — users without certain data sources get scores based on available signals
- **Actionable signals only** — the Finance score rewards things the user can actually influence (staying in budget, paying bills on time, saving) and doesn't penalize things they can't (income level, existing debt load)

### What Contributes (Directionally)

- **Budget adherence** — staying within weekly and monthly budgets
- **Bill on-time rate** — paying bills by due date
- **Savings rate** — percentage of income saved (if detected)
- **Spending consistency** — not having weeks of wildly oscillating spending
- **Emergency fund ratio** — savings balance relative to monthly spending (users who've set an emergency fund goal)

Exact weights deferred to Section 9.

### What Does Not Contribute

- Absolute net worth (reward for being rich — not actionable)
- Income level
- Number of accounts connected
- Credit score (Life Dashboard doesn't pull credit data)

## 4.5.11 Transaction Categorization

Per decision #4: hybrid approach — Plaid categories default, AI fills gaps, user overrides anything.

### Flow

**Step 1: Plaid provides a category.** For roughly 70% of transactions, Plaid returns a category hint. We display it immediately.

**Step 2: AI fills gaps.** For transactions Plaid couldn't categorize or where the category is suspiciously generic ("Other"), a background job sends the transaction to the AI for categorization.

**Step 3: User corrections update future similar transactions.** If the user manually re-categorizes a transaction (e.g., "this is Groceries not Shopping"), the system stores the correction and applies it to future transactions from the same merchant.

### AI Categorization Call

- **Function (new in v1):** `categorize_transaction`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Input (redacted per Section 4.5.4):** Merchant name (redacted), amount, Plaid category hint (if any), day of week, user's historical corrections for similar merchants (if any)
- **Output:** Structured JSON with category, confidence, reasoning (brief)
- **Categories available:** Groceries, Dining & Drinks, Transportation, Entertainment, Shopping, Bills, Health, Travel, Other, Income, Transfer
- **Cost per call:** ~$0.001
- **Target latency:** <2 seconds (background, not user-facing)
- **Counts against paywall:** No (background operation, not user-invoked)

### Why This Doesn't Count Against Paywall

Categorization is continuous background work. A heavy spender might have 200+ transactions per month, many of which need categorization. Counting each one against a daily paywall would:
- Make Core tier effectively unusable for high-transaction-volume users
- Create adversarial incentives (users avoiding connecting accounts to save queries)

Instead: AI categorization is part of the infrastructure cost of the Finance category, not a user-invoked AI action. Cost stays bounded by rate limits on the Plaid sync (max N transactions categorized per user per day).

### User Correction Learning (Rules-Based, Not AI)

When a user changes a transaction's category:
- A rule is created: `user_category_rules` table with user_id, merchant_name_redacted, category
- Future transactions from this merchant for this user automatically use the corrected category
- AI categorization is skipped entirely for future transactions where a user rule exists

This means:
- The system gets progressively smarter about the user's preferences
- Repeated AI calls for the same merchant are avoided (cost optimization)
- The user's categorization is respected even when it differs from common patterns

### Categorization Confidence

Each transaction has an implicit confidence:
- User-rule based: 100% (user said so)
- Plaid-provided: 90% (generally reliable)
- AI-categorized: per the AI's output confidence (typically 70-95%)

Low-confidence categorizations (<70%) get a subtle visual flag ("?" icon) encouraging user to verify. High-confidence categorizations have no flag.

## 4.5.12 Manual Transaction Entry

While transaction data comes almost entirely from Plaid, users occasionally need to add transactions manually:
- Cash purchases
- Transactions from accounts they chose not to connect
- Splitting a shared purchase (e.g., "I paid $60 but split $30 with roommate")

### Entry Flow

- FAB → opens chatbot → "Add transaction" shortcut button (per decision #11 pattern)
- Or: Spending detail view → "+" button → "Add manual transaction"
- Form fields:
  - Merchant (text, optional)
  - Amount (required)
  - Date (default today)
  - Category (picker with all categories)
  - Account (optional, defaults to "Cash")
  - Notes (optional)
- Save: adds to transactions table with source="manual"

### How Manual Transactions Interact With Budget

- Manual transactions count toward spending and budget calculations (same as Plaid transactions)
- They're clearly distinguishable in the transaction list (small "manual" icon)
- They can be edited and deleted freely

### Friction Reduction

Manual entry is not promoted. The app's posture is "connect accounts for full automation." Users who do need manual entry have a fast path; users who don't never see the feature prominently.

## 4.5.13 Financial Goals (Framework Only)

Per decision #8 and Section 4.10 (Goals System), the following finance goals are in the v1 goal library:

1. **Emergency fund** — save $N in an emergency fund
2. **Pay off credit card** — credit card balance $N → $0
3. **Pay off specific debt** — a user-named debt balance → $0
4. **Save for down payment** — save $N by date X for a specific purchase
5. **Save for vacation** — save $N by date X
6. **Save for wedding** — save $N by date X
7. **Monthly spending limit** — keep spending under $N per month
8. **Target savings rate** — save N% of income monthly
9. **Budget streak** — stay under budget for N consecutive weeks

Each goal has:
- Automatic progress tracking from connected accounts
- Configurable target and (for time-bound goals) deadline
- Progress visualization on the goal detail view
- Completion celebration per Section 4.10 specification

Full goal library specifications (UI, flow, celebration, social card) live in Section 4.10. Finance-specific algorithmic details:

**Emergency fund progress:** Current savings balance toward target. If user has designated a specific account as "emergency fund," use that account's balance; otherwise, use total savings balances.

**Debt payoff progress:** Plaid-reported balance compared to starting balance at goal creation. Additional manual payments from Plaid-detected transactions count toward progress.

**Savings rate target:** Monthly calculation — (income this month - spending this month) / income this month. Must meet target for the goal to be marked as hit.

### Relationship to Chatbot

The chatbot (Section 4.7) has visibility into all active finance goals via GoalsContext and FinanceContext. Questions like "how am I doing on my emergency fund?" or "what's my credit card payoff progress?" load the relevant goal state and current finance data, and generate a specific, numbers-backed answer. The chatbot does not create, modify, or delete goals — that stays in the goal library flow.

## 4.5.14 Connection Dependencies

Finance is entirely dependent on Plaid for v1. No other connections are planned for v1.

| Connection | Role | v1 Status |
|---|---|---|
| Plaid Transactions | Core product: transaction sync, account list, cached daily balance | Required |
| Plaid Liabilities | Student loan and mortgage data (for future net worth) | v1.1+ |
| Plaid Investments | Investment account data (for future net worth) | v2+ |
| Plaid Auth | Real-time ACH auth (not needed — Life Dashboard doesn't move money) | Not used |
| Plaid Identity | Identity verification (not needed — Clerk handles identity) | Not used |
| Plaid Balance | Real-time on-demand balance (not needed — cached daily balance from Transactions sufficient) | Not used |
| Manual entry | Fallback only | Supported but not promoted |

Plaid Transactions alone provides transaction history, account metadata, and cached daily balances. Life Dashboard does not require any other Plaid product at v1 because Life Dashboard is a read-only personal finance dashboard, not a payments or money-movement platform. Plaid authentication details, approval timeline, and technical connection specs are fully covered in Section 8.

## 4.5.15 AI Usage In Finance

Summary of all AI calls in the Finance category:

| Call | Where Used | Model | Cost/Call | Counted Against Paywall? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| categorize_transaction | Background categorization of uncategorized or corrected transactions | Haiku 4.5 | $0.001 | No (infrastructure) |
| Chatbot finance queries | User-initiated chatbot questions about finance | Haiku/Sonnet (per 4.7) | Varies | Yes (counts as chatbot interaction) |

The Finance category itself has minimal direct AI usage. Most AI interaction with finance data happens through the chatbot (Section 4.7), where the user explicitly asks a question and the chatbot uses finance context to answer.

### Cost Modeling Per User

**Daily AI cost from Finance (estimated):**
- 5 transactions categorized in background: 5 × $0.001 = $0.005
- Total daily Finance AI cost: ~$0.005

Very low compared to Nutrition (~$0.020) because most AI usage is chatbot-driven, and those costs are attributed to the Chatbot section.

## 4.5.16 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Specific reductions applied to the Finance category:

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Manual transaction entry | Automated via Plaid; manual is fallback only |
| Manual bill tracking | Auto-detection from recurring transactions |
| Category setup from scratch | Pre-populated categories; Plaid auto-categorizes |
| Budget configuration | Smart default based on recent spending history |
| Re-categorizing same merchant repeatedly | User correction creates rule for future transactions |
| Setting weekly + monthly budgets separately | Auto-sync between them (user can override) |
| Privacy concerns in public settings | Hide amounts toggle |
| Connecting multiple banks requiring re-authentication | Plaid Link handles auth once per institution |
| Unclear what AI sees from bank data | Redacted transaction data sent to AI; user-visible audit trail in Settings |
| Budget category breakdown being too much for most users | Simple mode default; category mode optional |

## 4.5.17 Accessibility Requirements

- All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast (4.5:1 for body text)
- All tappable targets: 44pt iOS, 48dp Android minimum
- VoiceOver and TalkBack tested end-to-end for Plaid connection flow
- Hide amounts: VoiceOver reads "hidden" for dollar values when toggle is on
- Charts have accessible text alternatives (trend direction + key values)
- Color state indicators paired with text labels or icons
- Plaid Link itself has Plaid's own accessibility certifications
- Transaction amounts read with currency and unit by screen readers ("$1,847 dollars")

## 4.5.18 Edge Cases and Error Handling

- **Plaid connection fails mid-setup** — user returns to Finance entry, error toast explains, option to retry
- **Plaid token expires** — subtle banner on Finance view: "Your bank connection needs refreshing"; tap to re-auth via Plaid Link
- **Plaid sync returns duplicate transactions** — deduplicated by transaction_id server-side
- **Pending transaction posts as a different amount** — transaction record updated with posted amount; budget calculations adjust automatically
- **User disconnects an account mid-month** — transactions up to disconnection preserved; budget calculations continue with remaining accounts
- **User reconnects a previously-disconnected account** — transactions merge with existing history; no duplicates
- **User has multiple accounts with identical names at same bank** — distinguished by last-4 digits (mask)
- **Large purchase pushes user over weekly budget** — hero shows negative safe-to-spend; visual shifts to red; no notification interruption (per decision #10, notifications deferred to 4.9)
- **Refund transaction from a previous week** — counts as negative spending in the week of the refund, not the week of the original purchase
- **Transfer between user's own accounts** — auto-detected as transfer (Plaid generally identifies these), excluded from spending; if misidentified as spending, user can mark as transfer via transaction detail
- **Income detected as spending** — auto-corrected (Plaid categorizes deposits as income); user can flag if still wrong
- **Timezone change affects which day transactions are on** — transactions stay on their original logged date (per Plaid), not adjusted to user's current timezone
- **User changes weekly budget mid-week** — new budget applies to current week; previous week's performance preserved with old budget
- **Plaid reports a balance change but no transaction** — balance history updated; no transaction created; user can add manual transaction to explain if desired
- **Multiple bills at same merchant with different amounts** — auto-detection treats as separate bills if amounts differ by more than 5%
- **Bill amount drifts over time (e.g., electricity bill)** — auto-detection tolerates 5% variance; larger changes trigger re-detection
- **User has no income in Plaid** — savings rate calculation disabled; goal progress for savings-rate goal shows as "insufficient data"

## 4.5.19 Analytics Events

Events captured in the Finance category:

- `finance_detail_viewed`
- `plaid_connection_started`
- `plaid_connection_completed` (institution_count, account_count)
- `plaid_connection_failed` (reason)
- `plaid_account_toggled` (account_id, new_include_state)
- `plaid_account_disconnected`
- `transaction_viewed`
- `transaction_category_edited` (old_category, new_category)
- `transaction_marked_transfer`
- `transaction_hidden`
- `manual_transaction_added`
- `budget_created` (weekly, monthly)
- `budget_edited` (field, old_value, new_value)
- `budget_category_mode_enabled`
- `budget_category_mode_disabled`
- `bill_auto_detected`
- `bill_confirmed_from_detection`
- `bill_rejected_from_detection`
- `bill_manually_added`
- `bill_marked_paid`
- `bill_edited`
- `savings_detail_viewed`
- `manual_asset_added`
- `manual_debt_added`
- `net_worth_info_tooltip_viewed`
- `hide_amounts_toggled` (new_state)
- `finance_ai_categorization_succeeded` (latency_ms, confidence)
- `finance_ai_categorization_failed` (reason)
- `finance_goal_created` (goal_type)
- `finance_goal_progress_viewed`
- `subsystem_card_tapped` (subsystem: budget | spending | bills | savings)

All events respect user privacy preferences (Section 6). Dollar amounts are never sent to analytics — events record actions, not financial values.

---

# Section 4.6 — Time Category

## 4.6.1 Overview

The Time category is the most novel surface in Life Dashboard. It is the tab where the *shape of the user's day* is measured, visualized, and scored — derived almost entirely from passive platform data the user did not consciously produce. Users reach the Time category by tapping the Time card on the Home Dashboard or selecting the Time tab from the bottom navigation.

Time is also the most connection-dependent category in the app. Calendar, email, tasks, screen time, and passive location all flow into Time. A user with nothing connected sees a Time tab that clearly shows its potential but isn't yet functional. A user with the key connections granted sees a dashboard that reflects how they actually lived their day — minute by minute, with no user data entry required.

### What The Time Category Is (And Isn't)

**Time IS:**
- A measurement of how coherent the user's day was (Time Score, §4.6.15)
- The unified visualization of how the user actually spent their time (Day Timeline, §4.6.5)
- The productivity subsystem (Calendar + Email + Tasks) that gives users native control over their day's scaffolding
- The attention subsystem (Screen Time + Location) that surfaces where attention and presence went
- The Time Patterns surface — deterministic aggregations that show the user's own rhythm

**Time is NOT:**
- A productivity app replacement (users still manage tasks/calendars/emails in their source apps; Time ingests them)
- A coach that tells users what to do
- A surveillance tool (privacy is explicit, consent is explicit, all signals are opt-in per §4.6.18)
- A mood-check-in app (explicitly killed during hardening; not returning)
- A catch-all "other" bucket — every Time signal earns its place with a defined data source and a scoring contribution

### The Design Principle That Governs Time

One principle dominates Time category design: **the dashboard is a mirror, not a coach.**

Time shows the user what their day actually looked like. It surfaces patterns, timelines, and aggregations from real data. It does NOT generate unsolicited AI advice or motivational content. Users who want interpretation ask the chatbot (Section 4.7). Users who just want to see their day get the mirror.

This is a departure from most wellness/productivity apps that push unsolicited AI content. Life Dashboard respects that users can draw their own conclusions from accurate data.

### Success Metrics

- **Time connections connected rate** — percentage of users with at least two Time-scoring connections (HealthKit sleep, Screen Time, calendar, CoreLocation). Target: 70%+
- **Day Timeline engagement** — percentage of users who view the Day Timeline at least once per week. Target: 50%+
- **Time Score populated rate** — percentage of users for whom the Time Score displays a number (≥2 signals with data per §4.2.4). Target: 80%+ within two weeks of install
- **Cross-category goal engagement** — percentage of users with goals in 2+ categories simultaneously. Target: 30%+

## 4.6.2 Why Time Is The Connection Hub

Time is structurally different from the other three categories. Fitness, Nutrition, and Finance each track a well-defined domain with user-logged events at its center — workouts, meals, transactions. Time measures what happens *between and around* those events: when the user sleeps, how fragmented their attention is, where they went, how their calendar filled up, whether their daily rhythm held.

### The Time Category's Unique Posture

Every other category score is about **what the user did**. Fitness scores workouts. Nutrition scores meals. Finance scores spending.

Time scores **the shape of the day itself** — something the user doesn't log, can't easily game, and emerges from data they didn't consciously produce.

- The user doesn't tell Life Dashboard when they slept. HealthKit does.
- The user doesn't tell Life Dashboard where they went. CoreLocation does.
- The user doesn't tell Life Dashboard when they picked up their phone. Screen Time does.
- The user doesn't tell Life Dashboard when meetings happened. Calendar does.

The Time Score therefore measures **day-structure integrity** — objectively derived, personally baselined, un-gameable. The only way to "win" at Time is to actually live a coherent day: sleep consistently, focus, go to the places you planned to go, maintain a rhythm. Gaming the signals requires doing the thing the signals measure.

### Time-Domain Only (v1.18 architecture lock)

Earlier versions of this PRD specified the Life score (Time's predecessor) as using Life-domain + cross-domain inputs. v1.18 reversed this: **the Time score uses only Time-domain signals**. Cross-domain aggregation happens exclusively at the Overall Score level via the weighted category rollup (§4.2.4). This keeps every category score internally coherent ("Time score went down because your sleep rhythm broke and your phone pickups spiked") and prevents double-counting of goal adherence (which is already part of each category's own signals and of the Overall Score).

The practical upshot: Day Timeline, Time Patterns, and the Goals Dashboard still surface cross-domain *visualizations* in the Time detail view — but none of those visualizations flow into the Time Score. The Time Score is derived entirely from:
1. Sleep Regularity (HealthKit / Health Connect sleep timing consistency)
2. Attention Fragmentation (Screen Time pickup/session patterns)
3. Location Intentionality (CoreLocation place-time patterns vs personal baseline)
4. Schedule Density Balance (Calendar meeting-hour load vs personal baseline)
5. Rhythm Adherence (cross-domain anchor timestamps — but used here as *timing data only*, not as cross-domain achievement signals)

Full Time Score specification: §4.6.15.

### Why This Architecture Makes Sense

Three reasons:

1. **Clean separation of concerns.** Domain scores are fast, interpretable, and owned by their category. Overall Score owns the cross-domain aggregation layer via weighted rollup.

2. **Graceful degradation.** If a user disconnects all Time connections, Fitness/Nutrition/Finance still work normally. If a user only has Time connections connected, Time scores on its own merits.

3. **Product clarity.** When a user opens Fitness, they get fitness. When they open Time, they get the shape of their day. No confusion about "which tab has what."

### The Time Tab As "Day Command Center"

In the mental model the app establishes, Time is where the user goes when they ask:
- "What does my day actually look like?"
- "How is my sleep rhythm holding up?"
- "Where is my attention going?"
- "How fragmented is my focus?"
- "What patterns am I falling into?"
- "How am I progressing across all my goals?" (Goals Dashboard is surfaced here for convenience, but goals themselves live in their categories — §4.10)

Fitness/Nutrition/Finance answer domain-specific questions. Time answers day-shape questions.

## 4.6.3 Time Detail View Layout

The Time detail view is the home for day-shape visibility. Like other category detail views in v1.18, it uses its own tab structure tuned to the category's nature. For Time: **Today / Patterns / Timeline**. The tab bar is fixed just below the header.

### Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)

- Back arrow (or tab indicator if arrived via bottom nav)
- Title: "Time"
- Profile icon (tap → Settings)
- Overflow menu (•••) with options: Edit focus priorities, Export timeline, Help

### Tab bar (fixed, below header)

Three tabs: **Today** · **Patterns** · **Timeline**. "Today" is the default.

### Today tab (default)

**3. What's Next strip**

- Slim horizontal strip below Today's Focus
- Shows the single most imminent item: next calendar event OR next high-priority task OR most urgent email
- Format: icon + label + time ("📧 Reply to Sarah · in 30 min" or "📅 Standup · 10:00 AM")
- Tap: opens the relevant surface (email view, calendar event, task detail)

**4. Active goals strip**

- Cross-category goals summary row
- Up to 3 goals visible with progress + pace indicators
- Per-goal: goal name, progress %, pace state (green "on track" / amber "behind" / blue "ahead")
- Tap any goal: opens category-specific detail view for that goal
- "Goals Dashboard" chevron: opens full Section 4.6.14 view

**5. Day Timeline subsystem card (FLAGSHIP)**

- Compact timeline preview card
- Shows today's reconstructed timeline as a horizontal visual ribbon
- Tap: opens full Day Timeline view (Section 4.6.5)
- Details in Section 4.6.5

**6. Productivity subsystem card**

- Summary: today's calendar density, inbox state, tasks complete/remaining
- Tap: opens Productivity detail view (Section 4.6.6)

**7. Attention subsystem card**

- Summary: today's screen time total, time at primary places (home/work/gym)
- Tap: opens Attention detail view (Section 4.6.10)

**8. Time Patterns subsystem card**

- Surface for deterministic cross-domain insights
- Shows 2-3 current pattern highlights (e.g., "Your workouts peak on Tuesdays and Thursdays")
- Tap: opens Time Patterns full view (Section 4.6.13)

**9. Goals Dashboard card**

- Cross-category goals aggregation
- Tap: opens full Goals Dashboard (Section 4.6.14)

**10. Floating Action Button (FAB)**

- Same FAB pattern as other categories (Section 4.2.6) — opens the chatbot overlay
- The overlay contains two surfaces: the chatbot for life and cross-domain questions, and shortcut buttons that open native flows directly
- Time-specific shortcut buttons:
  - Add Task (opens task creation card)
  - Add Calendar Event (opens account picker → event form)
  - View Today's Schedule (navigates to the Day Timeline view in Section 4.6.5)
- Task descriptions or calendar titles typed into the chat are redirected to the corresponding shortcut. The chatbot answers questions about the user's day, calendar, email priorities, tasks, screen time, and cross-domain life questions — including "tell me about my day," which the user types or speaks directly into the chat input.

**11. Bottom tab bar**

- Standard five-tab bar; Time tab highlighted

### Layout Constraints

Time has more content than any other category detail view. On iPhone SE, Today's Focus + What's Next + Active Goals strip are above the fold. The rest requires scrolling. This is acceptable because the above-the-fold content answers "what matters right now" — the rest is for deeper exploration.

On larger devices, content density is unchanged.

## 4.6.4 Today's Focus (Hero)

Today's Focus is the single most important element in the Time category. Per the "mirror, not coach" principle, it is deterministic — built from real data, not AI-generated priorities.

### Visual Structure

**Card header:**
- Label: "Today's Focus"
- Subtle timestamp: "Updated 8:47 AM" (recomputes throughout the day)

**Focus items (1–3 items):**

Each focus item is a card within the Today's Focus section:

- Item icon (task, email, calendar, bill, goal, workout)
- Item title (the specific thing)
- Source context (what category it comes from)
- Real sub-items or supporting detail (not fluff)
- Action affordance (tap to open)

**Example layout:**

> **Today's Focus**
>
> 🎯 **1. Finish Q3 report for Monday**
>     Self-set priority task · Due tomorrow
>     • Outline section 3
>     • Get data from analytics team
>     • Draft executive summary
>
> 📧 **2. Reply to Sarah about project scope**
>     Priority email · Gmail classified as important · 3 messages in thread
>
> 💰 **3. Pay credit card**
>     Bill due in 2 days · $1,247 on Capital One

**What's deliberately excluded:**
- No motivational copy
- No "you got this!" or "let's crush it today!"
- No AI-generated explanations
- No trying to be a coach

### The Algorithm (Deterministic)

Today's Focus is computed from real data sources via weighted ranking:

**Candidate pool:**
- Overdue tasks from Life Dashboard Tasks
- User-marked priority tasks (due today or tomorrow)
- Bills due today or within 2 days
- Calendar events needing prep (events with prep notes or new unfamiliar attendees)
- Unreplied emails classified as important by Gmail AI importance classifier
- Active goals with deadlines approaching within 7 days
- Scheduled workouts for today per training plan
- Recent user-marked "important" items

**Ranking formula:**

Each candidate item gets a score based on:
- **Deadline proximity** (higher weight for sooner deadlines)
- **User-marked priority** (explicit user flag = significant boost)
- **Category urgency** (overdue > due today > due tomorrow)
- **Dependency signals** (if a task blocks other things, boost)

Top 1–3 items surface as Today's Focus. If fewer than 3 items meet the minimum threshold, fewer appear — no padding with random tasks.

### Sub-Items / Breakdown Display

Each focus item can have sub-items pulled from:
- **Task sub-items** (if user created nested tasks)
- **Email thread context** (latest messages summarized to 1 line each, existing Flask AI capability)
- **Calendar event prep notes** (user-added notes in the calendar event)
- **Goal progress steps** (milestones the user has defined)

Sub-items are only shown when they exist in real data. No AI-generated sub-items. If a task has no sub-items, it displays without them.

### Recomputation Frequency

Today's Focus recomputes:
- On app open
- After any logging action that affects candidates (task completed, email replied, bill paid)
- Every 15 minutes in the background while the user is in the app
- On explicit pull-to-refresh

### Handling Empty State

If the user has no qualifying focus items today (no overdue tasks, no priority emails, no urgent bills, no goal deadlines), the card shows:

> **Today's Focus**
>
> Nothing urgent today. Enjoy the breathing room, or add something you want to get done.
>
> [+ Add task]

Calm, not empty. Doesn't force a fabricated focus item.

### Interactive Details

- Tap a focus item: opens the source (task detail, email thread, bill detail, etc.)
- Long-press: shows the ranking reasoning ("Top focus because: overdue + user priority + blocks other tasks")
- Swipe left on a focus item: mark as done / complete the task (inline completion)
- Swipe right on a focus item: snooze / dismiss for today

### What's Next (Secondary)

Directly beneath Today's Focus is the "What's Next" strip — a single-line forward-looking affordance:

- **Next calendar event** (if within 4 hours) — "📅 Standup at 10:00 AM (in 32 min)"
- **OR next high-priority task** (if no imminent calendar event) — "🎯 Review Q3 draft (next up)"
- **OR next bill due** (if nothing else imminent) — "💰 Rent due in 3 days"

Only ONE item shown. Whichever is most imminent. Tap to open.

### Data Persistence

Today's Focus is computed server-side and cached for fast display. The `today_focus_cache` table stores the current focus per user, invalidated on data changes.

### AI Usage

None. Today's Focus is entirely deterministic. If a user wants AI analysis of their priorities, they ask the chatbot.

## 4.6.5 Day Timeline (Flagship)

Day Timeline is the flagship feature of the Time category and arguably the most differentiating feature in Life Dashboard. It is a minute-by-minute reconstruction of the user's day using all available data sources — a genuine "mirror" of how the user actually spent their time.

### Why This Feature Matters

No competitor aggregates this territory. Apple Health shows sleep and workouts fragmentarily. Google Fit shows activity fragmentarily. Screen Time shows app usage fragmentarily. Life360 shows location. RescueTime shows work patterns. Nobody builds the integrated chronological view.

When a user sees their day reconstructed accurately from real data, the product stops being "another tracker" and starts being "the system that knows my life." This is Life Dashboard's differentiator made visible.

### Three Views Of The Timeline

**View 1: Home Dashboard Strip (compact, per Section 4.2)**
- Horizontal ribbon on Home Dashboard
- ~6 items visible: past done + upcoming next
- Past items dimmed, now marker, upcoming items bright
- Tap any item → opens Time Timeline full view
- Purpose: glance at "what's happening today"

**View 2: Time Detail Timeline Card (mid-depth)**
- Card on Time detail view
- Vertical timeline showing today's full day as a stacked list of time blocks
- Each block: time range + activity type + location/context
- Scrollable within the card for long days
- Tap any block → opens Timeline Deep-Dive fullscreen
- Purpose: review the day in context of other Life data

**View 3: Timeline Deep-Dive (fullscreen, interactive)**
- Full-screen visualization
- Vertical time axis, activity blocks with icons and colors
- Zoom: pinch to zoom into specific time ranges (e.g., zoom in on 2-3pm)
- Pan: scroll up/down through the day
- Tap any block: inspector panel with full details (location, activity, what apps were used, what workout occurred, what was eaten)
- Swipe horizontally: navigate to previous/next day
- Swipe to archive of past days (up to user's history limit per tier)
- Filter: toggle which activity types are shown (hide screen time, or show only workouts, etc.)
- Purpose: explore the day deeply

### Timeline Data Model
