- **Dark mode:** chat bubbles are elevated gray on near-black background; assistant bubbles slightly lighter than user bubbles. Background dim is ~50% opacity black to maintain contrast with the dark app surface.

Specific color values, typography tokens, and motion easing curves are specified in the design system section, not here. This section defines behavior, layout, and anatomy.

## 4.7.5 Shortcut Buttons — Per-Screen Mapping

Shortcut buttons are the direct-action affordances that sit above the X in the overlay. Each button opens a **native flow, not the chatbot**. Tapping "Log Meal" opens the meal logging card; it does not pre-fill the chat input.

### Home Dashboard Shortcuts

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| Log Meal | Opens Nutrition text/voice meal logger (Section 4.4.4) |
| Log Workout | Opens Fitness workout logger (Section 4.3.12) — template view if plan exists today, else freestyle |
| Add Task | Opens Life task creation card (Section 4.6.9) |
| Add Transaction | Opens Finance manual transaction card (Section 4.5.12), visible only if Plaid is connected |

### Fitness Shortcuts

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| Log Workout | Opens workout logger (template or freestyle) |
| Log Weight | Opens body weight entry card |
| Log Freestyle Workout | Opens freestyle workout logger explicitly |

### Nutrition Shortcuts

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| Log Meal | Opens text/voice meal logger |
| Scan Meal | Opens camera directly |
| Barcode | Opens barcode scanner directly |
| Saved Meals | Opens saved meals list |
| Log Water | Visible only if hydration is activated; opens +8/+16/+24 oz buttons |
| Pantry Scan | Pro-only; opens pantry camera |

### Finance Shortcuts

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| Add Transaction | Opens manual transaction card |
| Mark Bill Paid | Opens bills list with paid toggle |
| Check Balances | Opens savings detail |

### Life Shortcuts

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| Add Task | Opens task creation card |
| Add Calendar Event | Opens calendar event creation (account picker → event form) |
| View Today's Schedule | Navigates to Time detail's Day Timeline view (Section 4.6.5) |

### Button Ordering Within Each Set

Buttons are ordered by expected frequency of use, most-used at the bottom (closest to thumb). This is determined by the existing Flask PWA's log-type histogram and adjusted post-launch based on analytics. The ordering is not user-configurable in v1.

### Time-of-Day Emphasis (Soft Touch)

Per the design direction in 4.7.1 philosophy ("no unprompted suggestions"), shortcut buttons do not change which appear based on time. However, on the Home Dashboard and Nutrition screens, the most-contextually-relevant shortcut gets a subtle visual emphasis (slightly thicker accent border — no size change, no label change):

- 5am–10am local: "Log Meal" emphasized (breakfast)
- 11am–2pm local: "Log Meal" emphasized (lunch)
- 5pm–9pm local: "Log Meal" emphasized (dinner)
- After a detected workout-complete event from a wearable in the last 30 min: "Log Workout" emphasized

This is deterministic, free, and doesn't speak. It just makes the likely-useful shortcut fractionally easier to spot. No AI involvement.

## 4.7.6 Input Modes — Text & Voice

The chatbot accepts input via text typing or voice dictation. Both flow into the same chat input bubble before being sent.

### Text Input

- Tap the chat input bubble → keyboard raises
- User types, then taps the send arrow (▲) that appears on the right side of the bubble when there's content (voice mic button hides while content is present)
- Return key sends (same as send arrow)
- No character limit imposed in UI; server enforces a 2000-character input cap with a graceful "message too long" error

### Voice Input

Voice is on-device native speech recognition — iOS `Speech` framework, Android `SpeechRecognizer`. No server-side transcription in v1. Zero incremental cost, works offline for transcription, sub-300ms to first transcription frame.

**The Voice Button**

The mic icon sits on the right side of the chat input bubble when the input is empty. It is a first-class visual element:

- **Idle state:** static mic icon, subtle pulse every 3 seconds to indicate aliveness (very subtle — breath-like, not attention-grabbing)
- **On tap:** icon morphs into a waveform visualization that reacts to audio input amplitude in real time. The morph animation is 200ms and uses spring physics.
- **Active listening:** waveform animates with audio input. Below the input bubble, small text appears: "Listening…"
- **Silence detected** (1.5 seconds of no speech): auto-stops recording, transcription populates the input field
- **Manual stop:** tap the waveform → stops recording, transcription populates
- **Error state:** if on-device recognizer fails, revert to static mic icon with small toast: "Couldn't hear that — try again"

The animation is designed to feel responsive and premium. This is a touchpoint users will see daily; it earns the extra polish budget.

**The Confirmation Pattern**

Transcribed text lands in the input field and the user must tap send to commit. No auto-commit on transcription. The user can edit the transcription before sending.

This is consistent with the chatbot's "no silent actions" posture. A user speaking a question into the mic should see what was understood before it goes to the AI.

### Input State Validation

Before sending, the client performs:

- Trim whitespace
- Empty-input check (disables send if empty)
- Length check (2000 chars)
- Rate limit check (client-side prevents double-submits within 500ms of prior send)

## 4.7.7 Conversation Flow & Session Behavior

### Session Definition

A chatbot session begins when the user opens the overlay for the first time in an app launch. It ends when:

- The app is force-closed
- The app has been backgrounded for 30+ consecutive minutes
- The user explicitly resets the conversation (via the overflow menu — see below)

Within a session, closing and reopening the overlay does **not** end the session. Conversation history persists in RAM across overlay open/close cycles.

### No Server-Side Persistence (v1)

The session is entirely client-side and in-memory. No conversation history is written to any database. When the session ends, the conversation is gone.

This is a deliberate v1 constraint. Reasoning:

- Simpler privacy story — there is no chat history database to breach, export, or request deleted
- Simpler engineering — no sync, no backup, no cross-device state
- Lower storage cost — no per-user chat history tables
- Zero lock-in risk — the chatbot's utility is driven by today's context, not yesterday's conversation

A future release may add optional history persistence as a Pro feature, but v1 ships without it.

### Conversation Reset

Within the overlay, the overflow menu (•••) in the top-right of the conversation area provides:

- **Clear conversation** — wipes the current conversation from RAM. Shortcut buttons reappear. Useful if the user wants to start a fresh topic.
- **Report this response** — only appears next to assistant responses via a long-press menu on the bubble. Submits the conversation turn for quality review (see 4.7.24 analytics).

### Multi-Turn Context Within A Session

When the user asks a follow-up, the full conversation history for the session (up to the current turn) is included in the AI call context, capped at the last 8 turns (4 user + 4 assistant). Older turns drop out of context silently.

The per-query context containers (see 4.7.10) are **re-assembled on every query**. User data changes between turns (a meal logged, a transaction synced, a workout completed), so the chatbot always sees current data, not data from when the conversation started.

### Follow-Up Examples

This multi-turn behavior enables natural conversation:

- Turn 1: *"How's my protein today?"* → *"You're at 84g of your 150g target, about 56% with 5 hours left in your eating window."*
- Turn 2: *"How was I last Tuesday?"* → The chatbot understands "I" means the user and "last Tuesday" means 7 days ago; it pulls Tuesday's protein total from nutrition history and compares.
- Turn 3: *"What about sleep on that day?"* → The chatbot still has the "last Tuesday" reference from turn 2 and pulls sleep data for that date.

Within 8 turns, follow-ups work as expected. At turn 9, turn 1 drops, which rarely matters in practice.

## 4.7.8 Logging Intent Redirect

When a user types a message that reads as a logging attempt ("log 3 eggs", "I ate a sandwich", "spent $14 at Starbucks", "add a task to call mom"), the chatbot does not log the item. It responds with a redirect.

### Detection

Logging intent is detected client-side by the Intent Classifier (see 4.7.9) via pattern matching:

- Verbs: "log", "add", "I ate", "I had", "I spent", "paid", "bought"
- Explicit structure: a sentence starting with a food, transaction amount, or action verb paired with a domain-recognizable object

If the classifier is confident (>0.8 probability of logging intent), the client intercepts **before** making the AI call. The user's query does not burn an AI interaction counter or a Core daily quota query. Zero AI cost, zero latency.

### The Redirect Message

The response is templated, not AI-generated, and matches the detected domain:

- **Meal:** *"I don't log meals directly — tap the Log Meal shortcut (or swipe to Nutrition) to log that. I can answer nutrition questions though, like how you're doing on protein today or what's in a typical scrambled egg."*
- **Workout:** *"I don't log workouts directly — tap the Log Workout shortcut to record that. I can answer training questions, like how your strength progress looks this month or whether you're on pace with your plan."*
- **Transaction:** *"I don't log transactions — tap Add Transaction to record that. I can answer finance questions, like whether you're safe to spend or what bills are due this week."*
- **Task:** *"I don't create tasks directly — tap the Add Task shortcut to add one. I can answer life questions, like what's on your calendar today or what emails need replies."*
- **Generic (unknown domain):** *"I don't log entries directly — use the shortcut buttons on the right to log quickly. I can answer questions about your data, like how your week is going."*

### If The Classifier Is Wrong

If the classifier misfires and intercepts a question that was not a logging attempt (e.g., *"spent $14 at Starbucks — is that over my coffee budget?"*), the user can tap a "That was a question — answer it" link in the redirect message. That reclassifies the intent and routes to the AI call normally.

This is rare but needed — the 10–15% false positive rate of cheap client-side classifiers means we must give users an escape hatch. The escape-hatch tap is logged for classifier tuning.

## 4.7.9 Query Classification

Every chatbot message goes through a client-side classifier before making it (or not making it) to the AI.

### Classifier Output

The classifier produces one of:

| Intent | Action |
|---|---|
| `logging_intent` | Show redirect (see 4.7.8); no AI call |
| `query_intent` with domain tags | Assemble context containers per 4.7.10 and 4.7.11; call AI |
| `ambiguous` | Route to AI with broad context; fallback behavior |
| `out_of_scope` | Show polite out-of-scope message; no AI call |

### Out-Of-Scope Cases

Some messages are not logging and not in-scope queries:

- **Medical advice** ("is it safe for me to fast for 48 hours?") — out of scope; responds *"I can't give medical advice. I can share what the data shows — want me to summarize your last week?"*
- **Specific investment advice** ("should I buy AAPL?") — out of scope; same pattern.
- **External-world information** ("what's the weather tomorrow?", "what time is the Lakers game?") — out of scope; the chatbot does not search the web.
- **Actions outside scope** ("draft an email", "send a message to my friend") — redirect to the appropriate feature (email drafts are in Life 4.6.8; the chatbot itself does not compose external communications).

### Domain Tagging

For `query_intent`, the classifier tags which domains are relevant:

- **Single-domain tag:** e.g., `["nutrition"]` — load only NutritionContext
- **Multi-domain tag:** e.g., `["nutrition", "finance"]` — load both
- **Broad tag:** `["all"]` — load summary-level containers from all four domains

Tagging drives Context Container Selection (4.7.11) and directly affects token cost. A conservative tag saves money; an over-broad tag produces better answers. The classifier's default when uncertain is to err broader — better answer quality is worth the extra ~1000 tokens.

### Classifier Implementation

The classifier runs on-device using a small rule-based engine:

1. **Pattern match** — regex and keyword rules for the 80th-percentile cases ("log", "add", dollar signs → logging intent; "how am I", "what's my", "can I afford" → query_intent with domain inferred from keywords).
2. **Keyword-to-domain mapping** — "protein", "calories", "carbs" → nutrition; "debit", "spent", "budget" → finance; "sleep", "workout", "reps" → fitness; "calendar", "email", "task" → life.
3. **Unknown fallback** — if no pattern matches, default to `ambiguous` with all domains tagged broadly. One Haiku call with broader context resolves these.

The classifier is version-bumped as Life Dashboard learns what users actually ask. In v1, it ships with ~200 keyword rules derived from the existing Flask chatbot prompts (the "momentum insight" and "scale summary" prompts give strong signal on what users phrase).

## 4.7.10 Context Container Architecture

This is the engineering heart of Section 4.7. The chatbot's entire value proposition rests on the ability to pass relevant, correctly-scoped, privacy-filtered context to the AI efficiently. Doing this well is why the app matters; doing it poorly means the answer quality is no better than a generic chatbot asking about generic data.

The architecture centers on **typed context containers** — structured payloads per domain with defined schemas, token budgets, staleness policies, and privacy gates. A query's total context is assembled from a selected subset of containers based on the classifier's domain tags (4.7.9).

### Container Principles

Every container:

1. **Has a fixed schema.** Fields, types, and cardinalities are defined at design time. No free-form blob injection.
2. **Has a token budget.** Each container has a target and hard-cap token count. Assemblers enforce the hard cap by truncating lower-priority fields first.
3. **Has a staleness policy.** Some containers cache for a session; others re-fetch on every query. Cache semantics are per-container.
4. **Has a privacy gate.** Some containers load only if the user has consented to the underlying data source feeding AI. Consent is per-source (see Section 4.6.18 and Section 6).
5. **Is versioned.** Container schemas are versioned alongside the app. The server respects the client's requested version and returns a v1-formatted container even if v2 exists.

### The Containers

Nine containers are defined for v1:

| Container | Target Tokens | Cap | Load Cost | Staleness | Privacy Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ProfileContext | 250 | 350 | Free (cached) | Session | None (always available) |
| GoalsContext | 150 | 250 | Free (cached) | Session | None |
| NutritionContext | 400 | 600 | Server query | Per-query (realtime) | Nutrition data present |
| FitnessContext | 500 | 700 | Server query | Per-query (realtime) | Fitness data present |
| FinanceContext | 400 | 600 | Server query, redacted | Per-query (realtime) | Finance connected + AI consent |
| LifeContext | 600 | 900 | Server query, redacted | Per-query (realtime) | Life data connected + AI consent |
| HistoricalContext | 800 | 1200 | Server query (on-demand) | Per-query (realtime) | Matches parent domain |
| PatternsContext | 300 | 400 | Free (cached Time Patterns) | Session | Matches parent domain |
| DayTimelineContext | 500 | 800 | Server query (on-demand) | Per-query (realtime) | Life AI consent |

Total possible context for a single query with all containers loaded: ~3900 target tokens, ~5900 hard cap. Plus system prompt (~500 tokens) and conversation history (~1000 tokens). Total cap per query: ~8K tokens, which is within Haiku's efficient range.

### Container Schemas

**ProfileContext** — always loaded, cached for session

```
{
  first_name: string,
  age: int,
  biological_sex: "male" | "female",
  height_in: number,
  current_weight_lbs: number,
  goal_label: string (e.g., "lose_weight", "build_muscle"),
  activity_level: string,
  occupation: string,
  stress_level: "low" | "moderate" | "high",
  rmr_kcal: int,
  tdee_kcal: int,
  calorie_target: int,
  protein_g_target: int,
  carbs_g_target: int,
  fat_g_target: int,
  archetype: string (AI-generated archetype label from onboarding),
  primary_leverage_points: string[] (up to 3),
  household_size: int?,
  typical_wake_hour: int? (0-23),
  typical_sleep_hour: int? (0-23)
}
```

**GoalsContext** — active goals across categories

```
{
  active_goals: [{
    category: "fitness" | "nutrition" | "finance" | "life",
    goal_type: string (e.g., "weight_loss", "emergency_fund"),
    target_value: number,
    current_value: number,
    percent_complete: number,
    pace: "ahead" | "on_track" | "behind",
    days_remaining: int,
    target_date: ISO date
  }]
}
```

**NutritionContext** — today + last 7 days rolled up

```
{
  today: {
    calories_consumed: int,
    calories_remaining: int,
    protein_g: int,
    carbs_g: int,
    fat_g: int,
    fiber_g: int,
    sugar_g: int,
    sodium_mg: int,
    meal_count: int,
    meals: [{ name: string, calories: int, logged_at: ISO time }] (max 5, most recent first),
    water_oz: number? (if hydration active),
    hours_since_last_meal: number?
  },
  last_7_days: {
    avg_calories: int,
    avg_protein: int,
    target_hit_rate: number (0.0-1.0),
    protein_target_hit_rate: number,
    days_in_deficit: int (for weight-loss users)
  }
}
```

**FitnessContext** — today + last 7 days rolled up

```
{
  today: {
    workout_logged: boolean,
    workout_summary: { type: string, duration_min: int, calories_burned: int }?,
    steps: int?,
    current_weight: number?,
    sleep_last_night: { hours: number, quality_score: int }?,
    recovery_score: int? (0-100, if available from wearable),
    hrv_ms: number?
  },
  last_7_days: {
    workout_count: int,
    plan_adherence_pct: number (0-100 if plan exists),
    avg_sleep_hours: number,
    weight_change: number,
    steps_avg: int,
    recent_prs: [{ exercise: string, value: string, date: ISO date }] (max 3)
  }
}
```

**FinanceContext** — current week + last 30 days, redacted

```
{
  today: {
    safe_to_spend: number,
    spent_today: number,
    transactions_today: [{ category: string, amount: number }] (merchant names REDACTED — category only),
    bills_due_this_week: [{ category: string, amount: number, due_day: string }]
  },
  current_week: {
    budget: number,
    spent: number,
    remaining: number,
    percent_used: number,
    days_left_in_week: int,
    top_category: string
  },
  last_30_days: {
    avg_daily_spend: number,
    top_3_categories: [{ category: string, total: number }],
    savings_rate_pct: number?
  },
  net_worth: number?
}
```

**LifeContext** — today's life state, heavily redacted

```
{
  today: {
    calendar_event_count: int,
    next_event: { category: "work" | "personal" | "health" | null, time_until_min: int }? (titles NOT included by default),
    focus_time_blocks_min: int,
    unread_important_emails_count: int,
    top_email_senders: string[] (names only, max 3, from importance classifier),
    task_count: int,
    tasks_due_today: int,
    tasks_completed_today: int,
    screen_time_today_hours: number?,
    screen_time_top_category: string?,
    current_location_name: string? ("at Home", "at Work", "at Gym" — user-labeled only, null if unlabeled)
  },
  last_7_days: {
    avg_screen_time_hours: number?,
    avg_daily_event_count: number,
    avg_task_completion_rate: number
  }
}
```

**HistoricalContext** — on-demand, loaded only when query mentions "last week" / "last month" / "usually" / "typical"

```
{
  timeframe: "last_week" | "last_30_days" | "last_90_days",
  nutrition?: { avg_calories, avg_protein, target_hit_rate, days_logged },
  fitness?: { workout_count, avg_sleep, weight_change, steps_avg },
  finance?: { total_spent, avg_weekly_spend, top_categories, bills_paid_on_time_rate },
  life?: { avg_screen_time, avg_task_completion, avg_focus_time }
}
```

**PatternsContext** — pre-computed deterministic patterns from Time Patterns subsystem (Section 4.6.13)

```
{
  active_patterns: [{
    pattern_type: string (e.g., "workout_day_correlation"),
    description: string (deterministic, no AI narrative),
    confidence: number (0.0-1.0),
    data_points: string[] (e.g., "Tue-Thu workouts", "+20% tasks completed")
  }]
}
```

**DayTimelineContext** — today's timeline, loaded when query is day-specific

```
{
  timeline_events: [{
    start_time: ISO time,
    end_time: ISO time,
    event_type: "calendar" | "workout" | "meal" | "location_visit" | "focus_block",
    category: string,
    summary: string (e.g., "at Gym", "logged lunch" — no raw titles unless explicitly requested)
  }] (max 30 most relevant to query time range)
}
```

### Selection Matrix

The classifier's domain tags plus query characteristics determine which containers load:

| Query Type | Always Loaded | Conditionally Loaded |
|---|---|---|
| Single-domain query (nutrition only) | Profile, Goals | NutritionContext |
| Single-domain query with history hint ("last week") | Profile, Goals | NutritionContext, HistoricalContext |
| Cross-domain query ("can I afford dinner") | Profile, Goals | Finance, Nutrition |
| Life query ("tell me about my day") | Profile, Goals | Life, DayTimeline, summary slices of Nutrition + Fitness + Finance |
| Why-am-I-X query ("why am I tired?") | Profile, Goals | Fitness (sleep), Life (screen time, calendar), Nutrition (calorie deficit), Patterns |
| Ambiguous / catch-all | Profile, Goals | Summary slices from all four domains (~200 tokens each) |

### Container Assembly on the Server

Server-side assembly pipeline per query:

1. Receive query + classifier output + conversation history from client
2. Authenticate user (Clerk JWT)
3. Check per-source AI consent flags from user settings
4. For each requested container:
   a. Check privacy gate — skip if consent not granted
   b. Query relevant database tables
   c. Apply redaction rules (merchant names, email titles, location coords, etc.)
   d. Serialize to container JSON
   e. Token-count; truncate lower-priority fields if over cap
5. Assemble final payload: system prompt + containers + conversation history + user message
6. Call Anthropic API with streaming enabled
7. Stream response tokens back to client

Average server-side assembly time: <200ms. First token from Anthropic: <600ms. Target first-paint on the client: <800ms from send.

## 4.7.11 Context Loading Pipeline

The pipeline from user send to AI response streaming back is specified end-to-end for engineering clarity.

### Step-by-Step

1. **Client: intent classification (synchronous, ~5ms)**
   - Regex + keyword rules on query
   - Output: intent, domain tags, confidence

2. **Client: redirect check**
   - If `logging_intent` with confidence >0.8 → show redirect, end
   - If `out_of_scope` → show out-of-scope message, end
   - Else continue

3. **Client: send request**
   - `POST /api/v1/chatbot/query`
   - Body: `{ query: string, classifier_output: {...}, conversation_history: [...] }`
   - Headers: `X-Client-Date`, `X-Client-Timezone`

4. **Server: authenticate and authorize**
   - Verify Clerk JWT
   - Check Core quota (see 4.7.16)
   - If over quota → return 402 with upgrade prompt, end

5. **Server: container selection**
   - Based on classifier output + query heuristics
   - Determines which containers to build

6. **Server: privacy gate check**
   - For each container, check per-source AI consent
   - Skip containers whose underlying data source has AI consent OFF
   - Note skipped containers in response metadata so the UI can tell the user ("I didn't look at your calendar because you haven't enabled that")

7. **Server: data queries (parallel)**
   - SQL queries to Neon Postgres for each container's data
   - Redis cache check for session-cached containers (Profile, Goals)
   - Target total query time: <150ms

8. **Server: redaction**
   - Per container, apply domain-specific redaction:
     - Finance: merchant names → category names; amounts preserved
     - Life/Calendar: event titles → category tags; times preserved
     - Life/Email: subject + first 200 chars of body only; full bodies never sent
     - Life/Location: GPS coords → user-labeled place name or "unlabeled"
     - Life/Screen Time: per-app data → category totals

9. **Server: token budget enforcement**
   - For each container, count tokens
   - Truncate lower-priority fields if over hard cap
   - Compute total payload size

10. **Server: prompt assembly**
    - System prompt (see 4.7.12 for content)
    - Container JSON blocks
    - Recent conversation history (last 4 user + 4 assistant turns)
    - Current user query

11. **Server: call Anthropic**
    - Model: claude-haiku-4-5 (default; see 4.7.14)
    - Streaming: enabled
    - Max tokens: 800 output
    - Timeout: 15s

12. **Server: stream response to client via SSE**
    - First-token latency target: <800ms end-to-end from send
    - Stream tokens as they arrive
    - On error: emit error event, close stream

13. **Client: render streaming response**
    - Assistant bubble appears with typing indicator
    - Tokens stream in
    - On complete: typing indicator fades, full response visible

14. **Client: decrement quota counter (Core users)**
    - Update local count
    - Update displayed counter ("18 of 10 daily chats remaining")

15. **Server: log query for analytics**
    - Query metadata only — no raw query text stored unless user reports the response
    - Tokens used, containers loaded, latency, model, result status

### Parallelization

Step 7 (data queries) parallelizes across containers. Step 8 (redaction) parallelizes per container. The server aims for the whole pipeline before the Anthropic call to complete within 200ms, leaving headroom for the AI call itself.

### Failure Isolation

Any single container failure (e.g., Finance query timeout) does not fail the entire query. The failed container is dropped; a note is added to the response metadata; the AI call proceeds with the remaining containers.

## 4.7.12 Cross-Domain Queries

Cross-domain queries are the chatbot's signature capability. They are the reason the unified-data architecture matters.

### Examples and Container Loads

| User Query | Classifier Tags | Containers Loaded |
|---|---|---|
| "How am I on protein today?" | `[nutrition]` | Profile, Goals, Nutrition |
| "Can I afford dinner tonight?" | `[finance, nutrition]` | Profile, Goals, Finance, Nutrition (for "dinner" context) |
| "Why am I tired this week?" | `[fitness, life, nutrition]` | Profile, Goals, Fitness, Life, Nutrition, Patterns |
| "Tell me about my day" | `[life, all]` | Profile, Goals, Life, DayTimeline, summary slices of Nutrition + Fitness + Finance |
| "Am I on track with my goals?" | `[goals, all]` | Profile, Goals, summary slices from all domains |
| "What did I eat after my last workout?" | `[nutrition, fitness]` | Profile, Goals, Nutrition (with meal timing), Fitness (last workout time) |
| "Did I sleep less on gym days?" | `[fitness, patterns]` | Profile, Goals, Fitness, Patterns, Historical (30-day) |
| "How does this week's spending compare to last week?" | `[finance, historical]` | Profile, Goals, Finance, Historical (finance slice) |
| "What do I usually do on Saturdays?" | `[life, historical]` | Profile, Goals, Life, Historical, DayTimeline |

### The System Prompt For Cross-Domain Queries

The system prompt reminds the model of its role, constraints, and the data it has access to. Key points:

- "You are Life Dashboard, a personal data consultant. You answer questions about the user's data. You do not give medical or legal advice. You do not coach or moralize. You cite specific numbers."
- "You have access to typed data containers passed below. Each container includes fields the user has consented to share. Some containers may be absent if the user has not connected that data source."
- "If a relevant container is missing (e.g., user asks about finance but no Finance container is present), say so directly: 'I don't have finance data connected — connect a bank to answer that.' Do not speculate."
- "Responses are concise — 1–4 sentences unless the user asks for more detail. Lead with the answer. Include specific numbers where possible."
- "You do not remember previous sessions. You only have the current conversation and the data containers for this query."

### Container Combining Examples

**"Can I afford dinner tonight?"** pulls FinanceContext (safe_to_spend, bills_remaining, current_week) and NutritionContext (calories_remaining — because a good answer might also reference eating at home vs out). The system prompt framing:

> *"User asked: 'Can I afford dinner tonight?'*
> 
> *FinanceContext: safe_to_spend = $84, days_left_in_week = 2, top_category_today = 'food' at $38.*
> 
> *NutritionContext: calories_remaining = 850 of 2200 target, last_meal_logged = '6h ago'.*
> 
> *Answer concisely using these numbers."*

Expected response: *"You have $84 safe to spend with 2 days left this week. A $30-40 dinner keeps you comfortably in budget. You've got 850 calories left in your target too, so go ahead."*

**"Why am I tired this week?"** pulls FitnessContext (sleep), LifeContext (screen time, calendar load), NutritionContext (deficit), and PatternsContext. The AI synthesizes without inventing data:

Expected response: *"Sleep is lower than your normal — averaging 6.1 hours this week vs your 7.3-hour typical. Screen time after 10pm has also been up, at 1.4h/night. Calorie intake has been near target. Sleep debt + late screen time are the signals that show up."*

### Containers That Should NOT Be Combined

Some combinations are bad-faith — they would attempt to correlate data the user hasn't granted AI consent for. Privacy gates stop those at the container level. If a user's Life AI consent is OFF, a query about "why am I tired?" would proceed with Fitness data only and explicitly say: *"I don't have screen time or calendar data connected for AI — I can only look at sleep and workouts."*

## 4.7.13 Streaming & Latency Targets

Latency is a first-class feature. The chatbot must feel snappy even on deep queries.

### Target Latencies

| Event | Target | Hard Cap |
|---|---|---|
| Overlay open animation | 200ms | 300ms |
| Send tap → first token on screen | 800ms | 1500ms |
| Full short response complete (1-2 sentences) | 1.5s | 3s |
| Full medium response complete (3-5 sentences) | 3s | 6s |
| Full long response complete (list / compare / deep analysis) | 6s | 10s |

### Streaming Mechanics

- Server uses Server-Sent Events via Express `res.write()` with `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`
- Client consumes via React Native `EventSource` polyfill
- Each SSE event is one token (or a few tokens) from the Anthropic streaming response
- On stream close (complete or error), client finalizes the bubble

### Visual States During Streaming

1. **Send tap → AI bubble appears empty with typing indicator.** Three dots animating.
2. **First token arrives → dots fade, text begins typing in.** Text is rendered as it streams; no layout jank.
3. **Streaming in progress → dots replaced by a subtle cursor.** Same typography as the text.
4. **Stream complete → cursor fades, bubble is done.**

### Fallback If Streaming Fails

If the SSE connection drops mid-stream:

- Client shows "Connection interrupted — tap to retry" on the partial response
- Retry button re-submits the query with the same classifier output
- Partial response is kept visible but marked incomplete

## 4.7.14 Model Selection & Cost Controls

### v1 Default: Haiku For Everything

v1 uses **claude-haiku-4-5** for all chatbot queries regardless of complexity. Reasons:

- **Cost:** Haiku is ~$0.001-0.005 per query at our context sizes, vs ~$0.008-0.020 for Sonnet
- **Speed:** Haiku streams ~2x faster than Sonnet
- **Quality:** For the structured-context queries the chatbot handles, Haiku's quality is within 10-15% of Sonnet by internal eval. That gap is tolerable at the cost and speed trade.

### Escape Hatch

If post-launch eval shows Haiku quality is insufficient for specific query types (e.g., complex cross-domain synthesis), Sonnet 4.6 can be enabled for tagged query patterns without a full re-release. The server supports a runtime model flag per query classification.

### Never-Opus Rule

Opus 4.6 is never used from the chatbot. Opus is reserved for Nutrition's Premium Scan (Pro-only, photo input). The chatbot's Haiku default is cheap and acceptable; Opus's 30x cost is unjustified for text-only queries.

### Cost Controls

- **Max input tokens per query:** 8000 (hard cap at server)
- **Max output tokens per query:** 800 (enforced via API parameter)
- **Max conversation history in context:** last 8 turns (~1000 tokens)
- **Core daily quota:** 20 queries (see 4.7.16)
- **Per-call timeout:** 15s (after which the stream aborts and shows an error)

### Estimated Per-Query Cost

- **Small query** (~1.5K input, 150 output): ~$0.0018
- **Medium cross-domain** (~3.5K input, 400 output): ~$0.0044
- **Large "tell me about my day"** (~6K input, 700 output): ~$0.0076

Average expected cost per query: ~$0.004. At 10 queries/day (Core cap), max per-user daily cost: $0.04. At Pro 50-query/day cap, realistic heavy user cost stays within Pro's contribution margin. Pro at $9.99 sustains this at blended user distribution per §13.7.

## 4.7.15 Privacy & Redaction in Chatbot Context

The chatbot is the single largest AI data movement in Life Dashboard. Privacy posture here is specified more carefully than anywhere else.

### Principles

1. **No raw PII ever leaves the server.** Every container passes through a redaction layer.
2. **Consent is per-source and checked per query.** A container whose data source has AI consent OFF is not loaded.
3. **The user can see what was sent.** Settings → Privacy → Chatbot Audit shows the containers sent for every recent query.
4. **Redaction is stricter than it technically needs to be.** Merchant names are never sent by default; category names are. Email subjects are truncated to 200 chars. Calendar titles are never sent by default.

### Redaction Rules

| Data Type | Raw | Sent To AI |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction merchant | "Whole Foods Market" | "grocery" |
| Transaction amount | $47.32 | $47 (rounded to dollar) |
| Email subject | Full subject | Subject (if classifier flagged important) |
| Email body | Full body | First 200 chars (for classification/drafting only; NOT for chatbot queries) |
| Calendar event title | "Therapy with Dr. Smith" | "personal" category only |
| Location coordinates | 37.7749, -122.4194 | "at Home" (user-labeled) or "unlabeled visit" |
| Screen time app-level | "Instagram: 2h 10m" | "social: 2h" (category roll-up) |
| Task description | Full description | Full description (tasks are inherently user-authored and low-sensitivity) |

### Override For Explicit User Queries

If the user explicitly asks about specific calendar events ("what's my 3pm meeting today?") or transactions ("what did I spend at Whole Foods this week?"), they are implicitly granting one-time context elevation for that query. The client flags this to the server, which includes the specific raw data for the duration of that query only. The user's audit log records the elevation.

This is honesty about when raw data is sent. The chatbot is useful because of these specific queries; they must work. Making them work requires temporary elevation with a visible audit trail.

### Data That Never Leaves The Server

- Full email bodies
- Exact GPS coordinates
- Payment details (account numbers, card numbers, bank passwords)
- Anything flagged PII by the server-side classifier (SSN, phone in free text, credit card digits)

These are stripped before any AI call, irrespective of consent settings.

### The Chatbot Audit

Settings → Privacy → Chatbot Audit provides:

- Last 30 days of queries
- For each query: timestamp, query text, containers loaded, elevated fields (if any)
- Ability to delete a specific query's audit record
- Export all chatbot audit records as JSON (per GDPR Article 15)

## 4.7.16 Core vs Pro Differences

| Feature | Core | Pro |
|---|---|---|
| Chatbot access | ✓ | ✓ |
| Daily query quota | 10 queries/day | 50 queries/day (300/day soft cap) |
| Streaming responses | ✓ | ✓ |
| All context containers | ✓ | ✓ |
| Cross-domain queries | ✓ | ✓ |
| Session memory (within session) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Model used | Haiku 4.5 | Haiku 4.5 |
| Audit log (Chatbot Audit screen) | Last 30 days | Last 30 days |
| Query history persistence | In-session only | In-session only (v1) |

The Pro 50/day quota is the user-facing product commitment (v1.21 — tightened from prior 100/day per §10.7.1 and §13.7 margin protection). Behind the scenes, abuse and fraud protections engage above that number — a user hitting 50 in a day is near their committed limit, and sustained usage above the 300/day soft cap triggers a fraud-check flag on the account. Legitimate heavy users rarely approach the soft cap. User-facing copy avoids "unlimited" framing because it would imply infinite backend spend, which the product is deliberately not architected to support.

Chatbot Audit retention moved to 30 days across both tiers in v1.21 per §13.3.3 (transparency is not monetized as a differentiator).

### The Daily Cap Mechanics (Core)

- Counter resets at midnight user-local time
- Counter is visible in the overlay: "8 of 10 daily chats remaining" shown below the chat input when count is ≤5
- Logging-intent redirects do NOT count against the cap (client-side interception before AI call)
- Out-of-scope messages do NOT count against the cap
- Failed AI calls (server error) do NOT count against the cap
- Only successfully-returned responses count against the cap

### Hitting The Cap

On the 11th query of the day (Core):

- Overlay shows a friendly message: *"You've used your 10 daily chats. Life Dashboard Pro gives you a much higher daily quota — and chats reset at midnight tomorrow if you'd rather wait."*
- Two buttons: "Upgrade to Pro" (primary) and "OK" (dismiss)
- Tapping Upgrade → paywall flow (Section 4.1.9)
- Tapping OK → overlay dismisses; shortcut buttons still work for logging
- Next midnight → counter resets, chat available again

## 4.7.17 Paywall Connection

The chatbot interacts with the paywall counter system (Section 4.1.9) in two stages of a user's lifecycle.

### Stage 1 — First-Time User (Pre-Subscription)

A new user, post-onboarding, has 3 free AI interactions across the whole app. The chatbot queries are one of the AI-interaction types that decrement this counter.

On opening the chatbot overlay for the first time:

- No special onboarding or tutorial
- The user types a question and sends
- Counter decrements: 3 → 2
- Below the chat input, text reads: "2 of 3 free AI interactions remaining. Pro users get unlimited."
- At 0, the 4th attempt triggers the paywall modal (Section 4.1.9)

### Stage 2 — Subscribed User (Core or Pro)

Once the user is subscribed:

- Pro: no visible counter, no quota (soft cap only)
- Core: visible daily counter ("17 of 10 daily chats remaining"), resets midnight

### Mid-Response Quota Depletion

A user on query 20 of 20 sends a message. The response is mid-stream when the counter decrements to 0. Behavior:

- The current streaming response completes normally (it was already paid for)
- After the response finishes, the overlay shows the "used your 10 daily chats" message
- The user cannot send another query until midnight or upgrade

This is deliberate: interrupting a streaming response mid-sentence would feel broken. The counter is a pre-call check, not a mid-call cutoff.

### Dismiss-And-Return

If the user hits the cap, dismisses the upgrade modal, and returns later the same day:

- The cap message reappears each time the overlay is opened
- Shortcut buttons still function — only the chat input is blocked

## 4.7.18 Error States & Fallbacks

### AI Call Failures

| Error Type | User-Facing Behavior | Counter Decrements? |
|---|---|---|
| Timeout (>15s) | "That's taking longer than expected — try again" with retry button | No |
| Anthropic 5xx | "Chat is offline — try again in a moment" | No |
| Anthropic 429 (rate limit) | "Life Dashboard is busy — try again in 30 seconds" | No |
| Network offline | "You're offline — chat needs a connection" | No |
| Streaming connection drop | "Connection interrupted" on partial response, retry button | No (partial response kept visible) |
| Invalid response (unparseable) | Generic "Something went wrong" with retry | No |
| Quota exceeded (Core) | Cap message (see 4.7.16) | N/A |

### Data Container Failures

A container query failing (e.g., Finance timeout) does not fail the whole chatbot call. Instead:

- The failing container is dropped from the payload
- The response metadata flags that it was dropped
- The AI response continues with remaining containers
- If the user's question directly depended on the dropped container (e.g., asked about finance, Finance container failed), the response may be unsatisfactory. The client shows a small inline note: "I had trouble loading finance data for this — tap to retry with full data."

### Privacy-Gated Missing Containers

When a container is omitted because AI consent for that source is OFF:

- The response metadata lists omitted sources
- The chatbot's answer may note the gap: "I don't have calendar data to work with — enable Calendar AI access in Settings if you want that in answers."
- This is always non-judgmental — the user's privacy choice is respected, not nagged at.

### Voice Transcription Failures

- Transcription returns empty → input field stays empty, toast "Couldn't hear that — try again"
- Transcription gets the text wrong → user edits in the input field before sending (this is why we require tap-to-send after voice)

## 4.7.19 AI Usage Summary

### All Chatbot AI Calls

| Call | Model | Avg Input | Avg Output | Avg Cost/Call | Counts Against Paywall? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| chatbot_query (simple) | Haiku 4.5 | 1500 tok | 150 tok | $0.0018 | Yes |
| chatbot_query (cross-domain) | Haiku 4.5 | 3500 tok | 400 tok | $0.0044 | Yes |
| chatbot_query (day-long, deep context) | Haiku 4.5 | 6000 tok | 700 tok | $0.0076 | Yes |
| intent_classification (fallback) | Haiku 4.5 (cheapest tier) | 200 tok | 30 tok | $0.0003 | No (infrastructure) |

Note: the fallback AI classifier (4.7.9, for ambiguous queries) is a separate cheap Haiku call that runs only for queries the rule-based classifier couldn't confidently tag. In production this is expected to run for ~10% of queries.

### Daily Cost Modeling Per User

**Core user, moderate use (6 queries/day):**
- 6 × $0.003 (avg) = $0.018
- Monthly: ~$0.54 in chatbot cost

**Core user, heavy use at cap (10 queries/day):**
- 10 × $0.004 (avg, they ask harder questions) = $0.040
- Monthly: ~$1.20 in chatbot cost
- At $4.99/mo Core price → manageable at blended AI cost of ~$0.77/month per §13.7; thin but viable at heavy usage

**Pro user, moderate use (20 queries/day — below 50/day Pro quota):**
- 20 × $0.004 = $0.080
- Monthly: ~$2.40

**Pro user, heavy use (40 queries/day, below 50 quota):**
- 40 × $0.005 = $0.200
- Monthly: ~$6.00
- At $9.99/mo Pro price → sustainable at moderate blended AI cost of ~$4.97/month per §13.7

**Pro user, at quota (50 queries/day):**
- 50 × $0.005 = $0.250/day
- Monthly: $7.50
- At $9.99/mo → contribution margin compressed to ~$1/month on chatbot alone; soft cap at 300/day catches scripted abuse; legitimate heavy users factor into the 10% heavy-user cohort modeled in §13.7.3.

### Annual Chatbot Spend Estimate

Assuming 10,000 users split 60/40 Core/Pro, average realized usage of ~8 queries/day/user (most users don't approach their cap):

- 10,000 × 8 × 365 = 29.2M queries/year
- 29.2M × $0.004 avg = **~$117,000/year in Anthropic spend**
- Per user: ~$11.70/year in chatbot AI cost

This is contribution-positive against projected subscription revenue at both tiers after mobile platform fees. Detailed unit economics live in Section 13 (Business Requirements).

## 4.7.20 Chatbot Capabilities Matrix

What the chatbot can and cannot do in v1. This matrix is the user-facing source of truth.

### Can Do

| Capability | Example Query |
|---|---|
| Answer questions about today's data | "How am I on calories today?" |
| Answer questions about recent data | "How did I sleep last week?" |
| Cross-domain reasoning | "Can I afford this dinner?" |
| Pattern identification | "When do I usually work out?" |
| Trend comparison | "Is my spending up compared to last month?" |
| Current status on goals | "Am I on pace for my weight-loss goal?" |
| Data retrieval with specificity | "What did I eat yesterday for dinner?" |
| Timeline reconstruction | "What did I do Saturday morning?" |
| Summary generation | "Tell me about my day" |
| Explanation of own numbers | "Why is my Time score low today?" |
| Data-informed recommendations | "What's a good dinner to hit my protein?" |

### Cannot Do (v1)

| Non-Capability | Response Pattern |
|---|---|
| Log data | Redirects to shortcut (see 4.7.8) |
| Medical advice | "I can't give medical advice — share what the data shows?" |
| Investment advice | Same pattern — refuses with data offer |
| Compose external communications | Redirects to email draft feature (4.6.8) |
| External-world knowledge (weather, news, sports) | "I don't search the web — I only know your data." |
| Remember prior sessions | Response ignores references to past sessions |
| Set reminders or notifications | Redirects to Notifications settings (4.9) |
| Modify user data ("change my calorie target") | Redirects to Settings or Profile |
| Anything requiring real-time external APIs | "I don't have access to [X] in v1." |

### Cannot Do (Yet — Deferred)

Flagged for potential v1.5 or v2+ expansion:

- Chatbot-driven goal creation (user says "I want to lose 10 pounds"; v1 redirects to goal library in Settings)
- Cross-session memory of user preferences ("I'm vegetarian"; user must re-state each session in v1)
- Scheduled recurring queries ("remind me every morning how I'm doing")
- Web search connection for external context ("what's a typical calorie count for a chipotle bowl?" — v1 relies on training data only)

## 4.7.21 Friction-Reduction Commitments

Specific reductions applied to the chatbot surface relative to a naive implementation:

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Opening a separate app for AI queries | Chatbot is one tap (FAB) from every main screen |
| Explaining every query ("I'm on a diet and I want to know…") | Profile and Goals containers always loaded — AI already knows the context |
| Re-typing common queries | Within-session conversation memory supports follow-ups |
| Unclear what data AI can see | Chatbot Audit in Settings shows exactly what was sent |
| Paying per query anxiously | Clear counter display below input for Core users |
| Logging accidentally via chat | Intent classifier intercepts with redirect, zero cost |
| Waiting for slow AI | Streaming responses start in <800ms |
| Privacy surprises | Per-source AI consent defaulted OFF; user must opt in; redaction on every call |
| Wrong transcription in voice | Transcription lands in input field for review before send |
| Generic chatbot answers | Typed context containers ensure user-specific data in every response |
| Ambiguous cross-domain queries failing | Broad classifier fallback loads summary from all domains |
| "You need to upgrade" on every feature | Core has full chatbot access, just quota-capped |

## 4.7.22 Accessibility Requirements

- Chatbot input has VoiceOver/TalkBack label "Ask a question about your data"
- Voice input mic button has label "Start voice dictation"
- Send arrow has label "Send message"
- Shortcut buttons have labels matching their visible text ("Log meal", "Log workout", etc.)
- X dismiss button has label "Close chat"
- Streaming responses announce completion: "Response complete" via aria-live polite after stream ends
- Text contrast meets WCAG 2.1 AA: 4.5:1 for body text in both light and dark modes
- All tap targets meet 44pt iOS / 48dp Android minimum
- Voice button animation respects Reduce Motion setting — static icon with simple color change when that setting is enabled
- Chat bubbles support dynamic text size scaling up to 200%
- Screen reader users can navigate the conversation area using swipe gestures; each bubble is a landmark
- The overlay traps focus while open (keyboard users don't Tab out of the overlay into the hidden dashboard behind it)

## 4.7.23 Edge Cases & Error Handling

- **User opens the chatbot with zero connections connected:** Chatbot works with Profile and Goals containers only. Answers like "I don't have fitness data connected yet — connect Apple Health to answer that." Shortcut buttons still functional for manual logging.
- **User asks in a non-English language:** v1 is English-only. Chatbot responds: "I only speak English in v1 — Spanish and others are coming." (Localization is a v2+ concern.)
- **User sends an extremely long message (>2000 chars):** Client-side rejects with "Message is too long — try asking a focused question."
- **User rapidly sends many messages (attempted abuse):** Client-side rate limit: max 1 send every 500ms. Server-side: max 5 queries in any 10-second window. Beyond that, 429 with "Slow down — you're sending too fast."
- **User closes overlay mid-streaming:** Stream is cancelled client-side. The partial response is discarded. The Core counter is NOT decremented for cancelled streams. Opening the overlay again shows a fresh input with no in-flight response.
- **User backgrounds app during streaming:** Stream continues server-side to completion; client reconnects on return and shows the completed response. If the user was away >30min, the session resets and the response is not restored.
- **Server returns empty response:** Displays "I didn't get a response — try asking differently." Does not decrement counter.
- **Conversation history exceeds 8 turns:** Oldest turns drop out silently. User can still see older bubbles by scrolling; they just aren't in the AI's context anymore.
- **User has Finance connected but AI consent is OFF and asks a finance question:** "You have finance data connected but haven't enabled AI access to it. Turn on Finance AI access in Settings to answer this." with a deep link to the settings.
- **Connection disconnection during active session:** If the user disconnects Apple Health mid-session, subsequent queries correctly show "no fitness data" even if the session had fitness context earlier. Container loads are per-query.
- **Query suggests harmful interpretation:** (e.g., user with disordered eating patterns asks about aggressive restriction) — the AI is system-prompted to not give harm-reinforcing advice and instead report neutral numbers. Detailed behavior in Section 7 (Security) and Section 10 (AI Requirements).
- **User asks question that requires data they've explicitly deleted:** Chatbot says "I don't have that data — you may have deleted it."
- **Timezone discrepancy (user traveling):** Container queries respect the user's current timezone (passed in `X-Client-Timezone` header). "Today" is computed client-local.
- **App is offline:** Overlay opens but shows "You're offline — chat needs a connection" below the input. Shortcut buttons still function if they don't require network (Log Workout freestyle works offline; Log Meal text parse does not).

## 4.7.24 Analytics Events

Tracked chatbot events for product analytics. All events respect privacy — no raw query text is sent to analytics unless the user explicitly reports the response.

- `chatbot_overlay_opened` (source_screen: home | fitness | nutrition | finance | life)
- `chatbot_overlay_dismissed` (duration_ms, message_count)
- `chatbot_shortcut_tapped` (shortcut_type, source_screen)
- `chatbot_query_sent` (intent_classification, domain_tags, input_method: text | voice, input_length_bucket)
- `chatbot_response_received` (latency_ms, token_count, containers_loaded, model_used, success)
- `chatbot_response_reported` (response_id, reason: inaccurate | unhelpful | offensive | other)
- `chatbot_response_thumbs_up` (response_id)
- `chatbot_response_thumbs_down` (response_id)
- `chatbot_voice_input_started`
- `chatbot_voice_input_success` (transcription_length_bucket)
- `chatbot_voice_input_failed` (reason: silence | error | cancelled)
- `chatbot_logging_intent_redirected` (detected_domain: meal | workout | transaction | task)
- `chatbot_redirect_overridden` (user tapped "that was a question — answer it")
- `chatbot_quota_warning_shown` (Core users at 5 queries remaining)
- `chatbot_quota_exceeded` (Core users hit cap)
- `chatbot_quota_upgraded` (user upgraded from quota prompt)
- `chatbot_container_dropped` (container_type, reason: timeout | consent_off | data_missing)
- `chatbot_out_of_scope_triggered` (category: medical | investment | external_world | other)
- `chatbot_privacy_elevation_occurred` (user query caused raw data access — e.g., merchant name lookup)

All events respect user privacy preferences. The raw query text, response text, and any PII are never sent to analytics platforms. Only metadata.

---

# Section 4.8 — Settings & Profile

## 4.8.1 Overview

The Settings & Profile area is where users see, edit, and audit everything Life Dashboard knows about them. It is the control surface for identity, subscription, connected data sources, privacy posture, app behavior, and account actions. It is deliberately not a dashboard, not a narrative, and not a place for AI interpretation — Settings is the utility layer.

### Philosophy

Three principles govern Settings:

1. **Single source of truth.** Every setting appears in exactly one place. No mirrored toggles on connection detail screens, no shortcut copies on category overflow menus. If a user wants to change something, there is one screen to find it.
2. **No AI in Settings.** Settings never invokes an AI call. The profile archetype and personalized insight generated during onboarding are preserved as a static artifact the user can re-read but never regenerates from this surface. Re-running AI from Settings would add cost for no user value and introduce a surprise paywall interaction.
3. **Every control is legible.** If the user changes a value with downstream effects (goal, timezone, units, consent), the consequence is shown before the save — not in a notification after the fact.

Settings is the most sensitive surface in the app for trust. A user who cannot find their consent toggles, cannot see what their subscription status actually is, or cannot delete their data confidently will churn. This section specifies every row, every toggle, every modal, and every downstream effect with the assumption that a skeptical privacy-conscious user will audit it line by line.

### What Settings Is Not

- Not a content feed. No daily brief, no "you could improve X," no motivational copy.
- Not a second home for features. Workout plan lives in Fitness; goal detail lives in the relevant category; notifications spec lives in Section 4.9. Settings shows entry points, not duplicate surfaces.
- Not a place for unsolicited AI. The archetype/insight shown on the Profile screen is a static record of what was generated at onboarding. No regenerate button.

### Success Metrics

- **Setting findability** — time-to-locate for common tasks (change goal, disconnect a source, change password, cancel subscription). Target: <20 seconds for any task via a usability study of 8+ users.
- **Consent comprehension** — percentage of users who, when shown a consent toggle, can correctly describe what it controls. Target: 85%+ on a post-change survey prompt.
- **Account deletion drop-off** — percentage of users who initiate account deletion and complete the flow. Target: >90% complete (the flow should not be so scary that legitimate deletes are abandoned; it should only be scary enough to prevent accidents).
- **Subscription self-service rate** — percentage of users who manage their subscription (upgrade/downgrade/cancel) without contacting support. Target: 95%+.

---

## 4.8.2 Entry Points & Navigation

### Primary Entry

A profile icon — a generic silhouette SVG at v1, placeholder for a future avatar system — appears in the top-right of the header on every top-level screen: Home Dashboard, Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Life. Tapping it opens the Main Settings screen.

- **Icon size:** 28×28pt tap target, 20×20pt glyph
- **Position:** Fixed far-right of the header strip, 16pt padding from screen edge
- **State:** Always rendered the same — no badge, no notification dot, no unread count. Settings is not a feed; nothing in it should appear "new" from a distance.
- **Haptic on tap:** Light impact on iOS / equivalent on Android

### Navigation Model

Settings is a push-navigation stack, not a modal. The user sees a primary Settings screen with grouped rows; tapping a row pushes a detail screen onto the nav stack. Back arrow returns one level.

- Swipe-to-go-back (iOS edge swipe, Android back gesture) works everywhere
- Back from the Main Settings screen returns to the originating top-level screen
- Opening Settings from Home then navigating to a detail screen (e.g., Profile) and backgrounding the app — on foreground, the user returns to the Profile detail screen, not the originating Home Dashboard. Standard iOS/Android stack behavior.

### Deep Links

Certain flows elsewhere in the app deep-link directly into a specific Settings screen:

- Onboarding "Connect your data" step → deep-links to Settings → Connections when a user wants to add more later
- Chatbot cap hit on Core → "Upgrade to Pro" button deep-links to Settings → Subscription
- Any "Manage consent" link in a privacy explainer modal → deep-links to Settings → Privacy
- Notification permission prompt dismissal → later, user taps "Enable notifications" → deep-links to Settings → Notifications
- Paywall modal's "See what Pro includes" → deep-links to Settings → Subscription → Compare plans

Deep-linked screens always include a back path to the originating context.

---

## 4.8.3 Main Settings Screen Layout

### Structure

The Main Settings screen is a vertically scrollable grouped list, matching the native iOS Settings pattern and the equivalent Android Material grouped list. Grouping provides visual separation between distinct concerns without requiring tabs or a sub-navigation model.

### Layout Top to Bottom

**1. Header strip (fixed, non-scrolling)**

- Left: Back arrow (returns to originating screen)
- Title: "Settings"
- Right: no actions (nothing lives at the top-right of Settings itself)

**2. Profile hero row**

A full-width row at the top of the list. Not a card — an elevated row matching the native grouped-list idiom with slightly larger vertical padding than standard rows.

- Left: Profile icon (same generic silhouette as the header entry point; v1 does not support photo upload)
- Primary label: User's first name (from onboarding Step 1) + last initial if provided, or just first name
- Secondary label: Email address
- Tertiary label: Plan status
  - Active paying user: "Life Dashboard Pro" or "Life Dashboard Core" (no decorative badge, no color accent — just text)
  - Trial user: "Pro trial · 5 days left" (italic for distinction)
  - Expired/lapsed: "Subscription expired — tap to renew"
- Tap target: entire row → Profile detail screen (4.8.4)

**3. Section: Account**

Grouped rows:
- Subscription → Subscription & Billing detail screen (4.8.5)
- Connections → Connections Management screen (4.8.6)
- Notifications → Notifications settings screen (deep-link into Section 4.9 UI)
- Privacy → Privacy Controls screen (4.8.7)
- Security → Security settings screen (4.8.10)

Each row shows label + right-chevron. Optional right-aligned secondary text shows state at a glance:
- Subscription row: "Pro" / "Core" / "Trial · 5d" / "Expired"
- Connections row: "4 connected" (count of active data sources)
- Notifications row: "On" / "Limited" / "Off"
- Privacy row: (no state shown)
- Security row: "Face ID on" / "App lock on" / (blank)

**4. Section: Preferences**

Grouped rows:
- Theme → Theme picker screen (4.8.9)
- Units → Units settings screen
- Language → Language settings screen
- Timezone → Timezone settings screen

Each row shows current value right-aligned: "Dark" / "Imperial" / "English" / "Auto · Mountain"

**5. Section: Support & About**

Grouped rows:
- Help & FAQs → FAQ screen (4.8.12)
- Contact Support → opens platform mail client with pre-filled `mailto:`
- Give Feedback → Feedback form screen
- Rate Life Dashboard → deep-link to App Store / Play Store review (shown once per 90 days max)
- What's New → Changelog screen
- Terms of Service → in-app web view
- Privacy Policy → in-app web view
- Open-Source Licenses → Licenses screen

**6. Section: Data & Account**

Grouped rows (last group, with visual distance and a warning color on destructive rows per platform convention):
- Export My Data → deep-link to Section 4.11 export flow
- Sign Out → Sign out confirmation modal
- Delete Account → deep-link to Section 4.11 account deletion flow (destructive, red text)

**7. Footer**

- "Life Dashboard v1.0.0 · build 1" (version + build pulled from app binary metadata)
- "Made with care in the USA"

Footer is not a row — plain text, centered, muted color, 32pt vertical padding above and below.

### Layout Constraints

- All rows are 56pt tall minimum (iOS Human Interface Guidelines 44pt minimum target, upsized for comfortable readability)
- Section headers are small uppercase text, muted color, 12pt font, 16pt padding above
- Destructive rows (Delete Account) use the platform's standard destructive color (iOS red, Android equivalent)
- No icons in row left positions (unlike iOS Settings default); this is a conscious call — Life Dashboard row labels are short enough to carry themselves, and icons on every row add visual noise without aiding scan

---

## 4.8.4 Profile Section

Tapping the Profile hero row on the Main Settings screen opens the Profile detail screen. The Profile screen is structured as **direct-edit sections** (v1.27). Each section captures a narrow block of user state and uses the minimum computation path for that edit — deterministic recomputation for math-bound fields, no AI calls except where explicitly user-initiated. There is no "redo onboarding" action; onboarding is a first-time-only flow (see §4.1.1).

### Profile Detail Screen Layout

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Profile"
- Right: no actions

**2. Identity block**

Top of the screen, large-format:
- Profile icon (generic silhouette — v1 has no avatar customization)
- Full name (first name + last initial if provided)
- Email (muted, small)
- Member since date ("Member since April 2026")

No edit affordance on this block in v1 — name and email changes happen via specific flows in §4.8.10 (email) and a dedicated "Change name" action at the bottom of this screen.

**3. Your Plan (static onboarding record)**

A collapsible card showing the plan summary that was generated during onboarding.

- Header: "Your Plan" with expand/collapse chevron
- When expanded:
  - Plan name matching the user's primary fitness goal context at onboarding (e.g., "Lose Weight", "Build Muscle", "Lean & Muscular", "Maintain")
  - Computed targets at the time of onboarding: RMR (with formula used), TDEE breakdown (RMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF), calorie target, protein/fat/carb targets (snapshot — current live targets live in the Body Stats and Goals sections below)
  - Scientific sources citations (collapsible inner list, same reference pool as §4.1.5)
  - Footer text: "Generated [date] from your onboarding answers. This is a static record and won't regenerate as your data changes."

This card is a snapshot of what the user saw at onboarding. It is a deterministic record of the profile computation, not a living artifact. The user cannot tap "regenerate" from this card — the explicit regenerate action is separate (see the Regenerate AI profile map section below).

**4. Body Stats section**

Direct-edit rows for body-composition fields. Each row shows the field label on the left and the current value on the right. Tapping a row opens a focused edit sheet.

| Field | Display format | Edit behavior | On save |
|---|---|---|---|
| Height | "5'10"" or "178 cm" | Sheet: height picker (ft/in or cm, matches user's unit pref) | Deterministic recompute of RMR → TDEE → calorie and macro targets |
| Weight | "185 lbs" or "84 kg" | Sheet: numeric input with unit label | Deterministic recompute of RMR → TDEE → calorie and macro targets |
| Birthday | "April 12, 1998 (age 28)" | Sheet: date picker | Deterministic recompute of RMR → TDEE → calorie and macro targets |
| Sex | "Male" / "Female" | Sheet: segmented control | Deterministic recompute of RMR → TDEE → calorie and macro targets |
| Body Fat % | "18%" or "—" if unset | Sheet: numeric input, optional (allow clear) | Deterministic recompute; if body fat % set, Katch-McArdle formula preferred over Mifflin-St Jeor |

All Body Stats edits are deterministic. **No AI calls.** Brief toast confirms: "Saved — targets updated." If any value was locked via the Advanced section, locked values are not overwritten.

**5. Daily Life section**

Direct-edit rows for lifestyle inputs captured during onboarding Step 3. Same edit pattern: tap row → focused sheet.

| Field | Display | On save |
|---|---|---|
| Occupation | "Engineer" (or custom string) | Deterministic recompute of NEAT → TDEE → macro targets |
| Work style | "Sedentary / Desk" | Deterministic recompute of NEAT → TDEE → macro targets |
| Activity level | "Lightly active" (from 4 discrete options) | Deterministic recompute of NEAT → TDEE → macro targets |
| Stress level | "Moderate" (from 4 discrete options) | No numeric target change; affects Life-category pattern detection only |

All Daily Life edits are deterministic. **No AI calls.**

**6. Diet Preferences section**

Direct-edit rows for dietary context.

| Field | Display | On save |
|---|---|---|
| Dietary pattern | "No restrictions" / "Vegetarian" / "Vegan" / "Pescatarian" / "Keto" / "Custom" | Flagged for next AI profile regeneration; no automatic regeneration |
| Food allergies | comma-separated list or "None" | Flagged for next AI profile regeneration; no automatic regeneration |
| Disliked foods | comma-separated list or "—" | Flagged for next AI profile regeneration; no automatic regeneration |

Editing Diet Preferences **does not automatically trigger any AI call.** It sets a flag on the user record indicating the AI profile map is out of sync with stated preferences. The user controls when to regenerate via the standalone action below.

#### Out-of-sync flag mechanics

The user record carries a single boolean `profile_map_out_of_sync` flag and a `profile_map_out_of_sync_reason` string. Behavior:

| Event | Flag change |
|---|---|
| Edit any Diet Preferences field (pattern, allergies, disliked foods) | Set flag to TRUE, reason := "diet_preferences_changed" |
| Regenerate AI profile map action completes successfully | Set flag to FALSE, reason cleared |
| Regenerate AI profile map action fails | Flag remains TRUE, reason preserved |
| Edit Body Stats, Daily Life, Goals, Advanced overrides | **No change to flag** (these don't affect the AI profile map — they drive deterministic math only) |
| Account deletion grace period restore | Flag state preserved from pre-deletion |

Flag only has one reason at a time; repeated Diet Preferences edits keep the flag TRUE with the same reason. The flag is a product UI hint (drives the "Out of sync" status indicator on the Regenerate action), not a scoring or math input.

A Pro user who changes Diet Preferences, then never regenerates, sees the "Out of sync" indicator indefinitely until they either (a) explicitly regenerate, (b) revert their Diet Preferences back to what they were before the last regeneration (the app compares current state to the last-regenerated snapshot; if they match, flag auto-clears), or (c) delete their account.

**Reset-on-regenerate:** the Regenerate action captures a snapshot of the Diet Preferences values at regeneration time into `last_regenerated_profile_state`. The flag auto-clears when current state matches this snapshot.

**No flag for other AI-adjacent state.** Chatbot context containers, meal scan history, workout parsing history — none set this flag. The flag exists solely for the AI profile map and its dependence on user-declared preferences.

**7. Goals**

The Goals row is a deep-link, not an editable list inline:

- Row label: "Goals"
- Row right: "3 active · 2 completed" (counts current and lifetime)
- Tap: navigates to Goals screen (§4.10)

Per the v1.27 unified goal system (§4.10.2), all goal editing happens in the Goals screen. Changing a fitness body-composition goal (or changing which fitness goal is the user's primary fitness goal) deterministically recomputes calorie and macro targets — but that recomputation flows from the Goals screen, not Settings. There is no goal-change confirmation modal in Settings because Settings does not edit goals.

**8. Advanced section — manual overrides (collapsed by default)**

An expandable section at the bottom of the screen, labeled "Advanced — manual overrides" with expand chevron. Collapsed state shows only the section header.

When expanded, reveals rows for power users who want to override deterministic computations. Each row has an edit sheet with a value input and a "Lock this value" toggle.

| Row | Display | Lock semantics |
|---|---|---|
| RMR | "1,685 kcal · Auto" or "1,700 kcal · Locked" | Locked value persists through Body Stats / Daily Life / Goal edits |
| NEAT multiplier | "1.4 · Auto" or "1.5 · Locked" | Locked value persists through Daily Life edits |
| Daily deficit/surplus | "-500 kcal · Auto" or "-400 kcal · Locked" | Locked value persists through Goal edits |
| Protein target | "156g · Auto" or "180g · Locked" | Locked value persists through all upstream edits |
| Carb target | Auto/Locked same pattern | Same |
| Fat target | Auto/Locked same pattern | Same |
| Sugar ceiling | "50g · Auto" or "40g · Locked" | Same |
| Fiber target | "30g · Auto" or "35g · Locked" | Same |
| Sodium ceiling | "2300mg · Auto" or "2000mg · Locked" | Same |

**Lock semantics:** A value marked "Locked" is not recomputed when upstream inputs change (weight, goal, work style, etc.). A value marked "Auto" recomputes silently whenever upstream inputs change. Locking is explicit — the user must toggle a switch inside the edit sheet; there is no accidental lock.

All Advanced edits are deterministic. **No AI calls.**

### Regenerate AI Profile Map (standalone action)

Below the Advanced section, a dedicated action row:

- Label: "Regenerate AI profile map"
- Right: status indicator — "Updated Apr 12, 2026" or "Out of sync (diet preferences changed)" if the out-of-sync flag is set
- Tap: confirmation modal before executing

**Confirmation modal:**
- Title: "Regenerate profile?"
- Body: "This will use 1 AI call and update your personalization profile based on your current settings. Your scores, calorie targets, and macro math are not affected — those are deterministic."
- Primary button: "Regenerate"
- Secondary button: "Cancel"

On confirm: single Haiku-class call per §10 AI inventory (same call profile as onboarding profile generation, estimated <$0.01). Updates the stored profile map used by chatbot context. **Never fires automatically** — always explicit user action.

### Profile-Specific Actions (not rows)

At the bottom of the Profile screen, below the Regenerate action, two utility rows:

- Change name → Sheet with first name + last name (optional) inputs
- Change email → see §4.8.10 Security section for email change flow (includes verification)

### Design Rationale — Why Sectional Direct Edit Replaces "Re-do Onboarding"

Earlier drafts (v1.9-v1.24) included a "Re-do onboarding" section with five focused flows (About You / Goal / Daily Life / Workout Plan / Connections). v1.27 reframes these as direct-edit sections within Profile:

- **Onboarding is first-time-only.** There is no user-facing concept of "redoing" it. Users edit individual sections as their life changes.
- **Goals are not edited in Settings.** Goals live in the Goals screen (§4.10). Fitness goal changes deterministically recompute calorie/macro targets from the Goals screen.
- **Minimum computation per edit.** Body Stats edit → RMR/TDEE/macros recompute (deterministic, fast). Daily Life edit → NEAT/TDEE/macros recompute (deterministic). Diet Preferences edit → flag only, no computation. Advanced edit → override that specific value. AI regeneration → explicit standalone action with its own confirmation.
- **No AI calls fire from any direct-edit section.** Only the explicit "Regenerate AI profile map" action uses AI.

Users change their life in chunks — a new job (Daily Life changed), a cut (Body Stats changed, perhaps a goal swap), a dietary shift (Diet Preferences changed). Sectional direct edit matches the real update patterns; a single "re-do onboarding" button or a bundled re-capture wizard would force them through unchanged questions and add friction.

**For Workout Plan changes:** plan editing is handled by the Fitness → Training Plan screen (§4.3.10), accessible directly from the Fitness tab. The Training Plan detail view supports Build a Plan (no plan exists), Edit Plan (opens Workout Builder with pre-populated answers), and Switch Plan (deactivate current and build new from scratch) — covering the full range of post-onboarding plan changes without requiring a "re-do" Settings path.

**For Connections changes:** the Connections row in the main Settings screen (§4.8.6) handles all integration management. The Profile screen does not deep-link to Connections because connection state is not profile state.

---

## 4.8.5 Subscription & Billing

### Subscription Screen Layout

Tapping Subscription from the Main Settings screen opens a screen structured to make plan status unambiguous and action paths clear.

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Subscription"
- Right: no actions

**2. Current plan block**

Full-width block at the top of the screen. Contents depend on plan state.

**State: Pro trial (active)**
- Large label: "Life Dashboard Pro"
- Status line: "Trial · 5 days remaining"
- Sub-line: "Then $9.99/month starting April 27, 2026"
- Primary button: "Manage subscription" (deep-links to App Store / Play Store subscription settings — platform requirement)
- Secondary text link: "Cancel trial" (deep-links to platform cancellation)

**State: Pro (paying)**
- Large label: "Life Dashboard Pro"
- Status line: "Active"
- Sub-line: "Renews April 20, 2026 · $9.99/month"
- Primary button: "Manage subscription"
- No cancel button — cancellation happens in platform settings and Life Dashboard cannot cancel in-app per Apple/Google policy

**State: Pro annual (paying)**
- Large label: "Life Dashboard Pro · Annual"
- Status line: "Active"
- Sub-line: "Renews September 15, 2026 · $99.99/year"
- Primary button: "Manage subscription"

**State: Core (paying)**
- Large label: "Life Dashboard Core"
- Status line: "Active"
- Sub-line: "Renews April 20, 2026 · $4.99/month"
- Primary button: "Upgrade to Pro" (opens Plan Compare sheet — see below)
- Secondary button: "Manage subscription"

**State: Expired / lapsed**
- Large label: "No active subscription"
- Status line: "Your Pro trial ended April 12, 2026"
- Sub-line: "Pick a plan to continue using Life Dashboard"
- Primary button: "Subscribe to Pro" → paywall flow (4.1.9)
- Secondary button: "Subscribe to Core"

**3. Plan comparison table**

A two-column table showing Core vs Pro feature parity, displayed below the current plan block. Not collapsible — always visible so users can see what they get (or are missing).

| Feature | Core | Pro |
|---|---|---|
| All four categories (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Manual and device-native logging | ✓ | ✓ |
| Plaid finance connection | ✓ | ✓ |
| Email and calendar accounts (Gmail + Outlook, any mix) | Unlimited | Unlimited |
| Active goals (v1.27) | 3 | 6 |
| AI chatbot | 10/day | 50/day (300/day soft cap) |
| Meal photo scan (standard, Sonnet) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Premium Scan (Opus meal photo) | — | ✓ (10/day) |
| AI meal parsing (text/voice) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Pantry Scanner | — | ✓ (10/day) |
| AI workout parsing + burn estimation | ✓ | ✓ |
| Workout plan generation | 1 / 30 days | 3 / 30 days |
| Workout plan revision | 3 per plan | 10 per plan |
| AI email drafts + style learning | — | ✓ (20/day) |
| Daily email summary | ✓ (counts against chatbot quota) | ✓ (does not count) |
| History | 90-day hot | 90-day hot + unlimited archived |
| Historical score immutability | 7 days | 30 days |
| CSV data export | ✓ | ✓ |
| PDF formatted report export | — | ✓ |
| Chatbot Audit retention | 30 days | 30 days |
| Priority email support | — | ✓ |
| Price | $4.99/mo or $49.99/yr | $9.99/mo or $99.99/yr |

The comparison table is the authoritative Core-vs-Pro reference in the product. Section 13 (Business Requirements) is the authoritative reference in the spec; this screen reflects Section 13's decisions at launch.

**4. Billing history (Pro and Core only)**

A collapsible section showing past transactions:
- Date
- Plan
- Amount
- Status (Paid / Refunded / Failed)

Deep-linking out to the App Store / Play Store for transaction receipts (neither platform exposes receipts inside third-party apps). Tapping a row opens the platform's transaction detail.

**5. Restore purchases**

A utility row at the bottom of the screen:
- "Restore purchases" → triggers RevenueCat's `restorePurchases()` call
- Shows a toast: "Looking for your subscription..." while the call runs
- Success toast: "Restored: Life Dashboard Pro"
- Failure toast: "No active subscription found for this Apple ID / Google account"

This exists because Apple requires a restore-purchases button in any app with IAP, and because it's the legitimate recovery path for users who deleted and reinstalled, switched devices, or changed Apple IDs.

### Trial Countdown Banner Behavior

During a Pro trial, a banner appears at the top of the Home Dashboard **only during the final 3 days of the trial**. Before day 5, the trial is only visible in Settings → Subscription.

**Banner spec (last 3 days only):**
- Thin strip, 32pt tall, full-width
- Background: subtle accent color
- Text: "Trial ends in 2 days" or "Trial ends tomorrow" or "Trial ends today"
- Right side: "View plan" text link → deep-links to Settings → Subscription
- Dismissal: tap X on the banner → hides until the next midnight local, then reappears the next day

**Rationale:** Persistent banners throughout a 7-day trial cross into nagging. The final 3 days is when the user actually needs a reminder; earlier than that, it's noise. The per-midnight re-appearance after dismissal balances user control (they can hide it for the rest of today) with Life Dashboard's legitimate interest in the user knowing their trial is ending.

### Payment Method Management
