Life Dashboard does not manage payment methods directly. All billing is routed through Apple App Store Connect or Google Play Billing via RevenueCat. The "Manage subscription" button deep-links to the platform's subscription management surface.

This is an Apple/Google requirement: apps cannot process payments outside their IAP systems for digital goods. We comply; we don't try to work around it.

### Upgrade, Downgrade, Cancel Mechanics

- **Core → Pro upgrade:** Opens the paywall modal (4.1.9) with Pro plan preselected. On purchase, the upgrade is immediate — Pro features unlock within seconds.
- **Pro → Core downgrade:** Handled in App Store / Play Store subscription settings. Downgrade takes effect at the end of the current billing period.
- **Cancel:** Handled in App Store / Play Store. Cancellation takes effect at the end of the current period; user retains access until then.
- **Re-subscribe after cancel:** Shows the paywall again with a friendly "Welcome back" copy variant.

---

## 4.8.6 Connections Management

### Naming Note

Life Dashboard uses the term **Connections** in all user-facing language. The engineering spec still refers to these as "connections" in section titles (Section 8 — Connections), API documentation, and database schema for precision. Users see "Connections"; developers read "connections" in technical references. The two terms refer to the same thing.

### Connections Screen Layout

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Connections"
- Right: no actions

**2. Connected section**

At the top of the screen, a grouped list of currently connected data sources. If none are connected, this section is hidden.

Each row shows:
- Connection icon (vendor-provided logo or Life Dashboard-designed icon)
- Connection name ("Apple Health" / "Google Calendar" / "Plaid — Chase Sapphire" / "Gmail — nate@gmail.com")
- Sync status line:
  - "Last synced 4 minutes ago" (green dot) — healthy
  - "Last synced 3 hours ago" (yellow dot) — stale but not broken
  - "Sync failed — tap to reconnect" (red dot) — needs attention
  - "Syncing now..." (spinner) — in progress
- Chevron right → Connection Detail screen

Tap target: entire row → opens Connection Detail screen.

**3. Available section**

Below Connected, a grouped list of unconnected sources the user could add, organized by category.

**Fitness**
- Apple Health (iOS) / Google Fit / Health Connect (Android)
- Garmin Connect (flagged "coming soon" if not available at launch)
- Oura
- Whoop
- Strava (flagged based on 2024 ToS status at launch)

**Nutrition**
- Apple Health water sync (iOS only)

**Finance**
- Plaid — Link a bank account

**Life**
- Google Calendar
- Apple Calendar (iOS EventKit)
- Microsoft Outlook (Calendar + Mail)
- Gmail
- Screen Time (iOS Family Controls) / Android Usage Stats
- Location & Places (Core Location / Android Location)

Each row shows:
- Connection icon
- Connection name
- One-line value prop: "Auto-sync weight, steps, workouts, sleep"
- Right-side "Connect" text button

Tap: launches the provider's connect flow (OAuth for cloud services, native permission prompt for system services). See 4.1.7 for the connection flow pattern — same flow, just reachable from Settings.

**4. Empty state (no connections)**

If the user has no connections and the Available list is also empty (edge case: user is on a device where no connections are supported), show:
- "No connections available on this device"
- Sub-text explaining what's missing (rare edge case — likely only happens on tablets or web)

If the user has no connections but Available is non-empty, the Connected section is hidden and Available is the first thing on screen. No illustrative empty-state imagery — just the Available list, with a small header text: "Connect your data sources to make Life Dashboard more accurate."

### Connection Detail Screen

Tapping a connected source opens a detail screen specific to that connection.

**Layout top to bottom:**

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: Connection name (e.g., "Apple Health")
- Right: no actions

**2. Status block**
- Connected as: provider account identifier (e.g., "nate@gmail.com" for Gmail, "Chase Sapphire Preferred ···4829" for Plaid)
- Last successful sync: relative timestamp
- Next scheduled sync: "in 4 hours" or "automatic"
- Manual sync button: "Sync now" (if the connection supports on-demand sync)

**3. What this connection pulls**

A static bulleted list of what Life Dashboard receives from this source. Exact list is connection-specific; examples:

**Apple Health**
- Weight from your scale or manual entries
- Daily step count
- Workouts (duration, type, heart rate, calories)
- Sleep duration and stages
- Heart rate variability and resting heart rate

**Plaid (Chase Sapphire Preferred)**
- Account balance (updated daily)
- Transactions (updated hourly)
- No access to moving money — read-only

**Gmail**
- Email metadata (sender, subject, received time)
- First 200 characters of email body (for classification only)
- We never access your full email content

This list is static content per connection — not AI-generated, not dynamic. It is the plain-language disclosure of what the connection does.

**4. AI context controls**

Per-data-type toggles for this connection's AI context inclusion. These mirror the canonical controls in Settings → Privacy (4.8.7); changes made here sync immediately to the Privacy screen and vice versa.

Wait — per Q12, every setting lives in exactly one place. These toggles do **not** appear on the Connection Detail screen. Instead, the Connection Detail screen has a text link at this position: "Manage what this connection sends to AI →" which deep-links to Settings → Privacy with the relevant section expanded.

**5. Disconnect button**

At the bottom of the screen, in destructive styling:
- "Disconnect [Connection Name]"
- Tap: confirmation modal
  - Title: "Disconnect [Connection Name]?"
  - Body: "Life Dashboard will stop receiving data from [Connection Name]. Your historical data from this source will be deleted from Life Dashboard within 30 days."
  - Primary button: "Disconnect" (destructive red)
  - Secondary button: "Cancel"
- On confirm:
  - OAuth token is revoked server-side
  - Connection row moves from Connected to Available
  - 30-day server-side deletion job is scheduled
  - Toast: "[Connection Name] disconnected. Your data will be deleted within 30 days."

### Multi-Account Connections

Some connections support multiple accounts. The Connected list shows each account as a separate row.

**Tier limits:**
- **Core**: up to 2 email accounts total (any mix of Gmail and Outlook) + up to 2 calendar accounts total (any mix of Google Calendar and Outlook Calendar). Plaid supports as many bank accounts as the user has (not tier-gated).
- **Pro**: unlimited email and calendar accounts. Plaid unchanged.

Example Connected list for a Core user with one Gmail, one Outlook, two calendars, and two bank accounts — all within Core limits:
- Apple Health
- Gmail — nate@gmail.com
- Outlook — nate@topspin.biz
- Google Calendar — personal
- Outlook Calendar — work
- Plaid — Chase Sapphire Preferred ···4829
- Plaid — Ally Savings ···1102

**Paywall trigger behavior:**
- On Core, tapping "Add email account" when already at 2 email accounts triggers the paywall with messaging: *"Life Dashboard Core supports 2 email accounts. Upgrade to Pro to add more."*
- Same behavior for calendar accounts at Core's 2-account limit
- The paywall deep-links to Subscription (4.8.5) with Pro pre-selected

**Plaid is not tier-gated for account count.** Users with 5 bank accounts on Core see all 5 connections normally. The multi-account tier gate applies specifically to email and calendar because those are the cloud connections where each additional account carries linear backend cost (more OAuth maintenance, more API polling, more storage).

### Sync Errors & Recovery

When a connection's sync fails:
- The row shows a red status dot and "Sync failed — tap to reconnect"
- Tapping the row opens the Connection Detail screen
- A banner at the top of the detail screen explains the failure:
  - OAuth expired: "Your login expired. Reconnect to continue syncing."
  - API error: "We couldn't reach [Provider]. We'll try again automatically."
  - Permission revoked: "You revoked access in your [Provider] account. Reconnect to continue."
- Primary button on the banner: "Reconnect" → launches OAuth flow again

No AI is invoked in any connection management flow.

### Connection Icons and Branding

Connection icons are either:
- Vendor-provided assets (Apple Health, Google Calendar) following their brand guidelines
- Life Dashboard-designed icons where vendors don't supply one

All icons follow the same sizing and color treatment so the Connected list feels coherent, not like a logo grab-bag.

---

## 4.8.7 Privacy Controls & Chatbot Audit

Privacy is the single source of truth for what data Life Dashboard sends to AI and how historical data is handled. No privacy controls live on any other screen.

### Privacy Screen Layout

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Privacy"
- Right: no actions

**2. Plain-language summary**

Top of screen, single paragraph:

> "Life Dashboard sends specific data to AI (Anthropic's Claude) when you use AI features like the chatbot. You control what gets sent. Nothing happens to your data without your consent, and you can delete any category at any time."

No link to privacy policy in this summary (there's a separate Terms & Privacy section in Support & About). The summary is in-app language, not legal language.

**3. AI context inclusion section**

Grouped list of per-data-type toggles controlling what the chatbot and other AI features can see. Each row is a toggle (iOS switch / Android material switch) with a label and a short descriptor below.

| Toggle | Default | Descriptor |
|---|---|---|
| Include Fitness data in AI context | ON (if connected) | Workouts, weight, sleep, recovery |
| Include Nutrition data in AI context | ON (if any logged) | Meals, macros, targets |
| Include Finance data in AI context | ON (if connected) | Budget, spending categories (never raw merchant names by default) |
| Include Calendar in AI context | ON (if connected) | Event categories and counts (never event titles by default) |
| Include Email in AI context | ON (if connected) | Sender + subject + first 200 chars (never full body) |
| Include Location in AI context | OFF | Labeled places only, never coordinates |
| Include Screen Time in AI context | OFF | Category totals, never app-by-app data |
| Include Tasks in AI context | ON | Full task descriptions (user-authored, low-sensitivity) |

Defaults follow the sensitivity gradient established in 4.6.18: location and screen time require explicit opt-in; less sensitive sources default ON when the connection is present.

Toggling any switch OFF takes effect immediately — the next chatbot query will not load that container. A brief toast confirms: "[Data type] excluded from AI context."

**4. Data retention section**

Shows how long each data type is retained on Life Dashboard servers before deletion or coarsening:

| Data type | Retention |
|---|---|
| Meal, workout, weight logs | Permanent until account deleted |
| Transaction data | Permanent until account deleted or connection disconnected |
| Email cache (for AI classification) | 30 days rolling |
| Calendar cache | 90 days rolling |
| Location visits (exact coordinates) | 24 hours; then coarsened to labeled places |
| Screen time per-app data | 7 days rolling (iOS) / 30 days rolling (Android) |
| Chatbot audit records | 7 days (Core) / 30 days (Pro) |
| Anonymous analytics events | 90 days |

This section is static informational content. No controls — retention is set by the product; users who want shorter retention use the "Delete specific data" actions below.

**5. Delete specific data section**

Destructive actions grouped together. Each row is a deletion trigger with a descriptive label and a confirmation modal.

- Delete all meal history → modal: "Delete all meals? This will permanently remove [N] meals logged over [date range]. This cannot be undone."
- Delete all workout history → same pattern
- Delete all weight history → same pattern
- Delete all financial transactions → same pattern, includes language about disconnecting Plaid automatically
- Delete all calendar cache → same pattern
- Delete all email cache → same pattern
- Delete all location history → same pattern
- Delete all screen time history → same pattern
- Delete all chatbot audit records → same pattern

Each modal requires the user to tap a destructive button to confirm. No typed confirmation for per-category deletes (reserved for full account deletion — 4.11).

Deletion is immediate on the client (data hidden from UI) and triggers a server-side deletion job that completes within 72 hours. A toast confirms: "Deleted. Data will be purged from our servers within 72 hours."

**6. Chatbot Audit section**

A row: "Chatbot Audit" with right chevron → Chatbot Audit screen.

### Chatbot Audit Screen

This screen surfaces exactly what data was sent to Anthropic for each of the user's recent chatbot queries. It is the transparency mechanism that makes privacy claims verifiable.

**Layout:**

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Chatbot Audit"
- Right: no actions

**2. Retention note**
- Muted text at top: "Showing queries from the last 7 days" (Core) or "Showing queries from the last 30 days" (Pro)

**3. Query list**

Reverse chronological list of chatbot queries. Each row shows:
- Timestamp (relative: "2 minutes ago", "Yesterday at 3:14 PM")
- First line of user's query (truncated at 80 chars with ellipsis)
- Chevron right

Tap: expands into detail.

**4. Per-query detail (expanded state or separate screen)**

For each query, show:
- Full user query text
- Timestamp
- Model used (e.g., "Haiku 4.5")
- Response length (e.g., "answered in 187 tokens")
- **Context containers loaded** — list of containers with their field names:
  - ProfileContext: age, sex, primary_goal, archetype
  - NutritionContext: today_calories_remaining, today_protein_eaten, weekly_avg_calories
  - FinanceContext: safe_to_spend_week, weekly_budget, spending_categories (redacted to category names)
- **Elevated fields** (if any): list of specific raw data that was included because the query required it
  - Example: "User asked about 'my Whole Foods spending'. Raw merchant names for Whole Foods transactions were included for this query only."
- Delete button: "Delete this audit record"

The container list shows container names + field names (the fields of the schema that were populated and sent). It does not show the raw values of those fields — displaying those would require additional storage and wouldn't meaningfully improve user trust (the user already knows what values their data has). It also does not show the full prompt text — that would require a separate prompt-logging pipeline that v1 doesn't build.

**5. Export chatbot audit**

A button at the bottom of the screen: "Export audit as JSON" → downloads a JSON file with all audit records (last 7 or 30 days depending on tier). GDPR Article 15 compliance. File lands in the platform's standard downloads location.

### Why This Transparency Pattern

Skeptical users need to verify our privacy claims, not just read them. By showing containers + fields per query, we make it possible for a user to:
- See that their finance data was loaded when they asked "can I afford dinner?"
- See that their location data was NOT loaded if their location toggle is off (the container is absent from the list)
- Verify that merchant names were redacted to categories unless they explicitly asked about a merchant
- Audit what happened across a suspicious-feeling query they want to double-check

The Chatbot Audit is buried (two taps from Main Settings: Privacy → Chatbot Audit) because it's for hardcore skeptics, not casual users. Casual users never need to see it. Skeptics find it immediately because it's clearly labeled in the Privacy screen.

---

## 4.8.8 Notifications (Entry Point)

Notifications are a cross-domain system with their own full spec in Section 4.9. Settings surfaces the user-facing controls; Section 4.9 is authoritative for the signal pool architecture, AI evaluator, scheduling engine, and trigger rules.

### Notifications Settings Screen

**1. Header**
- Back arrow
- Title: "Notifications"
- Right: no actions

**2. System permission state**

At the top, a status block showing the OS-level notification permission:

- **Granted:** "Life Dashboard can send you notifications" with green checkmark
- **Denied:** "Notifications are off for Life Dashboard" with a button: "Open System Settings" (deep-links to iOS Settings / Android app settings)

Life Dashboard does not use iOS provisional authorization in v1 — notifications are either explicitly granted or not available. If system permission is denied, all toggles below are disabled (visible but dimmed, showing their last known state).

**3. Master toggle**

Single toggle: "Push notifications" (iOS/Android switch). Default ON when system permission is granted.

Toggling this OFF disables all Life Dashboard notifications without changing individual category toggles below. Toggling it back ON restores the prior per-category settings.

**4. Aggressiveness**

Row: "Aggressiveness" with current value shown right-aligned ("Medium"). Tapping opens the Aggressiveness Detail Screen.

**Aggressiveness Detail Screen:**
- Three single-select rows:
  - **Low** — Up to 2 notifications/day. Only critical and high-priority alerts.
  - **Medium** (default) — Up to 5 notifications/day. Critical, high, and medium-priority alerts. Most users pick this.
  - **High** — Up to 8 notifications/day. All alerts including gentle nudges. *Pro only* — on Core, shown greyed with "Pro required" badge; tapping opens the paywall.

Selection applies immediately. Section 4.9.6 is authoritative for what each level includes.

**5. Category toggles**

| Toggle | Default |
|---|---|
| Fitness notifications | ON |
| Nutrition notifications | ON |
| Finance notifications | ON |
| Life notifications | ON |
| Account & billing | ON (non-disablable — critical billing alerts always fire) |

Toggling a category OFF stops signal emission at the rule layer (Section 4.9.4) — not just delivery suppression. Zero AI spend accrues for a disabled category.

**6. Quiet hours**

- Toggle: "Respect quiet hours" (default OFF)
- When ON, two time inputs: "From [9:00 PM] to [7:00 AM]"
- Sub-toggle (nested, only visible when quiet hours ON): "Allow critical alerts during quiet hours" (default ON)
  - When ON: critical signals (payment failed, bill due today, trial ending today) fire with full alert even during quiet hours
  - When OFF: even critical signals wait until end of quiet hours

Section 4.9.10 specifies the batching behavior for signals that arrive during quiet hours.

**7. Show amounts in notifications**

- Toggle: "Show amounts in notifications" (default OFF)
- When OFF: dollar amounts are never shown on the lock screen ("Rent is due tomorrow")
- When ON: dollar amounts appear in notification text ("Rent of $1,200 is due tomorrow")

Merchant names are never shown in notifications regardless of this toggle — merchant data is considered more sensitive than amounts.

**8. Notification audit**

Row: "Notification audit" → Notification Audit Screen (specified in Section 4.9.15).

The audit surfaces a history of recently-fired notifications with the evaluator's reasoning. Retention: 7 days Core / 30 days Pro.

### What This Settings Surface Does Not Do

Per single-source-of-truth principle (Section 4.8.1), all notification behavior is configured here. There are no per-notification-type toggles — the aggressiveness dial is the primary control, and the evaluator handles individual firing decisions based on it.

Users who want to disable one specific notification type (e.g., "I never want meal reminders") can either:
- Configure it at the rule layer (meal reminders require user to set specific times; leaving times unset means no meal reminders fire)
- Use the Notification Audit to delete past records if the concern is retrospective

This intentionally trades per-type granularity for interface simplicity. The aggressiveness dial + category toggles + time-based rules cover the vast majority of user preferences.

---

## 4.8.9 App Preferences

Preferences are non-privacy, non-account settings that affect how the app looks and behaves. Each preference has its own detail screen.

### Theme Screen

**1. Header** — "Theme"

**2. Options** — three single-select rows:
- Dark (default on first launch)
- Light
- Match System (follows OS dark mode setting; toggles automatically)

The Flask app included a "medium" theme; v1 cuts this. Medium is a compromise that no user actually prefers; Dark or Light is the cleaner offer.

Selection applies immediately — the entire app re-renders in the new theme within 300ms. Status bar color updates to match. No confirmation, no reload required.

### Units Screen

**1. Header** — "Units"

**2. Primary toggle** — single-select:
- Imperial (lb, ft/in, miles, °F, fl oz)
- Metric (kg, cm, km, °C, ml)

On first install, Life Dashboard auto-detects based on device locale and sets this toggle accordingly. The user can override at any time.

Changing units is global — one toggle flips every number in the app. Split toggles per domain (weight in kg but distance in miles) are not supported; mixed systems are confusing and not how real people actually want data presented.

**3. Change effect disclosure**

Below the toggle, in muted text:
- "Changing units re-displays all data in the new system. Your actual stored values don't change."

### Language Screen

**1. Header** — "Language"

**2. Options** — single-select:
- English (default)
- Spanish

A footer note: "More languages coming in future updates."

v1 supports English and Spanish only. Flask supports 10 languages with significant dynamic-string gaps (TECH_DEBT P3-22); rather than inherit that partial translation state, v1 ships with two fully-translated languages and adds more in v1.1+.

Selecting Spanish applies immediately across all UI, including dynamic strings. No restart required.

### Timezone Screen

**1. Header** — "Timezone"

**2. Mode toggle**
- Automatic (follows device timezone)
- Manual (user selects from a list)

When Automatic is selected, shows a read-only row below: "Current: Mountain Time (UTC-6)"

When Manual is selected, shows a searchable dropdown of IANA timezone names with friendly labels (per Flask's existing pattern: "Mountain (UTC−7/−6)", "Eastern (UTC−5/−4)", etc.).

**3. Why timezone matters**

Below the controls, muted text:
- "Timezone controls when your daily scores reset at midnight, when daily chatbot limits reset, and when notifications are scheduled."

Timezone is load-bearing for midnight-local events: daily score calculation, Core chatbot quota reset (4.7.16), streak rollover, notification scheduling. A single source of truth here prevents bugs where different parts of the app use different timezones.

### Haptics Screen

Not a detail screen — a single toggle on the Preferences screen:
- "Haptic feedback" (iOS/Android switch, default ON)

When OFF, the app suppresses all haptic feedback — button taps, pull-to-refresh, successful saves, sliders. Users on older devices or with sensory sensitivities appreciate this.

No further granularity (per-action haptic control) in v1.

---

## 4.8.10 Security

Security covers biometric unlock, app lock, password changes, email changes, and session management.

### Security Screen Layout

**1. Header** — "Security"

**2. Biometric Unlock section**

- Toggle: "Unlock with Face ID" / "Unlock with Touch ID" / "Unlock with biometric" (label depends on device capability)
- State: ON if user enrolled during onboarding (per 4.1.3), OFF if declined

Toggling ON triggers the same enrollment flow as onboarding — the OS prompts for biometric, and on success, Life Dashboard stores a flag that future app-lock events should use biometric authentication.

Toggling OFF is instant and requires no confirmation.

If the device doesn't support biometrics or the user has none enrolled at the OS level, this toggle is hidden entirely.

**3. App Lock section**

- Toggle: "Require unlock to open Life Dashboard"
- When ON, expands to show sub-option:
  - Timeout: [Always | After 1 min backgrounded | After 5 min backgrounded | After 15 min backgrounded]
- Default: OFF

**App Lock behavior when ON:**
- User backgrounds the app
- After the selected timeout passes, the next foreground event triggers a lock screen
- Lock screen: Life Dashboard logo + "Unlock with [biometric name]" or "Enter passcode"
- Biometric attempt falls back to passcode after 3 failed tries
- Passcode entry uses the OS-level device passcode (not a Life Dashboard-specific PIN) — set via the platform's native authentication API
- On successful unlock, app resumes where the user left off

App Lock is opt-in. Users with sensitive Finance and Life data appreciate it; casual users find it friction. Defaulting OFF respects the majority; making it opt-in-friendly covers the minority.

**4. Password Change**

A row: "Change password" → opens Password Change Sheet

**Password Change Sheet:**
- Current password field (masked)
- New password field (masked)
- Confirm new password field (masked)
- Primary button: "Change password"
- On tap:
  - Biometric re-auth prompt (if biometric is enabled)
  - Validates current password with Clerk
  - Validates new password against Clerk's strength rules
  - Confirms new password matches confirmation field
  - Clerk API call to update password
  - Signs out all other devices for security
  - Success toast: "Password changed. Other devices have been signed out."

**5. Email Change**

A row: "Change email" → opens Email Change Flow

**Email Change Flow:**
- Screen 1: "Change your email"
  - Current email shown (disabled field)
  - New email field
  - Current password field (required for auth re-verification)
  - Button: "Send verification"
- On submit:
  - Clerk API call to initiate email change
  - Verification email sent to new address
  - Screen 2: "Check your new email"
    - "We sent a link to [new email]. Tap the link to confirm the change."
    - Resend button
- User taps link in email → returns to app with change confirmed
- Success toast: "Email updated to [new email]"

Until verification completes, the account email remains the old one. Clerk manages the pending state.

**6. Sessions**

A row: "Active sessions" → opens Sessions screen.

**Sessions Screen:**
- Header: "Active sessions"
- Informational text: "You can sign out of all devices except this one."
- Current device shown at top with "This device" label
- Below: button "Sign out of all other devices"
- On tap: confirmation modal
  - "Sign out of all other devices?"
  - "You'll stay signed in on this device. Other devices will be signed out immediately."
  - Primary button: "Sign out others" (destructive red)
  - Secondary button: "Cancel"

**Per-device session listing is deferred to v1.1+.** Clerk's API provides it, but surfacing individual device rows adds UI complexity for a feature most users will never use. The "sign out of all others" bulk action is the 80% value.

---

## 4.8.11 Data & Account

This section surfaces entry points to flows fully specified in Section 4.11 (Data Export & Account).

### Rows

**Export My Data**
- Row on Main Settings screen: "Export My Data"
- Tap: deep-links to Section 4.11 Export flow
- Available to all tiers (GDPR Article 15 compliance — access to personal data must not be conditioned on payment)
- Section 4.11 is authoritative for the export mechanics

**Sign Out**
- Row: "Sign Out"
- Tap: confirmation modal
  - "Sign out of Life Dashboard?"
  - "You'll need to sign in again next time."
  - Primary: "Sign out"
  - Secondary: "Cancel"
- On confirm: Clerk session terminated, user returned to the splash/sign-in screen (4.1.2)
- No data is deleted — sign out is recoverable

**Delete Account**
- Row: "Delete Account" (destructive red color)
- Tap: deep-links to Section 4.11 Account Deletion flow

### Deletion Flow Summary (per Q24)

Section 4.11 is authoritative for this flow; the summary here is for cross-section coherence:

1. User taps Delete Account → Section 4.11 flow begins
2. Warning screen: "Deleting your account cannot be undone"
3. Consequences list: what's deleted (all categories of data), what stops (subscription auto-cancels), grace period (30 days)
4. Type confirmation: user must type "DELETE" in a text field to enable the final button
5. Final button: "Delete my account"
6. Biometric re-auth prompt
7. Account enters 30-day grace period — user is signed out, data is retained but not accessible
8. Email confirmation sent with a link to cancel deletion during grace period
9. After 30 days, full server-side purge per existing cascade (per DATABASE_SCHEMA.md)

Deletion is immediate from the user's perspective (signed out, no longer able to log in), but reversible within the grace period. Full details in Section 4.11.

---

## 4.8.12 Support & About

### Help & FAQs

A screen of curated FAQs covering the most common support questions. v1 ships with a hand-written FAQ list (50-80 entries); no web-based help center at launch.

**FAQ Screen Layout:**
- Header: "Help & FAQs"
- Search bar at top (client-side filter)
- Grouped list by topic:
  - Getting Started
  - Tracking Meals
  - Tracking Workouts
  - Connecting Your Data
  - Subscription & Billing
  - Privacy & Security
  - Troubleshooting
- Each FAQ: question as row label, tap → expands or opens detail screen with answer + relevant screenshots

FAQs are shipped in the app bundle as markdown files (or a structured JSON) — no network call required to read them. This means FAQs work offline and load instantly.

FAQ content itself is out of scope for this PRD; the Support team writes and maintains the content. The Settings surface is the entry point and the app-side rendering.

### Contact Support

Row: "Contact Support" → opens platform mail client with pre-filled `mailto:`

- To: support@apexlife.app (exact address TBD)
- Subject: "Life Dashboard Support — [auto-appended user ID + app version]"
- Body: empty, cursor placed at top

The auto-appended user ID and app version help the support team diagnose issues without requiring users to hunt for diagnostic info. The user can delete this metadata before sending; we prefill it for convenience.

### Give Feedback

Row: "Give Feedback" → opens Feedback Form Screen

**Feedback Form Screen:**
- Header: "Give feedback"
- Category picker: Bug report / Feature request / General feedback
- Text area: "What's on your mind?"
- Attach screenshot button (optional): opens platform photo picker
- Submit button

On submit:
- POST to Life Dashboard feedback endpoint with: user ID, app version, OS + device, feedback text, attached image (if any)
- Success toast: "Thanks — we read every piece of feedback."
- Screen auto-dismisses after 2 seconds

Feedback is triaged by the Support team; no automated AI processing in v1.

### Rate Life Dashboard

Row: "Rate Life Dashboard" — shown only if:
- User has been active for at least 14 days
- User has not rated in the last 90 days
- User hasn't dismissed the rate prompt in the last 14 days

Tap: platform's native rate-app prompt (iOS `SKStoreReviewController`, Android Play In-App Review API). Both platforms cap how often these prompts can appear per year — Life Dashboard respects those caps.

### What's New

Row: "What's New" → opens Changelog Screen

**Changelog Screen:**
- Header: "What's New"
- List of recent app versions, reverse chronological
- Each version shows: version number + release date + bulleted list of user-facing changes
- Tap a version to expand full notes

Changelog content is maintained by the product team and shipped in the app bundle.

### Terms of Service

Row: "Terms of Service" → in-app web view loading the public ToS URL. Back button returns to Settings.

### Privacy Policy

Row: "Privacy Policy" → in-app web view loading the public Privacy Policy URL.

### Open-Source Licenses

Row: "Open-Source Licenses" → Licenses Screen

**Licenses Screen:**
- Header: "Open-Source Licenses"
- Scrollable list of every open-source package used in the app with its license text
- Required by MIT, Apache 2.0, and other permissive licenses when redistributing code

Content is auto-generated from the app's dependency manifest at build time, not hand-written.

### App Version Footer

Already specified in 4.8.3 — plain text at the bottom of the Main Settings screen showing version and build number. Tap 7 times to reveal a hidden debug screen (dev-only feature, not exposed to production users).

---

## 4.8.13 Data Persistence

### New Database Tables

Settings-specific data extends existing tables rather than introducing a large number of new ones. The key new tables:

**user_preferences** — app preferences keyed to user_id:
- user_id (PK, FK to users)
- theme (enum: dark, light, system)
- units (enum: imperial, metric)
- language (enum: en, es)
- timezone_mode (enum: auto, manual)
- timezone (string, IANA identifier)
- haptics_enabled (boolean, default true)
- app_lock_enabled (boolean, default false)
- app_lock_timeout (enum: always, 1min, 5min, 15min)
- biometric_enabled (boolean, default false)
- trial_banner_dismissed_date (date, nullable) — tracks the last date the trial banner was dismissed so it reappears next day

**user_ai_consent** — per-data-type AI context toggles:
- user_id (PK, FK to users)
- fitness_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- nutrition_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- finance_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- calendar_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- email_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- location_ai_consent (boolean, default false)
- screen_time_ai_consent (boolean, default false)
- tasks_ai_consent (boolean, default true)
- last_updated_at (timestamp)

**user_macro_locks** — which macro/energy targets are user-locked vs auto:
- user_id (PK, FK to users)
- rmr_locked (boolean, default false) + rmr_override (integer, nullable)
- neat_locked (boolean, default false) + neat_override (decimal, nullable)
- deficit_locked (boolean, default false) + deficit_override (integer, nullable)
- protein_locked (boolean, default false) + protein_override (integer, nullable)
- carbs_locked (boolean, default false) + carbs_override (integer, nullable)
- fat_locked (boolean, default false) + fat_override (integer, nullable)
- sugar_locked (boolean, default false) + sugar_override (integer, nullable)
- fiber_locked (boolean, default false) + fiber_override (integer, nullable)
- sodium_locked (boolean, default false) + sodium_override (integer, nullable)

**user_archetype** — preserved onboarding artifact:
- user_id (PK, FK to users)
- archetype_name (string)
- archetype_insight (text, 2-3 sentences)
- generated_at (timestamp)

This table is written once during onboarding (4.1.5) and read-only thereafter.

**chatbot_audit** — per-query audit records for Chatbot Audit screen:
- audit_id (PK)
- user_id (FK)
- query_text (text)
- timestamp
- model_used (string)
- containers_loaded (JSON array of container names + fields populated)
- elevated_fields (JSON array, nullable)
- response_token_count (integer)

Retention: 7 days for Core, 30 days for Pro. A nightly job purges records past the tier's retention.

### Existing Tables Affected

- **users table:** no schema changes; Settings surfaces existing fields.
- **user_onboarding:** read by the Profile screen to show captured onboarding values; written during Re-do onboarding flows.
- **user_goals:** read by the Goal section; written during Goal Change flow and Re-do Goal.

### Server-Side vs Client-Side State

All Settings state is persisted server-side (per Q2 architectural decision from Section 4.6). This matches the multi-device continuity principle: a user who installs on a new device gets their full Settings state restored after sign-in.

Exception: the trial banner dismissal state (`trial_banner_dismissed_date`) is primarily client-side for responsiveness but synced to the server on next API call so the banner doesn't reappear on a different device where the user already dismissed it today.

---

## 4.8.14 AI Usage in Settings

**Zero AI calls originate from the Settings surface in v1.**

This is a deliberate decision:
- The archetype and personalized insight from onboarding are preserved as a static artifact on the Profile screen; regeneration is not offered.
- Profile edits trigger deterministic recomputation (RMR, TDEE, macros) — no AI.
- Re-do onboarding flows re-use the onboarding AI calls when applicable (workout plan generation on Re-do Workout Plan), but those calls happen inside the Fitness context, not Settings.
- All category-specific AI features (chatbot, meal parsing, photo scan, etc.) happen in their native category tabs.

Settings is a utility layer. It costs nothing to run per session and introduces zero cost exposure from the subscription economics model.

### Re-do Workout Plan AI Call (Exception Note)

The Re-do Workout Plan flow invokes the same AI call specified in 4.1.12 (Workout Plan Generation Call): Claude Sonnet, ~$0.008, ~10s. This call is not an "AI Usage in Settings" cost because:
- It's user-invoked via an explicit Re-do button
- It's attributed to the Fitness category's AI budget in 4.3.14
- It respects the paywall: Core users pay with their free interaction counter; Pro users have unlimited plan regenerations

The Settings surface launches the flow; the AI call executes inside it. Settings itself logs zero tokens.

---

## 4.8.15 Friction-Reduction Commitments

| Potential Friction | Reduction Applied |
|---|---|
| Settings hidden in a tab or hamburger menu | Removed — profile icon fixed in top-right of every top-level screen |
| Multiple screens for related toggles | Removed — single Privacy screen is the canonical location for AI consent |
| Consent toggles reset after an OAuth reconnect | Removed — consent state persists across OAuth lifecycle |
| Accidental macro slider edits during scroll | Removed — read-only rows on Profile screen, sliders live only inside dedicated edit sheets |
| "Regenerate profile" button creating surprise AI cost | Removed — archetype is a static onboarding artifact; no regeneration |
| Full onboarding repeat required to change one thing | Removed — five focused Re-do flows target specific sections |
| Silent goal change rippling through targets without warning | Removed — Goal Change sheet shows numeric before/after preview |
| Mixed imperial/metric display (weight in lb, distance in km) | Removed — single global units toggle |
| Trial countdown nagging throughout 7-day trial | Reduced — banner appears only in final 3 days, dismissable per-day |
| Unclear subscription state | Removed — current-plan block shows plan, status, renewal date, and action in one place |
| Subscription cancel impossible in-app (Apple/Google requirement) | Acknowledged honestly — "Manage subscription" deep-links to platform; no pretense of in-app cancel |
| Account deletion trivially easy (accidental) | Controlled — typed "DELETE" confirmation + biometric re-auth + 30-day grace |
| Account deletion so scary users abandon legitimate deletes | Managed — confirmation is explicit but not multi-step-verification heavy |
| Restoring a lost subscription requires contacting support | Removed — Restore Purchases button uses platform APIs |
| Signing out one device requires signing out everywhere | Addressed — "Sign out of all other devices" as a bulk action in v1; per-device in v1.1+ |
| Privacy claims unverifiable | Removed — Chatbot Audit shows containers + fields loaded per query |
| Per-data-type deletion requires contacting support | Removed — granular deletion buttons in Privacy screen |
| Notification permission prompt feels hostile | Managed — system permission state shown in-app; clear path to Open System Settings if denied |
| Unknown app version for bug reports | Removed — version + build shown at footer of Main Settings |

---

## 4.8.16 Accessibility Requirements

- All interactive rows are minimum 56pt tall (well above 44pt native guideline)
- Every row has a descriptive accessibility label, including its state ("Language, currently English" for a row that shows "Language" + "English" secondary)
- Every toggle announces its new state on change ("Haptic feedback, on" → toggle → "Haptic feedback, off")
- Every destructive action (Disconnect, Delete, Sign Out) is clearly labeled and announces as destructive via platform accessibility APIs
- Modal confirmations are fully navigable via assistive tech — focus moves to the modal on open and returns on close
- Color is never the sole indicator of state — status dots (green/yellow/red on connection rows) are accompanied by text ("Last synced 4 minutes ago" / "Sync failed — tap to reconnect")
- Font scaling: the entire Settings area respects the OS's Dynamic Type / font scale. All layout is flex-based; nothing breaks at 200% scale.
- Biometric prompts use the OS-native authentication UI, which is fully accessible
- The Chatbot Audit screen's container-and-fields expanded view is navigable via screen reader — each container is a heading, each field is a list item
- All deep-linked screens have visible back paths — no dead-end states

---

## 4.8.17 Edge Cases & Error Handling

**User has no connections:** Connected section hidden; Available section leads the Connections screen.

**User has no connections and device supports no connections:** Empty state on Connections screen: "No connections available on this device."

**User has 10+ connections:** Connected section is scrollable within the grouped list; no hard cap.

**Subscription state changes while user is on Subscription screen:** RevenueCat pushes an event; the screen re-renders with the new state within 1 second.

**User changes email but never verifies:** Old email remains the account email. Clerk sends a reminder 24 hours after initiation; Life Dashboard shows a banner in Settings → Security: "Email change pending verification."

**User toggles app lock ON, then force-closes the app, then reopens:** App lock applies on the next foreground per the timeout setting (Always / 1min / 5min / 15min). For "Always", the first foreground after any force-close triggers the lock screen.

**Biometric fails 3 times:** Fallback to device passcode; 3 more failed passcode attempts disables authentication for 1 minute (OS-native behavior).

**User deletes a connection while syncing is in progress:** Sync aborts immediately; 30-day purge is scheduled; connection moves to Available.

**User changes timezone while a daily quota reset is in flight:** The reset completes at the old timezone's midnight if it's within 1 hour of the change; otherwise, the reset schedules for the new timezone's next midnight.

**User changes units from imperial to metric while a meal edit sheet is open:** The edit sheet closes and re-opens with the new units preserved. Saved values are stored in metric internally and displayed in either system.

**User revokes AI consent for a data type mid-chatbot-query:** The query completes with the data that was already loaded; the next query uses the new consent setting.

**User hits the Advanced macro section with locked values, then changes their primary goal:** Locked values are preserved; only auto (unlocked) values recompute. A toast informs: "Goal updated. Your locked macro targets are unchanged."

**User tries to export data while disconnected from the network:** Export fails with a clear error: "We need an internet connection to export. Try again when you're online."

**User on Core hits the Chatbot Audit retention limit (>7 days):** Older records are automatically purged; the screen shows "Showing queries from the last 7 days" and the query list stops at that boundary.

**User on Pro downgrades to Core:** Chatbot audit records older than 7 days become unreadable the next day (during the nightly purge job). The user gets 24 hours to export them before purge.

**Trial banner on the day the trial ends:** On the morning of the trial end day, banner reads "Trial ends today — subscribe to keep Pro." Tapping the "View plan" link opens Subscription. After midnight of the trial end day, banner disappears; a new banner appears: "Trial ended. You're on [Core] now" — visible for 48 hours, then disappears.

**User is signed in on multiple devices and deletes their account on one:** All devices are signed out within 60 seconds via Clerk's session invalidation. Data is retained for 30 days per grace period.

**Account deletion during active subscription:** User is warned in the deletion confirmation modal: "Deleting your account will cancel your subscription at the end of the current billing period. You won't be refunded for the remaining time." User must proceed explicitly.

**User cancels deletion during grace period:** Sign in from any device with the account credentials; the account is reactivated and all data restored.

**Settings loads with a sync failure (server unreachable):** Most settings are cached client-side; the screen renders from cache with a small banner "Working offline — some changes will sync when you reconnect." Changes queue locally and push on reconnect.

---

## 4.8.18 Analytics Events

All events are fired with `user_id` (anonymized per Section 6 privacy rules), `timestamp`, `app_version`, and `tier` (lite/pro/trial).

**Settings surface**
- `settings_opened`
- `settings_section_tapped` (section_name: subscription/connections/notifications/privacy/security/preferences/support/data_account)

**Profile**
- `profile_opened`
- `profile_archetype_viewed`
- `profile_field_edited` (field_name)
- `profile_advanced_expanded`
- `profile_macro_locked` (macro_name)
- `profile_macro_unlocked` (macro_name)
- `profile_redo_launched` (section: about_you/goal/daily_life/workout_plan/connections)
- `profile_redo_completed` (section, duration_seconds)
- `goal_change_initiated`
- `goal_change_completed` (from_goal, to_goal)
- `goal_change_cancelled`

**Subscription**
- `subscription_screen_opened`
- `upgrade_tapped` (source: subscription_screen / trial_banner / paywall / chatbot_cap)
- `manage_subscription_tapped`
- `restore_purchases_tapped`
- `restore_purchases_result` (success_boolean, plan_restored)
- `trial_banner_shown` (days_remaining)
- `trial_banner_dismissed`
- `trial_banner_view_plan_tapped`
- `plan_compare_viewed`

**Connections**
- `connections_screen_opened`
- `connection_connect_initiated` (connection_type)
- `connection_connect_completed` (connection_type)
- `connection_connect_failed` (connection_type, reason)
- `connection_detail_opened` (connection_type)
- `connection_sync_manual` (connection_type)
- `connection_disconnect_initiated` (connection_type)
- `connection_disconnect_confirmed` (connection_type)
- `connection_sync_failed_surfaced` (connection_type, reason)

**Privacy**
- `privacy_screen_opened`
- `ai_consent_toggled` (data_type, new_state)
- `data_deletion_initiated` (data_type)
- `data_deletion_confirmed` (data_type)
- `chatbot_audit_opened`
- `chatbot_audit_record_expanded`
- `chatbot_audit_record_deleted`
- `chatbot_audit_exported`

**Notifications**
- `notifications_screen_opened`
- `notifications_master_toggled` (new_state)
- `notifications_category_toggled` (category, new_state)
- `quiet_hours_changed`
- `system_permission_banner_tapped`

**Preferences**
- `theme_changed` (new_theme)
- `units_changed` (new_units)
- `language_changed` (new_language)
- `timezone_mode_changed` (new_mode)
- `timezone_manual_set` (timezone)
- `haptics_toggled` (new_state)

**Security**
- `biometric_toggled` (new_state)
- `app_lock_toggled` (new_state)
- `app_lock_timeout_changed` (new_timeout)
- `password_change_initiated`
- `password_change_completed`
- `email_change_initiated`
- `email_change_verified`
- `sessions_sign_out_all_initiated`
- `sessions_sign_out_all_confirmed`

**Data & Account**
- `export_data_tapped`
- `sign_out_initiated`
- `sign_out_confirmed`
- `delete_account_tapped`

**Support**
- `faqs_opened`
- `faq_searched`
- `faq_opened` (faq_id)
- `contact_support_tapped`
- `feedback_submitted` (category)
- `rate_app_prompt_shown`
- `whats_new_opened`
- `terms_opened`
- `privacy_policy_opened`
- `licenses_opened`

Events never include raw setting values that could identify personal information. Email changes log the event but not the email addresses; password changes log only the completion event, never the passwords.

---

# Section 4.9 — Notifications

## 4.9.1 Overview

Notifications are Life Dashboard's most ambitious AI system and its most sensitive trust surface. They are the mechanism by which Life Dashboard earns its place on the user's lock screen — or loses it.

### Philosophy

The core objective: deliver just the right notification at the right time to improve the user's life.

Every Life Dashboard notification must clear two bars:

1. **Actionable** — the user can do something with it. "Rent is due tomorrow" → pay the bill. "Meeting in 15 min" → start preparing. "Remember to grab eggs" (arriving at the grocery store) → grab eggs. Vague nudges ("check your macros," "you're doing great") never fire.
2. **Right-time contextual** — the notification fires when action is still possible and relevant. A budget breach at 11pm when the week is already over is worse than no notification; a protein pacing alert at 4pm when there's still time to eat is useful.

This is only achievable by letting AI evaluate cross-domain data. A rules-only notification system (bill due = fire; budget at 80% = fire; email important = fire) fires independently, overlaps, feels robotic, and misses the compositional value where two signals together tell a coherent story. The signal pool + AI evaluator architecture in 4.9.2 exists because the "dream scenarios" — you're at the grocery store and a task reminds you about eggs; you have 3 hours till midnight and you're 60g under protein; your rent is due tomorrow and you're tight on budget — all require cross-domain awareness.

### Hard rules

- **No unsolicited "you got this" content.** Notifications do not coach, cheerlead, or motivate. They inform and suggest action.
- **No sensitive data in notification text.** Dollar amounts, merchant names, specific medical terms, and email content are never rendered on the lock screen. Full-content notifications are used (not teaser "Life Dashboard has an update"), but the text paraphrases around sensitive values. "Rent is due tomorrow," never "$1,200 rent is due tomorrow."
- **Aggressiveness is user-controlled.** Three levels (Low / Medium / High) cap daily notification volume and govern which signals qualify for a push.
- **No duplicate pushes.** Anti-spam dedup prevents the same signal from firing twice within a rolling 2-hour window.
- **Quiet hours are respected** except for a narrow critical category (payment failed, subscription expiring today).

### Scope

Notifications in v1 cover all four categories (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Life), multi-signal cross-domain merges, location-arrival task reminders, and account/billing events. Deferred to v1.1+:

- AI-generated meeting prep suggestions (judged too hard to do well in v1 — requires deep per-meeting context that we don't yet have a reliable source for; a simple "meeting in 15 min" reminder fires, but without AI prep assistance)
- Social notifications (none, no social features exist)
- Email/SMS delivery channels (push only in v1)
- In-app notification inbox / history (deliberately not built — see 4.9.15)

### Success metrics

- **Notification retention rate** — percentage of users who keep notifications enabled 30 days after first grant. Target: 70%+. Users turning off notifications en masse is the clearest signal the system is too noisy, too irrelevant, or miscalibrated.
- **Action rate per notification** — percentage of fired notifications that the user taps (as a proxy for perceived relevance). Target: 25%+ across all notification types. Below 15% = the system is firing things users ignore.
- **False-positive complaint rate** — feedback-form submissions that reference notifications as "too many" or "irrelevant." Target: <2% of notification-enabled users per month.
- **Aggressiveness distribution** — we expect Medium to be the dominant setting (default). If users skew heavily to Low, the system is too noisy at Medium. If users skew to High, we're underdelivering at Medium.
- **AI cost per active user per month** — target <$0.50/user/month in AI spend for notification evaluation and compose calls.

---

## 4.9.2 Architecture

Notifications in Life Dashboard are built on three layers:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Data events (sync, log, tick, webhook, location)    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Rules layer (deterministic; produces Signals)       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Signal pool (server-side, time-bounded)             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AI evaluator (decides fire/merge/defer/drop;        │
│  composes message; Haiku 4.5)                        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Delivery (APNs / FCM via Expo Notifications)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Layer 1: Data events

Events originate from normal app data flow — no notification-specific pipeline is needed. Examples:

- Plaid sync completes → bill and transaction data updated
- User logs a meal → nutrition totals updated
- User logs a workout → PR detection runs
- User arrives at a known location → CoreLocation/Android Location visit event
- Calendar sync completes → upcoming events updated
- Email classifier processes new mail → importance flags updated
- Periodic timer (every 10 min on the server) → time-based evaluations (is it past meal reminder time? Is midnight approaching with protein behind?)
- RevenueCat webhook → subscription state change
- Connection sync fails → error state

### Layer 2: Rules layer (deterministic)

Each data event is processed by a rules layer that checks a fixed set of conditions. When a condition is met, the rule emits a **Signal** into the pool. Rules never send notifications directly; they only produce signals.

Rules are deterministic: no AI, no stochasticity. A bill due in 24 hours always emits a `bill_due_1d` signal. Budget crossing 80% always emits a `budget_threshold_crossed` signal. This is auditable, testable, and predictable.

See 4.9.4 for the full rules catalog.

### Layer 3: Signal pool

The signal pool is a server-side, per-user, time-bounded buffer of recent signals. Each signal:

- Has a type, a priority (critical / high / medium / low), a timestamp, a payload, and a staleness window (default: 15 minutes)
- Expires after its staleness window without producing a notification, OR is consumed by the evaluator when it fires
- Is keyed to the user and the day (midnight-local resets)

Pool size bounds: typical user has 0-10 signals in-flight at any time; the pool never exceeds 50 active signals per user (architectural limit).

The pool allows multi-signal observation within a window — when a new signal arrives, the evaluator examines all fresh signals together and can choose to merge them into a single composite notification.

### Layer 4: AI evaluator & composer

When a signal is added to the pool, the evaluator wakes up (subject to debouncing — see 4.9.5) and examines the pool. The evaluator:

1. Reads current pool contents (typically 1-3 active signals)
2. Reads user context: aggressiveness level, today's notification count, quiet hours state, time of day, current tier
3. Decides: **fire** / **merge** / **defer** / **drop**
4. If firing: composes the message, selects deep-link target, assigns priority
5. Returns a decision object that the delivery layer acts on

The evaluator is a single Claude Haiku 4.5 call. Cost and latency specs in 4.9.5.

### Layer 5: Delivery

Push delivery happens via Apple Push Notification Service (APNs) on iOS and Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) on Android. In v1 the React Native client registers its push token via `expo-notifications`; the server stores the token and sends through Expo's push service (which proxies to APNs/FCM).

Delivery is server-side — all scheduling logic lives on the server (per Q7 decision). Local notifications scheduled on the device are NOT used in v1, even for time-based reminders like meal times. This is a deliberate simplification: server-side scheduling means every notification decision goes through the full evaluator (aggressiveness, quiet hours, dedup), even for seemingly "simple" time-based ones. The alternative — local notifications for time-based, server-push for event-based — splits the logic and makes quiet hours / aggressiveness enforcement inconsistent.

The tradeoff: server-side scheduling requires the device to have network connectivity at delivery time. For v1 this is acceptable — the notifications that matter are network-dependent by nature (new bill detected, new email classified, payment failed).

### Why this architecture over alternatives

**Alternative A: Pure rules (no AI).** A set of fixed rules firing templated messages. Simpler and cheaper but cannot compose cross-domain signals into coherent stories ("rent is due tomorrow and budget is tight") — would fire as two separate notifications, duplicating interruptions without adding value. Also cannot contextually adjust for the user's aggressiveness preference with nuance.

**Alternative B: Periodic AI evaluation cycles.** Every 30 minutes, an AI call ingests the entire user state and decides whether to notify. More flexible, but wasteful — most evaluation cycles will produce no notification. At $0.001-0.002 per call × 32 evaluations/day × 365 days = $12-24/user/year in AI spend, with the vast majority of calls producing nothing.

**Chosen: Rules pre-filter + event-driven evaluator.** Rules catch deterministic conditions cheaply and emit signals; AI only runs when a signal has been emitted and a decision is actually needed. Expected call volume: 3-10 evaluator calls per user per day. At ~$0.001-0.002 per call, that's $1-4/user/year — affordable, and only spending AI budget when there's a real candidate notification.

---

## 4.9.3 The Signal Catalog

This is the authoritative list of signal types produced by the rules layer in v1. Each signal has a fixed schema; the evaluator reads from a fixed vocabulary.

### Signal schema (general)

```
Signal {
  type: string (enum from catalog below)
  priority: enum { critical, high, medium, low }
  timestamp: ISO datetime
  user_id: string
  payload: object (type-specific)
  staleness_window: integer (minutes; default 15)
  category: enum { fitness, nutrition, finance, life, account }
  suggested_deep_link: string
  source_event: string (what data event produced this signal, for audit)
}
```

### Catalog

**Finance**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `bill_due_3d` | medium | Bill detected with due date 3 days out | `{bill_name, days}` | 24h |
| `bill_due_1d` | high | Bill due in 24-48h | `{bill_name, days}` | 12h |
| `bill_due_today` | critical | Bill due today | `{bill_name}` | 6h |
| `bill_overdue` | critical | Bill past due, not marked paid | `{bill_name, days_overdue}` | 6h |
| `budget_threshold_80` | medium | Weekly spending crossed 80% of weekly budget | `{week_used_pct, days_remaining}` | 4h |
| `budget_threshold_100` | high | Weekly spending crossed 100% | `{week_used_pct, days_remaining}` | 4h |
| `large_transaction` | medium | Transaction detected above user threshold | `{amount_bucket, category}` | 2h |
| `low_balance` | high | Account balance below user threshold | `{account_name}` | 24h |
| `payment_failed` | critical | RevenueCat payment_failed webhook | `{}` | 48h |

**Nutrition**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `protein_pacing_behind` | medium | 3h+ before midnight, user 30%+ under daily protein | `{hours_to_midnight, pct_behind}` | 1h |
| `macro_overshoot` | low | Daily calorie target exceeded by 20%+ and it's past 8pm | `{calories_over}` | 2h |
| `meal_reminder_time` | low | User-configured meal time reached | `{meal_label, time}` | 30min |

**Fitness**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `workout_scheduled_soon` | medium | Training plan has workout scheduled within 30 min | `{workout_type, minutes_until}` | 30min |
| `pr_achieved` | high | PR detection finds a new personal record on a completed workout | `{lift_type, pr_value, previous}` | 30min |
| `recovery_anomaly` | low | HRV/sleep/RHR outside user baseline range | `{metric, severity}` | 6h |

**Life**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `calendar_event_soon` | medium | Calendar event starts within 15 min | `{event_category, minutes_until}` | 15min |
| `task_due_today` | medium | Task with due_date=today becomes active (morning fire at 8am local) | `{task_count}` | 4h |
| `task_location_matched` | high | User arrives at a location tagged to an open task | `{task_title, place_label}` | 1h |
| `important_email_arrived` | medium | Email classifier flags newly-arrived email as important | `{sender_preview, count_in_window}` | 4h |
| `connection_sync_stale` | low | Integration has not synced in >24h | `{connection_name, hours_stale}` | 48h |

**Account / billing**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `trial_ending_2d` | medium | User is on trial, 2 days from end | `{days}` | 24h |
| `trial_ending_1d` | high | Trial 1 day from end | `{days}` | 24h |
| `trial_ending_today` | critical | Trial ending today | `{}` | 12h |
| `subscription_renewed` | low | Renewal webhook received | `{plan}` | 48h |

**Goals (added in v1.11 per Section 4.10.15)**

| Signal type | Priority | Emitted when | Payload | Staleness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `goal_deadline_3d` | medium | Active cumulative/period-count goal has deadline 3 days away | `{goal_id, goal_name, progress_pct}` | 24h |
| `goal_deadline_1d` | high | Goal deadline 1 day away | `{goal_id, goal_name, progress_pct}` | 12h |
| `goal_deadline_today` | high | Goal deadline today | `{goal_id, goal_name, progress_pct}` | 6h |
| `goal_milestone_50` | low | Progress crossed 50% on any goal | `{goal_id, goal_name}` | 6h |
| `goal_milestone_75` | low | Progress crossed 75% on any goal | `{goal_id, goal_name}` | 6h |
| `goal_completed` | high | Goal hit 100% completion condition — also triggers in-app celebration | `{goal_id, goal_name, goal_type}` | 48h |
| `goal_behind_pace_sustained` | medium | Pace ratio <0.7 for 7 consecutive days | `{goal_id, goal_name, current_pace_ratio}` | 24h |
| `goal_auto_extended` | low | Deadline was just auto-extended after miss | `{goal_id, goal_name, new_deadline}` | 24h |
| `goal_streak_broken` | low | Streak goal streak just broke | `{goal_id, goal_name, streak_length_achieved}` | 6h |

Note on `goal_completed`: this signal bypasses the normal aggressiveness-based suppression and always fires a push (unless master notifications off), because goal completion is a positive moment the product is designed to celebrate. See Section 4.10.14 for celebration modal details.

### Priority semantics

- **Critical** — fires even during quiet hours; never suppressed by aggressiveness setting; never subject to daily cap
- **High** — fires during quiet hours silently (no sound/vibration); subject to daily cap; always included when aggressiveness ≥ Low
- **Medium** — respects quiet hours (queues for morning); subject to daily cap; included when aggressiveness ≥ Medium
- **Low** — respects quiet hours; subject to daily cap; included when aggressiveness = High only

### Signal freshness

Signals carry a staleness window. If a signal enters the pool and the evaluator defers the decision (e.g., waiting to see if more signals arrive), the signal expires at the end of its staleness window and is silently dropped. Staleness prevents firing a notification about something the user no longer cares about (e.g., a "bill due in 3 days" signal that sat in the pool for 3 days without firing should not then fire as the bill is now "due today" — a new signal fires instead).

### New signal types post-launch

Adding new signals is a server-side deployment: a new rule is added, a new signal type is added to the catalog, and the evaluator prompt is updated to understand the new type. No client changes required in most cases.

---

## 4.9.4 Rules Layer

The rules layer is a deterministic function that runs synchronously after specific data events. Rules do one thing: check conditions and emit signals.

### Rule execution model

Rules are registered per-event-type. When an event fires, all registered rules for that event type are evaluated in sequence. Each rule produces 0 or 1 signals. The resulting signals are pushed into the pool atomically.

### Example rule definitions

**Rule: bill-due detection (runs after Plaid sync or bill table update)**

```
For each active bill in user_bills:
  days_until = bill.due_date - today
  if days_until == 3 and signal not already emitted today:
    emit bill_due_3d { bill_name: bill.display_name }
  elif days_until in [1, 2] and signal not already emitted today:
    emit bill_due_1d { bill_name: bill.display_name, days: days_until }
  elif days_until == 0 and signal not already emitted today:
    emit bill_due_today { bill_name: bill.display_name }
  elif days_until < 0 and bill.status == 'unpaid':
    emit bill_overdue { bill_name: bill.display_name, days_overdue: -days_until }
```

**Rule: protein pacing (runs on meal log and on periodic timer at 3pm, 6pm, 9pm local)**

```
hours_to_midnight = 24 - current_local_hour
if hours_to_midnight <= 9 and hours_to_midnight >= 3:
  protein_consumed_today = sum of protein from today's meals
  protein_target = user's daily protein target
  pct_behind = (protein_target - protein_consumed_today) / protein_target
  if pct_behind >= 0.30 and signal not already emitted today:
    emit protein_pacing_behind {
      hours_to_midnight,
      pct_behind: pct_behind
    }
```

**Rule: task-location match (runs on CoreLocation visit event)**

```
On location_visit event with place_label:
  tasks = query user_tasks where status='open' and location_tag=place_label
  if tasks not empty and signal not emitted for this place in past 4h:
    emit task_location_matched {
      task_title: tasks[0].title,
      place_label: place_label,
      task_count: len(tasks)
    }
```

### Rule guardrails

Every rule applies these guardrails before emitting:

1. **Dedup**: has this exact signal already been emitted today (or within the dedup window for that signal)? If yes, skip.
2. **Quiet hours pre-check**: if current time is in quiet hours AND signal priority is not critical/high, queue signal for end-of-quiet-hours delivery. Medium and low signals during quiet hours are batched.
3. **Consent gate**: is the relevant category notification toggle on for this user? If off, don't emit.
4. **Integration gate**: does the user have the relevant connection active? (e.g., bill rules require Plaid connected; important email rules require Gmail connected)

Rules respect consent at emission time, which means turning off a category toggle stops signals from even entering the pool — not just from being delivered.

### Periodic rule runs

Some rules fire on specific times regardless of events:

- **8:00 AM local** — `task_due_today` evaluation (for all tasks with today as due_date)
- **3:00 PM local** — `protein_pacing_behind` check (early warning)
- **6:00 PM local** — `protein_pacing_behind` check (primary)
- **9:00 PM local** — `protein_pacing_behind` check (last call)
- **Every 15 min between 6am–10pm local** — `calendar_event_soon` check
- **Daily at 10:00 AM local** — `connection_sync_stale` check
- **Daily at 9:00 AM local** — `bill_due_*` full sweep
- **Daily at midnight local** — reset per-day dedup state; clear stale signals from pool

These are server-side cron jobs per user (or sharded cron across the user base, keyed to user's local timezone).

### What happens when consent is OFF

If a user has Fitness notifications toggled OFF:
- No fitness-category rules produce signals
- No fitness signals enter the pool
- No AI evaluation for fitness happens
- Zero server or AI cost for fitness notifications for that user

This is meaningful: toggling off a category cleanly stops all processing, not just delivery. It matters for privacy, for cost, and for user control.

---

## 4.9.5 AI Evaluator & Composer

The evaluator is the single AI call in the notification pipeline. It runs when a signal enters the pool and all gating conditions pass.

### Invocation

When a new signal enters the pool:

1. **Debounce window** (30 seconds) — wait briefly to see if related signals arrive. Example: if `bill_due_1d` arrives, the system waits 30s before invoking the evaluator. If during that 30s a `budget_threshold_80` also arrives, the evaluator sees both signals together — opportunity for multi-signal merge.
2. **Gating**:
   - User has notifications enabled at OS level? If not, skip.
   - User is within today's daily cap (per aggressiveness)? If at cap, skip unless signal is critical.
   - Category toggle on? (Already checked at rule level, but re-checked here as a safety net.)
   - User's device has a valid push token? If not, skip (but log for retry).
3. **Invoke evaluator**

### Evaluator input

The evaluator receives a compact context:

```
{
  user_id: "u_...",
  current_time_local: "2026-04-20T15:32:00-06:00",
  quiet_hours_active: false,
  aggressiveness: "medium",
  today_notifications_fired: 2,
  daily_cap_remaining: 3,
  tier: "pro",
  fresh_signals: [
    {
      type: "bill_due_1d",
      priority: "high",
      payload: { bill_name: "rent", days: 1 },
      timestamp: "2026-04-20T15:30:00-06:00"
    },
    {
      type: "budget_threshold_80",
      priority: "medium",
      payload: { week_used_pct: 82, days_remaining: 3 },
      timestamp: "2026-04-20T15:31:00-06:00"
    }
  ],
  recent_notifications_today: [
    {
      time: "2026-04-20T09:15:00-06:00",
      category: "fitness",
      type: "pr_achieved",
      summary: "New squat PR"
    },
    {
      time: "2026-04-20T12:00:00-06:00",
      category: "nutrition",
      type: "meal_reminder_time",
      summary: "Lunch reminder"
    }
  ]
}
```

Total input size: typically 400-800 tokens.

### Evaluator output

The evaluator returns a strict JSON object:

```
{
  action: "fire" | "merge" | "defer" | "drop",
  reason: string (one-line audit note),
  notification: {
    title: string (≤50 chars, natural tone),
    body: string (≤140 chars, natural tone, no sensitive data),
    deep_link: string (Life Dashboard deep-link URI),
    priority: "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low",
    category: string,
    signal_refs: [array of signal type strings consumed]
  } | null
}
```

Examples:

**Single signal fire:**

```
Input signals: [bill_due_1d { bill_name: "rent", days: 1 }]
Output:
{
  action: "fire",
  reason: "Single high-priority bill signal, no conflict",
  notification: {
    title: "Rent is due tomorrow",
    body: "Tap to view your upcoming bills.",
    deep_link: "apex://finance/bills",
    priority: "high",
    category: "finance",
    signal_refs: ["bill_due_1d"]
  }
}
```

**Multi-signal merge:**

```
Input signals: [bill_due_1d { bill_name: "rent" }, budget_threshold_80 { week_used_pct: 82 }]
Output:
{
  action: "fire",
  reason: "Two finance signals active — merging into coherent message about tight week",
  notification: {
    title: "Rent is due tomorrow",
    body: "You're at 82% of your weekly budget too — might be a tight finish.",
    deep_link: "apex://finance",
    priority: "high",
    category: "finance",
    signal_refs: ["bill_due_1d", "budget_threshold_80"]
  }
}
```

**Drop (low relevance + aggressiveness):**

```
Input signals: [macro_overshoot { calories_over: 300 }]
User context: aggressiveness=low, today_notifications_fired=2
Output:
{
  action: "drop",
  reason: "Macro overshoot is low-priority; aggressiveness=low excludes low-priority signals",
  notification: null
}
```

**Defer (waiting for more signals):**

```
Input signals: [calendar_event_soon { minutes_until: 14 }]
User context: quiet_hours_active=true, priority=medium
Output:
{
  action: "defer",
  reason: "Medium-priority signal during quiet hours; queue for end-of-quiet-hours delivery",
  notification: null
}
```

### System prompt for the evaluator

The evaluator runs with a fixed system prompt (abbreviated structure):

```
You are the notification evaluator for Life Dashboard. You decide which
notifications to send to users based on incoming signals from their data.

YOUR JOB:
Given a list of fresh signals, user context, and today's notification history,
output a JSON decision object: fire / merge / defer / drop. If firing, compose
a natural, actionable message.

RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE):
- No sensitive data in notification text: no dollar amounts, no merchant names,
  no email content, no specific medical terms
- Natural tone, not preachy — no "you got this!" or "let's crush it"
- Actionable — user must be able to do something with the notification
- Respect daily cap: if daily_cap_remaining <= 0, only fire if priority == critical
- Respect quiet_hours_active: if true and priority in {medium, low}, defer
- Aggressiveness gating:
  * low: only priority in {critical, high}
  * medium: priority in {critical, high, medium}
  * high: all priorities
- Merge signals when they tell a coherent cross-domain story
- Do NOT merge signals that are genuinely independent (e.g., a PR and a bill
  should not be merged; they have nothing to do with each other)

OUTPUT: Return ONLY the JSON object. No surrounding text.
```

The full prompt includes few-shot examples, the complete signal catalog, and output schema validation guidance. Total system prompt ~1,200 tokens; combined with input context ~500 tokens; output typically ~100 tokens. Per-call cost at Haiku 4.5 pricing: ~$0.0012.

### Latency

Target: <3 seconds from signal arrival to push token dispatch. Breakdown:
- 30s debounce (intentional delay; not counted against target)
- AI call: 1-2s typical (Haiku is fast)
- Push dispatch: <500ms

Users experience the notification as "within 30-45 seconds of the underlying event" for most event types. For time-based signals (protein pacing, meal reminders), the timing is exact — the rule fires at the scheduled moment, and the user receives the notification in the same 30-45s window.

### Model choice rationale

Haiku 4.5 is used because:
- Low latency requirement (users expect push notifications to feel immediate)
- Simple structured output (not reasoning-heavy)
- Cost sensitivity (this runs ~5-10 times per user per day)
- Output quality is adequate for composing 140-char messages

Sonnet or Opus are not used. The evaluator does not need advanced reasoning; it needs fast, disciplined adherence to a fixed output schema and a narrow set of rules.

### Fallback when AI fails

If the AI call fails (timeout, 500 error, rate limit):

1. Retry once with exponential backoff (max 5s)
2. If still failing, fall back to the **highest-priority signal** in the pool and fire using a pre-written template for that signal type
3. Log the failure event for observability
4. The failure does not re-trigger the evaluator — a degraded notification delivers, and the pool is cleared

Fallback templates exist for every signal type in the catalog (4.9.3). These are hand-written, natural-tone, single-signal messages that comply with the sensitive-data rules. They ensure the system degrades gracefully when AI is unavailable.

### Audit logging

Every evaluator invocation writes an audit record:
- Input signals
- User context snapshot
- Evaluator decision (fire/merge/defer/drop)
- Reason string
- Resulting notification (if any)
- AI call latency
- AI call token counts
- AI call cost (computed)

Audit records are retained for 30 days (Pro) / 7 days (Core), matching the Chatbot Audit retention (4.7.16). Users can see their notification audit in Settings → Notifications → Audit (new row added to the Settings 4.8.8 screen — see retroactive edit notes).

---

## 4.9.6 Aggressiveness Levels

Aggressiveness is the single most important user-facing control in notifications. It's a three-way dial that governs both daily volume and which priority classes of signals qualify for a push.

### Levels

| Level | Daily max | Priorities fired |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | 2/day | Critical + High |
| **Medium** (default) | 5/day | Critical + High + Medium |
| **High** | 8/day | Critical + High + Medium + Low |

### What the dial actually does

- **Daily cap** — the evaluator checks `today_notifications_fired` against the cap for the user's level. If at cap, only critical signals fire.
- **Priority filter** — signals below the level's threshold are dropped by the evaluator before composition. A `macro_overshoot` (low priority) at Medium aggressiveness is dropped without AI spend.
- **Merge sensitivity** — at higher aggressiveness, the evaluator is more willing to combine signals. At lower aggressiveness, it's more selective.

### Critical signals bypass

Regardless of aggressiveness level:
- `payment_failed` fires
- `bill_overdue` fires
- `bill_due_today` fires
- `trial_ending_today` fires

These are the irreducible minimum — losing access to your subscription or being late on a bill is a meaningful action cost.

### Default level: Medium

A new user gets Medium by default. Medium represents "most users, most of the time." It fires the dream-scenario notifications (rent due tomorrow + budget tight, protein behind with dinner window, important email, PR achieved) while filtering out the pure engagement-style nudges.

### Changing the dial

The dial is in Settings → Notifications. Changing it takes effect immediately for future signals. Signals already in the pool are re-evaluated against the new setting at their next evaluator wake.

### Rationale

Daily caps (2 / 5 / 8) are informed by:
- Industry data: most messaging apps hover at 3-6 notifications/day before uninstall risk spikes
- Life Dashboard's position as a trusted life dashboard: users accept more from trusted utilities than from engagement apps
- The rule "actionable only": every fired notification should be genuinely actionable, so even at High (8/day), every push earns its place

The difference between Low and Medium is philosophical: Low = "only tell me what I absolutely cannot miss"; Medium = "tell me things that would meaningfully improve my day"; High = "tell me everything that might be useful."

---

## 4.9.7 Scheduling & Delivery Engine

All scheduling happens server-side. The client never schedules local notifications in v1.

### Server-side scheduling

A notification worker runs as a background service (Node.js worker process) with:
- Access to the signal pool (read/write)
