| Integrations lost on restore | Disclosed — restore screen explains integrations need reconnection |
| Email export to wrong address | Prevented — uses verified account email only |
| Download link expiration unclear | Specified — 7 days from generation, next export allowed in 30 days |
| Users on vacation miss grace period | Mitigated — 30 days is a month; industry-standard window |
| Users on trial feel locked in | Managed — trial users can delete identically to paid users |

---

## 4.11.20 Accessibility Requirements

- Export flow UI uses standard form semantics; all controls have descriptive accessibility labels
- Previous export rows announce: "Export generated on [date], [status], [file size]"
- Rate limit message is read aloud to screen readers: "Export available on [date]"
- Deletion confirmation screens use clear, jargon-free language compatible with assistive tech
- Typed-DELETE input field announces: "Type the word DELETE in all capital letters to enable the delete button"
- Biometric prompts use OS-native UI (fully accessible)
- Destructive actions (Delete button in deletion flow) are announced as destructive via platform accessibility APIs
- Confirmation email content is plain-text accessible (screen readers read it cleanly)
- Download links in emails are keyboard-accessible and focusable
- All modals in deletion flow allow keyboard dismissal (Esc or equivalent)
- Font scaling at 200% preserves layout and legibility

---

## 4.11.21 Edge Cases & Error Handling

**User initiates deletion, then tries to export during grace:**
- Export flow is inaccessible (account is signed out during grace)
- User must restore account first, then export, then re-initiate deletion
- Info banner on Restore screen prompts: "Need your data? Export first, then delete if you still want to."

**User's account email changes mid-export-job:**
- Export email is sent to the email on file at job creation time
- Email change mid-job does not redirect the export email

**User's email is unverified when requesting export:**
- Blocked — banner on Export screen: "Verify your email before exporting. [Resend verification]"

**User signs in during grace, views Restore screen, dismisses without deciding:**
- User signed out; deletion continues on schedule
- Reminder emails continue firing

**Deletion-pending user receives a password reset email (triggered by someone else typing the email into "forgot password"):**
- Password reset flow continues through Clerk
- After reset, user signs in → lands on Restore screen (same as before)
- No special "someone tried to restore your account" alert in v1; deferred to v1.1+

**User on day 30 restores account in the same hour the purge job runs:**
- Database-level lock on the `users` row during purge
- If restore succeeds first: purge job sees `state = active`, skips this user
- If purge succeeds first: restore fails with "Account not found"; user sees Sign In error
- Race condition window is <1 minute per user

**User's payment method fails during grace period:**
- RevenueCat reports payment failure
- Life Dashboard ignores (account is in deletion-pending)
- User does not receive `payment_failed` notification (signed out)

**User creates a new account with the same email 60 days after deletion:**
- Clerk treats this as a new user (old Clerk user ID was purged)
- No data restored; genuinely new account
- Onboarding runs from scratch

**User attempts to export, reaches rate limit, then genuinely needs sooner:**
- Support email can override
- Manual support-triggered export bypasses the rate limit check

**Export job runs out of memory on a massive account:**
- Worker logs failure with memory metric
- Job retries with larger worker allocation (if auto-scaling allows)
- If still fails, flagged for manual fulfillment by support

**ZIP file exceeds 1 GB:**
- Split across multiple ZIPs with part numbering (`LifeDashboard_Export_2026-04-20_abc123_part1of3.zip`)
- Email contains links to all parts
- Manifest file in part 1 describes contents of all parts

**Download link from past export is clicked after 7 days:**
- Cloudflare R2 returns 404
- User email directed to initiate new export

**User in deletion-pending state is subject to a legal hold:**
- Admin flag on account prevents purge
- User remains in deletion-pending indefinitely
- User cannot restore (per legal hold) but cannot be purged either
- This is an edge case for legal compliance; process documented in Section 12

**User on Pro with audit retention >30 days has their retention drop due to downgrade mid-grace:**
- Retention applies at export generation time based on current tier
- If user downgraded just before deletion, Core retention (7 days) applies to the export

**Network failure during deletion initiation:**
- Client catches network error
- Shows: "Couldn't connect. Please try again."
- No partial deletion — initiation is atomic on the server
- User retries

**Server failure during purge job:**
- Transaction rolls back; account remains `deletion_pending`
- Retries on next nightly run
- After 7 failed retries, flagged for ops review

**User tries to export while a previous export is still pending:**
- Button disabled with status "Preparing..."
- No duplicate job created
- Second tap shows toast: "Your export is still being prepared"

**Email bounces or is rejected:**
- Job marked `delivery_failed`
- User sees in-app banner on next app open: "We couldn't deliver your export. Verify your email and try again."

**User uninstalls app during grace period, never reinstalls:**
- Account still purges on day 30
- Final email delivers if email address is valid
- No data retained post-day-30 regardless of user awareness

---

## 4.11.22 Analytics Events

All events fired with `user_id` (anonymized), `timestamp`, `app_version`, and `tier`.

**Export**
- `export_screen_opened`
- `export_initiated` (file_size_estimated)
- `export_generation_completed` (file_size_bytes, generation_latency_ms)
- `export_email_delivered`
- `export_email_bounced`
- `export_download_link_clicked` (hours_since_generation)
- `export_job_failed` (failure_reason, retry_count)
- `export_rate_limited` (days_until_next)
- `export_from_deletion_flow_tapped`
- `previous_export_viewed`

**Account deletion**
- `deletion_screen_opened`
- `deletion_step1_viewed`
- `deletion_continue_tapped`
- `deletion_step2_viewed`
- `deletion_type_confirm_attempted` (matched_correctly: boolean)
- `deletion_biometric_prompted`
- `deletion_biometric_passed`
- `deletion_biometric_failed` (attempt_count)
- `deletion_subscription_disclosure_viewed`
- `deletion_manage_in_store_tapped`
- `deletion_initiated` (had_active_subscription: boolean)
- `deletion_cancelled_mid_flow` (step_cancelled_at)

**Grace period**
- `grace_reminder_day_23_sent`
- `grace_reminder_day_29_sent`
- `restore_screen_shown` (days_into_grace)
- `restore_completed` (days_into_grace)
- `restore_declined` (days_into_grace)

**Purge**
- `account_purge_started`
- `account_purge_completed` (approximate_data_size_bytes, days_in_grace)
- `account_purge_failed` (failure_reason)
- `oauth_revocation_result` (provider, success: boolean)
- `final_purge_email_sent`
- `final_purge_email_bounced`

**Compliance**
- `manual_export_request_received` (via support — fired from admin tooling)
- `legal_hold_applied`
- `legal_hold_lifted`

Events include metadata only. No raw user data, no email addresses, no file contents. Deletion audit records in `deletion_audit` table are separate from analytics — the analytics events describe the process; the audit records are the permanent compliance artifact.

---

## 4.11.23 Compliance (GDPR, CCPA)

This subsection maps Life Dashboard's Data Export & Account flows to specific regulatory requirements. Section 12 (Compliance Requirements) is the authoritative compliance spec; this subsection documents which user-facing flows serve which laws.

### GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU)

**Article 15 — Right of Access**

Life Dashboard's compliance: the Export flow (4.11.3–4.11.8).

- Request processed: self-service, within 24 hours typical (far below 30-day legal requirement)
- Fee: free
- Format: CSV + JSON (commonly used, machine-readable)
- Comprehensive: includes all personal data Life Dashboard holds (see 4.11.4)
- No conditions: not gated by subscription, not gated by past account behavior

**Article 17 — Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten)**

Life Dashboard's compliance: the Account Deletion flow (4.11.9–4.11.13).

- Self-service from Settings
- Grace period allows reconsideration (not required by GDPR but is good practice)
- Permanent deletion on day 30
- Third-party providers notified (OAuth revocation)
- Anonymous audit record retained for compliance reporting only

**Article 20 — Right to Data Portability**

Life Dashboard's compliance: partially covered by export JSON format. Users can import exported data into other apps that accept standard formats.

**Article 30 — Record of Processing Activities**

Not user-facing; documented in Section 12.

### CCPA (California Consumer Privacy Act)

**Right to Know**

Life Dashboard's compliance: Export flow serves this right (same mechanism as GDPR Article 15).

**Right to Delete**

Life Dashboard's compliance: Account Deletion flow serves this right (same mechanism as GDPR Article 17).

**Right to Opt-Out of Sale**

Life Dashboard does not sell personal information to third parties. This is stated in the Privacy Policy. No in-app opt-out mechanism needed because there's nothing to opt out of.

**Right to Non-Discrimination**

Life Dashboard's compliance: export and deletion are available to all tiers without additional cost or service degradation. Confirms commitment in 4.11.3.

### Other jurisdictions

Brazil (LGPD), UK (UK GDPR), Canada (PIPEDA), and similar regimes have analogous rights. Life Dashboard's export and deletion flows satisfy the core requirements across these. Jurisdiction-specific nuances (e.g., Brazilian Portuguese language requirements for notices) are handled in Section 12.

### SAR (Subject Access Request) emergency channel

Users whose legal timeline requires a faster-than-self-service export (edge case) email support@apexlife.app. Support initiates a manual export within the legally-required timeline (30 days GDPR / 45 days CCPA). Documentation of this channel is in the Privacy Policy.

### Data residency

Life Dashboard's primary data store (Neon Postgres) is in US-based availability zones. EU users' data is not automatically stored in EU-region infrastructure in v1 (a compromise for v1 launch simplicity). Section 12 specifies the path to EU data residency for v1.1+ if required by user growth patterns.

### Compliance-user interface relationship

This section (4.11) spec's the user-facing flows that satisfy the legal rights. The legal interpretations, audit trails, and reporting obligations for Life Dashboard as a company are Section 12's scope.

---

# Section 5 — Technical Architecture

## 5.1 Overview & Architectural Principles

Section 5 is the blueprint. It describes the shape of APEX's system — client, backend, data, external services, and the connections between them. It does not specify vendor pricing, service provisioning details, or cost models (Section 11 — Infrastructure Requirements handles those). It does not spec security controls (Section 7) or scoring math (Section 9) or AI prompt strategy (Section 10). Section 5 is the architectural diagram, rendered as text.

### Architectural Principles

The following principles govern every choice in this section. They flow from Section 3 (Design Principles), particularly 3.6 (Speed Above All), 3.7 (Every Connection Is Optional), and 3.8 (Margins Are A Product Decision).

1. **Offline-first on the client.** The mobile app is fully functional without network. Reads hit a local SQLite mirror of the user's recent data. Writes queue locally and sync to the server when network is available. Network latency never blocks user-facing interactions.

2. **Server-side source of truth.** Every piece of user data lives authoritatively on APEX's server infrastructure. The client holds a synchronized replica. Cross-device continuity, data export, account deletion, and AI context assembly all read from the server.

3. **Event-driven background processing.** User-facing request paths (API calls, dashboard loads, chat queries) are fast and synchronous. Anything that can be deferred — sync jobs, AI evaluation, notification composition, export generation, progress recomputation — runs asynchronously in worker processes driven by queued events.

4. **Separation of hot paths from ingestion workloads.** API compute and worker compute are separate runtime shapes. The API is sized for request-response latency; workers are sized for throughput and long-running jobs. Scaling each independently prevents a backlog of sync jobs from starving user-facing requests.

5. **AWS-first, pragmatic at launch.** The long-term production target is AWS-native. Early launch uses a small number of third-party managed services (Neon for Postgres, Upstash for Redis, Cloudflare R2 for blob storage) where their economics at startup stage beat the AWS-native equivalent. The architecture does not depend on these services remaining in place; each has a documented migration path to the AWS-native alternative.

6. **Deterministic computation is the foundation, AI is the surface.** Scoring, progress calculation, pace, notifications rules, signal emission, and data validation are deterministic — written in code, testable, auditable. AI sits on top for synthesis, ambiguity resolution, drafting, and messy-input parsing. Section 10 (AI Requirements) governs AI scope; Section 5 governs how AI calls are wired into the runtime.

7. **Low idle cost.** Fixed monthly burn is kept low enough that the business can run on a founder's personal resources at pre-launch and early-launch scale. Scale-to-zero patterns (Lambda, Neon, Upstash) are preferred where latency allows.

8. **Observability as a first-class requirement.** Every AI call, every external vendor call, every background job, and every cost-attributable request is observable. Cost metrics are aggregated per user, per feature surface, and per vendor. The founder can see at any time what the marginal cost of each user is.

### Success Metrics for the Architecture

- **p95 API response time**: <500ms for dashboard loads, <1s for most endpoints
- **Client dashboard render time from cold open**: <500ms using local SQLite, per 3.6
- **Sync latency (server event → client reflects)**: <10s for push-available events, <5 min for background sync
- **AI response first-token latency**: <3s p95, per 4.7
- **Background job processing time**: 95th percentile <30s per job
- **Infra cost per MAU**: <$0.50/mo at 10k+ users (excluding AI, which is tracked separately per 10.x)
- **Architecture survives 3-5x v1 scale target** (50k users → 250k users) without re-architecture

---

## 5.2 System Topology

The system has five tiers, connected by well-defined interfaces.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       CLIENT TIER                               │
│  React Native (iOS + Android) via Expo                          │
│  Local SQLite mirror + queued-writes + delta sync               │
│  Local business logic: scoring, progress calc, rendering        │
└────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │ HTTPS (Hono API over Lambda)
                                 │ JWT auth via Clerk
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────┐
│                      API TIER                                   │
│  Hono + TypeScript on AWS Lambda                                │
│  Stateless request-response; reads + writes; AI gateway         │
└──────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┘
       │                                          │
       │ Postgres over PgBouncer                  │ SQS enqueue
       │                                          │
┌──────▼──────────────────────────────────────────▼───────────────┐
│                      DATA & QUEUE TIER                          │
│  Neon PostgreSQL (primary datastore)                            │
│  Upstash Redis (cache + session + rate limit state)             │
│  AWS SQS (background job queues)                                │
│  Cloudflare R2 (export ZIPs, archived blob storage)             │
└──────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┘
       │                                          │
       │ Postgres reads/writes                    │ SQS consume
       │                                          │
┌──────▼──────────────────────────────────────────▼───────────────┐
│                      WORKER TIER                                │
│  Node.js + TypeScript on AWS Fargate                            │
│  Notification worker, sync workers, export generator, cron      │
│  Long-running processes; listens on SQS; talks to vendors       │
└────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                                 │
                                 │ External vendor APIs
┌────────────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────┐
│                    EXTERNAL VENDOR TIER                         │
│  Anthropic API · Plaid · Google (Gmail/Calendar/Fit) · Apple    │
│  (HealthKit on-device + EventKit) · Microsoft Graph · Clerk ·   │
│  RevenueCat · Expo Notifications (APNs/FCM) · SendGrid · Google │
│  Places                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Tier responsibilities

**Client tier.** Owns the rendering and interactive experience. Holds a local replica of the user's recent data. Computes derived values (scores, pace, progress bars) client-side whenever the input data is already local — avoiding round-trips for every tab switch. Queues writes to a local outbox when offline. Receives push notifications independent of the app's foreground state.

**API tier.** Owns the request-response surface. Authenticates every request via Clerk JWT. Serves reads from Postgres (with Redis-cached hot paths). Accepts writes, validates them, persists to Postgres, and enqueues any downstream work to SQS. Makes synchronous AI calls when the user is waiting (chatbot queries, meal photo scans). Stateless — horizontal scale is automatic.

**Data & queue tier.** Owns durable state. Postgres holds the authoritative data model. Redis holds transient state (sessions, rate-limit counters, signal pool working set, cached AI context containers). SQS holds background work-in-flight. R2 holds blob storage (export ZIPs). Each has its own durability and availability characteristics; the architecture never assumes Redis or SQS data is durable across full regional outages.

**Worker tier.** Owns background processing. Consumes SQS. Handles sync jobs (Plaid webhook processing, calendar refresh, Gmail classification). Evaluates notification signals and composes push notifications. Generates export ZIPs. Runs scheduled cron (progress recomputation at midnight-local, deletion purge at 03:00 UTC, notification cron per-user-timezone).

**External vendor tier.** Everything APEX does not own. Each vendor is integrated via a dedicated client module in the worker or API tier, with retry policy, circuit-breaker semantics, and cost observability.

---

## 5.3 Client Architecture

### Runtime & stack

- **React Native** via **Expo** managed workflow (SDK version locked per release)
- **TypeScript** strict mode across the client codebase
- **expo-sqlite** for local persistent storage
- **drizzle-orm** for typed local database queries
- **React Query (TanStack Query)** for server state management and background refetch
- **Zustand** for transient UI state
- **React Navigation** for screen routing
- **expo-notifications** for push token registration and notification handling
- **expo-secure-store** for local secrets (Clerk tokens, biometric-gated keys)
- **expo-local-authentication** for biometric (Face ID / Touch ID / Android biometric)

### Application structure

```
app/
  src/
    screens/           # One file per screen, following the 4.x section structure
      home/
      fitness/
      nutrition/
      finance/
      life/
      chatbot/
      settings/
      onboarding/
    components/        # Reusable UI primitives
    features/          # Business logic per category
      nutrition/
        logMeal.ts
        parseMeal.ts
        scoring.ts
      fitness/
        ...
    data/              # Local DB layer
      schema/          # drizzle schema
      migrations/
      queries/
      mutations/
    sync/              # Sync engine (see 5.3.5)
    api/               # HTTP client for the backend API
    auth/              # Clerk integration
    notifications/     # Push handling + deep-link routing
    ai/                # Client-side intent classifier, chatbot UI state
    navigation/        # React Navigation setup
    theme/             # Design tokens (Section 14 will finalize)
    lib/               # Shared utilities
```

### Local database (SQLite)

APEX ships with a full SQLite schema on-device that mirrors the server's user-data tables. Not every server table is mirrored — only the tables the client reads directly in its own flows. Tables that are server-only (audit trails, signals pool, export jobs) stay server-side.

Tables mirrored locally (representative list, not exhaustive):

- `meals`, `saved_meals`, `custom_products`, `hydration_logs`
- `body_weight_logs`, `strength_workouts`, `strength_sets`, `cardio_sessions`, `daily_movement`, `sleep_logs`, `recovery_logs`, `personal_records`, `training_plans`
- `transactions`, `bills`, `connected_accounts`, `user_budget`
- `calendar_events`, `tasks`, `day_timeline_events`
- `goals`, `goal_progress_log`
- `notifications` (for history display)
- `user_preferences`, `user_primary_goal`, `user_ai_consent`
- `sync_state` — client-only, tracks per-entity sync cursors and outbox state

Tables NOT mirrored (server-only):

- `notification_signals` (pool)
- `chatbot_audit`, `notification_audit` (retrieved on-demand when user opens audit views)
- `plaid_tokens`, `gmail_tokens` (credentials never live on the client)
- `export_jobs`, `account_deletion_state`, `deletion_audit`
- Server logs and analytics

### Offline-first pattern

Every read and every write goes through the local DB. The server is an asynchronous source of truth that reconciles with the local state in the background.

**Read path:**
1. UI component renders from React Query
2. React Query reads from local SQLite via a query function
3. In parallel, React Query kicks off a background server-fetch if data is stale (TTL per entity)
4. When server-fetch returns, local DB is updated and the UI re-renders

**Write path:**
1. User action triggers a client mutation (e.g., log a meal)
2. Mutation writes to local SQLite immediately with a local UUID and `sync_status = 'pending'`
3. UI reflects the new state instantly (optimistic)
4. Sync engine (5.3.5) picks up the pending entry and POSTs to the server
5. Server responds with canonical server UUID + server timestamps
6. Client updates the local row: local UUID → server UUID, `sync_status = 'synced'`
7. If server returns a validation error (e.g., invalid macros), local row is marked `sync_status = 'failed'` and UI shows an error badge on that entry with retry/edit affordances

**Network-offline behavior:**
- Reads always succeed (local data)
- Writes succeed locally and queue in the sync outbox
- AI features (chatbot, meal photo scan, AI workout parsing) gracefully degrade to "offline — tap to retry when connected" states
- Push notifications received while offline land in the OS notification center and deep-link correctly when the user taps

### Sync engine

A dedicated module (`src/sync/`) orchestrates bidirectional sync with the server.

**Pull sync (server → client):**
- Runs on app foreground, on periodic interval (every 60s while foregrounded), and on explicit pull-to-refresh
- Calls `GET /v1/sync/delta?since={cursor}&entities={entity_list}`
- Server returns all entity changes since the cursor for each requested entity type
- Client applies changes: inserts, updates, soft-deletes
- Updates cursor in `sync_state`

**Push sync (client → server):**
- Runs continuously while online
- Reads from the `sync_state` outbox (entries with `sync_status = 'pending'`)
- POSTs each outbox entry to the appropriate endpoint
- On success, updates `sync_status = 'synced'`
- On failure, uses exponential backoff; after 5 retries marks `sync_status = 'failed'` and surfaces to user

**Conflict resolution:**
- Default: last-write-wins using server-authoritative timestamps
- Per-entity overrides for specific cases:
  - **Goal progress**: server always wins (computed authoritatively)
  - **User preferences**: last-write-wins, but server pushes a warning if two devices made conflicting changes within a short window
  - **Meal logs**: additive (both devices' meals end up in the canonical list — users create, not mutate, meals)

### Client-side AI

Certain AI interactions happen with the client holding context and the API proxying to Anthropic:

- **Chatbot queries**: client assembles context containers from local SQLite, sends to API which forwards to Anthropic
- **Meal photo scan**: client captures image, POSTs to API, API forwards to Anthropic Sonnet/Opus
- **Intent classifier** (per 4.7.8): client-side, no AI — rule-based. Only ambiguous classifications fall back to a server-side Haiku call
- **AI workout parsing**: client sends text, API forwards to Haiku

All AI calls go through the API tier — the client never holds the Anthropic API key.

### Push notification handling

- App cold-start registers an Expo push token with the server
- Server stores token in `user_push_tokens` per 4.9.7
- Incoming push: OS delivers notification
- If app is in foreground, client handles in-app routing via `deep_link` payload
- If app is background/killed, OS displays notification; tap triggers app open to deep-link target

### Client state persistence

- Local SQLite: persistent across app kills, survives OS restarts, wiped only on app uninstall or user-initiated local-data-clear
- Zustand stores: in-memory, RAM-only (chatbot session state per 4.7)
- Secure store: persistent, encrypted at OS level (Clerk session tokens)

---

## 5.4 Backend Architecture

### API tier (Hono on Lambda)

The API is a single Hono application deployed as a Lambda function behind API Gateway (or Lambda Function URL, TBD in Section 11).

**Framework: Hono.** Chosen for low cold-start overhead, native TypeScript, serverless-optimized ergonomics. Hono's middleware model cleanly handles authentication, rate limiting, request logging, and cost attribution.

**Routing structure:**
```
/v1/
  /auth/
    /webhooks/clerk
  /users/
    /me
    /me/preferences
    /me/onboarding
  /sync/
    /delta                  GET
    /entity/{type}          POST, PUT, DELETE
  /nutrition/
    /meals                  CRUD
    /parse                  POST (AI call)
    /scan                   POST (AI call)
  /fitness/
    /workouts               CRUD
    /plans                  CRUD
    /parse-workout          POST (AI call)
  /finance/
    /transactions           GET + user-override PUT
    /bills                  CRUD
    /budget                 GET, PUT
  /life/
    /calendar               CRUD (proxies to external)
    /email/threads          GET
    /tasks                  CRUD
    /timeline               GET
  /goals/
    /                       CRUD
    /library                GET
  /chatbot/
    /query                  POST (AI call)
    /audit                  GET, DELETE
  /notifications/
    /preferences            GET, PUT
    /audit                  GET, DELETE
  /exports/
    /                       POST
    /{id}                   GET
  /account/
    /deletion               POST, GET, DELETE
  /webhooks/
    /plaid                  POST (from Plaid)
    /revenuecat             POST (from RevenueCat)
    /sendgrid               POST (delivery events)
```

**Request path:**
1. API Gateway / Function URL receives HTTPS request
2. Hono middleware stack runs:
   - **cors**: validate origin
   - **rate-limit**: check Redis counter per user + per IP
   - **auth**: validate Clerk JWT; attach `user_id` to request context
   - **cost-attribution**: start a cost-tracking span
   - **request-logging**: structured log to Axiom with request metadata
3. Route handler executes
4. Response returned
5. cost-attribution span closes; cost recorded if the handler made metered calls

**Cold start mitigation:**
- Hono's minimal runtime keeps cold starts under 300ms typical
- Provisioned concurrency for the core endpoints (home dashboard, sync delta) if cold-start latency proves user-visible

### Worker tier (Node on Fargate)

The worker is a Node.js service running as a long-lived Fargate task. It loops: pull from SQS, process, acknowledge or return-to-queue.

**Worker process responsibilities:**
- Consume `sync-jobs` queue: Plaid sync, Gmail classification runs, calendar refresh
- Consume `notification-signals` queue: run the evaluator per 4.9.5
- Consume `export-jobs` queue: generate export ZIPs, upload to R2, trigger email
- Consume `ai-jobs` queue: async AI calls (daily AI brief, email draft pre-generation)
- Run cron: progress recomputation, deletion purge, stale-connection detection
- Own the Expo Notifications push dispatch

**Concurrency model:**
- Each Fargate task runs multiple concurrent workers (one per core)
- Each worker has its own SQS long-poll loop
- Multiple Fargate tasks run in parallel for horizontal scale
- Visibility timeout per job controls retry semantics

**Worker-side vendor calls:**
- Plaid: full transaction sync after webhook; periodic reconciliation
- Anthropic: signal evaluator calls, async AI briefs, pre-generation jobs
- Google APIs: Gmail list + metadata fetch, Calendar sync
- Apple/Microsoft: OAuth-based calendar/email access
- Expo Notifications: push dispatch
- SendGrid: transactional email
- Cloudflare R2: export upload

### Code structure (shared between API and worker)

```
backend/
  src/
    api/                    # Hono routes
    workers/                # Worker entrypoints and handlers
    domain/                 # Shared business logic (scoring, progress calc, notifications rules)
      nutrition/
      fitness/
      finance/
      life/
      goals/
      scoring/
      notifications/
    data/                   # Data access layer
      schema/               # drizzle schema (shared with client)
      queries/
      migrations/
    integrations/           # External vendor clients
      anthropic/
      plaid/
      gmail/
      calendar/
      expo-push/
      sendgrid/
      revenuecat/
      clerk/
    cost/                   # Cost attribution, observability
    auth/                   # Clerk wiring
    config/                 # Env, feature flags, secrets loading
    lib/                    # Shared utilities
  infra/                    # IaC definitions (see Section 11)
  package.json
  tsconfig.json
```

Single repo, single `package.json`, single TypeScript build. API and worker entrypoints compile from the same codebase with different bundles. Domain logic is shared.

### Why a modular monolith

Microservices are the wrong architecture for v1:
- Team of one: multiple services means multiple deploy pipelines, multiple release cycles, multiple monitoring targets, for no coordination benefit
- Domain logic is tightly coupled (scoring + goals + signals all read the same data), and splitting would require heavy RPC or messaging overhead
- Pricing and scale at v1 do not justify the ops overhead

The modular monolith splits at runtime (API Lambda vs Worker Fargate) without splitting at the codebase. Future extraction is possible when specific domains outgrow the shared runtime.

### Deployment independence

- API changes can deploy without restarting workers
- Worker changes can deploy without affecting API serving
- Shared domain logic changes require both to redeploy (rare, but handled via CI/CD pipeline that deploys both on merge to main)

---

## 5.5 Data Layer

### PostgreSQL — Neon

**Role**: primary relational datastore. Every user-data entity is stored here as source of truth.

**Why Neon at launch:**
- Scale-to-zero pricing (cold start on first query)
- PgBouncer included (handles Lambda connection pool pressure)
- Managed backups, point-in-time recovery
- Developer ergonomics (branching, easy schema migrations)
- No AWS VPC configuration required
- Solo-founder-friendly cost at <10k users

**Connection pooling:**
- Lambda functions connect through Neon's PgBouncer pooler, not direct connections
- Each Lambda invocation gets a pooled connection; pool is managed by Neon
- Workers hold longer-lived direct connections (Fargate tasks are warm)

**Connection string rotation:**
- Credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager
- Lambda fetches connection string on cold start; caches for invocation lifetime
- Rotation flow: update secret, bump cache TTL, Lambdas pick up new creds on next cold start

**Schema ownership:**
- Schema is defined with **drizzle-orm** in the backend repo
- Migrations run via `drizzle-kit` on deploy
- Staging and prod each have their own database
- Schema version tracked in a `schema_migrations` table

**Read replicas:**
- Not needed at v1 scale
- Neon supports branch-based replicas if needed later

**Migration path (Section 11):**
- When scale demands, migrate to AWS Aurora Serverless v2 in the same region as compute
- Migration is a point-in-time dump + restore plus application config change
- Documented in Section 11; executed only when metrics justify

### Upstash Redis

**Role**: low-latency key-value store for cache, ephemeral state, and coordination.

**Use cases:**
- **API rate limiting**: per-user request counters with sliding window
- **AI cost tracking**: per-user AI spend counters with daily reset
- **Chatbot session state** (per 4.7): in-session RAM equivalent, keyed by session ID with 30-minute TTL
- **Notification signal pool** working set: active signals per user with staleness TTL
- **AI context container cache**: per-user context fragments (profile, recent patterns) cached with short TTL to avoid repeat computation
- **Feature flag read cache**: feature flag values cached for 60s to avoid hot-path Postgres reads
- **Distributed lock**: occasional locks for critical-section code (e.g., export job deduplication)

**Access pattern:**
- Upstash Redis via HTTP (REST API) — no TCP connection pool needed
- Lambda connects via HTTP per invocation; no warm-up required
- Workers connect similarly or can use the TCP client if performance demands

**Why Upstash:**
- HTTP-accessible (no VPC, no connection pool)
- Pay-per-request pricing
- Multi-region replicas available
- No idle cost

**Durability expectations:**
- Redis data is NOT durable across a Redis failure. Nothing relies on Redis being the source of truth. Signal pool state, cache entries, rate-limit counters — all reconstructable from Postgres or tolerable as lost.

### AWS SQS

**Role**: background job queue.

**Queue topology:**
- `sync-jobs` — external data sync tasks
- `notification-signals` — signals awaiting evaluator processing
- `notification-dispatch` — notifications ready for push delivery
- `export-jobs` — user export generation
- `ai-jobs` — async AI tasks (daily brief, email draft pre-gen)
- `scheduled-jobs` — cron-scheduled work (progress recompute, purge)
- Each queue has a corresponding **dead-letter queue** (`*-dlq`) for poison messages

**Message format:**
- JSON payload with `job_type`, `user_id`, `job_params`, `enqueued_at`, `retry_count`
- Max message size 256KB; larger payloads reference S3/R2 blobs

**Semantics:**
- Standard queues (not FIFO) — Life Dashboard doesn't require strict ordering within a queue
- Visibility timeout tuned per queue based on worst-case processing time
- Retry policy: 3 attempts on the main queue, then move to DLQ
- DLQ review is manual (Sentry alert on DLQ depth > 0)

### Cloudflare R2

**Role**: blob storage.

**Use cases:**
- Export ZIPs (per 4.11)
- Social card template assets (shared between client and potentially server-rendered previews)
- Meal scan image temporary storage (during AI call, deleted after response)

**Why R2:**
- Zero egress fees (exports downloaded by users don't incur cost)
- S3-compatible API (migration to S3 is trivial if needed)
- Global edge presence (fast downloads worldwide)

**Bucket structure:**
```
apex-exports/
  {user_id}/
    {export_id}/
      APEX_Export_{date}_{short_id}.zip
apex-social-cards/
  templates/
    {library_id}.json
apex-meal-scans/
  temp/
    {user_id}/{scan_id}.jpg   (auto-deleted 24h)
```

**Lifecycle policies:**
- Exports: auto-delete after 7 days
- Meal scans temp: auto-delete after 24h
- Social card templates: no expiry (bundled assets)

**Access:**
- All bucket access uses signed URLs generated by the API
- No public-read buckets
- Signed URLs expire per use case (7 days for exports, 5 minutes for meal scan upload)

### Hot vs archived storage tiering

Per Section 3.8 principle:

**Hot tier (Postgres, full indexes):**
- Last 90 days of user-data entities for Core users
- All data for Pro users within a recent window (exact boundary in Section 13)
- Fast read performance; supports all product queries

**Archived tier (Postgres partition or R2 JSON snapshots):**
- Older history beyond the hot window
- Read performance relaxed (slower queries acceptable)
- Still queryable for user-initiated flows (export, "show history" deep dives)
- Implementation: partitioned Postgres tables by month, with older partitions on cheaper storage OR nightly export of older data to R2 as JSON snapshots

**Migration policy:**
- Nightly cron moves data past the hot window boundary
- Moved data still belongs to the user and is exportable
- Reading archived data is a separate code path from hot reads (query indicates archive scope)
- Default product views (dashboard, category detail views) query hot only; "view older history" UI explicitly crosses into archive queries

### Schema conventions

Applied across all user-data tables:

- `id` — UUID v7 (time-ordered UUIDs for sortability + index locality)
- `user_id` — FK to users table, indexed
- `created_at` — timestamp with timezone, default `now()`
- `updated_at` — timestamp, updated by trigger on any row change
- `deleted_at` — nullable timestamp for soft-delete (used for sync deltas)
- `sync_version` — monotonic integer incremented on each update (supports delta sync cursors)

Tables that don't have user ownership (e.g., `goal_library`, `exercise_library`, `feature_flags`) omit `user_id` and follow their own conventions.

---

## 5.6 Authentication & Authorization

### Clerk as the identity provider

- **Clerk** handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, email verification, OAuth (Google sign-in), and session management
- Clerk issues JWTs that the client includes on every API request
- Clerk webhooks keep APEX's local `users` table in sync (new user created, user deleted, email changed)

### Request authentication

Every API request flow:
1. Client includes `Authorization: Bearer <clerk_jwt>` header
2. Hono auth middleware validates JWT using Clerk's public key (cached in Lambda memory)
3. On valid JWT: `user_id` extracted from `sub` claim, attached to request context
4. On invalid JWT: 401 response, no further processing
5. Downstream handlers read `request.user_id` to scope all queries

JWT validation is fully offline after first public-key fetch (JWKS caching per standard JWT pattern). No roundtrip to Clerk on every request.

### Session model

- JWT TTL: 1 hour
- Refresh token handled by Clerk SDK on the client; refreshes transparently
- Session revocation: when user initiates deletion (4.11) or "sign out all devices" (4.8), backend calls Clerk admin API to invalidate all sessions for that user

### Authorization scope

v1 has a simple authorization model:
- Users can only access their own data (enforced by `WHERE user_id = request.user_id` on every query)
- No admin routes exposed to end users (admin tooling is separate, out of v1 scope)
- Tier-gated features (Pro-only features) check `user.tier` from a cached lookup and enforce at the handler level

Role-based access control (admin, support, read-only) is deferred to v1.1+.

### Biometric auth (local)

- Biometric gating for App Lock (per 4.8.10) runs client-side via `expo-local-authentication`
- Biometric gating for Account Deletion confirmation (per 4.11.10) also client-side
- The backend does not know whether biometric was used — it trusts the client for these user-experience gates
- Clerk JWT validation remains the actual authentication layer; biometric is a local UX gate

### OAuth token storage (for integrations)

For connected third-party integrations (Gmail, Calendar, Outlook, Plaid):
- Access tokens stored in Postgres, **encrypted with AWS KMS**
- Refresh tokens stored alongside, also KMS-encrypted
- Decryption happens at read time in the worker or API tier
- Tokens never leave the backend (never sent to the client)
- Per 4.11.15, tokens are revoked at the third-party provider when the user deletes their account

---

## 5.7 Sync & Ingestion Pipelines

External data flows into Life Dashboard through one of three patterns: webhook, periodic poll, or device-native.

### Webhook ingestion (Plaid, RevenueCat, SendGrid)

**Pattern:**
1. Vendor POSTs webhook to `/v1/webhooks/{vendor}` on the API tier
2. API handler validates the webhook signature (per vendor)
3. Handler enqueues the job to SQS (`sync-jobs` or appropriate queue)
4. Handler responds 200 OK immediately to the vendor
5. Worker consumes from SQS and processes the actual data pull/change

Why enqueue instead of process inline:
- Webhook response must be fast (vendor timeouts)
- Processing may involve multiple DB writes, AI calls, downstream signal emissions
- Failures should retry without the vendor needing to resend

**Plaid-specific:**
- `TRANSACTIONS` webhooks arrive when new transactions are available
- Handler calls Plaid's `/transactions/sync` API in the worker to pull delta
- Worker decrypts Plaid access token with KMS, calls Plaid, persists transactions
- Signal emission: `large_transaction`, `low_balance`, `bill_due_*` rules may fire

**RevenueCat-specific:**
- Webhooks on subscription events (trial_started, renewed, cancelled, expired, payment_failed)
- Handler updates user's subscription state in `users` table
- Signal emission: `trial_ending_*`, `payment_failed`, `subscription_renewed`

**SendGrid-specific:**
- Webhooks on email delivery events (delivered, bounced, opened)
- Handler updates `export_jobs.delivery_status` or relevant state

### Periodic poll ingestion (Gmail, Calendar, sometimes others)

Some vendors don't provide push webhooks or provide unreliable ones. For these, APEX polls on a schedule.

**Pattern:**
1. Cron job runs every N minutes per user (N varies by vendor — 15 min for Gmail, 5 min for Calendar)
2. Worker dequeues the scheduled job
3. Worker decrypts OAuth token, calls vendor API with delta cursor (`historyId` for Gmail, `syncToken` for Calendar)
4. Fetched data persists to Postgres
5. Signal emission as appropriate

**Cron scheduling:**
- Per-user cron jobs are spread across a window to avoid thundering herd (users distributed by hash-mod)
- Cron entries stored in Postgres (`scheduled_jobs` table); scheduler worker pulls due jobs into SQS every minute

### Device-native ingestion (HealthKit, Google Fit, Screen Time)

These data sources live on the user's device and don't have a server-side API. The client reads them and syncs to the backend.

**Pattern:**
1. Client reads data from the device (via HealthKit API or equivalent)
2. Client writes to local SQLite
3. Client's sync engine POSTs to the API as a delta write
4. API persists to Postgres
5. Signal emission happens in the worker after the write propagates

**Background refresh:**
- iOS BackgroundTasks framework handles periodic background wakes
- Android WorkManager equivalent
- Client syncs on foreground entry + periodic background wakes
- Users on "background app refresh off" lose the automatic sync — data syncs when they open the app

### Manual data entry

User-entered data (meal logs, workout logs, manual weights, tasks, bills, etc.):
- Follows the client write path from 5.3
- Local SQLite first, then sync to server
- Server persists and may emit signals as a result

### Sync delta endpoint

The core sync surface: `GET /v1/sync/delta?since={cursor}&entities={list}`

**Request:**
- `since`: Unix timestamp of last successful sync
- `entities`: comma-separated list of entity types to pull (client chooses based on what's in view)

**Response:**
```json
{
  "cursor": 1713500000,
  "entities": {
    "meals": {
      "created": [...],
      "updated": [...],
      "deleted": ["uuid1", "uuid2"]
    },
    "workouts": {
      "created": [...],
      "updated": [...],
      "deleted": []
    }
  }
}
```

**Server implementation:**
- Query each requested entity table with `WHERE user_id = ? AND updated_at > ?`
- Soft-deleted rows included with only their ID and deletion flag
- Response size capped at 5MB; if larger, multiple pages with `next_cursor`

**Client application:**
- Each delta entity is applied to local SQLite transactionally
- Cursor updated only after all entities applied successfully
- On partial failure, cursor stays at last successful state — next sync re-fetches the unapplied entities

---

## 5.8 Background Job Architecture

### Job taxonomy

| Job type | Queue | Trigger | Worker class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plaid sync | sync-jobs | Webhook | Short-running |
| Gmail classification | sync-jobs | Cron per-user + on-demand | Short-running |
| Calendar refresh | sync-jobs | Cron per-user | Short-running |
| Notification signal evaluation | notification-signals | Signal emit event | Latency-sensitive |
| Push notification dispatch | notification-dispatch | After evaluator decision | Latency-sensitive |
| Export ZIP generation | export-jobs | User request | Long-running |
| Daily AI brief (Pro) | ai-jobs | Cron per-user at 6am local | Scheduled |
| Email draft pre-generation (Pro) | ai-jobs | On important email arrival | Async |
| Progress recomputation | scheduled-jobs | Midnight-local per-user | Batch |
| Streak recomputation | scheduled-jobs | Midnight-local per-user | Batch |
| Connection staleness check | scheduled-jobs | Daily cron | Batch |
| Account deletion purge | scheduled-jobs | 03:00 UTC daily | Critical |
| Hot-to-archived data migration | scheduled-jobs | Nightly | Batch |

### Worker process model

- Multiple Fargate tasks run in parallel
- Each task runs a Node process that consumes from multiple SQS queues (weighted round-robin)
- Concurrency per task tunable via env var
- Autoscaling based on SQS queue depth + task CPU

### Job handler pattern

Every job handler implements:
```typescript
interface JobHandler {
  name: string
  handle(job: Job): Promise<void>
  onFailure?(job: Job, error: Error, attempt: number): Promise<void>
}
```

Handler execution:
1. Worker pulls message from SQS
2. Job type dispatched to handler
3. Handler runs; on success, SQS delete (ack)
4. On error, handler's `onFailure` runs (logging, metrics)
5. SQS visibility timeout expires; message requeues with incremented retry count
6. After 3 retries, moves to DLQ

### Idempotency

Every job handler is idempotent. Re-running a job with the same parameters produces the same result. This is enforced through:
- Deduplication keys (a job for "sync user X transactions" uses "sync-txn-user-X" as dedup key in Redis for 5 minutes)
- Upsert semantics in database writes (ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE)
- Check-then-act patterns for state transitions (skip if already in target state)

### Scheduled jobs (cron)

Cron is implemented as a Postgres table with a scheduler worker:
- `scheduled_jobs` table: (job_id, job_type, params, next_run_at, recurrence)
- Scheduler worker runs every minute
- Queries `WHERE next_run_at <= now()`
- For each due job: enqueue to SQS, advance `next_run_at` per recurrence rule
- Handles cron-per-user-timezone by storing target timezone and resolving `next_run_at` in UTC per user

Why not AWS EventBridge:
- Per-user cron with timezone awareness would require thousands of EventBridge rules
- Postgres scheduler is simpler to reason about and cheaper at v1 scale
- Migration to EventBridge is possible if scale demands

### Deduplication & race conditions

For jobs that might be triggered multiple times for the same user/event:
- Use Redis to acquire a short-lived lock per dedup key
- If lock acquired, process; if not, another worker has it
- Lock TTL matches worst-case job duration

Example: if a Plaid webhook fires twice rapidly, the dedup key `plaid-sync-user-{id}` prevents double processing.

### Priority & latency

Not all jobs are equal:
- **Latency-sensitive** (notification evaluation, push dispatch): handled by a dedicated worker with low-latency poll interval, no backlog tolerance
- **Short-running** (sync jobs): 1-30s typical, handled by general-purpose worker pool
- **Long-running** (export generation): 30s-10min, handled by dedicated worker with long visibility timeout
- **Batch** (overnight recomputations): run in bulk windows, tolerate latency

Worker pool segmentation is by queue: each Fargate task subscribes to specific queues based on its role.

---

## 5.9 AI Integration Layer

### Unified AI gateway

All Anthropic API calls go through a single internal module (`backend/src/integrations/anthropic/`). This centralizes:

- API key management (from Secrets Manager)
- Cost tracking per call (token counts → cost)
- Model selection logic (Haiku vs Sonnet vs Opus per call type)
- Prompt caching configuration
- Retry and fallback policy
- Observability (Sentry spans, Axiom logs)

### Call types and models

Per Section 10 (AI Requirements) principles, models are chosen per call site:

| Call site | Model | Section |
|---|---|---|
| Chatbot query | Haiku 4.5 | 4.7 |
| Meal photo scan (standard) | Sonnet 4.6 | 4.4 |
| Meal photo scan (Premium, Pro) | Opus | 4.4 |
| AI meal parsing (text/voice) | Haiku 4.5 | 4.4 |
| AI workout parsing | Haiku 4.5 | 4.3 |
| AI email draft | Sonnet 4.6 | 4.6 |
| AI email importance classification | Haiku 4.5 | 4.6 |
| Notification evaluator | Haiku 4.5 | 4.9 |
| Onboarding profile generation | Haiku 4.5 | 4.1 |
| Daily AI brief (Pro) | Haiku 4.5 | 4.6 |
| AI intent classifier (ambiguous) | Haiku 4.5 | 4.7 |

### Prompt caching

Every call site with a stable system prompt uses Anthropic's prompt caching:
- Chatbot system prompt + context container schemas: cached
- Notification evaluator system prompt + signal catalog: cached
- Meal parser system prompt + output schema: cached

Cache invalidation aligned with prompt-template deploy (a new deploy invalidates caches naturally; TTL handles gradual invalidation).

### Cost attribution

Every AI call records:
- `user_id`
- `call_site` (e.g., "chatbot_query", "notification_evaluator")
- `model` (e.g., "claude-haiku-4-5")
- `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`
- `cache_read_tokens`, `cache_write_tokens`
- `cost_usd` (computed from token counts × model pricing)
- `latency_ms`
- `timestamp`

Stored in a time-series-shaped `ai_call_log` table with retention policy (30 days Pro / 7 days Core for user-linkable records; aggregated-only retention indefinitely).

### Quota enforcement

Per 4.7.16 and 4.10 quota rules:
- Before a chatbot call, API checks Redis counter for daily quota
- Counter increments on successful call
- Failures, cancelled streams, and intent-classifier redirects do NOT increment

Quotas:
- Core: 10/day (chatbot)
- Pro: 50/day (chatbot, 300/day soft cap)

### Fallback & degradation

When Anthropic API is unavailable or slow:
- **Chatbot**: shows "Life Dashboard is temporarily unable to respond. Tap retry." No fallback text.
- **Meal scan**: falls back to text-parsing mode ("Tell us what you ate"). User re-enters.
- **Notification evaluator**: uses pre-written templates per signal type (per 4.9.5)
- **Importance classification**: all emails treated as "stream" until service recovers (no false-important, just less filtering)

### Streaming

Chatbot responses stream token-by-token (per 4.7):
- API endpoint accepts chatbot query, opens SSE stream to client
- API proxies Anthropic streaming response
- Client renders tokens as they arrive
- Cost tracking finalizes on stream close

---

## 5.10 Real-time & Notification Pipeline

Detailed spec in Section 4.9. This subsection covers the architectural wiring.

### Signal emission

Signals are emitted by rule handlers running in the worker tier. When user data changes (meal logged, transaction synced, calendar event updated), the worker evaluates relevant rules and writes signals to the `notification_signals` table. Simultaneously, the worker enqueues a `signal-emitted` message to the `notification-signals` queue.

### Evaluator invocation

A dedicated worker consumes `notification-signals`. Each message:
1. Wait 30 seconds (debounce) — additional signals for the same user may arrive in this window
2. Gather all fresh signals for this user from Postgres
3. Check aggressiveness, quiet hours, daily cap, category toggles
4. Invoke Anthropic evaluator with signals + user context
5. On fire decision: enqueue to `notification-dispatch`
6. On drop/defer: mark signals accordingly

### Push dispatch

The push dispatch worker consumes `notification-dispatch`:
1. Read user's active push tokens from Postgres
2. Call Expo Notifications SDK to send push
3. Record delivery attempt in `notifications` table
4. Handle delivery failures (invalid token → mark inactive, bad response → log for investigation)

### Receipt handling

Expo provides push receipts asynchronously:
- Receipt endpoint called after push dispatch
- Updates `notifications.delivered_at` or `delivery_error`
- Invalid tokens removed or flagged as inactive

### Latency path

- Signal emission → SQS: <100ms
- SQS → evaluator worker: <1s
- Evaluator debounce: 30s intentional
- Evaluator AI call: <3s
- Evaluator decision → dispatch SQS: <100ms
- Dispatch → Expo: <500ms
- Expo → APNs/FCM: <1s
- User device notification: total <35s from signal emission

---

## 5.11 External Vendor Integrations

Each vendor has a dedicated client module with its own connection, retry, and cost-tracking semantics.

### Vendor list

| Vendor | Purpose | Access pattern | Cost model |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anthropic | AI (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) | HTTPS REST, streaming supported | Per-token |
| Clerk | Authentication | HTTPS REST + webhooks | Per-MAU, flat tier |
| RevenueCat | Subscription management | HTTPS REST + webhooks | Per-MAU tiers |
| Plaid | Financial data (Transactions product only) | HTTPS REST + webhooks | Per-connection subscription |
| Google APIs | Gmail, Calendar, Fit, Places | OAuth + REST | Varies per product |
| Microsoft Graph | Outlook Mail + Calendar | OAuth + REST | Free tier ample |
| Apple HealthKit | On-device health data | Native SDK | Free |
| Apple EventKit | On-device calendar | Native SDK | Free |
| Android Health Connect | On-device health data (primary) | Native SDK | Free |
| Google Fit | Android health data (fallback for pre-Health-Connect devices) | OAuth + REST | Free |
| Strava | Cardio activities (read-only, AI-excluded per 6.6.3) | OAuth + REST | Free under Strava API terms |
| Apple WeatherKit | Home dashboard weather context (iOS) | Native SDK | Free tier for Apple developers |
| OpenWeatherMap | Home dashboard weather context (Android) | HTTPS REST | Free tier (60 calls/min, 1M calls/month) |
| Open Food Facts | Barcode product lookup | HTTPS REST | Free (rate-limited, server-cached per 8.7) |
| Expo Notifications | Push delivery (APNs/FCM proxy) | HTTPS REST | Free tier ample |
| SendGrid | Transactional email | HTTPS REST + webhooks | Per-email tiered |
| Cloudflare R2 | Blob storage | S3-compatible REST | Storage + operations |
| Upstash Redis | Cache + state | HTTPS REST | Per-request |
| Neon | PostgreSQL hosting | TCP/PgBouncer | Compute-seconds + storage |
| AWS | Lambda, Fargate, SQS, KMS, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch | SDK | Per-service |
| Sentry | Error tracking | SDK | Per-event tiered |
| Axiom | Log aggregation | SDK | Per-GB ingest |

### Integration patterns

**Every vendor integration implements:**
- Client class with typed methods
- Retry policy (exponential backoff, max 3 attempts for most)
- Circuit breaker (after N consecutive failures, short-circuit for M minutes)
- Cost observability span (start/end markers per API call)
- Structured logging on failure

**Credentials:**
- API keys stored in AWS Secrets Manager
- Fetched by backend on cold start or first use, cached in memory
- Rotation flow: update secret, force cache invalidation on next deploy

**Webhooks (inbound):**
- Each webhook endpoint validates signature per vendor's method (HMAC, JWS, etc.)
- Invalid signatures return 401 and log for security review
- Valid webhooks enqueue to SQS; 200 OK returned immediately

### Failure isolation

A failure at one vendor does not cascade:
- Plaid outage: transaction data stops flowing; dashboard shows "sync delayed" but all other functions work
- Anthropic outage: AI features degrade; manual logging, scoring, dashboard all functional
- Clerk outage: existing sessions continue; new logins fail until Clerk recovers
- Expo push outage: notifications queue in SQS and retry; user experience briefly delayed
- SendGrid outage: transactional emails queue; user-visible effect limited to exports and account deletion emails being delayed

Circuit breakers prevent retry storms against failing vendors. Alerts fire to Sentry when breakers trip.

---

## 5.12 Observability & Cost Monitoring

### Three-layer observability stack

**Sentry** — errors and performance monitoring
- Every Lambda invocation and worker job wrapped in Sentry transaction
- All unhandled exceptions captured with stack trace and user context (no PII beyond user_id)
- Performance traces on key paths (dashboard load, chatbot query, meal scan)
- Alerts configured on: error rate spike, new error types, high-priority error (e.g., payment processing)

**CloudWatch** — infrastructure metrics
- Lambda invocation counts, duration, error rates
- Fargate CPU, memory, task health
- SQS queue depth, age of oldest message, DLQ depth
- API Gateway latency and 4xx/5xx rates

**Axiom** — structured logs
- Every request logged with user_id (anon), endpoint, latency, status
- Every worker job logged with job_type, duration, result
- Every AI call logged with model, tokens, cost
- Every vendor call logged with vendor, latency, result
- Query-optimized for cost attribution and performance debugging

### Cost attribution

A `cost_events` table (append-only, time-series-shaped) records every cost-incurring event:
- AI calls (Anthropic): token counts → cost
- Plaid calls: flat per-call cost
- Vendor API calls with metered pricing
- R2 storage operations (aggregated hourly)
- Lambda invocations (aggregated hourly from CloudWatch)

Queries supported:
- Total cost per user per month
- Cost breakdown by call site / vendor / feature
- Cost trend over time
- Cost per query type (e.g., "what's the avg cost of a chatbot query?")

### Alerting

Alerts route to Sentry + email (solo founder at v1):
- **Critical**: payment processing errors, account deletion purge failures, sustained API 5xx rate >5%
- **High**: DLQ depth >0, AI quota abuse patterns, Clerk webhook failures
- **Medium**: individual vendor circuit-breaker trips, export generation failures
- **Informational**: deployment events, cron completion summaries

### Dashboards

At launch, observability dashboards cover:
- **System health**: API p95 latency, error rates, worker backlog
- **User funnel**: onboarding completion, feature usage, chatbot queries
- **Cost**: daily spend per vendor, per-MAU cost, AI cost per tier
- **Reliability**: uptime per integration, sync freshness per user

---

## 5.13 Deployment & Environment Strategy

### Three environments

**Development (local)**
- Local Postgres via Docker
- Local Redis (Upstash dev free tier) or Docker
- SQS replaced with `elasticmq` local emulator for testing
- Clerk, RevenueCat, Anthropic, Plaid all use sandbox/dev credentials
- Single developer; no cloud cost

**Staging**
- AWS account separate from production
- Neon staging branch
- Upstash staging database
- Clerk staging application
- Plaid sandbox
- RevenueCat sandbox
- Anthropic production key with low quota (staging doesn't use Anthropic heavily)
- Used for: final QA before production release, testing third-party webhook behavior, staging data migrations

**Production**
- AWS production account (separate from staging per IAM isolation best practice)
- All production credentials
- Monitoring and alerting live
- Access restricted

### CI/CD pipeline

**GitHub Actions** runs on every push:
1. Lint (`eslint`, `prettier`)
2. Type check (`tsc --noEmit`)
3. Unit tests (`vitest`)
4. Integration tests (against ephemeral Neon branch)
5. Build (bundle Lambda and worker artifacts)

On merge to `main`:
6. Deploy to staging automatically
7. Smoke tests against staging
8. Manual promote step to production (until autoconfidence is high)

On merge to `production` branch (or manual trigger):
9. Deploy to production
10. Smoke tests against production
11. Canary rollout for Lambda (progressive traffic shift over 5 minutes)
12. Monitor; auto-rollback on alarm

### Deployment shape

- Lambda functions deploy via IaC (likely SST or AWS CDK — finalized in Section 11)
- Fargate tasks deploy via ECR image push + service update
- Database migrations run automatically via `drizzle-kit migrate` in CI before the code deploy
- Static assets (client bundle, social card templates) uploaded to R2

### Rollback

- Lambda: previous version preserved; alias swap rolls back in seconds
- Fargate: previous task definition redeployed
- Database: migrations are forward-only; rollback requires a new migration that reverses. Strict guidance: never deploy a breaking schema migration without a compatible rollback migration prepared

### Release cadence

- v1 launch: one hard cut-over
- Post-launch: continuous deployment for bug fixes; weekly release cadence for features
- Mobile app updates via EAS Build and App Store / Play Store (typically 1-3 day review)

---

## 5.14 Data Residency & Compliance Boundaries

Per 4.11.23 and Section 12 (Compliance Requirements — to be drafted).

### v1 data residency

- All user data stored in **US-based infrastructure** (Neon US region, Upstash US region, R2 US-east)
- No EU data residency at launch
- Users can be anywhere; their data is in the US
- Privacy Policy discloses this

### EU path

- Deferred to v1.1+
- When triggered (by user demand, compliance requirement, or growth pattern), strategy:
  - Neon supports multi-region; spin up EU branch
  - Upstash has EU regions
  - Lambda deploys to EU regions
  - User records tagged by residency; requests routed to correct region

### Compliance boundaries (summary)

- **GDPR**: Export and deletion flows (4.11.23) are the primary compliance surface
- **CCPA**: Same flows serve this
- **HIPAA**: Life Dashboard does not fall under HIPAA (consumer wellness product, not healthcare provider)
- **COPPA**: Life Dashboard's target market is 20–30 year olds. No user under 13 is targeted. ToS requires 18+ (per Section 12).
- **PCI**: Life Dashboard does not process payment card data directly; all card handling is through Apple/Google billing + RevenueCat. Out of PCI scope beyond basic vendor diligence.

Section 12 owns the formal compliance documentation; Section 5 only notes that the architecture supports these regimes and doesn't create unnecessary compliance exposure.

### Data handled specially

- **Plaid access tokens**: encrypted with KMS, never exported, revoked at deletion
- **OAuth tokens**: same
- **Full email bodies**: never stored (per 4.6 — only metadata + first 200 chars)
- **Meal scan images**: temporary storage in R2, auto-deleted after 24h
- **Location precise coordinates**: coarsened to ~100m after 24 hours (per 4.6.12)

---

## 5.15 Failure Modes & Resilience

### Failure scenarios and responses

**Lambda cold start spike.** Mitigation: provisioned concurrency on hot endpoints (dashboard load, sync delta). Accept cold starts on cold endpoints.

**Neon connection pool exhaustion.** Mitigation: PgBouncer pooling, connection limits tuned to Lambda concurrency. Alert on pool utilization.

**SQS queue backlog.** Mitigation: Fargate autoscaling on queue depth. Alert on sustained depth > threshold.

**Anthropic API rate limit.** Mitigation: per-user quota enforcement prevents hitting provider limits. If hit, request queues briefly then returns 503 to client with retry-after.

**Plaid outage.** Mitigation: sync jobs fail and retry with backoff. Dashboard shows "sync delayed." Webhooks queue (Plaid retries). No data loss.

**Clerk outage.** Mitigation: cached JWT validation continues for up to 1 hour (JWT TTL). New logins fail gracefully with "Please try again in a few minutes."

**Neon database outage.** Mitigation: this is the hardest failure. API returns 503 for all non-cached reads. Client falls back to local SQLite for read-only operation. Writes queue locally until DB recovers. Full outage >15 minutes triggers incident response.

**R2 outage.** Mitigation: export downloads fail (user retry). Meal scans fail during outage. No data loss (Postgres is source of truth).

**Expo push outage.** Mitigation: notifications queue in SQS. Retry on recovery. User experience: brief delay.

**SendGrid outage.** Mitigation: transactional emails queue. Export download emails delayed. Account deletion proceeds; final confirmation email may be delayed.

**Cloudflare outage.** Mitigation: R2 becomes unavailable temporarily. Product degrades on exports and meal scans. Core product continues.

### Data durability

- **Postgres**: continuous backups with point-in-time recovery (Neon default). RPO <1 hour, RTO <4 hours.
- **R2**: 11-9s durability per Cloudflare SLA. Exports regeneratable from Postgres if ever lost (no loss of user data).
- **Redis**: not durable by design — all data reconstructable from Postgres.
- **SQS**: at-least-once delivery with DLQ for failures. Worst case: duplicate job processing (handled by idempotency).

### Graceful degradation by feature

- AI features: degrade to manual entry on AI outage
- Integrations: degrade to "sync delayed" on vendor outage
- Push: queue and retry
- Export: retry on R2 recovery
- Read operations: served from local SQLite on server outage
- Write operations: queued locally on server outage, synced on recovery

### Disaster recovery

At v1 scale, DR plan is:
- Nightly database backups retained 30 days
- Code deployed from git; repo is the source of truth
- Infrastructure in IaC; can be recreated from code
- Recovery procedure documented and tested quarterly

RTO target: 4 hours for a full regional disaster. RPO target: 1 hour (from continuous backups).

### Incident response

For v1 with a solo founder:
- Sentry pagerduty-equivalent on critical alerts
- Runbook for common failures (Plaid outage, AI outage, DB connection storm)
- Documented escalation procedure
- Post-incident review for any incident affecting >10 users or lasting >30 minutes

---

## 5.16 Scaling Envelope

### v1 scale target

The architecture specified in this section is sized for:

- **50,000 registered users**
- **20,000 daily active users**
- **200,000 daily AI queries** (across all AI call sites)
- **10M total transactions** (across all users, lifetime)
- **1M daily API requests**
- **50,000 daily background jobs**

At this scale, the architecture runs comfortably without modification. Expected v1 cost envelope (excluding AI, tracked separately per Section 10): <$0.50 per MAU per month in infrastructure.

### 3-5x headroom

The architecture handles 3-5x the v1 target (up to ~250,000 users) without re-architecture. Scaling within this range is:
- Increase Lambda concurrency limits
- Scale Fargate task count on queue depth
- Upgrade Neon compute tier
- Upgrade Upstash plan
- Adjust SQS batching

None of these are re-architectures.

### Beyond 250k users

At this scale, several architectural shifts are needed:

**Database:**
- Migrate from Neon to Aurora Serverless v2 (or AWS RDS Multi-AZ)
- Read replicas for hot-path queries
- Partitioning on user_id for largest tables (transactions, meals, workouts)
- Consider Citus or similar for horizontal scaling if partitioning insufficient

**Compute:**
- Lambda may hit account-level concurrency limits; evaluate dedicated tenancy or ECS
- Fargate task count scaling; possibly move to EKS for more control

**Observability:**
- CloudWatch + Sentry + Axiom may become expensive; evaluate consolidation
- Sampling aggressive tracing to control cost

**Data tiering:**
- Hot tier growth forces more aggressive hot-to-archived migration
- Archive tier may move from Postgres partitions to dedicated cheap storage (S3 Glacier-class)

**Caching:**
- Redis workload may outgrow Upstash's per-request pricing; consider ElastiCache reserved instances

**AI cost:**
- At 250k users with current per-user AI cost, annual spend grows to ~$3M; warrants dedicated negotiation with Anthropic on volume pricing

### Triggers for re-architecture

The following metrics trigger re-architecture review:
- **DAU >100k**: database migration planning begins
- **Daily AI queries >1M**: vendor negotiation + caching strategy review
- **p95 API latency exceeds 500ms sustained**: infrastructure review
- **Infra cost per MAU >$1.00**: cost optimization sprint
- **Single-region failure impact >2h**: multi-region planning begins

### Multi-region

Not in v1. Path documented:
- When EU residency required: spin up EU region mirror
- User pinning by residency preference
- Cross-region replication for shared config (goal_library, feature flags)
- Independent scaling per region

Multi-region is a year-2+ concern, not a v1 architectural constraint.

---

---

# Section 6 — Data & Privacy Requirements

## 6.1 Overview & Privacy Principles

Section 6 is APEX's data contract. It specifies what data APEX holds, why it holds each piece, how long it keeps it, who inside and outside APEX can see it, and what controls the user has over it. Section 6 is the authoritative reference for every privacy-affecting decision in the product.

Section 6 is not:
- The Privacy Policy (legal artifact, lives separately)
- The security implementation (Section 7 specifies encryption, access control, key management, incident response tooling)
- The compliance framework (Section 12 specifies GDPR filings, CCPA regulator contact, supervisory authority relationships)
- The user-facing export and deletion flows (Section 4.11 specifies those)

Section 6 sits between the product (Sections 4.x) and the legal framework (Section 12). It is the engineering specification of what privacy means in APEX.

### Privacy principles

Eight principles govern every decision in this section. They flow from Design Principle 3.5 (Privacy As A Feature, Not A Footnote).

1. **Minimize data collection.** APEX collects the minimum data required to deliver the specific feature the user activated. Nothing is collected speculatively. No feature exists whose purpose is "collect more data."

2. **Consent is per-source and explicit.** Every third-party data source requires explicit user consent to connect. Every source that sends data to AI requires a separate, explicit AI consent toggle. Both default OFF at signup.

3. **Use what we're given, unless told otherwise.** Once a user has granted a connection, the data from that connection is actively used to power features by default. The user's control after that point is the AI consent toggle and the ability to disconnect. Life Dashboard does not require a separate "opt in to use this" gate beyond the connection itself; that would be consent theater.

4. **Redact before AI.** Sensitive fields (financial amounts, merchant names, email content, raw location coordinates, calendar titles) are redacted to categories or placeholders before any Anthropic API call, except where the user's own query explicitly requests the raw data and that data source has AI consent enabled.

5. **Retain only as long as needed.** Every data class has a defined retention policy. Nothing is kept indefinitely unless it has been transformed into a form that cannot be linked to the user.

6. **The user owns their data.** The user can see what APEX has, export it, correct it on APEX's end, and delete it. No dark patterns in any of these flows.

7. **No data for sale. No ads. No training.** Life Dashboard does not sell user data. APEX does not serve ads. APEX does not use user data to train Anthropic's models (via Anthropic's API data handling defaults). This is the business model commitment that underwrites every other privacy principle.

8. **Design for deletion from day one.** Every table, every integration, every downstream vendor is selected and architected with the assumption that the user will one day ask to be forgotten. Deletion paths are tested; cascading dependencies are mapped; orphaned data is audited.

### Relationship to other sections

- **Section 4.x** specifies user-facing flows that manifest privacy decisions (consent flows, privacy toggles, audit screens)
- **Section 5** specifies the technical architecture that enables privacy controls (KMS encryption, isolated data stores, AI gateway)
- **Section 7** specifies the security controls that enforce the data contract (access control, encryption, incident response)
- **Section 10** specifies the AI integration details that honor the redaction and consent rules
- **Section 11** specifies the infrastructure operations that execute retention and purge policies
- **Section 12** specifies the compliance proof for regulators

Section 6 is the hinge. Changes in 6 cascade to implementation changes in 4, 5, 7, 10, 11, and 12.

---

## 6.2 Data Inventory

Complete inventory of every user-data class APEX collects, stores, or processes.

### 6.2.1 Account & identity data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Email address | User entry / OAuth | `users` table (Clerk mirror) | Authentication, transactional email |
| Password hash | Clerk-managed | Clerk (not stored by APEX) | Authentication |
| First name | User entry / OAuth | `users` table | Personalization |
| Clerk user ID | Clerk | `users` table (primary key) | Authentication linkage |
| Subscription tier | RevenueCat | `users.tier` | Tier gating, billing state |
| Account creation date | System | `users.created_at` | Account history |
| Account state | System | `users.state` | active / deletion_pending per 4.11.15 |
| Timezone | User entry / device | `user_preferences.timezone` | Midnight-local calculations |
| Locale | Device | `user_preferences.locale` | Language + units |

### 6.2.2 Profile & body data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Height | User entry (onboarding) | `users.height_cm` | RMR calculation, personalization |
| Birthday | User entry (onboarding) | `users.birthday` | RMR calculation, age-based baselines |
| Weight (current) | User entry / HealthKit / Garmin | `users.weight_kg` + `body_weight_logs` (history) | TDEE, progress tracking |
| Sex | User entry | `users.sex` | RMR calculation |
| Goal type | User entry (onboarding) | `user_primary_goal` | Macro target computation |
| Target weight | User entry (conditional) | `user_primary_goal` | Goal progress |
| Occupation | User entry | `users.occupation` | Archetype generation |
| Work style | User entry | `users.work_style` | NEAT estimation |
| Stress level | User entry | `users.stress_level` | Recovery modeling |
| Archetype (derived) | Onboarding AI | `user_archetype` | Static onboarding souvenir |

### 6.2.3 Nutrition data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Meal logs | Manual / AI parsing / photo scan | `meals` | Nutrition tracking, scoring |
| Saved meals | User-generated | `saved_meals` | Quick logging |
| Custom products | User-generated | `custom_products` | Per-user food library |
| Hydration logs | Manual (opt-in) | `hydration_logs` | Hydration tracking |
| Macro targets | Derived from profile + goal | `user_macro_targets` | Daily targets |
| Macro locks | User toggle | `user_macro_locks` | Override auto-computed targets |

### 6.2.4 Fitness data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strength workouts | Manual logging | `strength_workouts` | Progression tracking |
| Strength sets | Manual logging | `strength_sets` | Workout detail |
| Cardio sessions | Manual / HealthKit / Garmin | `cardio_sessions` | Cardio tracking |
| Daily movement | HealthKit / Google Fit | `daily_movement` | Active minutes, steps |
| Sleep logs | HealthKit / Garmin | `sleep_logs` | Recovery scoring |
| Recovery metrics | HealthKit / Garmin | `recovery_logs` | HRV, readiness |
| Personal records | Computed from workouts | `personal_records` | Fitness score |
| Training plan | User-selected / AI-generated | `training_plans` | Plan adherence |
| Body weight history | Manual / HealthKit | `body_weight_logs` | Trend tracking |

### 6.2.5 Finance data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plaid access tokens | Plaid Link flow | `plaid_tokens` (KMS-encrypted) | API access |
| Connected accounts | Plaid | `connected_accounts` | Account metadata |
| Transactions | Plaid | `transactions` | Spending tracking, budget |
| Bills (detected) | Transaction pattern analysis | `bills` | Bill tracking |
| Bills (manual) | User entry | `bills` | Bill tracking |
| Budget | User entry | `user_budget` | Budget adherence |
| Account balances (historical daily snapshots) | Plaid Transactions cached balance | `account_balances_history` | Savings balance trend chart |
| Category rules | User-defined | `user_category_rules` | Categorization overrides |

### 6.2.6 Life data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calendar events | Google Calendar / Apple EventKit / Outlook | `calendar_events` | Day Timeline, Today's Focus |
| Email accounts (metadata) | Gmail / Outlook OAuth | `email_accounts` | Multi-account management |
| Email threads (metadata + first 200 chars) | Gmail / Outlook | `email_threads` | Classification, triage |
| Tasks | User-entered | `tasks` | Task management |
| Screen time daily | iOS Family Controls / Android UsageStatsManager | `screen_time_daily` | Attention tracking |
| Screen time hourly | Same | `screen_time_hourly` | Timeline |
| Screen time per-app | Same | `screen_time_apps_daily` | App breakdowns |
| Location visits | CoreLocation / Activity Recognition | `location_visits` | Places, Timeline |
| Named places | User labels + Google Places | `named_places` | Place naming |
| Day Timeline events | Computed | `day_timeline_events` | Timeline display |
| Day Timeline source data | Raw source pulls | `day_timeline_source_data` | Timeline computation |
| Time Patterns | Computed | `life_patterns` | Pattern display |
| Today's Focus cache | Computed | `today_focus_cache` | Dashboard performance |

### 6.2.7 Goals data

| Data | Source | Storage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active goals | User-selected | `goals` | Goal tracking |
| Goal library (config) | Server-side | `goal_library` | Goal templates (not user data) |
| Goal progress snapshots | Computed daily | `goal_progress_log` | Charts, trend |
