**OAuth scopes requested:**
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar` — full read/write access to all user calendars
- Scope is required for CRUD per 4.6 — Life Dashboard needs to create, modify, and delete events

**OAuth flow:**
- User taps "Connect Google Calendar" in Connections screen
- In-app browser opens Google's consent screen (SafariViewController on iOS, Custom Tabs on Android)
- User grants; Google redirects back to `apex://oauth/google/callback` with authorization code
- Client exchanges code for access + refresh tokens at Google
- Client sends tokens to APEX server; server KMS-encrypts and stores
- Plaintext discarded from client

**Token lifecycle:**
- Access token: 1-hour TTL
- Refresh token: long-lived (no fixed expiry at Google's end)
- Refresh happens automatically in the worker tier when access token is <10 minutes from expiry

**Sync cadence:**
- Webhook support: Google Calendar Push Notifications via channels
- Channel lifetime: up to 7 days per channel; renewed automatically by the worker
- On webhook fire: worker fetches changed events
- Fallback poll: every 4 hours if webhook missed (rare)

**Data retrieved:**
- Event title, start, end, duration, attendee count (NOT full attendee list)
- Event category (derived from title + attendee count via a small Haiku call per 6.6.3)
- Location (if set — stored as string, no geocoding)
- Is-organizer boolean
- Recurrence info for recurring series

**What Life Dashboard does NOT retrieve:**
- Event descriptions / notes (bodies)
- Full attendee list with names and emails (only count)
- Attachment contents
- Contact list or directory data

**Tier placement:**
- **Core:** up to 2 total calendar accounts (shared with Outlook Calendar and device-native calendar). Adding a 3rd triggers Pro paywall.
- **Pro:** unlimited Google Calendar accounts.

**Fallback if disconnected:** Day Timeline loses calendar events; Time category shows "Connect Calendar" empty state.

**Consumed by:** Time category (4.6).

**Privacy posture:** high sensitivity. Event titles redacted to categories for AI; descriptions never stored.

### 8.6.2 Gmail

**Purpose:** read email metadata for classification (important vs stream), triage, and Day Timeline signals. Compose and send drafts (Pro-only AI email drafts feature).

**OAuth scopes requested:**
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly` — read metadata and message bodies (for first-200-char extraction only)
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.send` — send drafts (Pro AI email drafts, only invoked on explicit user action)
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.modify` — apply labels (for triage feature if user enables it)

**OAuth verification requirement (launch-blocker):**
- Gmail's readonly scope is classified by Google as "restricted" — requires Google's OAuth verification process including a security assessment
- Verification timeline: 4-8 weeks for restricted scopes
- Documentation: Google requires a privacy policy URL, terms of service URL, in-product justification screens, and third-party security assessment (CASA Tier 2)
- If verification is not complete by launch, APEX ships without Gmail at v1 — users see "Gmail connection coming soon" and can use Outlook Mail instead (Microsoft's verification is lighter)

**OAuth flow:** identical to Google Calendar's pattern (in-app browser, authorization code exchange, KMS-encrypted storage).

**Token lifecycle:** matches Google Calendar.

**Sync cadence:**
- Gmail Push Notifications via Pub/Sub
- Webhook delivers message ID on new mail arrival
- Worker fetches the message metadata via `messages.get`

**Data retrieved:**
- Message ID, thread ID
- From address, subject, timestamp
- First 200 characters of body (for AI context and classification)
- Labels (for triage)
- Attachment counts (NOT attachment contents)

**What Life Dashboard does NOT retrieve:**
- Full message bodies beyond first 200 characters
- Attachment contents
- Contact suggestions
- Mail settings

**Tier placement:**
- **Core:** up to 2 total email accounts (shared with Outlook Mail). Adding a 3rd triggers Pro paywall.
- **Pro:** unlimited Gmail accounts.

**Fallback if disconnected or verification delayed:** Time category email subsystem shows "Connect Email" empty state; Day Timeline loses email signals; AI email drafts feature unavailable.

**Consumed by:** Time category (4.6 — email, triage, draft features).

**Privacy posture:** very high sensitivity. Full bodies never stored. Subjects + sender + first 200 chars only.

### 8.6.3 Microsoft Outlook Mail

**Purpose:** read email from Outlook.com, Microsoft 365, and Exchange Online accounts. Equivalent to Gmail for Microsoft users.

**OAuth scopes requested (Microsoft Graph):**
- `Mail.Read` — read message metadata and bodies
- `Mail.Send` — send drafts (Pro feature)
- `Mail.ReadWrite` — apply categories/flags (for triage feature)
- `User.Read` — basic profile for account display name

**OAuth verification requirement:**
- Microsoft Graph verification is lighter than Google's for personal-context apps
- Multi-tenant app registration in Azure AD with consumer + organizational accounts allowed
- Review timeline: typically 1-4 weeks

**OAuth flow:**
- In-app browser opens Microsoft's consent screen
- Authorization code exchange at `https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/oauth2/v2.0/token`
- Tokens KMS-encrypted and stored server-side

**Token lifecycle:**
- Access token: 1 hour
- Refresh token: long-lived with periodic rotation

**Sync cadence:**
- Microsoft Graph change notifications (subscriptions) via webhook
- Subscription lifetime: up to 3 days (Outlook Mail), renewed automatically
- Fallback poll: every 4 hours if webhook missed

**Data retrieved:** same schema as Gmail — sender, subject, timestamp, first 200 chars, labels/categories.

**Tier placement:**
- **Core:** shares the 2-total-email-account limit with Gmail.
- **Pro:** unlimited Outlook accounts.

**Fallback if disconnected:** same as Gmail.

**Consumed by:** Time category (4.6).

**Privacy posture:** identical to Gmail.

### 8.6.4 Microsoft Outlook Calendar

**Purpose:** read and write Outlook.com / Microsoft 365 calendar events.

**OAuth scopes requested (Microsoft Graph):**
- `Calendars.ReadWrite` — read and write calendar events in any calendar the user owns
- `User.Read` — basic profile

**OAuth flow:** shares authorization flow with Outlook Mail if connected at the same time; single consent covers both if user grants. APEX requests scopes individually if user wants only one.

**Sync cadence:**
- Microsoft Graph change notifications
- Subscription lifetime for calendar events: up to 3 days, renewed automatically

**Data retrieved:** matches Google Calendar schema — title, start, end, attendee count, location string, organizer flag.

**Tier placement:**
- **Core:** shares the 2-total-calendar-account limit with Google Calendar and device-native calendar.
- **Pro:** unlimited Outlook Calendar accounts.

**Fallback if disconnected:** same as Google Calendar.

**Consumed by:** Time category (4.6).

**Privacy posture:** matches Google Calendar.

### 8.6.5 Strava

**Purpose:** read the user's Strava activities (runs, rides, swims, other cardio) for the Fitness category's cardio history and deterministic scoring.

**OAuth scopes requested:**
- `read` — read public profile info
- `activity:read` — read user's activities (public + private)

**v1 restriction:**
- Strava data is read-only at v1; APEX does not write to Strava
- Strava-sourced rows are filtered out of AI context per 6.6.3 — see that section for the hard exclusion rule
- Strava data DOES contribute to Fitness scoring (deterministic), cardio history display, and session detail views

**OAuth flow:**
- In-app browser opens `https://www.strava.com/oauth/authorize`
- User grants; Strava redirects to `apex://oauth/strava/callback`
- Authorization code exchange at `https://www.strava.com/oauth/token`
- Tokens KMS-encrypted and stored

**Token lifecycle:**
- Access token: 6 hours
- Refresh token: long-lived; refresh uses POST to token endpoint
- Refresh handled automatically before expiry

**Sync cadence:**
- Strava webhooks ("Events API") deliver activity created/updated/deleted events in real time
- Webhook subscription managed per-app (not per-user); one subscription covers all users
- On webhook fire: worker fetches the activity details

**Data retrieved per activity:**
- Activity type (Run, Ride, Swim, etc.)
- Start time, duration, distance, elevation gain
- Average heart rate, max heart rate (when sensor data available)
- Average pace / speed
- Workout name (user-assigned label)
- GPS summary (start coordinates, polyline)
- Per-mile splits (when available)

**Data handling:**
- Activities stored with `source = 'strava'` flag in the fitness tables
- Display paths query activities including Strava rows
- AI context assembly query filters `WHERE source != 'strava'` — the single enforcement point for the compliance rule
- Deduplication: if the same activity appears via HealthKit and Strava (common for Garmin users who sync to both), the display layer picks one (HealthKit preferred for consistency); scoring uses one; both source_tag copies retained at the row level for audit and the v1.1 lift path

**Tier placement:** all tiers. Not tier-gated on account count — users have one Strava identity.

**Fallback if disconnected:** Strava activities disappear from fitness history. Activities from other sources (HealthKit, Health Connect, manual) unaffected. No impact on AI context (since Strava was already filtered out).

**Consumed by:** Fitness category (4.3).

**Privacy posture:** standard OAuth token posture. Display data visible to user; AI exclusion enforced at gateway.

**v1.1 lift path:** APEX will submit a written clarification request to Strava developer relations describing the intended use (pre-trained-model inference for user-facing feedback, no model training, no data leaving Life Dashboard for AI-provider purposes). If Strava confirms this falls under the "feedback to users" exemption in their blog framing, the AI gateway's Strava filter is lifted in v1.1 and Strava data joins the main AI context.

---

## 8.7 Vendor-Metered Connections

Three connections where APEX pays per unit of use. Each has a specific cost-mitigation strategy.

### 8.7.1 Plaid (Transactions product only)

**Purpose:** retrieve the user's bank transactions from 12,000+ financial institutions via Plaid's API. Sole data source for the Finance category at v1.

**Plaid products used:**
- **Transactions** — transaction history, account list, cached daily balance

**Plaid products NOT used at v1 (per v1.17 scope reduction):**
- **Auth** — not used (APEX does not move money; real-time account auth unnecessary)
- **Identity** — not used (Clerk handles user identity; Plaid Identity adds no value for APEX's use case)
- **Balance** — not used (Transactions' cached daily balance sufficient for Savings balance trend; real-time balance not needed)
- **Liabilities** — v1.1+ (unlocks student loan and mortgage balances → enables Net Worth)
- **Investments** — v2+ (unlocks brokerage/retirement balances → completes Net Worth)

**Data retrieved:**
- Transaction list: date, amount, merchant name, category (Plaid's PFCv2 taxonomy), pending flag
- Account list: account type (checking, savings, credit), account subtype, mask (last 4 digits), institution
- Cached balance per account: `current` and `available`, updated at least daily when Transactions webhook fires

**Plaid Link flow (user-facing connection):**
- User taps "Connect Bank" in Finance onboarding or Connections screen
- APEX backend creates a `link_token` via `/link/token/create` with `products=["transactions"]`
- Client launches Plaid Link SDK with the link_token
- Plaid's own UI handles institution selection, credential entry, MFA, and account selection
- On success, Link returns a `public_token` to the client
- Client sends `public_token` to APEX backend
- Backend exchanges for `access_token` via `/item/public_token/exchange`
- `access_token` KMS-encrypted per 7.3.2 and stored in `plaid_tokens` table

**Token lifecycle:**
- Access tokens do not expire for Transactions-only scope
- User-initiated credential re-authentication (every ~12 months at some institutions) handled via Plaid Link's update mode
- Access tokens revoked on disconnection or account deletion

**Sync cadence:**
- Plaid Transactions webhooks fire on: `SYNC_UPDATES_AVAILABLE`, `TRANSACTIONS_REMOVED`, `DEFAULT_UPDATE` (legacy), `INITIAL_UPDATE`, `HISTORICAL_UPDATE`, `RECURRING_TRANSACTIONS_UPDATE`
- On webhook: worker calls `/transactions/sync` with the stored cursor, fetches incremental changes, updates local transactions table
- Initial sync: 90 days of transaction history (configurable up to 730 days; APEX uses 90 for baseline, user can request extended history as a v1.1 feature)
- No polling — webhook-driven entirely

**Approval requirements (launch-blocker):**
- Plaid Production access requires business application, compliance review, and use-case validation
- Review timeline: variable — typically 2-8 weeks depending on use case clarity
- APEX's use case (consumer personal finance dashboard, read-only, no money movement) is a standard Plaid pattern with established precedent
- If approval is not secured by launch: Finance category is deferred to v2 entirely (per 4.5 contingency)

**Cost model:**
- Per-connection monthly fee (~$0.30/connection/month for Transactions at Production tier)
- Volume pricing available at scale

**Tier placement:** all tiers. Plaid is explicitly in Core per v1.13 packaging philosophy and 3.8 hard rules. Finance is a core user promise.

**Fallback if disconnected:** Finance category shows "Connect bank" empty state; all Finance features unavailable until reconnection. Manual transaction entry remains available (per 4.5) but is not the promoted path.

**Consumed by:** Finance category (4.5).

**Privacy posture:** highest sensitivity. Financial transactions redacted to MCC categories + amount ranges for AI context per 6.6.3. Raw transaction data never leaves APEX's server infrastructure for AI purposes. Plaid tokens field-level KMS-encrypted per 7.3.2.

### 8.7.2 Google Places

**Purpose:** translate coordinates of user visits (from CoreLocation / Android Geofencing) into recognizable place names (e.g., "Starbucks," "Planet Fitness," "Whole Foods Market"). Powers the Time category's Places subsystem.

**API used:**
- Google Places API (New) — `places:searchNearby` endpoint
- Single integration for both iOS and Android
- No user OAuth — Life Dashboard calls with a server-side API key

**Data requested:**
- Given lat/lng + small radius, return nearest place with name, category, and verified-business status
- Primary type and type tags for categorization (restaurant, gym, pharmacy, etc.)

**Cost model:**
- Per-call pricing: ~$17 per 1,000 Place Nearby Search calls at standard SKU
- APEX mitigates cost via aggressive caching (8.7.2.1)

**Caching strategy (critical for cost control):**
- Every successful lookup cached in Postgres `place_cache` table indexed by (lat, lng) quantized to ~10-meter grid
- Cache TTL: 90 days (place names rarely change; if a business closes or relocates, cache staleness is acceptable)
- Cache hit rate target: >95% after 30 days of operation (most users revisit the same ~20 places)
- Call volume estimate at 10k users × 5 new places/user/month with 95% cache hit: ~2,500 paid lookups/month = ~$42/month total

**Sync cadence:** on-demand. A location visit triggers a place name lookup only after N visits to the same coordinate area (per 4.6 Places subsystem — 3+ visits for commercial naming, 5+ for residential prompting).

**Data flow:**
- Coordinates arrive from CoreLocation or Android Geofencing
- Server-side place_cache check first
- On cache miss: Google Places API call
- Result cached + stored as `named_places` row
- Raw coordinates retained server-side; only named place strings reach AI context

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure, not user-choice).

**Fallback if unavailable:** locations remain as "Unlabeled location" in Day Timeline. User can manually name places via the Places subsystem UI. All other features work.

**Consumed by:** Time category (4.6 — Places, Day Timeline).

**Privacy posture:** APEX sends coordinates to Google Places API on the user's behalf. Coordinates are quantized to 10-meter grid before sending to reduce precision. No user identifier is sent to Google Places — Life Dashboard's server-side API key is used. Resulting place names are stored in APEX; AI context never sees raw coordinates, only labeled strings.

### 8.7.3 Open Food Facts

**Purpose:** look up packaged food product data by barcode. Powers the Nutrition category's barcode scanner (4.4).

**API used:**
- Open Food Facts API (free, open-source food database)
- No authentication required — Life Dashboard calls directly with User-Agent identifying APEX

**Data retrieved per barcode:**
- Product name, brand
- Macros (calories, protein, carbs, fat) per serving
- Serving size, unit
- Ingredients list (not used at v1 but available)
- Allergen tags

**Cost model:** free. Open Food Facts runs on donations. APEX contributes back by submitting product updates when users correct data (optional).

**Rate limit and server-side caching:**
- Open Food Facts asks API consumers to avoid hammering the service
- APEX caches barcode lookups permanently server-side (product macros don't change meaningfully; reformulations are rare)
- Cache key: barcode (EAN-13 / UPC-A numeric string)
- Cache TTL: 365 days (products reformulate occasionally; annual refresh acceptable)
- First user to scan a barcode pays the lookup cost (server fetches from OFF); every subsequent user with the same barcode hits APEX's cache

**Sync cadence:** on-demand. Called only when a user scans a new barcode.

**Data flow:**
- Client scans barcode via device camera (on-device ZXing decoder)
- Barcode string sent to APEX backend
- Backend checks `barcode_cache` table
- On cache miss: fetch from `https://world.openfoodfacts.org/api/v0/product/{barcode}.json`
- Parse and normalize to APEX's macro schema
- Store in cache
- Return to client

**Fallback if unavailable or barcode not found:**
- AI estimation fallback (Haiku 4.5) — the client sends the raw barcode + any on-pack photo + user's best guess as name/brand to a small AI call that estimates macros
- User can edit any AI-estimated values
- Result stored in `custom_products` table (user's own private reference for future scans of the same product)

**Tier placement:** all tiers. Barcode scanning is a core Nutrition feature.

**Consumed by:** Nutrition category (4.4).

**Privacy posture:** barcodes sent to OFF are not personally identifying. No user ID sent. Cache hits never reach OFF.

---

## 8.8 Infrastructure / Delivery Connections

Four infrastructure connections that are not user-choice features but that the user encounters at product edges (sign-in, push notifications, transactional email, subscription management).

### 8.8.1 Clerk (authentication)

**Purpose:** user authentication, including signup, login, password reset, email verification, Google OAuth (for sign-in-with-Google), and session management.

**Integration pattern:**
- Clerk SDK in client handles all auth UI
- Clerk issues JWTs; APEX backend validates via Clerk's JWKS
- Life Dashboard does not store passwords (ever); Clerk owns that storage

**Data flow (user sees):**
- User enters email + password (or taps Continue with Google) at signup/signin
- Clerk handles auth, returns JWT to client
- Client includes JWT in Authorization header on all API calls
- APEX validates JWT on every request

**Webhook events APEX processes:**
- `user.created` — create corresponding row in APEX `users` table
- `user.updated` — sync profile changes
- `user.deleted` — trigger APEX account deletion flow
- `session.created` / `session.revoked` — audit logging

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure).

**Privacy posture:** Clerk is a processor (documented in 6.11). Authentication flow is the minimum data path; user email and password are at Clerk, not APEX.

### 8.8.2 RevenueCat (subscription management)

**Purpose:** manage iOS App Store and Google Play subscription state. Handles receipt validation, entitlement synchronization, trial tracking, and cross-platform subscription unification.

**Integration pattern:**
- RevenueCat SDK in client handles subscription purchase, restore, upgrade, downgrade
- RevenueCat webhooks notify APEX backend of subscription state changes
- APEX reads entitlement state from RevenueCat for tier gating

**Webhook events APEX processes:**
- `INITIAL_PURCHASE`, `RENEWAL`, `CANCELLATION`, `EXPIRATION`, `BILLING_ISSUE`, `PRODUCT_CHANGE`, `TRIAL_STARTED`, `TRIAL_ENDED`

**Data flow:**
- User purchases Pro via App Store or Play Store
- Apple/Google processes payment
- RevenueCat validates receipt
- RevenueCat webhook fires to APEX
- Life Dashboard updates user's `subscription_tier` and `subscription_status`
- Client re-queries entitlements on launch

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure).

**Privacy posture:** processor. User subscription state only; no financial account data flows through RevenueCat.

### 8.8.3 Expo Notifications (push delivery)

**Purpose:** deliver push notifications to user's device. Expo's push service is a convenient proxy over APNs (iOS) and FCM (Android).

**Integration pattern:**
- Expo Notifications SDK in client registers for push and returns an Expo push token
- Client sends Expo push token to APEX backend
- APEX stores token per user per device
- When the notification evaluator (per 4.9) decides to fire a notification, APEX calls Expo's push API with the user's token

**Cost model:** free at APEX's expected scale (Expo's free tier handles millions of pushes/month).

**Sync cadence:** event-driven. No polling.

**Reliability:**
- Expo provides delivery status via receipts
- Failed deliveries (invalid token) trigger token invalidation in APEX's push_tokens table
- Users who haven't opened the app in >30 days may have stale tokens; expected pattern

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure).

**Privacy posture:** push token is not PII. Notification content avoids PII by design (per 4.9 — no financial amounts, no full email content, no specific health values).

### 8.8.4 SendGrid (transactional email)

**Purpose:** deliver transactional emails — account verification (via Clerk, but APEX also sends some), export-ready notifications, account deletion confirmations, security alerts, subscription receipts (where not handled by app store).

**Integration pattern:**
- SendGrid REST API for send
- SendGrid Event Webhook for delivery status (delivered, bounced, deferred, opened, clicked)

**Email types APEX sends:**
- Export bundle ready (with signed R2 download link)
- Account deletion initiated confirmation
- Account deletion reminder (day 23 of grace)
- Account deletion final reminder (day 29 of grace)
- Account deletion complete confirmation
- Security alerts (new device login notifications — v1.1+)
- Plaid reconnection needed
- OAuth reconnection needed

**Cost model:** per-email tiered pricing. At 10k users with ~10 transactional emails/user/year = 100k emails/year, easily within low-tier plans.

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure).

**Privacy posture:** SendGrid is a processor. Email content is low-sensitivity (no financial amounts in alert emails; export emails contain signed URL but data itself is not in the email body).

### 8.8.5 Anthropic (AI provider)

**Purpose:** AI inference for chatbot queries, meal photo scanning, meal text parsing, workout text parsing, email classification, triage recommendations, and Pro features (AI email drafts, daily AI brief, cross-category correlations, Pantry Scanner, Premium Scan).

**Models used at v1:**
- **Claude Haiku 4.5** — default model for most calls (chatbot, classifications, simple parsing)
- **Claude Sonnet 4.6** — reasoning-heavy calls (meal photo scan, email draft composition)
- **Claude Opus 4.6** — Pro-only Premium Scan feature

**Integration pattern:**
- Anthropic SDK in backend workers and API tier
- Requests include system prompts (versioned, stored centrally) + user-assembled context
- Streaming used for chatbot responses

**Call boundaries:**
- AI gateway (per 6.6.1) is the single point all AI calls pass through
- Gateway applies consent redaction, Strava filter, and context container assembly before dispatching to Anthropic
- Gateway logs token counts and estimated cost per call for cost observability

**Cost model:** per-token pricing. APEX mitigates via:
- Deterministic-first design (per 3.2) — AI only where it's the best tool
- Cheapest-acceptable-model default
- Quota gating: 10 queries/day Core, 50/day Pro (300/day soft cap)
- Aggressive context redaction to minimize input token count

**Tier placement:** all tiers (infrastructure), with per-user quota by tier per 4.7.16.

**Fallback if unavailable:** AI-dependent features show friendly failure messages ("AI is temporarily unavailable"). Deterministic features (scoring, manual logging, dashboards) continue to work per the failure isolation commitment in 5.11.

**Privacy posture:** Anthropic is a processor. Redaction rules per 6.6.3 strictly limit what reaches Anthropic's servers. No raw transaction data, no full email bodies, no precise coordinates. Strava data hard-excluded per 6.6.3.

**Consumed by:** all categories (4.7 chatbot, 4.4 nutrition scanning, 4.9 notifications, etc.).

---

## 8.9 Connections NOT In v1 (Deferred or Blocked)

Explicit enumeration of connections APEX does not support at v1, with rationale.

### 8.9.1 Deferred — planned for v1.1+

**Strava AI context lift:** Strava data is available for display and deterministic scoring at v1, but excluded from AI context per 6.6.3 at launch. If Strava developer relations confirms in writing that Life Dashboard's inference-only user-facing insights fall under their "feedback to users" exemption, the AI filter is lifted in v1.1. No user-visible product change is required to flip this — the filter is a single gateway rule.

**Plaid Liabilities:** enables student loan and mortgage balances. Unlocks Net Worth (cut from v1 per v1.17 decision — see 4.5.9). Timeline: v1.1+ dependent on Plaid approval for additional product.

**Plaid Investments:** enables brokerage and retirement account balances. Completes Net Worth picture. Timeline: v2+.

**Garmin Connect direct API:** at v1, Garmin data flows through Apple HealthKit (iOS) and Google Health Connect (Android) — Garmin watches sync to both native platforms. Direct Garmin Connect Developer Program integration would add some metrics (Body Battery, Training Readiness) not exposed through HealthKit/Health Connect, but adds integration complexity and requires Garmin approval. Deferred to v1.1+ if user demand is clear.

**Google Fit API:** treated as Android fallback only (8.5.2). Google has announced long-term deprecation of Google Fit API in favor of Health Connect. APEX's direction is Health Connect primary with Google Fit as transitional fallback.

### 8.9.2 Evaluated and declined

**Strava with full AI context at v1:** see 6.6.3 rationale. Conservative posture at launch; lift pending Strava clarification.

**Plaid Auth, Identity, Balance:** not used at v1 per 4.5.14. APEX does not move money (Auth unnecessary), Clerk handles user identity (Identity unnecessary), and Transactions' cached daily balance is sufficient for all Savings features (Balance unnecessary).

**Expanded HealthKit/Health Connect scope:** per v1.16 founder decision, HealthKit integration remains at the baseline scope (weight, steps, workouts, sleep, HRV, heart rate, water). Additional data types (mindful minutes, time in daylight, audio exposure, wrist temperature, blood glucose, menstrual cycle, etc.) not added at v1.

**Uber ride history:** evaluated, declined for v1. OAuth available and free, but niche use case and additional feature surface not justified for launch.

**Apple Wallet passes:** evaluated, declined for v1. iOS-only, free, would put boarding passes in Day Timeline. Niche and platform-asymmetric.

**Receipt OCR:** evaluated, declined for v1. On-device (Apple Vision / Android ML Kit), free, could supplement Plaid transaction data with line-item detail. Additional feature surface; deferred.

**Spotify listening history:** evaluated, declined for v1. Free OAuth, privacy-sensitive (reveals mood/activity patterns). Deferred pending clearer use case.

**HomeKit / Google Home environment data:** v2+ consideration for sleep-environment correlation. Not at v1.

**GitHub / Slack / Notion / Linear / Jira:** developer and knowledge-worker productivity signals. Niche per-user. Not at v1.

**Coinbase (crypto holdings):** niche audience. Deferred pending clearer signal.

**Social media activity (Instagram, Twitter, TikTok, LinkedIn):** deliberately out of scope per APEX's privacy posture. Most of these APIs are B2B-only; none are appropriate for personal-data aggregation.

### 8.9.3 Technically not accessible

**iCloud Mail content:** Apple provides no third-party API for iCloud Mail. Users with iCloud Mail addresses can set up mail forwarding to a Gmail or Outlook account they connect to APEX, but iCloud Mail itself cannot be read. Not documented user-facing per v1.17 decision (support handles the question on inquiry).

**iMessage content:** no third-party API. Apple provides zero integration path for consumer apps.

**WhatsApp personal messages:** Meta's WhatsApp API is business-to-consumer only. No consumer API for reading a user's own message history.

**Facebook Messenger personal messages:** same restriction as WhatsApp.

**SMS/text content:**
- iOS: restricted, no API
- Android: `READ_SMS` permission exists but Play Store policy restricts use to default SMS apps and specific categories (anti-theft, etc.); not viable for APEX

**Personal social media activity:** deliberately out of scope per privacy principle.

### 8.9.4 Strava ToS compliance posture (summary)

Strava is the one connection where APEX's integration depth is explicitly constrained by third-party terms beyond the technical API surface. Per Strava's November 2024 API Agreement:

- ✅ Read user's Strava activities (OAuth with `activity:read`)
- ✅ Display activities in APEX to the authenticated user
- ✅ Use activities for deterministic scoring and goals
- ✅ Store activities on Life Dashboard servers for the user's own access
- ✅ Export activities in GDPR user export
- ❌ Send activity data to AI models or similar applications (at v1)
- ❌ Display another user's Strava activities (not a feature APEX offers)
- ❌ Modify activities received from Strava (not a feature APEX offers)
- ❌ Use Strava branding in ways implying endorsement

The AI restriction is the only one that shapes APEX's integration architecture. Details in 6.6.3 redaction rules. Lift path in 8.9.1.

---

## 8.10 Connection Lifecycle

The standard lifecycle for connections — applies to all OAuth and device-permission-based connections with minor vendor-specific variations.

### 8.10.1 Addition (user connects)

**1. User taps connection in Connections screen (or during onboarding Connection Connect step)**

**2. Client initiates provider-specific flow:**
- Device-native: system permission prompt (HealthKit `requestAuthorization`, CoreLocation `requestAlwaysAuthorization`, etc.)
- OAuth: in-app browser opens provider's consent screen
- Plaid: Plaid Link SDK launches

**3. User grants at provider's UI**

**4. Provider returns:**
- Device-native: authorization state callback
- OAuth: authorization code via redirect URI
- Plaid: `public_token` via SDK callback

**5. Token exchange (OAuth and Plaid):**
- Client sends auth code to APEX backend
- Backend exchanges for access_token + refresh_token (OAuth) or `access_token` (Plaid)
- Backend KMS-encrypts tokens per 7.3.2
- Backend stores in appropriate table

**6. Connection record created:**
- `connected_accounts` row created: `user_id`, `source_key`, `connected_at`, `status = 'active'`, provider-specific identifiers
- For multi-account connections, additional rows per account

**7. Initial sync triggered:**
- Device-native: immediate data query on next app foreground
- OAuth cloud: worker queued for initial historical pull
- Plaid: initial transaction sync triggered via first webhook (Plaid fires `INITIAL_UPDATE` after establishing the connection)

**8. UI reflects connected state:**
- Connection row shows green dot and "Connected" status
- Data begins populating in category views as initial sync completes

### 8.10.2 Token refresh (OAuth and Plaid)

**OAuth connections:**
- Worker monitors expiry timestamps
- When access_token < 10 minutes from expiry, worker calls provider's refresh endpoint
- New access_token (and sometimes new refresh_token) replaces old values
- KMS re-encrypts and updates stored rows
- User never sees refresh; transparent

**Plaid connections:**
- Plaid access tokens do not expire for Transactions scope (permanent until revoked)
- Some institutions require periodic credential re-authentication (~every 12 months)
- Plaid fires `PENDING_EXPIRATION` or `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` webhook
- APEX marks the connection as requiring reauth
- User sees "Update connection" prompt in Connections screen
- Plaid Link opens in update mode; user re-enters credentials
- On success, connection reactivated

### 8.10.3 Disconnection (user-initiated)

**1. User taps "Disconnect" in Connection Detail screen**

**2. Confirmation modal:**
- Explains what disconnecting does
- Secondary warning if this connection powers critical features
- Primary button: "Disconnect" (destructive red)
- Secondary: "Cancel"

**3. On confirm:**
- OAuth: worker calls provider's revocation endpoint (Google `oauth2.revoke`, Microsoft `v2.0/logout`, Strava `oauth/deauthorize`)
- Plaid: worker calls `/item/remove`
- Device-native: no provider-side revocation needed (permission persists in OS until user revokes)
- APEX-side: `connected_accounts` row status → `disconnected`
- Tokens in `plaid_tokens` / `email_accounts` / `calendar_tokens` etc. → deleted (not retained; no need once revoked)
- Syncs stopped; webhook subscriptions cancelled

**4. Data retention:**
- Previously-synced data remains in user's account by default (user granted us the data; disconnecting doesn't auto-delete)
- Connection Detail screen shows "Delete all my data from this source" action for user-initiated purge
- Full purge: 30-day soft-delete window per 4.11 retention policy, then hard delete

**5. UI:**
- Connection row moves from Connected to Available list
- Toast: "[Connection] disconnected"

### 8.10.4 Revocation (provider-initiated)

Scenarios where the provider severs the connection unilaterally:
- User revokes APEX in their Google / Microsoft / Strava / Plaid settings
- Provider's ToS changes APEX's eligibility
- Provider decides to revoke for security/abuse reasons
- User's provider account is deleted

**Detection:**
- Scheduled sync fails with HTTP 401 or provider-specific revocation error
- Webhook subscription delivery fails
- Token refresh returns 401/403

**Handling:**
- Worker marks `connected_accounts.status = 'revoked'`
- Tokens deleted (already invalid)
- User notified via push + in-app banner on next app open: "Your [Provider] connection was revoked. Reconnect to continue."
- Reconnection flow matches addition flow (fresh OAuth)

### 8.10.5 Permission loss (partial revocation)

Some providers allow the user to revoke specific scopes without disconnecting entirely (especially Google).

**Detection:**
- API calls for the revoked scope return 403 `insufficient_permissions`
- Other scopes may still work

**Handling:**
- Worker marks the specific scope as disabled in connection metadata
- Features that require the revoked scope show degraded state
- User notified with clear guidance: "APEX can no longer read your email because Gmail read access was revoked. Reconnect to restore email features."

### 8.10.6 Account deletion cascade

Per 4.11.14, account deletion triggers:
- All OAuth tokens revoked at providers
- All device-native permissions become moot (app uninstalled or account state purged)
- All cached data entering 30-day grace-period soft-delete
- All webhook subscriptions cancelled

---

## 8.11 Multi-Account Support & Tier Gating

### 8.11.1 What "multi-account" means

For connections where the user may have more than one identity (multiple Gmail accounts, multiple Outlook calendars, multiple Plaid-connected banks), APEX supports adding each as a separate connection row.

**Connections supporting multi-account:**
- Gmail — users often have personal + work + side-business addresses
- Microsoft Outlook Mail — same pattern
- Google Calendar — personal + work + shared family calendar
- Microsoft Outlook Calendar — same pattern
- Plaid — users have multiple bank accounts across multiple institutions

**Connections that are inherently single-stream per device:**
- Apple HealthKit — one per device
- Apple EventKit — one per device
- CoreLocation — one per device
- All other device-native — tied to the device, not an identity
- Strava — users have one Strava account

### 8.11.2 Tier gating

Per the v1.17 packaging decision:

**Core tier:**
- **Email accounts (Gmail + Outlook Mail, any mix):** up to 2 total. A user can have 1 Gmail + 1 Outlook, or 2 Gmail and no Outlook, or 2 Outlook and no Gmail. The 2-total-cap is enforced in the email account category.
- **Calendar accounts (Google Calendar + Outlook Calendar + Apple EventKit + Android Calendar Provider, any mix):** up to 2 total. Same mix rules.
- **Plaid:** unlimited bank accounts. Not tier-gated by count.
- **All other connections:** no multi-account concept.

**Pro tier:**
- **Email accounts:** unlimited.
- **Calendar accounts:** unlimited.
- **Plaid:** unlimited (unchanged from Core).

### 8.11.3 Paywall trigger behavior

When a Core user taps "Add email account" with 2 email accounts already connected:
- Client detects the tier limit client-side
- Paywall modal appears with specific messaging: *"Life Dashboard Core supports 2 email accounts. Upgrade to Pro to add more."*
- Modal has Pro pricing (monthly and annual) and CTA
- User either subscribes (immediate unlock) or cancels

Same behavior for calendar accounts at the 2-total limit.

No paywall for Plaid — users with 5 bank accounts on Core see all 5 connected normally.

### 8.11.4 Downgrade behavior (Pro → Core)

If a user cancels Pro and has 5 email accounts connected:
- At downgrade effective date, all 5 accounts remain connected but read-only is preserved (APEX continues to sync existing accounts rather than breaking functionality)
- User cannot ADD a 6th email account on Core
- User can REMOVE accounts freely until they drop to 2 total
- If user adds a 7th Pro-era account then downgrades, no lockout — existing accounts remain; adds are blocked

This is consistent with 4.8.5 downgrade policy and 13's business rules: Pro features gracefully degrade rather than hard-lock on downgrade, except for explicitly capped features (AI quota, export frequency).

### 8.11.5 Database representation

Per Section 6.2.6 and 5.4:
- `email_accounts` table: one row per connected email account; `user_id`, `provider` (gmail/outlook), `email_address`, `oauth_token` (encrypted), `refresh_token` (encrypted), etc.
- `calendar_tokens` table: one row per cloud calendar account
- Device-native calendars (Apple EventKit, Android Calendar Provider) treated as a single row per device

---

## 8.12 Sync Cadence Matrix

Summary of when each connection exchanges data with its source.

| Connection | Trigger | Cadence | Webhook support |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apple HealthKit | `HKObserverQuery` wake | Background delivery + foreground poll | Native observer callback |
| Apple EventKit | `EKEventStoreChanged` | On-change + foreground | Native NSNotification |
| Apple Family Controls | Scheduled extension runs | ~hourly per Apple | No third-party webhook |
| CoreLocation | `locationManager(_:didVisit:)` | Event-driven on visits | Native delegate callback |
| Core Motion | On-demand query | Foreground only | No |
| Apple WeatherKit | Client request | On app open + 60-min refresh foregrounded | No |
| Google Health Connect | `getChanges()` | Foreground + 4-hour background | Android change notification |
| Google Fit (fallback) | Worker poll | 4-hour polling | No |
| Android UsageStatsManager | Client query | On app open + 4-hour background | No |
| Android Geofencing | Intent receiver | Event-driven | On-device intents |
| Android Calendar Provider | `ContentObserver` | On-change + foreground | Yes (content observer) |
| OpenWeatherMap | Client request | On app open + 60-min refresh foregrounded | No |
| Google Calendar | Push channels + fallback poll | Webhook-driven; 4-hour poll fallback | Yes (push channels) |
| Gmail | Push + poll fallback | Webhook-driven; 4-hour fallback | Yes (Pub/Sub) |
| Outlook Mail | Graph subscriptions + poll | Webhook-driven; 4-hour fallback | Yes (Graph subscriptions) |
| Outlook Calendar | Graph subscriptions + poll | Webhook-driven; 4-hour fallback | Yes (Graph subscriptions) |
| Strava | Events API webhook | Webhook-driven; no polling | Yes (Events API) |
| Plaid | Transactions webhooks | Webhook-driven; `/transactions/sync` on each | Yes (Plaid webhooks) |
| Google Places | On-demand | Per visit after N visits threshold; cached 90 days | No |
| Open Food Facts | On-demand | Per unique barcode scan; cached permanently | No |
| Anthropic | On-demand | Per feature invocation | No |
| Clerk | Clerk webhooks | User lifecycle events | Yes (Svix) |
| RevenueCat | RevenueCat webhooks | Subscription lifecycle events | Yes |
| Expo Notifications | Outbound only | Per notification send | No inbound |
| SendGrid | Outbound + event webhooks | Per email + delivery events | Yes (events) |

### 8.12.1 Polling discipline

APEX polls only when webhooks are unavailable or as a fallback safety net against missed webhooks. Polling cadences are chosen to balance freshness vs cost:
- 4-hour fallback polling for webhook-driven services catches any missed events
- On-demand polling (Open Food Facts, Google Places, Anthropic) happens only when user action triggers a data need
- No polling for connections that update event-driven (Strava, Plaid — fully webhook-driven)

### 8.12.2 Sync batching

Workers batch sync operations where possible:
- HealthKit data uploaded in batches per-category (not one call per data point)
- Plaid transactions processed in the same worker cycle as they arrive
- Gmail message metadata batched per thread where the thread has multiple new messages

### 8.12.3 Background execution budgets

**iOS:** background fetch is limited by iOS's discretion. APEX uses:
- Background delivery for HealthKit (unlimited when data arrives)
- Silent push for time-sensitive webhook processing (when APEX server needs the client to sync specifically)
- Background App Refresh for scheduled syncs (iOS-controlled cadence)

**Android:** WorkManager schedules:
- Periodic workers for 4-hour fallback polling
- One-time workers for event-driven uploads
- Doze mode and App Standby respected

---

## 8.13 Webhook Behavior Per Connection

Expands on 7.12 with connection-specific event catalogs.

### 8.13.1 Plaid

**Events received:**
- `SYNC_UPDATES_AVAILABLE` — new transactions available; worker calls `/transactions/sync`
- `TRANSACTIONS_REMOVED` — transactions deleted at the bank; worker removes from local table
- `INITIAL_UPDATE` — initial historical pull complete
- `HISTORICAL_UPDATE` — older history available
- `PENDING_EXPIRATION` — credentials expiring; prompt user to reauth
- `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` — reauth needed now
- `ERROR` — item-level error; worker investigates

**Signature:** JWS signature header; validated against Plaid's public key per 7.12.1.

**Idempotency:** each webhook has a unique `webhook_event_id`; deduplication via `webhook_processed_events` table.

### 8.13.2 Google Calendar

**Mechanism:** push notification channels. Life Dashboard creates a channel per user via `events.watch`. Channel expires after up to 7 days; worker renews proactively.

**Payload:** minimal — sync token and resource ID. Worker calls `events.list` with the sync token to get changed events.

### 8.13.3 Gmail

**Mechanism:** Google Pub/Sub topic subscription. APEX subscribes to `users.messages` changes via `watch` API.

**Payload:** history ID. Worker calls `users.history.list` to get changed message IDs, then fetches metadata via `messages.get`.

### 8.13.4 Microsoft Graph (Outlook Mail + Calendar)

**Mechanism:** subscriptions via `/subscriptions` endpoint; APEX provides webhook URL.

**Payload:** resource URI + change type. Worker fetches the specific resource.

**Subscription lifetime:** up to 4,230 minutes (~3 days) for most Outlook resources; renewed automatically.

### 8.13.5 Strava

**Mechanism:** Events API push subscription — application-level, not per-user. Single subscription covers all APEX users.

**Events received:**
- `activity` with `create` / `update` / `delete` — new or modified activity
- `athlete` with `update` — profile changes (rarely material for APEX)
- `athlete` with `delete` (aspect_type = `update`, `authorized = false`) — user deauthorized APEX; treat as revocation

**Payload:** user ID, activity ID, event type. Worker fetches activity details via `/activities/{id}` for create/update events.

### 8.13.6 Clerk

**Mechanism:** Svix-signed webhooks.

**Events received:** user lifecycle — `user.created`, `user.updated`, `user.deleted`, `session.created`, `session.ended`, `email.created`, `email.updated`.

### 8.13.7 RevenueCat

**Mechanism:** HMAC-signed webhooks.

**Events received:** subscription lifecycle — `INITIAL_PURCHASE`, `RENEWAL`, `CANCELLATION`, `EXPIRATION`, `BILLING_ISSUE`, `PRODUCT_CHANGE`, `TRIAL_STARTED`, `TRIAL_ENDED`, `TRIAL_CANCELLED`, `TRIAL_CONVERTED`, `NON_RENEWING_PURCHASE`, `UNCANCELLATION`, `REFUND`.

### 8.13.8 SendGrid

**Mechanism:** ECDSA-signed event webhooks.

**Events received:** delivery status — `processed`, `delivered`, `bounce`, `deferred`, `open`, `click`, `spamreport`, `unsubscribe`.

### 8.13.9 Common webhook handling requirements

Per 7.12:
- Signature validation on every inbound webhook
- Timestamp validation (5-minute skew window)
- Idempotency via processed_events table
- 200 OK returned immediately, processing queued via SQS

---

## 8.14 Fallback & Degradation Behavior

### 8.14.1 Per-connection fallback summary

| Connection | If unavailable / denied | Features affected | Fallback |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apple HealthKit | iOS permission denied | Weight, workouts, sleep, recovery | Manual entry (weight, workouts); sleep/HRV lost |
| Apple EventKit | Permission denied | Calendar CRUD on iOS, Day Timeline | Connect Google / Outlook Cal; or lose calendar features |
| Family Controls | Entitlement denied or user denies | Screen Time on iOS | Android Screen Time still works; feature hidden on iOS |
| CoreLocation | Permission denied | Day Timeline location, Places | Manual entry; feature hidden |
| Core Motion | Permission denied | Day Timeline motion labels | Transitions shown as generic "movement" |
| WeatherKit | Location denied | Weather widget | Widget hidden |
| Health Connect | Not installed / denied | Android fitness data | Google Fit fallback; then manual |
| Google Fit | Denied | Android fitness fallback | Manual entry |
| UsageStatsManager | Special permission denied | Android Screen Time | iOS works; feature hidden on Android |
| Android Geofencing | Permission denied | Day Timeline, Places on Android | Manual entry |
| Android Calendar Provider | Permission denied | Android calendar | Connect Google / Outlook Cal |
| OpenWeatherMap | API unavailable | Weather widget on Android | Widget hidden |
| Google Calendar | Disconnected | Cloud calendar, Day Timeline | Other calendar sources; or manual |
| Gmail | Disconnected or verification delayed | Email features | Connect Outlook; or lose features |
| Outlook Mail | Disconnected | Email features | Connect Gmail; or lose features |
| Outlook Calendar | Disconnected | Cloud calendar | Other calendar sources |
| Strava | Disconnected | Strava activities in cardio | HealthKit sync; or manual |
| Plaid | Disconnected or approval not secured | Finance category entirely | Deferred — v2 |
| Google Places | API unavailable | Place name lookup | Locations labeled as "Unlabeled location" |
| Open Food Facts | API unavailable or barcode not found | Barcode lookup | AI macro estimation fallback |
| Anthropic | API unavailable | All AI features | Friendly failure message; deterministic features continue |
| Clerk | API unavailable | Auth | App unusable until Clerk recovers; existing sessions may continue briefly |
| RevenueCat | API unavailable | Subscription state | Entitlements cached; degraded state on longer outage |
| Expo Notifications | Push service unavailable | Push delivery | Events queue in SQS and retry |
| SendGrid | Email delivery unavailable | Transactional emails | Events queue in SQS; in-app messages unaffected |

### 8.14.2 Cascading failure isolation

Per 5.11 failure isolation commitment: a failure at one vendor does not cascade to break unrelated features. Examples:
- Plaid outage: Finance category shows "Sync delayed"; Nutrition and Fitness fully functional
- Anthropic outage: chatbot and AI meal parsing unavailable; dashboard, scoring, and manual logging all work
- Clerk outage: new logins fail; existing sessions continue (Clerk JWTs have 1-hour TTL; users can use the app for up to an hour past an outage start)
- Strava outage: Strava activities not updating; HealthKit/Health Connect activities unaffected; scoring continues from other sources

### 8.14.3 Graceful degradation in scoring

Per the Scoring Engine (Section 9), each category score is computed from signals that may or may not be available. When a data source is disconnected:
- Signals derived from that source are excluded from the score
- Score weighting re-normalizes across available signals
- A minimum data threshold prevents scoring with too little data (shows "—" instead)

---

## 8.15 Launch-Blocker Connections

Five connections require external approval before v1 launch. Each has a separate timeline and fallback posture. Garmin (8.15.5) is new in v1.18; all prior blockers are unchanged.

### 8.15.1 Apple Family Controls entitlement

**Required for:** iOS Screen Time data (8.4.3).

**Process:**
- Apply via Apple Developer Portal with written justification
- Life Dashboard's justification: consumer self-awareness app; Screen Time is one input among many for the Time category's attention subsystem; no parental control framing; no family-sharing requirements
- Review timeline: typically 1-4 weeks

**Launch posture:**
- Apply the moment the Apple Developer account is active and bundle ID registered (pre-code setup complete)
- Launch blocker: #1 priority to resolve before v1
- If entitlement is granted: iOS Screen Time ships at v1 on par with Android
- If entitlement is denied or delayed: iOS Screen Time ships at v1.1+ after resubmitting with refined framing; Android Screen Time works at v1 independently

**Risk:** Apple rejects ~50% of Family Controls applications on first submission. Life Dashboard's framing must be tight and non-parental-control-adjacent.

### 8.15.2 Plaid Production approval

**Required for:** all Finance category functionality.

**Process:**
- Apply via Plaid Dashboard with business details, use case description, and compliance documentation
- Life Dashboard's use case (personal finance dashboard, read-only, no money movement) is a standard and well-understood Plaid pattern
- Review timeline: 2-8 weeks; includes compliance review, sometimes a brief product review

**Launch posture:**
- Apply as soon as Plaid Production application readiness is met (business entity formed, basic product demo available)
- Launch blocker: #2 priority
- If approved by launch: Finance ships at v1
- If not approved by launch: Finance is deferred to v2 entirely per 4.5.2 contingency. The other three categories launch without Finance.

**Mitigation:** submit early; Plaid reviews are generally predictable for clean use cases like Life Dashboard's. Founder's prior familiarity with Plaid integration (current Flask prototype) speeds the application.

### 8.15.3 Google OAuth verification (Gmail sensitive scopes)

**Required for:** Gmail integration (8.6.2).

**Process:**
- Submit for Google OAuth verification through Google Cloud Console
- Requires: privacy policy URL, terms of service URL, in-product OAuth consent screen justification, completion of CASA Tier 2 security assessment (via third-party assessor for restricted scopes)
- Review timeline: 4-8 weeks for restricted scopes

**Launch posture:**
- Begin verification process early in development (the application can reference a beta version of the app)
- Launch blocker: #3 priority
- If verified by launch: Gmail ships at v1
- If not verified: Gmail deferred to v1.1+; users see "Gmail coming soon" and can use Outlook Mail instead

**Mitigation:** start the verification process even before final UI is polished. CASA assessment is the bottleneck; book with an assessor early.

### 8.15.4 Microsoft Graph verification (Outlook Mail sensitive scopes)

**Required for:** Outlook Mail integration (8.6.3).

**Process:**
- Register multi-tenant app in Azure AD
- Submit for Microsoft OAuth verification for `Mail.Read` (and related scopes)
- Review timeline: 1-4 weeks (Microsoft's process is lighter than Google's)

**Launch posture:**
- Start the verification after Google's process is underway; Microsoft's is a faster follow-on
- Launch blocker: #4 priority (lowest risk)
- If verified by launch: Outlook Mail ships at v1
- If not verified: Outlook Mail deferred to v1.1+; users rely on Gmail (if Google verified) or have email features unavailable

### 8.15.5 Garmin API access (v1.18 addition)

**Required for:** Garmin Connect integration (optional at v1 — see §1.7 launch requirements).

**Context:** Per §1.7, Life Dashboard will not ship v1 without at least one of Strava or Garmin as a native Fitness integration. Strava is the backstop (OAuth is commercially available to new developers without lengthy approval). Garmin is applied-for in parallel because the Ambitious Generalist target user overlaps heavily with Garmin users and the data fidelity is materially higher (HRV quality, body battery, training load, stress score).

**Process:**
- Register Garmin developer account at developer.garmin.com
- Apply for access to the Garmin Health API and Activity API (consumer app scope)
- Garmin's review has two stages: (1) developer account acceptance (1-4 weeks historically), (2) production-scope approval for consumer apps (4-12 weeks, varies by requested scope breadth and app category)
- Submission includes product description, data use declaration, privacy policy, and expected user volume
- Life Dashboard's request scope: daily summaries, activities, sleep, HRV, body battery, training status — all read-only

**Launch posture:**
- Apply at **Week 0** of build, simultaneously with Apple Developer and Plaid applications
- Decision gate at **Week 16** of build:
  - If Garmin approval is secured by Week 16: Garmin ships at v1 alongside Strava. Users connecting Garmin get the richer data fields; Strava connection remains available for users who prefer it.
  - If Garmin approval is delayed past Week 16: v1 ships Strava-only. Garmin is added as a v1.1+ connection when approval completes. Users with Garmin watches can still pipe summary data via HealthKit / Health Connect (loses the structured advanced metrics but preserves basic workouts and sleep).
- Not a hard launch blocker (Strava satisfies the §1.7 floor), but a high-value approval to secure

**Mitigation:** apply immediately in Week 0 so the clock starts. Garmin's approval is notoriously inconsistent for solo developers; having the application in flight early maximizes chance of catching the Week-16 gate. If approval arrives after launch, it becomes a v1.1 feature announcement rather than a scrambled addition.

### 8.15.6 Launch-blocker resolution roadmap

**Week 0 (company formation + Apple Developer setup):**
- Register Apple Developer account
- Register bundle ID
- Submit Family Controls entitlement application
- Begin Plaid Production application paperwork
- **Submit Garmin developer account application (v1.18 addition)**

**Week 2-3:**
- Family Controls decision typically arrives
- Plaid application submitted
- Google Cloud Console project set up; begin CASA assessment booking
- Garmin developer account status expected; if accepted, begin production-scope application

**Week 4-8:**
- Plaid approval typically arrives
- Google OAuth verification submitted (CASA assessment may still be in flight)
- Microsoft Graph verification submitted
- Garmin production-scope application underway

**Week 8-12:**
- Google verification typically resolves
- Microsoft verification typically resolves
- Garmin production scope may or may not resolve (high variance)
- All launch-blocker connections approved or fallback posture confirmed

**Week 16 decision gate:**
- Garmin status evaluated — if approved, integration ships at v1; if not, Strava-only at v1 and Garmin becomes v1.1+

**v1 launch:**
- Ship with whichever approvals are complete
- Features gated on unapproved connections show clear "Coming soon" messaging
- No shipped feature depends on an unapproved connection

---

## 8.16 Per-Connection Tier Placement (Detailed)

The authoritative tier-mapping table for business purposes. Section 13 consolidates this into the final cut-line spec; Section 8.16 is the source of truth for connection-level decisions.

| Connection | Core | Pro | Pro differentiator | Launch-blocker |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple HealthKit | ✓ | ✓ | None (same experience) | No |
| Apple EventKit | ✓ | ✓ | None (unlimited accounts per v1.18) | No |
| Apple Family Controls + DeviceActivityReport | ✓ | ✓ | None | Yes (entitlement) |
| CoreLocation + Visit Monitoring | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Core Motion | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Apple WeatherKit | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Google Health Connect | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Google Fit (fallback) | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Android UsageStatsManager | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Android Geofencing + Activity Recognition | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Android Calendar Provider | ✓ | ✓ | None (unlimited accounts per v1.18) | No |
| OpenWeatherMap | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Google Calendar | ✓ (unlimited) | ✓ (unlimited) | None (v1.18: multi-account gate dropped from Core) | No |
| Gmail | ✓ (unlimited) | ✓ (unlimited) | None (v1.18: multi-account gate dropped from Core) | Yes (Google verification) |
| Outlook Mail | ✓ (unlimited) | ✓ (unlimited) | None (v1.18: multi-account gate dropped from Core) | Yes (Microsoft verification) |
| Outlook Calendar | ✓ (unlimited) | ✓ (unlimited) | None (v1.18: multi-account gate dropped from Core) | No |
| Strava | ✓ | ✓ | None (one Strava identity per user) | No |
| Plaid | ✓ (unlimited banks) | ✓ (unlimited banks) | None — Plaid is explicitly Core | Yes (Plaid approval) |
| Google Places | ✓ (infrastructure) | ✓ (infrastructure) | None | No |
| Open Food Facts | ✓ | ✓ | None | No |
| Anthropic | ✓ (10 AI queries/day) | ✓ (50/day with 300/day soft cap) | 5x quota + Pro-only features (email drafts, Pantry Scanner, Premium Scan) | No |
| Clerk | ✓ (infrastructure) | ✓ (infrastructure) | None | No |
| RevenueCat | ✓ (infrastructure) | ✓ (infrastructure) | None | No |
| Expo Notifications | ✓ (infrastructure) | ✓ (infrastructure) | None | No |
| SendGrid | ✓ (infrastructure) | ✓ (infrastructure) | None | No |

### 8.16.1 Observations

- **Plaid is in Core with no tier cap.** Explicit commitment. Users with 10 bank accounts on Core see all 10.
- **Email and calendar are the primary tier differentiators.** The "2 total" Core limit + Pro unlimited is where meaningful business differentiation lives for cloud connections.
- **Anthropic is tier-differentiated by quota, not presence.** Both tiers can use AI; Pro gets 10x the daily quota plus Pro-only AI features.
- **All device-native connections are in both tiers equivalently.** No cost reason to gate.
- **Infrastructure connections don't participate in tier gating.** They are present because the app works.

### 8.16.2 Tier math (verification)

For a Core user running at the typical case:
- 1-2 email accounts + 1-2 calendar accounts (likely personal + work each)
- 1-3 bank accounts via Plaid
- Apple Health or Google Health Connect on their device
- Screen time, location, weather on their device
- 10 AI queries/day

This covers the vast majority of users. The 2-total limit on email/calendar is generous enough to serve the personal + work pattern without forcing Pro.

For a Pro user:
- 3+ email accounts (personal, work, side business, etc.)
- 3+ calendars (personal, work, partner, shared family)
- 100 AI queries/day
- Daily AI brief, email drafts, cross-category correlations, Pantry Scanner, Premium Scan, long-term history

Pro's differentiation is meaningful but not punitive to Core.

---

## 8.17 Privacy Posture Per Connection

Each connection has a specific privacy posture governing what data reaches where.

### 8.17.1 Sensitivity tiers (from Section 6.3)

- **Very High:** financial transactions, email content, location coordinates, screen time per-app data, AI conversation logs
- **High:** calendar events, body weight history, workout details, sleep data, HRV, email metadata (sender/subject)
- **Medium:** step counts, active minutes, heart rate ranges, place names (labeled)
- **Low:** weather, motion category, device platform

### 8.17.2 Per-connection sensitivity and handling

| Connection | Sensitivity tier | Stored server-side | Sent to AI (with consent) | Special handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple HealthKit | High (weight, workouts, sleep, HRV); Medium (steps, HR ranges) | Yes | Per-category consent per 6.4 | None beyond baseline |
| Apple EventKit | High | Event metadata (title, times, attendee count); bodies stay on-device | Title redacted to category per 6.6.3 | Bodies never leave device |
| Apple Family Controls | Very High | Aggregated daily/hourly rollups + per-category app breakdowns | Category-level yes; per-app names default-OFF | Per-app sub-toggle for AI |
| CoreLocation | Very High | Visit events with coordinates | Coordinates never sent to AI; labeled place names only | Place naming via Google Places |
| Core Motion | Low | Yes | Yes | None |
| Apple WeatherKit | Low | Not stored (client-side cache only) | If `weather` consent ON | Client-side only |
| Google Health Connect | Matches HealthKit per data type | Yes | Per-category consent | Matches HealthKit |
| Google Fit | Matches HealthKit | Yes | Per-category consent | OAuth token encrypted per 7.3.2 |
| Android UsageStatsManager | Very High | Aggregated rollups | Category-level yes; per-app names default-OFF | Same as Family Controls |
| Android Geofencing | Very High | Visit events | Labeled place names only | Same as CoreLocation |
| Android Calendar Provider | High | Event metadata | Title redacted to category | Matches EventKit |
| OpenWeatherMap | Low | Not stored | If `weather` consent ON | Coarse location only sent out |
| Google Calendar | High | Event metadata + category | Title redacted to category per 6.6.3 | Bodies never retrieved |
| Gmail | Very High | Sender, subject, first 200 chars; full body never stored | Subject + sender + 200 chars if consent ON | Hard cap on body length; KMS-encrypted tokens |
| Outlook Mail | Very High | Same as Gmail | Same as Gmail | Same as Gmail |
| Outlook Calendar | High | Same as Google Calendar | Same as Google Calendar | Same as Google Calendar |
| Strava | High | Full activity data | Hard-excluded from AI context per 6.6.3 regardless of consent | Compliance exclusion |
| Plaid | Very High | Transactions, account metadata, cached balance | Redacted to MCC categories + amount ranges per 6.6.3 | Field-level KMS encryption; raw data never to AI |
| Google Places | Low (aggregated) | Cached place names per coordinate | Place names in labeled form; coordinates never | Coordinates quantized before API call |
| Open Food Facts | Low | Cached product data (global, not user-specific) | N/A (no user-specific data flows) | Public open dataset |
| Anthropic | Inherits query context | Query + response in `chatbot_audit` (tier-specific retention) | N/A (is the AI) | Prompts versioned and auditable |
| Clerk | Auth data only | Via Clerk (not in APEX Postgres) | N/A | Processor per 6.11 |
| RevenueCat | Subscription metadata | Via RevenueCat | N/A | Processor per 6.11 |
| Expo Notifications | Push tokens | Yes | Never sent to AI | Low sensitivity token |
| SendGrid | Email metadata for delivery | Delivery status logs | Never | Processor |

### 8.17.3 Cross-cutting redaction policies

Per 6.6.3, the AI gateway applies redaction before any request to Anthropic:
- Financial: amount ranges + MCC categories; raw amounts only when user's query explicitly asks
- Email: subject verbatim (already visible to user); sender verbatim; first 200 chars verbatim
- Calendar: title → category; notes / bodies never
- Location: coordinates → labeled names; unlabeled visits as "unlabeled location"
- Screen time: category-level yes; per-app names require separate opt-in
- Health: values as-is with per-category consent
- Strava: hard-excluded regardless of consent

### 8.17.4 Consent sub-structure (6.4 recap)

Each connection has two consent gates:
1. **Connect consent:** does APEX have permission to sync from this source at all? Default OFF at signup; user grants by tapping Connect.
2. **AI consent:** can APEX send this source's data to AI features? Default OFF; granular per-source. The Strava source_key is a compliance-locked-OFF exception per 8.6.5 and 6.4.2.

Both can be toggled off independently. Connect OFF → no sync, no data collected; AI OFF → data syncs and is used for non-AI features (display, deterministic scoring) but not sent to Anthropic.

---

## 8.18 Connection Approval & Evaluation Roadmap

Living document of external approval dependencies and their timelines.

### 8.18.1 Pre-launch approval tracker

| Approval | Status at PRD time | Target resolution | Contingency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apple Family Controls entitlement | Not applied | Week 4 | iOS Screen Time deferred to v1.1 |
| Plaid Production | Not applied | Week 8 | Finance category deferred to v2 |
| Google OAuth verification (Gmail) | Not applied | Week 12 | Gmail deferred to v1.1; Outlook as primary email |
| Microsoft Graph verification (Outlook Mail) | Not applied | Week 10 | Outlook Mail deferred to v1.1; Gmail as primary |
| Strava developer clarification (AI use) | Not submitted | v1.1 | Current read-only-with-AI-exclusion posture maintained |

### 8.18.2 Post-launch evaluation queue

Connections to evaluate for v1.1, v2, or later based on user demand and business signals.

**v1.1 candidates:**
- Strava AI context lift (if Strava clarifies favorably)
- Plaid Liabilities (enables Net Worth with student loans and mortgages)
- Garmin Connect direct API (if Garmin approves developer access)
- Apple Wallet passes (boarding passes in Day Timeline, iOS-only)
- Uber ride history (trips in Day Timeline + finance categorization)

**v2 candidates:**
- Plaid Investments (completes Net Worth)
- Coinbase (crypto holdings)
- Receipt OCR (line-item detail supplementing Plaid transactions)
- HomeKit environment data (bedroom temperature for sleep correlation)
- Additional regional banking connectors (MX, Finicity) for institutions Plaid doesn't cover well

**v2+ / indefinite:**
- Spotify listening history
- GitHub / Slack / Notion / Linear work-productivity connections
- Google Docs activity
- Additional wearable-direct APIs (Oura, Whoop, Polar) if their HealthKit/Health Connect sync proves insufficient

**Never (policy):**
- Social media activity scraping
- SMS/message content reading
- Any connection requiring accessibility permissions (invasive)
- Any connection requiring default-SMS-app status on Android

### 8.18.3 Connection addition process (post-launch)

When a new connection is added in a future release:
1. Evaluation against the three cost tiers in 8.2
2. Specific cost estimate and tier placement decision
3. Privacy posture documented per 8.17 template
4. Consent model updated (new source_key in 6.4.2)
5. Fallback behavior specified per 8.14 template
6. Sync cadence and webhook pattern specified per 8.12 and 8.13 templates
7. Tier placement table updated (8.16)
8. Section 5.11 processor inventory updated
9. Section 6.11 updated if the new connection introduces a new sub-processor

This catalog structure makes new-connection additions systematic rather than ad-hoc.

### 8.18.4 Connection removal process

If a connection is sunset (e.g., Google Fit retirement in future Android releases):
1. User-facing communication 90 days before sunset
2. In-app migration prompt to alternative connection (Health Connect in the Google Fit case)
3. Data from the retiring connection preserved (user granted us the data; retirement doesn't auto-delete)
4. Section 8 catalog updated with `v1.X — deprecated` notation
5. Section 5.11 and 6.11 updated

---

# Section 9 — Scoring Engine

Section 9 is the deterministic math that powers every score in Life Dashboard. It is the authoritative specification for the Overall Score, the four category scores (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time), their constituent signals, baselines, safe-range bands, proration curves, graceful degradation mechanics, recomputation cadence, and debug surfacing.

Section 9 contains no AI. Every calculation specified here is a pure function of user data and time. The AI layer exists in Section 10 and runs alongside the scoring engine, never inside it.

This section is dense by necessity. It is the engine that makes the product's core promise — fast, free, reproducible, explainable scores — into something an engineer can build against. Every paragraph is specification, not narrative.

## 9.1 Overview & Purpose

The Scoring Engine answers five questions for every user every time the app loads:

1. How is each of the four categories doing today?
2. How is the user's life doing overall?
3. Why are those numbers what they are?
4. Is today's data reliable enough to score, or should we show "—"?
5. Has the user's pattern shifted in a way that should update their baselines?

The engine is invoked from two surfaces:

- **API tier (synchronous):** when the user opens the Home Dashboard, any category detail view, Day Detail, or asks a chatbot query that references a score. Scoring must return in under 50ms for cached results, under 500ms for fresh computation.
- **Worker tier (asynchronous):** nightly at 03:30 UTC, the engine snapshots every user's prior-day scores to an immutable `daily_scores` table and recomputes 30-day personal baselines. This job is the source of truth for historical scores and for the `Progress` tab trend charts in every category.

The engine lives in the shared `domain/` layer per §5.3 and is callable from both contexts without modification. It has zero external dependencies (no AI, no third-party APIs, no network calls) — only the user's own data.

## 9.2 Design Principles

Seven principles govern every decision in this section. When ambiguity arises during implementation, these decide.

**1. Deterministic only.** Same inputs → same output, always. No AI anywhere in the scoring path. No randomness. No time-of-computation variance beyond explicit time-dependent inputs (e.g., "hour of day" as a prorated signal input).

**2. Penalty-from-perfect-day.** Every category starts at 100 (a perfect day) and penalties subtract from it. The user sees the result as positive progress ("87 of 100"). Internal math treats it as penalty subtraction because this composes cleanly with missing signals — weight redistribution happens automatically when penalty calculations are skipped for unavailable signals.

**3. Personal-baseline-first.** After a 14-day warmup, every baselineable signal uses the user's own 30-day rolling median rather than a population default. Users are not ranked against a population; they are compared to themselves.

**4. Graceful under sparse data.** A user with two connections scores meaningfully on what they have; a user with nothing scores "—" with a CTA. The engine never fabricates data, never extrapolates to fill gaps, never dings a user for missing a connection they never granted.

**5. Un-gameable where it matters.** The Time score (per §4.6.15) is specifically designed so that gaming = winning. Other category scores have some user-input signals (logged meals, logged workouts) that are technically gameable, but the scoring math doesn't reward volume — it rewards alignment with goal targets, which is the point.

**6. Explainable at every level.** For every score, the engine returns not just a number but a structured `raw_deltas` breakdown: which signals contributed, what their raw values were, what targets they were measured against, what deviation they had, and how much they contributed to the final score. This blob is the foundation of Settings → Advanced debug view and the chatbot's ability to answer "why is my score X?"

**7. Backward-stable historical scores.** A score computed for yesterday doesn't change tomorrow unless yesterday's data changes. Snapshotted scores in `daily_scores` are immutable after 7 days for Core users and 30 days for Pro users. This prevents the "my trend chart moves every time I open the app" problem.

## 9.3 Score Hierarchy

Scores in Life Dashboard are computed in four tiers, each building on the tier below.

**Tier 1 — Signal scores.** A signal is the smallest scoreable unit: calorie adherence today, sleep regularity over 14 days, budget adherence this week. Each signal produces a normalized score in [0, 1]. There are approximately 25 signals across the four categories.

**Tier 2 — Subsystem scores** (Fitness only). Fitness has seven subsystems (Body, Strength, Cardio, Movement, Sleep, Recovery, Plan). A subsystem score is a weighted combination of the signals within that subsystem, output as a 0–100 number for surfacing in the detail view. Other categories have signals but not subsystems.

**Tier 3 — Category scores.** Each of Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, and Time produces a single 0–100 score. Fitness = weighted sum of subsystem scores. Nutrition / Finance / Time = weighted sum of signals directly.

**Tier 4 — Overall Score.** The single 0–100 number shown on the Home Dashboard. Overall = Σ(category_score × category_weight / 100), where weights default to 25 each and are user-adjustable in [5, 55] summing to 100.

The engine computes bottom-up: signals first, then subsystems (Fitness only), then category, then Overall. A cache invalidation at any tier triggers recomputation of everything upstream.

## 9.4 The Universal Score Formula

Every category score uses the same formula shape, parameterized by its signal set and weights.

```
# Pseudocode, category_score function
def compute_category_score(user_id, date, category):
    signals = get_active_signals(user_id, category, date)

    # Filter signals that have data; excluded signals' weights redistribute automatically
    scoreable = [s for s in signals if has_sufficient_data(s, user_id, date)]

    if count(scoreable) < MIN_SIGNALS_PER_CATEGORY:  # currently 2
        return { score: None, reason: "insufficient_data", raw_deltas: [] }

    total_weight = sum(s.weight for s in scoreable)
    weighted_score_sum = 0
    raw_deltas = []

    for s in scoreable:
        signal_score_01 = compute_signal_score(s, user_id, date)  # returns value in [0, 1]
        penalty = (1 - signal_score_01) * s.weight
        weighted_score_sum += s.weight - penalty
        raw_deltas.append({
            signal_id: s.id,
            raw_value: get_raw_value(s, user_id, date),
            target: get_target(s, user_id, date),
            deviation: get_deviation(s, user_id, date),
            normalized_score: signal_score_01,
            weight: s.weight,
            redistributed_weight: s.weight / total_weight * 100,
            contribution: (weighted_score_sum / total_weight) * 100,
        })

    category_score = round((weighted_score_sum / total_weight) * 100)
    category_score = clamp(category_score, 0, 100)

    return { score: category_score, reason: "ok", raw_deltas: raw_deltas }
```

Key properties:

- **Normalized weights:** the division by `total_weight` (rather than by the sum of all defined weights) is how graceful degradation works. Missing signals are simply not in the sum — their weight "vanishes" and the remaining signals' relative influence increases proportionally.
- **Integer output, 2-decimal internal precision:** the final `round()` is for display. Internally, the 2-decimal-precision `weighted_score_sum / total_weight` is stored so that delta indicators (▲2, ▼1) can distinguish true changes from rounding flips.
- **Clamped:** a score cannot go below 0 or above 100. In practice, penalties sum to exactly 100 at worst (every signal scores 0), so clamping is a safety rail not a regular path.
- **`raw_deltas` is always returned** alongside the score, regardless of whether it's consumed. It is the basis for the debug view and the chatbot's "why" answers.

## 9.5 Signal Normalization

Every signal translates a raw measurement into a [0, 1] score via a **piecewise-linear-with-safe-range** curve. The choice of piecewise-linear is deliberate: it is fully auditable, explainable in the debug view (the user can see exactly what slope applied at what value), and has no hidden nonlinearities that would surprise a user who asks "why did my score drop."

### 9.5.1 The Safe Range Concept (v1.18 locked)

Every signal has two critical parameters:

- **Target** — the ideal value (e.g., calorie target, protein target, personal sleep baseline, zero bills overdue)
- **Safe range** — a tolerance band around the target within which the signal scores a full 1.0

The safe range is signal-specific and set to match real-world tolerance: you do not need to hit your calorie target within 1 kcal to score a perfect day. Safe ranges are generous by design — the engine rewards alignment, not perfection.

### 9.5.2 Piecewise-Linear Normalization — The Base Pattern

For a signal with target `T`, safe range `±R` around the target, and a maximum tolerable deviation `D_max` beyond which the signal scores 0:

```
deviation = abs(raw_value - target)

if deviation <= R:
    signal_score = 1.0  # within safe range, full points
elif deviation < D_max:
    signal_score = 1.0 - (deviation - R) / (D_max - R)  # linear decay
else:
    signal_score = 0.0  # beyond tolerable deviation, zero points
```

**Worked example — calorie adherence, no proration:**
- Target: 2,140 kcal
- Safe range: ±100 kcal
- D_max: 500 kcal (i.e., ±500 kcal beyond target scores zero)
- User consumed 2,310 kcal today: deviation = 170, which is past the safe range but within D_max. Signal score = 1 − (170 − 100) / (500 − 100) = 1 − 70/400 = 0.825.
- User consumed 2,050 kcal: deviation = 90, within safe range. Signal score = 1.0.
- User consumed 2,700 kcal: deviation = 560, past D_max. Signal score = 0.0.

### 9.5.3 Asymmetric Safe Ranges

Some signals care about direction. Protein has a floor but no ceiling (going over target is fine or good); sodium has a ceiling but a generous floor (going under is fine or good); budget adherence has a ceiling (going over is bad) but no floor (coming in under is fine or excellent).
