For asymmetric signals, the curve uses separate `R_low` and `R_high`, with separate `D_max_low` and `D_max_high`. When the raw value is on the "good" side of the target, the signal scores 1.0 regardless of magnitude.

**Worked example — protein (floor, no ceiling):**
- Target: 150g
- Safe range low: −10g (141g is still within safe range)
- D_max low: 60g below target (90g is zero score)
- D_max high: infinity (going over target always scores 1.0)

User consumed 145g: signal score = 1.0 (within safe range low). User consumed 250g: signal score = 1.0 (over target is fine for protein). User consumed 105g: deviation = 45, past safe range. Signal score = 1 − (45 − 10) / (60 − 10) = 1 − 35/50 = 0.3.

### 9.5.4 Ceiling Signals

Budget adherence, sodium, rollover surplus — these have a target at the ceiling. Going under the target is the good side; going over is what drives penalty.

**Worked example — weekly budget adherence:**
- Weekly budget target: $500 (ceiling)
- Safe range high: +$25 (up to $525 scores full)
- D_max high: +$250 ($750 scores zero)
- Any spend below $500: signal score = 1.0 (under budget is strictly fine)

User spent $540 this week: deviation above ceiling = 40, past safe range. Signal score = 1 − (40 − 25) / (250 − 25) = 1 − 15/225 = 0.933.

### 9.5.5 Event Signals — Binary or Graded

Some signals aren't continuous measurements; they're discrete events. These use a graded mapping rather than a piecewise curve.

- **Bill on-time rate** (this-week signal): raw value is `on_time_count / total_bills_due_this_week`. Signal score = raw value directly (already in [0, 1]). Special case: if zero bills were due this week, the signal is excluded from the computation (its weight redistributes).
- **Workout happened** (daily, event-gated): 1 if at least one workout was logged today; 0 otherwise. Does not use a safe range. Does not count as a "signal with data" on rest days (user's plan specifies rest days; rest days are excluded from the denominator).
- **Weight logged today** (daily, event-gated): used as a data-availability input (feeds the minimum data threshold), not as a scoring contribution on its own.

### 9.5.6 Baseline-Referenced Signals

Many Time signals and some Fitness signals score against the user's personal baseline rather than a fixed target.

For a baseline-referenced signal:
- After 14-day warmup: `target = 30-day rolling median of raw_value for this signal`
- During warmup (days 0–14): `target = population default` (see §9.6.3)

The same piecewise-linear formula applies; only the source of `target` changes.

**Worked example — attention fragmentation (pickup count, baseline-referenced, ceiling signal):**
- Personal baseline (user's 30-day rolling median): 34 pickups/day
- Safe range high: +15% (up to 39 pickups scores full)
- D_max high: +50% (51 pickups scores zero)
- User had 60 pickups today: deviation above baseline = 26, past D_max. Signal score = 0.0. The raw_deltas entry will note "baseline = 34 (personal), raw = 60, deviation +76%."

## 9.6 Baselines & Personalization

Baselines are the mechanism that makes the product personal. A user who runs naturally on 4.5 hours of sleep doesn't get dinged against a population "8 hours is healthy" default; a user whose weekday norm is 3 meetings doesn't get flagged for a 3-meeting Tuesday.

### 9.6.1 What Gets Baselined

Not every signal benefits from personalization. Some signals have target values that are universal (calorie target, protein target — these are per-user but derived from the user's own profile, not from a rolling median). Others (sleep timing, attention fragmentation, meeting density) are intrinsically personal and benefit enormously from baselines.

**Signals that use personal baselines (v1.18 locked):**

| Category | Signal | Baseline statistic | Window |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fitness | Strength — weekly volume | 30-day rolling median weekly volume | 30 days |
| Fitness | Cardio — weekly volume | 30-day rolling median weekly minutes | 30 days |
| Fitness | Movement — daily steps | 30-day rolling median daily steps | 30 days |
| Fitness | Sleep — duration (Fitness subsystem) | 30-day rolling median nightly duration | 30 days |
| Fitness | Recovery — HRV | 14-day EMA (HRV is high-noise; EMA smooths) | 14 days |
| Time | Sleep Regularity — bedtime SD | 14-day rolling bedtime standard deviation | 14 days |
| Time | Sleep Regularity — wake SD | 14-day rolling wake standard deviation | 14 days |
| Time | Attention Fragmentation — pickups | 30-day rolling median daily pickups | 30 days |
| Time | Attention Fragmentation — session length | 30-day rolling median mean session length | 30 days |
| Time | Attention Fragmentation — longest focus block | 30-day rolling median longest off-screen block | 30 days |
| Time | Location Intentionality — place pattern | 30-day rolling distribution of time-at-place | 30 days |
| Time | Schedule Density Balance — meeting hours | 30-day rolling median weekday meeting hours | 30 days |
| Time | Rhythm Adherence — wake time | 30-day rolling median wake time | 30 days |
| Time | Rhythm Adherence — first meal time | 30-day rolling median first-meal time | 30 days |
| Time | Rhythm Adherence — workday start | 30-day rolling median inferred workday-start time | 30 days |
| Time | Rhythm Adherence — workday end | 30-day rolling median inferred workday-end time | 30 days |

**Sleep signal clarification (v1.27):** Fitness Sleep signal measures sleep *quantity and quality* (duration, efficiency, deep/REM proportions, HRV overnight) using a 30-day rolling median. Time Sleep Regularity signal measures sleep *timing consistency* (variance in bedtime and wake time) using a 14-day rolling standard deviation. The same HealthKit source feeds both, but the signals are non-overlapping and both count toward their respective category scores without double-counting — a user with stable 7-hour nights at 11pm/6am scores well on both; a user with 7-hour nights at wildly variable times scores well on Fitness Sleep but poorly on Time Sleep Regularity; a user with erratic 4-hour nights at consistent times scores poorly on Fitness Sleep but neutrally on Time Sleep Regularity.

**Signals that do not use personal baselines (fixed or profile-derived targets):**

| Category | Signal | Target source |
|---|---|---|
| Nutrition | Calorie adherence | User's goal-adjusted calorie target from §4.1.5 |
| Nutrition | Protein adherence | User's goal-derived protein target from §4.1.5 |
| Nutrition | Macro distribution | User's goal-derived fat/carb targets |
| Nutrition | Sugar / Fiber / Sodium | User-adjustable, FDA defaults per §4.4.9 |
| Nutrition | Hydration (if active) | User-set water goal |
| Nutrition | Logging consistency | Binary per meal-window (did user log at least one meal in each typical meal window?) |
| Finance | Budget adherence | User's weekly/monthly budget from §4.5 |
| Finance | Bill on-time rate | Fixed 100% target (all bills paid on time) |
| Finance | Savings rate | User's savings goal rate |
| Finance | Spending consistency | 7-day rolling standard deviation — low is good |
| Finance | Emergency fund ratio | User's emergency fund goal target |
| Fitness | Body — weight trend | User's weight goal trajectory |
| Fitness | Plan adherence | User's training plan (1.0 if scheduled workout completed, 0.0 if missed; rest days excluded) |

### 9.6.2 Baseline Computation

Baselines recompute nightly as part of the 03:30 UTC score-snapshot job. For each baselineable signal:

```
baseline = median(
    raw_values[signal, user, day]
    for day in range(today - window_days, today)
    if has_data(signal, user, day)
)
```

Median is chosen over mean because it is robust to outliers — a week of travel, one terrible night of sleep, or a single wild day of meetings will not warp the baseline.

**Minimum sample size:** for the baseline to be used, at least `ceil(window_days / 3)` valid data points must exist in the window (10 days for a 30-day window, 5 days for a 14-day window). If fewer data points exist, the signal falls back to its population default or is excluded entirely (depending on the signal — see §9.6.3).

**No recency weighting.** Flat median across the full window. This is a deliberate simplicity choice: exponential recency weighting would make baselines drift unpredictably when users have brief pattern disruptions (a sick week, a vacation). If the user's true pattern genuinely changes (e.g., starts a new training block, new job with different schedule), the baseline catches up in one window length on its own.

### 9.6.3 14-Day Warmup & Population Defaults

During the first 14 days of a user's history, baselineable signals use population defaults rather than personal baselines. Population defaults are conservative midpoints:

| Signal | Population default | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Sleep duration | 7.5 hours | CDC adult recommendation midpoint |
| Sleep start SD | 30 min | Clinical "regular sleep" threshold (Lunsford-Avery 2018) |
| Wake SD | 30 min | Same |
| Daily pickups | 60 | User research median for smartphone adults |
| Mean session length | 3 min | User research median |
| Longest focus block | 90 min | User research median for productive adults |
| Location place pattern | — (no default; signal excluded during warmup) | Intrinsically personal |
| Weekday meeting hours | 3 hours | Knowledge-worker midpoint |
| Wake time | 7:00 AM | Weekday midpoint |
| First meal time | 8:30 AM | Weekday midpoint |
| Workday start | 9:00 AM | Knowledge-worker weekday |
| Workday end | 6:00 PM | Knowledge-worker weekday |
| HRV (Fitness) | Personalized only; signal excluded during warmup | Intrinsically personal |
| Daily steps | 6,000 | U.S. adult median |
| Strength weekly volume | Excluded during warmup | Too variable by goal |
| Cardio weekly volume | Excluded during warmup | Too variable by goal |

**Location Intentionality** and **HRV** both default to "excluded during warmup" rather than a population midpoint — for these, no meaningful universal default exists, and a fabricated one would produce worse scoring than simply redistributing weight to other signals during warmup.

**Transition from warmup to personalized:** On the 15th day (local time), the nightly baseline job switches the signal from population default to personal baseline. The user sees no prompt; the "Calibrating" pill on the category card (per §4.2.4) removes itself automatically. If the user had <10 data points in the 30-day window (i.e., the signal was barely captured during warmup), the signal continues to use population default until the threshold is met.

### 9.6.4 Per-Signal Baseline Scope

Baselines are computed per-signal, not per-subsystem or per-category. This means, for example:

- A user's bedtime baseline is separate from their wake-time baseline
- A user's strength weekly volume baseline is separate from their cardio weekly volume baseline
- A user who sleeps well on weekends but erratically on weekdays has one combined Sleep Regularity baseline; weekday/weekend separation is not supported at v1 (deferred to v1.1+ as a potential refinement)

### 9.6.5 Baseline Update Cadence

Nightly at 03:30 UTC as part of the score-snapshot job. No per-request recomputation — baselines do not change during the day, only across midnight boundaries. This is both a performance choice (median over 30 days is not cheap to recompute hot) and a stability choice (a user's baseline should not shift mid-day based on a single new data point).

## 9.7 Graceful Degradation & Weight Redistribution

Life Dashboard's scoring must work for everyone, from a user who has nothing connected to a user with HealthKit + Strava + Garmin + Plaid + Gmail + Outlook + Calendar. Missing signals must not drag scores down.

### 9.7.1 The Redistribution Mechanic

Per the Universal Score Formula (§9.4), graceful degradation is automatic: missing signals are simply excluded from the `scoreable` set, the sum of weights used in the divisor is the sum of *active* weights only, and the remaining signals' relative influence increases proportionally.

**Worked example — Fitness score with only Movement and Sleep connected:**

Fitness default weights: Movement 20, Strength 20, Cardio 15, Sleep 15, Recovery 10, Body 10, Plan 10 (total 100).

A user with only HealthKit connected has Movement (steps) and Sleep data. Strength, Cardio, Recovery, Body, and Plan have no data.

- Scoreable signals: Movement (weight 20), Sleep (weight 15). Total active weight: 35.
- If Movement signal scores 0.8 and Sleep signal scores 0.9:
  - Movement contribution: 0.8 × 20 = 16
  - Sleep contribution: 0.9 × 15 = 13.5
  - Weighted sum: 29.5
  - Normalized: 29.5 / 35 = 0.843
  - Fitness score: 84

The user sees a meaningful Fitness score of 84 despite only having 2 of 7 subsystems with data. The `raw_deltas` shows which signals contributed and that 5 signals were absent.

### 9.7.2 Signal-Level Data Availability

A signal is "available" for the day's score if it has sufficient data per its own rules. The rules are signal-specific:

| Signal family | Data availability rule |
|---|---|
| Today-accumulating (calories, steps, screen time) | ≥1 data point logged today |
| Trailing-window (strength volume, sleep avg, budget adherence) | ≥1 data point in the trailing window (7d, 14d, or 30d as specified) |
| Event-gated (workouts, bills, weight) | At least 1 scheduled event on the day (e.g., a rest day has 0 workouts scheduled and the signal is excluded) |
| Baseline-referenced (Time signals) | Window has ≥`ceil(window_days/3)` valid days; else signal excluded |

### 9.7.3 Minimum Category Threshold

Per v1.18 locked policy, a category score computes only when at least **2 signals in that category** have sufficient data. Otherwise the category returns `score = None` and the detail view shows "—" with a CTA.

**Worked example — first-time user on Day 1:**

User completes onboarding with HealthKit granted. They log a meal, log their weight, and have their calendar connected.

- Fitness signals with data: Body (weight logged) and Movement (HealthKit steps). 2 signals → **Fitness scores.**
- Nutrition signals with data: Calorie adherence (1 meal logged) and Logging consistency (logged at least one meal). 2 signals → **Nutrition scores.**
- Finance signals with data: none (no Plaid). 0 signals → **Finance shows "—" with CTA "Connect bank to activate Finance."**
- Time signals with data: Schedule Density Balance (from calendar). 1 signal → **Time shows "—" with CTA "Connect at least 2 of: sleep, screen time, location."**
- Overall Score: 2 categories (Fitness + Nutrition) have scores. Threshold is ≥2, so Overall computes. The Overall label reads "Overall · Fitness + Nutrition."

### 9.7.4 Minimum Overall Threshold

Overall Score shows a number only when ≥2 categories have computed scores. Otherwise "—" is displayed with a CTA. The label beneath the Overall number reflects which categories are contributing (e.g., "Fitness + Nutrition" when only those two score).

### 9.7.5 Weight Redistribution vs. Score Compression

A design choice: when weights redistribute, the remaining signals carry more relative influence. A user with only 2 of 7 Fitness signals active has those 2 signals effectively scoring out of 35 rather than 100. The math divides by 35 to normalize back to a 0–100 output.

This is the "correct" behavior for the product's philosophy (score what we have, don't punish missing connections) but has a subtle consequence worth naming: a user with sparse data has a less-diversified signal base, so their score is more volatile. A single bad sleep night moves a sparse-data user's Fitness score more than it moves a full-stack user's. This is acceptable — the user can see from the `raw_deltas` that redistribution is happening, and the "connect more" CTA nudges toward a more stable scoring base over time.

## 9.8 Minimum Data Thresholds

Summarized from §9.7 for quick reference, v1.18 locked:

| Tier | Threshold | Below threshold display |
|---|---|---|
| Signal | ≥1 valid data point per signal-specific rule | Signal excluded from computation |
| Subsystem (Fitness only) | ≥1 contained signal has data | Subsystem score = `—`, card shows empty state |
| Category | ≥2 signals with data | Category shows `—` with CTA |
| Overall | ≥2 categories with scores | Overall shows `—` with CTA |

These thresholds are tuned for "app usable immediately" per v1.18 locked policy: a Day-1 user who logs one meal and has HealthKit gets a Nutrition score and a Fitness score, which is enough for an Overall Score. The CTAs on "—" states are the upgrade path — connect more sources to unlock more categories.

## 9.9 Tiered Proration

Per v1.18 locked decision, proration applies only to specific signal types. Flask's single-formula proration across all signals (`day_progress = clamp((hour-6)/15, 0.33, 1.0)`) was insufficient because it produces weird scores for signals that don't accumulate hourly (events, trailing windows). Section 9 uses a three-tier approach.

### 9.9.1 Tier 1 — Accumulating Signals (prorated per tuned curve)

An accumulating signal has a daily target that fills up as the day progresses. At 9am you haven't eaten your full day's calories yet; the score shouldn't penalize you for being "behind target" at 9am.

**Accumulating signals (v1.18):**
- Calorie adherence (Nutrition)
- Protein adherence (Nutrition)
- Macro distribution (Nutrition)
- Sugar / Fiber / Sodium (Nutrition, when user has targets set)
- Hydration (Nutrition, when active)
- Daily steps (Fitness — Movement subsystem)
- Daily screen time (Time — Attention Fragmentation)

For each accumulating signal, the engine computes a `prorated_target` based on a per-signal tuned curve, and deviation is measured against the prorated target rather than the full daily target.

### 9.9.2 Per-Signal Proration Curves

**Calorie and macro signals — Front-loaded sigmoid:**

Users don't eat their calorie target linearly through the day; they eat in chunks around meals. A linear proration (12% of target by 9am, 50% by noon, etc.) dings users who haven't had lunch yet. The curve should match realistic meal distributions.

```
# Anchored to typical meal times: breakfast ~8am, lunch ~12:30pm, dinner ~7pm
# Curve is a sigmoid that rises slowly before 8am, accelerates through midday, plateaus after 8pm
day_progress_calories(hour) =
    0.05  if hour < 7
    0.15  if hour < 9   # breakfast completed
    0.35  if hour < 12  # mid-morning
    0.55  if hour < 14  # lunch completed
    0.75  if hour < 18  # pre-dinner
    0.95  if hour < 21  # dinner completed
    1.00  if hour >= 21
```

prorated_target = daily_target × day_progress_calories(hour)

Example: at 10am, a user with a 2,140 kcal target has a prorated target of 2,140 × 0.35 = 749 kcal. If they've consumed 700 kcal, deviation is 49 kcal — within the ±100 safe range, score 1.0.

**Steps signal — Linear with late-day flattening:**

Steps accumulate more linearly through the day for most users. However, after 9pm, the curve flattens — most users don't do significant walking after dinner.

```
day_progress_steps(hour) =
    0.02  if hour < 6
    linear(0.02 → 0.85, from hour 6 to hour 21)
    0.95  if hour < 23
    1.00  if hour >= 23
```

**Screen time signal — Inverted (user's screen time is a ceiling to not exceed):**

Because screen time is a ceiling, proration for it means "how much screen time should we expect by this hour?" A user whose baseline is 4 hours of screen time per day will typically accumulate that across waking hours. The curve is the accumulation curve for a typical phone user's day.

```
day_progress_screen_time(hour) =
    0.03  if hour < 7
    linear(0.03 → 0.92, from hour 7 to hour 22)
    1.00  if hour >= 22
```

User at 3pm with baseline 4 hours: prorated_target = 4 × day_progress_screen_time(15) ≈ 4 × 0.54 = 2.16 hours. Actual screen time if 1.9 hours: deviation from prorated target = 0.26 hours under, which for this ceiling signal is on the "good" side → signal score 1.0.

### 9.9.3 Tier 2 — Event Signals (no proration)

Event signals score instantly on occurrence. They do not accumulate through the day.

**Event signals (v1.18):**
- Workout completed today (Fitness — Plan adherence)
- Bill paid on/before due date (Finance — Bill on-time rate)
- Weight logged today (Fitness — Body, event-gated signal feeding data availability)
- Meal logged within each typical meal window (Nutrition — Logging consistency)

For a Bill signal: if a bill was due yesterday, it either was paid on time or it wasn't. No proration. The signal score is deterministic at the moment of payment.

For a Workout signal: on a plan day where the user has a scheduled workout, the signal is binary — 1.0 if completed, 0.0 if missed (and the day is past). On a current day where the workout hasn't yet happened, the signal is excluded from scoring for today (it will populate tomorrow). On rest days, the signal is excluded entirely (the denominator doesn't include rest days).

### 9.9.4 Tier 3 — Trailing-Window Signals (no proration, rolling window)

Trailing-window signals look back over a fixed period and measure average or cumulative state. They use their natural window directly.

**Trailing-window signals (v1.18):**
- Strength weekly volume (Fitness — Strength subsystem; 7-day trailing)
- Cardio weekly volume (Fitness — Cardio subsystem; 7-day trailing)
- Savings rate (Finance; 30-day trailing)
- Spending consistency (Finance; 7-day trailing)
- Budget adherence, weekly (Finance; current week)
- Emergency fund ratio (Finance; current balance)
- Sleep duration avg (Fitness — Sleep subsystem; 7-day trailing)
- HRV avg (Fitness — Recovery; 14-day trailing)
- Sleep Regularity (Time; 14-day trailing)
- Attention Fragmentation (Time; today's data vs 30-day baseline)
- Location Intentionality (Time; today's pattern vs 30-day baseline)
- Schedule Density Balance (Time; today's meetings vs 30-day baseline)
- Rhythm Adherence (Time; today's anchor times vs 30-day baseline)

Time's signals use today's data against a trailing-window-baselined target. Proration doesn't apply because you're comparing a full day's data (or today's partial-day data for the current day's score) against a baseline derived from full-day history.

**Current-day Time signals:** On the current day before sufficient data exists for some signals (e.g., you haven't slept yet for tonight's Sleep Regularity, or you haven't fully accumulated today's Attention Fragmentation), the signal behaves as follows:

- **Sleep Regularity** uses the prior 14 nights (ending last night). Today's sleep, when it happens, affects tomorrow's Sleep Regularity signal. Score is always a trailing measurement, not a prediction.
- **Attention Fragmentation** is a today-accumulating signal at the pickup-count level but compared to baseline at each point — so effectively it uses a partial-day scaling: today's data so far vs baseline's data-so-far at this hour. The comparison is proportional: if the user has had 18 pickups by noon and their baseline median for pickups-by-noon is 22, deviation = −18% (better than baseline), signal score = 1.0.
- **Location Intentionality** and **Schedule Density Balance** use today's full-day data as it accumulates. Both are day-level measurements that update as events occur.
- **Rhythm Adherence** computes per-anchor-event as each anchor occurs. If the user has woken up and had their first meal, those two anchors score; if the workday hasn't started yet, that anchor is deferred.

## 9.10 Category Compositions

Full subsystem and signal weight tables for every category. Weights sum to 100 per category and redistribute when signals are excluded.

### 9.10.1 Fitness Category

Fitness is the only category with a subsystem layer. Subsystem scores compute first, then combine into the Fitness score.

**Fitness subsystem weights (v1.18):**

| Subsystem | Default weight | Redistributed when excluded |
|---|---|---|
| Movement | 20 | Yes |
| Strength | 20 | Yes |
| Cardio | 15 | Yes |
| Sleep | 15 | Yes |
| Recovery | 10 | Yes |
| Body | 10 | Yes (excluded entirely if user has no weight goal and no weight data) |
| Plan | 10 | Excluded entirely if user has no active training plan |

**Per-subsystem signal compositions:**

**Movement (20% of Fitness):**
- Daily steps vs baseline: 100% of subsystem score
- Safe range: ±15% of personal 30-day median

**Strength (20% of Fitness):**
- Weekly volume vs baseline: 70% of subsystem score (7-day trailing total weight lifted ÷ baseline)
- Plan adherence for strength days: 30% of subsystem score (if plan active; else redistributes to weekly volume)

**Cardio (15% of Fitness):**
- Weekly cardio minutes vs baseline: 100% of subsystem score
- Safe range: ±20% of personal 30-day median

**Sleep (15% of Fitness):**
- Duration vs baseline: 60% (safe range ±30 min of personal median nightly duration)
- Consistency (night-to-night duration SD): 40% (safe range ±45 min)

**Recovery (10% of Fitness):**
- HRV vs 14-day EMA: 100% (safe range ±10% of EMA)
- Excluded entirely if user has no HRV data source

**Body (10% of Fitness):**
- Weight trend alignment with goal pace: 70% (measured as actual weekly delta vs goal-implied pace)
- Logging consistency: 30% (did user log weight at least once in last 7 days?)
- Excluded entirely if user has no weight goal and no scale-connected weight data

**Plan (10% of Fitness):**
- Adherence rate across last 7 scheduled training days: 100% (rest days excluded from denominator)
- Excluded entirely if user has no active training plan

### 9.10.2 Nutrition Category

**Nutrition signal weights (v1.18):**

| Signal | Default weight | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Calorie adherence | 35 | Prorated target per §9.9.2 |
| Protein adherence | 25 | Floor signal; over-target always scores full |
| Logging consistency | 20 | Did user log in each typical meal window (breakfast, lunch, dinner)? |
| Macro distribution | 15 | Fat + carbs within targets; composite of 2 sub-signals |
| Hydration | 5 | Only if user has activated hydration (per §4.4.12); otherwise excluded |
| Micronutrient adherence (sugar + fiber + sodium) | 0 at v1 | Tracked but does not contribute to Nutrition score at v1; surfaced for awareness only per §4.4.9 |

**Signal detail specifications:**

- **Calorie adherence safe range:** ±100 kcal of prorated target. D_max: ±500 kcal.
- **Protein adherence safe range:** −10g below target (floor), no ceiling. D_max below: 60g below target.
- **Logging consistency:** binary per window — if user logged at least one meal in breakfast window (5-10am local), lunch window (11am-2pm), and dinner window (5-9pm), subsignal = 1.0; else proportional (2/3 if two windows logged, 1/3 if one, 0 if none). On current day, only completed meal windows count toward denominator.
- **Macro distribution:** each of fat and carbs scored independently against target with ±10% safe range; subsystem score is average of the two.
- **Hydration safe range:** ±15% of user's water goal.

### 9.10.3 Finance Category

**Finance signal weights (v1.18):**

| Signal | Default weight | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Weekly budget adherence | 35 | Ceiling signal |
| Bill on-time rate | 25 | Event signal; trailing 30 days |
| Savings rate | 20 | Trailing 30 days; only if user has savings goal |
| Spending consistency | 10 | 7-day trailing SD; low is good |
| Emergency fund ratio | 10 | Only if user has emergency fund goal (FIN-01) |

**Signal detail specifications:**

- **Weekly budget adherence safe range:** 0 to budget (under budget = 1.0). Safe range high: +5% of budget. D_max high: +50% of budget.
- **Bill on-time rate:** raw value = `bills_paid_on_time / bills_due`, over trailing 30 days. Target: 1.0. Safe range: 0.95–1.0. D_max: 0.7 (below 70% on-time rate is zero signal score).
- **Savings rate:** actual savings / income over trailing 30 days. Target: user's savings goal rate. Safe range: −2 percentage points from target, no ceiling.
- **Spending consistency:** 7-day rolling SD of daily spending, normalized against user's 30-day median daily spending. Target: low SD (coefficient of variation < 0.3). Safe range: CoV < 0.4.
- **Emergency fund ratio:** current savings balance / monthly spending. Target: 3 months (industry standard). Safe range: ≥2.5 months = 1.0.

If user has no Plaid connection, all Finance signals are excluded and the category shows "—".

### 9.10.4 Time Category

Full spec is §4.6.15. Recapped here for §9 completeness:

| Signal | Default weight | Baseline source | Safe range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sleep Regularity | 25 | 14-day personal bedtime+wake SD | ±45 min of personal median |
| Attention Fragmentation | 25 | 30-day personal baseline (pickups, session length, focus block) | ±15% of personal baseline |
| Location Intentionality | 20 | 30-day personal place pattern | Within normal-variance bounds |
| Schedule Density Balance | 15 | 30-day personal meeting-hour median | ±25% of personal baseline |
| Rhythm Adherence | 15 | 30-day personal anchor-time medians | ±60 min of personal median per anchor |

Signal weights redistribute when missing. Minimum 2 of 5 signals for Time category to score.

## 9.11 Overall Score Composition

The Overall Score rolls up the four category scores.

### 9.11.1 Formula

```
overall = Σ (category_score_i × user_weight_i) / Σ user_weight_i
```

Over only the categories that have a computed score (`score is not None`). If fewer than 2 categories have scores, Overall = None.

### 9.11.2 User Weight Customization

Default weights: Fitness 25 / Nutrition 25 / Finance 25 / Time 25. Each weight is user-adjustable in Settings → Overall Score Weights.

**Constraints (v1.18 locked):**
- Each weight must be in [5, 55]
- All four weights must sum to 100

The UI enforces these constraints via a four-slider control that auto-rebalances when one slider is moved. If a user pushes Fitness to 55, the other three compress proportionally to sum to 45 collectively, respecting each individual [5, 55] constraint. If any would go below 5, the drag is clamped.

### 9.11.3 Weight Redistribution When Categories Score "—"

When a category scores "—", its weight is excluded from the denominator. The remaining categories' weights normalize over the active subset.

**Worked example:**
- User weights: Fitness 40 / Nutrition 25 / Finance 20 / Time 15
- Fitness scores 84, Nutrition scores 76, Finance scores "—" (no Plaid), Time scores 71
- Active total weight: 40 + 25 + 15 = 80
- Weighted sum: 84×40 + 76×25 + 71×15 = 3,360 + 1,900 + 1,065 = 6,325
- Overall: 6,325 / 80 = 79
- Label beneath Overall number: "Overall · Fitness + Nutrition + Time"

### 9.11.4 Score Precision & Rounding

Category scores are stored as 2-decimal floats internally (e.g., 84.37) and displayed as integers (84). Overall Score is computed from the unrounded category values and then rounded for display, preventing rounding-cascade drift.

## 9.12 Recomputation & Caching

### 9.12.1 Cache Architecture

Three layers:

- **Layer 1 — Redis (hot):** key `score:{user_id}:{category}:{date}`, TTL 15 minutes. Used by API tier for synchronous requests. Cache hit on the Home Dashboard is the common path.
- **Layer 2 — `daily_scores` table (immutable snapshots):** nightly snapshot of every user's prior-day scores. Populated by the 03:30 UTC worker job. Read by trend charts and historical Day Detail views.
- **Layer 3 — On-demand computation:** if a request misses both Redis and `daily_scores` (e.g., Day Detail for today, which hasn't snapshotted yet), the engine computes fresh from source data. Result is then cached to Redis.

### 9.12.2 Cache Invalidation

Triggers that invalidate cached scores for a user + date + category:

- User logs, edits, or deletes a record that contributes to that date's signals (meal, workout, weight, transaction category, etc.) — invalidates the relevant category's cache for that date
- User connects or disconnects a data source — invalidates the affected category's cache for today
- User changes their goal profile or macro targets — invalidates all Nutrition cache for recent dates
- User changes Overall Score weights in Settings — invalidates the Overall Score cache only (category scores are unaffected)

Invalidation is fine-grained: logging today's meal invalidates today's Nutrition cache but not yesterday's, and does not invalidate Fitness/Finance/Time caches.

### 9.12.3 Eager vs Lazy Recomputation

Lazy: the engine does not eagerly recompute on cache invalidation. The next score request triggers a fresh computation. This is correct for the product's access pattern — users read scores far more often than signals change.

Exception: when a user edits data and the UI remains on a screen that is actively displaying the affected score, the client optimistically invalidates local state and re-requests. The server's response will be a freshly-computed score.

### 9.12.4 Nightly Snapshot Job

At 03:30 UTC (a time chosen because it is after local midnight for all US timezones and before the 05:00 UTC hot-to-archived migration per §6.8.6):

```
FOR each user with activity in last 7 days:
    FOR each date in range(today - 1, today):  # last full local day
        FOR each category in [Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time]:
            score, raw_deltas = compute_category_score(user, date, category)
            INSERT INTO daily_scores (user_id, date, category, score, raw_deltas_json, computed_at)
        overall_score = compute_overall_score(user, date)
        INSERT INTO daily_scores (user_id, date, 'overall', overall_score, ..., computed_at)

    recompute_baselines(user)  # per §9.6.5
```

The job is idempotent: a second run on the same day produces the same values and overwrites the prior snapshot (via `ON CONFLICT` upsert on `(user_id, date, category)`). In practice, a second run only occurs during disaster recovery — normal operation runs once per UTC day.

### 9.12.5 Historical Immutability

Snapshotted scores in `daily_scores` for dates older than **7 days for Core users, 30 days for Pro users** are immutable. Data edits to those dates do not trigger recomputation. Rationale: the "my historical trend chart keeps moving" problem is worse than slightly-stale old scores. Users who need to correct very old data can do so — the data is stored — but the score stays as it was snapshotted.

**Exception carved out in v1.18:** recomputation for edits that would trigger AI cost is hard-capped at 7 days for all tiers. AI-cost-bearing recomputes include any calculation that calls the meal parser, workout parser, or any AI surface to re-derive data from source records. Non-AI recomputes are unbounded for Pro users and 7-day-capped for Core per the general immutability rule.

### 9.12.6 Mid-Day Target Changes (v1.27 clarification)

When a user changes their primary fitness goal or edits Body Stats / Daily Life / Advanced overrides mid-day, calorie and macro targets recompute. This raises the question of how scores for the current day should be computed — against the old target, the new target, or some blended value.

**Rule:** scores for the current day recompute against the **new target from midnight user-local of that day.** Scores for prior days are governed by the historical immutability rule above (§9.12.5) and are not affected by the mid-day change.

**Worked example:** User has goal "Reach 180 lbs" with calorie target 2,100 at 9am Tuesday. At 2pm Tuesday they switch primary fitness goal to "Reach 165 lbs" and target drops to 1,850. They had consumed 1,400 calories by 2pm.

- Tuesday's scoring: uses 1,850 target for the full day (from midnight). The 1,400 calories consumed are evaluated against 1,850, not against 2,100. At 2pm, user has 450 calories remaining (1,850 − 1,400) rather than 700 (2,100 − 1,400).
- Monday and prior: uses whatever target was in force at midnight of that day (2,100 for Monday if Monday's snapshot locked it there). No recomputation against the new target unless the day is inside the user's non-AI-recompute immutability window.

**Rationale:** treating the change-day as "new target from midnight" keeps daily scoring logic simple (one target per day, no piecewise intra-day math) and aligns with user intent (the user changed their goal expecting to see their new target reflected today). Blended intra-day math would be more technically accurate at the margin but would confuse users ("why does my calorie-remaining indicator jump when I change my goal?") and add edge-case complexity to every score calculation.

**Edge case — user sees same-day score move at 2pm switch:** if the Nutrition category score for Tuesday was already computed at 9am against the old target (e.g., cached per §9.12.1), the switch invalidates the cache (§9.12.2) and next score retrieval recomputes against the new target. User sees the score change. This is the correct behavior — they changed their targets, scores reflect new targets.

**Edge case — user reverts the change later same day:** if the user switches again before midnight (e.g., at 5pm switches back to the original goal), that day's target returns to the original value. Scores recompute against the original target. No history of the mid-day flip is reflected in the score snapshot — the nightly 03:30 UTC snapshot captures whatever the target was at midnight-of-the-following-day.

**No special treatment for fitness body-comp goal archival:** when a user archives their primary fitness body-comp goal mid-day and falls to maintenance targets (per §4.10.10), the same rule applies — maintenance target takes effect from midnight user-local of that day.

## 9.13 Anti-Oscillation & Score Stability

Scores must change meaningfully when data changes, but not oscillate noisily from one recompute to the next with no real underlying movement.

### 9.13.1 Smoothing Policy

**Category scores: no smoothing.** The day's score reflects the day's signals directly. Smoothing daily category scores would mask genuine shifts in user behavior.

**HRV signal (Fitness Recovery): 14-day EMA.** HRV is intrinsically noisy (night-to-night variation can exceed 20% without underlying physiological change). The 14-day exponential moving average dampens noise without introducing lag for real trends.

**No other smoothing at v1.** Section 9's position: if a user's score oscillates day-to-day, that reflects genuine day-to-day variation in their life, and hiding that variation is worse than exposing it.

### 9.13.2 Delta Indicators

The Home Dashboard and category cards show a small ▲/▼ indicator next to the score, comparing today's score to yesterday's snapshotted score.

- ▲+N — today's score is at least 1 point higher than yesterday's
- ▼−N — today's score is at least 1 point lower
- — (em-dash) — change is less than 1 point in either direction, or yesterday's score is None

The indicator is rendered from the snapshotted `daily_scores` yesterday value vs today's current score (fresh or cached), not from raw internal precision. This prevents +0.3 → +0.4 rounding-only movements from flashing indicators.

## 9.14 Raw Deltas & Debug View

The `raw_deltas` blob returned with every score is the foundation of explainability.

### 9.14.1 Schema

Per category score:
```
raw_deltas: [
  {
    signal_id: "nutrition.calorie_adherence",
    raw_value: 1820,
    target: 2140,
    prorated_target: 1840,  # if applicable
    deviation: -20,
    normalized_score: 1.0,
    default_weight: 35,
    redistributed_weight: 35,
    contribution: 35.0,  // points contributed to the 100 scale
    was_prorated: true,
    proration_curve_id: "calories_sigmoid_v1",
    uses_personal_baseline: false,
    baseline_status: "n/a"
  },
  { ... next signal ... }
]
```

### 9.14.2 Surfacing — Settings → Advanced

Per v1.18 locked policy, raw deltas are surfaced only in Settings → Advanced → Score Debug for v1. The debug view:

- Lists each category with its current score
- Tapping a category expands to show the `raw_deltas` in a table: signal name, raw value, target/baseline, deviation, normalized score, weight, contribution
- Each signal row is tappable to show its curve visualization (a small piecewise-linear chart with the user's current value marked)
- Is explicitly not an end-user feature surface in v1 — it is a debugging/power-user-transparency tool

The chatbot is the primary user-facing surface for score explanation per §3.2's descriptive-AI rule. A user asking "why is my Fitness score 72 today?" receives a narrated response derived from the raw_deltas for Fitness.

### 9.14.3 v1.1+ Candidate — "Explain This Score" Button

Not in v1 but candidate for v1.1+: a subtle "Explain" action on each score that opens a modal showing the top 3 drivers of the score (top-3 absolute contribution values), in plain English. Would use a deterministic template fed from raw_deltas, not AI. Deferred because user testing showed the Debug view covers power users and the chatbot covers casual users; an additional surface may not earn its place.

## 9.15 Testing Framework

Given the math density of this section, Section 9's correctness is validated by a deterministic test suite run on every build.

### 9.15.1 Test Categories

**Golden tests:** hand-crafted scenarios with expected score outputs.
- Scenario: "New user day 1, HealthKit connected, 1 meal logged, 1 weight logged" → expected Fitness 64, Nutrition 57, Overall 60, others "—"
- Scenario: "Week-old user with HealthKit + Plaid + Calendar, missing a budget by $40" → expected Fitness 72, Nutrition 81, Finance 73, Time 69, Overall 74

**Boundary tests:** edge-case inputs.
- Signal at exact safe-range boundary → score = 1.0
- Signal at D_max → score = 0.0
- Signal just past D_max → clamped to 0.0, not negative

**Redistribution tests:** sparse-data users.
- Only 2 signals active → ensure weights re-normalize correctly
- Only 1 signal active → ensure category returns `score = None`

**Baseline tests:** warmup behavior.
- Day 13 → population default in use
- Day 14 with 10+ valid days of data → personal baseline in use
- Day 14 with 6 valid days → population default still in use

**Proration tests:** time-of-day calculations.
- Calorie signal at 9am, no meals logged → score near 1.0 (prorated target is ~150 kcal)
- Calorie signal at 9pm, exactly at target → score 1.0
- Calorie signal at 9pm, 500 kcal over → score decreases per curve

**Idempotency tests:** same inputs → same output, across runs, across cache states.

### 9.15.2 Test Data Fixtures

A dedicated `scoring_fixtures` test harness maintains 50+ user scenarios covering:
- New users, veteran users
- Sparse connections, full-stack connections
- Various goal types (lose / build / maintain / recomp)
- Weekday vs weekend patterns
- Warmup vs post-warmup states

Fixtures are versioned; a change to Section 9's math requires updating the fixtures (intentional — the expected values encode the product's score semantics).

## 9.16 Edge Cases & Error Handling

**Timezone transitions (daylight saving):** scoring runs in user's local timezone per §4.8.9. A 23-hour or 25-hour day due to DST shift is treated as a normal day — proration curves operate on "hours since local midnight" which naturally handles the shift.

**User changes timezone mid-day:** the user's new timezone takes effect for the next local midnight. Today's accumulating signals are not retroactively re-prorated.

**Missing yesterday for today's delta indicator:** if `daily_scores` has no entry for yesterday (user was inactive, user was in their first day, snapshotting job failed), delta indicator shows "—" (em-dash) rather than a direction.

**Clock skew / future-dated logs:** data logged with timestamps in the future (e.g., user's device clock is wrong) is clamped to "now" for scoring purposes. The original timestamp is preserved for display and audit but the scoring engine treats it as an edge-case input.

**Very old edits:** a user editing data 6 months old (if they're Pro with archived storage) will not trigger score recomputation for that date — the score is immutable per §9.12.5. The edit persists in the data and affects any export, but the snapshotted score remains.

**Goal changes mid-period:** if the user changes their calorie goal on a Wednesday, the week's prior days retain their original goal-based score. Thursday onward uses the new goal. This avoids retroactively relabeling "good" days as "bad" just because the user adjusted ambition.

**Data source disconnection:** the moment a user disconnects a source, the signals derived from it stop being available for today's score (the signals redistribute to others). Historical scores snapshotted before the disconnection are unaffected.

**Signal with zero weight after customization:** currently not possible at v1 because the [5, 55] weight constraints only apply to category weights, not signal weights. Signal weights are not user-customizable in v1.

## 9.17 Data Persistence

### 9.17.1 Tables

**`daily_scores`** — immutable historical score snapshots
- `user_id` (FK)
- `score_date` (DATE, user-local)
- `category` (enum: fitness, nutrition, finance, time, overall)
- `score` (smallint 0–100, nullable for "—" state)
- `raw_deltas_json` (JSONB)
- `active_signal_count` (smallint)
- `total_weight_active` (smallint)
- `computed_at` (timestamp)
- `computation_version` (smallint — bumped when §9 math changes, for forward-migration)
- Index: `(user_id, score_date)` primary
- Index: `(user_id, category, score_date)` for trend chart queries

**`user_score_baselines`** — personal baselines updated nightly
- `user_id` (FK)
- `signal_id` (string)
- `baseline_value` (numeric)
- `baseline_sample_count` (smallint — supports the "≥10 days" threshold)
- `last_updated` (timestamp)
- Primary key: `(user_id, signal_id)`

**`user_score_weights`** — Overall Score user customization
- `user_id` (FK)
- `fitness_weight` (smallint 5–55, default 25)
- `nutrition_weight` (smallint 5–55, default 25)
- `finance_weight` (smallint 5–55, default 25)
- `time_weight` (smallint 5–55, default 25)
- `last_updated` (timestamp)
- Constraint: `fitness + nutrition + finance + time = 100`

**`score_cache_invalidations`** — audit trail of cache invalidations (for debugging)
- `user_id` (FK)
- `category` (enum)
- `score_date` (DATE)
- `trigger_event` (string: "meal_logged", "weight_edited", "goal_changed", etc.)
- `invalidated_at` (timestamp)
- Retention: 7 days (rolling window)

### 9.17.2 Redis Keys

- `score:{user_id}:{category}:{date}` — cached score JSON, TTL 900s (15 min)
- `overall:{user_id}:{date}` — cached Overall Score JSON, TTL 900s
- `baseline_fresh:{user_id}` — boolean flag set to `true` after nightly baseline job for this user completes, cleared if any signal's data changes materially (rarely)

### 9.17.3 Archived Storage Migration

Per §6.8.5, score records older than the user's hot-window boundary migrate to archived storage. For Core users, `daily_scores` rows older than 90 days move to archived. For Pro, the hot window is longer (TBD in Section 13). Archived scores remain queryable for export and historical Day Detail but are not eligible for recomputation.

## 9.18 Worked End-to-End Example

A complete walkthrough of how the engine scores a representative day for a representative user.

### 9.18.1 User State

**User:** Day 45 (past warmup), Fitness + Nutrition + Finance + Time all active.

**Connections:** HealthKit, Strava, Plaid, Google Calendar, Gmail, CoreLocation, iOS Screen Time.

**Active goals:** Get Leaner (primary), Hit new bench PR (secondary), Emergency fund $5K (secondary).

**User weights:** Fitness 30 / Nutrition 25 / Finance 20 / Time 25.

### 9.18.2 Today's Raw Data (measured at 9:00 PM local)

- **Meals logged:** breakfast (520 kcal, 38g protein), lunch (680 kcal, 42g protein), snack (220 kcal, 12g protein), dinner (780 kcal, 55g protein). Total: 2,200 kcal, 147g protein.
- **Workouts:** 1 strength workout (push day, 47 min, 6 exercises, 18 sets, 4,200 lbs total volume)
- **Weight logged:** 184.2 lbs (down 0.4 from 7-day median)
- **Steps:** 6,820
- **Sleep last night:** 7h 20m, bedtime 11:18 PM, wake 6:38 AM
- **HRV this morning:** 54 ms
- **Plaid transactions today:** 3 transactions totaling $47.30; weekly total so far: $412 vs $500 budget; 1 bill paid on time today; savings account +$150 this week
- **Calendar today:** 3 meetings (10am, 2pm, 4pm), total 2.5 hours
- **Screen time so far:** 2h 40m, 38 pickups, longest focus block 74 min
- **Location visits:** Home → Gym → Home → Office (via public transit) → Coffee shop (meeting) → Home

### 9.18.3 Baseline Values (from nightly job)

- Steps baseline: 7,200
- Sleep duration baseline: 7h 15m
- Bedtime SD (14-day): 22 min
- Wake SD (14-day): 18 min
- Pickup baseline: 42/day
- Session length baseline: 3.2 min
- Longest focus block baseline: 68 min
- Meeting hours baseline: 2.8/weekday
- HRV 14-day EMA: 57 ms

### 9.18.4 Fitness Subsystem Scores

- **Movement:** steps 6,820 vs baseline 7,200; deviation −5.3%, within ±15% safe range. Signal score 1.0. Subsystem score 100.
- **Strength:** weekly volume (sum of last 7 days) 18,400 lbs vs baseline 17,800 lbs; deviation +3.4%, within safe range (±20%). Signal score 1.0. Plan adherence 3/3 scheduled strength days in last 7 days. Signal score 1.0. Subsystem score = 0.7×1.0 + 0.3×1.0 = 1.0 → 100.
- **Cardio:** weekly minutes 0 vs baseline 40 min/week (user just started a cardio-less cycle). Signal is in trailing 7-day, has data (zero counts), so not excluded. Deviation −100%, past D_max. Signal score 0.0. Subsystem score 0.
- **Sleep:** duration 7h 20m vs baseline 7h 15m, within ±30 min. Signal 1.0 × 0.6 = 0.6. Consistency (duration SD last 7 nights) 32 min, within ±45 min. Signal 1.0 × 0.4 = 0.4. Subsystem score 100.
- **Recovery:** HRV 54 vs 14-day EMA 57, deviation −5.3%, within ±10%. Signal 1.0. Subsystem 100.
- **Body:** weight 184.2 vs 7-day median 184.6 (Goal: 1 lb/week loss → expected −0.14/day, actual −0.06/day). Pace alignment: 43% of expected. Past safe range (±50%? — Section 9 signal spec for body-trend-alignment safe range lands at ±30%). Signal 0.35. Logging consistency: logged 5 of last 7 days. Signal 0.71. Body subsystem = 0.7×0.35 + 0.3×0.71 = 0.245 + 0.213 = 0.458 → 46.
- **Plan:** 3 of 3 scheduled workout days completed in last 7 days (today's push counted). Adherence 1.0. Subsystem 100.

Weighted Fitness: (100×20 + 100×20 + 0×15 + 100×15 + 100×10 + 46×10 + 100×10) / 100 = (2000 + 2000 + 0 + 1500 + 1000 + 460 + 1000) / 100 = 7,960 / 100 = **Fitness score: 80.**

### 9.18.5 Nutrition Signals

At 9 PM, day_progress_calories(21) = 0.95. Prorated calorie target: 2,140 × 0.95 = 2,033.
- **Calorie adherence:** consumed 2,200, prorated target 2,033, deviation +167. Past safe range (±100), within D_max (500). Signal = 1 − (167 − 100)/(500 − 100) = 1 − 0.168 = 0.832.
- **Protein adherence:** consumed 147g, target 150g (floor). Deviation −3g, within safe range (−10g). Signal 1.0.
- **Logging consistency:** 4 meals logged, covers all 3 meal windows. Signal 1.0.
- **Macro distribution:** fat and carbs both within ±10%. Composite 1.0.
- **Hydration:** not active. Excluded.

Active weights: 35 + 25 + 20 + 15 = 95.
Weighted Nutrition: (83.2×35 + 100×25 + 100×20 + 100×15) / 95 = (2,912 + 2,500 + 2,000 + 1,500) / 95 = 8,912 / 95 = **Nutrition score: 94.**

### 9.18.6 Finance Signals

- **Weekly budget adherence:** $412 of $500 week-to-date, under budget. Signal 1.0.
- **Bill on-time rate:** 28/28 bills paid on time in last 30 days. Signal 1.0.
- **Savings rate:** +$150 this week, monthly projection $650 vs goal rate $625/month → 104% of target. Signal 1.0.
- **Spending consistency:** 7-day CoV 0.28, within safe range (<0.4). Signal 1.0.
- **Emergency fund ratio:** current savings $1,800, monthly spending $2,100, ratio 0.86 months. Target 3. Past D_max. Signal 0.0.

Weights: 35 + 25 + 20 + 10 + 10 = 100 (all active).
Weighted Finance: (100×35 + 100×25 + 100×20 + 100×10 + 0×10) / 100 = (3,500 + 2,500 + 2,000 + 1,000 + 0) / 100 = 9,000 / 100 = **Finance score: 90.**

### 9.18.7 Time Signals

- **Sleep Regularity:** bedtime SD 22 min (within ±45), wake SD 18 min (within ±45). Both signals 1.0. Composite 1.0.
- **Attention Fragmentation:** at 9 PM, partial-day scaling. Pickups so far: 38 vs baseline-at-this-hour 37 (extrapolated from 42/day baseline with day_progress_screen_time(21) ≈ 0.92 → 38.6). Deviation near zero. Session length 3.5 min vs 3.2 min baseline, +9% within safe range. Longest focus block 74 min vs 68 min baseline, +8% (good direction). Composite 1.0.
- **Location Intentionality:** Home + Gym + Office + Coffee Shop + Home matches the user's typical weekday pattern (office, gym before work, meetings). Calendar had a meeting at coffee shop at 4pm → location matched. Intentionality composite 1.0.
- **Schedule Density Balance:** 2.5 meeting hours vs 2.8 baseline, deviation −11%, within ±25%. Signal 1.0. No back-to-back stacking penalty.
- **Rhythm Adherence:** wake 6:38 AM vs baseline 6:45 AM, deviation 7 min within ±60 min. Signal 1.0. First meal 7:15 AM vs baseline 7:10 AM, 5 min within safe. Signal 1.0. Workday start 9:10 AM (inferred from office arrival + first meeting) vs baseline 9:00 AM, 10 min within safe. Signal 1.0. Workday end data not yet available at 9 PM. Anchor excluded from today's composite. Composite of 3 active anchors: 1.0.

Weights: 25 + 25 + 20 + 15 + 15 = 100 (all active).
Weighted Time: (100×25 + 100×25 + 100×20 + 100×15 + 100×15) / 100 = 100×100/100 = **Time score: 100.**

### 9.18.8 Overall Score

User weights: Fitness 30, Nutrition 25, Finance 20, Time 25. All four active.
Total weight: 100.
Weighted sum: 80×30 + 94×25 + 90×20 + 100×25 = 2,400 + 2,350 + 1,800 + 2,500 = 9,050.
**Overall: 9,050 / 100 = 91.**

### 9.18.9 What The User Sees

- **Home Dashboard:** Overall Score **91** ▲+4 (up from yesterday's 87). Green band.
- **Fitness card:** 80 ▼−2. Amber-edge (still green band but trending).
- **Nutrition card:** 94 ▲+6. Green.
- **Finance card:** 90 ▼0. Green.
- **Time card:** 100 ▲+3. Green.
- **Primary goal bar:** "Lose Weight — 4.2 lbs to go" (from Get Leaner goal).

In Settings → Advanced → Score Debug, the user tapping Fitness would see the raw_deltas showing Cardio at 0.0 driving the score down (no cardio this week) and Body at 0.46 (weight trend behind pace). These are the two levers the user could pull.

## 9.19 Analytics Events

- `score.computed` (category, score_value, was_cached, computation_ms)
- `score.cache_miss` (category, reason)
- `score.daily_snapshot_job` (user_count, duration_ms, errors)
- `baseline.updated` (signal_id, sample_count, baseline_value)
- `baseline.warmup_complete` (user_id) — fires once per user on their 15th day
- `overall_weights.changed` (user_id, new_weights_json)
- `score.debug_viewed` (user_id, category)

---

# Section 10 — AI Requirements

Section 10 is the authoritative AI call inventory and governance layer for Life Dashboard. It specifies every AI call site in the v1 product, binds each to the principles in §3.2 (Descriptive vs Prescriptive AI), §5.9 (AI Integration Layer), and §6.6 (AI Data Handling), and governs the operational machinery around model selection, prompt engineering, quota enforcement, cost attribution, fallback, and quality evaluation.

Section 10 does not re-open decisions locked elsewhere. Redaction rules live in §6.6.3. The AI gateway architecture lives in §5.9. Consent model lives in §6.6.2. Chatbot context containers live in §4.7.10. This section is the single-surface reference for "everywhere AI touches the product, and what each call is allowed to do."

If a call site is not listed in §10.2, it does not exist. Every AI integration in Life Dashboard flows through the gateway and is enumerated here.

## 10.1 Overview & Philosophy

### 10.1.1 The AI Budget

Life Dashboard's AI philosophy is fundamentally scope-limited. Per §3.2, AI is reserved for four jobs where it is the best available tool:

1. **Synthesis** — stitching data from multiple sources into a natural-language answer (chatbot queries spanning fitness, nutrition, finance, time)
2. **Ambiguity resolution** — parsing messy user input when deterministic parsing fails (meal text, workout descriptions)
3. **Drafting** — generating content the user then approves (email replies, workout plans, plan revisions)
4. **Messy-input parsing** — extracting structure from unstructured input (meal photos, voice-transcribed meals, freeform workout descriptions)

Everything else is deterministic. Scoring is deterministic (§9). Email importance classification is deterministic (§4.6.8). Transaction categorization defaults to Plaid's categories + user rules without AI gap-fill at v1. Timeline block classification is deterministic. Pattern computation is deterministic (§4.6.13). The Scoring Engine does not call AI. Neither does the Goal progress calculator. Neither does the Streak counter.

Every AI call in the v1 spec has been asked the question "could a deterministic algorithm do this?" Where the answer was yes, the AI call was removed. The v1.19 removal of the Profile Insight Call is the latest example — the insight was narrating already-computed numbers, which a user can read for themselves or ask the chatbot about.

### 10.1.2 AI Is Never The Product's Identity

Life Dashboard's identity is **unified deterministic scoring + a life-aware AI assistant, in that order.** The four category scores are what the product IS; the chatbot is what the product DOES when asked. If Anthropic's API went offline for 48 hours, the product would lose the chatbot, the meal-photo-scan magic moment, and the daily email summary — but every score, every chart, every category detail view, every Day Timeline reconstruction, every goal, every notification that matters would continue to work. This is a deliberate design.

### 10.1.3 Descriptive vs Prescriptive AI (reference)

Per §3.2, two modes of AI-generated copy are distinguished. **Descriptive AI** (user-invoked only): narrates already-computed numbers when the user explicitly asks. **Prescriptive AI** (banned everywhere except Notifications): tells the user what to do, coaches, moralizes, nudges without invitation.

§3.2 is the principle; §10.13 specifies how each call site enforces it.

### 10.1.4 What Section 10 Is Not

Section 10 is not:

- **The redaction spec.** §6.6.3 is authoritative. Section 10 references and trusts those rules.
- **The consent architecture.** §6.4 and §6.6.2 are authoritative.
- **The gateway implementation spec.** §5.9 specifies the `backend/src/integrations/anthropic/` module.
- **The chatbot UX spec.** §4.7 is authoritative for the overlay, interaction model, and context container architecture.
- **The notification spec.** §4.9 specifies the notification evaluator; §10 just confirms its AI call placement.
- **The pricing spec.** Section 13 owns tier-based AI quota cut-lines. Section 10 states the current quotas but the final cut-lines (especially any pricing-driven adjustments) are Section 13's call.

## 10.2 The AI Call Inventory

The complete v1 AI call inventory. If a call site is not listed here, it does not exist. Every entry is spec'd in detail in §10.3.

| # | Call site | Model | Est. cost/call | Surface | User-invoked | Section |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chatbot query | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.004 | FAB overlay | Yes | §4.7 |
| 2 | Meal photo scan (standard) | Sonnet 4.6 | ~$0.015 | Nutrition FAB | Yes | §4.4.5 |
| 3 | Meal photo scan (Premium Scan, Pro) | Opus 4.6 | ~$0.080 | Nutrition FAB | Yes | §4.4.5 |
| 4 | Meal text/voice parsing | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.0024 | Nutrition FAB | Yes | §4.4.4 |
| 5 | AI Edit Meal | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.0024 | Meal detail | Yes | §4.4.11 |
| 6 | Workout parsing (free-text) | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.002 | Fitness FAB — Freestyle | Yes | §4.3.5 |
| 7 | Workout burn estimation | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.002 | Workout log completion | Yes | §4.3 |
| 8 | AI Edit Workout | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.002 | Workout detail | Yes | §4.3.12 |
| 9 | Workout plan generation | Sonnet 4.6 | ~$0.008 | Onboarding / Fitness tab | Yes | §4.1.6 / §4.3.10 |
| 10 | Workout plan understanding narration | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.0009 | Plan display | Yes (bundled with #9) | §4.1.6 |
| 11 | Workout plan revision | Sonnet 4.6 | ~$0.006 | Plan detail | Yes | §4.3.10 |
| 12 | Pantry ingredient identification | Haiku 4.5 (vision) | ~$0.005 | Pantry Scanner (Pro) | Yes | §4.4.8 |
| 13 | Meal suggestion (from pantry) | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.0036 | Pantry Scanner (Pro) | Yes | §4.4.8 |
| 14 | AI-drafted email reply | Sonnet 4.6 | ~$0.008 | Email detail (Pro) | Yes | §4.6.8 |
| 15 | Daily email summary | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.0015 | Email detail + notification | Proactive (opt-in) | §4.6.8 |
| 16 | Notification evaluator + composer | Haiku 4.5 | ~$0.001-0.002 | Notification pipeline | Proactive (opt-in) | §4.9.5 |

**Total: 16 call sites. 13 user-invoked, 3 proactive (all in the opt-in notification/daily-summary surface per §3.2 carve-out).**

**Removed from prior drafts (v1.18-v1.19):**
- Archetype classification (Sonnet, onboarding — removed v1.18 because the label had no downstream effect)
- Profile Insight narration (Haiku, onboarding — removed v1.19 because it was unsolicited descriptive AI violating §3.2)
- Per-email importance classifier (Haiku, every email — removed v1.18, replaced with deterministic 3-signal classifier in §4.6.8)
- Transaction categorization AI gap-fill (Haiku, per transaction — removed at v1, deferred to v1.1+ as Plaid + user rules are sufficient)

### 10.2.1 Expected AI Cost Per User Per Month

Rough modeling at the two tier archetypes:

**Core user, moderate usage:**
- Chatbot: 7 queries/day × 30 × $0.004 = $0.84
- Meal photo scan: 1/day × 30 × $0.015 = $0.45
- Meal text parsing: 2/day × 30 × $0.0024 = $0.14
- Workout parsing + burn: 0.5/day × 30 × $0.004 = $0.06
- Daily email summary: 1/day × 30 × $0.0015 = $0.045
- Notification evaluator: ~10/day × 30 × $0.0015 = $0.45
- **Total: ~$1.98/month**
- Core price $8.99 → 22% gross AI cost ratio before platform fees

**Pro user, heavy usage:**
- Chatbot: 50 queries/day × 30 × $0.004 = $6.00
- Premium Scan: 2/day × 30 × $0.080 = $4.80
- Meal text parsing: 3/day × 30 × $0.0024 = $0.22
- Workout parsing + burn: 1/day × 30 × $0.004 = $0.12
- Plan revision: 2/month × $0.006 = $0.01
- AI email reply: 3/day × 30 × $0.008 = $0.72
- Email style learning context: ~$0.10/month
- Daily email summary: 1/day × 30 × $0.0015 = $0.045
- Notification evaluator: ~20/day × 30 × $0.0015 = $0.90
- **Total: ~$12.92/month**
- Pro price $17.99 → 72% gross AI cost ratio on heavy users (before platform fees)

Heavy Pro users are the margin compression risk. This is why Pro exists at the specific price point it does — and why Premium Scan, AI email drafts, and heavy chatbot use are Pro-gated. Section 13's pricing review should validate these ratios against actual usage data post-launch.

### 10.2.2 The Call Inventory Is Closed

Adding a new AI call site to Life Dashboard is a **document change**, not a code change. A new AI integration requires:

1. Adding the call site to §10.2 inventory and §10.3 per-call specification
2. Justifying the call per §3.2 (AI Only When It Is The Best Tool)
3. Updating the cost model in §10.2.1
4. Routing the call through the §5.9 gateway
5. Adding golden tests per §10.15

This process deliberately adds friction. The goal is to prevent "AI creep" — the pattern where products add AI features because they can, not because users need them. Engineering cannot ship a new AI call site without updating this section.

## 10.3 Per-Call Specifications

Every call site in §10.2 gets a full specification below. Each spec includes: function name, model, input structure, output structure, token budget, cost target, latency target, caching policy, fallback behavior, prompt MODE header (per §10.13), and special considerations.

### 10.3.1 Chatbot Query

- **Function:** `chatbot_query`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.7 FAB overlay, chat input
- **User-invoked:** Yes (user taps input and sends)
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (with user-invoked prescriptive allowance per §10.13.2)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~500 tokens): voice guidelines, refusal handling, MODE header, container schema references
  - Typed context containers (§4.7.10, variable size, total budget ~7,500 tokens)
  - Conversation history (RAM-only session, up to 20 turns)
  - User query (≤500 chars per §4.7)
- **Output:** Streamed text response via SSE (see §10.9)
- **Token budget:** ~8,000 input + 1,500 output
- **Target latency:** first-token <1.5s, full response <8s for 95th percentile
- **Cost target:** ~$0.004/call
- **Caching:** system prompt + container schemas cached (saves ~60% on repeated prefix tokens)
- **Quota counting:** yes, counts against daily chatbot quota (Core 10/day, Pro 50/day with 300/day soft cap)
- **Redaction:** per §6.6.3 (merchants → categories, locations → place names, email bodies never included, Strava rows excluded)
- **Fallback:** "Life Dashboard is temporarily unable to respond. Tap retry." No synthetic fallback text.

### 10.3.2 Meal Photo Scan — Standard

- **Function:** `scan_meal_photo`
- **Model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6
- **Surface:** §4.4.5 Meal Logging — Photo Scanning
- **User-invoked:** Yes (user taps scan, takes photo)
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output; no natural-language prescriptive content)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~800 tokens): 9-technique prompt engineering strategy per §4.4.5a, JSON output schema, few-shot examples
  - User photo (up to 1024×1024 compressed before upload, ~5-15K image tokens)
  - Optional user hint text ("this is a turkey sandwich with a side salad")
- **Output:** Structured JSON — items array (each with name, portion, calories, protein, carbs, fat, sugar, fiber, sodium, confidence), totals, shortened label
- **Token budget:** ~7,000 input (including image) + 800 output
- **Target latency:** <8s for 95th percentile
- **Cost target:** ~$0.015/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against user's per-day meal scan count (part of overall AI interaction monitoring per §10.7)
- **Redaction:** photo itself is not redacted but is ephemeral per §6.8 (R2 24h lifecycle); the photo is not stored long-term
- **Fallback:** "Scan unavailable, try again or type your meal instead." Falls through to text-parse flow (§10.3.4).

### 10.3.3 Meal Photo Scan — Premium (Pro-gated)

- **Function:** `scan_meal_photo_premium`
- **Model:** Claude Opus 4.6
- **Surface:** §4.4.5 Pro-tier Premium Scan
- **User-invoked:** Yes (user explicitly taps "Use Premium Scan")
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:** same schema as standard scan but with expanded prompt engineering (larger few-shot set, deeper portion-anchoring guidance)
- **Output:** same JSON schema as standard scan, with `premium: true` flag and higher confidence values
- **Token budget:** ~10,000 input + 1,000 output
- **Target latency:** <15s for 95th percentile (Opus is slower than Sonnet)
- **Cost target:** ~$0.080/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached (shared with standard scan where structure overlaps; Opus-specific prompt variant cached separately)
- **Quota counting:** Premium Scan counts as 1 Pro chatbot-quota-equivalent (higher cost warrants accounting parity); capped at 20/day for abuse protection
- **Fallback:** degrades to Sonnet-based standard scan; user is notified "Premium Scan unavailable — used standard scan instead" with no re-charge.

### 10.3.4 Meal Text/Voice Parsing

- **Function:** `parse_meal_text`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.4.4 Meal Logging — Text and Voice Input
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~600 tokens): parsing guidelines, portion inference rules, JSON output schema, few-shot examples
  - User text (up to 500 chars; voice-transcribed text follows same flow)
- **Output:** Structured JSON — items, totals, shortened label (same schema as photo scan)
- **Token budget:** ~800 input + 600 output
- **Target latency:** <3s for 95th percentile
- **Cost target:** ~$0.0024/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against daily meal-log count (soft cap)
- **Fallback:** "Couldn't parse that meal. Try rephrasing or tap through to manual entry." Manual entry form is always available.

### 10.3.5 AI Edit Meal

- **Function:** `edit_meal_ai`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.4.11 Meal Edit and Delete
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~500 tokens): edit-specific instructions, JSON schema
  - Original meal (existing items + totals)
  - User correction text ("this was 8 oz chicken not 6" or "I also had a cookie")
- **Output:** Revised JSON meal with updated items, totals, and optional edit-reason annotation
- **Token budget:** ~800 input + 600 output
- **Target latency:** <3s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.0024/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against daily meal-log AI-interaction count
- **Fallback:** user reverts to manual edit of the meal.

### 10.3.6 Workout Parsing (Free-Text)

- **Function:** `parse_workout_text`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.3.5 Path C (Freestyle Workout)
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~700 tokens): exercise recognition, set/rep/weight parsing, superset detection, JSON schema
  - User text (up to 1,000 chars; voice-transcribed text follows same flow)
- **Output:** Structured JSON — exercises array (each with name, sets, reps, weight, notes), total volume, total duration estimate, shortened session label
- **Token budget:** ~1,200 input + 800 output
- **Target latency:** <4s for 95th percentile
- **Cost target:** ~$0.002/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against daily workout-log AI count
- **Fallback:** user manually picks exercises from the library.

### 10.3.7 Workout Burn Estimation

- **Function:** `estimate_workout_burn`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.3 — logged workouts without HealthKit/device-provided burn
- **User-invoked:** Yes (automatic on workout log completion, but triggered by user's log action)
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (numeric + short label output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~400 tokens): estimation methodology, user profile fields (weight, age, sex), JSON schema
  - Workout description text (from Path C) OR structured workout data (from Path A/B)
  - User stats snapshot (weight, HRV baseline if available)
- **Output:** JSON — calories_burned (integer), shortened_label (string), confidence ("high"/"medium"/"low")
- **Token budget:** ~800 input + 150 output
- **Target latency:** <2s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.002/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against daily workout-log AI count
- **Fallback:** use HealthKit-provided burn if available; else show "Burn estimate unavailable" with option to manually enter calories.

### 10.3.8 AI Edit Workout

- **Function:** `edit_workout_ai`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.3.12 Logging Flows
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:** existing workout + correction text ("add 2 more sets of bench at 195")
- **Output:** revised JSON workout
- **Token budget:** ~1,000 input + 700 output
- **Target latency:** <3s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.002/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Fallback:** user reverts to manual edit.

### 10.3.9 Workout Plan Generation

- **Function:** `generate_workout_plan`
- **Model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6 (reasoning-heavy structured output — genuine plan composition requires inference)
- **Surface:** §4.1.6 (onboarding) or §4.3.10 (Fitness tab)
- **User-invoked:** Yes (user explicitly chose "Build My Plan")
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~1,500 tokens): plan generation rules, exercise selection guidelines, progression principles, JSON schema, few-shot example plans for each common goal
  - Workout Builder answers (~300 tokens): experience level, days/week, equipment, preferences, injuries, goal
  - User profile snapshot (weight, RMR, primary nutrition goal for calorie-goal context)
- **Output:** Full weekly plan JSON — days array, each day with exercises, sets, reps, weight guidance, rest intervals, progression notes
- **Token budget:** ~2,000 input + 1,500 output
- **Target latency:** <10s for 95th percentile
- **Cost target:** ~$0.008/call
- **Caching:** system prompt + few-shot examples cached
- **Quota counting:** counts as 1 plan-generation event per user per 30 days (soft cap to prevent plan-grinding abuse); Pro has higher cap
- **Fallback:** deterministic rule-based plan generator (takes the 8-step wizard inputs and produces a template plan matching the user's experience/equipment/days). Less tuned but serviceable.

### 10.3.10 Workout Plan Understanding Narration

- **Function:** `narrate_plan_understanding`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.1.6 — displayed alongside the generated plan
- **User-invoked:** Yes (bundled with #9 when user chose Build My Plan)
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~400 tokens): narration style, descriptive-only rule, max length 80 words
  - Generated plan JSON from #9 + user's goal context
- **Output:** 3-sentence rationale paragraph (~80 words)
- **Token budget:** ~1,200 input + 150 output
- **Target latency:** <3s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.0009/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Fallback:** plan displays without the narration paragraph; no error shown.

### 10.3.11 Workout Plan Revision

- **Function:** `revise_workout_plan`
- **Model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6
- **Surface:** §4.3.10 — Fitness tab, plan detail view
- **User-invoked:** Yes ("Revise this plan")
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~1,200 tokens): revision guidelines, preservation rules (don't change what the user didn't ask to change), JSON schema
  - Current plan JSON
  - User revision request (up to 500 chars): "make Wednesday lighter" / "add more pulling" / "swap deadlifts for RDLs"
- **Output:** Revised plan JSON with `change_summary` field listing what was modified and why
- **Token budget:** ~2,500 input + 1,500 output
- **Target latency:** <8s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.006/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** 5 revisions/plan soft cap; beyond that prompt user to re-generate instead
- **Fallback:** user manually edits plan in the plan editor.

### 10.3.12 Pantry Ingredient Identification

- **Function:** `identify_pantry_ingredients`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5 (vision)
- **Surface:** §4.4.8 Pantry Scanner (Pro-gated)
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~500 tokens): pantry/fridge identification rules, JSON output schema
  - Up to 3 photos per scan (user can take multiple angles)
- **Output:** JSON array of identified ingredients (flat list, no quantities — user confirms/edits list before next step)
- **Token budget:** ~8,000 input (photos) + 400 output
- **Target latency:** <6s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.005/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against Pro Pantry Scanner usage (soft cap 10/day)
- **Fallback:** user types ingredients manually.

### 10.3.13 Meal Suggestion (from Pantry)

- **Function:** `suggest_meal_from_pantry`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.4.8 — after ingredient ID completes and user confirms list
- **User-invoked:** Yes
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE (structured output)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~600 tokens): meal-suggestion guidelines, JSON schema, simplification rule (do not use full user profile context; use only ingredients + meal type + calories remaining)
  - Confirmed ingredients list
  - Meal type (breakfast/lunch/dinner/snack — inferred from time of day, user can override)
  - Calories remaining today
- **Output:** JSON array of 3 meal suggestions, each with name, brief description, estimated macros, ingredients used
- **Token budget:** ~1,000 input + 700 output
- **Target latency:** <4s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.0036/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Fallback:** suggest block hidden; user types meal idea manually.

### 10.3.14 AI-Drafted Email Reply

- **Function:** `draft_email_reply`
- **Model:** Claude Sonnet 4.6 (drafting nuanced replies is reasoning-heavy; Haiku produces stiff drafts users rewrite)
- **Surface:** §4.6.8 Email Detail View (Pro-gated)
- **User-invoked:** Yes ("AI Draft Reply")
- **MODE:** DESCRIPTIVE-DRAFTING (generates user-approved content)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~1,000 tokens): drafting guidelines, style-matching rules, formality inference
  - Email thread (sender + subject + first 200 chars per message, never full bodies per §6.6.3)
  - Optional user hint ("decline politely" / "ask about pricing")
  - User's style features (if Pro user opted into style learning — deterministic features extracted from sent emails: sentence length, formality, greeting/signoff patterns, common phrases)
- **Output:** Draft reply text (up to 400 words) + confidence ("match-user-style: high/medium/low")
- **Token budget:** ~2,000 input + 800 output
- **Target latency:** <5s
- **Cost target:** ~$0.008/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached; user-specific style features cached in Redis per session
- **Quota counting:** 20 drafts/day Pro soft cap
- **Fallback:** "AI draft unavailable — please write reply manually."

### 10.3.15 Daily Email Summary

- **Function:** `summarize_daily_emails`
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.6.8 — scheduled 7 AM user local time + notification delivery
- **User-invoked:** No — **proactive (opt-in via notification settings)**
- **MODE:** PROACTIVE-DESCRIPTIVE (per §3.2 carve-out: proactive surfaces are permitted in Notifications)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~500 tokens): summary format (3-5 bullets), descriptive-only constraint, no prescriptive language
  - Top 10 Important-classified emails from last 24h (sender + subject + first 200 chars each, never full bodies)
- **Output:** 3-5 bullet point summary (~100 words)
- **Token budget:** ~2,500 input + 200 output
- **Target latency:** <4s (runs in background worker; latency not user-visible)
- **Cost target:** ~$0.0015/call
- **Caching:** system prompt cached
- **Quota counting:** counts against Core daily AI quota (per §4.7.16); does not count on Pro
- **Fallback:** skip today's summary; tomorrow's runs normally. No error shown to user.

### 10.3.16 Notification Evaluator + Composer

- **Function:** `evaluate_notification` (combined evaluator + composer per §4.9.5)
- **Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5
- **Surface:** §4.9 Notifications pipeline
- **User-invoked:** No — **proactive (opt-in via notification permission)**
- **MODE:** PROACTIVE (permitted surface per §3.2)
- **Input:**
  - System prompt (cached, ~2,000 tokens): §4.9.5 evaluator prompt with signal catalog, aggressiveness level, quiet-hours policy, composer rules, redaction rules
  - Signal bundle (aggregated signals from the Signal Pool within the 30s merge window)
  - User state (aggressiveness level, current quiet-hours status, recent-notifications history for frequency cap)
- **Output:** Structured JSON — decision ("notify"/"suppress"), reason, composed notification content (if notify), deep-link URI
- **Token budget:** ~3,000 input + 500 output
- **Target latency:** <3s per evaluation (background worker; latency not user-visible)
- **Cost target:** ~$0.001-0.002/call (varies with signal bundle size)
- **Caching:** system prompt + signal catalog cached aggressively
