## 14.17 Data Quality Commitments

### 14.17.1 Internal Accuracy Targets (Not User-Facing)

Engineering targets for measured AI and deterministic outputs:

| Output | Internal target | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| Meal text parse accuracy | ≥90% | Golden test set agreement rate |
| Meal photo scan (standard) accuracy | ≥85% | User-edit rate on scans (proxy) |
| Meal photo scan (Premium Opus) accuracy | ≥92% | User-edit rate on scans |
| Workout text parse accuracy | ≥92% | Golden test set agreement rate |
| Workout burn estimate accuracy | ±10% of ground truth | HealthKit burn comparison |
| Email importance classification | ≥88% | User reclassification rate (low means high accuracy) |
| Transaction categorization (Plaid default) | ≥92% | User override rate |
| Chatbot response factual correctness | ≥95% | Manual sample review quarterly |
| Score computation correctness | 100% | Golden tests per §9.15 |
| Goal progress calculation | 100% | Golden tests per §4.10 |

Targets below 100% for AI-derived outputs acknowledge inherent variance. Targets for deterministic calculations are 100% — a scoring bug is a P1 regression, not a data-quality shortfall.

### 14.17.2 Accuracy Measurement Cadence

- **Continuous:** user-edit rates tracked as proxy for accuracy
- **Weekly:** golden test runs produce regression reports
- **Quarterly:** manual sample review of chatbot outputs, meal scans, email classifications (20-50 samples per surface)
- **Annually:** formal accuracy audit with external review (starting year 2)

### 14.17.3 Accuracy Regression Response

Regression detected:
1. Verify regression is real (not test data drift)
2. Identify cause (prompt change, model upgrade, data input change)
3. If prompt-caused: rollback prompt template per §10.18
4. If model-caused: evaluate model selection per §10.4
5. If input-caused: validate upstream data pipeline
6. Retest; close incident when back to target

### 14.17.4 No Public Accuracy Claim

Life Dashboard does not publish specific accuracy numbers in marketing. Accuracy varies by user and by case (meal photos of complex plates vs simple ones, workout descriptions in different styles, etc.). Specific numeric claims risk misleading users about outcomes for their specific use. The product should be accurate in practice; the claim is implicit via user trust, not explicit via marketing.

## 14.18 Maintainability

### 14.18.1 Code Quality Standards

- **TypeScript strict mode:** no implicit any, strict null checks
- **Linting:** ESLint + Prettier enforced in CI
- **Code review:** all changes reviewed by founder (self-review via PR diff for solo phase; team growth expands review)
- **Naming conventions:** descriptive names over clever abbreviations
- **File organization:** feature-grouped, not type-grouped
- **No dead code:** unused imports/exports flagged by linter

### 14.18.2 Documentation Standards

- **Code comments:** explain *why* decisions were made, not *what* code does (code should be self-documenting)
- **Section headers:** each file opens with a brief purpose comment
- **API documentation:** generated from Hono route definitions + JSDoc; no separately maintained docs
- **Architectural decision records (ADRs):** significant decisions captured in `docs/adrs/` directory
- **PRD updates:** material product changes updated in this PRD (this document is the living reference)

### 14.18.3 Module Coupling

Per §5.3, modular monolith pattern:
- `api/` and `workers/` share `domain/` and `data/` — clean layering
- Feature modules loosely coupled; can be extracted to microservices if scale demands
- Dependency graph auditable; circular dependencies forbidden

### 14.18.4 Technical Debt Tracking

- Explicit `TODO` comments with tracking issue reference
- Quarterly tech-debt review: prioritize cleanup of accumulating debt
- Every release includes at least one tech-debt item (15-20% of engineering time)

### 14.18.5 Refactoring

- Refactoring done in dedicated PRs, not mixed with feature work
- Large refactors piloted via feature flags
- Refactoring validated by existing test coverage

## 14.19 Testability & Test Coverage

### 14.19.1 Test Coverage Targets (Internal)

- **Domain layer (`domain/`):** ≥80% line coverage — scoring, macros, baselines, goal logic
- **API layer (`api/`):** ≥70% coverage — integration tests against staging database
- **Data layer (`data/`):** ≥60% coverage — migrations, query builders
- **Mobile client:** ≥50% coverage — critical UI components have snapshot tests
- **Overall:** ≥65% line coverage across the codebase

### 14.19.2 Test Categories

- **Unit tests:** Vitest for backend, Jest+React Native Testing Library for mobile
- **Integration tests:** run against staging Neon branch with seeded data
- **Golden tests:** AI outputs (§10.15) and scoring outputs (§9.15.1)
- **End-to-end tests:** critical user flows (signup → onboarding → log meal → view dashboard) via Maestro or Detox
- **Accessibility tests:** manual VoiceOver/TalkBack per release
- **Load tests:** k6 against staging per §14.6.5

### 14.19.3 CI Gates

Per §11.14.1:
- All tests must pass for merge
- Coverage threshold enforcement: prevents coverage regression
- Lint/typecheck must pass
- CDK synth must succeed (validates infra changes)

### 14.19.4 Flaky Test Policy

Flaky tests (passing/failing non-deterministically) are P3 tech debt:
- Quarantined (marked `skip`) if flakiness prevents merges
- Investigated and fixed within 2 sprints
- Root cause documented (race condition, external dependency, time sensitivity)

Zero-tolerance for flaky tests in critical paths (auth, payment, data integrity).

### 14.19.5 Test Environment Parity

Tests run against environments that match production behavior:
- Unit tests: isolated, fast, no external dependencies
- Integration tests: against Neon dev branch (matches prod schema)
- E2E: against staging (production-shape)

Mock usage minimized: mocks don't catch integration bugs.

## 14.20 Deployability

### 14.20.1 Deploy Frequency

Per §11.14.2:
- **Launch through first 90 days:** daily manual promote to production
- **After 90 days:** continuous deploy on green main, gated by canary + alarms

### 14.20.2 Deploy Safety Mechanisms

- **Canary traffic shifting** per §11.14.3: 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with CloudWatch alarm gates
- **Feature flags** per §11.14.7: deploy/release separation
- **Database migrations** per §11.6.3: forward-only, pre-deploy step, destructive migrations gated
- **Rollback capability** per §11.14.3: Lambda alias shift (seconds), Fargate service update (minutes), database rollback (rare, via reverse migration)

### 14.20.3 Deploy Time

Target: deploy duration (merge to prod 100% traffic) <90 minutes at v1.

Breakdown:
- Staging deploy + smoke tests: ~15 min
- Manual promote trigger: 0 (instant action when ready)
- Canary traffic shifting: ~60 min (5%/15%/50%/100% with 15-min soak between)
- Post-deploy verification: ~15 min

Faster deploys possible for hotfixes (skip canary staging in emergencies with founder approval).

### 14.20.4 Mobile Release Timeline

Mobile app releases have a different cadence due to App Store/Play Store review:
- **Cadence:** biweekly feature releases; hotfixes as-needed
- **Review time:** Apple 24-48h typical, Google 2-12h typical
- **Expedited review:** Apple offers expedited review for critical bugs; use sparingly
- **Phased rollout:** iOS and Android both offer phased rollout (1% → 10% → 50% → 100% over 7 days); enabled by default

### 14.20.5 Deploy Failure Response

Per §11.14.3:
- Automatic rollback if canary metrics regress
- Manual rollback for founder-detected issues
- Incident logged; post-mortem within 48 hours if rollback was needed

## 14.21 Portability & Interoperability

### 14.21.1 Data Portability

Per §4.11 and §12.4:
- Full data export in CSV + JSON (ZIP archive)
- GDPR Article 20 / CCPA portability requirements met
- Export formats are documented and stable (no user surprises when they open the export)
- Third-party tool import not guaranteed (we don't certify compatibility with specific tools) but formats are standard enough for most tools

### 14.21.2 No Vendor Lock-In For User Data

Users can export their data and leave at any time. Data export is tier-universal (not Pro-gated per §13.3.3). This is an explicit anti-lock-in commitment.

### 14.21.3 API Versioning (Internal)

No public API at v1. Internal API (mobile client ↔ backend) versioning:
- API version is part of the URL path (`/v1/`)
- Breaking changes require a new version number
- Old versions supported for 6 months after successor ships
- Client apps include version-check logic; prompt user to update if on unsupported version

Future public API (v2+): RESTful + stable versioning; OpenAPI spec; rate limits; authentication. Out of scope at v1.

### 14.21.4 File Format Standards

- **CSV exports:** RFC 4180-compliant, UTF-8 encoded, comma-separated, header row
- **JSON exports:** UTF-8, pretty-printed for user readability, ISO 8601 timestamps
- **PDF exports:** PDF/A-compliant where possible for long-term archival compatibility
- **Meal photos:** JPEG, ≤1024×1024, color-managed

Standards compliance enables users to open exports in any standard tool without conversion.

### 14.21.5 Integration Openness

Life Dashboard consumes from open standards:
- OAuth 2.0 for cloud connections
- Open Food Facts for barcode-to-food lookup (open data)
- Open Weather Map (one of the Android weather providers)

Life Dashboard does not provide integration APIs for third parties at v1. Partnerships and integrations are post-$1M ARR considerations.

## 14.22 API Versioning Strategy

### 14.22.1 Current State

Internal REST API on `api.apex-life.app/v1/*`. Mobile client is the only consumer. No public API.

### 14.22.2 Versioning Rules

- **Version in URL path:** `/v1/`, `/v2/`, etc.
- **Breaking changes require new version**: anything that changes request/response shape or semantics
- **Non-breaking additions**: allowed within a version (new optional fields, new endpoints)
- **Deprecation window**: 6 months minimum between deprecation notice and removal

### 14.22.3 Client Compatibility

Mobile clients:
- Include version in User-Agent or custom header
- Prompt user to update if on unsupported version
- Fallback gracefully on minor API changes (forward-compatible parsing)

### 14.22.4 Future Public API

Deferred to v2+ when business justification clear. At that point:
- OpenAPI 3.0 spec as documentation
- Rate limiting per API key
- API keys managed via developer portal
- Tiered access (free for basic, paid for higher volume)
- Webhooks for subscription events

## 14.23 Analytics Events (Quality Telemetry)

Quality-attribute telemetry events:

- `quality.slo_breach` (surface, sli, target, actual, duration_minutes)
- `quality.error_budget_consumed_percent` (category, percent)
- `quality.reliability_sprint_triggered` (trigger, duration_planned)
- `quality.performance_regression` (surface, metric, baseline, current)
- `quality.restore_drill_completed` (success)
- `quality.accessibility_audit_completed` (score, findings_count)
- `quality.data_accuracy_measured` (surface, accuracy_percent, samples)
- `quality.load_test_completed` (target_load, actual_results)
- `quality.test_coverage_snapshot` (component, coverage_percent)
- `quality.flaky_test_quarantined` (test_name, quarantine_duration_days)
- `quality.battery_profile_captured` (platform, typical_user_daily_percent, heavy_user_percent)
- `quality.deploy_completed` (environment, duration_minutes, success)
- `quality.deploy_rolled_back` (trigger)

These events feed quarterly quality reviews (§14.16) and trend analysis.

---

# Section 15 — Out of Scope

Section 15 is the explicit declaration of what Life Dashboard v1 does not include, organized by timeline and category. Out-of-scope items are grouped by when (if ever) they are revisited:

- **v1.1+ deferred** — planned for near-term post-launch releases, explicitly tracked
- **v2+ deferred** — medium-term, requires material expansion or architectural investment
- **Forever out of scope** — excluded by design, not planned to ever ship
- **Explicitly blocked** — impossible due to platform or third-party constraints

Section 15 is not an exhaustive inventory of everything the product could do but doesn't — that list is infinite. It is the authoritative record of items that have been **considered and deliberately excluded**. Items not mentioned here are not necessarily in scope; they simply haven't surfaced as explicit exclusion decisions.

The PRD's default stance is: if a feature isn't specified in §4 or elsewhere, it's not in v1. Section 15 names the items where that absence was a conscious decision rather than an oversight.

## 15.1 Overview & Scope Definition

### 15.1.1 What "Out of Scope" Means

A feature is out of scope at v1 if one or more of these apply:
- The engineering investment exceeds v1 launch timeline capacity
- The feature adds user-experience complexity without clear product-market fit signal
- Dependencies (platform, vendor, regulatory) are not met
- The feature expands the product beyond its four-category structure
- The feature conflicts with locked design principles (§3)

### 15.1.2 v1 Scope Recap

Life Dashboard v1 ships with:
- Four categories (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time) with full scoring
- Overall Score with user-weighted composition
- FAB-launched chatbot overlay as universal input
- 22 goals across categories
- Core deterministic scoring + 16 AI call sites (§10.2)
- iOS + Android native apps via React Native/Expo
- Two subscription tiers: Core $4.99/mo, Pro $9.99/mo
- US primary market with global availability under US law

Everything else is out of scope at v1.

### 15.1.3 Rationale Transparency

Each OOS item has a documented reason. Users, investors, and engineering collaborators should be able to look up any deferred feature and understand why. This prevents re-litigation of settled decisions and preserves institutional memory.

## 15.2 v1.1+ Near-Term Deferred

These items are planned for post-launch releases within the first 12 months. They are not in v1 because launch timeline or specific dependencies constrain them, but they are actively tracked in the roadmap.

### 15.2.1 EU Market Launch

**What:** Full EU/EEA/UK support with local data residency, localized compliance (GDPR, UK GDPR), and EU infrastructure per §11.19.3.

**Why deferred:** 6-8 weeks engineering time for infrastructure + legal counsel for EU entity + localization. Would delay launch materially.

**Revisit:** Targeted for v1.1 (Q3 2026). Until then, EU users accept US-governing-law ToS with SCCs covering cross-border transfer (§12.11.2).

### 15.2.2 Aurora Serverless v2 Migration

**What:** Migration from Neon Postgres to AWS Aurora Serverless v2 per §11.6.4.

**Why deferred:** Neon Pro tier is sufficient for launch-scale operations; migration is operationally disruptive with no user-facing benefit until scale demands.

**Revisit:** Triggered by sustained >70% Neon CPU, >2GB/day storage growth, outages >4h cumulative in 30-day window, or compliance requirement for AWS-native infrastructure.

### 15.2.3 SOC 2 Type 1 Attestation

**What:** Formal SOC 2 Type 1 audit and attestation.

**Why deferred:** Not required at v1 scale; enterprise buyer demand does not exist yet; ~$15-30k audit cost and 6-month prep is premature.

**Revisit:** Triggered by enterprise customer procurement requirement, institutional investor due diligence, or $500k ARR maturity threshold. Pursued in stages — Type 1 first, Type 2 six months later.

### 15.2.4 Lite Tier Experiment

**What:** $2.99/month Lite tier with reduced features — read-only scoring, manual logging only, 3 chatbot queries/day, 1 connected account, 30-day history, no Premium Scan / Pantry / AI email drafts per §13.14.

**Why deferred:** Unproven price elasticity at v1; three-tier structure adds permanent complexity to paywall, marketing, and quota logic for speculative gain.

**Revisit:** Post-launch A/B experiment — Lite offered to 50% of new signups, measured against Core-only control over 90 days. Roll out if total paid signups lift >20% without >15% Core cannibalization; kill otherwise.

### 15.2.5 Additional Languages

**What:** French, German, Italian, Portuguese language support.

**Why deferred:** Professional translation cost + review cycle; no material user base in non-English/Spanish markets until EU launch.

**Revisit:** Co-timed with EU launch (v1.1). Additional languages (Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Polish) triggered by measurable market demand.

### 15.2.6 Referral Program

**What:** User-to-user referral with reward mechanics (e.g., free month for referrer, free month for referee).

**Why deferred:** Speculative machinery without launch-phase WOM data to calibrate reward economics; typical consumer app referral programs activate at 6-12 months post-launch.

**Revisit:** 6 months post-launch based on organic growth signals.

### 15.2.7 Winback & Reactivation Discounts

**What:** Discount offers to recently-churned users (e.g., 50% off for 3 months).

**Why deferred:** No churned-user cohort exists at launch; reactivation funnel tooling is post-launch work.

**Revisit:** 90 days post-launch when first churned cohort is measurable.

### 15.2.8 Student / Military / Educator Discounts

**What:** Verified-identity discounts via SheerID or UNiDAYS.

**Why deferred:** Operational overhead of verification integration; negligible addressable market at launch scale.

**Revisit:** v1.2+ at 25k+ subscriber scale.

### 15.2.9 Chatbot Audit History Deletion

**What:** In-app ability for users to delete specific chatbot audit entries (not the entire history).

**Why deferred:** §4.8.7 (Chatbot Audit) at v1 supports full-history deletion via account settings; per-entry deletion is additional UI.

**Revisit:** Post-launch if user feedback identifies demand.

### 15.2.10 Proactive AI Warnings

**What:** Proactive AI messages identifying potential concerns (e.g., "Your sleep has been trending down — want to talk about it?").

**Why deferred:** Violates §3.2 descriptive AI principle and §3.8 anti-AI-slop stance. All AI is user-invoked at v1.

**Revisit:** Not planned. Would require founder-level reversal of core product principle.

### 15.2.11 Email Style Learning Improvements

**What:** Enhanced AI email-draft style learning from sent emails across more sophisticated pattern detection (tone calibration, recipient-specific style).

**Why deferred:** v1 ships baseline style learning per §4.6.10; advanced patterns require post-launch iteration.

**Revisit:** Informed by Pro user feedback on email draft quality.

### 15.2.12 Apple Family Controls Entitlement Retry

**What:** If Apple rejects the Family Controls entitlement application, Time-category Attention Fragmentation signal is unavailable on iOS until approval.

**Why deferred:** Entitlement approval is Apple's timeline; beyond Life Dashboard's control.

**Revisit:** On Apple's schedule. If rejected, iteration on application with Apple's feedback.

### 15.2.13 Plaid Liabilities & Investments Products

**What:** Additional Plaid products for mortgage/loan data (Liabilities) and brokerage/401k data (Investments) per §4.5.

**Why deferred:** v1 ships with Plaid Transactions only. Additional products have separate Plaid approval processes, additional per-connection costs, and require new category UI.

**Revisit:** v1.1+ targeting Investments (most-requested user feature); Liabilities post-Investments.

### 15.2.14 Home Dashboard Widget

**What:** iOS/Android home screen widget showing Overall Score or category scores without opening the app.

**Why deferred:** Widget implementation is additional surface area not critical for launch. Widget-specific privacy and performance considerations.

**Revisit:** v1.1+ as a user-delight addition.

### 15.2.15 Dark Mode Customization

**What:** Custom dark mode with user-selectable accent colors or themes.

**Why deferred:** System dark mode (iOS + Android native) is supported at v1; custom themes are polish not critical for launch.

**Revisit:** v1.1+ if user demand surfaces.

### 15.2.16 Tabletop DR Exercise Automation

**What:** Quarterly automated DR simulation beyond the monthly restore drill — full scenario testing from §11.13 runbooks.

**Why deferred:** Monthly restore drill covers the most common DR scenario; additional simulation is operational maturity work.

**Revisit:** Year 2.

## 15.3 v2+ Medium-Term Deferred

These items require material expansion — new platforms, new product surfaces, or significant architectural investment. Not planned within first year of operations.

### 15.3.1 Public Web App

**What:** Browser-accessible version of Life Dashboard with dashboard, category views, and settings.

**Why deferred:** Mobile-first strategy locked per §1; web app requires separate codebase (React vs React Native), separate design system adaptation, separate deploy pipeline, cookie compliance layer, and dedicated frontend team.

**Revisit:** v2+ triggered by user demand, partnership opportunities, or specific use case (e.g., desktop power users for Finance).

### 15.3.2 Tablet-Optimized Layouts

**What:** iPad and Android tablet layouts that take advantage of larger screens (multi-column views, expanded charts).

**Why deferred:** v1 ships phone-only-optimized UI; tablets functional but scaled-up. Full tablet optimization is a separate design cycle.

**Revisit:** v2+ based on tablet user population signal.

### 15.3.3 Apple Watch Companion App

**What:** watchOS app for quick score view, rapid meal log, workout start/stop, notifications handling.

**Why deferred:** watchOS development is a distinct platform with separate SDK, UX patterns, and constraints. Not a core v1 deliverable.

**Revisit:** v2+ as a significant product expansion.

### 15.3.4 Wear OS Companion App

**What:** Wear OS app parallel to Apple Watch.

**Why deferred:** Same reasoning as Apple Watch, separate platform investment.

**Revisit:** v2+, likely after Apple Watch (larger user base).

### 15.3.5 macOS Native App

**What:** Dedicated macOS app via SwiftUI or Mac Catalyst.

**Why deferred:** v1 is mobile-only; macOS is closer to web app than to phone app in UX patterns.

**Revisit:** v2+ as part of broader desktop strategy (likely after web app).

### 15.3.6 Chromebook Native Support

**What:** Official Chromebook support with optimized Chromebook UI.

**Why deferred:** Android app runs on Chromebooks via Play Store but with tablet-like caveats; dedicated optimization is v2+ work.

**Revisit:** v2+ based on Chromebook user population.

### 15.3.7 Public API for Third-Party Developers

**What:** Documented REST API with OpenAPI spec, rate-limited tiers, webhooks, developer portal, sandbox environment.

**Why deferred:** v1 API is mobile-client-only, internal, not documented for external use. Public API requires stability commitments, rate-limiting infrastructure, documentation, developer support.

**Revisit:** v2+ as ecosystem expansion — potentially enabling integrations with workflow tools, coaches, or specialty apps.

### 15.3.8 Family Plans

**What:** Multi-user family subscription with shared billing, per-member data privacy, optional family visibility toggles.

**Why deferred:** Family plan pricing structure, cross-user privacy model, payment arrangement all require product design and legal review.

**Revisit:** v2+ at 50k+ subscriber scale when demand is measurable.

### 15.3.9 Team / Business Plans

**What:** Employer-sponsored wellness subscriptions for small teams (10-100 users) with group dashboards, manager visibility (opt-in), billing consolidation.

**Why deferred:** B2B pivot requires different sales motion, legal agreements, and product features.

**Revisit:** v2+ triggered by enterprise customer inbound.

### 15.3.10 Gift Subscriptions

**What:** Ability for user A to purchase a subscription for user B.

**Why deferred:** Apple/Google gift subscription mechanics require explicit platform support; RevenueCat integration for gifts has additional complexity.

**Revisit:** v2+ if identified as growth lever.

### 15.3.11 Asia-Pacific Regional Expansion

**What:** Japan, South Korea, Australia, Singapore market launch with local data residency, localized compliance, regional payment methods.

**Why deferred:** Same architectural investment pattern as EU launch (6-8 weeks per region).

**Revisit:** v2+ post-EU success, based on market opportunity analysis.

### 15.3.12 LATAM Regional Expansion

**What:** Mexico, Brazil, Argentina market launch.

**Why deferred:** Same as APAC.

**Revisit:** v2+.

### 15.3.13 Integrations Marketplace

**What:** Third-party integration catalog within Settings — e.g., Notion sync, IFTTT, Zapier webhook targets, Shortcuts recipes.

**Why deferred:** Requires public API (§15.3.7) as precondition. Ecosystem play, not core product.

**Revisit:** v2+ after public API stability.

### 15.3.14 White-Labeled Corporate Wellness

**What:** Branded Life Dashboard for employers/organizations with custom branding, SSO, admin dashboard.

**Why deferred:** Different business model entirely. Requires B2B sales infrastructure.

**Revisit:** v2+ or v3+ as a parallel business line if identified.

### 15.3.15 Coaching Marketplace

**What:** In-app directory of fitness coaches / nutritionists / financial advisors who can subscribe to coach Life Dashboard users with consent-gated visibility.

**Why deferred:** Two-sided marketplace with vetting, liability, payment routing — substantial product investment.

**Revisit:** v2+ or v3+ as an ecosystem expansion.

### 15.3.16 Advanced Investment Tracking

**What:** Investment portfolio analytics, tax-lot-level tracking, asset allocation recommendations, crypto wallet integration.

**Why deferred:** Life Dashboard's Finance category is designed for cash-flow management, not investment advisory. Advisory features have SEC/FINRA regulatory implications.

**Revisit:** v2+ if Life Dashboard pivots toward finance-dominant positioning.

### 15.3.17 Real-Time Collaboration Features

**What:** Shared goals between users, shared meal logs (e.g., couple's shared fitness goals), accountability partnerships.

**Why deferred:** Social features fundamentally shift product model; privacy-model redesign required.

**Revisit:** v2+ as product direction decision.

### 15.3.18 HITRUST / FedRAMP Attestations

**What:** Formal healthcare (HITRUST) or federal (FedRAMP) compliance attestations.

**Why deferred:** Life Dashboard is consumer product; neither attestation relevant without pivot.

**Revisit:** Only if customer segment materially changes.

### 15.3.19 Enterprise Sales Motion

**What:** Dedicated enterprise sales team, custom contracts, SLA negotiations, implementation services.

**Why deferred:** Consumer product does not require this at v1 or v2 typically.

**Revisit:** v3+ or not at all depending on business direction.

## 15.4 Forever Out of Scope

Items explicitly excluded by design. Not planned for any future version. Would require founder-level reversal of core product principles or business model.

### 15.4.1 Medical Advice, Diagnosis, or Treatment

Life Dashboard is a **general wellness product** per §12.6.2. The product and AI:
- Do not diagnose medical conditions
- Do not prescribe medications or supplements
- Do not provide medical treatment plans
- Do not substitute for healthcare provider consultation
- Disclaim medical decision-making throughout

This exclusion is forever because violation changes regulatory status (FDA medical device regulation, HIPAA, state medical practice laws).

### 15.4.2 Mental Health Diagnosis or Therapy

Life Dashboard may surface emotional wellness patterns via Time-category signals but explicitly does **not:**
- Diagnose mental health conditions
- Provide therapy or counseling
- Replace a licensed therapist
- Make mental-health predictions or interventions

Crisis-signal detection (e.g., patterns suggesting depression) redirects to professional resources with explicit disclaimers, not self-directed interventions.

### 15.4.3 Addiction Recovery Support

Life Dashboard is not a substance use disorder treatment tool. Specific patterns that might suggest addiction concerns are not flagged with clinical framing; patterns are visible to the user neutrally.

### 15.4.4 Ads & Sponsored Content

**No ads ever.** Not in the app, not in notifications, not in email, not on social cards shared to other platforms. Per §1.8 commitment and reinforced in §13.2.5.

Life Dashboard's business model is subscription. This is not a bootstrap choice pending "when we have scale" — it's permanent.

### 15.4.5 Data Brokering / Sale of User Data

Life Dashboard does not and will not sell user data, share data for cross-context advertising, or broker data to third parties. Per §12.3.2 and §6.11. This is written into ToS/Privacy Policy as a binding commitment, not aspirational.

### 15.4.6 Training Other AI Models With User Data

User data sent to Anthropic is covered by Anthropic's commercial terms which exclude use for training their models. User data is also not used to train any other third-party AI model. Per §6.11 DPAs and §12.3.2.

### 15.4.7 Cryptocurrency / Crypto Wallet Integration

Outside Life Dashboard's cash-flow-focused Finance category. Cryptocurrency is speculative asset tracking, not cash-flow management. Excluded unless Life Dashboard pivots toward investment advisory (not planned).

### 15.4.8 Lending / Credit Decisioning

Life Dashboard reads financial data via Plaid; it does not make loans, refer users to lenders, or facilitate credit decisions. This would change regulatory status materially.

### 15.4.9 Money Movement / Payments

Life Dashboard reads financial transactions; it does not move money, transfer funds, pay bills, or initiate transactions. Money movement would trigger money-transmission licensing requirements across ~50 US jurisdictions.

### 15.4.10 Dating / Social Matching

Life Dashboard is not a social or dating product. Not planned to add friend discovery, dating matching, or social graphs of any kind.

### 15.4.11 Gambling or Betting Features

No features that facilitate, encourage, or track gambling activity. Out of scope regardless of user demand.

### 15.4.12 Scoring Users Against Other Users

Life Dashboard's scoring is against a user's own baselines (§9.4) — not against other users, not against demographic peer groups, not leaderboards. Explicit design principle per §3.

### 15.4.13 Social Leaderboards or Competition

No public or semi-public ranking of users. No "how does your score compare to people like you" feature. Comparison anchors a user against their own history, not others.

### 15.4.14 Shaming / Aversive Motivation

Per §3.2 and §3.7 design principles, no shame-based copy, no guilt-inducing notifications, no aversive motivation techniques. Forever.

### 15.4.15 Gamification with Points / Badges / Trophies

Deliberate omission. Scoring is for self-awareness, not game rewards. Streaks exist (§4.x) but are one-shot per-day indicators, not accumulating reward currency. No unlockable achievements, no badges, no trophies.

### 15.4.16 Unsolicited AI Narrative

Per §3.2 and reinforced throughout (removed Archetype call in §4.1, Pattern Insight, Profile Insight) — AI does not speak unless the user asks. This is a core design principle, not a launch-phase limitation.

### 15.4.17 Lifetime Subscription Purchase Option

Subscription-only. No one-time-purchase lifetime access. Lifetime subscriptions undermine recurring revenue economics and create long-tail cost liability.

### 15.4.18 Third-Party Analytics SDKs

Per §6.13, no Amplitude, Mixpanel, Firebase Analytics, Segment, or similar third-party analytics. Self-hosted PostHog only. This is a privacy commitment, not a launch-phase choice.

### 15.4.19 Email Account Syncing Via IMAP

Life Dashboard uses Gmail API and Microsoft Graph for email access — OAuth-mediated, explicit permissions. Never IMAP (which requires storing user's email password, fundamentally worse privacy/security posture).

## 15.5 Category-Specific Out of Scope

### 15.5.1 Fitness Category OOS

**Social Fitness Features:** No friends lists, no shared workout feed, no public workout leaderboards, no commenting on others' workouts. Fitness data is private to the user always.

**Live Workout Streaming:** Life Dashboard does not integrate with live workout streaming services (Peloton live classes, Mirror classes). Logged workouts are the level of abstraction.

**Heart Rate Zone Training Plans:** v1 plans are volume-and-intensity based, not HR-zone-based. HR-zone plan generation deferred if demand emerges.

**Swimming Workout Automatic Parsing:** Swim-specific workout types with lap detection are out of v1 scope; logged as generic cardio with optional notes.

**Climbing Session Tracking:** Climbing-specific metrics (grade, attempts, flashes, redpoints) not tracked at v1. Logged as generic workout.

**Yoga / Flexibility Specific Tracking:** Yoga sessions logged as generic workout without pose-level tracking. Flexibility progress tracking not in v1.

**Team Sports Specific Features:** Basketball / soccer / etc. league-play tracking with opponent stats, wins/losses — not in scope. Logged as generic cardio workout.

**Coach-Client Plan Assignment:** A coach cannot assign a workout plan to a Life Dashboard user at v1. Users generate their own plans via AI or manual entry.

### 15.5.2 Nutrition Category OOS

**Recipe Book / Cookbook:** No saved recipe database with ingredient-level scaling. Saved Meals (§4.4) approximate this at the macro level, not the recipe level.

**Meal Prep Planning:** No multi-day meal prep scheduler with shopping list generation. Scoped out — meal prep is a user's own workflow, not a Life Dashboard feature.

**Grocery List Generation:** No AI-generated grocery lists based on pantry + planned meals. Out of v1 scope.

**Macro Target Cycling:** Different macro targets by day (e.g., "training days" vs "rest days" with different carb targets) not supported at v1. Single daily macro target per user.

**Supplement Tracking:** Vitamin D, fish oil, protein powder, creatine tracking not a dedicated feature at v1. Users can log supplements as meal entries with macros.

**Alcohol-Specific Tracking:** Alcohol consumption is logged as a meal entry (calories + carbs); dedicated alcohol tracking with drinks/units is not in v1.

**Caffeine Tracking:** Caffeine intake not dedicated; can be logged as food entry but not as a tracked metric.

**Water Temperature / Type Differentiation:** Hydration is water volume only; no hot-water/tea/sparkling differentiation.

**Restaurant Menu Database:** No database of restaurant menu items with macros. Users who eat out either use restaurant-provided data, photo-scan, or estimate manually.

**Grocery Receipt Scanning:** No scanning of grocery receipts to populate pantry inventory. Pantry Scanner (Pro) is photo-based on pantry shelves.

### 15.5.3 Finance Category OOS

**Credit Score Tracking:** Life Dashboard does not pull credit reports or track credit scores (requires specific FCRA-regulated integrations).

**Investment Tracking:** Deferred to v1.1+ per §15.2.13.

**Liabilities / Mortgage Tracking:** Deferred to v1.1+ per §15.2.13.

**Retirement Planning / 401k Analysis:** Out of v1 scope. Requires Investments product + retirement-specific calculations.

**Tax Tracking / Tax Optimization:** No integration with tax preparation tools, no tax-loss-harvesting suggestions, no 1099/W-2 aggregation.

**Subscription Detection / Management:** While Life Dashboard sees subscription-pattern transactions, there's no "we detected 27 recurring subscriptions totaling $423/month — want to cancel some?" feature at v1. Bills subsystem detects recurring but doesn't evaluate worth.

**Budget Category Subcategorization:** Budget categories are flat at v1 (Food, Transport, Utilities, etc.), not hierarchical (Food → Groceries → Organic Groceries).

**Business Expense Tracking:** No segregation of personal vs business expenses. Small business users tracking mixed-use accounts will see business expenses mixed into personal budget.

**Receipt Photo Archive:** Receipts not photo-scanned, categorized, or archived within Life Dashboard. Users who want receipt archiving use dedicated tools.

**Currency Conversion for Travel:** Transactions in foreign currencies are shown in account's native currency per Plaid; no auto-conversion for travel abroad tracking.

**Net Worth Over Time Graph (Beyond Basics):** Net worth is a secondary metric with best-effort tracking via Plaid balances at v1; detailed net-worth time-series with historical asset allocation is v1.1+ Investments work.

### 15.5.4 Time Category OOS

**Social Media Activity Tracking:** Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok, Facebook — per §8 confirmed-not-accessible. No social media activity tracking at v1.

**SMS / Text Message Content:** iOS restricted, Android heavily policy-restricted per §8.15. No message content access.

**iMessage Content:** Apple provides zero third-party API access. Forever inaccessible.

**WhatsApp / Messenger Personal Messages:** Meta's API is B2C-only, not consumer-accessible. Forever inaccessible.

**iCloud Mail:** Apple provides no third-party API access. Forever inaccessible at iCloud-level; users with iCloud mail can use Gmail/Outlook integration if they have those accounts.

**Call Log / Voicemail:** Out of scope; iOS restricts, Android heavily restricts.

**Browser History Tracking:** Out of scope; privacy-sensitive even with consent, not core to Time signals.

**Location History Beyond Visits:** Life Dashboard tracks meaningful Location Visits (home, work, recurring places); not continuous GPS trails, not driving routes, not precise movement tracking.

**Voice Note Transcription:** Voice notes recorded in the chatbot are transcribed for meal/workout logging (§4.4, §4.3) but not retained as archived audio artifacts.

**Meeting Transcription:** Meetings are scheduled events from Calendar; Life Dashboard does not transcribe meeting audio.

**Email Drafting from Scratch (Without Reply Context):** AI email drafts respond to a received email. Drafting outbound-initiated emails from scratch is not a v1 feature.

**Calendar Event Auto-Creation from Email:** "Your email mentions a meeting on Tuesday — want to add it to calendar?" — deferred to v1.1+.

**Smart Reply Suggestions:** Short-click-to-send replies (like Gmail Smart Reply) deferred to v1.1+.

**Email Unsubscribe Management:** No "bulk unsubscribe from 24 mailing lists" feature. Out of v1 scope.

**Email Schedule Send:** No scheduled-send for AI-drafted emails. Drafts open in native email client for user to send.

### 15.5.5 Chatbot OOS

**Voice Chatbot Mode:** Text input and voice-to-text for input at v1; no dedicated voice-conversation mode (TTS response spoken back).

**Image Generation:** Chatbot does not generate images at v1.

**Image Interpretation Outside Meals:** Chatbot accepts text input at v1; image input is only via meal scan and pantry scan flows, not as general chatbot attachment.

**Chat History Persistence Beyond Session:** Per §4.7, chat session memory is RAM-only per session, not persisted across chat openings. Users re-start each conversation fresh.

**Multi-Turn Context Window Beyond Session:** Related to above. Each chat opening is a fresh context window.

**File Attachment in Chat:** Chatbot does not accept PDF/DOC attachments.

**Chat Export:** Individual chat logs not exportable at v1 (audit log is exportable per §4.8.7).

**Code Generation:** Chatbot does not generate code. Life Dashboard is not a development tool.

**External Web Search:** Chatbot does not search the web for users. Knowledge is from the user's data + Anthropic's trained knowledge. No browsing tool.

**Chat Groups / Shared Chats:** No multi-user chat sessions.

### 15.5.6 Notifications OOS

**SMS Notifications:** No SMS-based notifications at v1. Platform push only.

**Phone Call Notifications:** No telephonic notifications.

**Per-Notification Snooze Controls:** Notifications can be muted/quieted at category and time level (§4.9), not per-notification-type with custom snooze durations.

**Notification Action Buttons With Deep Logic:** Quick-action buttons on notifications perform simple actions (e.g., "Log meal now"); no complex multi-step flows initiated from lock screen.

**Custom Notification Sounds Per Category:** System notification sounds only at v1; no in-app custom sound selector.

**Notification A/B Testing for Individual Users:** Notification copy is deterministic per category at v1; no personalized-copy A/B.

**Smart Scheduling Beyond Quiet Hours:** Quiet hours block notifications by time range; no learning-based "Rob doesn't respond to notifications between 2-4pm, suppress during that window" — aspirational, v1.1+.

**Email Notifications Beyond Daily Summary:** Daily email summary is the only scheduled email. No ad-hoc email notifications for events.

### 15.5.7 Goals OOS

**Shared Goals Between Users:** Goals are individual at v1. No spouse/partner shared weight-loss goal; no accountability-partner visibility. Per §15.4.13 (no social competition) — individual always.

**Custom User-Created Goals:** v1 ships with a 22-goal library (§4.10). Users can't create arbitrary custom goals at v1. Custom goal templates deferred to v1.1+.

**Goal Coaching From AI:** AI provides context-aware responses when users query about their goal progress (§4.7), but doesn't proactively coach toward goals.

**Goal Recommendations Based on Data:** Life Dashboard doesn't recommend goals ("Based on your data, try the Stronger Deadlift goal") — users pick their own. Avoids prescriptive AI.

**Goal Difficulty Auto-Adjustment:** If user is falling behind, goals don't auto-adjust target values lower. User either pushes through or changes goal manually.

**Multiple Concurrent Weight-Loss Goals:** One active weight-loss goal at a time (conflicting targets would confuse deterministic scoring).

### 15.5.8 Account / Settings OOS

**Account Merging:** Users cannot merge two Life Dashboard accounts at v1. Each user account is independent.

**Account Transfer:** Users cannot transfer an account to another person or email. New user → new account.

**Multi-Tenant Single User:** A single user cannot have "work" and "personal" account toggles within one login at v1. If separation needed, two accounts.

**Email Change Without Re-Verification:** Changing account email requires verification of new email.

**Phone Number as Primary Identity:** Email + password (via Clerk) is the identity model. SMS-only signup not supported.

**Social Login Via Apple/Google/Facebook:** Not at v1 (Clerk supports these if added later). Email/password only.

**Biometric App Lock Beyond Clerk:** Clerk provides device-native biometric auth; no separate PIN-based app lock on top of Clerk.

**Shared Device Mode:** No "this iPad is shared by the family" mode with per-user profiles on same device.

**Parental Controls:** No parent/guardian visibility into child's account (children under 18 not eligible per §6.16 / §12.5).

**App Clip / Instant App Experience:** Full app only at v1.

### 15.5.9 Data / Export OOS

**Continuous Sync to Cloud Drives:** Export is user-triggered; no ongoing auto-sync to Google Drive, Dropbox, iCloud Drive.

**Real-Time API for Personal Use:** Users cannot query their own Life Dashboard data via API at v1 (no public API per §15.3.7).

**Webhook Subscriptions for Personal Events:** Users cannot set up webhooks for their own events (e.g., "POST to my Slack when I complete a workout").

**Zapier / IFTTT Integration:** No Zapier or IFTTT app at v1 (requires public API as precondition).

**Export to Specific Health App Formats:** Export is CSV + JSON in Life Dashboard's schema; not in MyFitnessPal's CSV format, Apple Health's XML format, or other app-specific formats. Users can transform manually.

**Automated Backup to User's S3 / Google Drive:** Backups are operational (Life Dashboard's infrastructure); users can export their data to local device but not push to their own cloud storage automatically.

**Partial Exports:** Export is "everything, once" per §4.11 — not per-category or per-date-range. Simplifies GDPR framing.

### 15.5.10 Subscription / Business OOS

**Free Tier:** No free tier, ever. Subscription or 14-day trial only. Per §13.2.4.

**Ad-Supported Free Option:** No ad-supported tier, ever. Per §15.4.4.

**One-Time Purchase:** Subscription-only. No lifetime purchases. Per §15.4.17.

**Multi-Currency Pricing:** Apple/Google handle regional currency automatically per §13.2.3; Life Dashboard doesn't manually price per-country.

**Annual Subscription With Split-Monthly Billing:** Annual plan is paid upfront annually; not annual-committed-with-monthly-payment.

**Pay-As-You-Go Pricing:** No per-use pricing for AI features. Pricing is flat per tier.

**Volume Discounts for Individual Users:** No individual discount for users who've been paying longer. Price is the same.

**Coupons / Promo Codes at Scale:** Promo codes used sparingly for press/influencer/goodwill per §13.9.5; no mass distribution at v1.

## 15.6 Technical & Infrastructure OOS

### 15.6.1 Multi-Region Active-Active

**What:** Production running simultaneously in multiple AWS regions with automatic failover.

**Why OOS at v1:** Single region per §11.19; cost and complexity of multi-region active-active is a scale-demand decision, not a launch feature.

**Revisit:** Triggered by compliance requirement or scale.

### 15.6.2 Edge Computing / CDN for API

**What:** API served from CloudFront or Cloudflare Workers at edge locations for reduced global latency.

**Why OOS at v1:** API latency from us-east-1 is acceptable for US users; global users are minority population until EU launch.

**Revisit:** v1.1+ with EU launch, or earlier if user-experience data shows latency concerns.

### 15.6.3 GraphQL API

**What:** GraphQL in addition to REST.

**Why OOS:** REST API meets mobile client needs. GraphQL adds complexity without clear benefit for a single internal client.

**Revisit:** Only if public API (v2+) design warrants.

### 15.6.4 Microservices Split

**What:** Break modular monolith into per-category microservices (Fitness service, Nutrition service, etc.).

**Why OOS:** Per §5.3 architecture decision, modular monolith is correct at v1 scale. Microservices premature without >10-person engineering team.

**Revisit:** At 100k+ user scale or team size growth.

### 15.6.5 Database Sharding

**What:** Horizontal sharding of Postgres by user_id.

**Why OOS:** Neon handles v1 scale vertically; Aurora handles v2 scale. Sharding is 500k+ user concern.

**Revisit:** Triggered by Aurora capacity ceiling.

### 15.6.6 Kubernetes / EKS

**What:** Container orchestration beyond Fargate.

**Why OOS:** Fargate is sufficient for single-task worker at v1 scale; EKS adds operational complexity.

**Revisit:** If worker count exceeds ~10 tasks or complex pod-scheduling needs emerge.

### 15.6.7 Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)

**What:** Service-mesh infrastructure for inter-service traffic management.

**Why OOS:** No multi-service traffic to manage at v1.

**Revisit:** Only with microservices split.

### 15.6.8 Self-Hosted LLM

**What:** Run open-weight LLM on our own infrastructure instead of using Anthropic.

**Why OOS:** Cost (GPU infrastructure) and quality (frontier models outperform open-weight at comparable cost points at v1); Anthropic's commercial terms cover privacy needs.

**Revisit:** Only if Anthropic cost escalation or policy change materially affects economics.

### 15.6.9 Dedicated Anthropic Capacity

**What:** Reserved inference capacity from Anthropic via enterprise contract.

**Why OOS:** Not needed at v1 volume; on-demand pricing is sufficient.

**Revisit:** Triggered by >$50k/month Anthropic spend per §11.18.1.

### 15.6.10 Custom AI Model Fine-Tuning

**What:** Fine-tuned Claude model on Life Dashboard-specific data.

**Why OOS:** Anthropic's current fine-tuning offering is limited; privacy implications of training on user data conflict with §15.4.6.

**Revisit:** Only if Anthropic offers enterprise fine-tuning that doesn't require using user data.

## 15.7 Platform Out of Scope

### 15.7.1 Smart TV Apps

Life Dashboard on Apple TV, Fire TV, Roku, Google TV: out of scope. No product-market fit.

### 15.7.2 Alexa / Google Assistant Skills

Voice-assistant integrations: out of scope at v1. Voice interaction via in-app voice-to-text chat only.

### 15.7.3 Browser Extensions

Chrome, Safari, Firefox browser extensions: out of scope. Requires web app as precondition.

### 15.7.4 macOS Menu Bar App

Quick-glance menu bar app for macOS: out of scope. Requires macOS app as precondition.

### 15.7.5 Windows / Linux Native Apps

Not in scope. Mobile-first for v1, web app would extend to other desktops indirectly.

### 15.7.6 Embedded Device Support

Hardware-specific wellness devices (Oura rings, Whoop straps, Garmin watches beyond basic sync): integration via OAuth where available (Garmin per §8 conditional), no native device app development.

## 15.8 Compliance & Regulatory OOS

### 15.8.1 Formal SOC 2 (Deferred Per §15.2.3)

### 15.8.2 ISO 27001 (Deferred)

### 15.8.3 HITRUST (Not Applicable)

### 15.8.4 PCI DSS Level 1 (Not Applicable, Never Will Be)

### 15.8.5 GDPR Formal Certification

Formal GDPR certification via recognized body — not pursued at v1. GDPR compliance is demonstrated through operational controls, not third-party certification.

### 15.8.6 FTC Safe Harbor Programs

Specific FTC safe harbor programs (e.g., TRUSTe self-regulatory) — not pursued. Direct compliance with state/federal laws is sufficient.

### 15.8.7 Age-Verified Child Safety Programs

Dedicated age-verification infrastructure for COPPA parental consent paths — not pursued because under-13 users are excluded, not onboarded (§12.5).

## 15.9 Explicitly Blocked Integrations

These integrations cannot be delivered at v1 due to third-party constraints.

### 15.9.1 iCloud Mail Content

**Why blocked:** Apple provides zero third-party API for iCloud Mail content access. Not a Life Dashboard decision.

**Mitigation:** Users with iCloud accounts can use Gmail or Outlook instead; Life Dashboard integrates with those.

### 15.9.2 iMessage Content

**Why blocked:** Apple provides zero third-party API for iMessage content.

**Mitigation:** None — feature not deliverable without Apple cooperation.

### 15.9.3 WhatsApp Personal Messages

**Why blocked:** Meta's WhatsApp Business API is for business-to-consumer communication; personal WhatsApp account access is not available to third parties.

**Mitigation:** None.

### 15.9.4 Facebook Messenger Personal Messages

**Why blocked:** Same as WhatsApp — Meta does not provide third-party access to personal Messenger content.

**Mitigation:** None.

### 15.9.5 SMS / Text Message Content

**Why blocked:** iOS restricts; Android heavily policy-restricts. Even with user permission, SMS-reading apps face Play Store policy challenges.

**Mitigation:** None for content; metadata (message sent/received patterns) also not tracked for privacy posture.

### 15.9.6 Strava AI-Forwarded Data

**Why blocked:** Strava's November 2024 ToS change prohibits third-party use of user activity data for AI inference.

**Mitigation:** Strava data remains read-only for display and manual review; excluded from AI processing per §6.6.3.

### 15.9.7 Personal Instagram / Twitter / TikTok Activity

**Why blocked:** None of these platforms provide third-party APIs for personal activity data. Even public profile scraping is against ToS.

**Mitigation:** None.

## 15.10 OOS Review Cadence

Out-of-scope decisions are revisited:

- **Quarterly:** v1.1+ deferred items reviewed for promotion to in-scope based on roadmap and user feedback
- **Semi-Annually:** v2+ items reviewed for timeline signal (user demand, competitive landscape, scale triggers)
- **Annually:** Forever-OOS items reviewed only if fundamental business/product assumptions have changed
- **On Trigger:** specific events (Plaid Liabilities approval, EU launch decision, enterprise customer inbound) trigger immediate re-evaluation of related OOS items

Review outcomes are documented. Items promoted from OOS to in-scope have specific PRD updates; items confirmed as OOS after review have the confirmation date noted so the decision doesn't get silently re-litigated.

---

# Section 16 — Appendices

Section 16 consolidates reference material from across the document into navigable quick-reference form. Appendices are compact: each cross-references the authoritative section for full detail rather than duplicating content.

## Appendix A — Glossary

**Archetype** — Original onboarding AI call (killed in v1.18). Structurally AI slop per §3.2. Do not reintroduce.

**Attention Fragmentation** — Time Score signal (§4.6.15) measuring how scattered attention is across the day via Screen Time data (app-switching frequency, session length distribution).

**Baseline** — User's personal 30-day rolling median for a signal, replacing population defaults after 14-day warmup per §9.5.

**Calibration** — The 14-day onboarding warmup during which scores use population defaults rather than personal baselines.

**Canary** — Deployment pattern (§11.14.3) where new Lambda version gets 5% of traffic before progressive shift to 100%.

**Cardio** — Fitness subsystem covering heart-rate-driving activity (running, cycling, rowing, etc.).

**Category** — One of Life Dashboard's four top-level product areas: Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time.

**Chatbot Audit** — User-facing transparency surface (§4.8.7) showing all chatbot queries with full prompt + response for 30 days.

**Circuit Breaker** — Vendor-failure response pattern (§10.10.3) halting calls to a failing external service for a cooldown period.

**Connection** — User-authorized integration with a third-party data source (HealthKit, Plaid, Gmail, etc.).

**Container** (Chatbot) — Structured data context block (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time) loaded into chatbot queries per relevance.

**Core** — Life Dashboard's $4.99/month subscription tier.

**Cumulative numeric** — Goal type (§4.10) where progress accumulates toward a target value.

**Day Detail** — Full-screen view (§4.2a) of a specific past date with all logged data and scores.

**Day Timeline** — Visual representation of a day's events (§4.6) combining calendar, tasks, location, and screen time.

**Deficit** (Calorie) — Negative daily balance between calories consumed and TDEE, tracked in §4.4.

**Descriptive AI** — AI that narrates what already exists in the data. Permitted per §3.2 when user-invoked.

**Deterministic scoring** — The core scoring architecture (§9) — no AI in scoring math, no probabilistic outputs, fully reproducible calculations.

**DLQ (Dead-Letter Queue)** — SQS queue receiving messages that exceed max receive count, for later investigation.

**DPA (Data Processing Agreement)** — Legal contract between Life Dashboard and a third-party processor governing data handling per §12.12.

**DSAR (Data Subject Access Request)** — User exercise of privacy rights (access, deletion, correction, portability) per §12.4.

**Engaged user** — Cohort in §13.7 unit economics (20% of subscribers; 65% of heavy user's AI spend).

**Event signal** — Score signal (§9) that fires instantly on occurrence without proration (workout happened, bill paid, weight logged).

**FAB** — Floating Action Button. Life Dashboard's universal input mechanism (§4.2.6) opening the chatbot overlay with shortcut buttons.

**Finance** — Category for cash-flow tracking: Budget, Spending, Bills, Savings per §4.5.

**Fitness** — Category covering Body, Strength, Cardio, Movement, Sleep, Recovery, Plan subsystems per §4.3.

**Golden test** — Test case with expected output used to detect regressions in AI calls (§10.15) or scoring (§9.15.1).

**Grace period** — 30-day account-deletion window (§4.11) during which a user can restore.

**Graceful degradation** — System continues functioning at reduced capability when a dependency fails; §5.15 / §10.10 document paths.

**HealthKit** — Apple iOS framework for health/fitness data (§8).

**Heavy user** — Cohort in §13.7 unit economics (10% of subscribers; top AI spend).

**Home Dashboard** — Main landing screen (§4.2) after app open.

**Hot storage** — Tier 1 storage for recent user data (90-day window), fast-access Postgres per §5.

**IaC (Infrastructure as Code)** — AWS CDK in TypeScript per §11.4; infrastructure defined as code.

**Intentionality** (Location) — Time Score signal measuring how purposeful location visits are per §4.6.15.

**KMS (Key Management Service)** — AWS encryption key management per §7 / §11.9.3.

**Life Dashboard** — The product. Legal entity is Apex Leadership LLC.

**Lite** — $2.99/month tier deferred to v1.1+ A/B experiment per §13.14.

**MAU (Monthly Active User)** — User who opens the app at least once in a calendar month.

**Meal Scan** — AI-powered meal photo analysis (§4.4, §10.3.2). Standard (Sonnet) in Core; Premium (Opus) in Pro.

**Mifflin-St Jeor** — RMR formula (§4.1, §4.4) used as one of two RMR calculation methods.

**MODE** — System prompt header (§10.6) declaring call site purpose (descriptive/prescriptive/parsing/etc.).

**Momentum** — Original Flask scoring system. Replaced by the deterministic penalty-from-perfect-day architecture in §9.

**NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)** — Calorie burn from non-workout movement (§4.4); contributes to TDEE.

**Non-functional requirement** — Quality-attribute specification per §14 (performance, reliability, accessibility, etc.).

**Nutrition** — Category for food/hydration tracking per §4.4.

**OAuth** — Authorization protocol for cloud connections (Gmail, Outlook, Google Calendar, etc.).

**Outbox** — Client-side offline-first write queue per §5.3.

**Overall Score** — Weighted composite of four category scores (§9.8) with user-adjustable weights in range [5, 55] per category.

**P1 / P2 / P3 / P4** — Incident severity tiers per §11.16.2.

**Pantry Scanner** — Pro-tier feature (§4.4, §10.3.12) photo-identifying pantry ingredients.

**Pattern Insight** — Original AI narrative (killed — structurally slop per §3.2). Do not reintroduce.

**Penalty-from-perfect-day** — Score architecture (§9.3) where each category starts at 100 and subtracts penalties for missing signals.

**Period count** — Goal type (§4.10) counting events within a rolling window.

**Personal baseline** — See Baseline.

**Plaid** — Financial data aggregator (§8.11) providing bank/card transactions, balances.

**PRD (Product Requirements Document)** — This document.

**Premium Scan** — Opus-powered Pro-tier meal photo analysis (§4.4, §10.3.3).

**Prescriptive AI** — AI that recommends action. Restricted per §3.2 to user-invoked chatbot contexts.

**Pro** — Life Dashboard's $9.99/month subscription tier.

**Profile Insight** — Original onboarding AI narrative (killed in v1.20). Do not reintroduce.

**Profile Reveal** — Post-onboarding moment (§4.1.5) showing calculated RMR, macros, archetype-free.

**Proration** — Score adjustment (§9.6) for partial-day completion of accumulating signals.

**RCA (Root Cause Analysis)** — Post-incident review deliverable.

**RevenueCat** — Subscription management and cross-platform entitlement (§13.5).

**Restore drill** — Monthly automated backup restoration test (§11.12.4).

**RMR (Resting Metabolic Rate)** — Calories burned at rest; dual-formula computation (Mifflin-St Jeor primary, Katch-McArdle if body fat % available) per §4.1.

**ROPA (Record of Processing Activities)** — GDPR Article 30 requirement; Life Dashboard maintains lightweight ROPA per §12.13.

**RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — Maximum acceptable data loss in DR event. Life Dashboard target: 1 hour.

**RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — Maximum acceptable recovery time. Life Dashboard target: 4 hours.

**Rhythm Adherence** — Time Score signal (§4.6.15) measuring consistency of daily routine.

**SCC (Standard Contractual Clauses)** — Legal mechanism for EU data transfer outside EEA per §12.11.

**Schedule Density Balance** — Time Score signal measuring meeting load distribution per §4.6.15.

**Screen Time** — Time category data source (§4.6) — iOS Family Controls + Android UsageStatsManager.

**SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** — Not implemented at v1; monitoring via CloudWatch + Sentry + Axiom.

**Signal** — Input to scoring engine per §9.3. Can be accumulating, event, or trailing-window type.

**SLO (Service Level Objective)** — Internal reliability target per §14.2.

**SLI (Service Level Indicator)** — Measurable signal corresponding to an SLO per §14.3.

**Sleep Regularity** — Time Score signal measuring consistency of sleep timing per §4.6.15.

**SOC 2** — Compliance attestation; deferred per §12.17.1.

**Streak** — Goal type (§4.10) counting consecutive days/weeks of success.

**Strength** — Fitness subsystem covering weightlifting (§4.3.3).

**TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure)** — RMR + NEAT + TEF + workout burns (§4.4).

**TEF (Thermic Effect of Food)** — ~10% of calories consumed burned in digestion (§4.4).

**Time** — Fourth Life Dashboard category (renamed from "Life" in v1.18) covering Calendar, Email, Tasks, Screen Time, Location per §4.6.

**Tiered proration** — Score proration differentiated by signal type (accumulating vs event vs trailing-window) per §9.6.

**Trailing-window signal** — Score signal using rolling N-day window (e.g., 7-day strength volume, 30-day savings rate) per §9.3.

**Training Plan** — Fitness subsystem covering AI-generated workout plans (§4.3.8).

**Warmup** — See Calibration.

**WCAG 2.1 AA** — Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.1, Level AA. Life Dashboard's accessibility commitment per §12.8 / §14.12.

**Workout Checklist Overlay** — Strength workout execution screen (§4.3.5a) preserving Flask PWA pattern.

**Blended monthly AI cost** (per §13.7.1–2): Core ~$0.77/user blended, Pro ~$4.97/user blended.

## Appendix E — Database Tables Inventory

Consolidated from §5.5, §6, §9, §10.8, §11. Authoritative per respective section.

**User identity & subscription:**
- `users` — user profile, state (active / deletion_pending)
- `subscription_events` — RevenueCat webhook log
- `account_deletion_state` — per-§4.11

**Fitness domain:**
- `body_weight_logs`, `body_measurements`
- `strength_workouts`, `strength_sets`, `exercise_library`, `personal_records`
- `daily_movement`, `sleep_logs`, `recovery_logs`
- `training_plans`, `plan_adherence`

**Nutrition domain:**
- `meals` (rich schema per §4.4)
- `saved_meals`, `custom_products`
- `hydration_logs`
- `suggestion_feedback`

**Finance domain:**
- `plaid_tokens` (encrypted), `connected_accounts`
- `transactions`, `bills`, `user_budget`
- `account_balances_history`
- `manual_assets_debts`
- `user_category_rules`

**Time domain:**
- `calendar_events_cache`, `tasks`
- `email_metadata_cache` (no bodies stored)
- `screen_time_daily_rollup`
- `location_visits`, `location_place_labels`

**Goals:**
- `goal_library` (server config, cached client-side)
- `user_goals` (active + archived)
- `goal_progress_snapshots`

**Scoring (§9):**
- `daily_scores` (immutable snapshots per tier retention)
- `user_score_baselines`
- `user_score_weights`
- `score_cache_invalidations`
- `raw_deltas` (Settings → Advanced only)

**AI observability (§10.8):**
- `ai_call_log` — every AI call, model, tokens, cost, latency, response code

**Notifications:**
- `notification_signals` (rolling signal pool per §4.9)
- `notification_log` (dispatched notifications audit)

**Chatbot:**
- `chatbot_audit` — per-§4.8.7, 30 days retention both tiers

**Privacy & consent:**
- `user_consents` — per-source consent state per §6.4
- `user_redaction_state` — redaction ruleset per §6.6

**Feature flags & config:**
- `feature_flags`

**Cost & analytics:**
- `cost_events` — per-user/vendor/feature cost attribution per §5.12

**Export & deletion:**
- `user_export_state`, `export_jobs`
- `deletion_audit` (anonymous post-purge record)

**Compliance:**
- DSAR response records (3-year retention)
- `subprocessor_changes` (Notion, not DB)

## Appendix F — Scoring Engine Quick Reference

Authoritative math in §9.

**Architecture:** Penalty-from-perfect-day. Each category score starts at 100; penalties subtract based on signal deltas from target.

**Overall Score:** Weighted composite of Fitness + Nutrition + Finance + Time. User weights sum to 100, each weight in range [5, 55], default 25 each.

**Signal types:**
- **Accumulating** — prorated by time of day (calorie intake vs target, steps vs goal, screen time vs typical)
- **Event** — instant on occurrence (workout logged, bill paid, weight entered)
- **Trailing-window** — rolling window (7-day strength volume, 30-day savings rate, 14-day sleep rhythm)

**Piecewise-linear normalization:**
- Symmetric (over/under equally penalized — e.g., calorie target)
- Asymmetric (directional — e.g., protein shortfall penalized, excess isn't)
- Ceiling (binary threshold — e.g., bill paid)

**Baselines:**
- Days 0–13 post-signup: population defaults
- Day 14+: user's 30-day rolling median replaces population defaults

**Graceful degradation:** missing data → reduced max score, not zero. Minimum data thresholds per signal per §9.7.

**Cache:** 15-minute TTL in Redis; historical scores computed once at 03:30 UTC nightly and locked immutable (7 days Core / 30 days Pro).

**Retroactive recomputation cap:** 7 days if recompute would incur AI cost (v1.18 decision).

**Scoring outputs:**
- `category_score` (0-100) per category per day
- `overall_score` (0-100) per day
- `momentum_breakdown` — deterministic component scores debug
- `daily_scores` table row per user per day

## Appendix G — Goal Library Quick Reference

22 goals in v1 per §4.10.3. Full specs in §4.10.

**v1.27 unification:** all 22 goals below are first-class goals in the unified goal system (§4.10.2) — tracked with identical rigor regardless of category. Fitness body-composition goals (FIT-01) additionally drive calorie and macro target recomputation as a deterministic side effect when designated as the user's primary fitness goal. All other goals (FIT-02 through FIT-06, all Nutrition, Finance, Time goals) track progress without affecting user settings. A user can hold multiple fitness goals simultaneously (FIT-01 + FIT-02 + FIT-06 all active) within their tier's active-goal cap.

| ID | Category | Name | Type | Data source | Affects calorie math |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FIT-01 | Fitness | Reach goal weight | Cumulative numeric | HealthKit / manual | ✓ (when primary) |
| FIT-02 | Fitness | Hit new squat PR | Best attempt | Strength workout logs | — |
| FIT-03 | Fitness | Hit new bench PR | Best attempt | Strength workout logs | — |
| FIT-04 | Fitness | Hit new deadlift PR | Best attempt | Strength workout logs | — |
| FIT-05 | Fitness | Complete N workouts this month | Period count | Workout logs | — |
| FIT-06 | Fitness | N-week workout consistency | Streak | Workout logs | — |
| NUT-01 | Nutrition | Protein streak | Streak | Meal logs | — |
| NUT-02 | Nutrition | Calorie target streak | Streak | Meal logs | — |
| NUT-03 | Nutrition | Log every meal streak | Streak | Meal logs | — |
| NUT-04 | Nutrition | 30-day protein average | Rate | Meal logs | — |
| NUT-05 | Nutrition | Alcohol-free streak | Streak | Meal logs | — |
| FIN-01 | Finance | Emergency fund | Cumulative numeric | Plaid (savings account) | — |
| FIN-02 | Finance | Pay off credit card | Cumulative numeric (reducing) | Plaid (credit card) | — |
| FIN-03 | Finance | Save for specific target | Cumulative numeric | Plaid (designated account) | — |
| FIN-04 | Finance | Monthly spending limit | Period count (reducing) | Plaid transactions | — |
| FIN-05 | Finance | Budget streak | Streak | Budget + transactions | — |
| TIME-01 | Time | Task completion streak | Streak | Life Dashboard tasks | — |
| TIME-02 | Time | Screen time target | Streak | Screen time | — |
| TIME-03 | Time | Social media cap | Streak | Screen time | — |
| TIME-04 | Time | Phone-down after X:00 | Streak | Screen time | — |
| TIME-05 | Time | Focus time per week | Period count | Calendar + screen time | — |
| TIME-06 | Time | Location visit target | Streak | Location visits | — |

**Goal slots (v1.27):** 3 active for Core, 6 active for Pro. Completed goals move to Archive. Goal library is server-side config (`goal_library` table) updatable without app release.

## Appendix H — Notification Categories Quick Reference

Authoritative per §4.9.3. User-configurable per category per §4.9.9.

| Signal category | Purpose | Default state |
|---|---|---|
| Workout streak at risk | Reminds user to log workout before streak breaks | ON |
| Calorie balance drift | Alerts mid-afternoon if on track to overshoot daily target | OFF (opt-in) |
| Protein target gap | End-of-day reminder if protein target not met | OFF (opt-in) |
| Bill due reminder | 3 days before auto-detected recurring bill | ON |
| Budget breach | Weekly budget category exceeded | ON |
| Large transaction | Transaction > 2× category baseline | ON |
| Goal celebration | Goal completed | ON |
| Goal at risk | Goal pace falling behind with deadline in ≤7 days | ON |
| Calendar event reminder | 15 minutes before high-importance event | ON |
| Task due | Task marked due today not yet completed | ON |
| Screen time cap hit | Category exceeded user-set limit | OFF (opt-in) |
| Location-based nudge | Rare; tied to user-configured geofence goals | OFF (opt-in) |
| Weekly Monday summary | Weekly email summary (Pro free, Core counts against quota) | Configurable |
| Daily email summary | Daily email summary if enabled | Configurable |

**Aggressiveness levels (§4.9.6):** Minimal / Balanced / High. Default Balanced. Quiet hours honored per §4.9.10.

**AI Evaluator (§4.9.5):** Single Haiku call per signal candidate evaluates against user's recent patterns before dispatch. Cost ~$3/user/year per §13.

## Appendix I — US Privacy Law Coverage Map

Unified baseline (§12.3.1) satisfies all states below.

| State | Law | Effective | Life Dashboard compliance mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| California | CCPA (amended by CPRA) | 2020 / 2023 | Unified baseline + Privacy Policy disclosures |
| Virginia | CDPA | 2023 | Unified baseline |
| Colorado | CPA | 2023 | Unified baseline + appeal mechanism |
| Connecticut | CTDPA | 2023 | Unified baseline + appeal mechanism |
| Utah | UCPA | 2023 | Unified baseline |
| Texas | TDPSA | 2024 | Unified baseline + appeal mechanism |
| Oregon | OCPA | 2024 | Unified baseline |
| Montana | MCDPA | 2024 | Unified baseline |
| Florida | FDBR | 2024 | Unified baseline |
| Delaware | DPDPA | 2025 | Unified baseline |
| Washington | MHMDA (health) | 2024 | Dedicated MHMDA-compliant posture per §12.6.3 |

**DSAR SLA:** 30 days, exceeds all state maxima (45-day typical).

**No-sale, no-sharing, no-targeted-advertising** commitment satisfies opt-out right for all states without requiring user-facing toggle.

**Non-discrimination:** export is Core-available (not Pro-gated) — key compliance decision ensuring subscription tier does not condition data access.

**Appeals mechanism:** `privacy@apex-life.app` with "appeal" subject line; 60-day review window per §12.4.6.

## Appendix J — Key SLO & Target Summary

Consolidated from §14.2–§14.6. Internal targets (no public SLAs).

**Availability (monthly rolling):**
- API tier: 99.5%
- Mobile critical paths: 99.5%
- Background workers: 99%
- Export generation: 98%
- Data durability: 100% (zero tolerance)

**Latency (p95 unless noted):**
- API non-AI endpoints: <500ms
- Dashboard cold open: <500ms
- Chatbot first token: <1.5s
- Meal photo scan (standard): <8s
- Meal photo scan (Premium): <15s
- Score computation (fresh): <500ms

**Error rates:**
- API 5xx: <0.1%
- Mobile crash-free: >99.5%
- Background job failure: <2%
- AI call failure: <3%

**DR targets:**
- RTO: 4 hours
- RPO: 1 hour

**Deployment:**
- Daily manual promote first 90 days → continuous deploy after
- Deploy duration end-to-end: <90 minutes
- Canary progression: 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%

**Scaling envelope (§5.16):**
- 50k users / 20k DAU / 200k AI queries/day at v1
- 3-5x headroom (up to 250k users) without re-architecture

**Cost projections (§11.11):**
- Launch (~1k users): ~$450/month total
- 10k users: ~$16,200/month (92% AI)
- 50k users: ~$70,200/month (AI-dominated)

**Unit economics (§13.7):**
- Core blended contribution: $2.98/month (70% of net)
- Pro blended contribution: $2.92/month (34% of net)

# Change Log

**2026-04-22 — v1.27** — Migration-surfaced update resolving three product decisions and three documentation bugs. No new AI calls, no new tables, no new integrations, no new tier cut-lines beyond the explicit goal-slot adjustment.

**DECISION 1 — Unified goal system.** The v1.18-era split between "Primary Goal" (body-composition configuration driving calorie math) and "Library Goals" (discrete catalog goals) is collapsed into a single unified goal system. There is ONE goal system at v1.27. Every goal — fitness body-comp, strength PR, streak, finance cumulative, time streak — is a first-class goal object tracked with identical rigor: progress calculation, pace indicators, deadline handling, completion celebration, auto-restart where applicable. Fitness body-composition goals (FIT-01 at v1) drive calorie and macro target recomputation as a deterministic side effect when designated as the user's primary fitness goal; all other goals have zero side effect on user settings. A user can hold multiple fitness goals simultaneously (Reach goal weight + Hit new squat PR + 6-week workout consistency) within their tier's active-goal cap, with at most one fitness body-comp goal marked as primary-fitness-goal for calorie math purposes. **Goal slot counts updated: Core 3 active goals, Pro 6 active goals** (up from Pro's prior 3-goal cap). Core stays at 3 to preserve focus; Pro's increase to 6 reflects that unifying the system makes it natural for engaged users to carry more goals concurrently (fitness PR goal + fitness streak + finance goal + time goal at once). Library catalog unchanged at 22 goals — unification is a framing change, not a catalog expansion. Onboarding §4.1.5a reframed: user picks "first goal" from a fitness-focused pre-filter (because calorie settings need a fitness body-comp goal to compute non-maintenance targets); picking a non-fitness goal or skipping entirely leaves the calorie engine on maintenance defaults. Calorie recomputation flows are deterministic — no AI call fires from goal creation, editing, or primary-fitness-goal toggle. Retroactive edits applied: §4.10.1 Overview reframed with unification note; §4.10.2 rewritten as "The Unified Goal System"; §4.10.3 intro corrected to "22 goals total" (from stale "24 goals") with unification context; §4.10.10 Active Goal Slots rewritten for 3/6 tier caps and primary-fitness-goal designation; §4.10.19 Core vs Pro table updated with active-goal counts and fitness body-comp side effect row; §4.10.8 at-cap paywall copy updated (3→4 trigger for Core, 6→7 trigger for Pro); §4.1.5a First Goal Selection fully rewritten for unified model with per-goal-type calorie math implications table; §4.1.1 Overview gains first-time-only clarification; §4.2.2 Home Dashboard goal strip principles updated for 3/6 caps; §13.3.1 Core goal row preserved at 3; §13.3.2 Pro gains "6 active goals" line; §4.8.5 plan comparison table gains "Active goals" row (3 / 6); Appendix G intro rewritten with unification note + new "Affects calorie math" column marking FIT-01 as the sole side-effect goal.

**DECISION 2 — Sectional direct-edit settings, no "redo onboarding".** §4.8.4 Profile Section fully rewritten to reframe the five "Re-do onboarding" flows (About You / Goal / Daily Life / Workout Plan / Connections) as **direct-edit sections** within Profile rather than re-capture wizards. The change: onboarding is first-time-only; individual editable sections handle post-onboarding state changes with minimum-necessary computation per edit. New Profile section structure: Identity block → Your Plan (static onboarding snapshot) → Body Stats (direct edit, deterministic RMR/TDEE/macros recompute) → Daily Life (direct edit, deterministic NEAT/TDEE/macros recompute) → Diet Preferences (direct edit, flag for next AI profile regeneration but NO automatic AI call) → Goals row (deep-link to §4.10 Goals screen) → Advanced overrides (direct edit with Lock toggle) → Regenerate AI profile map (explicit standalone action with confirmation modal: "This will use 1 AI call and update your personalization profile"). Goal editing is removed from Settings entirely — goals live in §4.10 exclusively. Workout plan editing is removed from Settings — handled in Fitness tab (§4.3.8). Connections management stays as a direct-link from main Settings screen (§4.8.6). **No AI calls fire from any direct-edit section** — only the explicit "Regenerate AI profile map" action triggers AI. §4.1.1 Overview gains first-time-only clarification referencing §4.8.4 as the post-onboarding edit surface.

**DECISION 3 — Deterministic email-importance classifier formalized.** §4.6.8 deterministic 3-signal classifier rewritten with exact v1.27 locked math. Signal weights, defaults, and thresholds pinned: Signal 1 (Sender reputation, clamped to [-2.0, +2.0], +1.0/−1.0 sender increment, +0.5/−0.5 domain half-credit on Mark Important/Mark Stream); Signal 2 (Content cues, new 15-entry Boost and 15-entry Demote default seeds updated — Boost: urgent/tomorrow/meeting/interview/offer/contract/invoice/deadline/action required/RSVP/flight/appointment/confirmation/canceled/reschedule; Demote: unsubscribe/deal/newsletter/trending/recommended for you/limited time/sale/% off/you might like/weekly digest/reminder to check/invitation to/upgrade/beta/feedback; +0.3 per boost match / −0.3 per demote match, per-email cap ±1.0 to prevent keyword-stuffing exploitation); Signal 3 (Thread behavior with three sub-signals — prior reply +0.5, direct address +0.3 when recipients ≤3 / −0.2 when CC with >5 recipients, bulk-headered mass mailings −0.4 via List-Unsubscribe header detection; output range −0.6 to +0.8). Final: sum clamped to [-3.0, +3.0]; threshold ≥1.0 → Important, else Stream. Ties at exactly 1.0 are Important (previous spec was "sign-based" which treated 0 as Stream — new spec is explicit threshold). Cold-start behavior documented: new user leans on Signals 2 and 3 for week 1; Signal 1 builds naturally as user marks senders; conservative-at-cold-start by design. "Why this classification?" breakdown formalized with display format showing per-signal contribution and total. Keyword list editing confirmed as Core-tier (no Pro gate) and added to §13.3.3 equal-across-tiers list. Zero AI calls — no change to §10 inventory.

**BUG 1 (§3.2 prescriptive AI overreach).** Paragraph rewritten. Prior framing claimed prescriptive AI "banned everywhere in v1" with a single Notifications exception, which contradicted §4.7 chatbot's legitimate prescriptive user-invoked output ("should I work out today?"). v1.27 framing: prescriptive AI banned as an UNSOLICITED surface with two explicit exceptions — (1) Notifications (user opted in via permission), (2) AI Chatbot when user directly invokes prescriptive output with a prescriptive question. The chatbot exception does not extend to offering prescriptive content when the user asked a descriptive question; chatbot matches the mode of the user's query.

**BUG 2 (§3.8 stale 3+ accounts language).** Two bullets in §3.8 "Margins Are A Product Decision" still referenced the pre-v1.18 multi-account gate ("cloud breadth beyond the typical case — 3+ email/calendar accounts — is packaged into Pro").
