These contradicted the v1.21 locked §13.3.1 unlimited-accounts-at-all-tiers decision. Both bullets rewritten to reflect unlimited accounts at both tiers; tier differentiation comes from AI feature richness, history depth, and formatted export (not account breadth). Cost discipline for cloud accounts is handled at the AI gateway redaction layer and configurable per-account sync cadence, not at an account-count gate.

**BUG 3 (§9.6.1 Sleep signal clarification).** Fitness Sleep signal and Time Sleep Regularity signal both source from HealthKit but measure non-overlapping statistics. Clarifying note added under §9.6.1 baseline table explaining: Fitness Sleep measures quantity + quality (duration, efficiency, deep/REM, HRV overnight) via 30-day rolling median; Time Sleep Regularity measures timing consistency (variance in bedtime and wake time) via 14-day rolling standard deviation. Both count toward their respective category scores without double-counting. Three worked examples documented (stable nights at consistent times, stable nights at variable times, erratic nights at consistent times).

**Appendix K updated** with v1.27 row. No TOC changes required. No new subsections. Version bumped v1.26 → v1.27.

**v1.27 follow-up resolutions (same date, inline fixes to open questions surfaced by the v1.27 diff summary — not new decisions):** The eight open questions flagged at v1.27 draft completion have been resolved inline. (1) §4.8.4 reference to plan editing corrected from §4.3.8 to §4.3.10 with explicit call-out of Build/Edit/Switch Plan coverage in the Training Plan detail view. (2) §4.10.10 adds archival-transition UX for the primary fitness goal — 48-hour maintenance-target banner when user archives their only fitness body-comp goal, celebration takes priority over transition UX on completion. (3) §4.8.4 adds `profile_map_out_of_sync` flag mechanics (boolean + reason string on user record; Diet Preferences edits set TRUE; successful Regenerate clears; auto-clears when current state matches last-regenerated snapshot; no other edits set the flag). (4) §9.12.6 added, specifying mid-day target change behavior — new target takes effect from midnight user-local of the change day; prior days governed by §9.12.5 immutability; cache invalidation on change; symmetric treatment for primary-fitness-goal archival. (5) §4.6.8 multi-word keyword phrase matching clarified — phrase matches as single contiguous token with word boundaries at phrase start and end; special-character phrases (`% off`) match literally. (6) §4.6.8 bulk-mailing header detection list formalized with concrete RFC-anchored headers (List-Unsubscribe RFC 2369/8058, List-ID RFC 2919, Precedence:bulk/list/junk, X-Bulk, X-Auto-Response-Suppress, X-Mailer mass-distribution indicator list, Feedback-ID RFC 6591). (7) §4.2.2 goal strip rewritten for multi-goal density — 0/1/2-3/4-6 goal count variants with ~130pt cards, horizontal scroll for Pro 4-6 case, iPhone SE fallback to single-card-visible-with-scroll, aggressive name truncation at 6-goal density, accessibility targets preserved. (8) §4.10.17 goals schema updated with `is_primary_fitness_goal` (replacing prior `is_primary`) and `affects_calorie_math` columns; partial unique constraint ensures at most one primary fitness goal per user; `affects_calorie_math` is immutable after insert, copied from `goal_library.affects_calorie_math`. Stale §4.10.17 "Updates to `user_goals`" subsection rewritten — the Flask `user_goals` table is migrated into the unified `goals` table (not renamed to a separate `user_primary_goal` table as prior drafts implied). No version bump — these are clarifications of v1.27 intent, not new product decisions.

**2026-04-21 — v1.26** — Section 16 (Appendices) drafted in full, 12 appendices (A through L), ~700 lines. Section 16 is compilation work — consolidating reference material from across the document into navigable quick-reference form without duplicating authoritative content. **Appendices:** (A) Glossary — ~90 defined domain terms used throughout the PRD, covering product vocabulary (Archetype, Momentum, NEAT, TDEE, Penalty-from-perfect-day, MODE, FAB), technical terms (CDK, DLQ, KMS, PITR, IaC), scoring concepts (baseline, proration, event signal, trailing-window signal), and regulatory terms (DSAR, ROPA, SCC, DPO, MHMDA). (B) Abbreviations — ~60 acronyms and initialisms with expansions. (C) AI Call Inventory Quick Reference — compact 16-row table per §10.3 with model, mode, tier, quota, per-call cost, and authoritative section pointer. (D) Integration Catalog Quick Reference — compact 22-row table per §8.3 covering device-native (HealthKit, EventKit, Family Controls, Core Motion, WeatherKit, Health Connect, UsageStatsManager, Android Geofencing, Android Calendar Provider), cloud/OAuth (Google Calendar, Gmail, Outlook Mail/Calendar, Strava), vendor-metered (Plaid, Google Places, Anthropic), and infrastructure (Clerk, RevenueCat, Expo, SendGrid) with launch blockers noted. (E) Database Tables Inventory — consolidated list across §5.5, §6, §9, §10.8, §11 covering user identity/subscription, fitness domain, nutrition domain, finance domain, time domain, goals, scoring (§9 tables: daily_scores, user_score_baselines, user_score_weights, score_cache_invalidations, raw_deltas), AI observability (ai_call_log), notifications, chatbot, privacy/consent, feature flags, cost/analytics, export/deletion, compliance records. (F) Scoring Engine Quick Reference — architecture summary (penalty-from-perfect-day), Overall Score composition with user-adjustable [5,55] weights, three signal types (accumulating/event/trailing-window), piecewise-linear normalization variants (symmetric/asymmetric/ceiling), 14-day warmup + 30-day rolling median baselines, graceful degradation, 15-minute Redis cache TTL + nightly 03:30 UTC immutable snapshots, 7-day retroactive recompute cap. (G) Goal Library Quick Reference — all 22 goals from §4.10.3 (6 Fitness / 5 Nutrition / 5 Finance / 6 Time) in single compact table with ID, category, name, type, data source. (H) Notification Categories Quick Reference — 14 signal categories per §4.9.3 with purpose and default state (ON/OFF/opt-in) noted. (I) US Privacy Law Coverage Map — 10 state privacy laws plus WA MHMDA mapped to unified baseline compliance mechanism; DSAR 30-day SLA noted; non-discrimination commitment (Core-available export) highlighted. (J) Key SLO & Target Summary — consolidated internal targets from §14 (availability, latency, error rates, DR, deployment, scaling envelope, cost projections, unit economics) with explicit note these are internal targets not public SLAs. (K) Version History Summary — high-level version log from v1.0 through v1.26 showing major milestones (v1.13 pricing/AWS retcon, v1.18 product rename + Time category + 22-goal trim + multi-account drop, v1.21 $4.99/$9.99 pricing lock, per-section completions). (L) Document Completion Declaration — checklist confirming all 16 sections drafted; final document statistics (~27k lines, 16+ sections, 150+ subsections, 16 AI calls, 22 integrations, 22 goals, 14+ processors, 9 DR runbooks, 11 state privacy regimes, 14 notification categories); next stage flagged as founder novel-read review. **Document is complete at v1 scope as of v1.26.** No retroactive edits required — appendices are pure reference consolidation with every entry cross-referencing the authoritative section. Version bumped v1.25 → v1.26.

**2026-04-21 — v1.25** — Section 15 (Out of Scope) drafted in full, 10 subsections, ~900 lines. Section 15 is the authoritative record of features considered and deliberately excluded from v1, organized by timeline and category. Structure: (15.1) overview and scope definition with rationale-transparency commitment, (15.2) 16 v1.1+ deferred items with trigger conditions (EU launch, Aurora migration, SOC 2 Type 1, Lite tier experiment, additional languages, referral program, winback discounts, student/military discounts, Chatbot Audit per-entry deletion, proactive AI warnings explicitly killed, email style learning improvements, Family Controls retry, Plaid Liabilities/Investments, home screen widget, dark mode customization, tabletop DR automation), (15.3) 19 v2+ medium-term deferred items (public web app, tablet-optimized layouts, Apple Watch, Wear OS, macOS native, Chromebook, public API, family plans, team/business plans, gift subscriptions, APAC/LATAM regional expansion, integrations marketplace, white-labeled corporate wellness, coaching marketplace, advanced investment tracking, real-time collaboration, HITRUST/FedRAMP, enterprise sales motion), (15.4) 19 forever-out-of-scope items committed as permanent (medical advice/diagnosis/treatment, mental health diagnosis or therapy, addiction recovery clinical features, ads, data brokering, training other AI models with user data, cryptocurrency integration, lending/credit decisioning, money movement/payments, dating or social matching, gambling features, user-vs-user scoring, social leaderboards, shaming/aversive motivation, gamification with points/badges, unsolicited AI narrative, lifetime subscription, third-party analytics SDKs, IMAP email sync), (15.5) category-specific OOS covering Fitness (no social features, no HR-zone plans, no swim/climb/yoga specific tracking, no coach-assignment), Nutrition (no recipe book, no meal prep planner, no macro cycling, no supplement tracking, no alcohol-specific or caffeine tracking, no restaurant menu DB, no receipt scanning), Finance (no credit score, investments/liabilities deferred, no retirement planning, no tax features, no subscription management, no budget subcategorization, no business expense segregation, no receipts, no currency conversion), Time (social media inaccessible, SMS/iMessage/WhatsApp/Messenger blocked, iCloud Mail inaccessible, no call logs, no browser history, no continuous location, no voice note archival, no meeting transcription, no scratch email drafting, no calendar auto-creation from email, no unsubscribe management, no scheduled send), Chatbot (no voice mode, no image generation, no persistent chat history across sessions, no file attachments, no code generation, no web search, no group chats), Notifications (no SMS, no phone calls, no per-notification snooze, no custom sounds per category, no A/B testing, no smart scheduling beyond quiet hours, no ad-hoc email beyond daily summary), Goals (no shared goals per §15.4 exclusion, no custom user-created goals at v1, no AI goal coaching, no data-based goal recommendations, no auto-difficulty, single weight-loss goal max), Account (no account merging, no account transfer, no multi-tenant single-user, no phone-number primary identity, no social login v1, no parental controls), Data (no continuous cloud drive sync, no personal API, no webhook subscriptions, no Zapier/IFTTT, no partial exports, no app-specific format conversions, no auto-backup to user cloud), Subscription/Business (no free tier ever, no ad-supported tier ever, no one-time purchase, no split-monthly billing, no pay-as-you-go, no volume discounts, no mass promo distribution). (15.6) Technical/infrastructure OOS — multi-region active-active, edge computing, GraphQL, microservices split, database sharding, Kubernetes, service mesh, self-hosted LLM, dedicated Anthropic capacity, custom model fine-tuning. (15.7) Platform OOS — Smart TV, Alexa/Google Assistant, browser extensions, macOS menu bar, Windows/Linux native, embedded device apps. (15.8) Compliance OOS — formal GDPR certification, FTC safe harbor programs, age-verified child safety programs. (15.9) Explicitly blocked integrations (not Life Dashboard's decision) — iCloud Mail, iMessage, WhatsApp personal, Messenger personal, SMS content, Strava AI forwarding, personal social media. (15.10) OOS review cadence — quarterly for v1.1+ items, semi-annually for v2+, annually for forever-OOS review (only if fundamental assumptions change), triggered reviews for specific events. **Rationale:** §15 makes the silent exclusions explicit. Items not mentioned aren't necessarily in scope, but items mentioned were actively considered and deliberately excluded — preserving institutional memory and preventing re-litigation of settled decisions. Several items in §15.4 (no ads, no data sale, no medical advice, no gamification, no shaming, no unsolicited AI narrative) are business-model and design-principle commitments — not just launch-phase deferrals — that would require founder-level reversal to change. No retroactive edits required. Version bumped v1.24 → v1.25.

**2026-04-21 — v1.24** — Section 14 (Non-Functional Requirements) drafted in full, 23 subsections, ~1,400 lines. Section 14 is the consolidated declaration of quality-attribute commitments — performance, scalability, reliability, observability, security, privacy, accessibility, usability, i18n/l10n, platform support, energy efficiency, data quality, maintainability, testability, deployability, portability — that span functional sections. Most technical substance already lives in §5, §6, §7, §9, §10, §11, §12, and §13; §14 is the authoritative aggregation reference. **Critical founder decision (applied throughout):** Section 14 creates zero user-facing commitments for any quality attribute. Every target is an internal engineering discipline. Consumer apps at founder scale do not make public SLA commitments they cannot operationally defend; user-facing numeric commitments create liability without proportionate user benefit. This posture is explicitly documented in §14.1.3, §14.16.5 (no public battery claim), §14.17.4 (no public accuracy claim), and runs through the spec. **Availability SLOs (internal):** API tier 99.5% monthly, mobile critical paths 99.5%, background workers 99%, export generation 98%, data durability 100% (zero tolerance for user data loss). **Latency SLOs (internal, aggregated from §5.1 and §10.9):** full per-surface p50/p95/p99 matrix covering API endpoints, dashboard loads, score computation, chatbot first-token, meal scan variants, workout parsing, daily email summary, notification evaluator, Day Detail, and sync. **Error rate SLOs:** <0.5% overall API errors, <0.1% 5xx, <0.5% mobile crash rate, <2% background job failures, <3% AI call failures per §10.16. **Informal error budget framework:** 2 consecutive months of SLO miss triggers reliability sprint (feature work paused for 2 weeks while reliability investments ship); single-month breach triggers post-mortem only. No ceremony, no SRE process overhead, but disciplined response. **Performance budget** with CI enforcement: mobile build size, Lambda bundle size, load test gates, React Native render performance. Budget applies at any scale within §5.16 envelope — performance doesn't degrade with growth. **Scalability commitments** reaffirm §5.16 envelope (50k users / 20k DAU / 200k AI queries/day with 3-5x headroom to 250k users); re-architecture triggers (Neon→Aurora, Lambda concurrency ceiling, observability cost consolidation) documented at 250k+ user threshold. **Reliability**: 99.5% availability, honest status page, graceful degradation paths for all major vendor failures (Anthropic circuit breaker, Clerk JWT cache, Plaid retry on backoff, R2 queue), offline-first mobile client that operates fully on cached SQLite when network is unavailable. **DR targets** reaffirm §5.15: RTO 4 hours, RPO 1 hour, supported by Neon continuous PITR + nightly R2 dumps + weekly Glacier backups + monthly restore drills per §11.12. **Observability coverage:** 100% of production requests traced via Axiom, 100% of errors captured via Sentry, 10% performance trace sampling. **Alert response times internal targets** — P1 acknowledge 15 min / resolve 4h, P2 1h / 24h, P3 1 business day / 1 week. **Accessibility**: WCAG 2.1 AA commitment per §12.8 with product-level requirements (4.5:1 contrast, 44pt/48dp touch targets, VoiceOver/TalkBack, Dynamic Type, no color-only state indication), accessibility smoke test every release, annual outside audit starting year 2. **Usability commitments** on platform-native feel (iOS HIG, Material Design) — cross-platform convergence at brand level, divergence at interaction level; optimistic UI updates; no action feels unresponsive even when backend takes 8 seconds. **i18n at v1:** English + Spanish (per §4.8.9 lock). RTL languages deferred. French/German/Italian/Portuguese with EU launch v1.1+. Localization quality via professional translation service, native speaker review, quarterly review of AI-generated localized content. **Platform support:** iOS 16.0+ (covers ~95% of addressable market; required for Family Controls + DeviceActivityReport for Time score's Attention Fragmentation signal), Android 10 API 29+ (Scoped Storage + UsageStatsManager floor). iPad/Android tablet functional but not optimized v1. Apple Watch and Wear OS v2+. Low-end device performance baseline iPhone SE 2020 + Pixel 6a. Storage footprint targets: <50MB binary, <200MB SQLite heavy user, <350MB total. **Energy efficiency as internal engineering discipline only** (no user-facing commitment per founder decision) — internal targets: <2% daily battery impact typical user, <4% heavy user, <5min background CPU per day, <10 wake events per day. Monitoring via Xcode Energy Impact + Android Battery Historian in QA. **Data quality internal targets** (not user-facing): meal text parse ≥90%, meal photo standard ≥85%, Premium Opus ≥92%, workout parse ≥92%, workout burn ±10%, email classification ≥88%, transaction categorization ≥92%, chatbot factual correctness ≥95%, score computation 100% (deterministic — bug is P1), goal progress 100%. Measured via golden tests weekly + quarterly manual sample review + annual external audit year 2. **Test coverage targets internal:** 80% domain layer, 70% API, 60% data, 50% mobile, 65% overall. Test categories: unit (Vitest + Jest+RNTL), integration (staging Neon), golden (AI outputs per §10.15 + scoring per §9.15.1), E2E (Maestro/Detox), accessibility (manual VoiceOver/TalkBack), load (k6 quarterly). Zero-tolerance flaky tests in critical paths. **Maintainability**: TypeScript strict, ESLint+Prettier, founder code review at v1, feature-grouped file organization, ADRs for significant decisions, 15-20% tech debt allocation per release. **Deployability**: daily manual promote first 90 days, continuous after per §11.14.2; canary 5→25→50→100% with alarm gates; <90min deploy duration launch-to-100%-traffic; mobile biweekly with phased rollout. **Portability**: full data export CSV+JSON (GDPR Article 20 / CCPA portability), Core-tier available (not Pro-gated — anti-lock-in commitment), RFC 4180 CSV, ISO 8601 timestamps, PDF/A-compliant where possible. **API versioning strategy documented for future use** — internal `/v1/` API at launch; public API deferred to v2+ with OpenAPI 3.0, tiered access, webhooks. **No retroactive edits required** — §14 is pure synthesis of already-locked commitments across §5, §6, §7, §9, §10, §11, §12, §13. Version bumped v1.23 → v1.24.

**2026-04-21 — v1.23** — Section 12 (Compliance Requirements) drafted in full, 21 subsections, ~950 lines. Section 12 is the authoritative compliance regime mapping that operationalizes decisions already locked in §4.11, §6, §7, §11.19, §13.10 into a single authoritative reference. **Jurisdictional posture locked (founder call):** Life Dashboard offered globally via App Store/Play Store under US governing law (Delaware); users outside US accept US law via ToS; EU users covered interim by Standard Contractual Clauses in vendor DPAs with full GDPR compliance deferred to v1.1+ when EU infrastructure ships per §11.19.3. **SOC 2 deferred (founder call):** no formal attestation at v1; triggered pursuit when enterprise buyer, institutional investor, or $500k ARR threshold warrants. Unified privacy baseline satisfies all US state privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA, Virginia CDPA, Colorado CPA, Connecticut CTDPA, Utah UCPA, Texas TDPSA, Oregon OCPA, Montana MCDPA, Florida FDBR, Delaware DPDPA) with a single set of controls meeting the strictest jurisdiction's requirements rather than state-specific variations. HIPAA confirmed not applicable (Life Dashboard is not Covered Entity or Business Associate — consumer wellness app with user-entered data outside HIPAA scope); Washington MHMDA confirmed applicable for WA users with compliance integrated into unified baseline; PCI DSS confirmed not applicable (Apple StoreKit / Google Play Billing / RevenueCat handle all payment flows — Life Dashboard never receives card data); GLBA confirmed not applicable (Plaid intermediary model — Plaid carries financial-institution compliance burden, Life Dashboard is downstream consumer of aggregated data with user authorization). COPPA posture is exclusion-based (18+ ToS, no under-13 onboarding path, data purge on evidence discovery) — same posture locked in §6.16, documented here as COPPA compliance strategy. Children's state laws (CAADCA, SCOPE Act, Connecticut's 13-17 provisions) analyzed with position that Life Dashboard is not "likely to be accessed by children" given 18+ ToS and adult-targeted marketing, but monitoring for enforcement posture. WCAG 2.1 AA confirmed as accessibility commitment (ADA Title III best-effort proxy). No DPO appointed at v1 (not legally required for founder-scale operations); founder serves as privacy contact via `privacy@apex-life.app`. No EU Representative appointed (deferred with EU launch). Legal entity structure: Apex Leadership LLC (Delaware) as sole Controller/Business for all users; single-entity structure at v1. User rights operationalized: 30-day SLA for all DSARs (exceeds 45-day CCPA max; matches future GDPR 30-day), in-app self-service for most requests (real-time export and deletion per §4.11), `privacy@apex-life.app` as backup channel; Clerk authentication verifies in-app requests, email verification for support-channel requests. Non-discrimination: confirmed export is Core-available (not Pro-gated) per v1.12 reversal — conditioning data access on subscription would be CCPA dark pattern. Cross-border transfer via SCCs with all processors (14+ vendors documented in §6.11 with DPA status); Data Transfer Impact Assessment documented for EU users at v1 under Schrems II requirements. ROPA maintained as lightweight Notion doc even though GDPR not yet formally applicable — good-practice record keeping that becomes immediately useful at EU launch. No cookies (native mobile, no web presence); ATT prompt explicitly not shown (Life Dashboard does not track); no third-party analytics SDK (self-hosted PostHog per §6.13). Law enforcement request handling framework: outside counsel review of all formal legal process, no compliance with informal requests except emergency disclosure per ECPA, user notification within 10 business days when legally permitted, annual transparency report starting year 2. Breach notification aligned with §6.14: 72h GDPR supervisory authority (future), state breach notification laws (30-day unified commitment across all states), 7-day user notification for High-tier breaches, 1,000-user threshold for Medium-tier. App Store and Play Store compliance declared (subscription disclosures, Restore Purchases, Privacy Labels, in-app account deletion — all implemented per §13.10). Pre-submission compliance checklist documented as GitHub Actions release step. **Explicitly accepted compliance gaps documented (§12.18):** GDPR/UK GDPR gap (SCCs mitigate interim), age verification gap (self-attestation only), no formal attestations at v1, single-founder operations risk, limited transparency reporting until year 2 — gaps named with mitigation + acceptance rationale rather than left unstated. Compliance operational cadence: quarterly retention/consent audits, semi-annual DPA and Privacy Policy reviews, annual Section 6 + Section 12 revisions + accessibility audits (starting year 2) + transparency report (starting year 2), triggered reviews for new regulations. Regulatory change monitoring via IAPP, state AG updates, Apple/Google developer news, quarterly outside counsel check-ins. Compliance contacts designated: `privacy@`, `security@`, `legal@`, `support@` at `apex-life.app`. No retroactive edits required — §12 is synthesis of already-locked mechanisms. Version bumped v1.22 → v1.23.

**2026-04-21 — v1.22** — Section 11 (Infrastructure Requirements) drafted in full, 23 subsections covering the complete operational infrastructure spec. Length: ~1,000 lines. Section 11 operationalizes everything §5 (Technical Architecture) locked — it specifies how infrastructure is provisioned, monitored, backed up, deployed, and scaled rather than re-opening architectural choices. Key operational decisions: **Infrastructure as Code via AWS CDK in TypeScript** (single-language across app + infra for solo founder; Terraform rejected for multi-cloud portability that goes unused under AWS-first lock); **two-account AWS topology** (apex-prod + apex-dev; full AWS Organizations deferred until >$20k/month spend or team >3 engineers); **public-Lambda networking with no VPC** (saves $768/year in NAT Gateway costs, eliminates VPC cold-start penalty, acceptable security posture given all outbound is TLS to managed vendors, WAF on API Gateway handles inbound); **on-demand Lambda with no provisioned concurrency at launch** (add reactively if p95 latency regresses past §5.16 targets); **Neon Pro tier at launch with documented Aurora Serverless v2 migration path** (triggered by sustained >70% CPU, sustained >2GB/day storage growth, outages >4h cumulative in 30-day window, or compliance requirement); **three-tier backup strategy** — Neon continuous 7-day PITR + nightly logical dump to R2 (30-day retention) + weekly full to R2 Glacier Deep Archive (1-year retention); **monthly automated restore drill** (GitHub Action triggers Fargate task that restores recent backup to new Neon branch, runs smoke tests, tears down — failed drill is P2 incident); **daily manual promote to production for first 90 days** then continuous deploy after 14 consecutive zero-rollback days; **Lambda alias traffic-shifting for canary rollout** (5% → 25% → 50% → 100% over 60 minutes with CloudWatch alarm gate); **secrets rotation cadence** — 90 days for database credentials (partially automated), annual for vendor API keys (manual), automatic annual KMS key rotation (KMS-managed); **alert channel Slack-primary** with email + SMS for P1 only (no PagerDuty at v1 — founder-stage); **single us-east-1 region at launch** with documented EU v1.1+ path (6-8 week engineering effort to deploy apex-prod-eu). **Cost projections documented** — launch month ~$450/month total (AWS + vendors + ~$200 Anthropic at 1k users); at 10k users ~$16,200/month dominated by Anthropic AI at ~$15k with non-AI infra at $1,200/month (~$0.12/user/month — delivers on the "low fixed cost, cost scales with usage" architecture commitment); at 50k users ~$70k/month still AI-dominated. **Six SQS queue topology** (sync-jobs, notification-signals, notification-dispatch, export-jobs, ai-jobs, scheduled-jobs) with per-queue DLQ, 14-day DLQ retention, CloudWatch alarms at depth >100. **Three R2 buckets** — apex-exports (7-day lifecycle), apex-meal-images (24-hour lifecycle), apex-backups (tiered hot 30 days → Glacier 1 year → auto-delete). **Nine disaster recovery runbooks** inline in §11.13 — Neon outage, Anthropic outage, Plaid outage, Clerk outage, complete region failure, database corruption, credential compromise, mobile app critical bug, backup restore procedure. **Scaling response playbook** (§11.18) with named triggers per dimension — user count (10k/25k/50k/100k), DAU (5k/20k/50k), AI volume (200k/500k/1M queries/day), cost ($2k/$10k/$50k monthly spend). **Vendor SLA posture** documenting expected vs historical uptime for 10 vendor dependencies with per-vendor breach response patterns; Plaid explicitly noted as weaker SLA (99.5%) with occasional multi-hour degradations, mitigated by async-retry pattern that insulates users from short outages. Security operational posture (§11.20) covers identity, encryption, audit logging, vulnerability management with Dependabot + monthly dependency updates. Compliance-driven infrastructure (§11.21) maps §12's compliance requirements to operational controls — US-only launch via single-region, GDPR path via EU v1.1+ architecture, CCPA via §4.11 account deletion flow, App Store/Play Store compliance via CI checks. No retroactive edits required — Section 11 is pure operational synthesis of already-locked architectural decisions. Version bumped v1.21 → v1.22.

**2026-04-21 — v1.21** — Section 13 (Business Requirements) drafted in full, 16 subsections covering the complete business model spec for Life Dashboard. Length: ~900 lines. Section 13 locks pricing, feature cut-lines, trial mechanics, billing infrastructure, refund policy, unit economics, promotion policy, App Store / Play Store compliance, regional pricing, revenue recognition, subscription KPIs, pricing review cadence, and the deferred Lite tier consideration. **Key pricing decision (founder call):** Life Dashboard shifts from the v1.18 "$8.99/$17.99 under review" baseline to aggressive volume-pricing at **Core $4.99/mo ($49.99/yr) and Pro $9.99/mo ($99.99/yr)** — a ~44% cut on both tiers to match the Cal AI volume-over-margin model that drove millions of downloads at sub-$30/yr pricing. Annual pricing at 17% discount (≈10x monthly) is a moderate discount preserving per-user margin; deeper consumer-app discounts (25-30%) would compress Pro annual margin below sustainable thresholds given the AI cost profile. **Unit economics modeled against realistic blended user distribution** (30% dormant paid / 40% casual / 20% engaged / 10% heavy) rather than worst-case heavy-user assumptions — this was founder-driven correction of prior modeling that used heavy-user costs as baseline. Blended Core AI cost $0.77/mo, blended Pro AI cost $4.97/mo; Core contribution margin 70% of net, Pro contribution margin 34% of net. Heavy Pro users (~10%) have individually negative contribution margin (−$5/mo monthly, −$6.44/mo on annual) which is accepted under the thesis that heavy users drive WOM and engagement while the blended 90% non-heavy cohort carries the business. **Quotas tightened in v1.21 to match new pricing:** Pro chatbot 100/day → 50/day with soft cap 500/day → 300/day; Premium Scan 20/day → 10/day; standard meal scan Pro soft cap 50/day → 30/day. Core chatbot stays at 10/day. **Trial extended from 7 days to 14 days** to ensure users see personalized scoring (14-day baseline warmup per §9) before trial ends. Trial end uses force-choice screen (primary: Pro $9.99, secondary: Core $4.99, tertiary: Not yet) rather than auto-downgrade or auto-upgrade. **Multi-account gate permanently dropped** at Core per v1.18; confirmed in v1.21 as not reintroduced. Unlimited email, calendar, and bank accounts at both tiers. Feature cut-lines finalized: Premium Scan (Pro only), Pantry Scanner (Pro only), AI-Drafted Email Replies with style learning (Pro only), PDF formatted export (Pro only; CSV export remains Core — data portability is a user right), unlimited archived history (Pro; Core is 90-day hot window only), higher plan generation (Pro 3/30d vs Core 1/30d) and revision (Pro 10/plan vs Core 3/plan), 30-day historical score immutability (Pro vs 7-day Core), priority email support (Pro). Chatbot Audit retention standardized to 30 days at both tiers (transparency is not monetized). **Lite tier ($2.99/mo, read-only scoring + manual logging) explicitly considered and deferred to v1.1+ as a controlled experiment** per §13.14 — adding a third tier at v1 adds permanent complexity to paywalls, marketing, and quota logic for unproven price-elasticity gain. Post-launch, Lite can be A/B tested with 50% of new signups to measure net-new subscriber lift vs Core cannibalization; if total paid signups lift >20% without >15% Core cannibalization, roll out; else kill. Pricing review cadence locked at 90 days post-launch with named triggers (heavy-user share >12% for 60 consecutive days, blended free-cash-flow below $2/user/month, competitive pricing shifts >20%, conversion rates below target bands, Anthropic pricing changes >15%). Subscription KPIs specified across acquisition (signup rate, trial start rate, source attribution), conversion (trial-to-paid ≥35% target, tier mix at conversion), retention (monthly churn ≤5% blended target, cohort retention M6 ≥60% target, annual renewal ≥70% target), revenue (MRR, ARPU, LTV), cost (CAC target ≤$10-15, LTV:CAC ≥3:1, blended AI cost targets by tier), engagement (DAU/MAU ≥40% target), heavy user monitoring (% users above $10/mo AI cost target ≤10% Pro / ≤3% Core). App Store / Play Store compliance documented: required disclosures, Restore Purchases, subscription group structure, privacy labels, in-app account deletion. Regional pricing: Apple/Google automatic (USD anchor, platform-managed regional tiers). Revenue recognition per US GAAP ASC 606 with deferred revenue treatment for annual subscribers.

**Retroactive edits in same version:** §1.6 Business Model rewritten end-to-end with locked v1.21 pricing and feature summaries; §4.1.9 paywall copy updated (14-day trial, new tier prices, corrected annual discount); §4.4.8 Premium Scan economics reference updated; §4.7 chatbot cost modeling paragraph + §4.7.16 tier table + §4.7.19 per-user cost scenarios all rewritten with 50/day Pro quota and $9.99 pricing; §4.8.5 Subscription screen fully rewritten — current plan sub-lines updated to new prices, full plan comparison table rewritten with v1.21 feature cut-lines and quotas; §4.9 notifications ARR reference updated to new subscription math ($59.88/yr Core, $119.88/yr Pro monthly equivalents); §5.9 AI gateway Pro quota reference updated; §7 rate limit endpoint spec (`/v1/chatbot/query`) updated to 50/day Pro; §8.2.3 vendor-metered connection Anthropic mitigation reference updated; §8.3 connection catalog summary table — Email/Calendar account cap language cleaned (unlimited both tiers per v1.18), Anthropic quota updated; §8.16 tier placement table — stale 2-total-account caps on Gmail/Outlook/Calendar rows removed, Anthropic quota corrected, EventKit/Calendar Provider "counts in 2-calendar limit" notes removed; §10.3.1 chatbot call spec quota reference updated; §10.7.1 user-facing quota UI messages updated (Pro 50/day message, 300/day soft-cap message, upgrade CTA text); §10.7.2 per-feature quota table tightened with v1.21 values and explanatory note; §10.7.3 monthly spend review thresholds tightened to $2.50/mo Core / $10/mo Pro alert + $15/mo Core / $25/mo Pro hard review; Forward-Build Notes "Pricing & Packaging Retcon — v1.13" section renamed and rewritten as "Pricing & Packaging — v1.21 Locked" with every pricing, quota, account-cap, and tier value updated; Forward-Build Notes "For Section 13" block collapsed from open-questions list to a "completed v1.21" marker. TOC entry for Section 13 "to be drafted" marker removed. Version bumped v1.20 → v1.21. This is the final major pricing retcon before v1 launch; all downstream sections (§11, §12, §14, §15, §16) inherit v1.21 locked pricing and packaging.

**2026-04-21 — v1.20** — Section 10 (AI Requirements) drafted in full, 18 subsections covering the complete AI governance layer for Life Dashboard. Length: ~1,100 lines. Section 10 is the authoritative AI call inventory and operational governance spec. Contents: (1) overview and philosophy reiterating AI's four legitimate jobs (synthesis, ambiguity resolution, drafting, messy-input parsing), making explicit that AI is never the product's identity — deterministic scoring is; (2) the closed AI call inventory — **16 call sites total**, 13 user-invoked and 3 proactive (all in opt-in notification/daily-summary surfaces), with per-user monthly cost modeling ($1.98/month Core moderate, $12.92/month Pro heavy); (3) per-call specifications for every one of the 16 call sites — model, token budget, latency target, cost target, caching policy, quota counting, fallback, MODE declaration — covering chatbot, both meal scan variants, meal text parse, AI Edit Meal, workout parsing, burn estimation, AI Edit Workout, plan generation, plan understanding narration, plan revision, pantry ingredient ID, meal suggestion, AI email draft, daily email summary, notification evaluator; (4) model selection rationale (Haiku 4.5 as default, Sonnet 4.6 for reasoning-heavy synthesis, Opus 4.6 only for Pro Premium Scan); (5) prompt engineering standards with required system prompt structure including the MODE header; (6) prompt caching strategy — 5 high-volume call sites use caching with target hit rates; (7) quota and rate limiting — Core 10/day chatbot, Pro 100/day with 500/day soft cap (loosened from 200/day per Q3); per-feature quota table; **no hard monthly spend cap** (Q1 decision — soft observation-driven enforcement only, paying users never hit undisclosed ceiling); (8) cost observability — `ai_call_log` schema per §5.9, internal weekly cost dashboard, alert thresholds; no user-facing cost meter (Q2); (9) streaming and latency targets with complete per-call p50/p95 matrix; (10) fallback matrix covering all 16 call sites; retry policy (2-retry max synchronous, 3-retry max background); circuit breaker architecture; (11) prompt injection defenses — structural only per Q7 (schema validation, user-content quoting, MODE enforcement, output length caps, redaction-before-call, no cross-user context); soft redirect for content-policy violations per Q8; (12) model upgrades as config-driven with canary for chatbot only per Q16; (13) descriptive vs prescriptive AI governance — full MODE declaration table per call site, explicit user-invoked prescriptive allowance for chatbot when users ask for recommendations, banned-anywhere list; (14) AI audit and transparency — §4.8.7 Chatbot Audit remains sole user-facing audit at v1 per Q19, other surfaces rely on natural product visibility; (15) testing and evaluation — 5-10 golden prompts per call site per Q15, nightly regression runs, quarterly quality grading, user thumbs-down feedback loop; (16) edge cases (invalid JSON, policy violations, wrong language, quota exceeded mid-session, Anthropic outage, empty response, concurrent calls); (17) analytics events; (18) prompt template versioning with change protocol. **Companion retroactive edit in same version (v1.20):** Profile Insight AI call removed entirely per the v1.19 framing discussion that surfaced a contradiction — the Insight Block was structurally identical to the Pattern Insight slop that was killed earlier (unsolicited AI paragraph appearing on reveal screen, narrating numbers the user can see for themselves). Per §3.2, descriptive AI must be user-invoked; the Insight Block appeared without any user action, making it prescriptive-adjacent content that violated the descriptive-AI rule. The cut removes one AI call from the onboarding path. §4.1.5 Insight Block subsection replaced with removal rationale; loading screen copy updated (latency target dropped from 10s to 1s since the Haiku call is gone); §4.1.11 `personalized_insight` row removed from derived profile data table; §4.1.12 Profile Insight Call section replaced with removal note, Workout Plan Understanding Narration Call spec added separately (Haiku, bundled with plan generation, user-invoked because user chose Build My Plan, descriptive-only constraint); Total Onboarding AI Cost updated to reflect zero cost for users who skip plan builder, $0.009 max for users who build. **Onboarding AI surface reduced from 3 calls (archetype + insight + plan) to 2 conditional calls (plan generation + plan narration, both only if user chose Build My Plan).** Result: a user who opts out of plan building incurs zero AI cost during onboarding. Version bumped v1.19 → v1.20.

**2026-04-21 — v1.19** — Section 9 (Scoring Engine) drafted in full, 19 subsections covering the complete deterministic math specification for Life Dashboard's scoring system. Length: ~950 lines. Section 9 is the authoritative specification for every score the app produces. Contents: (1) overview and purpose locking the engine's invocation surfaces and zero-AI architectural commitment; (2) seven design principles governing ambiguous implementation decisions; (3) four-tier score hierarchy (signal → subsystem → category → overall); (4) the universal score formula with pseudocode, demonstrating how graceful degradation happens mathematically via weight normalization rather than explicit redistribution logic; (5) signal normalization using piecewise-linear curves with safe-range concept — symmetric, asymmetric-floor (protein), and ceiling (sodium, budget) variants all specified with worked examples; (6) baselines and personalization — full table of which 16 signals use personal baselines at what windows, which 14 signals use fixed or profile-derived targets, the 14-day warmup mechanic with population defaults, median-not-mean rationale, nightly recomputation cadence; (7) graceful degradation mechanics with step-by-step worked example showing a Fitness score for a user with only Movement and Sleep active (redistribution from 35/100 total weight to full 0-100 output); (8) minimum data thresholds table for all four tiers; (9) tiered proration with three signal-type categories, full per-signal curves for accumulating signals (calorie sigmoid, linear-with-late-flattening for steps, inverted-ceiling for screen time), event-signal binary scoring, trailing-window signals using their natural window; (10) full category composition tables for Fitness (7 subsystems with per-subsystem signal weights), Nutrition (5 signals with safe-range specs), Finance (5 signals), Time (5 signals cross-referenced to §4.6.15); (11) Overall Score formula, user weight customization with [5,55] constraints, weight redistribution when categories show "—", precision/rounding rules; (12) three-layer cache architecture (Redis hot / daily_scores immutable snapshots / on-demand computation), cache invalidation triggers, nightly snapshot job spec at 03:30 UTC, historical immutability window (7 days Core, 30 days Pro) with the v1.18 AI-cost-bearing 7-day cap carve-out; (13) anti-oscillation policy (no smoothing on category scores except HRV 14-day EMA), delta indicator rendering rules; (14) raw_deltas schema and surfacing in Settings → Advanced per v1.18 locked policy, with v1.1+ Explain-This-Score button flagged as candidate; (15) testing framework including golden scenarios, boundary tests, redistribution tests, baseline tests, proration tests, idempotency tests, 50+ user-scenario fixtures; (16) edge cases covering DST transitions, mid-day timezone changes, clock skew, goal changes mid-period, data-source disconnection, very-old-edits-vs-immutability; (17) data persistence — four new tables: daily_scores (immutable snapshots with raw_deltas_json), user_score_baselines (nightly-updated personal baselines), user_score_weights (Overall customization with sum-to-100 constraint), score_cache_invalidations (7-day debug audit); (18) a full worked end-to-end example walking through a Day-45 user's complete score computation with real numbers — 8-signal Fitness at 80, 4-signal Nutrition at 94, 5-signal Finance at 90, 5-signal Time at 100, Overall 91 after user-weight customization — demonstrating every concept in Section 9 applied to a concrete case; (19) analytics events for observability. The worked example in §9.18 is the specification's integration test: a reader can verify their understanding of the math by reproducing the numbers from the raw data. Key decisions locked this section: median over mean for baselines (robust to outlier weeks); no recency weighting in baselines (avoids vacation/sickness baseline warping); nightly 03:30 UTC baseline recomputation cadence (not per-request); Redis 15-minute TTL for hot cache (balances freshness vs recomputation cost); immutable historical scores with 7-day non-AI / 30-day Pro / 7-day hard AI-cost cap; raw_deltas never AI-generated; debug view is Settings → Advanced only in v1 (chatbot is the user-facing explain surface). Section 9 is now the authoritative math reference for §4.2.4, §4.3.11, §4.4.13, §4.5.10, §4.6.15 — all of which reference Section 9 for specific math. Version bumped v1.18 → v1.19 because Section 9 is a major new section, not a retcon.

**2026-04-21 — v1.18** — Largest retcon session in the document's history, consolidating a full Flask feature-parity audit with founder-driven product-identity and scoring-architecture decisions into a single atomic version. Executed in response to a gap-analysis showing that the in-progress PRD was silently dropping Flask PWA capabilities and had not yet addressed several key product decisions.

**(1) Product identity rename.** Product name changes from "APEX Life Dashboard" to **Life Dashboard**. APEX is retained as an internal codename for engineering / architecture / backend prose only — it appears in §5, §6, §7, §8, and §11 as shorthand for the platform. Legal entity is "Apex Leadership LLC." Document header, TOC, and naming-convention block updated. ~400 user-facing "APEX" → "Life Dashboard" substitutions across §1–§12. All tier names ("APEX Core" → "Life Dashboard Core", "APEX Pro" → "Life Dashboard Pro"), user-visible strings (settings labels, notification copy, email templates, error messages, chatbot system prompt), and the "APEX Overall Score" renamed to simply "Overall Score" (no brand prefix).

**(2) Fourth category renamed: Life → Time.** The fourth category is now **Time**. The rename reflects both the cognitive incoherence of a "Life" category inside a "Life Dashboard" app and the v1.18 rewrite of the category's scoring (see below). All references updated across §1.3, §2.2, §4.2, §4.6, §4.10, §8, and related. §4.6 section header updated from "Life Category" to "Time Category." Category color identity, tab bar label, detail view header, and all cross-section references flipped.

**(3) Time Score rewritten as a passive-data score (§4.6.15, new framework).** The Time category now measures **the shape of the user's day** from entirely passive platform data — data the user did not consciously produce. Five signals: **Sleep Regularity** (25%, HealthKit), **Attention Fragmentation** (25%, Screen Time), **Location Intentionality** (20%, CoreLocation), **Schedule Density Balance** (15%, Calendar), **Rhythm Adherence** (15%, cross-domain anchor timestamps used as timing data only). Rewards "rhythm with slack" — generous personal-baseline tolerance bands (±45 min sleep, ±15% attention, ±25% schedule, ±60 min rhythm anchors), only true outliers drop score. Minimum data threshold: ≥2 of 5 signals with data. Cross-domain goal adherence, task completion, email responsiveness, and mood signals are all removed from the Time score (either redundant with Overall Score, user-gameable, or killed earlier). Brand framing: "Your Time Score reflects the shape of your day — measured from what your phone saw, not from anything you logged." This is the Life Dashboard differentiator — a score competitors structurally cannot replicate without expanding into cross-domain data collection.

**(4) Scoring architecture locked (§4.2.4).** **Penalty-from-perfect-day model** — every category score starts at 100 and penalties subtract based on per-signal deltas from targets/baselines. User sees the result as positive progress ("87 of 100"); internal math is deterministic penalty subtraction. Each category contributes up to 25 points to the Overall Score (25×4 = 100). Overall Score weights are user-adjustable with constraints: each weight must be in [5, 55] and the four weights sum to 100. **Signal-specific safe ranges** — each signal has its own tolerance band for max-point scoring (protein ±10g, calories ±100kcal, sleep rhythm ±45 min, etc.). **Tiered proration** — accumulating signals (calories, steps, screen time) get per-signal tuned proration curves; event signals (workout happened, bill paid) score instantly on occurrence with no proration; trailing-window signals (7-day strength volume, 30-day savings rate) use their rolling window with no proration. Minimum data: category ≥2 signals each ≥1 data point; Overall ≥2 scored categories. Day 1: "—" with CTA. Color bands on scores (green ≥75, amber 50-74, red <50, grey for "—"). Historical recompute unbounded (7-day cap only when AI-involved). Raw deltas surfaced only in Settings → Advanced for debug.

**(5) Flask feature parity restored.** Audit-identified regressions fixed:
- **RMR dual-formula switching** (§4.1.5): explicit Katch-McArdle vs Mifflin-St Jeor branch on body-fat-% availability, formula_used persisted, disclosed in UI
- **TEF explicit formula** (§4.1.5 and §4.1.11): `protein_g × 4 × 0.25 + carbs_g × 4 × 0.08 + fat_g × 9 × 0.03` or 10% flat fallback
- **NEAT workout-step subtraction** (§4.1.5): full regex-based algorithm preserved from Flask — prevents device-logged runs from double-counting between EAT and NEAT
- **Expanded scientific citations** (§4.1.5): 13 references covering RMR, TEF, protein targets, NEAT; matched-or-exceeded Flask's reference pool
- **Archetype field removed** (§4.1.5, §4.1.11, §4.8.4): the onboarding archetype label was an AI call with no downstream effect on scoring, chatbot, or features — a demo-value-only label. Cut. Profile Insight AI call remains (one short Haiku narration on reveal; not persisted as reusable dashboard content).
- **Weight projection line** (§4.3.4): dashed trajectory from current weight toward target weight using current deficit and Flask's projection formula; user toggle "Show projection," default on for weight-goal users
- **Activity Calendar** (§4.3.2a, new subsection): 7-column color-coded month grid showing strength/cardio/both/rest/no-data per day; lives in the Fitness Progress tab; tap cell → Day Detail
- **Workout Checklist Overlay** (§4.3.5a, full spec): live in-session set-tracking full-screen modal with per-set checkboxes, weight/reps, session timer, rest timer with OS-level notifications, minimize-to-banner pattern, PR detection, long-idle prompt, persistence across backgrounding
- **90-day streak scroll** (§4.2.2): replaces minimal "7-day streak" indicator; day tap → full Day Detail
- **Streak "logged" definition expanded** — any meaningful interaction across any category (not just Nutrition/Fitness)
- **Deficit/surplus history chart** (§4.4 Progress tab): daily `TDEE − calories_consumed` line chart with goal-appropriate target line
- **Progress/History tab pattern** (§4.3.2, §4.4.2): each category detail view now has its own tab structure tuned to its nature — Fitness and Nutrition each get Today / Progress / History
- **Day Detail shared surface** (§4.2a, new section): full-day view showing all categories for the tapped date, stat grid + collapsible category sections + add-entry-for-this-date action; reachable from streak bar, activity calendar, all history charts, Day Timeline, chatbot deep-links
- **Sugar/fiber/sodium user-adjustable targets** (§4.4.9): FDA defaults (50g / 30g / 2300mg) restored to v1 (was deferred to v2+ in prior drafts); page 2 of the swipeable macro card; editable in Settings
- **Calorie rollover with +200 kcal cap** (§4.4.10): cap prevents binge-enabler pattern; visible indicator on the Daily Nutrition Summary when active ("Today's target: 2,200 kcal (2,000 base + 200 rollover from yesterday)")
- **Auto-adjust transparency + user-confirm** (§4.4.10): auto-adjust never changes targets silently; presents a confirm card with before/after values, the math, and Apply / Keep Current / Explain More buttons; evaluation runs on weight log (not nightly cron)
- **Recent Meals quick-chips** (§4.4.2): capped at 5 (trimmed from Flask's 20), ranked deterministically by frequency × recency × time-of-day correlation
- **Pantry Scanner preserved Pro-gated** (§4.4.8): unchanged from prior spec, verified in audit
- **Voice-to-text auto-stop** (§4.4.4, §4.3.12): auto-stop behavior preserved when user taps Log Meal / Log Workout

**(6) Email architecture rewrite (§4.6.8).** Deterministic 3-signal classifier replaces AI-per-email model. Signal 1: sender & domain rules (from Flask, preserved — +1/-1 on sender, ±0.5 on domain). Signal 2: content keywords (v1.18 new — user-tunable boost/demote keyword lists with 15 seeded defaults each). Signal 3: thread behavior (v1.18 new — +1 if user replied to prior message in thread, +0.5 if user marked prior sender Important, +0.3 if direct reply to user's sent message). Sub-millisecond classification, $0 per email, "Why is this Important?" breakdown view for explainability. Shared rules across Gmail + Outlook per user. AI retained for only two surfaces: (a) daily Haiku email summary (one scheduled call per user per day, 3-5 bullet output, descriptive-AI compliant per §3.2), (b) AI-Drafted Replies (Pro-gated, Sonnet, user-invoked). Style learning for draft replies uses deterministic statistical pattern analysis, not AI.

**(7) Descriptive vs Prescriptive AI distinction (§3.2).** Explicit language added distinguishing two modes of AI-generated natural-language copy. **Descriptive AI** (permitted, user-invoked only): AI narrating already-computed numbers when the user explicitly asks (tapping "Explain" on a score, chatbot queries). **Prescriptive AI** (banned everywhere except Notifications): AI telling users what they should do, coaching, moralizing, unsolicited insights. Notifications (§4.9) retain their carve-out as the sole proactive-AI surface, justified by the user's explicit opt-in to the push-notification channel. Closes a coherence gap between §1.3 / §2.6 / §3.4 principles and the §4.9 AI evaluator's proactive copy generation. Flask's Pattern Insight feature cited as correctly cut (proactive) vs. a potential user-invoked "Explain my week" surface that would be acceptable.

**(8) Goal library trimmed to 22 goals** (§4.10.3): from 40 to 22, at 5–7 per category. Cut goals that required fuzzy data, duplicated others, or depended on untracked features. Retained goals all have a clean deterministic data hook, clear target metric, and unambiguous progress calculation. Free-form custom goals remain explicitly out of scope at v1. Category counts: Fitness 6, Nutrition 5, Finance 5, Time 6.

**(9) Pricing review + 2-account cap dropped** (§1.6, §4.8.5, §4.8.6). Pricing is now under review pending founder-led cost modeling and user price-sensitivity testing. The 2-account cap on Core (locked in v1.13–v1.17) is dropped in v1.18 because linear OAuth cost doesn't justify the friction it creates for personal+work email/calendar users. Final pricing and any reintroduced softer account gate deferred to Section 13. The $8.99/$17.99 baseline remains as the working assumption but is not locked.

**(10) Garmin/Strava launch requirement** (§1.7, §8.15.5, new subsection). Life Dashboard will not ship v1 without at least one of Strava or Garmin as a native Fitness integration — the Ambitious Generalist target user's overlap with these services makes shipping without a material downgrade unacceptable. **Strava is the v1 floor** (already drafted in §8 as read-only with AI-exclusion per the Nov 2024 Strava ToS). **Garmin is applied-for Week 0** with a decision gate at Week 16: if approved by Week 16, Garmin ships at v1 alongside Strava; if delayed, v1 ships Strava-only and Garmin becomes v1.1+. Analytics vendor decision locked: self-hosted PostHog on existing AWS infrastructure (lowest cost + no third-party privacy implications).

**Retroactive edits in same session:** §1.1, §1.3, §1.4, §1.5, §1.6, §1.7, §1.8, §1.9, §1.10 (narrative rewrites for name, category, Garmin/Strava, descriptive-AI carve-out mention); §2.1, §2.2 (vision rewrite, Time category elevation from "catch-all container" to "rigorously-scoped passive-data score"); §3.2 (descriptive/prescriptive distinction); §4.1.5 + §4.1.11 + §4.1.12 (full rewrite for RMR dual-formula, TEF, NEAT, citations, archetype removal, equal-choice training plan fork, Haiku replaces Sonnet for insight); §4.2.2 (90-day streak bar); §4.2.4 (scoring architecture lock); §4.2.9 (color bands back); §4.2a (new Day Detail section); §4.3.2 (Today/Progress/History tab restructure); §4.3.2a (new Activity Calendar subsection); §4.3.4 (weight projection line); §4.3.5a (Workout Checklist Overlay full spec); §4.4.2 (Today/Progress/History tab restructure, Recent Meals chips); §4.4.9 (sugar/fiber/sodium targets); §4.4.10 (rollover cap + auto-adjust transparency); §4.6.8 (deterministic email rewrite); §4.6.15 (Time score rewrite); §4.8.4 (Your Plan card deterministic snapshot); §4.10.3 (goal library trim); §8.15.5 (Garmin subsection new); §8.15.6 (launch-blocker roadmap updated to include Garmin). This is the largest retcon session to date — approximately 1,700 lines of changes across 25+ sections. Sections 5–8 technical architecture prose retains "APEX" as internal codename where natural; ~159 APEX references remain and are intentional.

**Forward-Build Notes updated:** Section 9 (Scoring Engine) now inherits the full Time Score 5-signal spec, the penalty-from-perfect-day architecture, signal-specific safe ranges, tiered proration rules, Overall Score = Σ(category × weight / 100) composition, and the minimum-data thresholds. Section 10 (AI Requirements) inherits the descriptive/prescriptive AI distinction as a global rule, the deterministic email classifier architecture, and the archetype-AI-call removal. Section 13 (Business Requirements) inherits the pricing review status, the dropped 2-account cap, and the Pro cut-line candidates surfaced throughout (Pantry Scanner, AI Draft Replies, Premium Scan, CSV/PDF export, long-term archived history, style learning).

**2026-04-20 — v1.17** — Section 8 (Connections) drafted in full, 18 subsections covering the complete v1 connection catalog: 25 active connections spanning iOS and Android device-native, cloud/OAuth, vendor-metered, and infrastructure/delivery classes, each specified with OAuth scopes, sync cadence, webhook behavior, fallback posture, tier placement, and privacy posture. Three-tier cost classification framework locked (device-native/low, cloud/moderate, vendor-metered/higher) with explicit packaging implications per tier. Connection inventory by class — iOS device-native (6): Apple HealthKit, Apple EventKit, Apple Family Controls + DeviceActivityReport (entitlement launch-blocker), CoreLocation + Visit Monitoring, Core Motion, Apple WeatherKit. Android device-native (6): Google Health Connect (primary), Google Fit (fallback), Android UsageStatsManager, Android Geofencing + Activity Recognition, Android Calendar Provider, OpenWeatherMap. Cloud/OAuth (5): Google Calendar, Gmail (Google OAuth verification launch-blocker), Microsoft Outlook Mail (Microsoft verification launch-blocker), Microsoft Outlook Calendar, Strava (read-only, AI-excluded). Vendor-metered (3): Plaid Transactions only (Plaid approval launch-blocker), Google Places (aggressively cached), Open Food Facts (server-cached). Infrastructure/delivery (5): Clerk, RevenueCat, Expo Notifications, SendGrid, Anthropic. Explicitly NOT in v1 (documented in 8.9): Strava AI-context lift (v1.1 pending clarification), Plaid Liabilities (v1.1+ for Net Worth), Plaid Investments (v2+), Garmin direct API (via HealthKit/Health Connect instead), expanded HealthKit/Health Connect scope, Uber, Apple Wallet passes, receipt OCR, Spotify, HomeKit, developer/work productivity connections, and explicitly-unsupported consumer messaging (iCloud Mail, iMessage, WhatsApp, SMS) with reasons documented. Launch-blocker roadmap (8.15): four approvals required before v1 — Apple Family Controls entitlement (1-4 weeks, apply at Week 0), Plaid Production (2-8 weeks, apply at Week 0), Google OAuth verification for Gmail sensitive scopes (4-8 weeks including CASA Tier 2 assessment), Microsoft Graph verification (1-4 weeks). Per-connection tier placement table (8.16) is the authoritative source for Section 13 business cut-lines. Retcon applied in same session (v1.17 consolidated multiple interlocking scope decisions): (1) **Net worth cut from v1** — Section 4.5.9 fully rewritten from "Savings and Net Worth Subsystem" to "Savings Subsystem" with explicit v1-scope-limitation reasoning (v1's Plaid-Transactions-only scope lacks Liabilities for student loans and mortgages, so "net worth" would be misleading for users with significant debts); v1.1+ roadmap documented showing Liabilities → Net Worth path and Investments → completes-Net-Worth path; net worth references removed from Section 2 psychographic persona (tracks "spending" not "net worth"), Section 4.5 overview, 4.5.2 detail view ordering (Budget/Spending/Bills/Savings), 4.5 aggregation logic, 4.2.6 FAB table "Check Balances" label, Section 6.2.5 data inventory (`manual_assets_debts` row deleted, `account_balances_history` repurposed to savings balance trend), Section 4.11 export manifest and ZIP tree (`manual_assets_debts.csv` removed), and DB schema list; the `manual_assets_debts` table fully deprecated from v1 with all references removed; Finance goal library verified clean (no net-worth goal in the 9-goal Finance library — all 9 work with Transactions-only scope). (2) **Plaid narrowed to Transactions only** — 4.5.14 Connection Dependencies restructured with Transactions as sole required product; Auth, Identity, and Balance explicitly marked "not used at v1" with rationale (Life Dashboard doesn't move money, Clerk handles user identity, Transactions' cached daily balance is sufficient for Savings balance trend per Plaid's documented behavior); Liabilities and Investments deferred to v1.1+ and v2+ respectively; Section 7.11.2 OAuth scopes updated to reflect Transactions-only; Section 7.11.6 Plaid security line updated; Forward-Build Notes Plaid list updated. (3) **Multi-account tier gate restructured** — from "Pro = multi-account, Core = single-account" to "Core = 2 email accounts total + 2 calendar accounts total (mixed vendors allowed), Pro = unlimited." Section 1.6 tier descriptions rewritten end-to-end with "unified life dashboard for most users" positioning for Core and "power-user version for heavier workflows" for Pro; Section 4.8.5 plan comparison table updated with "Email accounts (Gmail + Outlook, any mix): Up to 2 total / Unlimited" and equivalent for calendars; Section 4.8.6 Multi-Account Connections block rewritten with tier limits, paywall trigger behavior (3rd email on Core triggers paywall), Plaid-not-tier-gated clarification; Section 3.8 hard rules and Application bullets updated to reflect new positioning ("cloud breadth beyond typical case (3+ accounts) in Pro" rather than "multi-account in Pro"); Section 4.11.18 Pro differentiation list updated. Backend cost rationale preserved: each additional cloud account carries linear cost multiplier, so unbounded multi-account belongs in Pro tier. (4) **Strava added as read-only connection with AI-exclusion** — 4.3 cardio sections updated with Strava as v1 connection (corrected from previous "blocked" language); 6.6.3 hard exclusion rule added for Strava-sourced rows at AI gateway with full rationale and v1.1 lift path (submit written clarification to Strava developer relations; if confirmed Life Dashboard's inference-only user-facing insights fall under their "feedback to users" exemption, lift filter in v1.1); 6.4.2 consent model updated with `strava` source_key (always treated as OFF at gateway regardless of stored value, with UI explanatory copy), plus `weather`, `outlook_calendar`, `health_connect` source_keys added for completeness; Section 5.11 processor inventory row added with AI-excluded note. (5) **Weather context added to home dashboard** — Section 4.2.2 header strip updated with weather indicator (current temperature + condition icon) between Life Dashboard wordmark and profile icon; data source specified (Apple WeatherKit on iOS, OpenWeatherMap free tier on Android); privacy posture specified (weather is low-sensitivity ambient context, optional AI consent via `weather` source_key default OFF, widget works regardless of AI consent); fallback specified (widget hidden if location denied or weather fetch fails); Section 5.11 processor inventory rows added for WeatherKit and OpenWeatherMap. (6) **5.11 processor inventory expanded** to reflect the full v1 vendor list with accurate cost models (Plaid Transactions-only subscription, not multi-product; Strava with AI-exclusion note; WeatherKit and OpenWeatherMap as new weather providers; Open Food Facts as new free-but-metered food data source; Android Health Connect and Google Fit rows separated). Forward-Build Notes: obsolete v1.13 Section 8 editor note removed from the document (superseded by the actual Section 8 draft); Section 13 (Business Requirements) inherits the 8.16 tier placement table as the authoritative connection-level source; Section 9 (Scoring Engine) inherits the graceful-degradation posture from 8.14.3; Section 10 (AI Requirements) inherits the Strava exclusion rule and the general per-source redaction architecture from 6.6.3; Section 11 (Infrastructure) must provision the AWS KMS CMKs, WAF rules, and webhook signature validation paths referenced in Section 7 and Section 8. Section 8 is 1,796 lines — the largest section to date after Section 4.6 and Section 4.7. The v1.17 retcon was the largest single-version scope change since v1.13 (pricing/tiering retcon), consolidating Plaid-Transactions-only + net-worth cut + multi-account tier change + Strava addition + weather widget + Section 8 draft into a single atomic version.

**2026-04-20 — v1.16** — Section 7 (Security Requirements) drafted in full, 20 subsections covering threat model, encryption at rest and in transit, key management, secrets management, identity and access management, authentication and session security, API security, client-side security, OAuth and third-party credential security, webhook security, input validation and injection defense, audit logging, vulnerability management, security monitoring and alerting, incident response playbook, supply chain security, penetration testing posture, and security review cadence. Key decisions locked per founder's Q&A. Threat model (7.2): finance-app posture calibrated to Copilot/Monarch level — automated credential stuffing, banking trojans, targeted social engineering, phishing-mimic apps all in scope; nation-state actors, hardware-level attacks, and quantum adversaries explicitly out of scope; 8 ranked critical assets with Plaid tokens as highest-value target. Field-level encryption (7.3) minimal scope: KMS field-level encryption applied ONLY to OAuth tokens (Plaid, Gmail, Outlook, Calendar) with transaction data, email metadata, location visits, body weight, screen time all relying on database-at-rest encryption (Neon default) — matches industry-standard finance app posture per 4.5.4, avoids operational cost of encrypting queryable data. KMS architecture: single Customer Master Key `apex-prod-token-encryption-cmk`, AWS-managed annual automatic rotation, direct CMK encryption (no per-token derived keys at v1), scoped IAM access for API and worker tiers. Encryption in transit (7.4): TLS 1.2+ everywhere, TLS 1.3 preferred, HSTS with 1-year max-age on all API responses, ACM-managed certificates with automatic renewal, no certificate pinning at v1 (operational complexity > threat model benefit), no mTLS. Personnel access (7.7): solo-founder stage with full admin access documented as known single-person risk; MFA required on every access path (AWS SSO, provider consoles); all admin actions logged to CloudTrail; succession documentation; role separation deferred to team > 1 with roles pre-specified (developer / support / founder-CTO / finance). AWS accounts isolated three ways (dev/staging/prod), IAM Identity Center for humans, IAM roles for workloads, GitHub Actions OIDC federation for CI/CD — no long-lived AWS credentials anywhere except emergency break-glass IAM user (offline-stored, CloudTrail-alerted on any use, rotated annually). Authentication (7.8): Clerk password policy defaults (8+ chars, HIBP breach check, bcrypt storage) accepted; MFA NOT available in UI at v1 per founder scope call (Clerk supports but UI hidden, deferred to v1.1+); session JWTs 1-hour TTL with Clerk SDK transparent refresh, 90-day absolute lifetime, no idle timeout — App Lock (per 4.8.10) provides local device security gate; password change invalidates all other sessions; biometric unlock is local UX gate not server auth factor. API security (7.9): every endpoint requires authentication by default with explicit exceptions for webhooks and health check; rate limiting in Hono middleware with Redis sliding-window counters — per-user 100/min sustained + 500 burst, per-IP unauthenticated 30/min, endpoint-specific limits for chatbot/scan/export/deletion; AWS WAF with Core Rule Set + SQL Injection Rules + Known Bad Inputs + IP Reputation List; AWS Shield Standard for L3/L4 DDoS; Zod schema validation on every endpoint with strict mode (unknown fields rejected); authorization by user_id scoping at middleware layer (cannot forget to check); UUID v7 IDs prevent IDOR enumeration; CORS restricted to known mobile app origins. Client-side security (7.10): secure token storage in iOS Keychain / Android EncryptedSharedPreferences, no API keys on client (backend is AI gateway), NO jailbreak/root detection per founder Q10 decision (high false-positive, cat-and-mouse game, alienates power users), no certificate pinning at v1, Proguard/R8 for Android, no additional JS obfuscation on React Native bundle, debug output stripped in production, deep links validated against allowlist, local SQLite relies on OS file-based encryption not SQLCipher. OAuth security (7.11): standard OAuth 2.0 with PKCE where supported, minimum-necessary scopes requested per provider, state parameter CSRF defense on callback, KMS encryption on token storage, automatic refresh via worker, daily connection integrity check job; Life Dashboard has read-only Plaid access (Auth + Transactions + Identity products only, not Transfer or Payments — full token compromise enables data read but not financial action). Webhook security (7.12): signature verification required on every inbound webhook (Plaid JWT/JWKS, RevenueCat HMAC-SHA256, SendGrid ECDSA, Clerk Svix), 5-minute timestamp window for replay defense, idempotency key enforcement via Redis with 24-hour TTL, conservative rate limits above expected peak. Input validation (7.13): schema-based Zod validation on all endpoints, drizzle-orm parameterized queries only (no raw SQL string interpolation), SSRF defense via known-endpoint allowlist for outbound requests, AI prompt injection defense via data-not-instructions framing (detailed in Section 10), file upload validation with MIME type verification and size caps, UPC/EAN format + checksum validation for barcodes. Audit logging (7.14) standard depth per Q9 decision: authentication events, account-affecting actions, data access actions (exports, sync completions), integration actions, privacy/consent changes, administrative actions, security anomalies; explicit NO-LOG list for user-submitted content, passwords, tokens, session JWTs, HTTP request bodies; Axiom 30-day retention at launch, CloudTrail 90-day queryable + S3 archival per Section 12. Vulnerability management (7.15): Dependabot enabled with security updates and auto-merge for patch-level, weekly founder review of minor/major, GitHub Security Advisories integrated, optional Semgrep SAST, ECR container scanning, patch SLAs by CVSS severity (Critical 72h / High 7d / Medium 30d / Low 90d); vulnerability disclosure channel via monitored security@apexlife.app inbox with 72-hour acknowledgment commitment — no formal published responsible disclosure policy at v1 per founder Q4 decision given APEX has no website yet; published policy deferred to when website ships. Monitoring and alerting (7.16): three-layer observability (Sentry errors, CloudWatch infrastructure, Axiom logs), 4-tier alert severities (Critical/High/Medium/Low) with routing (Critical → Sentry+email+SMS, High → Sentry+email, Medium → Sentry, Low → Sentry daily review), specific critical-alert triggers documented (credential stuffing signal, KMS operation denied, unexpected Secrets Manager access, export job anomaly, purge failure, webhook signature failure threshold), founder reachable 24/7 for Critical via SMS; scale-out trigger to pager-duty rotation at sustained >5 High alerts/week for 4 weeks. Incident response (7.17): 4-tier incident classification (P0/P1/P2/P3), 5-phase response (Detect / Contain / Eradicate / Recover / Review) with specific timelines aligned to 6.14.3 (72h regulator notification, 7-day user notification for High-tier breach), evidence preservation requirements, user communication templates pre-drafted for common incident types, annual tabletop rehearsal, post-incident review doc required for every P0 and P1. Supply chain security (7.18): pinned package versions, npm ci in CI (strict lockfile), base image SHA-pinning, ECR scanning, GitHub Actions SHA-pinned actions, OIDC federation, SBOM generation per release. Pentest (7.19): no pre-launch pentest — responsible disclosure channel + internal review at 6-month mark covers launch window; annual external penetration test starting year 2, scope includes API + auth + OAuth + client-side; no red team or bug bounty at v1. Security review cadence (7.20): weekly Dependabot and alert triage, monthly Critical/High alert review and admin action review, quarterly backup restoration drill + access review + webhook validation spot-check, semiannual internal OWASP self-assessment + playbook review, annual threat model revision + secret rotation + pentest + Section 7 PRD revision. Cross-section impacts: Section 10 (AI Requirements) inherits the AI prompt injection defense framing from 7.13.8; Section 11 (Infrastructure) must provision AWS WAF, Shield Standard, and KMS CMK per specifications; Section 12 (Compliance) owns the operational side of the 7.17 incident response playbook for regulator notifications and SOC 2 / certification-path if pursued. Retroactive edit assessment: Section 4.8.10 (Security Settings) verified consistent with 7.8.2 MFA-not-available-at-v1 decision — no changes needed; 4.1.9 paywall, 5.6 authentication, 6.14 breach response all verified consistent. Section 7 establishes the defense specification; Section 6 is the protection commitment; Section 12 is the compliance proof layer.

**2026-04-20 — v1.15** — Section 6 (Data & Privacy Requirements) drafted in full, 17 subsections covering privacy principles, full data inventory with classification, sensitivity tiers, consent architecture, data use matrix, AI data handling, redaction and pseudonymization rules, canonical retention policies, deletion and purge mechanics, user rights architecture, third-party processor inventory, data residency and cross-border transfer, analytics and telemetry posture, breach response framework, privacy-by-design commitments, minor and vulnerable user protections, and data governance review cadence. Key decisions locked per founder's direction. Sensitivity tiers: three tiers — Low / Medium / High (no Public tier since APEX holds no public data). Low covers preferences, archetype, anonymous telemetry. Medium covers routine tracking (meals, workouts, tasks, goals, derived scores, notifications history, audit-log metadata). High covers financial transactions, email metadata + body text, OAuth tokens, screen time per-app, location visits, raw calendar titles, Day Timeline source data, chatbot queries, and all biometric and body-composition data. Consent architecture: per-source Connect toggle + per-source AI toggle, both defaulting OFF at signup, no finer granularity (explicit rejection of per-purpose or per-data-type sub-consents to avoid consent fatigue). Once Connect is granted, data flows actively into features; the only secondary control is the per-source AI toggle. Revocation is one tap from Settings → Privacy. Manual-entered data sources (meals, workouts, tasks) also have AI consent toggles so users can exclude even their own typed data. AI data handling (6.6) consolidated as single source of truth referenced by Sections 4.7, 4.9, 4.4, 4.3, 4.6, 10 — every Anthropic call routes through the unified gateway (per 5.9), consent gate runs before context assembly, redaction rules apply before serialization, audit record written with metadata only (no prompt content archived). Redaction ruleset (6.7) formal: categorical (merchants → MCC categories, calendar titles → classified category), range (transaction amounts → buckets), tokenization (contact names → [Contact-N]), suppression (full email bodies, raw coordinates, event descriptions), pseudonymization (unlabeled locations). Raw escalation allowed only when user query explicitly references the data AND AI consent for that source is ON — logged in chatbot audit as `raw_escalation: [field]`. Notification payload redaction stricter than AI redaction (lock-screen visible): amounts suppressed by default with Show Amounts opt-in, merchant names and medical terms and event titles suppressed unconditionally. Retention (6.8) canonical matrix: hot tier 90 days for Core / Pro extended recent window (placeholder 2 years, finalized in Section 13); archived tier via Postgres partitioning or R2 JSON snapshots for older data; purge triggers at account deletion or time-based; derived data (scores, progress snapshots, pattern computations, chart aggregations) stays hot indefinitely per founder Q3 decision independent of raw data retention; chatbot/notification/AI call audits at 7 days Core / 30 days Pro. Nightly purge crons at 03:00 UTC (deletion), 04:00 UTC (time-based), 05:00 UTC (hot-to-archived migration), weekly Sunday 06:00 UTC (R2 sweep). User rights (6.10) fully specified for GDPR Articles 15, 16, 17, 18, 20, 21, 22 and CCPA Right to Know/Delete. Correction mechanism (6.10.2): users can edit all APEX-owned data directly in-app; for third-party source errors, users must correct at source (Plaid dispute, Gmail mark-sender) — Life Dashboard cannot force third-party corrections, makes this clear in Settings → Privacy → Data Corrections. Third-party processor inventory (6.11): 14 processors documented in table with DPA status — Clerk, RevenueCat, Plaid, Google, Microsoft, Anthropic, Neon, Upstash, Cloudflare, SendGrid, Expo, AWS, Sentry, Axiom. Explicit no-sale commitment and structural absence of any data-sharing path to advertisers or data brokers. Data residency: US-only at v1 with EU users covered by Standard Contractual Clauses; EU residency deferred to v1.1+ (Neon multi-region, Upstash EU, AWS EU regions, user-pinning-by-residency). Analytics and telemetry (6.13): no third-party analytics at v1 (no PostHog, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Firebase) — all product analytics captured in APEX's own `analytics_events` table with hashed user_id and no PII content in event properties; opt-out toggle default ON. Breach response framework (6.14): detection → 72h regulator notification (GDPR Article 33) → 7-day user notification for any High-tier data breach, 1000-user threshold for Medium-tier, contextual for Low-tier. Aggregate transparency report published annually starting year 2. Minor user posture (6.16): 18+ in ToS, self-attestation only, no age verification, no birthday-based flagging — userbase growth prioritized per founder direction; response only to affirmative signals (parental report, obvious evidence in product data). Vulnerable user safeguards: no shame framing in any category, neutral numbers approach for eating-disorder or financial-distress patterns in chatbot, crisis-signal detection points to professional resources without AI coaching.
