Data lineage (6.15.2) per Q11 decision: every user-data record carries provenance metadata (source, captured_at, captured_via, processed_by, confidence) — NOT surfaced in main product UI (keeps product clean), INCLUDED in export ZIP for users who want to audit. Data governance review cadence: quarterly retention audit + consent state integrity check, semiannual processor inventory review + DPA expiration check, annual Section 6 revision + Privacy Policy review + compliance attestation; triggered reviews for new regulations, processor changes, breaches, material product changes. Cross-section impacts: Section 7 (Security) inherits the encryption-by-tier model from 6.3 — High-tier data requires field-level KMS encryption for OAuth and Plaid tokens; Section 10 (AI Requirements) must reference 6.6 as authoritative AI data handling spec and cannot re-open redaction rules; Section 11 (Infrastructure) must provision purge crons per 6.8.6 schedule and support hot/archived tiering per 6.8.5; Section 12 (Compliance) owns DPA execution per 6.11.5 and breach-response playbook execution per 6.14.6; Section 13 (Business Requirements) must finalize Pro recent-window retention boundary per 6.8.4 based on cost modeling. Sections 4.7, 4.8, 4.9, 4.11 already align with Section 6 commitments (no retroactive edits required). Section 6 is the data contract — changes here cascade to Sections 4, 5, 7, 10, 11, and 12.

**2026-04-20 — v1.14** — Section 5 (Technical Architecture) drafted in full, 16 subsections covering system topology, client architecture, backend architecture, data layer, authentication, sync and ingestion pipelines, background job architecture, AI integration layer, real-time and notification pipeline, external vendor integrations, observability, deployment strategy, data residency, failure modes, and scaling envelope. Key architectural decisions locked per founder's deferral to engineering judgment. Compute: Lambda (API endpoints) + Fargate (long-running workers) hybrid — Lambda for the request-response API layer, Fargate for the notification worker, export generation, purge cron, and other long-running or latency-sensitive work that needs warm processes. Queue: AWS SQS with dead-letter queues per queue; queue topology segments sync-jobs, notification-signals, notification-dispatch, export-jobs, ai-jobs, scheduled-jobs. API framework: Hono on Lambda — chosen for low cold-start overhead, native TypeScript ergonomics, and serverless-optimized middleware model. Client: full offline-first with local SQLite mirror of user's recent data (via expo-sqlite + drizzle-orm), queued writes through a local outbox, delta sync with per-user cursors, and background reconciliation with server-authoritative timestamps; last-write-wins conflict resolution with per-entity overrides for goals (server wins) and meals (additive). Database: Neon PostgreSQL at launch (scale-to-zero economics, PgBouncer included, developer-friendly), with documented migration path to AWS Aurora Serverless v2 when scale demands. Cache: Upstash Redis via HTTP (no VPC complexity from Lambda), pay-per-request — used for API rate limiting, AI cost tracking, chatbot session state (the "RAM-only" commitment from 4.7 is implemented on Redis), notification signal pool working set, AI context container caching, distributed locks. Blob storage: Cloudflare R2 (already locked in 4.11 for exports, extended in 5 to meal scan temp storage and social card templates). Secrets and encryption: AWS Secrets Manager for application secrets + AWS KMS for OAuth token encryption at rest — one AWS-native stack. Observability: three-layer — Sentry (errors + performance), CloudWatch (infrastructure metrics), Axiom (structured logs); dedicated `cost_events` append-only table for per-user / per-vendor / per-feature cost attribution. Feature flags: DIY Postgres `feature_flags` table with client fetch on cold start + 60s Redis cache, matching the `goal_library` pattern from 4.10. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with lint → typecheck → tests → build → staging deploy → smoke tests → manual promote to production, with Lambda alias-based canary rollout. Environments: three (dev local, staging, production), each with isolated AWS accounts and separate Clerk/Plaid/RevenueCat sandbox credentials. Codebase shape: modular monolith with two runtime entrypoints — a single TypeScript codebase containing `api/` routes (Hono on Lambda) and `workers/` handlers (Node on Fargate), sharing a common `domain/` and `data/` layer. Microservices explicitly rejected as premature for v1. Scaling envelope: v1 architecture sized for 50,000 registered users / 20,000 DAU / 200,000 daily AI queries / 10M total transactions / 1M daily API requests / 50,000 daily background jobs, handling 3-5x (up to ~250k users) without re-architecture; triggers for re-architecture documented (DAU >100k for database migration planning, daily AI queries >1M for vendor negotiation, p95 latency >500ms sustained for infrastructure review). Disaster recovery: RTO 4 hours, RPO 1 hour via continuous Neon backups, IaC-defined infrastructure, deployed-from-git code. Schema conventions locked: UUID v7 for all IDs, `updated_at` / `deleted_at` / `sync_version` on all user-data tables to support delta sync. Hot vs archived storage tiering specified: hot tier is the recent window in Postgres with full indexes, archived tier is older history either via Postgres partitioning or R2 JSON snapshots, with nightly migration crons and separate query code paths. Failure isolation comprehensive: per-vendor circuit breakers, graceful degradation documented for every major external dependency (Plaid, Anthropic, Clerk, Neon, R2, Expo push, SendGrid, Cloudflare), offline-first client continues operating on server outage for all reads and queues writes. Notification pipeline end-to-end latency specified: signal emit → push notification in <35 seconds including the intentional 30s evaluator debounce window. AI integration layer centralized in a single gateway module handling key management, cost tracking, model selection, prompt caching, retry policy, and observability — all Anthropic calls route through it. Cross-section impacts: Section 6 (Data & Privacy) must now reference the KMS encryption model and hot/archived tiering; Section 7 (Security) must reference the authentication path through Clerk JWT validation; Section 9 (Scoring Engine) should treat scoring as domain logic that runs in the shared `domain/` layer, callable from both API and worker contexts; Section 10 (AI Requirements) must reference the AI gateway as the single integration point and the `ai_call_log` cost-attribution table; Section 11 (Infrastructure Requirements) now has all major technology choices already specified and should focus on provisioning, IaC, and cost modeling rather than re-opening architectural decisions; Section 12 (Compliance) inherits the US-only-at-launch data residency commitment and the EU v1.1+ path. Section 5 is the architectural blueprint; Section 11 is the operational build order.

**2026-04-20 — v1.13** — Pricing, tiering, infrastructure direction, and cost-discipline retcon across the drafted PRD per founder brief. Pricing updated: Core $8.99/month or $79.99/year; Pro $17.99/month or $149.99/year. Tier naming: "Lite" → "Core" throughout user-facing and spec language, including the onboarding primary-goal "Lite" persona reference. Packaging philosophy locked: Core is the strong single-account version of APEX with Plaid included, one primary cloud account (one email, one calendar), device-native and manual logging broadly supported, 90-day active history, 10 AI queries per day. Pro is the fully connected power-user version with 100 AI queries/day (soft-capped for abuse protection; user-facing copy deliberately avoids "unlimited" framing per 3.8 principle), broader cloud-account coverage and multi-account support, daily AI brief, deeper cross-category correlations, CSV/PDF export, long-term history (recent data hot for fast interactions, older data archived in cheaper storage while remaining exportable and recoverable). The distinction between Core and Pro is scope, account multiplicity, history depth, AI volume, and premium automation — not the presence or absence of finance or any single category. Core delivers the full APEX promise for users with one primary life; Pro expands breadth, volume, and depth. Expensive vendor-backed and AI-heavy features are intentionally scoped to protect unit economics; device-native and local connections are cheaper to support than multi-account cloud and vendor-metered connections, and the tier design reflects that cost reality. Section 1.6 rewritten end-to-end: new tier descriptions, packaging philosophy paragraph, revised unit-economics language replacing the previous "$0.10–0.30 per user per month" commitment with contribution-positive framing after mobile platform fees; the business is designed around deterministic scoring, tightly scoped AI usage, and disciplined packaging of cloud and vendor-metered features so that gross margins improve with scale rather than depending on scale to exist. Section 1.7 backend direction revised: removed Railway as long-term production direction, replaced with AWS-first serverless architecture for compute, queues, storage, and key management, with PostgreSQL as the primary relational datastore; early launch may continue to use leaner managed PostgreSQL infrastructure where appropriate for startup speed and low idle cost, while long-term production architecture targets AWS-native scale patterns. Section 3.8 Margins Are A Product Decision strengthened with hard rules (metered features capped/scoped/packaged intentionally, user-facing "unlimited" never implies infinite backend spend, deterministic scoring as economic foundation, finance in Core while higher-cost cloud breadth and premium AI are Pro-expanded, hot vs archived storage as different cost classes) and expanded Application bullets (AI calls use cheapest acceptable model, prompt/context assembly cached aggressively, background jobs batched, vendor-metered APIs invoked only when user-visible value is produced, multi-account cloud features packaged more tightly than single-account Core flows, historical data tiered between hot interactive and archival storage). Section 4.1.9 paywall updated to new Core/Pro pricing; paywall body revised so it no longer implies only AI is costly — now acknowledges AI responses, cloud connections, and data services all cost money to operate; added Core annual ($79.99/year) to paywall subscription options. Section 4.2.6 FAB expanded with cost-control paragraph explaining that separating logging from chatbot routing is not only cleaner UX but also a cost-control decision; deterministic and native logging paths are used whenever the task does not require reasoning (zero AI cost); chatbot bandwidth is reserved for questions, synthesis, drafting, and ambiguity resolution. Editor notes inserted for undrafted Sections 5, 8, 10, 11 per founder brief, providing directional drafting guidance without drafting full content (AWS-first serverless for Section 5, connection cost tiers with explicit Plaid-in-Core for Section 8, AI-only-where-best for Section 10, AWS-first production backbone with hot/archived separation for Section 11). History language retcon applied globally: "unlimited history" replaced with "long-term history (recent hot, older archived in cheaper storage)" across Section 4.3 Pro-gated features (strength and cardio), Section 4.4.14 Meal History, Section 4.5 Finance trends, Section 4.6 Day Timeline retention, Section 4.10.19 goals tier table, Section 4.11.18 Pro differentiation language. Chatbot tier table (Section 4.7.16) rewritten: Pro quota changed from "Unlimited" to "100 queries/day (soft-capped)" with explanatory paragraph that user-facing copy deliberately avoids "unlimited" framing; cost modeling block (4.7.19) rewritten end-to-end with corrected quota arithmetic (bug fixed in Core heavy-use row), new 60 queries/day Pro-heavy scenario, at-soft-cap scenario showing near-breakeven on chatbot cost alone, annual spend estimate recalculated at 8 queries/day realized usage giving ~$117,000/year across 10,000 users or ~$11.70/user/year (contribution-positive after mobile platform fees at both tiers). Plan comparison table (Section 4.8.5) rewritten end-to-end to reflect new packaging: Plaid explicitly listed as included in both Core and Pro, Primary cloud account in Core vs Multi-account cloud in Pro as the meaningful differentiator, AI chatbot row shows "10/day vs 100/day (soft-capped)" rather than "Unlimited," History row shows "90-day active window" vs "Long-term — recent data hot, older data archived in cheaper storage," Daily AI brief and Cross-category correlations rows added as Pro differentiators, combined pricing row includes annual. Notifications ARR reference updated to new subscription prices. Connections (Section 4.8.6 Multi-Account) confirmed already consistent — Gmail/Outlook multi-account is Pro-gated, Plaid supports multiple accounts within Core. Change Log historical entries preserved as-is per PRD convention — they document decisions at the time of drafting; the v1.13 entry is the source of truth for current spec.

**2026-04-20 — v1.12** — Section 4.11 (Data Export & Account) drafted in full, 23 subsections covering comprehensive data export, account deletion, grace period mechanics, and compliance (GDPR, CCPA). The 4.x range is now complete. Key architectural decisions: Export is available to all tiers (NOT Pro-gated) — this reverses earlier commitments in v1.9/v1.11 where export was positioned as a Pro differentiator. The reversal is driven by GDPR Article 15 compliance: conditioning personal-data access on subscription is a dark pattern with EU regulatory exposure. Pro retains sufficient differentiation through unlimited chatbot, High notification aggressiveness, unlimited history, multi-email-account support, AI email drafts, and advanced pattern analysis. Export mechanics: async generation (target <5min light users, 24-hour SLA heavy users), single comprehensive bundle (no per-category or time-range selection — GDPR framing is "everything, once"), delivered via email with 7-day signed Cloudflare R2 download URL, CSV + JSON formats in a structured ZIP archive with README. Full export contents inventory specified: user profile, nutrition logs, fitness data, finance transactions, life data (calendar/tasks/email metadata/screen time/location visits), goals with progress history, audit trails (chatbot + notification, retention per tier), system state. Explicit exclusions: OAuth tokens, Plaid access tokens, password hashes, push tokens, server logs, cached images, full email bodies (never stored). Rate limit: 1 export per 30 days per user, with support override for genuine emergencies (SAR timelines, subpoenas). Account deletion flow: 4-step confirmation (review → type DELETE → biometric re-auth if enabled → subscription disclosure with App Store deep-link) → 30-day grace period → day-30 permanent purge via nightly cron job. Grace period behavior: signed out immediately, OAuth tokens revoked immediately at all third-party providers (Gmail, Calendar, Outlook, Plaid), push tokens invalidated, data flagged `deletion_pending` but preserved for restore. Restore flow: user signs in during grace → Restore Account screen → tap to restore. Integrations require manual reconnection after restore (OAuth was revoked at initiation). Grace period communications: initial confirmation email at day 0, reminders at day 23 and day 29. Permanent purge at day 30: transactional cascade delete across all user-data tables (20+ tables including v1 additions for goals, notifications, connections, screen time, location, etc.), Clerk user deletion, RevenueCat cleanup, anonymous audit record (no user ID retained), final purge confirmation email. Subscription interaction documented: Life Dashboard cannot cancel subscriptions (Apple/Google own them); deletion flow discloses this explicitly with deep-link to platform subscription management; subscription billing continues unless user cancels externally. Lite vs Pro difference in 4.11: minimal (only audit-retention-depth carries over from 4.7/4.9 into export contents); functionally identical account operations. Zero AI calls originate from 4.11. New database tables: account_deletion_state, user_export_state, export_jobs, deletion_audit (anonymous). State added to users table: active / deletion_pending. Retroactive edits applied: (1) Section 1.6 Business Model — removed "CSV/PDF export" from Pro tier description. (2) Section 4.8.5 Plan Comparison — "Data export" row changed from Lite `—` Pro `✓` to both `✓`. (3) Section 4.8.11 Export My Data — removed Pro-gating language, replaced with "Available to all tiers (GDPR Article 15 compliance)". (4) Section 4.8.17 Edge Cases — removed the now-obsolete edge case about "User is on Lite and taps a Pro-gated row (Export My Data)". Compliance posture: GDPR Article 15 (access), Article 17 (erasure), Article 20 (portability partially via JSON format) all served by user-facing flows. CCPA Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Non-Discrimination all covered. SAR emergency channel (support@apexlife.app) documented for jurisdictional edge cases. Data residency: US-based Neon Postgres at v1 launch; EU-region residency path deferred to Section 12 / v1.1+.

**2026-04-20 — v1.11** — Section 4.10 (Goals System) drafted in full, 23 subsections covering the 40-goal library, five goal types, progress/pace calculation, picker and creation flows, celebration and social card, failure handling with auto-restart, and data persistence. Architectural split locked: onboarding "primary goal" (from 4.1.4 Step 2) is a nutrition engine configuration stored in `user_primary_goal` (renamed from Flask's `user_goals`); "library goals" (Section 4.10) are discrete trackable objectives stored in the new `goals` table. The two are decoupled after creation — editing either does not automatically edit the other. Complete 40-goal v1 catalog specified: Fitness 10 (body weight, PRs, consistency, cardio volume, sleep), Nutrition 10 (streaks, rate goals, period counts for protein/calories/hydration/meal logging/fiber/sugar/alcohol), Finance 9 (per 4.5.13: emergency fund, debt payoff, savings goals, budget streak, savings rate), Life 11 (inbox zero, task completion streaks, screen time caps, calendar discipline, location-visit targets, focus time, active minutes). Five goal types with distinct schemas and math: cumulative numeric (start/current/target with deadline), streak (consecutive qualifying periods), best attempt (threshold achievement), rate (rolling average over window), period count (count within month/week). Each type has specific progress calculation, pace calculation, and deadline semantics. Auto-restart behavior: applies only to cumulative numeric goals; on deadline miss, silently extends deadline by original_duration_days and preserves progress; capped at 3 extensions; default ON via user toggle at goal creation. Streaks silently reset on break and continue; period-count goals archive silently at period end; best-attempt and rate goals have no failure mode. All failure transitions happen without push notifications, without shame framing. Completion experience: full-screen celebration modal with restrained tone (confetti animation, goal name, duration, goal-type-specific stats block, social card preview, Share / Pick Next Goal / Done actions). Social card is 1080×1920 PNG rendered client-side via template library per goal type — no AI, no server round-trip; opens via OS share sheet; user can disable share buttons globally via Privacy settings. Zero AI calls originate from Goals — matches Settings' zero-AI posture. Lite tier: 1 active goal (primary only); Pro tier: 3 active goals (1 primary + 2 secondary). Retroactive edits applied: (1) Section 4.1 — new onboarding step 4.1.5a "First Goal Selection" inserted between Profile Reveal (4.1.5) and Workout Builder (4.1.6), pre-fills a library goal based on the onboarding primary goal with 4 action paths (Use this goal / Customize / Browse all goals / Skip for now); flow update preserves all existing section numbering via "a" suffix pattern consistent with 4.1.7a. (2) Section 4.9.3 — new Goals signal category added with 9 signal types (goal_deadline_3d/1d/today, goal_milestone_50/75, goal_completed, goal_behind_pace_sustained, goal_auto_extended, goal_streak_broken); `goal_completed` explicitly bypasses aggressiveness suppression to always fire as a celebration moment. (3) Section 4.8.7 Privacy — added forward note that v1.11 introduces an optional "Show share buttons on completed goals" toggle (default ON). New database tables: goals, goal_library (server-side config fetched on cold-start), goal_progress_log (daily snapshots for charts), goal_edit_log (audit trail), goal_history (completed goals, 90-day retention Lite / unlimited Pro). Forward-Build Notes for Section 8 (Connections), Section 9 (Scoring Engine), Section 13 (Business Requirements) updated — goals have data-source dependencies on Apple Health / Plaid / calendar / Screen Time etc. that Section 8 must confirm, scoring weights should treat goal-related progress as inputs where applicable, and the 1-vs-3 active goals cut-line is locked for Section 13.

**2026-04-20 — v1.10** — Section 4.9 (Notifications) drafted in full, 21 subsections covering the signal pool architecture that underpins APEX's "robust cross-domain AI system" commitment. Core architectural decisions locked: notifications use a three-layer pipeline — deterministic rules emit typed signals into a server-side pool; a Claude Haiku 4.5 evaluator wakes on signal arrival (with 30s debounce for multi-signal merge opportunity) and decides fire/merge/defer/drop; delivery via APNs/FCM through Expo Notifications. All scheduling is server-side in v1 (no local notifications) for consistent aggressiveness, quiet hours, and dedup enforcement. The signal catalog (4.9.3) specifies ~20 signal types across Fitness/Nutrition/Finance/Life/Account categories with fixed schemas, priorities (critical/high/medium/low), staleness windows, and category tagging. The rules layer (4.9.4) specifies how each data event (Plaid sync, meal log, workout log, timer ticks, RevenueCat webhooks, CoreLocation visits, email classification) produces signals deterministically. The AI evaluator (4.9.5) runs on signal arrival, takes fresh signals + user context + recent notification history, and returns a JSON decision object with composed message text — fallback to hand-written templates on AI failure. Aggressiveness dial (Q3 decision: 2/5/8/day at Low/Medium/High) is the main user knob and gates both daily volume and which priority classes qualify. Medium is default for new users. Lite tier capped at 5/day maximum regardless of setting (High level is Pro-gated); Pro gets the full 8/day at High (Q12 decision: "limited magic at lite maybe cap at 5 per day"). Privacy in notification content: never put sensitive data (amounts, merchant names, medical terms, email content) in lock-screen text, but always push full content (not teasers) — Q10 decision. User-opt-in "Show amounts" toggle surfaces dollar amounts for users willing to accept the privacy tradeoff. Merchant names are never exposed regardless. Deep-link routing maps each signal type to a canonical in-app destination (4.9.12). Quiet hours (user-configured, default OFF) respect critical overrides with a user-overridable sub-toggle; medium/low priority signals queue for end-of-hours batch. AI cost modeling (4.9.16): ~3-10 evaluator calls per user per day, ~$0.0012 per call, estimated $3/user/year (~5% of ARR at both tiers, healthy margin). Notification Audit screen (4.9.15) shows evaluator reasoning per fired notification — transparency mechanism for AI-composed decisions matching the Chatbot Audit pattern. No general in-app notification inbox (Q3 decision) — OS notification center is the history surface; APEX doesn't duplicate it. Calendar event reminders deferred to native OS calendar apps; APEX does NOT duplicate (Q4 decision). Meeting-prep AI ("meeting in 15 min, are you prepared") deferred to v1.1+ per Nathan's call that it requires context APEX can't reliably source yet. New database tables: notification_signals, notifications, notification_preferences, user_push_tokens. Retroactive edits applied: (1) Section 4.1 — new onboarding step 4.1.7a "Notification Permission" inserted between 4.1.7 (Connections) and 4.1.8 (First Dashboard View), presenting the permission ask with context after user has granted data connections; retroactive 4.1.8+ section numbering preserved via "a" suffix rather than renumbering cascade. (2) Section 4.8.8 (Notifications entry point) rewritten end-to-end to reflect the v1.10 architecture: aggressiveness dial with Pro-gated High, category toggles now stop signal emission at rule layer (not just delivery), quiet hours with critical override sub-toggle, Show Amounts toggle, Notification Audit row replacing the stubbed per-type sub-screens. Removed Goal milestones and Subscription updates as separate category toggles — consolidated into Account & billing which is non-disablable for critical alerts. Forward-Build Notes for 4.9 closed out; new cross-section impacts added for Sections 7 (push infrastructure security), 8 (Expo Notifications as new infrastructure dependency), 10 (AI Requirements — Haiku evaluator + prompt caching strategy), 11 (Infrastructure — notification worker process, cron scheduling, push credential management), and 13 (Business — Pro-gated High aggressiveness level as a meaningful paywall differentiator).

**2026-04-20 — v1.9** — Section 4.8 (Settings & Profile) drafted in full, 18 subsections covering the utility layer of the app: profile editing, subscription management, connection management, privacy controls, app preferences, security, notifications entry point, data & account, and support. Key decisions locked: Settings entry is a generic profile silhouette SVG in the top-right of every top-level screen (Home, Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Time); Main Settings uses the iOS grouped-list pattern with push navigation; profile hero row at top shows name + email + plan status without decorative badges; archetype and onboarding insight are preserved as a static souvenir on the Profile screen with no regenerate button (zero AI calls originate from Settings in v1); sectional onboarding redo offers five focused flows (About You / Goal / Daily Life / Workout Plan / Connections) instead of a single "redo everything" button; goal change triggers a confirmation sheet showing numeric before/after of targets; manual macro sliders redesigned as read-only rows on the Profile screen that tap into dedicated edit sheets containing slider + Lock toggle (replaces Flask's inline slider + lock pattern to eliminate accidental drag edits); Subscription screen is comprehensive with current plan block, plan comparison table, billing history, and Restore Purchases; trial banner on Home Dashboard appears only in the final 3 days of trial, dismissable per-day; Connections screen lists Connected sources at top with sync status, Available sources below grouped by category; per-connection detail screen shows status, data pulled, Disconnect button; AI consent toggles are single-source-of-truth in Settings → Privacy (not mirrored on Connection Detail screens); Chatbot Audit shows containers + fields loaded per query without additional AI calls or raw value storage; Notifications is a thin Settings surface deep-linking to Section 4.9; Preferences cover Theme (Dark/Light/Match System — cut Flask's "medium" option), Units (single global toggle, auto-detected from locale), Language (English + Spanish at v1, cut Flask's 10-language partial translation state), Timezone (auto + manual), Haptics; Security covers biometric toggle (mirrors onboarding), opt-in App Lock with Off/Always/1min/5min/15min timeouts, in-app password change via Clerk API with all-devices sign-out after change, in-app email change with verification email, "Sign out of all other devices" bulk action (per-device sessions deferred to v1.1+); Data & Account surfaces Export (Pro-gated), Sign Out, Delete Account entry points with Section 4.11 as authoritative; Delete Account requires typed "DELETE" + biometric re-auth + 30-day grace; Support includes FAQs (in-app bundled markdown, no web help center at v1), Contact Support (pre-filled mailto), Give Feedback form, Rate Life Dashboard, What's New changelog, ToS, Privacy Policy, Open-Source Licenses, and app version footer. New database tables: user_preferences, user_ai_consent, user_macro_locks, user_archetype, chatbot_audit. Retroactive edits applied: Section 4.3.2 Fitness header adds Profile icon to right side; Section 4.4.2 Nutrition header adds Profile icon and removes "Settings" from overflow menu; Section 4.5.2 Finance header adds Profile icon and removes "Settings" from overflow menu; Section 4.6.3 Life header adds Profile icon and removes "Settings, Integrations, Privacy controls" from overflow menu (single-source-of-truth principle — those controls now live only in Settings). Terminology rename applied throughout the PRD body: "Integration" → "Connection" in all user-facing language. Section 8 renamed from "Integration Requirements" to "Connections". Analytics event names renamed: integration_connect_presented → connection_connect_presented, integration_connected → connection_connected, integration_skipped → connection_skipped, integration_connect_tapped_from_fitness → connection_connect_tapped_from_fitness, integration_id → connection_id, target_integration → target_connection. Two instances of "integration" preserved in Executive Summary (Section 1.8 "enterprise-level integration partnerships" and Section 1.9 "The integration work required to match APEX is itself the moat") because they refer to the software engineering sense of integration work, not the user-facing feature. Change Log entries from v1.0–v1.8 preserved as historical record with original "integration" language intact.

**2026-04-19 — v1.8** — Section 4.7 (AI Chatbot) drafted in full, 24 subsections covering the query-only chatbot model that replaces the "chatbot-as-universal-router" pattern locked in v1.6. Key decisions: the FAB opens an overlay containing two distinct surfaces — the chatbot for questions and shortcut buttons that open native logging flows directly; logging does not route through the chat. Overlay geometry: the `+` rotates to `X` in place, a compact rounded chat input bubble slides in to the left of the X with a voice-input mic icon, shortcut buttons stack vertically above the X, and the conversation area grows upward as the user converses. Voice input is on-device native STT with an animated waveform mic button; transcription lands in the input for the user to review and tap send. Conversation state is RAM-only within a session; sessions reset on force-close or 30-minute background. No server-side persistence of chat history in v1. Lite tier: 20 chatbot queries per day with midnight local reset and a soft upgrade prompt at the cap; Pro tier: unlimited with a 200/day soft cap to catch abuse. Model: Claude Haiku 4.5 for all chatbot queries in v1, with Sonnet 4.6 available as a runtime escape hatch per query pattern if quality proves insufficient post-launch. Open-ended cross-domain queries are a v1 capability ("can I afford dinner?", "why am I tired?", "tell me about my day") — the engineering approach is a Context Container Architecture: nine typed containers (ProfileContext, GoalsContext, NutritionContext, FitnessContext, FinanceContext, LifeContext, HistoricalContext, PatternsContext, DayTimelineContext) with fixed schemas, token budgets, staleness policies, and per-source privacy gates; a client-side classifier selects containers per query; total token cap ~8K per query. Redaction is strict by default (merchant names → categories, calendar titles → categories, locations → user-labeled place or "unlabeled") with temporary elevation only when a user's query explicitly asks for raw data. A client-side intent classifier intercepts logging attempts ("log 3 eggs") before any AI call and shows a templated domain-specific redirect at zero cost. Chatbot Audit in Settings → Privacy shows the exact containers sent for each recent query. Retroactive edits applied: Section 4.2.2 home dashboard FAB bullet rewritten; Section 4.2.4 "cross-domain is v2+" note updated to reflect v1 chatbot capability; Section 4.2.6 FAB subsection rewritten end-to-end to describe the two-surface model; Section 4.3.1 "cross-domain is v2+" note updated; Section 4.3.2 fitness FAB bullet rewritten; Section 4.4.2 nutrition FAB bullet rewritten; Section 4.5.2 finance FAB bullet rewritten; Section 4.6.3 life FAB bullet rewritten (removed the "Ask about my day" shortcut that previously pre-filled the chat — users now type that query directly). The v1.6 change log entry documenting the old chatbot-as-router model is preserved for historical completeness; this v1.8 entry supersedes it behaviorally.

**2026-04-19 — v1.7** — Section 4.6 (Time Category) drafted in full, 20 subsections across 70+ pages. Life established as the "integration crown" of APEX: the category where cross-domain data is unified into a single coherent picture of the user's day. Key decisions locked: Today's Focus hero is deterministic (no AI narrative), composed of real priority items from tasks + emails + bills + calendar + goals + workouts with data-backed sub-items; Day Timeline is the flagship feature — minute-by-minute reconstruction of the user's day using all integration sources, shown in three views (Home Dashboard compact strip, Time detail card, fullscreen interactive deep-dive); productivity subsystem unifies Calendar (full CRUD with Google + Apple + Outlook), Email (multi-account Gmail + Outlook with preserved AI importance classification + new AI draft replies + new triage mode), and Tasks (preserved from Flask with new sub-task support); Attention subsystem covers Screen Time (iOS via Family Controls entitlement, Android via UsageStatsManager) and Location/Places (hybrid auto-naming approach — commercial places auto-named via Google Places, residential places named via one-time user prompt after 5+ visits); Time Patterns subsystem shows deterministic cross-domain insights without AI narrative; Goals Dashboard aggregates goals across all four categories with pace indicators (no AI analysis); Time Score uses Time-domain signals plus cross-domain inputs per your Q11 decision. No unsolicited AI insights anywhere in the category per your Q4 decision (AI available on-demand via chatbot only). iCloud Mail constraint acknowledged — not possible, users informed clearly. Apple Family Controls entitlement flagged as launch-blocker requiring 1-4 week approval process; Android screen time available immediately. Privacy Deep Dive subsection added — Life collects the most sensitive data (calendar, email, location, screen time); per-source consent gated with features default ON once consent granted. All data stored server-side per your Q2 decision for cross-device continuity. New database tables: calendar_events, email_accounts, email_threads (unified), screen_time_daily, screen_time_hourly, screen_time_apps_daily, location_visits, named_places, day_timeline_events, day_timeline_source_data, life_patterns, today_focus_cache. Retroactive edit applied: Section 4.2 Home Dashboard layout now includes the Day Timeline preview strip between the four category cards and the FAB.

**2026-04-19 — v1.6 (pre-4.6 cleanup)** — Retroactive updates applied to align earlier sections with product decisions that emerged during 4.5 drafting. Section 4.2.6 (FAB) rewritten to reflect chatbot-as-universal-input pattern: FAB now opens chatbot overlay with category-specific shortcut buttons rather than a separate action menu. Section 4.3 FAB reference updated to match. Section 4.4 FAB reference updated to match. The chatbot is now the universal entry point for logging and AI interaction; shortcut buttons are optional fast paths for common actions, but the chat input always accepts any text/voice input and routes to the correct destination. Section 4.7 (Chatbot) remains the authoritative spec for chatbot UI, routing logic, and destination preview behavior. No other spec changes; only the FAB interaction model was updated.

## Forward-Build Notes (Tracked Dependencies For Future Sections)

This section tracks cross-section dependencies that affect how later sections should be drafted. Updated as the document evolves.

### For Section 4.6 (Time Category) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.7

### For Section 4.7 (AI Chatbot) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.8

### For Section 4.8 (Settings & Profile) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.9

Key commitments locked in during 4.8 drafting that affect other sections:

- **Profile icon entry point** is now on Home, Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, and Life headers (retroactive edits applied in v1.9).
- **Single source of truth for settings** — AI consent toggles, per-data-type deletion, theme, units, language, timezone, haptics, security all live only in Settings. No mirrored controls on category overflow menus.
- **Zero AI in Settings** — the Settings surface generates no AI calls. Archetype and onboarding insight are preserved as static artifacts. This constrains what Sections 4.9, 4.10, 4.11 can ask the Settings surface to do.
- **Connections terminology** is now authoritative in all user-facing language. "Integration" is retained in engineering/spec language and for software engineering concept references only.
- **Sectional onboarding redo** pattern (5 focused flows) is the model for all "redo X" flows elsewhere in the app. Section 4.11 may reference this pattern for data-reset flows.
- **Chatbot Audit** spec: containers + fields, no raw values, no full prompts. Section 10 (AI Requirements) should not assume prompt-logging infrastructure exists.
- **user_ai_consent table** is the canonical per-data-type consent store. Any other section needing to check consent should read from this table.
- **App Lock** is opt-in with Off default. Section 7 (Security) should reference this as the authoritative spec.


Original forward-build bullets (preserved below for historical reference — note that the "universal AI input that routes logging intents" model referenced here was superseded by the query-only two-surface model specified in Section 4.7.2):

- **4.7 is now the authoritative spec** for the FAB-opens-chatbot interaction model established in 4.5 and threaded through 4.6.
- **Cross-section touches:** 4.2 (dashboard FAB), 4.3 (fitness FAB), 4.4 (nutrition FAB), 4.5 (finance FAB), 4.6 (life FAB).
- **Required chatbot capabilities:**
  - Universal text/voice input that routes to correct category logger
  - Destination preview before commit (e.g., "Logging to Nutrition · 3 eggs and toast")
  - Category-specific shortcut buttons surrounding input, contextual to current screen
  - Full cross-domain query handling ("can I afford this dinner?", "how am I on my protein today?", "what did I eat after my last workout?", "tell me about my day")
  - Context awareness of all user data (fitness, nutrition, finance, life, goals, history, day timeline)
  - Free-tier AI interaction counter (3 interactions, then paywall)
  - Natural-language calendar event creation per Section 4.6
  - Natural-language task creation per Section 4.6
  - Privacy-aware context loading per Section 4.6 (respects per-data-type AI toggles)
- **Hybrid routing:** Chatbot must distinguish between logging intents ("log X") and query intents ("tell me about X"). Both are valid; routing differs.
- **Time-specific queries:** The chatbot is where users ask for analysis of their Life data. "How's my life going?" "What patterns should I know about?" "Why am I so tired this week?" — chatbot pulls Time Patterns + Day Timeline + cross-domain data for answers.

### For Section 4.9 (Notifications) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.10

Key commitments locked in during 4.9 drafting that affect other sections:

- **Signal pool + AI evaluator architecture** is the authoritative notification model. Any future "we should notify about X" request routes through emitting a new signal type in the catalog (4.9.3) and updating the rules layer (4.9.4) — not adding a new standalone notification surface.
- **Server-side scheduling** is the v1 commitment. All notification timing (including time-based meal reminders and morning task-due checks) runs through server cron jobs keyed to user timezone. Local notifications deferred to v1.1+ as a possible optimization.
- **Expo Notifications** is the v1 push infrastructure (APNs/FCM proxy). Section 11 (Infrastructure) should treat this as a hard dependency and plan credential management accordingly.
- **Haiku 4.5 evaluator with prompt caching** is the AI pattern. Section 10 (AI Requirements) should reference this as a cost-control pattern and consider it for other high-frequency low-complexity AI calls.
- **No in-app notification inbox.** Section 4.11 (Data Export) exports the `notifications` table via CSV/JSON for users who want portable history, but no browsable inbox UI is built.
- **Aggressiveness dial** is the user-facing control model for "how much AI initiative do I want." If future sections introduce other AI-initiated systems (e.g., an AI daily brief), they should use the same dial or reference it rather than inventing a parallel control.
- **Pro-gated High aggressiveness** is the established Pro differentiator for notifications. Section 13 (Business Requirements) should treat this as locked; changing it requires re-opening the tier economics.
- **Notification Audit table retention** matches Chatbot Audit (7 days Lite / 30 days Pro). Section 6 (Data & Privacy) should reference this pattern.

### For Section 4.10 (Goals System) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.11

Key commitments locked in during 4.10 drafting that affect other sections:

- **Primary goal vs library goals architectural split** — `user_primary_goal` table (renamed from Flask `user_goals`) continues to hold the nutrition engine configuration. New `goals` table holds library goals. Any future section referring to "goals" must disambiguate which it means.
- **40-goal library is the v1 catalog.** Adding goals post-launch is a server-side config deployment (no app update). Deprecating goals requires keeping existing instances functional.
- **Five goal types with distinct schemas.** Any feature introducing goal-like tracking should fit one of these types or propose a new type.
- **Zero AI in Goals.** Social card rendering, progress calc, pace calc are all deterministic. Section 10 (AI Requirements) should treat Goals as a no-AI-cost feature.
- **Goal signal catalog** (9 new signals) is locked in 4.9.3 in v1.11. Section 4.9 rules layer implementations must include goal deadline/milestone/completion/pace rules.
- **Pro-gated 3-goal slot differential** — Lite 1 active / Pro 3 active. Section 13 (Business Requirements) should treat this as a locked tier cut-line.
- **Social card template system** — 40 templates (one per library goal) rendered client-side via React Native Skia. Section 11 (Infrastructure) should include the template asset bundle in the client build.
- **Auto-restart mechanic** is the failure-handling norm for cumulative goals. Section 4.11 (Data Export) should include goal extension history in export.
- **Onboarding goal selection** adds step 4.1.5a between Profile Reveal and Workout Builder. Section 4.1 total step count for onboarding is now: 4.1.2, 4.1.3, 4.1.4 (3 sub-steps), 4.1.5, 4.1.5a, 4.1.6 (optional), 4.1.7, 4.1.7a, 4.1.8, 4.1.9.

### For Section 4.8 (Settings & Privacy) — v1.11 addition

- **Show share buttons toggle** — a new toggle "Show share buttons on completed goals" (default ON) needs to be added to the Privacy screen (4.8.7). The toggle controls whether the celebration modal and goal detail view show a Share button. This is a minor retroactive edit to 4.8.7 pending the final 4.8 polish pass.

### For Section 4.11 (Data Export & Account) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.12

Key commitments locked in during 4.11 drafting that affect other sections:

- **Export is available to all tiers** — reversal of earlier Pro-gating commitments. Section 13 (Business Requirements) must treat this as locked; export is not a Pro differentiator. Pro-gating export creates GDPR Article 15 regulatory exposure.
- **Export mechanics are async** — background worker generates ZIP, uploads to Cloudflare R2, sends signed URL via email. Section 11 (Infrastructure) needs to provision: Cloudflare R2 bucket with 7-day retention policy, SendGrid (or equivalent) for transactional email, a dedicated export worker process.
- **Account deletion state machine** adds `active` and `deletion_pending` states to the users table. Section 5 (Technical Architecture) and Section 6 (Data & Privacy) should reference this as the authoritative account state model. All user-data queries must filter on `state = 'active'`.
- **30-day grace period** is the authoritative deletion window. Cannot be extended by product decisions elsewhere. Support override exists for legal holds (covered in Section 12).
- **OAuth revocation at deletion initiation** — Section 7 (Security) should reference this as the canonical "account closure" pattern and extend it to other credential types if needed (e.g., future API tokens, webhook secrets).
- **Third-party service cleanup list** (Gmail, Calendar, Outlook, Plaid, Apple Health via sign-out) is authoritative. Section 8 (Connections) should add revocation behavior to the connection detail spec for each integration type.
- **Anonymous deletion_audit table** retained indefinitely for compliance reporting. Section 12 (Compliance) owns the reporting cadence; Section 11 (Infrastructure) treats this as a special retention-exempt table.
- **Rate limit: 1 export per 30 days** — server-enforced. Section 10 (AI Requirements) / Section 11 (Infrastructure) should treat this as a soft-coded policy constant, not hard-wired.
- **ZIP size cap at 1 GB** with multi-part splitting. Section 11 (Infrastructure) should size R2 storage accordingly for concurrent heavy exports.
- **Subscription cannot be cancelled by APEX** — confirmed at deletion flow (Apple/Google own the subscription). Section 13 (Business) should note this in any pricing discussion; Section 4.8.5 Subscription screen is already aligned.
- **Purge cron timing**: nightly at 03:00 UTC. Section 11 (Infrastructure) needs a dedicated purge worker with transactional guarantees.
- **Data residency**: US-based Neon Postgres at v1. EU-region residency deferred to v1.1+. Section 12 (Compliance) tracks the EU compliance timeline.

### 4.x Range Complete

All feature-requirements sub-sections (4.1 through 4.11) are now drafted. The document is ready for the engineering/compliance/business sections (5–16). Consolidated cross-section impacts from the 4.x range are captured in the individual `✅ COMPLETED` blocks above.

### For Section 7 (Security Requirements) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.16

Section 7 is drafted. Cross-section impacts:

- **Section 10 (AI Requirements)**: inherits the AI prompt injection defense framing from 7.13.8 (data-not-instructions, system-prompt hardening, output-trust model). Section 10 specifies the prompt engineering patterns; Section 7 establishes the threat framing. Section 10 cannot re-open redaction (owned by 6.7) or consent gates (owned by 6.4 / 6.6.2).
- **Section 11 (Infrastructure)**: must provision AWS WAF with the managed rule sets specified in 7.9.4 (Core Rule Set, SQL Injection, Known Bad Inputs, IP Reputation), AWS Shield Standard, and the KMS CMK `apex-prod-token-encryption-cmk` per 7.3.4. Must support the break-glass IAM credential model per 7.6.5. Must support three isolated AWS accounts (dev/staging/prod) per 7.7.2.
- **Section 12 (Compliance)**: owns the operational side of the incident response playbook per 7.17 for regulator notification execution and any certification-path work (SOC 2, ISO 27001 if pursued post-launch). Owns long-term CloudTrail archival to S3 per 7.14.3. Owns vulnerability disclosure policy publication when APEX website ships per 7.15.7.
- **Section 13 (Business Requirements)**: pen-test and security review cadence costs (year 2+ external pentest budget per 7.19.2) should be factored into operating cost modeling.
- **Section 14 (Non-Functional)**: the security review cadence in 7.20 establishes weekly/monthly/quarterly/semiannual/annual obligations that Section 14 may formalize as SLAs.

Retroactive edit assessment (completed):
- Section 4.8.10 (Security Settings): verified consistent with 7.8.2 MFA-not-available-at-v1 decision. Settings → Security has Biometric, App Lock, Password Change, Email Change, Sign Out All Other Devices rows — no MFA row per scope call.
- Section 4.1.9 paywall: verified consistent with 7.8 authentication flow
- Section 5.6 authentication: verified consistent with 7.8 session security (Clerk JWT 1-hour TTL, 90-day absolute, transparent refresh, no idle timeout)
- Section 6.14 breach response: verified consistent with 7.17 incident response playbook timelines (72h regulator, 7-day user)
- Section 5.4 API tier: verified consistent with 7.9 API security (middleware stack, WAF, rate limiting, IDOR defense)
- No retroactive edits required — Section 7 builds on 4.x, 5, and 6 without contradiction.

### For Section 6 (Data & Privacy Requirements) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.15

Section 6 is drafted. The data contract is authoritative for all downstream sections:

- **Section 7 (Security)**: must reference 6.3 sensitivity tiers when specifying encryption — High-tier data (Plaid tokens, OAuth tokens, body weight history, financial transactions) requires field-level KMS encryption; Medium-tier relies on database-level encryption at rest; threat model must treat High-tier breach as the critical scenario per 6.14.4.
- **Section 10 (AI Requirements)**: Section 6.6 is the single source of truth for AI data handling. Section 10 specifies prompt engineering and model selection; it cannot re-open redaction rules (6.7), consent gates (6.4.2 / 6.6.2), or audit records (6.6.6). All AI calls route through the unified gateway per 5.9 and 6.6.1.
- **Section 11 (Infrastructure)**: must provision three nightly purge crons (03:00 UTC deletion, 04:00 UTC time-based, 05:00 UTC hot-to-archived migration) per 6.8.6 schedule. Must support hot/archived tiering per 6.8.5 — either Postgres partitioning with older-partition cheaper storage, or R2 JSON snapshots. Must provide R2 lifecycle for temp meal scans (24h) and export ZIPs (7 days).
- **Section 12 (Compliance)**: owns DPA execution for the 14 processors in 6.11.1. Owns breach response playbook per 6.14.6. Owns legal hold procedure per 6.8.7. Owns SAR/rights-request workflow escalation path per 6.10.8. Inherits US-only residency commitment at v1 with EU v1.1+ path per 6.12.3.
- **Section 13 (Business)**: must finalize the Pro "recent window" retention boundary per 6.8.4 (placeholder: 2 years hot) based on per-user storage cost modeling. Tier cut-lines should respect the 6.8.2 retention matrix.
- **Section 14 (Non-Functional)**: data governance review cadence in 6.17 establishes quarterly/semiannual/annual obligations that Section 14 may formalize as SLAs.

Retroactive edit assessment (completed):
- Section 4.7 chatbot: verified consistent with 6.6 AI data handling
- Section 4.8.7 Privacy: verified consistent with 6.4 consent architecture
- Section 4.9 notifications: verified consistent with 6.7.4 payload redaction rules
- Section 4.11 export/deletion: verified consistent with 6.9 purge mechanics and 6.10 user rights
- Section 5.14 data residency: verified consistent with 6.12 cross-border transfer posture
- No retroactive edits required — Section 6 formalizes what 4.x and 5 already committed

### For Section 5 (Technical Architecture) — ✅ COMPLETED in v1.14

Section 5 is drafted. Cross-section impacts that downstream sections must honor:

- **Section 6 (Data & Privacy)**: must reference the KMS encryption model for OAuth tokens (`plaid_tokens`, `gmail_tokens`, Outlook tokens), the hot vs archived storage tiering (Core 90-day hot window, Pro long-term with archived tier), and the `cost_events` append-only table for compliance-relevant audit.
- **Section 7 (Security)**: authentication path runs through Clerk JWT validation with offline public-key caching in Lambda; biometric gates are client-side UX concerns not backend-enforced; OAuth token encryption at rest is KMS-backed and the threat model should reflect that.
- **Section 9 (Scoring Engine)**: scoring is domain logic in the shared `domain/` layer, callable from both API (synchronous chatbot context building) and worker (nightly recomputation) contexts. Zero AI in the scoring path is architecturally enforced — the AI gateway is only invoked from specific call sites.
- **Section 10 (AI Requirements)**: the AI gateway (`backend/src/integrations/anthropic/`) is the single integration point for all Anthropic calls. Every AI call records to the `ai_call_log` cost-attribution table. Quota enforcement runs through Redis counters. Streaming is via SSE proxied from Anthropic through the API tier. Model selection matrix is authoritative in 5.9.
- **Section 11 (Infrastructure Requirements)**: all major technology choices are already specified in Section 5 (Lambda + Fargate hybrid, SQS, Hono, Neon, Upstash Redis, Cloudflare R2, AWS KMS + Secrets Manager, Sentry + CloudWatch + Axiom). Section 11's job is provisioning, IaC authoring, cost modeling per service, reserved capacity decisions, and operational runbooks — not re-opening architectural decisions.
- **Section 12 (Compliance)**: inherits US-only data residency at launch and the documented EU v1.1+ path. Compliance regime coverage (GDPR, CCPA) confirmed as addressed through existing 4.11 flows.
- **Section 13 (Business)**: scaling envelope locked — v1 sized for 50k users / 20k DAU / 200k daily AI queries with 3-5x headroom. Pricing math in Section 13 should reference these numbers for unit economics modeling.

### Pricing & Packaging — v1.21 Locked

v1.13 originally applied a cross-document retcon of pricing, tiering, and cost-discipline language. v1.18 dropped the 2-account cap on Core and flagged pricing as under review. **v1.21 locks final pricing and packaging via Section 13.** These are the commitments all future sections must honor:

- **Pricing (v1.21 locked per §13.2)**: Core $4.99/mo or $49.99/yr; Pro $9.99/mo or $99.99/yr. These supersede every earlier reference. Section 13 is authoritative; any future pricing change requires a §13 pricing review per §13.13.
- **Tier naming**: "Core" replaces "Lite" throughout. Any future section must use "Core" in user-facing and spec language. A potential $2.99 Lite tier is explicitly deferred to v1.1+ as a controlled experiment per §13.14.
- **AI quota (v1.21 tightened per §10.7 and §13.7)**: Core 10/day chatbot; Pro 50/day with 300/day soft cap. Premium Scan 10/day Pro. Pantry 10/day Pro. AI email drafts 20/day Pro. User-facing copy must not use "unlimited" framing for any AI or storage surface — per the Section 3.8 principle.
- **Multi-account gate**: dropped entirely at Core per v1.18. Email, calendar, and bank accounts are unlimited at both tiers. Section 13 confirms this as permanent — not reintroduced as a Pro differentiator.
- **History**: Core 90-day hot window; Pro 90-day hot + unlimited archived. Section 11 (Infrastructure) must treat hot-vs-archived tiering as a first-class architectural requirement.
- **Finance in Core**: Plaid Transactions is explicitly included in Core. Section 8 (Connections) and Section 13 must not reopen this.
- **Infrastructure direction**: AWS-first serverless + PostgreSQL as long-term production target. Railway is prototype-only and must not appear in Section 11 as the production infrastructure choice. Section 5 (Technical Architecture) and Section 11 (Infrastructure) carry the editor notes that lock this direction.
- **Connection cost tiers**: Section 8 drafting must distinguish device-native/low-cost, cloud/moderate-cost, and vendor-metered/higher-cost connections and package accordingly.
- **AI discipline**: Section 10 (AI Requirements) is complete as of v1.20. Closed inventory of 16 AI call sites. No new AI call site may ship without updating §10.2 and the pricing model in §13.7.
- **Trial**: 14 days Pro (extended from 7 in v1.21 to cover 14-day baseline warmup window from §9), force-choice at end, no auto-downgrade or auto-upgrade.
- **Unit economics target**: Core blended contribution ≥60% of net; Pro blended contribution ≥30% of net. Heavy-user (~10%) Pro margin is modeled negative and accepted; quotas and monitoring (§10.8, §13.12) prevent runaway cost.


Legacy forward-build bullets from earlier versions (preserved for historical reference — superseded by the architecture in v1.10):

- ~~Notifications are a robust cross-domain AI system per user direction — not a list of per-category rules.~~ ✅ Delivered via signal pool + evaluator.
- ~~Smart intelligence requirement: Notifications should combine signals~~ ✅ Delivered via multi-signal merge window.
- ~~No per-category notification spec in sections 4.3, 4.4, 4.5 — all deferred to 4.9.~~ ✅ All category-specific notification behavior specified in 4.9.3 signal catalog.

### For Section 8 (Connections) — renamed from "Integration Requirements" in v1.9

Aggregated list of connections required across categories, to be evaluated for v1 approval:

**Fitness category connections:**
- Apple HealthKit (iOS) — weight, steps, workouts, sleep, HRV, resting HR
- Google Fit / Health Connect (Android) — equivalent to HealthKit
- Garmin Connect — advanced wearable data (blocked pending official API access)
- Oura — rings and recovery data
- Whoop — recovery score, HRV, strain
- Strava — cardio sessions (BLOCKED by November 2024 ToS change on AI forwarding — needs resolution)
- Smart scales (Withings, Renpho, Eufy) — typically via HealthKit/Google Fit

**Nutrition category connections:**
- Open Food Facts API — barcode lookup (no auth required)
- Anthropic API — meal parsing, scanning, ingredient ID, suggestions (covered in Section 10)
- Apple HealthKit — water intake sync (optional)

**Finance category connections:**
- Plaid Transactions — transaction sync, account list, cached daily balance (sole Plaid product at v1)
- Plaid Liabilities — loans/mortgages (v1.1+, unlocks net worth)
- Plaid Investments — investment accounts (v2+, completes net worth)

**Time category connections:**
- Google Calendar (OAuth)
- Apple EventKit (native iOS)
- Microsoft Graph — Outlook calendar + email (new in v1)
- Gmail (existing Flask, multi-account in v1)
- Apple Family Controls entitlement — required for iOS Screen Time (LAUNCH BLOCKER, apply immediately)
- Android UsageStatsManager — no approval needed
- CoreLocation with Visit Monitoring (iOS) — passive coarse location
- Android Geofencing + Activity Recognition API
- Google Places API — automatic place naming for commercial locations
- iCloud Mail — NOT SUPPORTED (Apple provides no third-party access)

**Infrastructure connections:**
- Clerk (authentication)
- RevenueCat (billing)
- AWS KMS (encryption keys)
- Cloudflare R2 (file storage)

### For Section 9 (Scoring Engine) — authoritative v1.18 lock

Section 9 drafting is bound by the following locks. These are not open for re-litigation during §9 drafting; they are the decisions that let §9 be drafted at all.

**Core model (v1.18):**
- **Penalty-from-perfect-day** — each category score starts at 100, per-signal penalties subtract from it, user-facing display frames the result as positive progress
- **Category score range:** 0–100, integer for display, 2-decimal internal precision
- **Overall Score composition:** Overall = Σ(category_score_i × weight_i) / 100, where weights sum to 100 and each is constrained to [5, 55]; default weights 25/25/25/25
- **Graceful degradation:** missing signals are excluded from the per-signal sum; remaining weights re-normalize automatically (mathematically equivalent to weight redistribution)

**Minimum data thresholds (v1.18 locked):**
- Category score: ≥2 signals in that category with ≥1 data point each; otherwise show "—"
- Overall Score: ≥2 categories have computed scores; otherwise show "—"
- Day 1 behavior: "—" with CTA until thresholds met; when met, scores populate with population-default baselines and refine over 14-day warmup

**Baselines (v1.18 locked):**
- 30-day rolling median, per-signal (only for signals where personalization is meaningful — Section 9 specifies which)
- 14-day warmup — population defaults during days 0–14, personal baselines from day 15
- Baselines update nightly as part of the 03:30 UTC score-snapshot job
- "Calibrating" pill on category cards for first 14 days, auto-removes

**Proration (v1.18 tiered model):**
- **Accumulating signals** (calories consumed vs target, steps vs goal, screen time vs typical): proration applies with per-signal tuned curves. Flask's `day_progress = clamp((hour-6)/15, 0.33, 1.0)` is insufficient — Section 9 must specify per-signal curves that don't break at the 6am/9pm edges. Suggested pattern: piecewise-sigmoid anchored to each signal's natural daily accumulation profile.
- **Event signals** (workout happened, bill paid, weight logged, meal logged on time): score instantly on occurrence, no proration
- **Trailing-window signals** (7-day strength volume, 30-day savings rate, 14-day sleep rhythm, 30-day attention baseline): use their natural rolling window directly, no proration

**Signal-specific safe ranges (v1.18 locked concept; per-signal values TBD in §9):**
- Each signal has a defined "safe range" — a tolerance band within which full points are earned
- Below the safe range lower bound or above the upper bound (where applicable), signal score decreases via the signal's tuned curve
- Signal-specific examples from v1.18 forward-build decisions: calories ±100 kcal of prorated target, protein ±10g, steps ±500, sleep rhythm ±45 min, rhythm anchors ±60 min, attention ±15% of personal baseline, schedule density ±25% of personal baseline
- Section 9's job: specify the full set across all ~20 signals with research-grounded defaults

**Raw deltas (v1.18 locked):**
- Every score computation returns a `raw_deltas` blob usable by the debug view and by the chatbot
- Schema per signal: `{signal_id, raw_value, target_or_baseline, deviation, normalized_score, weight, contribution_to_score}`
- Surfaced in Settings → Advanced (debug only); NOT exposed as an end-user "explain" button in v1 — the chatbot is the descriptive-AI surface for score explanation per §3.2

**Historical recompute (v1.18 locked):**
- **Unbounded** backfill for edits/additions (any day's score recomputes when that day's data changes)
- **7-day cap** on recompute when the computation would trigger AI cost
- Implementation: recompute on-demand (lazy, Redis 15-min cache) for today; nightly snapshot to immutable `daily_scores` table for historical days

**Category composition (v1.18 starting weights, tunable during §9 drafting):**

*Fitness (7 subsystems, only active ones counted):*
- Movement 20%, Strength 20%, Cardio 15%, Sleep 15%, Recovery 10%, Body 10%, Plan 10%
- Weights redistribute when subsystems have no data (e.g., no plan → redistribute 10% across the 6 active)

*Nutrition (5 signals):*
- Calorie adherence 35%, Protein 25%, Logging consistency 20%, Macro distribution 15%, Hydration 5% (only if hydration is active)

*Finance (5 signals):*
- Budget adherence 35%, Bill on-time rate 25%, Savings rate 20%, Spending consistency 10%, Emergency fund ratio 10%

*Time (5 signals — v1.18 locked, see §4.6.15):*
- Sleep Regularity 25%, Attention Fragmentation 25%, Location Intentionality 20%, Schedule Density Balance 15%, Rhythm Adherence 15%

**Time score cross-domain note (v1.18):** Rhythm Adherence uses data from Fitness (wake/workout times) and Nutrition (meal times), but only as *timestamp data*, not as achievement signals. This preserves the "each category score uses only its own domain's data" principle — timing of anchor events is a Time-domain measurement even when the events originated elsewhere. Overall Score's weighted rollup remains the only place cross-domain aggregation happens.

**Scale toggle (v1.18 locked):**
- Day / Week / Month toggle on category detail views and Home dashboard sparklines
- Deterministic aggregation only — no AI narrative at any scale
- Day = live score for today; Week = 7-day average + best/worst day + completion counts; Month = 30-day average + trend direction + streak stats

**Flask Momentum inheritance (v1.18):** The Flask PWA's Momentum concept is retired as a named user-facing feature. Its math and philosophy (penalty-from-perfect-day, proration, raw_deltas debug, streak logic) are inherited and restructured into the Overall Score as described above. Flask's specific weights (nutrition 40, macros 25, activity 25, tasks 10) are discarded; Section 9 derives fresh weights per category per the tables above.

**Time-score gameability audit (v1.18):** each of the 5 Time signals passed a "can the user game this?" check during v1.18 design. All five score highest when the user's phone passively records day coherence; active gaming (deleting calendar events, leaving phone at home) either breaks the user's actual workflow or produces the desired outcome. Section 9 must preserve this property when specifying per-signal curves — penalties for outliers, not rewards for system-gaming.

### For Section 10 (AI Requirements) — v1.18 additions

- **Descriptive vs prescriptive AI distinction** (§3.2) is the global rule across all AI surfaces. Section 10 must specify which specific AI call sites are prescriptive (notifications only) vs descriptive (chatbot, explain-on-demand, profile-insight narration). No AI call may generate prescriptive copy outside Notifications.
- **Archetype AI call removed** — Section 10's per-call inventory should drop this call.
- **Email classifier is deterministic, not AI** (§4.6.8). Section 10's AI-call inventory should list only: (a) daily email summary (one Haiku call per user per day), (b) AI Draft Reply (Pro-gated Sonnet), (c) meal parsing (Haiku/Sonnet), (d) pantry ingredient ID (Haiku vision), (e) meal suggestion (Haiku), (f) workout parsing (Haiku), (g) workout burn estimation (Haiku), (h) workout plan generation + revision (Sonnet), (i) profile insight narration (Haiku, v1.18 model downgrade from Sonnet), (j) chatbot queries (Haiku), (k) notification evaluator + composer (Haiku). Removed from prior drafts: archetype classification, per-email importance classification, transaction categorization (deterministic via Plaid categories + user rules — no AI in this path at v1).
- **Strava AI-exclusion** (inherited from §8.9 / §6.6.3) remains as a hard gateway filter.
- **Descriptive AI user-invoked pattern:** chatbot is the primary surface; future "Explain this score" buttons on scores and patterns are candidates for v1.1+ but not required in v1.

### For Section 13 (Business Requirements) — completed v1.21

Section 13 is drafted and authoritative as of v1.21. Pricing locked at Core $4.99/mo ($49.99/yr) and Pro $9.99/mo ($99.99/yr). Multi-account gate permanently dropped at Core. Feature cut-lines drawn for: Premium Scan (Pro), Pantry Scanner (Pro), AI-Drafted Email Replies (Pro), email style learning (Pro), PDF export (Pro; CSV remains Core), unlimited archived history (Pro), higher plan generation/revision quotas (Pro), 30-day historical score immutability (Pro; Core is 7 days), priority support (Pro), and higher chatbot quota (Pro 50/day, Core 10/day). Unit economics modeled at realistic blended user distribution (§13.7). Pricing review cadence every 90 days post-launch. Lite tier deferred to v1.1+ as controlled experiment per §13.14.

---

**2026-04-19 — v1.5** — Section 4.5 (Finance Category) drafted in full, 19 subsections covering overview, detail view layout, privacy posture, account connection/management, four subsystems (Budget, Spending, Bills, Savings & Net Worth), investments placeholder, scoring framework, transaction categorization strategy, manual entry fallback, financial goals framework, integration dependencies (Plaid), AI usage, friction-reduction commitments, accessibility, edge cases, and analytics events. Key decisions locked: "Safe to Spend This Week" as hero metric (forward-looking, actionable, mirrors Nutrition's calories remaining pattern); simple total budget as default with optional category mode; both weekly and monthly budgets with weekly as hero; hybrid transaction categorization (Plaid default → AI gap-fill → user override with rules learning); bills auto-detected from recurring patterns plus manual entry; investments deferred to v2+; net worth as secondary metric with best-effort using Plaid Auth balances in v1 (loans/mortgages manual until Plaid Liabilities product approved); persistent hide-amounts toggle in Finance header; 9 finance goals in v1 library (emergency fund, credit card payoff, specific debt, down payment, vacation, wedding, spending limit, savings rate, budget streak). Major cross-section insight surfaced: per decision #11, the FAB opens the chatbot overlay as universal intelligent input with log shortcuts surrounding it, rather than a menu of separate actions — this simplifies interaction model across all categories and will require retroactive updates to Sections 4.2, 4.3, 4.4 once Section 4.7 (Chatbot) is authoritative. Meta decision: Finance spec assumes Plaid works; if Plaid approval fails before launch, Finance category deferred to v2 entirely. Privacy posture heavily specified given finance sensitivity: redaction of transaction data before AI calls, encrypted Plaid tokens via AWS KMS, read-only access only (APEX cannot move money), one-tap disconnection with 30-day data purge. New database tables: plaid_tokens, connected_accounts, transactions, bills, user_budget, account_balances_history, manual_assets_debts, user_category_rules. Notifications deferred to Section 4.9 (noted as robust cross-domain AI system).

**2026-04-19 — v1.4** — Section 4.4 (Nutrition Category) drafted in full, 20 subsections covering overview, detail view layout, daily summary hero, six input modes (text, voice, photo, barcode, saved meals, pantry scanner), macro tracking and targets, calorie rollover and auto-adjust, edit/delete flows, hydration tracking (opt-in silent default), score framework, history and trends, integration dependencies, AI usage, friction-reduction commitments, accessibility, edge cases, and analytics events. Key decisions locked: calories remaining remains hero metric with animated daily progress ring; no meal timing labels (breakfast/lunch/dinner not required); hydration tracking opt-in and silent by default (no empty state if unused); meal photo scanning upgraded from Opus default to Sonnet 4.6 with Opus available as Pro-tier Premium Scan; pantry scanner becomes Pro-gated feature; barcode scanner falls back to AI estimation when Open Food Facts misses; smart-suggest saved meals with deterministic time-of-day algorithm (5 signals, weighted confidence score, 0.5 threshold); voice input with confirmation preview; current 7 micronutrients preserved (calories, protein, carbs, fat, sugar, fiber, sodium). New: nine-technique prompt engineering strategy for meal scan with detailed spec of portion anchoring, hidden ingredient prompting, confidence scoring, structured JSON output, few-shot examples, and chain-of-thought reasoning. New database tables: custom_products, hydration_logs, suggestion_feedback. Pro tier begins to take shape: unlimited AI queries, pantry scanner, Premium Scan, natural-language plan revision, unlimited history.

**2026-04-19 — v1.3** — Section 4.3 (Fitness Category) drafted in full, 18 subsections covering overview, detail view layout, subsystem model, seven subsystems (Body, Strength, Cardio, Movement, Sleep, Recovery, Plan), score framework, eight logging flows, integration dependencies, AI usage summary, friction-reduction commitments, accessibility, edge cases, and analytics events. Key decisions locked: all seven subsystems visible for all users with empty-state prompts (subsystem modularity: option A); training plan is first subsystem card but not hero (option B); three-path workout logging preserved from Flask (template-driven, repeat last, freestyle with AI parse); Strava-equivalent cardio experience for integration-connected users; automatic PR detection with celebration (no push notification); weight prompts only for users with weight-related goals or smart scales; sleep is integration-only (no manual entry); Recovery subsystem always visible with educational prompt; 90-day history baseline with Pro unlimited (cut-line deferred to Section 13); new AI workout parsing call (~$0.002/call, counted against paywall). New database tables specified: body_weight_logs, body_measurements, strength_workouts, strength_sets, exercise_library, personal_records, daily_movement, sleep_logs, recovery_logs, training_plans, plan_adherence. Existing Flask features preserved: AI burn estimation, label shortening, comprehensive plan generation, plan revision, rest timer, workout detail views.

**2026-04-18 — v1.2** — Consolidated v1.1 (full onboarding specification) and new Section 4.2 (Home Dashboard) into a single living markdown document. All 16 subsections of Section 4.1 restored in full from v1.1. Sections 1.5, 1.7, 1.8, 1.9 (competitive table), and 3.3/3.4/3.5/3.6/3.7 restored to full content where previously truncated. Section 4.2 decisions locked: four-category dashboard with raw supporting data under scores; FAB pattern for logging and chat; no Today's Focus card (killed); no daily check-in (killed); no drill chevrons; scoring framework referenced but composition deferred to Section 9 (new Scoring Engine section); goals framework referenced but library deferred to Section 4.10. Table of contents restructured to split Scoring Engine (§9) from AI Requirements (§10) — cleaner separation between deterministic math and AI calls.

**2026-04-18 — v1.1** — Section 4.1 (Onboarding Flow) drafted in full. Incorporated existing PWA onboarding screens as baseline with 7 approved improvements: username→email, tab order reversed, tighter privacy microcopy, stronger plan voice, autocomplete for occupation, discrete stress options replacing 0-10 slider, and defensive latency copy preserved. Paywall specified as post-onboarding trigger (3 free AI interactions, then paywall on 4th). Workout Builder specified as optional 8-step sub-flow. Integration Connect specified as pattern with specific integrations deferred to Section 8.

**2026-04-18 — v1.0** — Document initialized with Sections 1, 2, and 3. Section 3 (Design Principles) captures the eight non-negotiable constraints that govern all downstream decisions. Four-category structure (Fitness, Nutrition, Finance, Life) locked in throughout.
